**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute wasted space per block.**
The actual bytes occupied by 10 records is 10 ├Ś 100 = 1000 bytes. The remaining 24 bytes per block are wasted.

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= B - (\text{bfr} \times R) \\[6pt]
&= 1024 - (10 \times 100) \\[6pt]
&= 1024 - 1000 \\[6pt]
&= 24 \text{ bytes per block}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute block utilization.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= \dfrac{\text{bfr} \times R}{B} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{1000}{1024} \\[6pt]
&\approx 0.9766 \text{ or } 97.66\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Sanity check (KTU standard practice).**
Total file size = 3000 blocks ├Ś 1024 bytes = 3,072,000 bytes. Effective data = 30,000 ├Ś 100 = 3,000,000 bytes. Wasted total = 3,072,000 ŌłÆ 3,000,000 = 72,000 bytes = 3000 blocks ├Ś 24 bytes/block. Ō£ō

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Spanned vs. Unspanned ŌĆö Numerical Comparison

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> Same EMPLOYEE file, but record size is now **R = 350 bytes**, block size **B = 1024 bytes**, and pointer size **P = 4 bytes** (spanned case). Compare unspanned vs. spanned.

### Unspanned Case
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{bfr}_{\text{unspanned}} &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{1024}{350} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 2.928 \right\rfloor = 2 \text{ records/block} \\[6pt]
W_{\text{unspanned}} &= 1024 - (2 \times 350) = 1024 - 700 = 324 \text{ bytes/block} \\[6pt]
U_{\text{unspanned}} &= \dfrac{700}{1024} \approx 68.36\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Spanned Case
For spanned records, the effective record size becomes R + P (record plus pointer), so the blocking factor is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{bfr}_{\text{spanned}} &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{B}{R + P} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \dfrac{1024}{350 + 4} \right\rfloor \\[6pt]
&= \left\lfloor \dfrac{1024}{354} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 2.892 \right\rfloor = 2 \text{ records/block}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Wait ŌĆö** the pointer cost reduces bfr. But spanning allows a 3rd record's tail to occupy the 324 leftover bytes. So *spanning* gives effective storage for *more than 2 records per block* on average. The KTU treatment is:

> **Spanned allows the leftover space of 324 bytes to hold part of a 3rd record**, so the **average effective bfr** is between 2 and 3 records/block, mathematically:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Effective bfr (spanned)} &\approx \dfrac{B}{R} = \dfrac{1024}{350} \approx 2.93 \text{ records/block} \\[6pt]
U_{\text{spanned}} &\approx \dfrac{2.93 \times 350}{1024} \approx 100\% \text{ (approaches full)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Metric | Unspanned | Spanned |
|---|---|---|
| bfr (integer per block) | 2 | 2 (with cross-block tail) |
| Wasted space per block | 324 bytes | ~0 bytes (on average) |
| Block utilization | 68.36 % | ~100 % |
| Need for inter-block pointer? | No | Yes (4 bytes per spanned record tail) |
| Implementation complexity | Low | High (chain following) |
| Best suited for | Fixed-size records | Variable-length / large records |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture ŌĆö File Organization Decision Flow

This Mermaid diagram maps the *decision logic* a DBMS storage engine follows when a record is inserted, and how the choice of pinned vs. unpinned organization determines the write path.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Application Issues INSERT] --> B{Organization Type}
    B -->|Pinned Fixed| C[Append at EOF]
    B -->|Unpinned Dynamic| D[Consult Free-Slot Directory]
    C --> E[Compute Byte Offset = slotNo x R]
    D --> F{Free Slot Available}
    F -->|Yes| G[Write into First Free Slot]
    F -->|No| H[Append at EOF then Update Directory]
    E --> I[Write Record to Disk Block]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Return RID to Application]
    K[Application Issues DELETE] --> L{Organization Type}
    L -->|Pinned Fixed| M[Mark Deletion Flag in Slot]
    L -->|Unpinned Dynamic| N[Free Slot, Add to Free-Slot List]
    M --> O[Space NOT Reusable]
    N --> P[Space REUSABLE for Next INSERT]
```

## 4.2 Block Layout Diagram ŌĆö Blocking Factor Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BLK[Disk Block B = 1024 bytes]
        R1[Record 1 - 100 B] --> R2[Record 2 - 100 B]
        R2 --> R3[Record 3 - 100 B]
        R3 --> R4[Record 4 - 100 B]
        R4 --> R5[Record 5 - 100 B]
        R5 --> R6[Record 6 - 100 B]
        R6 --> R7[Record 7 - 100 B]
        R7 --> R8[Record 8 - 100 B]
        R8 --> R9[Record 9 - 100 B]
        R9 --> R10[Record 10 - 100 B]
        R10 --> WASTE[Wasted Space - 24 B]
    end
```

**Reading the diagram:** 10 records of 100 bytes each occupy 1000 bytes, leaving 24 bytes of unusable space at the tail of the block ŌĆö this is the *internal fragmentation* penalty of unspanned blocking.

## 4.3 Pinned vs Unpinned ŌĆö State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SlotOccupied: INSERT new record
    SlotOccupied --> SlotMarkedDeleted: DELETE issued
    SlotMarkedDeleted --> SlotOccupied: Pinned (no reuse)
    SlotMarkedDeleted --> SlotFree: Unpinned (added to free list)
    SlotFree --> SlotOccupied: Unpinned (next INSERT consumes it)
    SlotFree --> SlotFree: Pinned (slot remains free but unused)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the state machine:** In **Pinned** mode, once a record is deleted, the state never transitions to *SlotFree* (from a reusability perspective) ŌĆö the slot is wasted. In **Unpinned** mode, the cycle *SlotMarkedDeleted ŌåÆ SlotFree ŌåÆ SlotOccupied* permits a new record to occupy the same physical bytes.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024 Style]

**Q1. Define blocking factor. Why can it not be a non-integer?**
*(3 marks ŌĆö CO1, Bloom Level: Remember/Understand)*

**Model Answer:**
The **blocking factor (bfr)** of a file is defined as the number of logical records of a file that can be stored in one physical disk block. It is given by the formula:

$$
\text{bfr} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{B}{R} \right\rfloor
$$

where B is the block size and R is the record size. The blocking factor is **always a non-negative integer** because we cannot store a *fraction* of a record in a block under the standard unspanned organization; the floor function ensures we only count whole records. The fractional part of B/R represents the unused bytes at the tail of the block ŌĆö this is *internal fragmentation*.

---

**Q2. Differentiate between pinned and unpinned record organization.**
*(3 marks ŌĆö CO1, Bloom Level: Understand)*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Pinned (Fixed) Organization | Unpinned (Variable) Organization |
|---|---|---|
| Record position | Fixed at insertion, never moves | Records can move to any free slot |
| Deletion handling | Slot is marked deleted but **not reused** | Slot is added to **free-slot list**, reused |
| Space utilization | Suboptimal ŌĆö wastes space over time | Optimal ŌĆö empty slots are recycled |
| Address computation | O(1) direct offset | O(1) with directory lookup |
| Overhead | Low | High (directory maintenance) |
| Best for | Read-heavy, append-only workloads | Update-heavy, mixed workloads |

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Question Paper Style]
> *Answer any ONE full question from Q3A or Q3B. Each carries 14 marks split into two 7-mark sub-parts.*

---

### ŌØō Question 3A (14 marks)

**(a)** Consider a file of **r = 50,000** fixed-length records. Each record is **R = 200 bytes** long. The disk block size is **B = 4096 bytes**. Compute:
   (i) The blocking factor bfr
   (ii) The number of disk blocks b required
   (iii) The wasted space per block
   (iv) The block utilization percentage
   (7 marks ŌĆö CO1, Bloom Level: Apply/Analyze)

**(b)** Explain with a neat diagram **pinned** and **unpinned** record organization. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each.
   (7 marks ŌĆö CO1, Bloom Level: Understand)

#### Model Solution for Q3A(a)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute blocking factor.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{bfr} &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{B}{R} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \dfrac{4096}{200} \right\rfloor \\[6pt]
&= \left\lfloor 20.48 \right\rfloor = 20 \text{ records/block}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Number of blocks.**
$$
b = \left\lceil \dfrac{r}{\text{bfr}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{50{,}000}{20} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 2500 \right\rceil = 2500 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Wasted space per block.**
$$
W = B - (\text{bfr} \times R) = 4096 - (20 \times 200) = 4096 - 4000 = 96 \text{ bytes/block}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Block utilization.**
$$
U = \dfrac{\text{bfr} \times R}{B} \times 100\% = \dfrac{4000}{4096} \times 100\% \approx 97.66\%
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Sanity check.**
Total file size = 2500 ├Ś 4096 = 10,240,000 bytes. Effective data = 50,000 ├Ś 200 = 10,000,000 bytes. Wasted total = 240,000 bytes = 2500 ├Ś 96 bytes. Ō£ō

#### Model Solution for Q3A(b)

Refer to the state diagram and concept table in **Section 1.2** and **Section 4.3** above. The student must draw:

- A file showing fixed slots for **pinned** with deleted slot marked but not reused.
- A file showing dynamic slots for **unpinned** with a free-slot list pointing to deleted positions.

> **Pinned ŌĆö** Advantage: O(1) address calculation; Disadvantage: wasted space.
> **Unpinned ŌĆö** Advantage: optimal space utilization; Disadvantage: directory overhead and increased complexity.

---

### ŌØō Question 3B (14 marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice

**(a)** A file has **r = 1,00,000** records, each of size **R = 250 bytes**. The block size is **B = 2048 bytes**. An inter-block pointer of **P = 8 bytes** is required for spanned records.
   (i) Compute unspanned bfr and total blocks.
   (ii) Compute spanned bfr and the total number of blocks.
   (iii) Which organization uses fewer blocks? Justify.
   (7 marks ŌĆö CO1, Bloom Level: Apply/Analyze)

**(b)** Define **file organization**. Discuss any **four** file organization techniques with one-line merits and demerits.
   (7 marks ŌĆö CO1, Bloom Level: Remember/Understand)

#### Model Solution for Q3B(a)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Unspanned case.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{bfr}_{\text{unspanned}} &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{2048}{250} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 8.192 \right\rfloor = 8 \text{ records/block} \\[6pt]
b_{\text{unspanned}} &= \left\lceil \dfrac{1{,}00{,}000}{8} \right\rceil = 12{,}500 \text{ blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

Wasted per block = 2048 ŌłÆ (8 ├Ś 250) = 2048 ŌłÆ 2000 = **48 bytes/block**.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Spanned case.**
The effective record size with pointer is R + P = 250 + 8 = 258 bytes.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{bfr}_{\text{spanned}} &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{2048}{258} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 7.938 \right\rfloor = 7 \text{ records per full-block set} \\[6pt]
\text{Bytes used per block} &= 7 \times 258 = 1806 \text{ bytes} \\[6pt]
\text{Free space per block} &= 2048 - 1806 = 242 \text{ bytes (holds record tail)}
\end{aligned}
$$

On average, spanning uses approximately 2048/250 Ōēł 8.192 records/block. So:
$$
b_{\text{spanned}} \approx \left\lceil \dfrac{1{,}00{,}000}{8.192} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 12{,}207 \right\rceil \approx 12{,}207 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Comparison.**
Spanned organization uses **~12,207 blocks** vs. unspanned's **12,500 blocks**, a saving of ~293 blocks (Ōēł 2.34%).

> **Conclusion:** Spanned organization is more space-efficient; unspanned is simpler to implement.

#### Model Solution for Q3B(b)

**File organization** refers to the way data records are physically arranged on secondary storage to facilitate efficient retrieval, insertion, deletion, and update operations.

| Technique | Merit (1 line) | Demerit (1 line) |
|---|---|---|
| **Heap (Unordered) File** | Fastest insertion (append to end) | Slow retrieval ŌĆö full file scan required |
| **Sequential (Ordered) File** | Fast sequential read & range queries | Expensive inserts/deletes ŌĆö requires file reorganization |
| **Hashed File** | O(1) direct access on hashed key | Cannot support range queries |
| **B+ Tree File** | Balanced ŌĆö fast search, insert, delete, and range | Slight overhead for tree node maintenance |

---

## 5.3 ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions (KTU Board Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Using round() instead of floor():** KTU strictly expects the `ŌīŖ Ōīŗ` floor notation. Writing "bfr = 10.24 Ōēł 10" is penalized. Use `ŌīŖB/RŌīŗ = ŌīŖ10.24Ōīŗ = 10`.
> 2. **Forgetting ceiling for blocks:** If r is not exactly divisible by bfr, students often write `r/bfr` directly. Always use `Ōīłr/bfrŌīē` for the number of blocks.
> 3. **Confusing pinned with fixed-length:** "Pinned" does **not** mean fixed-length record. It means fixed **position**. A pinned file can still have variable-length records (rare in practice, but conceptually possible).
> 4. **Omitting the wasted space calculation:** Examiners frequently allocate 1 mark to wasted space. Skipping it is a guaranteed mark loss.
> 5. **Drawing wrong diagrams:** In Q3A(b), students often draw the file as a *list* rather than a *block-grid* with a free-slot list pointer for unpinned. Use proper block diagrams with **arrow pointers** for free-slot lists.
> 6. **Not showing units:** "bfr = 10" is incomplete. Write **"bfr = 10 records/block"**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> Ō£ģ **Rapid Revision Checklist (Board-Exam Ready)**

- **Blocking Factor (bfr)** = Number of records fitting in one block. Formula: $\text{bfr} = \lfloor B/R \rfloor$.
- **Number of blocks** needed: $b = \lceil r / \text{bfr} \rceil$.
- **Block utilization** $U = (\text{bfr} \times R)/B$. Always between 0 and 1.
- **Wasted space per block** $W = B - (\text{bfr} \times R)$.
- **Larger bfr** ŌćÆ Fewer blocks ŌćÆ Faster sequential scan, slower single-record update.
- **Smaller bfr** ŌćÆ More blocks ŌćÆ Slower scan, faster single-record update.
- **Unspanned:** Record cannot cross block boundary ŌĆö simple but wastes space.
- **Spanned:** Record can span two blocks via pointer ŌĆö efficient but complex.
- **Pinned organization:** Records are *immovable*; deleted slots are **not reused**. Append-only for inserts.
- **Unpinned organization:** Records are *movable*; deleted slots enter a **free-slot list** and are reused for new inserts.
- **Pinned ŌåÆ O(1) address** calculation, no directory, but suffers from **space wastage** over time.
- **Unpinned ŌåÆ Needs a slot directory**, but **optimizes space** utilization.
- **Standard values** to memorize: B Ōłł {512 B, 1 KB, 2 KB, 4 KB, 8 KB, 16 KB}.
- **Korth's notation** uses `f` for blocking factor; **Elmasri/Navathe** uses `bfr`. KTU 2024 follows **bfr**.
- **Pointer overhead** in spanned records: typically 4ŌĆō8 bytes per spanned record.
- **KTU marks split:** 2 marks for formula, 2 marks for computation, 1 mark for units, 1 mark for justification/wasted space, 1 mark for final answer.
- **Always use LaTeX floor `ŌīŖ Ōīŗ` and ceiling `Ōīł Ōīē` symbols** in the KTU answer sheet for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heap Files
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Heap Files

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Heap File** (also called an *unordered file* or *pile file*) is the simplest and most basic file organization used in a Database Management System (DBMS). In a heap file organization, records are inserted into the file without any specific ordering or clustering constraint. The data is placed at the **end of the file** in the order of insertion, much like a stack or a pile of papers on a desk.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PBEOT504 - Module 1):**
> A heap file is a collection of records (tuples) stored on disk pages where there is no inherent order imposed on the records based on any search key. Every new record is appended to the last available slot on the last page, or to a new page if no free space exists.

Mathematically, if a heap file $F$ contains a set of records $R = \{r_1, r_2, r_3, \dots, r_n\}$, the insertion order does not satisfy any total order relation:

$$\forall i, j : 1 \le i < j \le n, \quad \text{key}(r_i) \not\lessgtr \text{key}(r_j)$$

This means that for any two records $r_i$ and $r_j$ where $i < j$ (insertion order), there is **no guarantee** that the key value of $r_i$ is greater than, less than, or equal to that of $r_j$.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Library Drop-Box

Imagine a **college library book return drop-box** at the end of the day. Students return books throughout the day and simply **drop them on top of the pile** in no particular order. The librarian then must:
- Take the entire pile, sort it manually, and place books on the correct shelf.
- Or, search through the whole pile to find a specific book when a student requests it.

This is **exactly how a heap file works**:
- Books = **Records (tuples/rows)**
- The drop-box pile = **The Heap File**
- The shelf (where books belong in order) = **An Ordered File Organization**
- The librarian searching the whole pile = **Full File Scan / Linear Search**

When a query asks for a book titled "Database Systems," the librarian must sift through every book. This is inefficient ŌĆö and the same drawback applies to DBMS heap files when used for point queries or range queries.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is it called "Heap"?** The term *heap* here refers to an unorganized, piled-up data structure (like a heap of papers). It is **NOT** related to the *heap data structure* (binary heap) used in algorithms and priority queues, which is a complete binary tree.

---

### 1.3 Key Terminology Table

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Record (Tuple)** | A single row of data containing related field values |
| **Page (Block)** | The basic unit of I/O transfer between disk and memory, typically **4 KB** or **8 KB** |
| **Slot Directory** | A per-page header array storing offsets of records within the page |
| **Free Space Map (FSM)** | A data structure tracking which pages have available free space for new inserts |
| **Record ID (RID)** | A unique physical address of a record: `(PageID, SlotID)` |
| **Linked List of Pages** | A chain of pages forming the file's internal structure |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiners' Highlight:** In KTU exams, the term *page* and *block* are used interchangeably. The standard page size in PostgreSQL is **8 KB**, while in MySQL InnoDB it is **16 KB**. Always quote the DBMS context if a specific value is needed in numerical problems.

---

### 1.4 Insertion, Deletion & Retrieval ŌĆö At a Glance

- **Insertion:** *Very Fast* ŌĆö Append the new record to the last available space. Cost Ōēł **1 page I/O** (best case).
- **Retrieval (Search):** *Very Slow* ŌĆö Must scan every page linearly. Cost Ōēł **N / 2 pages** for average case (where $N$ is the total number of pages).
- **Deletion:** *Moderate* ŌĆö Mark the slot as empty in the slot directory; record remains physically until reorganization.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Internal Architecture of a Heap File

A heap file is stored as a collection of **disk pages**. Each page has the following internal layout:

```
ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ
Ōöé           PAGE HEADER                      Ōöé
Ōöé  - Page ID, Type, Free Space Pointer       Ōöé
Ōöé  - Number of records (slot count)          Ōöé
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöż
Ōöé         SLOT DIRECTORY (Top-Down)          Ōöé
Ōöé  Slot 0 : offset = 100, length = 56        Ōöé
Ōöé  Slot 1 : offset = 156, length = 56        Ōöé
Ōöé  Slot 2 : FREE (deleted)                   Ōöé
Ōöé  Slot 3 : offset = 212, length = 56        Ōöé
Ōöé  ...                                        Ōöé
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöż
Ōöé        RECORD DATA AREA (Bottom-Up)        Ōöé
Ōöé     [Record 0][Record 1][Record 3]...      Ōöé
ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś
```

The **slot directory grows downward** from the page header, while **records grow upward** from the bottom of the page. The two regions grow toward each other until all space is consumed.

---

### 2.2 Implementation Variants of Heap Files

A DBMS implements heap files using one of three classical strategies:

#### 2.2.1 Linked List of Pages (Simplest)

The file's first page is the **directory header page**, which points to the first data page. Each data page contains:
- A pointer (`next_page`) to the subsequent data page.
- A pointer (`prev_page`) to the previous data page (for doubly-linked variant).

**Insertion Process (Logical Steps):**
1. Check the directory for a page with free space using the **Free Space Map**.
2. If a page with sufficient space exists ŌåÆ insert the record there.
3. If no page has space ŌåÆ allocate a new page, link it to the list, and insert.
4. Update the Free Space Map.

**Disadvantage:** Finding a page with free space requires either a full scan of all pages (expensive) or maintaining a separate Free Space Map (extra I/O cost).

#### 2.2.2 Page Directory (Array of Page Entries)

Maintain an array of directory entries ŌĆö one per page ŌĆö stored in the directory header page. Each entry stores the page ID and the **amount of free space** on that page.

**Insertion Process:**
1. Scan the directory to find a page with free space $\geq$ record size.
2. Read the page, insert the record.
3. Update the directory entry with the new free space value.

**Disadvantage:** Directory itself can grow large; may itself need to be stored on multiple pages.

#### 2.2.3 Free Space Map with Bitmap

A **bitmap** (or array of counters) where the $i$-th bit/entry represents whether page $i$ has free space available. This is the most space-efficient and is used in modern systems like **PostgreSQL's FSM**.

For a file with $N$ pages, the bitmap size is:

$$\text{Bitmap Size} = N \text{ bits} = \left\lceil \frac{N}{8} \right\rceil \text{ bytes}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favorite Question Pattern:** "Explain the three implementations of heap files. Compare their search cost for free-space." This is a frequently asked **Part A (3-mark)** or **Part B (14-mark)** topic.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table provides every formula and I/O cost model that a KTU examiner can ask on heap file organization. The cost is measured in **number of disk page I/Os**.

| Operation | Best Case | Average Case | Worst Case | Formula / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Insertion** | **1 I/O** | **1 I/O** | **2 I/O** (if new page allocation) | $C_{ins} = 1$ (append) or $2$ (allocate page + write) |
| **Equality Search** ($Q_E$) | **1 I/O** (record on first page) | $\frac{N}{2}$ I/O | $N$ I/O (record on last page) | $C_{eq} = \frac{N}{2}$ |
| **Range Search** ($Q_R$) | $b_r$ I/O | $\frac{N}{2} + b_r$ I/O | $N$ I/O | $b_r$ = number of pages containing matching records |
| **Deletion** | **1 I/O** (in-place tombstone) | **1 I/O + 1 reorganization** | **$N$ I/O** (full reorganization) | Tombstone is preferred over physical delete |
| **Update (key field)** | N/A | **$\ge N$ I/O** | $N$ I/O + write | Update changes the RID ŌåÆ file reorganization required |
| **Sequential Scan** | $N$ I/O | $N$ I/O | $N$ I/O | $C_{seq} = N$ (mandatory) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation Key for the table above:**
> - $N$ = Total number of pages in the heap file
> - $b_r$ = Number of result pages (pages containing qualifying tuples)
> - All I/O counts are measured in **logical page reads/writes**
> - A *tombstone* is a marker that logically deletes a record without removing it physically

---

### 2.4 Free Space Management Mathematics

Given a record of size $R$ bytes and a page of size $P$ bytes, the **maximum number of records per page** is:

$$n_{max} = \left\lfloor \frac{P - H}{R + S} \right\rfloor$$

Where:
- $P$ = Page size in bytes
- $H$ = Page header size (typically **40ŌĆō64 bytes**)
- $R$ = Record size in bytes
- $S$ = Slot directory entry size (typically **4 bytes**)

**Total file size** in bytes for $M$ records:

$$\text{File Size} = \left\lceil \frac{M}{n_{max}} \right\rceil \times P$$

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Heap files are the *default storage* in most RDBMSs (PostgreSQL, MySQL, Oracle, SQL Server) because they provide the **fastest possible inserts**. When the table has no primary key queries that benefit from ordering, or when it is used as a staging/log table, heap files are the optimal choice. This is the same logic behind the *write-optimized* LSM-trees in NoSQL systems like Cassandra and RocksDB.

---

### 2.5 Why Choose a Heap File? The Engineering Trade-off

| Workload Type | Heap File Suitability |
|---|---|
| Bulk insertion (e.g., logs, IoT sensor data) | **Excellent** ŌĆö appends are O(1) |
| Frequent full table scans (analytics) | **Good** ŌĆö sequential reads are optimal |
| Point queries on a key (SELECT WHERE id=?) | **Poor** ŌĆö requires full scan |
| Range queries (BETWEEN, >, <) | **Very Poor** ŌĆö no clustering |
| OLTP with primary-key lookups | **Poor** ŌĆö use a hash or B+ tree index instead |
| Staging tables, temp tables, audit logs | **Excellent** ŌĆö exactly the intended use case |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Average Search Cost in a Heap File

We derive the **expected number of page I/Os** to find a single record using an equality search on a non-key attribute. This is the most fundamental calculation tested in KTU exams.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define the search model.**

Consider a heap file with $N$ pages. We want to find *any one* record matching a non-unique search key. The matching records can exist on any of the $N$ pages with **uniform probability**.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute probability of finding the record on page $k$.**

Since records are scattered uniformly:

$$P(\text{record on page } k) = \frac{1}{N}, \quad \text{for } k = 1, 2, 3, \dots, N$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute cost of finding the record on page $k$.**

If the record is on page $k$, we must scan pages $1, 2, \dots, k$ before finding it. The I/O cost is therefore exactly $k$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply the definition of expected value.**

$$E[\text{Cost}] = \sum_{k=1}^{N} k \cdot P(\text{record on page } k)$$

Substituting the probability:

$$E[\text{Cost}] = \sum_{k=1}^{N} k \cdot \frac{1}{N}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Use the arithmetic series formula.**

The sum of the first $N$ positive integers is:

$$\sum_{k=1}^{N} k = \frac{N(N+1)}{2}$$

Therefore:

$$E[\text{Cost}] = \frac{1}{N} \cdot \frac{N(N+1)}{2}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Simplify.**

$$E[\text{Cost}] = \frac{N+1}{2}$$

For large $N$, this is asymptotically:

$$E[\text{Cost}] \approx \frac{N}{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** The average I/O cost of an equality search in a heap file is $\dfrac{N+1}{2} \approx \dfrac{N}{2}$ page reads.

**Numerical Example:** If a heap file occupies $N = 1000$ pages, the average search requires **500.5 page I/Os** ŌĆö a clear indication of why heap files are not used directly for point lookups.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: File Size and Storage Efficiency

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define the parameters.**

Let:
- Page size: $P = 4096$ bytes (4 KB)
- Record size: $R = 200$ bytes
- Header size per page: $H = 64$ bytes
- Slot directory entry size: $S = 4$ bytes
- Total records to store: $M = 100,000$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute records per page.**

$$n_{max} = \left\lfloor \frac{P - H}{R + S} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{4096 - 64}{200 + 4} \right\rfloor$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Evaluate the numerator and denominator.**

$$n_{max} = \left\lfloor \frac{4032}{204} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 19.76 \right\rfloor = 19 \text{ records per page}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute total pages required.**

$$N = \left\lceil \frac{M}{n_{max}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{100{,}000}{19} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 5263.16 \right\rceil = 5264 \text{ pages}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute total file size in bytes.**

$$\text{File Size} = 5264 \times 4096 = 21{,}565{,}344 \text{ bytes} \approx 20.57 \text{ MB}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Compute storage efficiency.**

Useful data bytes: $M \times R = 100{,}000 \times 200 = 20{,}000{,}000$ bytes

$$\text{Efficiency} = \frac{20{,}000{,}000}{21{,}565{,}344} \times 100\% \approx 92.74\%$$

The remaining ~7.26% is overhead from page headers, slot directory, and unused space on the last page.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of File Organizations

The following table consolidates the cost model for **Heap**, **Sorted**, and **Hashed** file organizations across the four fundamental operations. This is one of the highest-weightage questions in KTU Module 1.

| Operation | Heap File | Sorted File | Hashed File |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Equality Search on Key** | $\dfrac{N}{2}$ I/O | $\log_2 N + \lceil \dfrac{b_r}{f_r} \rceil$ I/O (binary search) | $\sim 1.2$ I/O avg, $N$ I/O worst |
| **Range Search** | $N$ I/O (full scan) | $\log_2 N + b_r$ I/O | $N$ I/O (hash destroys order) |
| **Insertion** | $1$ I/O (append) | $\log_2 N + b_r + 1$ (locate + shift) | $\sim 1$ I/O (with overflow chains) |
| **Deletion** | $1$ I/O (tombstone) | $\log_2 N + b_r$ (locate + shift) | $\sim 1$ I/O |

Where:
- $N$ = total pages, $b_r$ = result pages, $f_r$ = records per page

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked "Which file organization is best for query $X$?", first identify the **query type** (point vs. range), then match to the right column. Heap = write-heavy; Sorted = range-heavy; Hashed = point-lookup heavy.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Logical Layout of a Heap File

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the high-level logical layout of a heap file with its directory and data pages.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Database Catalog] --> B[File Header Page]
    B --> C[Free Space Map FSM]
    B --> D[Data Page 1]
    B --> E[Data Page 2]
    B --> F[Data Page 3]
    B --> G[Data Page N]

    D --> D1[Slot 0: Record R1]
    D --> D2[Slot 1: Record R4]
    D --> D3[Slot 2: Free]
    D --> D4[Slot 3: Record R7]

    E --> E1[Slot 0: Record R2]
    E --> E2[Slot 1: Record R5]
    E --> E3[Slot 2: Record R8]

    F --> F1[Slot 0: Record R3]
    F --> F2[Slot 1: Free]
    F --> F3[Slot 2: Record R6]
```

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of an Insertion Operation

This block diagram traces the data flow when a new record is inserted into a heap file.

```mermaid
graph LR
    stepA[Client issues INSERT] --> stepB[Buffer Manager requests page]
    stepB --> stepC{Check Free Space Map}
    stepC -->|Page Found| stepD[Read Data Page into Buffer]
    stepC -->|No Free Page| stepE[Allocate New Page]
    stepE --> stepF[Link New Page to File List]
    stepF --> stepD
    stepD --> stepG[Append Record to Slot Directory]
    stepG --> stepH[Update Free Space Map Entry]
    stepH --> stepI[Mark Buffer Page as Dirty]
    stepI --> stepJ[Return Success to Client]
```

---

### 4.3 Internal Structure of a Single Heap File Page

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph pageA[Heap File Page Size 4 KB]
        hdr[Page Header - 64 bytes]
        slot[Slot Directory - grows downward]
        rec1[Record 1 - 200 bytes]
        rec2[Record 2 - 200 bytes]
        rec3[Record 3 - 200 bytes]
        free[Free Space in Middle]
    end
    hdr --> slot
    rec1 --> free
    rec2 --> free
    rec3 --> free
    free --> rec1
```

---

### 4.4 Heap File vs. Sorted File ŌĆö Conceptual Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph heap[Heap File Storage Order]
        h1[Page 1: 7, 2, 9]
        h2[Page 2: 4, 1, 6]
        h3[Page 3: 8, 3, 5]
    end
    subgraph sorted[Sorted File Storage Order]
        s1[Page 1: 1, 2, 3]
        s2[Page 2: 4, 5, 6]
        s3[Page 3: 7, 8, 9]
    end
```

The left sub-graph shows heap file with **no ordering**; the right sub-graph shows sorted file with **monotonically increasing key values** across pages. The sorted file enables binary search (O(log N)) but pays a heavy insertion cost.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1:** *Define a heap file. Why is it called "unordered"?* `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **heap file** is a file organization in which records are stored without any ordering on the values of the search key. The new records are inserted at the end of the file (or in any available free slot) in the order of their arrival.

It is called *unordered* because there is **no relationship** between the logical order of records (based on the primary key or any other attribute) and their physical placement on disk. Two consecutively inserted records may have completely unrelated key values.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse the *heap file* with the *heap data structure* (binary heap used in priority queues). They are **entirely different concepts** and the KTU examiner will deduct marks if the answer references a binary tree or priority queue.

---

#### **Question 2:** *What is the role of a Free Space Map (FSM) in heap file management?* `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Free Space Map (FSM)** is a metadata structure maintained by the DBMS that tracks which pages in a heap file have enough free space to accommodate a new record. It avoids the need to scan all pages of the file to find one with available space.

The FSM is typically implemented as a **bitmap** or a **list of (page\_id, free\_bytes) pairs**. When a record is inserted, the DBMS consults the FSM to locate a suitable page; after insertion, the FSM is updated.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question ŌĆö Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

#### **Question A (Choice 1):** *Discuss in detail the various implementation strategies of heap file organization. Compare their efficiency for insertion and search.* `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]`

**Sub-Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks:** *Explain the three main implementations of heap files with neat diagrams.*

**Model Solution:**

The three classical implementations of heap files are:

**1. Linked List of Pages**

The simplest form. A directory header points to the first data page. Each data page contains a pointer to the next page (and optionally the previous page for doubly-linked variant). To insert:
- Scan the linked list starting from the first page until a page with free space is found.
- If no such page exists by the end of the list ŌåÆ allocate a new page and link it.

**Disadvantage:** The free-space search is **O(N)** in the worst case.

**2. Page Directory (Directory Header)**

The directory is an array of entries, one per data page. Each entry stores the **page ID** and the **amount of free space** on that page. The directory itself is stored in a header page.

Insertion:
- Read the directory page into memory.
- Scan the array for an entry with free space $\geq$ record size.
- If found ŌåÆ fetch that page and insert.
- If not found ŌåÆ allocate a new page, append to directory, insert.

**Disadvantage:** The directory can grow beyond one page, requiring a hierarchical or B+ tree organization for the directory itself.

**3. Free Space Map (Bitmap)**

A bitmap with one bit per page (or $k$ bits storing a category like 0%, <25%, <50%, etc.) is maintained. The bitmap is compact and can be searched quickly. Used in modern systems like **PostgreSQL**.

The number of bits needed is equal to the number of pages $N$:

$$\text{Bitmap size} = N \text{ bits} = \left\lceil \frac{N}{8} \right\rceil \text{ bytes}$$

**Sub-Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks:** *Compare their efficiency for insertion and search operations using I/O cost analysis.*

**Model Solution:**

| Implementation | Free-Space Search Cost | Insertion Cost | Search Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Linked List of Pages** | $O(N)$ in worst case | Up to $N + 1$ I/O (scan + insert) | $N$ I/O (full scan) |
| **Page Directory** | $O(D)$ where $D$ = directory pages | $1 + 1 = 2$ I/O (directory + data page) | $N$ I/O (full scan) |
| **Free Space Map (Bitmap)** | $O(1)$ (in-memory scan) | $1 + 1 = 2$ I/O (FSM + data page) | $N$ I/O (full scan) |

**Key Observations:**
- All three implementations have the **same search cost for a record** (linear scan of all data pages).
- They differ only in the cost of finding a page for insertion.
- The **bitmap/FSM** is the most efficient and is the de-facto standard in modern RDBMS.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2):** *A heap file occupies 5000 pages. Compute (a) the average cost of an equality search, and (b) the worst-case cost. Compare the cost if a sorted file is used with binary search.* `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`

**Sub-Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks:** *Compute the average and worst-case equality search cost in the heap file.*

**Model Solution:**

Given: $N = 5000$ pages.

**Average case (heap file):**

Using the formula derived in Section 3.1:

$$E[\text{Cost}] = \frac{N + 1}{2} = \frac{5000 + 1}{2} = 2500.5 \approx 2501 \text{ I/Os}$$

**Worst case (heap file):**

The record is on the last page:

$$C_{worst} = N = 5000 \text{ I/Os}$$

**Sub-Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks:** *Compute the cost if a sorted file is used with binary search on the same dataset.*

**Model Solution:**

For a sorted file of $N$ pages, binary search requires:

$$C_{binary} = \left\lceil \log_2 N \right\rceil + 1$$

(The $+1$ accounts for fetching the page that contains the result.)

For $N = 5000$:

$$C_{binary} = \left\lceil \log_2 5000 \right\rceil + 1 = \left\lceil 12.29 \right\rceil + 1 = 13 + 1 = 14 \text{ I/Os}$$

**Comparison:**

$$\text{Speedup} = \frac{2500.5}{14} \approx 178.6 \times$$

The sorted file with binary search is **~178 times faster** for equality searches on a 5000-page file. This clearly demonstrates the disadvantage of heap files for point queries and motivates the use of indexes.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Warning ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks:**
> - Forgetting the **$+1$** in the binary search cost (it represents the final page fetch to read the actual record).
> - Using $\log_2$ without ceiling function, which gives non-integer page counts.
> - In part (a), students often confuse **average** with **worst case** and write the wrong formula. Remember: $N/2$ is *average*, $N$ is *worst case*.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for Heap Files:**

- **Definition:** Heap file = unordered file; records inserted at the end, no key-based ordering.
- **Default in RDBMS:** PostgreSQL, MySQL, Oracle, and SQL Server all use heap files as the default table organization.
- **Three Implementations:** (1) Linked List of Pages, (2) Page Directory, (3) Free Space Map / Bitmap.
- **Slot Directory:** Per-page metadata that maps slot IDs to record offsets; allows logical deletion via tombstone.
- **Insertion Cost:** $\sim 1$ I/O ŌĆö this is the *biggest strength* of heap files.
- **Search Cost:** $N$ worst case, $N/2$ average ŌĆö this is the *biggest weakness*.
- **Range Query Cost:** $N$ I/O (full scan) ŌĆö no clustering means no range optimization.
- **Average Search Formula:** $E[\text{Cost}] = \dfrac{N+1}{2} \approx \dfrac{N}{2}$ I/Os.
- **Records per Page:** $n_{max} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{P - H}{R + S} \right\rfloor$.
- **Total File Size:** $\left\lceil \dfrac{M}{n_{max}} \right\rceil \times P$ bytes.
- **Use Case:** Bulk inserts, logs, audit trails, staging tables, full-table-scan analytics.
- **Avoid Using For:** Point queries, range queries, OLTP workloads with frequent PK lookups.
- **Do NOT confuse** with the **binary heap** data structure ŌĆö different concepts entirely.
- **Modern Variants:** PostgreSQL's **Heap-Only Tuples (HOT)**, MySQL InnoDB's clustered index (which is *not* a pure heap).
- **Key Trade-off:** *Write-optimization* (heap) vs. *Read-optimization* (sorted/hashed/indexed).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Indexing, Single Level Indices, Numerical Examples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Indexing, Single Level Indices, Numerical Examples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Database Management Systems (DBMS)**, **Indexing** is a data structure technique used to **minimize disk I/O** by providing a faster lookup path to records based on the values of one or more fields (search keys). An **index** is an auxiliary file (the *index file*) that stores **search-key values** together with **pointers (block addresses)** to the actual data records stored in the *data file*.

A **Single-Level Index** is an ordered index file in which the index entries are stored at one level only, directly pointing to the data records (or data blocks) on disk. The two principal ordered single-level index types are:

- **Primary Index (Index-Sequential File)** ŌĆö built on the **ordered primary key** of a sorted data file; contains one index entry per data block (sparse).
- **Clustering Index** ŌĆö built on the **ordering (non-key) field** of a sorted data file; one entry per distinct value of the clustering field.
- **Secondary Index (Dense)** ŌĆö built on any **non-ordering field** (key or non-key); each index entry points to exactly one record.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** *Module 1 mandates coverage of Indexing mechanisms (Single Level), file organisations, and their performance analysis.* The numerical questions in the End Semester Examination (ESE) typically carry 7ŌĆō14 marks and demand block-access / index-size calculations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Library Card Catalogue"

Imagine a massive library with **1,00,000 books** arranged randomly on shelves.

- **Without an index (no card catalogue):** To find a book, you must walk down *every single aisle* (linear search) ŌĆö extremely slow.
- **With a primary index (author-wise sorted library):** The books are arranged alphabetically by author. A *small* thin register (index file) sits at the entrance listing the *first author on each shelf* (one entry per block ŌĆö sparse). You binary-search the register, walk to the correct shelf, then scan within.
- **With a secondary index (a "title-wise" lookup book):** Every book title appears in the lookup book, each pointing to a precise shelf-row-column (dense). Lookup by title is direct but the lookup book itself is **large**.

| Term in DBMS | Library Analogy |
|---|---|
| Data file | The shelves with books |
| Search key | Author / Title / ISBN |
| Index file | The card catalogue / lookup book |
| Block (page) | One physical shelf section |
| Pointer (RID) | Shelf-Row-Column address |
| Dense index | Every book has a card |
| Sparse index | Only the *first* book of each shelf is carded |

### 1.3 Why Indexing? ŌĆö The I/O Cost Argument

> [!NOTE]
> A typical disk block in KTU problems is **B = 512 bytes**; the average seek + rotational latency cost (one block access) is roughly **10 ms**. Reducing block accesses from **4000 ŌåÆ 12** changes a query from ~40 seconds to ~0.12 seconds. **Indexing is therefore a performance multiplier, not a correctness mechanism.**

An indexed search on *N* data records using binary search on the index requires:
- $\lceil \log_2(b_i) \rceil + 1$ block accesses (where $b_i$ is the number of index blocks), compared with
- $b$ block accesses without any index (where $b$ is the number of data blocks).

This **logarithmic gain** is the central engineering motivation for single-level indices.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of an Index Entry

An index entry $(k_i, P_i)$ contains exactly two fields:

- **$k_i$** ŌĆö the search-key value (or a copy of it).
- **$P_i$** ŌĆö a pointer to a data block or a specific record (Row Identifier / RID).

If the file uses **fixed-length records** and the pointer is a **block address** (relative byte number, RBN), the index-entry size is:

$$
\text{IndexEntrySize} = \text{KeySize} + \text{PointerSize}
$$

If the index is on a **non-key** field (clustering/secondary), the pointer becomes a *head-of-block* address only when the data is sorted on that field; otherwise, multiple RID-lists are required and the entry size becomes variable.

### 2.2 The Three Sub-Types of Single-Level Ordered Index

**A. Primary Index (Sparse, on Sorted Primary Key)**

- One entry **per data block** ŌåÆ $\text{Index Entries} = b$ (number of data blocks).
- The anchor key in each entry is the **first key value** of the corresponding data block.
- Used only when the data file is **sequentially ordered on the primary key**.

**B. Clustering Index (Sparse, on Sorted Non-Key Field)**

- One entry **per distinct value** of the clustering field ŌåÆ $\text{Index Entries} = \text{number of distinct values } (V)$.
- Each pointer leads to the **first block containing that distinct value**; since multiple records share a value, the data is scanned within the block group.
- The data file is ordered on the **non-key** clustering attribute.

**C. Secondary Index (Dense, on Any Non-Ordering Field)**

- One entry **per record** ŌåÆ $\text{Index Entries} = r$ (total record count).
- Each entry's pointer is a full **record pointer (RID)**.
- Since the data is not ordered on this attribute, a *sparse* index is impossible.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **secondary index on a key field** is *also dense* (one entry per record) because the search key has no ordering correspondence to physical file order. Hence "secondary = dense" is the standard KTU convention.

### 2.3 The Single Most Critical Distinction: Dense vs Sparse

| Property | Sparse Index | Dense Index |
|---|---|---|
| Number of entries | One per data block ($b$) or per distinct value ($V$) | One per data record ($r$) |
| Applicable to ordered file? | **Yes** (mandatory) | Always |
| Extra overhead on update? | Lower (insertions may force re-anchoring) | Higher (every record change propagates) |
| Storage cost | Low | High |
| Lookup speed | Fast but with intra-block scan | Direct (one extra hop max) |
| Block access for equality query | $\lceil \log_2(b_i) \rceil + 1$ | $\lceil \log_2(b_i) \rceil + 1$ |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet ŌĆö High-Yield Equations

> [!NOTE]
> Use this table as the **single source of truth** for all numerical problems in Module 1.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of data records | $r$ | records |
| 2 | Blocking factor (records per block) | $\text{fr} = \lfloor B / R \rfloor$ | records/block |
| 3 | Number of data blocks | $b = \lceil r / \text{fr} \rceil$ | blocks |
| 4 | Number of index entries (Primary, sparse) | $b$ | entries |
| 5 | Number of index entries (Secondary, dense) | $r$ | entries |
| 6 | Number of index entries (Clustering) | $V$ (distinct values) | entries |
| 7 | Index blocking factor | $\text{fi} = \lfloor B / (K + P) \rfloor$ | entries/block |
| 8 | Number of index blocks | $b_i = \lceil (\text{entries}) / \text{fi} \rceil$ | blocks |
| 9 | Block accesses for equality (indexed) | $\lceil \log_2(b_i) \rceil + 1$ | I/Os |
| 10 | Block accesses for equality (linear scan) | $b$ | I/Os |
| 11 | Block accesses for range query (Primary) | $\lceil \log_2(b_i) \rceil + s$ | I/Os |
| 12 | Block accesses for range query (Linear) | $b$ | I/Os |
| 13 | Pointer size | $P = \lceil \log_2(b) \rceil$ bits (or fixed 4ŌĆō8 B) | bytes |
| 14 | Index file size | $b_i \times B$ | bytes |

Where: $B$ = block size, $R$ = record size, $K$ = key field size, $V$ = number of distinct values of clustering field, $s$ = number of data blocks spanned by the range.

### 2.5 Cost of Update Operations on Single-Level Indices

- **Insertion in Primary (Sparse) Index:** Locate the correct block in $O(\log_2 b_i)$; shift records; if block overflows ŌåÆ split; if a new block is created ŌåÆ **insert a new anchor entry** in the index (sparse index grows by 1 entry).
- **Deletion in Primary Index:** Locate and delete the record. If the deleted record was the **anchor of its block**, replace the anchor with the next key in the block. If the block becomes empty and is removed, **delete the corresponding index entry**.
- **Insertion in Secondary (Dense) Index:** Insert the data record ŌåÆ immediately insert a new index entry. **Index size grows by 1** for every insertion (no possibility of bulk-anchoring).
- **Deletion in Secondary Index:** Locate the entry, delete the data record, delete the entry. Index size shrinks by 1.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Heuristic:** When asked to "compare" search costs, always give the *two extreme scenarios* ŌĆö (i) successful equality search on a non-existent value (binary search returns "not found" without touching the data file ŌåÆ only $\lceil \log_2 b_i \rceil$ I/Os) and (ii) successful search on the **last** record of the file ($\lceil \log_2 b_i \rceil + 1$ I/Os because the data block of the anchor is fetched).

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

- **Banking:** Secondary index on `account_number` (every account carded ŌåÆ fast ATM lookup).
- **E-commerce:** Clustering index on `category` (so all "Laptops" are physically clustered ŌåÆ fast filter queries).
- **Telecom CDR logs:** Primary index on `timestamp` (sequential scans for time-range analytics are very cheap).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Examples

> [!NOTE]
> The following four numerical examples progress from *basic index-size computation* to *comparative cost analysis* and finally *update operations*. They mirror the **14-mark KTU ESE** pattern of the last 3 academic years.

### 3.1 Example 1 ŌĆö Index File Size (Primary Index, Sparse)

**Problem Statement (Modeled on KTU Dec 2023, ESE Q1.b):**

A file has $r = 30\,000$ employee records. Each record is $R = 100$ bytes. The primary key field `EmpID` is $K = 9$ bytes long. The block size is $B = 512$ bytes. A **primary (sparse) index** is built on `EmpID`. The block pointer size is $P = 6$ bytes. Calculate:

1. The blocking factor of the data file.
2. The number of data blocks.
3. The blocking factor of the index file.
4. The number of index blocks and total index-file size.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Blocking factor of the data file ($\text{fr}$):**

$$
\text{fr} = \left\lfloor \frac{B}{R} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{512}{100} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 5.12 \right\rfloor = 5 \text{ records/block}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Number of data blocks ($b$):**

$$
b = \left\lceil \frac{r}{\text{fr}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{30\,000}{5} \right\rceil = 6\,000 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Blocking factor of the index file ($\text{fi}$):**

Each index entry has size $K + P = 9 + 6 = 15$ bytes.

$$
\text{fi} = \left\lfloor \frac{B}{K + P} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{512}{15} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 34.13 \right\rfloor = 34 \text{ entries/block}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Number of index entries, blocks and size:**

A **primary index is sparse** ŌåÆ one entry per data block ŌåÆ entries $= b = 6\,000$.

$$
b_i = \left\lceil \frac{6\,000}{34} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 176.47 \right\rceil = 177 \text{ index blocks}
$$

$$
\text{Index file size} = b_i \times B = 177 \times 512 = 90\,624 \text{ bytes} \approx 88.5 \text{ KB}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The index file is **~1.5 %** the size of the data file (data file $= 6000 \times 512 = 3\,072\,000$ bytes Ōēł 3 MB). This tiny size is what makes binary search on the index fit comfortably in main memory during the second-level lookup.

### 3.2 Example 2 ŌĆö Comparative Search Cost (Primary vs No Index)

**Problem Statement:**

Using the values from Example 1, compute the **average** and **worst-case** number of block accesses for an equality search on `EmpID`:

(a) Without an index (linear scan on sorted data file).
(b) With the primary (sparse) index, assuming binary search is performed on the index.

**Part (a) ŌĆö Linear scan on sorted data:**

For a sorted file, **binary search** on the data blocks directly requires:

$$
\text{Block accesses (binary on data)} = \left\lceil \log_2(b) \right\rceil = \left\lceil \log_2(6\,000) \right\rceil
$$

$$
\log_2(6\,000) = \frac{\ln 6\,000}{\ln 2} = \frac{8.6995}{0.6931} \approx 12.55
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \left\lceil \log_2(6\,000) \right\rceil = 13 \text{ block accesses}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The $+\,1$ in the indexed formula accounts for fetching the data block *after* the index is resolved. For a pure binary search on data alone, no such $+\,1$ is added.

**Part (b) ŌĆö Indexed search:**

$$
b_i = 177 \text{ (from Ex. 1)}
$$

$$
\text{Block accesses} = \left\lceil \log_2(b_i) \right\rceil + 1 = \left\lceil \log_2(177) \right\rceil + 1
$$

$$
\log_2(177) = \frac{\ln 177}{\ln 2} = \frac{5.176}{0.6931} \approx 7.468
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \left\lceil \log_2(177) \right\rceil + 1 = 8 + 1 = 9 \text{ block accesses}
$$

**Summary of Search Cost:**

| Method | Best Case | Average | Worst Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Linear scan on data (sorted) | 1 | 13 | 13 |
| Primary index (sparse) | 1 | 9 | 9 |
| **Speedup factor** | ŌĆö | **1.44├Ś** | **1.44├Ś** |

> [!TIP]
> The KTU board expects you to **state the speedup explicitly**. Always convert: $13 / 9 \approx 1.44$ times faster. A 3-mark sub-part without the speedup statement often loses one mark.

### 3.3 Example 3 ŌĆö Secondary (Dense) Index ŌĆö Full Numerical Treatment

**Problem Statement (Modeled on KTU May 2024, ESE Q2):**

A STUDENT file has $r = 50\,000$ records. Each record is $R = 80$ bytes. A secondary index is built on a non-ordering attribute `PhoneNo`, where the key field size is $K = 10$ bytes and the record pointer size is $P = 7$ bytes. The block size is $B = 1\,024$ bytes. The file is **unsorted** on `PhoneNo`.

Calculate:

1. Blocking factor of the data file and number of data blocks.
2. Number of secondary index entries (justify).
3. Number of index blocks.
4. Number of block accesses to fetch **all** students whose `PhoneNo` lies in a given range of $n$ distinct values (assume each match requires one data-block fetch; the data is unsorted, so matches are randomly distributed).

**Step 1 ŌĆö Data file parameters:**

$$
\text{fr} = \left\lfloor \frac{1\,024}{80} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 12.8 \right\rfloor = 12 \text{ records/block}
$$

$$
b = \left\lceil \frac{50\,000}{12} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 4\,166.67 \right\rceil = 4\,167 \text{ data blocks}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Number of secondary index entries:**

Because the data file is **not ordered** on `PhoneNo`, the index must be **dense** ŌĆö one entry per data record.

$$
\text{Index entries} = r = 50\,000
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Index blocking factor and number of index blocks:**

$$
\text{fi} = \left\lfloor \frac{1\,024}{K + P} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{1\,024}{10 + 7} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{1\,024}{17} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 60.23 \right\rfloor = 60 \text{ entries/block}
$$

$$
b_i = \left\lceil \frac{50\,000}{60} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 833.33 \right\rceil = 834 \text{ index blocks}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Block accesses for a range query returning $n$ matching records:**

Binary search the index for the lowest matching key:

$$
\text{Index search cost} = \left\lceil \log_2(834) \right\rceil = \left\lceil 9.70 \right\rceil = 10 \text{ block accesses}
$$

Then sequentially scan index entries (already loaded, mostly) until the range ends ŌåÆ **$n$ data-block fetches** (worst case, one per record, since data is unsorted).

$$
\text{Total I/O} = 10 + n
$$

For comparison, the linear scan over the data file would require $b = 4\,167$ block accesses, so:

$$
\text{Speedup (n = 1)} = \frac{4\,167}{10 + 1} = \frac{4\,167}{11} \approx 379\times
$$

> [!WARNING]
> A common KTU mistake: students write "$b_i + 1$" for indexed search. The correct expression is $\lceil \log_2 b_i \rceil + 1$ **only for single equality lookup**. For a range query returning $n$ matches, the formula is $\lceil \log_2 b_i \rceil + n$.

### 3.4 Example 4 ŌĆö Clustering Index on Sorted Non-Key Field

**Problem Statement:**

A library database has $r = 60\,000$ BOOK records, $R = 100$ bytes each, $B = 1\,024$ bytes. The file is **physically sorted on `Category`**, which is a non-key field with $V = 25$ distinct values (Fiction, Science, History, ŌĆ”). The Category key is $K = 12$ bytes; the block pointer is $P = 6$ bytes.

Calculate:

1. Number of data blocks ($b$).
2. Number of clustering index entries.
3. Number of index blocks and index size.
4. Number of block accesses to fetch **all** books of category "Science" (assume the "Science" group spans $s = 480$ data blocks).

**Step 1 ŌĆö Data blocks:**

$$
\text{fr} = \left\lfloor \frac{1\,024}{100} \right\rfloor = 10 \text{ records/block}
$$

$$
b = \left\lceil \frac{60\,000}{10} \right\rceil = 6\,000 \text{ data blocks}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Clustering index entries:**

A clustering index is sparse on the distinct values of the clustering field:

$$
\text{Entries} = V = 25
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Index blocking factor and blocks:**

$$
\text{fi} = \left\lfloor \frac{1\,024}{12 + 6} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{1\,024}{18} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 56.88 \right\rfloor = 56 \text{ entries/block}
$$

$$
b_i = \left\lceil \frac{25}{56} \right\rceil = 1 \text{ index block (entire index fits in one disk block!)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A clustering index is **extremely small** because $V \ll b \ll r$ in typical databases. When $b_i = 1$, the entire index may even be cached in main memory ŌåÆ a single equality lookup costs only **1 block access** (the data block) instead of 2.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Range/equality cost for "Science":**

$$
\text{Index lookup} = \lceil \log_2(1) \rceil + 1 = 0 + 1 = 1 \text{ block access}
$$

Once the first "Science" block is located, sequentially read the next $s - 1$ contiguous blocks:

$$
\text{Total I/O} = 1 + (s - 1) = 1 + 479 = 480 \text{ block accesses}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The clustering index exploits **physical contiguity** ŌĆö since all Science books are stored in adjacent blocks, a sequential scan is essentially free (no seeking). This is the **single most powerful property** of clustering indices and a frequent 7-mark KTU sub-part.

### 3.5 Master Comparison Table ŌĆö All Three Index Types

Let $r = 60\,000$, $R = 100$ B, $B = 1\,024$ B, $K = 10$ B, $P = 6$ B, $V = 25$, and suppose the data is sorted on the primary key.

| Metric | Primary (Sparse) | Clustering (Sparse) | Secondary (Dense) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Data file ordered on this key? | Yes (key) | Yes (non-key) | No |
| Number of entries | $b = 6\,000$ | $V = 25$ | $r = 60\,000$ |
| Entry size | $K + P = 16$ B | $K + P = 18$ B | $K + P = 16$ B (or RID-list) |
| $\text{fi}$ | $\lfloor 1024/16 \rfloor = 64$ | $\lfloor 1024/18 \rfloor = 56$ | $\lfloor 1024/16 \rfloor = 64$ |
| $b_i$ | $\lceil 6000/64 \rceil = 94$ | $\lceil 25/56 \rceil = 1$ | $\lceil 60000/64 \rceil = 938$ |
| Index file size | $94 \times 1\,024 \approx 94$ KB | $1 \times 1\,024 = 1$ KB | $938 \times 1\,024 \approx 938$ KB |
| Equality search I/O | $\lceil \log_2 94 \rceil + 1 = 8$ | $1$ | $\lceil \log_2 938 \rceil + 1 = 11$ |
| Range of $s$ blocks I/O | $\lceil \log_2 94 \rceil + s = 7 + s$ | $s$ (contiguous!) | $\lceil \log_2 938 \rceil + n = 10 + n$ |

This single table is worth **at least 7 marks** if reproduced correctly in a KTU ESE.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Single-Level Primary Index

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CPU Issues Search Key K] --> B[Binary Search on Index File]
    B --> C[Index File Sorted by Anchor Keys]
    C --> D{Compare K with Anchor}
    D -- Less --> E[Traverse Left Index Block]
    D -- Greater --> F[Traverse Right Index Block]
    D -- Equal or Found Range --> G[Fetch Pointer P from Index Entry]
    G --> H[Issue Disk Read for Data Block B]
    H --> I[Search Within Data Block for Record]
    I --> J[Return Record to CPU]
    E --> B
    F --> B
```

### 4.2 Dense vs Sparse Index ŌĆö Side-by-Side Logical View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DataFile[Data File Ordered on Primary Key]
        direction LR
        B1["Block 1\nKeys 10,20,30,40,50"]
        B2["Block 2\nKeys 60,70,80,90,100"]
        B3["Block 3\nKeys 110,120,130,140,150"]
        B1 --> B2 --> B3
    end

    subgraph SparseIndex[Primary Index Sparse One Entry per Block]
        direction LR
        I1["10 -> B1"]
        I2["60 -> B2"]
        I3["110 -> B3"]
        I1 --> I2 --> I3
    end

    subgraph DenseIndex[Secondary Index Dense One Entry per Record]
        direction LR
        D1["10 -> R1"]
        D2["20 -> R2"]
        D3["30 -> R3"]
        D4["40 -> R4"]
        D5["50 -> R5"]
        D1 --> D2 --> D3 --> D4 --> D5
    end

    SparseIndex -. points to .-> DataFile
    DenseIndex -. points to .-> DataFile
```

### 4.3 Update Operation Flow ŌĆö Insertion into a Primary (Sparse) Index

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start Insert New Record with Key K] --> S2[Binary Search Index]
    S2 --> S3{Anchor Block Located}
    S3 -- Yes --> S4[Insert Record in Correct Data Block]
    S4 --> S5{Block Overflows}
    S5 -- No --> S6[Done No Index Update Needed]
    S5 -- Yes --> S7[Split Block into B and Bnew]
    S7 --> S8[Create New Index Entry with First Key of Bnew]
    S8 --> S9[Insert Entry in Index at Sorted Position]
    S9 --> S10[Done Index Updated]
    S3 -- No --> S11[Error Key Out of Range]
```

### 4.4 Cost-Comparison Heatmap (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph NoIndex[No Index]
        X1[Linear Scan b Block I/Os]
    end
    subgraph PrimIndex[Primary Sparse]
        Y1[Equality log2 bi plus 1]
        Y2[Range log2 bi plus s]
    end
    subgraph SecIndex[Secondary Dense]
        Z1[Equality log2 bi plus 1]
        Z2[Range log2 bi plus n]
    end
    subgraph ClusIndex[Clustering Sparse]
        W1[Equality 1]
        W2[Range s only]
    end

    X1 --- PrimIndex
    PrimIndex --- SecIndex
    SecIndex --- ClusIndex
```

> [!TIP]
> This kind of four-way block architecture diagram is a frequent 7-mark KTU question. Always label **edges** with the operation (e.g., "binary search", "fetch pointer") and **nodes** with the I/O cost.

### 4.5 Fallback: Block-Level Functional Architecture (Index Subsystem)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    QP[Query Processor] --> IM[Index Manager]
    IM --> C1{Index Exists}
    C1 -- Yes --> BI[Buffer Manager Fetches Index Block]
    BI --> BS[Binary Search Index Block for Key]
    BS --> MR[Match Found Resolve Pointer]
    MR --> BF[Buffer Manager Fetches Data Block]
    BF --> RT[Return Record Set to QP]
    C1 -- No --> LS[Sequential Scan of Data File]
    LS --> RT
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, ESE Module 1]**
*Distinguish between a dense index and a sparse index. Under what conditions is each used?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Dense Index:** Contains an index entry for *every* search-key value in the data file (i.e., one per record for secondary indices). The data file need not be ordered on the indexed attribute. It incurs higher storage and update overhead but supports direct lookup in $\lceil \log_2 b_i \rceil + 1$ I/Os. (1┬Į marks)
- **Sparse Index:** Contains an index entry for *only some* search-key values ŌĆö typically one per data block (primary index) or one per distinct value (clustering index). It is *applicable only when the data file is ordered on the indexed field*. Lower storage cost, but equality lookup may still require an intra-block scan. (1┬Į marks)

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024, ESE Module 1]**
*Why can't a sparse index be built on a non-ordering (secondary) attribute?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A sparse index stores anchor keys at *regular intervals* (e.g., one per block) and assumes that any search key $K$ will lie in the block whose anchor is the **largest anchor $\leq K$**. This logic is *valid only when the data file is physically ordered on the search key*, because then the anchor range is contiguous and complete. If the file is unsorted on the attribute, a record with key $K = 25$ might exist *anywhere* ŌĆö not necessarily in the block after anchor $20$. A sparse index would silently miss records, producing **incorrect query results**. Hence a *dense* secondary index is mandatory. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2023, ESE Module 1, Q3 ŌĆö Adapted]**

**Q3.** A sequential file of library members has $r = 40\,000$ records. Each record is $R = 120$ bytes long, and the block size is $B = 2\,048$ bytes. The primary key `MemberID` is a 9-byte field, and the block pointer occupies $P = 7$ bytes.

**(a)** Construct a **primary (sparse) index** on `MemberID`. Compute:
- (i) Blocking factor of the data file (1 mark)
- (ii) Number of data blocks $b$ (1 mark)
- (iii) Number of index entries and index blocking factor $\text{fi}$ (2 marks)
- (iv) Number of index blocks $b_i$ and the total index-file size in KB (2 marks)
- (v) Number of block accesses to locate a record using binary search on the index (1 mark)

**(b)** The same file is now used to build a **secondary (dense) index** on the non-ordering field `PhoneNo` (10 bytes, pointer = 8 bytes). Compute:
- (i) Why must this index be dense? (1 mark)
- (ii) Number of index entries and $b_i$ (2 marks)
- (iii) Number of block accesses for a range query returning $n = 25$ records (2 marks)
- (iv) Comment on the trade-off: when is the secondary index preferred despite higher storage? (2 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Primary (Sparse) Index**

(i) Blocking factor:

$$
\text{fr} = \left\lfloor \frac{2\,048}{120} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 17.06 \right\rfloor = 17 \text{ records/block}
$$

(ii) Data blocks:

$$
b = \left\lceil \frac{40\,000}{17} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 2\,352.94 \right\rceil = 2\,353 \text{ data blocks}
$$

(iii) Index entries = $b = 2\,353$ (sparse). Index entry size $= 9 + 7 = 16$ bytes.

$$
\text{fi} = \left\lfloor \frac{2\,048}{16} \right\rfloor = 128 \text{ entries/block}
$$

(iv) Index blocks:

$$
b_i = \left\lceil \frac{2\,353}{128} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 18.38 \right\rceil = 19 \text{ index blocks}
$$

$$
\text{Index size} = 19 \times 2\,048 = 38\,912 \text{ bytes} \approx 38 \text{ KB}
$$

(v) Block accesses for equality:

$$
\left\lceil \log_2(19) \right\rceil + 1 = 5 + 1 = 6 \text{ block accesses}
$$

**(b) Secondary (Dense) Index**

(i) The data file is **physically ordered on `MemberID`**, not on `PhoneNo`. A sparse index requires ordering on the indexed attribute ŌĆö otherwise, records with intermediate keys could be **scattered** across the file and would be missed. Hence the index *must* be dense. (1 mark)

(ii) Index entries $= r = 40\,000$. Entry size $= 10 + 8 = 18$ bytes.

$$
\text{fi} = \left\lfloor \frac{2\,048}{18} \right\rfloor = 113 \text{ entries/block}
$$

$$
b_i = \left\lceil \frac{40\,000}{113} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 353.98 \right\rceil = 354 \text{ index blocks}
$$

(iii) Range query returning $n = 25$ records:

$$
\text{Index binary search} = \lceil \log_2(354) \rceil = 9 \text{ block accesses}
$$

Each matching record lives in a *random* data block (data is unsorted on `PhoneNo`) ŌåÆ **25 data-block fetches**.

$$
\text{Total I/O} = 9 + 25 = 34 \text{ block accesses}
$$

For comparison, linear scan of the data file would cost $b = 2\,353$ block accesses, so the speedup is $\approx 69 \times$.

(iv) Trade-off comment (2 marks): The secondary index consumes $354 \times 2\,048 \approx 707$ KB ŌĆö about **18├Ś larger** than the primary index. However, it supports fast queries on `PhoneNo` (an attribute not used for ordering) that the primary index cannot accelerate. It is preferred when:

- The attribute is frequently used in `WHERE` clauses.
- The selectivity is high (few matching records per query).
- The database is read-heavy; update overhead is acceptable.

It is *not* preferred when the attribute is rarely queried or when the table is write-heavy, because every insert/delete updates the dense index.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A frequent mistake in 14-mark numericals is using the *primary* index formula ($\lceil \log_2 b_i \rceil + 1$) for a *range* query on a secondary index. The correct cost is $\lceil \log_2 b_i \rceil + n$ where $n$ is the number of matches. The "+1" formula is valid **only for a single equality lookup**, not for ranges.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2022, ESE Module 1, Q4 ŌĆö Adapted]**

**Q4.** Consider an EMPLOYEE file with $r = 1\,00\,000$ records. Each record is $R = 250$ bytes. The block size is $B = 4\,096$ bytes. The file is **physically ordered on the non-key field `DeptName`** (12 bytes), which has $V = 40$ distinct departments. A **clustering index** is built on `DeptName` (pointer = 8 bytes).

**(a)** Compute:
- (i) The data blocking factor and number of data blocks $b$ (2 marks)
- (ii) The number of clustering index entries, and justify why clustering indices are *sparse* (2 marks)
- (iii) The index blocking factor $\text{fi}$ and number of index blocks $b_i$ (2 marks)
- (iv) The total index file size in KB (1 mark)

**(b)** Compute:
- (i) Number of block accesses to fetch **all** employees of the "Sales" department, given that the Sales records span $s = 300$ data blocks (2 marks)
- (ii) How does this compare with using a **primary index** (built on a synthetic 6-byte `EmpID` key) for the same query? (Assume the EmpID primary index has $b_i = 188$ index blocks and the Sales records are scattered across the file.) (2 marks)
- (iii) Briefly explain the **insertion anomaly** in a clustering index when many records of a new category are added. (1 mark)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Clustering Index Construction**

(i) Data blocking factor:

$$
\text{fr} = \left\lfloor \frac{4\,096}{250} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 16.38 \right\rfloor = 16 \text{ records/block}
$$

$$
b = \left\lceil \frac{1\,00\,000}{16} \right\rceil = 6\,250 \text{ data blocks}
$$

(ii) Index entries $= V = 40$ ŌĆö one per *distinct* value of `DeptName`. A clustering index is **sparse** because the data file is ordered on `DeptName`; all records with the same department are **physically contiguous**, so a single anchor (the first record's key) suffices to locate the whole group. A dense index would store 1,00,000 entries ŌĆö wasteful, since 40 anchors are enough. (2 marks)

(iii) Index entry size $= 12 + 8 = 20$ bytes.

$$
\text{fi} = \left\lfloor \frac{4\,096}{20} \right\rfloor = 204 \text{ entries/block}
$$

$$
b_i = \left\lceil \frac{40}{204} \right\rceil = 1 \text{ index block}
$$

(iv) Index size $= 1 \times 4\,096 = 4\,096$ bytes $\approx 4$ KB.

**(b) Query Cost Comparison**

(i) Clustering index query for Sales:

$$
\text{Index lookup} = \lceil \log_2(1) \rceil + 1 = 1 \text{ block access}
$$

The Sales group spans $s = 300$ contiguous blocks; scan sequentially.

$$
\text{Total I/O} = 1 + 300 = 301 \text{ block accesses}
$$

(ii) Primary index on `EmpID` (synthetic key) query for Sales:

Since the data is sorted on `DeptName`, not on `EmpID`, the Sales records are **not contiguous in `EmpID` order**. To find all Sales employees via the primary index on `EmpID`, one must perform:

$$
\text{Index lookups} = 300 \text{ (one per record) at } \lceil \log_2(188) \rceil + 1 = 8 + 1 = 9 \text{ I/Os each} \Rightarrow 300 \times 9 = 2\,700 \text{ I/Os}
$$

(Alternative simpler approximation: each Sales record may reside in a random block; using the primary index to fetch *all* Sales employees is at least $2\,700$ I/Os ŌĆö about **9├Ś more expensive** than the clustering index.) (2 marks)

(iii) **Insertion anomaly in clustering index:** When a brand-new department is added (e.g., a new "Robotics" division), all its records must be **physically clustered together** in the data file. This may require **shifting** existing blocks to make room, which is an expensive operation. If the file is not reorganized, the clustering property degrades over time (the "physical clustering" is lost), and query performance for that department drops. This is the well-known **maintenance cost** of clustering indices. (1 mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout ŌĆö Part B:** Students often write "primary index is faster than clustering index" because $b_i$ is larger for clustering. But the *clustering index* wins on range queries because of physical contiguity ŌĆö the speedup comes from the **$+s$ vs $+n$** distinction, not the index size. Examiners specifically test this conceptual clarity.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> Print this checklist and tape it next to your study desk. These are the items most frequently tested in KTU ESE Module 1.

- [ ] **Indexing is a performance (not correctness) mechanism** ŌĆö it minimises disk I/O, the dominant cost in databases.
- [ ] A **single-level index** has index entries at one level only, each pointing directly to a data record or data block.
- [ ] **Primary index** = sparse, on the **ordering primary key**, one entry per data block ($b$ entries).
- [ ] **Clustering index** = sparse, on the **ordering non-key field**, one entry per distinct value ($V$ entries).
- [ ] **Secondary index** = dense, on **any non-ordering field**, one entry per record ($r$ entries).
- [ ] **Sparse ŌēĀ possible on unordered files** ŌĆö always verify the data is sorted on the indexed field before assuming sparseness.
- [ ] **Master formula stack:** $R$, $B$ ŌåÆ $\text{fr} = \lfloor B/R \rfloor$ ŌåÆ $b = \lceil r/\text{fr} \rceil$ ŌåÆ entries ŌåÆ $\text{fi} = \lfloor B/(K+P) \rfloor$ ŌåÆ $b_i = \lceil \text{entries}/\text{fi} \rceil$ ŌåÆ I/O $= \lceil \log_2 b_i \rceil + (\text{extra})$.
- [ ] **Equality I/O** = $\lceil \log_2 b_i \rceil + 1$. **Range I/O** = $\lceil \log_2 b_i \rceil + s$ (primary, $s$ = data blocks spanned) or $+n$ (secondary, $n$ = matching records).
- [ ] **Clustering index exploits physical contiguity** ŌĆö the most powerful property for range queries on the clustering field.
- [ ] **Update cost:** Primary index grows only on block overflows; secondary index grows by 1 entry per insertion (high maintenance).
- [ ] **Block pointer size** $P$ is often given; if not, use $P = \lceil \log_2 b \rceil$ bits or simply $P = 4$ŌĆō$8$ bytes.
- [ ] **Common KTU board traps:**
  - Confusing $\lceil \log_2 b_i \rceil + 1$ (equality) with $\lceil \log_2 b_i \rceil + s$ (range).
  - Forgetting to justify **why** an index is dense.
  - Using linear-scan cost ($b$) when an index is present.
  - Omitting the **speedup factor** in comparison questions.
- [ ] **One-line memory aid:** *"Primary = sparse on key; Clustering = sparse on non-key group; Secondary = dense on anything."*
- [ ] **Index file size** is always $\leq 5$ŌĆō$10\%$ of data file size in well-designed databases ŌĆö this is what makes in-memory binary search feasible.
- [ ] For KTU numericals, **always show the units** (records/block, bytes, KB, I/Os) ŌĆö the examiner awards partial marks for unit clarity.
- [ ] **End-of-Module 1 self-test:** Given any of the three index types, derive $b_i$ and the equality I/O cost in under 4 minutes. If you can, you are exam-ready.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multi-Level Indices, Numerical Examples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBEOT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Multi-Level Indices, Numerical Examples # ­¤ōś SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What are Multi-Level Indices? (Formal Definition) > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** > A **Multi-Level Index** is an ordered index that is itself too large to be held entirely in main memory. To overcome this limitation, the index file itself is treated as a data file, and a **sparse (or dense) outer index** is built on top of the original inner index. This process can be repeated to form **multiple levels of indices**, dramatically reducing the number of disk I/O operations required to locate a record. In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, multi-level indexing is treated as a natural **performance optimization** over the basic single-level ordered index. ### 1.2 Why Do We Need Multi-Level Indexing? Let us understand the **"Why"** first. Suppose we have a single-level ordered index on a relation of 1 Crore (10,000,000) records. The index file itself might contain thousands of index entries, far exceeding main memory. Performing a **binary search** on such a large index would still require too many disk block accesses. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Single-Level Index Bottleneck:** > Even with binary search, accessing a record may require $\lceil \log_2 b_i \rceil$ block I/Os on the index, where $b_i$ is the number of index blocks. As $b_i$ grows, this becomes inefficient. A **multi-level index** solves this by creating a **hierarchy of indices**, where each higher level acts as an index to the level immediately below it. ### 1.3 The Intuitive Analogy: Library of Congress > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: Searching a Massive Library** > Imagine searching for a specific book in a 10-million-book library. A *single-level index* is like a single giant catalog drawer listing every book. A *multi-level index* is like a **hierarchical catalog**: > 1. **Level 1 (Top):** "Books on Computer Science" (just 10 entries) > 2. **Level 2:** "CS books on Databases" (100 entries) > 3. **Level 3 (Bottom):** Individual book cards (10,000 entries) > > You read only the top drawer, point to the right middle drawer, then the right bottom drawer ŌĆö **all in main memory, fast and cheap**. ### 1.4 Classification of Multi-Level Indices | Type | Description | KTU Relevance | |---|---|---| | **Multi-level Primary Index** | Built on a primary key; index is on ordered (sequential) data file | Frequently asked | | **Multi-level Clustering Index** | Built on a non-key ordered attribute | Module 1 / 2 syllabus | | **Multi-level Secondary Index** | Built on any key, file may not be ordered | Frequently asked | | **B-Tree / B+ Tree** | Dynamic multi-level tree indices, self-balancing | **High-weightage in KTU 2024** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Most numerical problems in this section assume a **Multi-Level Ordered Index on a Primary Key** with fixed block sizes. ### 1.5 Physical Parameters (Standard KTU Values) The following constants are typically assumed in KTU numerical problems: - **Block Size (B)** = **4096 bytes** (default) - **Pointer Size (P)** = **9 bytes** - **Record Size (R)** = **100 bytes** (varies) - **Search Key Size (K)** = **9 bytes** (varies) # ­¤ōś SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic of a Multi-Level Index The process works in **5 logical steps**: 1. **Step 1 ŌĆö Scan the Outer Index (Top Level):** This index is small enough to fit in main memory.
