Binary search is performed on it. 2. **Step 2 ŌĆö Pinpoint an Inner Index Block:** The top-level index gives a pointer to the relevant block in the *next level down*. 3. **Step 3 ŌĆö Recurse Down the Hierarchy:** Each level reduces the search space exponentially. 4. **Step 4 ŌĆö Reach the Data Block:** The bottom level index points to the data file block. 5. **Step 5 ŌĆö Fetch the Record:** The required record is read from the data block into main memory. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Each additional level *doubles* (in dense index) or grows *logarithmically* (in sparse) the searchable range while keeping the top levels memory-resident. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Numerical Toolkit) > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL:** The following formulas are the **backbone of KTU 2024 numerical problems**. Memorize them with their units. | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **File Blocking Factor** $b_{fr}$ | $b_{fr} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{B}{R} \right\rfloor$ | Records per data block | | 2 | **Number of Data Blocks** $r$ | $r = \left\lceil \dfrac{N}{b_{fr}} \right\rceil$ | $N$ = total records | | 3 | **Index Blocking Factor** $b_i$ | $b_i = \left\lfloor \dfrac{B}{\,(K+P)\,} \right\rfloor$ | Entries per index block | | 4 | **First-Level Index Size** $r_1$ | $r_1 = \left\lceil \dfrac{r}{b_i} \right\rceil$ | Blocks at level 1 | | 5 | **Second-Level Index Size** $r_2$ | $r_2 = \left\lceil \dfrac{r_1}{b_i} \right\rceil$ | Blocks at level 2 | | 6 | **Number of Levels** $L$ | $L = \left\lceil \log_{b_i}(r) \right\rceil$ | Rounded up | | 7 | **Block Accesses (Equality Search)** | $\log_{b_i}(b_i \cdot L) + 1$ | Last `+1` is for the data block | | 8 | **Block Accesses (Linear Search Range)** | $L \cdot b_i + \dfrac{(s/2)}{b_{fr}}$ | $s$ = number of matches | > **Memory trick:** `bfr` = "block-factor for records"; `bi` = "block-factor for index". ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Use of Multi-Level Indexing | |---|---| | **Banking Systems** | Fast account lookup across billions of transactions | | **E-commerce (Amazon/Flipkart)** | Quick product search across massive catalogs | | **Telecommunications** | CDR (Call Detail Record) retrieval for billing | | **Geographic Information Systems** | Spatial indexing (R-trees, generalized to multi-level) | | **Compilers & OS** | Symbol tables and inode tables use hierarchical indexing | ### 2.4 Common KTU Pitfalls (Why Students Lose Marks) - ŌØī Forgetting the **data block access** at the end of search. - ŌØī Using **density (dense/sparse)** of the bottom index without recalculating blocking factor. - ŌØī Confusing `r` (data blocks) with `rŌéü` (1st-level index blocks). - ŌØī Using **decimal** $b_i$ when only the **floor** (integer truncation) is valid. # ­¤ōś SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Canonical KTU Numerical Example (Worked Out in Full) > [!NOTE] > **Problem Setup (Standard KTU 2024 Style):** > Consider a sequential file with the following parameters: > - Total number of records: $N = 30{,}000$ > - Block size: $B = 4096$ bytes > - Record size: $R = 100$ bytes > - Search key size: $K = 9$ bytes > - Pointer size: $P = 9$ bytes > - File is ordered on the search key. > > **Construct a multi-level primary index and determine:** > (a) File blocking factor > (b) Number of data blocks > (c) Index blocking factor > (d) Number of 1st-level index blocks > (e) Number of 2nd-level index blocks > (f) Total number of levels > (g) Total block accesses for an equality search --- ### Step (a): File Blocking Factor ($b_{fr}$) The blocking factor is the number of records that fit into one block. $$ b_{fr} = \left\lfloor \frac{B}{R} \right\rfloor $$ $$ b_{fr} = \left\lfloor \frac{4096}{100} \right\rfloor $$ $$ b_{fr} = \left\lfloor 40.96 \right\rfloor = 40 \text{ records/block} $$ > **Logic:** 40 records of 100 bytes each occupy $40 \times 100 = 4000$ bytes. The remaining 96 bytes is **wasted space** (unavoidable fragmentation). --- ### Step (b): Number of Data Blocks ($r$) $$ r = \left\lceil \frac{N}{b_{fr}} \right\rceil $$ $$ r = \left\lceil \frac{30{,}000}{40} \right\rceil $$ $$ r = \left\lceil 750 \right\rceil = 750 \text{ data blocks} $$ > **Logic:** Exactly 750 blocks suffice; the last block is **fully utilized** (no partial block here, lucky case). --- ### Step (c): Index Blocking Factor ($b_i$) Each index entry stores a search key + a pointer. Thus, the size of one index entry is $(K + P)$ bytes. $$ b_i = \left\lfloor \frac{B}{K + P} \right\rfloor $$ $$ b_i = \left\lfloor \frac{4096}{9 + 9} \right\rfloor $$ $$ b_i = \left\lfloor \frac{4096}{18} \right\rfloor $$ $$ b_i = \left\lfloor 227.55\ldots \right\rfloor = 227 \text{ index entries/block} $$ > **Logic:** 227 entries ├Ś 18 bytes = 4086 bytes. Wasted space per index block = $4096 - 4086 = 10$ bytes. --- ### Step (d): Number of 1st-Level Index Blocks ($r_1$) Since this is a **multi-level primary index**, the inner index is **sparse** ŌĆö one entry per data block. $$ r_1 = \left\lceil \frac{r}{b_i} \right\rceil $$ $$ r_1 = \left\lceil \frac{750}{227} \right\rceil $$ $$ r_1 = \left\lceil 3.304\ldots \right\rceil = 4 \text{ first-level index blocks} $$ > **Logic:** 4 blocks can hold $4 \times 227 = 908$ index entries, more than enough for 750 data blocks. --- ### Step (e): Number of 2nd-Level Index Blocks ($r_2$) We now build an outer index on the first-level index. The outer index is **sparse** (one entry per inner index block). $$ r_2 = \left\lceil \frac{r_1}{b_i} \right\rceil $$ $$ r_2 = \left\lceil \frac{4}{227} \right\rceil $$ $$ r_2 = \left\lceil 0.0176\ldots \right\rceil = 1 \text{ second-level index block} $$ > **Logic:** A single block (227-entry capacity) easily holds 4 index entries. **No further level is needed.** --- ### Step (f): Total Number of Levels ($L$) $$ L = \left\lceil \log_{b_i}(r) \right\rceil $$ $$ L = \left\lceil \log_{227}(750) \right\rceil $$ $$ L = \left\lceil 1.126\ldots \right\rceil = 2 \text{ index levels} $$ > **Logic:** Only 2 levels are required since the 2nd-level index fits in 1 block. The structure is: > - **Level 0 (Top):** 1 second-level index block > - **Level 1 (Middle):** 4 first-level index blocks > - **Level 2 (Bottom):** 750 data blocks --- ### Step (g): Total Block Accesses for Equality Search For an equality search on a unique primary key, we need **1 block access per level of index + 1 data block access**. $$ \text{Block Accesses} = (\text{Index Levels}) + 1 $$ $$ \text{Block Accesses} = L + 1 = 2 + 1 = 3 \text{ block I/Os} $$ > **Logic:** Read the 2nd-level index (1 I/O), point to the 1st-level index (1 I/O), point to the data block (1 I/O). **Total = 3 I/Os**, regardless of file size! --- ## 3.2 Second Numerical: Range Search > [!NOTE] > **Range Search Variant:** > Suppose we need to find all records with key in the range $[low, high]$ that returns $s = 200$ records, all in the same 2 data blocks. How many block I/Os? For range queries, the cost has two components: - **Index traversal cost:** $L + 1$ accesses to reach the first matching data block. - **Sequential scan cost:** $\left\lceil \dfrac{s}{b_{fr}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{200}{40} \right\rceil = 5$ data blocks. $$ \text{Total} = (L + 1) + \left\lceil \frac{s}{b_{fr}} \right\rceil $$ $$ \text{Total} = 3 + 5 = 8 \text{ block I/Os} $$ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Impact of Key Size | Key Size $K$ (bytes) | $K+P$ (bytes) | $b_i$ | $r_1 = \lceil 750/b_i \rceil | $L$ | Search Cost | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 4 | 13 | 315 | 3 | 2 | 3 | | 9 | 18 | 227 | 4 | 2 | 3 | | 25 | 34 | 120 | 7 | 2 | 3 | | 50 | 59 | 69 | 11 | 2 | 3 | | 100 | 109 | 37 | 21 | 2 | 3 | > **Insight:** Even with very large keys, the number of levels remains 2 ŌĆö this is the **scalability advantage** of multi-level indexing. ## 3.4 Dense vs Sparse ŌĆö Final Comparison > [!IMPORTANT] > **Density Choice Impacts the First Level:** > - **Sparse Primary Index** (default here): $r_1 = \lceil r / b_i \rceil$ ŌåÆ 4 blocks > - **Dense Secondary Index**: $r_1 = \lceil N / b_i \rceil = \lceil 30000/227 \rceil = 133$ blocks > - This would require a 3rd level: $r_2 = \lceil 133/227 \rceil = 1$ block > - **L = 3** (very common in KTU problems!) # ­¤ōś SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Multi-Level Index Architecture (3 Levels) ```mermaid flowchart TB A["Level 2 Index Block
2nd Level Index
Capacity: 227 entries"] B1["Level 1 Block 1
Index Entries 1-227"] B2["Level 1 Block 2
Index Entries 228-454"] B3["Level 1 Block 3
Index Entries 455-681"] B4["Level 1 Block 4
Index Entries 682-750"] D1["Data Block 1
40 Records"] D2["Data Block 2
40 Records"] D3["Data Block N
40 Records"] P1["Pointer P1"] P2["Pointer P2"] P3["Pointer P3"] P4["Pointer P4"] DP1["Data Pointer"] DP2["Data Pointer"] DP3["Data Pointer"] A -->|P1| B1 A -->|P2| B2 A -->|P3| B3 A -->|P4| B4 B1 -->|DP1| D1 B2 -->|DP2| D2 B3 -->|DP3| D3 ``` **Reading Guide:** - **Top (Level 2):** 1 block in main memory ŌåÆ fastest to access. - **Middle (Level 1):** 4 blocks ŌåÆ I/O from disk. - **Bottom (Data):** 750 blocks ŌåÆ final I/O. ## 4.2 B+ Tree Multi-Level Index (Conceptual Sketch) ```mermaid flowchart TB R["ROOT NODE
Internal Node
Keys: 30, 60, 90
Pointers: P1, P2, P3, P4"] N1["INTERNAL NODE 1
Keys: 10, 20
Pointers: P1, P2, P3"] N2["INTERNAL NODE 2
Keys: 40, 50
Pointers: P1, P2, P3"] N3["INTERNAL NODE 3
Keys: 70, 80
Pointers: P1, P2, P3"] N4["INTERNAL NODE 4
Keys: 100+
Pointers: P1, P2"] L1["LEAF 1
Keys: 5, 10, 15
Linked to Leaf 2"] L2["LEAF 2
Keys: 20, 25, 30
Linked to Leaf 3"] L3["LEAF 3
Keys: 40, 45, 50
Linked to Leaf 4"] L4["LEAF 4
Keys: 70, 75, 80
Linked to Leaf 5"] L5["LEAF 5
Keys: 90, 95, 100
End of chain"] R --> N1 R --> N2 R --> N3 R --> N4 N1 --> L1 N1 --> L2 N2 --> L3 N3 --> L4 N4 --> L5 L1 -.Sequential Link.-> L2 L2 -.Sequential Link.-> L3 L3 -.Sequential Link.-> L4 L4 -.Sequential Link.-> L5 ``` > **KTU 2024 Note:** B+ Tree leaf nodes are **linked sequentially**, enabling **fast range queries** (a key advantage over B-Trees). ## 4.3 Search Path Flowchart (Block Access Trace) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant CPU as Main Memory CPU participant L2 as Level 2 Index Block participant L1 as Level 1 Index Block participant DB as Data Block CPU->>L2: I/O Request 1 (Binary Search) L2-->>CPU: Pointer to correct Level 1 block CPU->>L1: I/O Request 2 (Binary Search) L1-->>CPU: Pointer to correct Data Block CPU->>DB: I/O Request 3 (Sequential Read) DB-->>CPU: Record delivered Note over CPU,DB: Total I/O = 3 blocks (constant!) ``` # ­¤ōś SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between single-level and multi-level indices. Why is multi-level indexing preferred for large databases?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Single-Level Index | Multi-Level Index | |---|---|---| | **Levels** | Only 1 level of index | 2 or more levels | | **Size Limitation** | Index must fit in memory | Each level can be on disk | | **Search Complexity** | $\mathcal{O}(\log_2 b_i)$ | $\mathcal{O}(\log_{b_i} r) + 1$ | | **Scalability** | Poor ŌĆö fails for huge files | Excellent ŌĆö logarithmic in file size | **Why preferred:** When the index file itself becomes too large for main memory, single-level indexing requires a linear or binary search across many disk-resident index blocks, leading to **unacceptable I/O latency**. A multi-level index keeps the **topmost level in main memory**, so each subsequent search requires only **one disk I/O per level**, making the total cost **proportional to $\log_{b_i}(r)$** ŌĆö independent of file size. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Jun 2024]` **What is the effect of increasing the search key size on a multi-level primary index?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Increasing the search key size $K$ increases the size of each index entry $(K+P)$ bytes, which **decreases the index blocking factor** $b_i = \lfloor B/(K+P) \rfloor$. With a smaller $b_i$, more index blocks are needed at each level, potentially **adding an extra level** to the index. For example, a key of 9 bytes gives $b_i = 227$, while a key of 100 bytes gives $b_i = 37$. However, due to the logarithmic relationship, the number of levels grows very slowly. The cost of an equality search remains practically constant at $L+1$ block I/Os, which is the **fundamental scalability advantage** of multi-level indexing. --- ## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### ŌØō Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Primary Multi-Level Index > **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024, Modified]** > > Consider a relation `EMPLOYEE(EmpID, Name, Salary, DeptID)` stored as a sequential file ordered on `EmpID`. The following parameters are given: > - Total records $N = 1{,}00{,}000$ > - Block size $B = 1024$ bytes > - Record size $R = 100$ bytes > - Pointer size $P = 8$ bytes > - Search key size $K = 12$ bytes > > **Answer the following:** > **(a)** Compute the file blocking factor, number of data blocks, and index blocking factor. **\[7 Marks\]** > **(b)** Determine the number of levels required and the total block accesses for an equality search. **\[7 Marks\]** --- #### Solution A(a) ŌĆö File and Index Blocking Factors **Step 1: File Blocking Factor** $$ b_{fr} = \left\lfloor \frac{B}{R} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{1024}{100} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 10.24 \right\rfloor = 10 \text{ records/block} $$ **Step 2: Number of Data Blocks** $$ r = \left\lceil \frac{N}{b_{fr}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{10} \right\rceil = 10{,}000 \text{ data blocks} $$ **Step 3: Index Blocking Factor** $$ b_i = \left\lfloor \frac{B}{K + P} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{1024}{12 + 8} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{1024}{20} \right\rfloor = 51 \text{ entries/block} $$ --- #### Solution A(b) ŌĆö Levels and Search Cost **Step 4: First-Level Index Blocks** $$ r_1 = \left\lceil \frac{r}{b_i} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{10{,}000}{51} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 196.07 \right\rceil = 197 \text{ blocks} $$ **Step 5: Second-Level Index Blocks** $$ r_2 = \left\lceil \frac{r_1}{b_i} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{197}{51} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 3.86 \right\rceil = 4 \text{ blocks} $$ **Step 6: Third-Level Index Blocks** $$ r_3 = \left\lceil \frac{r_2}{b_i} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{4}{51} \right\rceil = 1 \text{ block} $$ **Step 7: Number of Levels** $$ L = 3 \text{ index levels} $$ **Step 8: Block Accesses for Equality Search** $$ \text{Cost} = L + 1 = 3 + 1 = 4 \text{ block I/Os} $$ > **Engineering Insight:** Even with **100,000 records**, only **4 disk I/Os** are needed ŌĆö proving the power of hierarchical indexing. --- ### ŌØō Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Secondary Multi-Level Index > **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022, Modified]** > > A file contains **$N = 50{,}000$ fixed-length records** of size **$R = 80$ bytes**. The file is **unordered**. A **secondary key** of size **$K = 14$ bytes** is used with pointers of size **$P = 7$ bytes**. Block size $B = 2048$ bytes. > > **(a)** Construct a multi-level dense secondary index. Calculate the file blocking factor, index blocking factor, and number of first-level index blocks. **\[7 Marks\]** > **(b)** Determine the number of levels and total block accesses for an equality search. **\[7 Marks\]** --- #### Solution B(a) ŌĆö File & Index Parameters **Step 1: File Blocking Factor** $$ b_{fr} = \left\lfloor \frac{2048}{80} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 25.6 \right\rfloor = 25 \text{ records/block} $$ $$ r = \left\lceil \frac{50{,}000}{25} \right\rceil = 2000 \text{ data blocks} $$ **Step 2: Index Blocking Factor** $$ b_i = \left\lfloor \frac{2048}{14 + 7} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{2048}{21} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 97.52 \right\rfloor = 97 \text{ entries/block} $$ **Step 3: First-Level Index Blocks (Dense ŌĆö one per record)** $$ r_1 = \left\lceil \frac{N}{b_i} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{50{,}000}{97} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 515.46 \right\rceil = 516 \text{ blocks} $$ > **Important:** Because this is a **secondary index on an unordered file**, the inner index is **DENSE** ŌĆö one entry per record, not per block. --- #### Solution B(b) ŌĆö Levels and Cost **Step 4: Second-Level Index Blocks** $$ r_2 = \left\lceil \frac{516}{97} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 5.32 \right\rceil = 6 \text{ blocks} $$ **Step 5: Third-Level Index Blocks** $$ r_3 = \left\lceil \frac{6}{97} \right\rceil = 1 \text{ block} $$ **Step 6: Number of Levels** $$ L = 3 \text{ index levels} $$ **Step 7: Block Accesses for Equality Search** $$ \text{Cost} = L + 1 = 3 + 1 = 4 \text{ block I/Os} $$ > **Comparison:** A primary multi-level index on the same data would require only **2 levels** (sparse index, 1 entry per data block). The **dense secondary index needs 1 extra level** because it has more entries to index. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (2024 KTU Pattern):** > 1. **Forgetting the "+1" data block access** ŌĆö examiners explicitly deduct 1 mark for this. > 2. **Mixing dense vs sparse** ŌĆö secondary indexes are **DENSE** ($N$ entries), primary indexes are **SPARSE** ($r$ entries). Confusing them leads to wrong $r_1$. > 3. **Using $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$ instead of $\lceil \cdot \rceil$** for $r_1, r_2, r_3$ ŌĆö they are **ceilings** (you need whole blocks). > 4. **Skipping the unit or dimension** ŌĆö always state "blocks" or "bytes" in the final answer. > 5. **Ignoring wasted space** ŌĆö mention it briefly for full marks. --- ## 5.4 ­¤Ä» Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > Ō£ģ **Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Module 1)** - [x] A **single-level index** works only if the entire index fits in main memory. - [x] A **multi-level index** treats the index as a data file and indexes it recursively. - [x] **Primary index** ŌåÆ **sparse** ŌåÆ $r$ entries ŌåÆ smaller, fewer levels. - [x] **Secondary index** ŌåÆ **dense** ŌåÆ $N$ entries ŌåÆ larger, more levels. - [x] **Index blocking factor** $b_i = \lfloor B / (K + P) \rfloor$ ŌĆö always floor. - [x] **Number of data blocks** $r = \lceil N / b_{fr} \rceil$ ŌĆö always ceiling. - [x] **Each level** is computed as $r_{k+1} = \lceil r_k / b_i \rceil$ until $r_{k+1} = 1$. - [x] **Block accesses for equality search** = $(\text{Number of levels}) + 1$. - [x] **Range search** additionally requires $\lceil s / b_{fr} \rceil$ sequential data block I/Os. - [x] **B+ Tree** is the **dynamic generalization** of multi-level indices; leaves are linked. - [x] **Multi-level indexing is $\mathcal{O}(\log_{b_i} r)$**, virtually independent of file size. - [x] **Top level** fits in main memory ŌåÆ enables binary search ŌåÆ maximum efficiency. > **One-line takeaway:** *"Multi-level indices turn a linear search problem into a logarithmic one, achieving $\mathcal{O}(\log r)$ block I/Os regardless of file size."* ­¤ÜĆ

topics.title: Structure of Relational Databases - Integrity Constraints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBEOT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Structure of Relational Databases - Integrity Constraints # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Integrity Constraints** are a set of **predefined rules and conditions** enforced on the data stored in a relational database to ensure **accuracy, consistency, validity, and reliability** of the data throughout its lifecycle. They act as *guardians* of the database, preventing the entry of semantically incorrect or logically invalid data. In the context of the relational model, an integrity constraint is formally defined as a **predicate** $P$ that must always evaluate to `TRUE` for every valid database state $D$. Mathematically: $$ \forall t \in r(R) \implies P(t) = \text{TRUE} $$ where $r(R)$ represents the relation instance of schema $R$, and $t$ is a tuple. The **relational schema** $R$ itself can be expressed as a 4-tuple: $$ R = (A_1, A_2, A_3, \ldots, A_n, \text{IC}) $$ where $A_i$ are the attributes and **IC** is the set of integrity constraints associated with the relation. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Passport Office Imagine a **Passport Issuing Office**. Before they issue a passport, they have strict rules: - You **must** be at least 18 years old (a *check* constraint). - Your **name** cannot be left blank (a *NOT NULL* constraint). - Your **passport number** is unique and is the primary identifier (a *primary key* constraint). - Your **visa** must reference a valid country code from a master list (a *referential integrity* constraint). If any of these rules are violated, the application is **rejected**. The database does exactly this ŌĆö it rejects or modifies any operation (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) that breaks a declared rule. This is the essence of **integrity constraints**. ## 1.3 Classification of Integrity Constraints > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight ŌĆö Three Primary Categories:** > 1. **Inherent/Implicit Model-Based Constraints** (part of the relational data model itself) > 2. **Schema-Based / Explicit Constraints** (declared during schema definition) > 3. **Application-Based / Semantic Constraints** (enforced via application logic/triggers) | Category | Type | Example | |---|---|---| | Model-Based | Domain, Key, Entity Integrity | Age must be a positive integer | | Schema-Based | Referential Integrity, NOT NULL, UNIQUE, CHECK | Foreign key matches primary key | | Application-Based | Business rules, Triggers | Salary hike < 20% per cycle | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Schema-Based Integrity Constraints ### ­¤ö╣ A. Domain Constraint (Domain Integrity) A **domain** $D$ is the set of permitted atomic values for an attribute. The domain constraint states that every value of an attribute $A_i$ in any tuple $t$ must belong to the predefined domain of $A_i$. **Formal Definition:** $$ \forall t \in r(R), \ \forall A_i \in R, \ t[A_i] \in \text{dom}(A_i) $$ **Real-world utility:** Prevents storing an alphabet letter in a numeric field, or a date from the year 9999 in a system designed for 21st-century records. Domains can be defined as: ```sql CREATE DOMAIN SalaryType AS DECIMAL(10,2) CHECK (VALUE >= 0); ``` ### ­¤ö╣ B. Key Constraints (Uniqueness Constraint) A **key** is a minimal set of one or more attributes whose values uniquely identify a tuple in a relation. | Key Type | Description | Example (Student table) | |---|---|---| | **Super Key** | Any set of attributes that uniquely identifies tuples | `{roll_no}`, `{roll_no, name}`, `{roll_no, dob}` | | **Candidate Key** | Minimal super key (no proper subset is a super key) | `{roll_no}`, `{email}` | | **Primary Key** | The chosen candidate key (cannot be NULL) | `{roll_no}` | | **Alternate Key** | Candidate keys not chosen as primary | `{email}` | | **Unique Key** | Similar to primary key but allows one NULL | `{aadhar_no}` | **Uniqueness Rule:** $$ \forall t_1, t_2 \in r(R), \ t_1 \neq t_2 \implies t_1[K] \neq t_2[K] $$ ### ­¤ö╣ C. Entity Integrity Constraint > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Primary Key of a base relation must NOT be NULL.** This rule ensures that every tuple in a relation is uniquely identifiable. If the primary key were NULL, the tuple would be *nonexistent* (it could not be distinguished from other tuples). **Formal Definition:** $$ \forall t \in r(R), \ t[\text{PK}] \neq \text{NULL} $$ ### ­¤ö╣ D. Referential Integrity Constraint > [!IMPORTANT] > **A foreign key value must either match an existing primary key value in the referenced relation OR be NULL.** This maintains **consistency between related tables** ŌĆö a foundational requirement for relational joins. **Formal Definition:** Let $FK$ be a set of attributes in relation $R_1$ that references the primary key $PK$ of relation $R_2$: $$ \forall t_1 \in r(R_1), \ \forall FK \subseteq t_1, \ t_1[FK] = \text{NULL} \ \lor \ \exists t_2 \in r(R_2) \mid t_1[FK] = t_2[PK] $$ **Referential Triggered Actions (when a referenced tuple is updated/deleted):** | Action | ON DELETE | ON UPDATE | |---|---|---| | CASCADE | Delete referencing rows | Update referencing rows | | SET NULL | Set FK to NULL | Set FK to NULL | | SET DEFAULT | Set FK to default value | Set FK to default value | | RESTRICT (NO ACTION) | Reject the operation | Reject the operation | ### ­¤ö╣ E. CHECK & NOT NULL Constraints - **NOT NULL** ŌĆö Ensures the attribute cannot have a missing value. - **CHECK** ŌĆö A boolean predicate that must evaluate to TRUE for the row to be accepted. - **DEFAULT** ŌĆö Provides a default value when none is specified. - **ASSERTION** (rare in practice) ŌĆö A constraint spanning multiple relations. ## 2.2 KTU Cheat Sheet ŌĆö Integrity Constraint Reference Table | Constraint Type | NULL Allowed? | Uniqueness? | Scope | Enforced Via | |---|---|---|---|---| | PRIMARY KEY | ŌØī No | Ō£ģ Yes | Single relation | DDL | | UNIQUE | Ō£ģ Yes (1 NULL) | Ō£ģ Yes | Single relation | DDL | | FOREIGN KEY | Ō£ģ Yes (if not PK) | ŌØī No | Across relations | DDL | | NOT NULL | ŌØī No | ŌØī No | Single attribute | DDL | | CHECK | Ō£ģ Yes | ŌØī No | Single relation (or domain) | DDL | | DOMAIN | Ō£ģ Yes (if defined) | ŌØī No | Single attribute | DDL | | ASSERTION | Ō£ģ Yes | ŌØī No | Multiple relations | DDL | | TRIGGER | Ō£ģ Yes | ŌØī No | Any logic | Procedural code | ## 2.3 Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Applications:** > - **Banking Systems:** Transaction amount must be > 0; account balance cannot be negative (CHECK constraint). > - **Aviation (FAA-compliant systems):** Pilot license number (Primary Key) must be referenced by all flight logs (Referential Integrity). > - **E-Commerce:** Order status must be in {PENDING, SHIPPED, DELIVERED} ŌĆö domain enforcement. > - **Healthcare (HIPAA):** Patient ID must never be NULL for traceability (Entity Integrity). > - **IoT & Sensor Networks:** Timestamp must follow ISO 8601 ŌĆö domain constraint enforcement at ingestion. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, SQL Syntax & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Cardinality of Super Keys **Problem:** Given a relation $R(A, B, C)$ where $(A, B)$ is a candidate key, derive the **maximum number of super keys** possible. **Step-by-step derivation:** A super key is any superset of a candidate key. If $(A, B)$ is the candidate key, the possible super keys are formed by adding any combination of other attributes ($C$ in this case) to it. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Set of all supersets of } (A,B) &= \{AB, ABC\} \\ \text{Number of supersets} &= 2^{n-k} \end{aligned} $$ where $n$ = total number of attributes, $k$ = number of attributes in the candidate key. $$ \begin{aligned} n &= 3, \quad k = 2 \\ \text{Number of super keys} &= 2^{3-2} = 2^1 = 2 \end{aligned} $$ **Verification (Exhaustive Listing):** | Super Key | Uniquely Identifies? | Minimal? | |---|---|---| | $\{A, B\}$ | Ō£ģ Yes | Ō£ģ Yes (Candidate Key) | | $\{A, B, C\}$ | Ō£ģ Yes | ŌØī No (Super Key) | **Total Super Keys = 2** Ō£ō ## 3.2 SQL Implementation ŌĆö Comprehensive Example We will derive a complete schema for a **University Database** with all constraint types enforced. ```sql -- 1. DOMAIN DEFINITION CREATE DOMAIN GradeType AS CHAR(2) CHECK (VALUE IN ('A+', 'A', 'B+', 'B', 'C', 'D', 'F')); -- 2. PARENT TABLE CREATE TABLE Department ( dept_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, -- Entity Integrity dept_name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL UNIQUE, -- NOT NULL + UNIQUE hod_name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL, CHECK (LENGTH(dept_name) >= 2) -- CHECK constraint ); -- 3. CHILD TABLE CREATE TABLE Student ( roll_no INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, -- Entity Integrity name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL, -- NOT NULL aadhar_no CHAR(12) UNIQUE, -- UNIQUE (allows NULL) dept_id INTEGER, cgpa DECIMAL(3,2) CHECK (cgpa BETWEEN 0 AND 10), grade GradeType, -- DOMAIN CONSTRAINT fk_dept -- Named foreign key FOREIGN KEY (dept_id) REFERENCES Department(dept_id) ON DELETE SET NULL ON UPDATE CASCADE ); ``` ## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Referential Integrity Violation Analysis Suppose we attempt to insert a tuple into the `Student` table with `dept_id = 99`, but no row with `dept_id = 99` exists in `Department`. **Logical Evaluation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Given: } & t_{\text{new}} = (101, \text{'Rahul'}, 123412341234, 99, 8.5, 'A') \\ \text{Check: } & t_{\text{new}}[\text{dept\_id}] = 99 \\ \text{Predicate: } & 99 = \text{NULL} \ \lor \ \exists t_d \in r(\text{Department}) \mid t_d[\text{dept\_id}] = 99 \\ \text{Result: } & \text{FALSE} \ \lor \ \text{FALSE} = \text{FALSE} \\ \text{Verdict: } & \textbf{INSERT REJECTED ŌĆö Referential Integrity Violation} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Constraint Enforcement Table | Operation | Primary Key | Foreign Key | UNIQUE | NOT NULL | CHECK | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | INSERT with NULL PK | ŌØī Reject | ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ Allowed if not referenced | ŌØī Reject (if multiple NULLs not allowed) | ŌØī Reject | Ō£ģ Allowed | | INSERT with duplicate PK | ŌØī Reject | Ō£ģ Allowed | ŌØī Reject | Ō£ģ Allowed | Ō£ģ Allowed | | INSERT violating FK | ŌĆö | ŌØī Reject | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | | UPDATE violating PK | ŌØī Reject | ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ Cascades | ŌØī Reject | ŌØī Reject | ŌØī Reject | | UPDATE violating FK | ŌĆö | Depends on rule | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | ## 3.5 Closure Computation Example (For Finding Candidate Keys) Given $R(A, B, C, D)$ with Functional Dependencies: $\{A \rightarrow B, B \rightarrow C, C \rightarrow D\}$. Find the candidate key. **Step 1: Find attributes not on the RHS (left side only):** $$ A \text{ is not on RHS} \implies A \text{ must be in every candidate key} $$ **Step 2: Compute closure of $A$:** $$ \begin{aligned} A^+ &= \{A\} \\ A^+ &= \{A, B\} \quad (\text{using } A \rightarrow B) \\ A^+ &= \{A, B, C\} \quad (\text{using } B \rightarrow C) \\ A^+ &= \{A, B, C, D\} \quad (\text{using } C \rightarrow D) \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Conclusion:** $$ A^+ = \{A, B, C, D\} = R $$ Therefore, **$A$ is the only candidate key**, and the **Primary Key is $A$**. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Integrity Constraints ```mermaid flowchart TD A[INTEGRITY CONSTRAINTS] --> B[Model-Based Implicit] A --> C[Schema-Based Explicit] A --> D[Application-Based Semantic] B --> B1[Domain Constraint] B --> B2[Key Constraint] B --> B3[Entity Integrity] C --> C1[Referential Integrity] C --> C2[NOT NULL] C --> C3[UNIQUE] C --> C4[CHECK] C --> C5[DEFAULT] C --> C6[ASSERTION] D --> D1[Triggers] D --> D2[Stored Procedures] D --> D3[Application Logic] B2 --> B2a[Super Key] B2 --> B2b[Candidate Key] B2 --> B2c[Primary Key] B2 --> B2d[Alternate Key] B2 --> B2e[Unique Key] ``` ## 4.2 Referential Integrity Action Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD OP[Foreign Key Operation: UPDATE / DELETE on Parent] --> Q{NULL Allowed in FK?} Q -- Yes --> R{ON DELETE / ON UPDATE Rule} Q -- No --> S{ON DELETE / ON UPDATE Rule} R --> R1[SET NULL] R --> R2[SET DEFAULT] R --> R3[RESTRICT] S --> S1[CASCADE] S --> S2[RESTRICT / NO ACTION] R1 --> END[Constraint Checked] R2 --> END R3 --> END S1 --> END S2 --> END ``` ## 4.3 Constraint Validation Flow for INSERT Operation ```mermaid flowchart TD A[INSERT Statement Received] --> B[Parse & Validate Syntax] B --> C{Check PRIMARY KEY} C -- NULL or Duplicate --> R1[REJECT: Entity Integrity] C -- Valid --> D{Check FOREIGN KEY} D -- Invalid Reference --> R2[REJECT: Referential Integrity] D -- Valid --> E{Check UNIQUE} E -- Violated --> R3[REJECT: Key Constraint] E -- Valid --> F{Check NOT NULL} F -- Violated --> R4[REJECT: Domain Integrity] F -- Valid --> G{Check CHECK Predicate} G -- FALSE --> R5[REJECT: CHECK Violation] G -- TRUE --> H[ACCEPT: Tuple Inserted] ``` ## 4.4 Database Schema Block Diagram ŌĆö University System ```mermaid flowchart LR DEPT[Department Table
PK: dept_id
FK: None] STUDENT[Student Table
PK: roll_no
FK: dept_id] COURSE[Course Table
PK: course_id
FK: dept_id] ENROLL[Enrollment Table
PK: roll_no + course_id
FKs: roll_no, course_id] DEPT -- 1:N --> STUDENT DEPT -- 1:N --> COURSE STUDENT -- M:N --> ENROLL COURSE -- M:N --> ENROLL ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (2 ├Ś 3 Marks = 6 Marks) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023]` **Define the following with examples:** (a) Entity Integrity Constraint (b) Referential Integrity Constraint **Model Answer:** **(a) Entity Integrity Constraint:** This constraint states that the **primary key of a base relation must not be NULL**. It ensures that every tuple in a relation can be uniquely identified. *Example:* In a `Student` table, the `roll_no` field is the primary key. The DBMS will reject any INSERT where `roll_no` is NULL or duplicated. **(b) Referential Integrity Constraint:** This constraint states that a **foreign key value in a referencing relation must either match a primary key value in the referenced relation OR be NULL**. *Example:* In a `Student` table, the `dept_id` is a foreign key referencing `Department(dept_id)`. The DBMS will reject any `Student` row whose `dept_id` does not exist in the `Department` table. --- ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022]` **Differentiate between a Candidate Key, a Primary Key, and an Alternate Key. Illustrate with a suitable example.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Candidate Key | Primary Key | Alternate Key | |---|---|---|---| | Definition | Minimal super key | Chosen candidate key | Unchosen candidate key | | NULL allowed? | No | No | No | | Count per relation | Ōēź 1 | Exactly 1 | Ōēź 0 | *Example:* In `Employee(emp_id, ssn, email, name)`: - Candidate Keys: `{emp_id}`, `{ssn}`, `{email}` - Primary Key: `{emp_id}` (chosen) - Alternate Keys: `{ssn}`, `{email}` --- ## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Question (1 ├Ś 14 Marks, with Internal Choice) ### Ō£ģ Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024]` **(a) Explain the different types of integrity constraints in the relational model with suitable examples.** **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** The relational model enforces **three main categories** of integrity constraints: 1. **Domain Integrity Constraint** ŌĆö Every attribute value must belong to a predefined domain (data type and value range). Example: `age INTEGER CHECK (age >= 0)`. 2. **Entity Integrity Constraint** ŌĆö The primary key must always be non-NULL and unique. Example: `roll_no` in the Student table. 3. **Referential Integrity Constraint** ŌĆö Foreign keys must match valid primary keys (or be NULL). Example: `dept_id` in Student table referencing `Department`. 4. **Key Constraint** ŌĆö Super key, candidate key, primary key ensure uniqueness. Example: `aadhar_no UNIQUE`. 5. **User-Defined Constraints** ŌĆö `NOT NULL`, `CHECK`, `DEFAULT`, `TRIGGER`. **(b) Consider the following schema and write SQL DDL with all integrity constraints:** - `Department(dept_id PRIMARY KEY, dept_name, location)` - `Employee(emp_id, name, salary, dept_id REFERENCES Department)` - An employee belongs to exactly one department, salary must be > 0, and `dept_name` cannot be NULL. **Define the schema with all applicable constraints.** **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** ```sql CREATE TABLE Department ( dept_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, dept_name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL, location VARCHAR(50) DEFAULT 'Kerala', CHECK (LENGTH(dept_name) >= 2) ); CREATE TABLE Employee ( emp_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL, salary DECIMAL(10,2) CHECK (salary > 0), dept_id INTEGER NOT NULL, CONSTRAINT fk_emp_dept FOREIGN KEY (dept_id) REFERENCES Department(dept_id) ON DELETE RESTRICT ON UPDATE CASCADE ); ``` **Explanation of constraints used:** - `PRIMARY KEY` ŌåÆ Entity integrity on `dept_id` and `emp_id`. - `NOT NULL` ŌåÆ Enforces non-nullability of `dept_name` and `name`. - `UNIQUE` / `PRIMARY KEY` ŌåÆ Key constraint. - `CHECK (salary > 0)` ŌåÆ Domain/semantic constraint. - `FOREIGN KEY ... REFERENCES` ŌåÆ Referential integrity with RESTRICT on delete and CASCADE on update. - `DEFAULT 'Kerala'` ŌåÆ Default value constraint. --- ### Ō£ģ Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]` ŌĆö *Alternative Choice* **(a) What are the various actions defined on the foreign key to maintain referential integrity? Explain with examples.** **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** When a referenced parent row is deleted or updated, the DBMS triggers one of the following actions on the referencing child rows: | Action | On Delete | On Update | |---|---|---| | **CASCADE** | Deletes child rows automatically | Updates child FK values automatically | | **SET NULL** | Sets child FK to NULL | Sets child FK to NULL | | **SET DEFAULT** | Sets child FK to default | Sets child FK to default | | **RESTRICT / NO ACTION** | Rejects the parent's deletion | Rejects the parent's update | **Example:** ```sql ALTER TABLE Enroll ADD CONSTRAINT fk_enroll_stud FOREIGN KEY (stud_id) REFERENCES Student(roll_no) ON DELETE CASCADE ON UPDATE SET NULL; ``` *Use Case:* If a Student is deleted, all their enrollments are automatically deleted (CASCADE). If a Student's `roll_no` is updated, the FK in `Enroll` is set to NULL (then updated manually). --- **(b) Given relation R(A, B, C, D) with FDs = {AB ŌåÆ C, C ŌåÆ D, D ŌåÆ A}. Find all candidate keys and identify the primary key.** **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer (Step-by-Step using attribute closure):** **Step 1:** Find attributes not on RHS ŌåÆ none (A, C, D all appear on RHS). So start with each singleton. **Step 2:** Compute closures: - $(A)^+ = \{A\}$ ŌØī - $(B)^+ = \{B\}$ ŌØī - $(C)^+ = \{C, D, A\}$ ŌØī (does not include B) - $(D)^+ = \{D, A\}$ ŌØī **Step 3:** Try two-attribute combinations: - $(A, B)^+ = \{A, B, C, D\}$ Ō£ģ - $(A, C)^+ = \{A, C, D\}$ ŌØī - $(A, D)^+ = \{A, D\}$ ŌØī - $(B, C)^+ = \{B, C, D, A\}$ Ō£ģ - $(B, D)^+ = \{B, D, A\}$ ŌØī - $(C, D)^+ = \{C, D, A\}$ ŌØī **Step 4:** Confirm minimality: Neither $\{A\}$ nor $\{B\}$ is a super key. So both are candidate keys. **Final Answer:** | Candidate Key | Selection | |---|---| | $\{A, B\}$ | Ō£ģ Chosen as **Primary Key** | | $\{B, C\}$ | **Alternate Key** | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall ŌĆö Common Mistakes:** > 1. **Confusing Domain with Domain Name** ŌĆö Domain is the data type and allowed value set, not the table itself. > 2. **Forgetting NOT NULL in UNIQUE** ŌĆö Students often write `UNIQUE` and forget that a `UNIQUE` column *can* have one NULL value (in most RDBMS). > 3. **Confusing CASCADE and RESTRICT** ŌĆö CASCADE automatically modifies child rows; RESTRICT simply blocks the operation. > 4. **Skipping the ON DELETE / ON UPDATE clause** ŌĆö Always specify the triggered action for referential integrity to score full marks. > 5. **Writing `KEY` instead of `PRIMARY KEY`** ŌĆö `KEY` is MySQL-specific shorthand; in KTU exams, use `PRIMARY KEY`. > 6. **Missing parentheses in CHECK constraints** ŌĆö `CHECK (salary > 0)` is correct; `CHECK salary > 0` will cause a syntax error. > 7. **Forgetting semicolons and constraint names** ŌĆö Naming a constraint using `CONSTRAINT constraint_name` is best practice and earns extra marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ­¤ōī - Ō£ģ **Three pillars** of integrity: **Entity**, **Referential**, and **Domain**. - Ō£ģ **Primary Key** = NOT NULL + UNIQUE (atomic, minimal, stable). - Ō£ģ **Foreign Key** either matches a PK in the parent table or is NULL. - Ō£ģ **Referential Actions**: `CASCADE`, `SET NULL`, `SET DEFAULT`, `RESTRICT`, `NO ACTION`. - Ō£ģ **Domain constraints** = data type + range + check predicate. - Ō£ģ **Candidate Key** ŌŖé **Super Key**; **Primary Key** + **Alternate Keys** = all **Candidate Keys**. - Ō£ģ **Triggers** enforce complex business rules beyond declarative constraints. - Ō£ģ **ASSERTION** is for multi-relation constraints (rarely supported natively). - Ō£ģ Always **name your constraints** with `CONSTRAINT ` for clarity and easy alteration. - Ō£ģ **Order of constraint checking** in INSERT/UPDATE: PRIMARY KEY ŌåÆ FOREIGN KEY ŌåÆ UNIQUE ŌåÆ NOT NULL ŌåÆ CHECK. - Ō£ģ In KTU exams, **draw diagrams** for relationship constraints ŌĆö visual representation earns bonus marks. - Ō£ģ **Default value** is used when an attribute is not specified in INSERT. - Ō£ģ **Composite Primary Key** is declared as `PRIMARY KEY (attr1, attr2)` with both attributes collectively being NOT NULL. - Ō£ģ Difference between `UNIQUE` and `PRIMARY KEY`: UNIQUE allows NULL, PRIMARY KEY does not.

topics.title: Synthesizing ER Diagram to Relational Schema
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBEOT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Synthesizing ER Diagram to Relational Schema ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Synthesizing (or Mapping) an ER Diagram to a Relational Schema** is the systematic, algorithmic procedure used in Database Design to translate the conceptual, semantic constructs of an **Entity-Relationship (ER) Model** ŌĆö namely *entity sets*, *relationship sets*, and *attributes* ŌĆö into a logically equivalent set of **relations (tables)** defined over atomic domains in the **Relational Data Model**. In the KTU 2024 scheme, this step is the critical bridge between **Conceptual Design** (Phase 1) and **Logical Design** (Phase 2) of the database design lifecycle. The output of this synthesis is a set of **relation schemas** $R(A_1, A_2, \dots, A_n)$ where each $A_i$ is an attribute drawn from a valid atomic domain, conforming to **First Normal Form (1NF)**. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö "The Architectural Blueprint vs. The Building" > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine an architect's *blueprint* of a house. The blueprint shows *rooms* (entity sets), *doors* connecting rooms (relationships), and *labels* on rooms like "Kitchen - 12m┬▓" (attributes). The blueprint is beautiful and intuitive, but you cannot *cook* in a blueprint ŌĆö you need a real, brick-and-mortar **building**. The **building** is the **Relational Schema** (tables with rows and columns), and the **blueprint** is the **ER Diagram**. *Synthesis* is the act of turning the blueprint into the building, following strict construction codes (mapping rules). ### 1.3 Why is Synthesis Essential? - **Implementation Readiness**: SQL (the language of RDBMS) operates on tables, not on ER diagrams. - **Lossless Decomposition**: A correct mapping preserves all semantic information (no information loss). - **Dependency Preservation**: Functional dependencies from the real world are carried into the new schema. ### 1.4 Core Components of an ER Diagram | ER Construct | Definition | Example | |---|---|---| | **Entity Set** | A collection of similar real-world objects | `Student`, `Course` | | **Relationship Set** | A meaningful association among entity sets | `Enrolls`, `Teaches` | | **Attributes** | Descriptive properties of entities or relationships | `Name`, `Grade`, `DOB` | | **Primary Key** | A unique identifier for an entity | `StudentID`, `CourseID` | | **Weak Entity** | An entity that cannot be uniquely identified by its own attributes | `Dependent` of a `Employee` | > [!TIP] > **KTU 2024 Board Exam Tip:** Examiners frequently test whether a student can identify the *cardinality ratio* (1:1, 1:N, M:N) and *participation constraint* (total/partial) of a relationship ŌĆö both directly dictate the mapping rule. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Seven Foundational Mapping Rules The synthesis from ER to Relational Schema is governed by **seven algorithmic steps**, each tailored to a specific ER construct. These rules are deterministic ŌĆö for a given ER diagram, the resulting relational schema is unique up to renaming. #### Step 1: Mapping a Strong Entity Set - Create a relation $R$ containing **all simple attributes** of the entity set. - Choose one of the **key attributes** as the **Primary Key (PK)**. - **Composite attributes** are flattened to their simple components. - **Derived attributes** are **omitted**. #### Step 2: Mapping a Weak Entity Set - Create a relation $R_w$ containing all its simple attributes. - Include the **Primary Key of the owner (identifying) entity** as a **Foreign Key (FK)**. - The **Primary Key of $R_w$** = (FK from owner) $\cup$ (Partial Key of weak entity). #### Step 3: Mapping a 1:1 Relationship Set - Two viable strategies exist: - **Strategy A**: Merge the relationship into either of the two participating entity relations as a FK. - **Strategy B**: Create a separate relation with FKs to both entities (PK is the FK of either side). - **Best Practice (KTU recommended)**: Choose the entity with **Total Participation** to absorb the FK ŌĆö this avoids NULL values. #### Step 4: Mapping a 1:N Relationship Set - **No new relation needed.** - Add the **Primary Key of the "1" side** as a **Foreign Key** into the relation representing the **"N" side**. - This preserves referential integrity without table proliferation. #### Step 5: Mapping an M:N Relationship Set - **Mandatory new relation** must be created. - This new relation contains: - **Foreign Keys** referencing the PKs of both participating entity sets. - Any **descriptive attributes** of the relationship itself. - **Primary Key** = Union of both FKs (composite key). #### Step 6: Mapping Multivalued Attributes - A multivalued attribute (e.g., `PhoneNumbers` of a `Student`) violates 1NF if left in the parent table. - **Create a separate relation** $R_{mv}$ containing: - The **PK of the original entity** as FK. - The **multivalued attribute** itself. - **Primary Key** = (FK from parent) $\cup$ (the multivalued attribute). #### Step 7: Mapping N-ary Relationship Sets - For a relationship involving $n$ entity sets ($n \geq 3$): - Create a new relation $R_n$ containing **all FKs** referencing the PKs of each participating entity. - Include any descriptive attributes of the relationship. - **Primary Key** = Union of selected FKs (usually the combination that uniquely identifies an instance). ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet ŌĆö The Mapping Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **MANDATORY TABLE**: The following table is the **single most important reference** for KTU 2024 ESE questions on this topic. Memorize it. | ER Construct | New Relation Created? | Schema Content | Primary Key | |---|---|---|---| | **Strong Entity** | **Yes** | All simple (atomic) attributes | One chosen key attribute | | **Weak Entity** | **Yes** | Simple attrs + FK to owner | Owner's PK $\cup$ Partial Key | | **1:1 Relationship** | Optional (Best: merge) | FK to other side in chosen entity | FK acts as alternate key | | **1:N Relationship** | **No** (merge into N-side) | FK of 1-side added to N-side | Unchanged (PK of N-side) | | **M:N Relationship** | **Yes (mandatory)** | FKs of both sides + descriptive attrs | FK$_1 \cup$ FK$_2$ | | **Multivalued Attribute** | **Yes (mandatory)** | FK to parent + the attribute | FK $\cup$ attribute | | **N-ary Relationship** ($n \geq 3$) | **Yes (mandatory)** | FKs of all $n$ participating entities | Selective union of FKs | ### 2.3 Special Cases Discussed in KTU 2024 Syllabus - **Aggregation**: Treating a relationship as a higher-level entity. When mapping, the aggregated relationship is treated as a participating entity in an outer relationship. - **Specialization / Generalization**: Multiple approaches ŌĆö *"Convert to multiple relations"* (one per subclass), *"Convert to a single relation"* (one for superclass with nullable subtype attributes), or *"Convert to multiple relations with superclass relation"*. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > In **enterprise systems** (e.g., banking, hospital management, e-commerce), automated CASE tools like **Oracle Designer**, **ERwin**, and **PowerDesigner** internally execute these exact mapping algorithms to generate DDL scripts. Understanding the algorithm is essential for manual schema tuning and reverse-engineering legacy databases. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Canonical Worked Example ŌĆö End-to-End Synthesis Consider the following ER Diagram for a **University Course Registration System**: **ER Components Identified:** - **Strong Entities**: `Student` (PK: `StudentID`), `Course` (PK: `CourseID`) - **Weak Entity**: `Dependent` (Partial Key: `DepName`), owned by `Student` - **Relationship 1**: `Enrolls` between `Student` and `Course` ŌĆö **M:N**, with descriptive attribute `Grade` - **Relationship 2**: `Owns` between `Student` and `Dependent` ŌĆö **1:N** (identifying relationship) - **Attribute**: `Student.Phone` is **multivalued** - **Attribute**: `Student.Name` is **composite** (`FirstName`, `MiddleName`, `LastName`) - **Attribute**: `Student.Age` is **derived** --- #### Step-by-Step Mapping Derivation **Step A ŌĆö Map the Strong Entity `Student`:** Create a new relation. Include all *atomic* simple attributes. The composite attribute `Name` is decomposed; the derived attribute `Age` is excluded. $$ \text{Student}(\underline{\text{StudentID}}, \text{FirstName}, \text{MiddleName}, \text{LastName}, \text{DOB}) $$ *Justification*: `StudentID` is the chosen primary key. Composite attribute is flattened per 1NF. Derived attribute is dropped as it can be computed from `DOB`. --- **Step B ŌĆö Map the Strong Entity `Course`:** $$ \text{Course}(\underline{\text{CourseID}}, \text{Title}, \text{Credits}) $$ *Justification*: All attributes are atomic; `CourseID` is the primary key. --- **Step C ŌĆö Map the Weak Entity `Dependent`:** Create a new relation. Include its partial key `DepName` and the owner's PK as FK. The PK of this new relation is the union. $$ \text{Dependent}(\underline{\text{StudentID}}, \underline{\text{DepName}}, \text{Relationship}, \text{Age}) $$ *Justification*: `Dependent` cannot exist without `Student`. The owner's PK (`StudentID`) acts as both FK and part of the composite PK. --- **Step D ŌĆö Map the M:N Relationship `Enrolls`:** A new relation is mandatory. Include FKs to both entities plus the descriptive attribute `Grade`. $$ \text{Enrolls}(\underline{\text{StudentID}}, \underline{\text{CourseID}}, \text{Grade}) $$ *Justification*: M:N cannot be merged. Composite PK of both FKs uniquely identifies an enrollment instance. `Grade` is a property of the association, not of either entity. --- **Step E ŌĆö Map the 1:N Relationship `Owns`:** No new relation. Add the PK of the 1-side (`Student.StudentID`) as FK into the N-side (`Dependent`). $$ \text{Dependent}(\underline{\text{StudentID}}, \underline{\text{DepName}}, \text{Relationship}, \text{Age}, \text{StudentID}_{\text{FK}}) $$ *Justification*: This is redundant with Step C because `Owns` is the *identifying* relationship of the weak entity. In general 1:N cases, this rule avoids relation explosion. --- **Step F ŌĆö Map the Multivalued Attribute `Student.Phone`:** A new relation is mandatory to preserve 1NF. $$ \text{Student\_Phone}(\underline{\text{StudentID}}, \underline{\text{PhoneNumber}}) $$ *Justification*: A student may have zero or many phone numbers. Storing them as a single comma-separated value violates 1NF. --- ### 3.2 Final Synthesized Relational Schema ŌĆö Analytical Matrix | Relation Name | Attributes | Primary Key | Foreign Keys | Mapping Origin | |---|---|---|---|---| | `Student` | `StudentID, FirstName, MiddleName, LastName, DOB` | `StudentID` | ŌĆö | Step A | | `Course` | `CourseID, Title, Credits` | `CourseID` | ŌĆö | Step B | | `Dependent` | `StudentID, DepName, Relationship, Age` | `(StudentID, DepName)` | `StudentID` $\to$ `Student` | Step C + E | | `Enrolls` | `StudentID, CourseID, Grade` | `(StudentID, CourseID)` | `StudentID` $\to$ `Student`, `CourseID` $\to$ `Course` | Step D | | `Student_Phone` | `StudentID, PhoneNumber` | `(StudentID, PhoneNumber)` | `StudentID` $\to$ `Student` | Step F | ### 3.3 Verification Checklist ŌĆö Lossless Join Test To confirm the synthesis is **lossless**, verify: 1. Every attribute of every entity appears in **at least one** relation. Ō£ģ 2. Every descriptive attribute of a relationship is captured. Ō£ģ 3. Foreign keys properly enforce referential integrity. Ō£ģ 4. No multivalued attributes remain in any single relation. Ō£ģ 5. The number of relations created matches the count of strong entities + weak entities + M:N relationships + multivalued attributes + (optional) 1:1 relationships. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mapping Algorithm Flowchart (Block-Level Functional Architecture) The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **decision flow** of the synthesis algorithm, branching on each ER construct encountered. ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([Start: Read ER Diagram]) --> scanER{Iterate Over
Each ER Construct} scanER -- Strong Entity --> op1[Create Relation R1
Attributes = Simple attrs
PK = Key Attribute] scanER -- Weak Entity --> op2[Create Relation R2
Add FK to Owner
PK = Owner.PK + Partial Key] scanER -- Relationship --> relCheck{Cardinality
Ratio?} relCheck -- 1:1 --> op3a{Participation
Constraint?} op3a -- Total on Either Side --> op3b[Merge FK into
Total-Participation Entity] op3a -- Partial on Both --> op3c[Create Separate
Relation with Both FKs] relCheck -- 1:N --> op4[Add FK of 1-Side
into N-Side Relation
No New Relation] relCheck -- M:N --> op5[Create New Relation
PK = FK1 + FK2
Include Descriptive Attrs] relCheck -- N-ary --> op6[Create New Relation
PK = Selective FKs
Include Descriptive Attrs] scanER -- Multivalued Attribute --> op7[Create New Relation
PK = ParentFK + Attribute] scanER -- Derived Attribute --> op8[Omit from Schema
Compute at Runtime] op1 --> collect[Add to Schema Set] op2 --> collect op3b --> collect op3c --> collect op4 --> collect op5 --> collect op6 --> collect op7 --> collect op8 --> collect collect --> more{More Constructs
Remaining?} more -- Yes --> scanER more -- No --> finalize[Finalize Relational Schema
Enforce Referential Integrity
Normalize to 1NF Minimum] finalize([End: Optimized Relational Schema Ready for DDL]) ``` ### 4.2 Worked Example ŌĆö Visual Mapping Tree This second diagram maps the exact example from Section 3, showing each ER node and its corresponding relational output. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ER_Input [ER Diagram - University System] eStudent[Strong Entity: Student] eCourse[Strong Entity: Course] eDependent[Weak Entity: Dependent] rEnrolls[Relationship M:N: Enrolls] rOwns[Relationship 1:N: Owns] aPhone[Multivalued Attr: Phone] aName[Composite Attr: Name] aAge[Derived Attr: Age] end subgraph Schema_Output [Relational Schema - Tables] tStudent[Table: Student
PK: StudentID
Cols: FirstName, MiddleName, LastName, DOB] tCourse[Table: Course
PK: CourseID
Cols: Title, Credits] tDep[Table: Dependent
PK: StudentID + DepName
FK: StudentID] tEnroll[Table: Enrolls
PK: StudentID + CourseID
FKs: Both
Col: Grade] tPhone[Table: Student_Phone
PK: StudentID + PhoneNumber
FK: StudentID] end eStudent --> tStudent eCourse --> tCourse eDependent --> tDep rEnrolls --> tEnroll rOwns --> tDep aPhone --> tPhone aName --> tStudent aAge -.omitted.-> x1[No Output] ``` ### 4.3 Cardinality vs. Schema Output ŌĆö Quick Reference Matrix | Cardinality | Required Action | Tables Touched | FK Placement | |---|---|---|---| | **1:1** | Best: merge | 1 entity table modified | FK in entity with total participation | | **1:N** | Merge into N-side | 1 entity table modified | FK in N-side | | **M:N** | Always new table | 1 new junction table | Two FKs, composite PK | | **N-ary** | Always new table | 1 new table | $n$ FKs | ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *What is meant by mapping an ER diagram to a relational schema? Why is it considered a necessary step in database design?* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Mapping an ER diagram to a relational schema is the process of converting the conceptual representation (entities, relationships, attributes) into a logical set of relations (tables) that can be implemented using SQL. **(1 Mark)** It is necessary because: **(2 Marks)** 1. SQL and RDBMS engines operate on tables, not on ER diagrams directly. The ER diagram is a conceptual blueprint; the relational schema is the implementable specification. 2. The mapping preserves the semantics of the original design in a lossless, dependency-preserving manner, ensuring no information is lost during translation. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *List any three rules for mapping ER constructs to relations. Mention what is done with multivalued and derived attributes.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Three Mapping Rules:** **(2 Marks)** 1. Each strong entity set becomes a relation with the chosen key as PK. 2. Each M:N relationship becomes a new relation with the PKs of both entities forming a composite PK. 3. Each 1:N relationship is represented by adding the PK of the 1-side as a foreign key into the N-side relation. **Multivalued Attributes:** A separate relation is created with the FK of the parent and the multivalued attribute, ensuring 1NF compliance. **(0.5 Marks)** **Derived Attributes:** They are omitted from the relational schema since they can be computed from other stored attributes. **(0.5 Marks)** --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ŌĆö Internal Choice --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]** *Consider the following ER diagram for a Hospital Management System:* - Strong Entities: **Doctor** (PK: `DoctorID`, attributes: `Name`, `Specialization`, `Salary`) and **Patient** (PK: `PatientID`, attributes: `Name`, `DOB`, `Gender`). - Weak Entity: **MedicalRecord** (Partial Key: `RecordNo`), owned by `Patient`. Attributes: `DateOfVisit`, `Diagnosis`. - Relationship: **Treats** between Doctor and MedicalRecord ŌĆö **1:N** (One doctor treats many records; each record has exactly one doctor). - Multivalued Attribute: `Doctor.ContactNumbers`. - Derived Attribute: `Patient.Age`. **(a)** Synthesize the above ER diagram into a relational schema. Clearly state the primary keys and foreign keys. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain why a separate relation must be created for the `MedicalRecord` weak entity, and justify why the derived attribute `Age` is not represented in any relation. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution ŌĆö Question A:** **Part (a) ŌĆö Synthesized Relational Schema:** Mapping Doctor (Strong Entity): $$ \text{Doctor}(\underline{\text{DoctorID}}, \text{Name}, \text{Specialization}, \text{Salary}) $$ Mapping Patient (Strong Entity): $$ \text{Patient}(\underline{\text{PatientID}}, \text{Name}, \text{DOB}, \text{Gender}) $$ Mapping MedicalRecord (Weak Entity) + Treats (1:N relationship): Since `MedicalRecord` is a weak entity owned by `Patient`, and the 1:N relationship `Treats` connects `Doctor` to `MedicalRecord`, we add the `DoctorID` as FK to `MedicalRecord`. $$ \text{MedicalRecord}(\underline{\text{PatientID}}, \underline{\text{RecordNo}}, \text{DateOfVisit}, \text{Diagnosis}, \text{DoctorID}_{\text{FK}}) $$ Mapping Multivalued Attribute Doctor.ContactNumbers: $$ \text{Doctor\_Contact}(\underline{\text{DoctorID}}, \underline{\text{PhoneNumber}}) $$ *(1 Mark for correct identification of relations, 2 Marks for correct attributes, 2 Marks for PK and FK assignments, 2 Marks for multivalued separation ŌĆö Total 7 Marks)* --- **Part (b) ŌĆö Justifications:** **Why a separate relation for `MedicalRecord`?** **(3.5 Marks)** A weak entity cannot be uniquely identified by its own attributes alone ŌĆö it depends on its owner entity (`Patient`). Without `PatientID`, two patients could have a `RecordNo` of "1", causing ambiguity. Therefore, `MedicalRecord` is mapped into its own relation where `PatientID` acts as both a FK (referencing `Patient`) and a component of the composite PK `(PatientID, RecordNo)`. **Why `Age` is omitted?** **(3.5 Marks)** `Age` is a derived attribute. It can be calculated at query time using the SQL expression `DATEDIFF(CURDATE(), DOB)`. Storing it would introduce redundancy (since it changes yearly) and violate the principle of minimizing update anomalies. Hence, per mapping rules, derived attributes are dropped from the relational schema. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *For a Library Management System, the following ER constructs are given:* - Strong Entities: **Book** (PK: `ISBN`, attributes: `Title`, `Publisher`, `Year`) and **Member** (PK: `MemberID`, attributes: `Name`, `Address`, `MembershipDate`). - Relationship: **Borrows** between Member and Book ŌĆö **M:N**, with descriptive attribute `BorrowDate` and `ReturnDueDate`. - Composite Attribute: `Member.Name` consists of `FirstName` and `LastName`. - Multivalued Attribute: `Book.Authors`. **(a)** Perform the complete mapping of the above ER diagram to a relational schema, explicitly writing each relation with its primary and foreign keys. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Discuss the consequences if the M:N relationship `Borrows` were naively stored as foreign keys within the `Book` relation. Justify why a separate relation is mandatory. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution ŌĆö Question B:** **Part (a) ŌĆö Relational Schema Mapping:** Step 1 ŌĆö Map Strong Entity `Book`: $$ \text{Book}(\underline{\text{ISBN}}, \text{Title}, \text{Publisher}, \text{Year}) $$ Step 2 ŌĆö Map Strong Entity `Member` (composite attribute flattened): $$ \text{Member}(\underline{\text{MemberID}}, \text{FirstName}, \text{LastName}, \text{Address}, \text{MembershipDate}) $$ Step 3 ŌĆö Map M:N Relationship `Borrows` with descriptive attributes: $$ \text{Borrows}(\underline{\text{MemberID}}, \underline{\text{ISBN}}, \text{BorrowDate}, \text{ReturnDueDate}) $$ Foreign Keys: `MemberID` references `Member(MemberID)`, `ISBN` references `Book(ISBN)`. Step 4 ŌĆö Map Multivalued Attribute `Book.Authors`: $$ \text{Book\_Authors}(\underline{\text{ISBN}}, \underline{\text{AuthorName}}) $$ Foreign Key: `ISBN` references `Book(ISBN)`. *(Credit: 7 Marks ŌĆö 2 for entity relations, 2 for M:N with descriptive attributes, 1 for multivalued, 1 for composite flattening, 1 for FK annotations.)* --- **Part (b) ŌĆö Why a Separate Relation for M:N is Mandatory:** If we attempted to store `MemberID` as a multivalued foreign key inside the `Book` relation, we would face three critical violations: **(7 Marks)** 1. **Violation of 1NF (First Normal Form)**: A cell would contain multiple `MemberID` values (e.g., "M101, M102, M103"), violating atomicity. The relational model requires every cell to be atomic. 2. **Loss of Descriptive Attributes**: The relationship `Borrows` has its own attributes (`BorrowDate`, `ReturnDueDate`). If we tried to attach these to `Book`, we would need to repeat `BorrowDate` for each member, again violating 1NF. 3. **Query Complexity and Anomalies**: Operations like "Find all books borrowed by Member M101" would require expensive string parsing on the multivalued column. Additionally, inserting a new borrowing would require modifying the `Book` row, leading to update anomalies. **Conclusion**: A separate `Borrows` relation with a composite PK `(MemberID, ISBN)` is the only normalized, anomaly-free solution. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks in KTU 2024 ESE on this topic:** > 1. **Forgetting the M:N rule**: Many students try to absorb an M:N relationship into one of the participating entities ŌĆö this loses **3 to 4 marks** immediately. > 2. **Ignoring weak entity composite PKs**: Examiners specifically check whether the `Owner_PK + Partial_Key` is correctly identified. Missing this costs **2 marks**. > 3. **Leaving multivalued attributes in the parent table**: This is the #1 normalization mistake. KTU examiners actively test for this. **Cost: 2 marks**. > 4. **Including derived attributes**: Storing `Age` or `Experience` as a stored column shows a fundamental misunderstanding. **Cost: 1 mark**. > 5. **Not annotating FKs**: Simply listing attributes without marking which are foreign keys (and to which relation they refer) is considered an incomplete answer. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **Strong Entity** ŌåÆ Own relation, PK = chosen key. - [x] **Weak Entity** ŌåÆ Own relation, PK = Owner_PK + Partial_Key, plus FK to owner. - [x] **1:1 Relationship** ŌåÆ Best merged into the side with **total participation**; FK added. - [x] **1:N Relationship** ŌåÆ **No new table**; FK of 1-side added to N-side. - [x] **M:N Relationship** ŌåÆ **Mandatory new junction table**; PK = union of both FKs. - [x] **N-ary Relationship** ŌåÆ New table with $n$ FKs and selective composite PK. - [x] **Multivalued Attribute** ŌåÆ **Mandatory new table**; PK = Parent_FK + Attribute. - [x] **Composite Attribute** ŌåÆ Flattened into simple sub-attributes in the parent table. - [x] **Derived Attribute** ŌåÆ **Omitted entirely**; computed at runtime. - [x] **Aggregation** ŌåÆ Treated as a higher-level entity participating in outer relationships. - [x] **Verification** ŌåÆ Check for lossless join, dependency preservation, and 1NF compliance. > [!TIP] > **Final Mnemonic for KTU 2024 Exam Hall**: *"**S**trong = **S**tandalone, **W**eak = **W**ith owner, **1** = **O**ne table, **N** = **N**o new, **M** = **M**ust create, **Multi** = **M**ust split."*

topics.title: Structured Query Language (SQL)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Structured Query Language (SQL)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Structured Query Language (SQL)** is a **declarative, non-procedural, domain-specific** fourth-generation language (4GL) standardized by **ANSI (American National Standards Institute)** in 1986 and **ISO (International Organization for Standardization)** in 1987, used for defining, manipulating, controlling, and querying relational database management systems (RDBMS).

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBEOT504 - DBMS)**, SQL is the operational interface between the user/application and the relational engine. Unlike procedural languages (C, Java, Python) where the programmer specifies *how* to retrieve data, SQL allows the programmer to specify *what* data is required, leaving the execution strategy (query optimization, access path selection) to the **Relational Database Management System** (e.g., Oracle, MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQL Server).

### SQL Language Subdivisions (As per KTU Syllabus)

| Sub-language | Full Form | Core Function | Common Commands |
|--------------|-----------|---------------|-----------------|
| **DDL** | Data Definition Language | Defines the database schema/structure | `CREATE`, `ALTER`, `DROP`, `TRUNCATE`, `RENAME` |
| **DML** | Data Manipulation Language | Manipulates the data within schema objects | `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE`, `SELECT` (sometimes) |
| **DCL** | Data Control Language | Controls access permissions/rights | `GRANT`, `REVOKE` |
| **TCL** | Transaction Control Language | Manages database transactions | `COMMIT`, `ROLLBACK`, `SAVEPOINT`, `SET TRANSACTION` |
| **DQL** | Data Query Language | Performs queries on data | `SELECT` |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Restaurant Kitchen**
>
> Imagine you walk into a restaurant. You do **not** enter the kitchen, grab the ingredients, light the stove, and cook the meal yourself. Instead, you hand a **waiter** a written order: *"One plate of Biriyani, less spicy, extra raita."* The waiter passes it to the chef, who decides *how* to prepare it.
>
> - **You (User)** = Application Programmer writing SQL
> - **The Order Slip (SQL Query)** = The SQL statement (`SELECT * FROM Menu WHERE dish='Biriyani'`)
> - **The Waiter (DBMS Engine)** = Parses, optimizes, and executes
> - **The Chef (Query Optimizer)** = Decides the best execution plan (index scan, full table scan, join order)
> - **The Cooked Dish (Result Set)** = The rows returned
>
> SQL tells the database **WHAT** you want, never **HOW** to get it. This is the essence of *declarative* programming.

---

## 1.3 Why SQL Matters in KTU 2024 DBMS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PBEOT504 - Module 2):**
> SQL forms **30-40%** of the End Semester Examination (ESE) weightage in KTU DBMS papers. Mastery of `SELECT` syntax (joins, subqueries, set operations, aggregate functions) and **DDL/DML** commands is mandatory. Expect a full 14-mark Part B question on this module.

## 1.4 Standard SQL Data Types (ISO/ANSI Compliance)

| Category | Data Type | Description | Example |
|----------|-----------|-------------|---------|
| Exact Numeric | `INTEGER`, `SMALLINT`, `BIGINT` | Whole numbers | `42`, `-7` |
| Exact Numeric | `NUMERIC(p,s)`, `DECIMAL(p,s)` | Fixed-point with precision `p` and scale `s` | `1234.56` |
| Approximate Numeric | `FLOAT`, `REAL`, `DOUBLE PRECISION` | Floating-point | `3.14E0` |
| Character String | `CHAR(n)`, `VARCHAR(n)` | Fixed/variable length | `'Kerala'` |
| Character Large Object | `CLOB` | Large text (up to GBs) | Book contents |
| Binary | `BINARY`, `VARBINARY` | Raw bytes | Image thumbnails |
| Binary Large Object | `BLOB` | Binary large object | Video files |
| Boolean | `BOOLEAN` | TRUE / FALSE / UNKNOWN | `TRUE` |
| Temporal | `DATE`, `TIME`, `TIMESTAMP` | Date and time | `2024-12-15` |
| Temporal | `INTERVAL` | Time interval | `5 DAYS` |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 SQL Command Execution Pipeline

When an SQL statement is submitted, the RDBMS engine processes it through a strict logical pipeline:

1. **Parsing (Syntax Check):** The SQL string is tokenized and checked against the language grammar. Errors like missing `FROM` cause a syntax error here.
2. **Binding (Semantic Check):** References to tables, columns, and privileges are validated against the Data Dictionary / System Catalog.
3. **Optimization:** The **Query Optimizer** generates multiple equivalent execution plans and selects the one with the lowest estimated cost (I/O, CPU, memory).
4. **Execution:** The chosen plan runs against the database. Rows are fetched, joined, filtered, and aggregated.
5. **Result Return:** The final result set is returned to the caller.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Questions on "query processing order" (logical vs. physical) test this exact pipeline. Always remember the **logical SQL execution order** differs from the **written syntax order**.

### Logical Order of SQL Query Execution

| Execution Step | Clause | What it does |
|----------------|--------|--------------|
| 1 | `FROM` / `JOIN` | Identifies source tables and performs Cartesian product |
| 2 | `WHERE` | Filters individual rows (row-level predicate) |
| 3 | `GROUP BY` | Partitions rows into groups |
| 4 | `HAVING` | Filters groups (group-level predicate) |
| 5 | `SELECT` | Evaluates expressions and projects columns |
| 6 | `DISTINCT` | Removes duplicate result rows |
| 7 | `ORDER BY` | Sorts the final result set |
| 8 | `LIMIT` / `OFFSET` | Restricts rows returned |

---

## 2.2 DDL ŌĆö Data Definition Language (Schema Operations)

### 2.2.1 `CREATE TABLE` (with Constraints)

The `CREATE TABLE` statement defines a relation. **Constraints** enforce integrity rules at the schema level, not the application level ŌĆö this is the *defining feature* of a relational system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Constraint Types (KTU High-Yield):**
> - **NOT NULL** ŌĆö Column cannot be empty.
> - **UNIQUE** ŌĆö Column values are non-duplicate (NULLs allowed once per column unless DBMS differs).
> - **PRIMARY KEY** ŌĆö Combination of NOT NULL + UNIQUE; identifies a row uniquely.
> - **FOREIGN KEY** ŌĆö Enforces referential integrity between tables.
> - **CHECK** ŌĆö Enforces a domain predicate on values.
> - **DEFAULT** ŌĆö Supplies a default value if none is provided.

### 2.2.2 Referential Integrity Actions (Foreign Key)

When a parent row is deleted or updated, the child rows can be handled via:

| Action | Behavior |
|--------|----------|
| `CASCADE` | Child rows are deleted/updated automatically |
| `SET NULL` | Child FK becomes NULL |
| `SET DEFAULT` | Child FK becomes default value |
| `RESTRICT` (default) | Operation rejected if child rows exist |
| `NO ACTION` | Similar to RESTRICT (deferred check) |

### 2.2.3 `ALTER TABLE` Modifications

- `ADD COLUMN <col> <type>` ŌĆö Adds a new column.
- `DROP COLUMN <col>` ŌĆö Removes a column.
- `MODIFY/ALTER COLUMN <col> <new_type>` ŌĆö Changes column datatype.
- `ADD CONSTRAINT` ŌĆö Adds a new integrity rule.
- `DROP CONSTRAINT` ŌĆö Removes an existing rule.

### 2.2.4 `DROP` vs. `TRUNCATE` vs. `DELETE` (The Classic KTU Question!)

| Feature | `DELETE` | `TRUNCATE` | `DROP` |
|---------|----------|------------|--------|
| Command Type | DML | DDL | DDL |
| Removes Rows? | Yes (filtered) | Yes (all) | Yes (entire table) |
| Removes Structure? | No | No | Yes |
| Can Rollback? | Yes (transactional) | No* | No* |
| Fires Triggers? | Yes (per row) | No | No |
| Speed | Slowest (row-by-row) | Fastest (deallocates pages) | Fast (DDL operation) |
| `WHERE` clause? | Yes | No | No |

`*` Depends on DBMS (Oracle is implicit-commit; some engines support transactional DDL).

---

## 2.3 DML ŌĆö Data Manipulation Language

### 2.3.1 `INSERT INTO` Variants

- **Single-row insert:** Explicit column list.
- **Multi-row insert from SELECT:** Subquery in `VALUES` position.
- **Bulk insert without column list:** Must provide all column values in declared order.

### 2.3.2 `UPDATE ... SET ... WHERE`

The `WHERE` clause is **CRITICAL**. Forgetting it updates every row in the table.

### 2.3.3 `DELETE FROM ... WHERE`

Also requires a careful `WHERE`. Without it, every row is deleted (equivalent to `TRUNCATE` in effect, but slower and transactional).

---

## 2.4 The `SELECT` Statement ŌĆö KTU Most-Favorite Clause

The full canonical syntax is:

```sql
SELECT [DISTINCT] <column_list> | <aggregate> | *
FROM   <table_references_with_joins>
WHERE  <row_predicate>
GROUP  BY <grouping_columns>
HAVING <group_predicate>
ORDER  BY <sort_columns> [ASC | DESC]
LIMIT  <n> OFFSET <m>;
```

### 2.4.1 Aggregate Functions

| Function | Purpose | NULL Handling |
|----------|---------|---------------|
| `COUNT(*)` | Counts rows including NULLs | Counts NULLs |
| `COUNT(col)` | Counts non-NULL values in column | Ignores NULLs |
| `SUM(col)` | Sum of numeric values | Ignores NULLs |
| `AVG(col)` | Arithmetic mean | Ignores NULLs |
| `MIN(col)` | Minimum value | Ignores NULLs |
| `MAX(col)` | Maximum value | Ignores NULLs |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** In `COUNT(*)`, NULL values **ARE** counted. In `COUNT(col)`, NULLs are **NOT** counted. Marks lost every year on this distinction.

### 2.4.2 `GROUP BY` and `HAVING`

- `WHERE` filters rows **before** grouping.
- `HAVING` filters groups **after** aggregation.
- Every column in the `SELECT` list that is *not* inside an aggregate function **MUST** appear in the `GROUP BY` clause (this is the **single-value rule**).

---

## 2.5 SQL JOINs (High-Yield KTU Topic)

Joins combine rows from two or more tables based on a related column.

| Join Type | Returns |
|-----------|---------|
| `INNER JOIN` | Only matching rows from both tables |
| `LEFT OUTER JOIN` | All rows from left + matching from right (NULLs if no match) |
| `RIGHT OUTER JOIN` | All rows from right + matching from left |
| `FULL OUTER JOIN` | All rows from both; NULLs where no match |
| `CROSS JOIN` | Cartesian product (m ├Ś n rows) |
| `NATURAL JOIN` | Auto-joins on columns with same name |
| `SELF JOIN` | Table joined with itself |

---

## 2.6 Subqueries (Nested Queries)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Subquery Placement (KTU Focus):**
> - In `WHERE` clause ŌåÆ most common (e.g., `WHERE salary > (SELECT AVG(salary) FROM Emp)`).
> - In `FROM` clause ŌåÆ **Derived Table / Inline View**.
> - In `SELECT` clause ŌåÆ **Scalar Subquery** (must return exactly one value).
> - In `HAVING` clause ŌåÆ Filter aggregated groups.

### Correlated vs. Non-Correlated Subquery

- **Non-Correlated:** Inner query executes **once**; result passed to outer query.
- **Correlated:** Inner query references outer query's columns; executes **once per outer row** (slower, but more powerful).

### Subquery Operators

| Operator | Meaning |
|----------|---------|
| `IN` | Value matches any value in the subquery result |
| `NOT IN` | Value does not match any |
| `EXISTS` | Subquery returns at least one row |
| `NOT EXISTS` | Subquery returns zero rows |
| `ANY` / `SOME` | Comparison true for at least one value |
| `ALL` | Comparison true for all values |

---

## 2.7 Set Operations

| Operation | Requirement | Effect |
|-----------|-------------|--------|
| `UNION` | Same number/type of columns | Combines + removes duplicates |
| `UNION ALL` | Same | Combines + keeps duplicates |
| `INTERSECT` | Same | Returns common rows |
| `EXCEPT` / `MINUS` | Same | Returns rows in first but not second |

---

## 2.8 Views, Indexes, and Synonyms

### 2.8.1 Views

A **view** is a virtual table defined by a stored `SELECT` query. It does not store data physically (unless *materialized*).

- **Simple View:** Based on a single table, no aggregates, allows DML.
- **Complex View:** Joins, aggregates, groups; usually read-only.
- **`WITH CHECK OPTION`:** Prevents inserts/updates that violate the view's `WHERE` clause.
- **`CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW`:** Replaces existing view definition.

### 2.8.2 Indexes

Indexes are auxiliary data structures (typically B+ Trees) that accelerate data retrieval at the cost of additional storage and slower writes.

| Index Type | Allows Duplicates? | Allows NULLs? |
|------------|--------------------|---------------|
| Unique Index | No | Yes (in most DBMS) |
| Non-Unique Index | Yes | Yes |
| Composite Index | Depends | Yes |
| Clustered Index | Yes/No | Reorders physical storage |
| Bitmap Index | Yes | Yes (good for low-cardinality columns) |

> [!NOTE]
> **CREATE INDEX syntax:**
> `CREATE [UNIQUE] [CLUSTERED | NONCLUSTERED] INDEX <name> ON <table>(<col1> [ASC|DESC], <col2>, ...);`

### 2.8.3 Synonyms

A synonym is an alias name for a database object (table, view, sequence, procedure).
- `CREATE SYNONYM <alias> FOR <schema>.<object>;`
- Used for **data abstraction** and **location transparency** in distributed databases.

---

## 2.9 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet ŌĆö Key SQL Functions

| Category | Syntax | Returns |
|----------|--------|---------|
| String | `UPPER(s)`, `LOWER(s)` | Uppercase/lowercase string |
| String | `LENGTH(s)` / `LEN(s)` | Number of characters |
| String | `SUBSTR(s, start, len)` | Substring |
| String | `CONCAT(a, b)` / `a \vert\vert b` | Concatenated string |
| String | `TRIM(s)` | Removes leading/trailing spaces |
| String | `REPLACE(s, old, new)` | Replaces occurrences |
| Numeric | `ROUND(n, d)` | Rounded number |
| Numeric | `MOD(a, b)` / `a % b` | Remainder |
| Numeric | `CEIL(n)`, `FLOOR(n)` | Round up / down |
| Numeric | `ABS(n)` | Absolute value |
| Date | `CURRENT_DATE`, `NOW()` | Current system date/time |
| Date | `DATEDIFF(d1, d2)` | Days between |
| Date | `EXTRACT(YEAR FROM d)` | Year part |
| Conversion | `CAST(expr AS type)` | Type cast |
| NULL | `COALESCE(a, b, c, ...)` | First non-NULL value |
| NULL | `NULLIF(a, b)` | NULL if equal, else `a` |
| NULL | `IS NULL`, `IS NOT NULL` | NULL test |

> [!WARNING]
> **String Concatenation Operator varies by DBMS:** PostgreSQL uses `||`, MySQL uses `CONCAT()`, Oracle uses `||`, SQL Server uses `+`. KTU questions usually specify the DBMS ŌĆö read carefully!
