| **Event** | The data modification that fires the trigger | `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE` |
| **Condition** | Optional boolean predicate; if false, action is skipped | `WHEN (new.Salary > 0)` |
| **Action** | The procedural code to execute | A block of SQL + control statements |

### 2.3.2 Trigger Timing ŌĆö BEFORE vs AFTER

| Timing | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **BEFORE** | Action runs *before* the modification is applied to the table. | Input validation, value correction, raising errors. |
| **AFTER** | Action runs *after* the modification is successfully applied. | Auditing, logging, updating derived data, replication. |

### 2.3.3 Trigger Granularity ŌĆö ROW vs STATEMENT

| Granularity | Meaning | KTU Tip |
|---|---|---|
| **FOR EACH ROW** | Trigger fires once **per affected row**. | Use `OLD` and `NEW` row variables. |
| **FOR EACH STATEMENT** | Trigger fires **once per SQL statement**, regardless of rows affected. | Use for bulk operations or auditing. |

### 2.3.4 The OLD and NEW Pseudo-Records (Critical for KTU)

| Operation | OLD | NEW |
|---|---|---|
| **INSERT** | ŌØī Not available | Ō£ģ The row being inserted |
| **DELETE** | Ō£ģ The row being deleted | ŌØī Not available |
| **UPDATE** | Ō£ģ The row before update | Ō£ģ The row after update |

### 2.3.5 KTU High-Yield Trigger Syntax (PostgreSQL / SQL:2003 Standard)

```sql
CREATE TRIGGER trigger_name
{BEFORE | AFTER} {INSERT | UPDATE | DELETE}
ON table_name
[FOR EACH ROW]                       -- Optional; default in some DBMS
[WHEN (condition)]
EXECUTE PROCEDURE function_name();    -- or EXECUTE FUNCTION in PostgreSQL
```

In modern PostgreSQL, the trigger body is a stored **function** that returns `TRIGGER`.

### 2.3.6 Why Triggers? (Engineering Utility)

| Benefit | Real-World Use |
|---|---|
| **Centralized business rules** | Rule lives in DB, not in 10 different apps. |
| **Automatic auditing** | Log every change to a sensitive table (e.g., bank accounts). |
| **Maintaining derived data** | Auto-update a denormalized total or summary table. |
| **Enforcing complex constraints** | Rules that CHECK constraints cannot express. |
| **Cascading operations** | Auto-archive old records when deleted. |

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Triggers are powerful but can become a **debugging nightmare** if overused. They form a hidden chain of execution that is hard to trace. Use them judiciously.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield "Formula Sheet" / Cheat Sheet

This is a conceptual cheat sheet of all the **SQL syntax templates** you must memorize for KTU 2024 exams:

| Concept | Canonical SQL Template |
|---|---|
| Create View | `CREATE VIEW v AS <SELECT query> [WITH CHECK OPTION];` |
| Drop View | `DROP VIEW v [CASCADE $\vert$ RESTRICT];` |
| Updatable View Rule | Single base table, no DISTINCT/GROUP/UNION/aggregate, all NOT NULL cols included |
| Create Assertion | `CREATE ASSERTION name CHECK ( <predicate> );` |
| Drop Assertion | `DROP ASSERTION name;` |
| Create Trigger | `CREATE TRIGGER name {BEFORE $\vert$ AFTER} {INSERT $\vert$ UPDATE $\vert$ DELETE} ON table [FOR EACH ROW] [WHEN (cond)] EXECUTE procedure;` |
| Drop Trigger | `DROP TRIGGER name;` |
| OLD/NEW in INSERT | Only NEW available |
| OLD/NEW in DELETE | Only OLD available |
| OLD/NEW in UPDATE | Both OLD and NEW available |
| WHERE clause in view updatability | Mandatory: must be simple, no subquery |
| Trigger timing choice | BEFORE for validation, AFTER for logging/cascading |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility of the Entire System:** In real-world enterprise systems (banking, ERP, e-commerce), Views + Assertions + Triggers together form the **"Active Database Layer"** ŌĆö a layer that *enforces* and *enriches* business logic at the database level, decoupling it from application code. Mastering this is essential for backend engineering roles.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Creating and Using a View

**Schema (assumed for all examples):**

```sql
CREATE TABLE Employee (
    EmpID       INT PRIMARY KEY,
    EmpName     VARCHAR(50),
    Department  VARCHAR(30),
    Salary      DECIMAL(10,2)
);

CREATE TABLE Department (
    DeptID      INT PRIMARY KEY,
    DeptName    VARCHAR(30) UNIQUE,
    Location    VARCHAR(50)
);
```

**Requirement:** Create a view showing all employees in the 'CSE' department with their names and salaries only.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Write the base query (what the view should display).**

```sql
SELECT EmpName, Salary
FROM Employee
WHERE Department = 'CSE';
```

**Step 2: Wrap it in `CREATE VIEW`.**

```sql
CREATE VIEW CSE_Employees AS
    SELECT EmpName, Salary
    FROM Employee
    WHERE Department = 'CSE';
```

**Step 3: Verify by querying the view.**

```sql
SELECT * FROM CSE_Employees;
```

The DBMS will internally rewrite your query as:
```sql
SELECT EmpName, Salary
FROM Employee
WHERE Department = 'CSE';
```
This is called **view resolution** or **view merging**.

**Step 4: Apply WITH CHECK OPTION (to prevent invisible insertions).**

```sql
CREATE VIEW CSE_Employees_Safe AS
    SELECT EmpName, Salary
    FROM Employee
    WHERE Department = 'CSE'
    WITH CHECK OPTION;
```

Now, if you try:
```sql
INSERT INTO CSE_Employees_Safe VALUES ('Ravi', 40000);
```
The DBMS will **reject** this because `Ravi`'s department is implicitly `NULL`, which violates the view's `WHERE` clause.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Assertion for Multi-Table Integrity

**Requirement:** *"The total salary paid to all employees in any single department must not exceed Ōé╣10,00,000."*

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1: Identify the predicate.**

For every department $D$, the sum of salaries of employees in $D$ must satisfy:

$$
\sum_{\text{emp} \in D} \text{Salary}(\text{emp}) \leq 10,00,000
$$

**Step 2: Translate to SQL subquery.**

We need a boolean expression that is true iff **no** department violates the rule.

The negation: *There exists a department whose total salary exceeds Ōé╣10,00,000.*

We want the assertion to be TRUE, so we use `NOT EXISTS`:

```sql
CREATE ASSERTION DeptSalaryCap
CHECK (
    NOT EXISTS (
        SELECT Department
        FROM Employee
        GROUP BY Department
        HAVING SUM(Salary) > 1000000
    )
);
```

**Step 3: Verify with a violating case.**

If someone runs:
```sql
INSERT INTO Employee VALUES (101, 'Arun', 'CSE', 800000);
INSERT INTO Employee VALUES (102, 'Bala', 'CSE', 300000);
```

The total CSE salary is now Ōé╣11,00,000, which violates the assertion. The DBMS will **reject** one of these inserts to keep the assertion true.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Trigger for Automatic Auditing

**Requirement:** *"Whenever a new employee is inserted, automatically log their ID and the timestamp into an `AuditLog` table."*

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 0: Pre-create the audit log table.**

```sql
CREATE TABLE AuditLog (
    LogID       SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    EmpID       INT,
    ActionType  VARCHAR(20),
    ActionTime  TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);
```

**Step 1: Create the trigger function (in PostgreSQL syntax).**

```sql
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION log_new_employee()
RETURNS TRIGGER AS $$
BEGIN
    INSERT INTO AuditLog (EmpID, ActionType, ActionTime)
    VALUES (NEW.EmpID, 'INSERT', CURRENT_TIMESTAMP);
    RETURN NEW;   -- IMPORTANT: For AFTER triggers, return value is ignored.
                  -- For BEFORE row triggers, you MUST return NEW.
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;
```

**Step 2: Create the trigger that fires the function.**

```sql
CREATE TRIGGER trg_log_new_employee
AFTER INSERT ON Employee
FOR EACH ROW
EXECUTE FUNCTION log_new_employee();
```

**Step 3: Test the trigger.**

```sql
INSERT INTO Employee VALUES (201, 'Chitra', 'ECE', 55000);
```

**Step 4: Verify the side effect.**

```sql
SELECT * FROM AuditLog;
```

You will see an automatic entry: `EmpID = 201, ActionType = 'INSERT', ActionTime = (current timestamp)`.

### Derivation of Execution Order:

The DBMS executes the operations in this sequence:

$$
\text{User: } \text{INSERT} \rightarrow \text{Trigger fires (AFTER)} \rightarrow \text{Function executes} \rightarrow \text{Function inserts into AuditLog} \rightarrow \text{Control returns to user}
$$

The internal evaluation rule can be written as:

$$
\text{Result}(\text{INSERT}) = \text{Result}(\text{BaseInsert}) \cup \text{Result}(\text{TriggerAction})
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: BEFORE Trigger for Input Validation

**Requirement:** *"Reject any INSERT or UPDATE on Employee where Salary is negative."*

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Create the validation function.**

```sql
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION check_salary_positive()
RETURNS TRIGGER AS $$
BEGIN
    IF NEW.Salary < 0 THEN
        RAISE EXCEPTION 'Salary cannot be negative. Got: %', NEW.Salary;
    END IF;
    RETURN NEW;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;
```

**Step 2: Create the BEFORE trigger.**

```sql
CREATE TRIGGER trg_check_salary
BEFORE INSERT OR UPDATE ON Employee
FOR EACH ROW
EXECUTE FUNCTION check_salary_positive();
```

**Step 3: Attempt a violating insertion.**

```sql
INSERT INTO Employee VALUES (301, 'Deepa', 'Mech', -5000);
```

**Result:** The DBMS **raises an exception** and **rolls back** the entire transaction. The Employee table remains unchanged.

### Derivation of the Rollback Logic:

$$
\text{IF } \text{Salary}_{\text{NEW}} < 0 \Rightarrow \text{RAISE EXCEPTION} \Rightarrow \text{ROLLBACK}
$$

$$
\text{IF } \text{Salary}_{\text{NEW}} \geq 0 \Rightarrow \text{RETURN NEW} \Rightarrow \text{COMMIT}
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: When to Use What?

| Scenario | Best Tool | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Hide sensitive columns from users | **View** | Restricts column visibility, no procedural logic needed. |
| Show only rows relevant to a department | **View** | Filtering at the data-access layer. |
| Enforce a rule involving a single table column | **CHECK constraint** | Simpler and more efficient than an assertion. |
| Enforce a rule spanning multiple tables | **Assertion** (or Trigger fallback) | CHECK cannot reference other tables. |
| Auto-log every change to a sensitive table | **AFTER Trigger** | Must fire *after* the change is committed. |
| Reject invalid data before it enters the table | **BEFORE Trigger** | Can `RAISE EXCEPTION` to block the row. |
| Maintain a derived column (e.g., running total) | **Trigger** | Automatic update on each base-table change. |
| Provide a customized, simplified query interface | **View** | Encapsulates complex joins for app developers. |

---

## 3.6 Conceptual "Proof": View Resolution Algorithm

When you query a view $V$ defined as:

$$
V \triangleq \text{SELECT } c_1, c_2, \dots, c_n \text{ FROM } T \text{ WHERE } P(x_1, \dots, x_m)
$$

The DBMS performs **view merging** (also called **query modification**):

$$
Q(V) \rightarrow Q(\text{merge}(Q, \text{def}(V)))
$$

In other words, the system substitutes the view's definition back into the outer query, transforming:

$$
\sigma_P(\pi_{c_1, \dots, c_n}(T))
$$

into a single optimized relational algebra expression before execution. This is why views are *virtual* ŌĆö no data is duplicated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** If asked *"How is a view executed?"*, the correct answer is: *By query modification (or query rewriting) ŌĆö the view's defining query is merged with the user's query, and the resulting expression is optimized and executed against the base tables. No data is physically retrieved from a view.*

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture: How a View Fits in the DBMS

The following diagram illustrates how a **View** sits between the user/application and the physical base tables.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Application Layer] --> B[SQL Query against VIEW]
    B --> C{View Exists in Data Dictionary?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Retrieve View Definition]
    D --> E[Query Rewriter Merges View with Outer Query]
    E --> F[Query Optimizer Generates Execution Plan]
    F --> G[Execute Against Base Tables]
    G --> H[Return Result to User]
    C -- No --> I[Error: View not found]
```

**Explanation of Flow:**
- The user writes a query referencing the view.
- The DBMS looks up the view's stored definition in the data dictionary.
- The query rewriter **merges** the view's `SELECT` with the outer query.
- The optimizer generates a plan, executed against the base tables.
- No separate view data is stored; the result is dynamically computed.

---

## 4.2 Trigger Execution Flow ŌĆö Event-Condition-Action (ECA) Model

This is the **canonical diagram** for understanding how triggers fire. Memorize this for KTU exams.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Issues INSERT UPDATE DELETE on Table T] --> B{Trigger Defined on T?}
    B -- No --> H[Normal Execution - No Trigger]
    B -- Yes --> C[Identify Event Type and Timing BEFORE or AFTER]
    C --> D{BEFORE Trigger?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Execute Trigger Action]
    E --> E1{Condition WHEN satisfied?}
    E1 -- Yes --> E2[Apply Modification to Table T]
    E1 -- No --> E3[Skip Action - Proceed with Modification]
    D -- No --> F[AFTER Trigger Path]
    F --> F1[Apply Modification to Table T First]
    F1 --> F2[Execute Trigger Action]
    F2 --> F3{Condition WHEN satisfied?}
    F3 -- Yes --> F4[Perform Side Effect - Logging Auditing etc]
    F3 -- No --> F5[No Action Taken]
    E2 --> G[Transaction Commits]
    E3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G
    H --> G
```

**Key Observations from the Diagram:**
- **BEFORE** triggers run *before* the modification is committed.
- **AFTER** triggers run *after* the modification is committed.
- The `WHEN` condition is **optional** ŌĆö if absent, the action always executes.
- A trigger's failure rolls back the entire transaction (atomicity).

---

## 4.3 View Categories ŌĆö Classification Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SQL Views] --> B[Simple Updatable View]
    A --> C[Complex Read Only View]
    A --> D[Materialized View - Advanced]
    B --> B1[Single Base Table]
    B --> B2[No Aggregates or Joins]
    B --> B3[Supports INSERT UPDATE DELETE]
    C --> C1[Multiple Tables JOIN]
    C --> C2[Uses GROUP BY or Aggregates]
    C --> C3[Read Only SELECT only]
    D --> D1[Physically Stores Result]
    D --> D2[Needs REFRESH to Update]
    D --> D3[Not in Basic KTU Syllabus]
```

---

## 4.4 Relationship: View vs Assertion vs Trigger

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Database Objects for Active Behavior] --> B[VIEW]
    A --> C[ASSERTION]
    A --> D[TRIGGER]
    B --> B1[Purpose: Virtual Relation for Queries]
    B --> B2[Activation: On Query]
    B --> B3[Scope: Read or Write Data]
    C --> C1[Purpose: Database Wide Constraint]
    C --> C2[Activation: On Every Modification]
    C --> C3[Scope: Whole Database]
    D --> D1[Purpose: Procedural Action on Event]
    D --> D2[Activation: On INSERT UPDATE DELETE]
    D --> D3[Scope: One Table per Trigger]
```

---

## 4.5 Trigger Components ŌĆö Anatomy Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Trigger Definition] --> B[Event INSERT UPDATE DELETE]
    A --> C[Timing BEFORE AFTER]
    A --> D[Table Target]
    A --> E[Granularity FOR EACH ROW or STATEMENT]
    A --> F[Optional WHEN Condition]
    A --> G[Action - Procedural Code]
    B --> B1[What modification]
    C --> C1[When to fire]
    D --> D1[On which table]
    E --> E1[How many times]
    F --> F1[Filter rows]
    G --> G1[What to do]
```

**For Your KTU Exam:** If a question asks *"List the components of a trigger"*, the answer is exactly these six: **Event, Timing, Table, Granularity, WHEN condition, Action**.

---

## 4.6 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Active Database Layer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Application Layer Frontend] --> L2[DBMS Engine]
    L2 --> L3[Query Processor]
    L2 --> L4[Active Database Layer]
    L3 --> L3a[Parser Optimizer Executor]
    L4 --> L4a[VIEW Definitions]
    L4 --> L4b[ASSERTION Constraints]
    L4 --> L4c[TRIGGER Definitions]
    L3a --> M1[Base Tables Physical Storage]
    L4a --> M1
    L4b --> M1
    L4c --> M1
    M1 --> R1[Transaction Commit or Rollback]
    R1 --> L1
```

**Explanation:** This shows the modern DBMS architecture. The **Active Database Layer** is where Views, Assertions, and Triggers live ŌĆö they all ultimately interact with the base tables to enforce integrity and customize behavior.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Q: Define a view in SQL. List any two advantages of using views.**

**Model Answer:**

A **view** is a virtual table that is defined by a query stored in the database's data dictionary. It does not contain any physical data of its own; instead, its rows and columns are produced dynamically each time the view is queried by executing its defining `SELECT` statement against the underlying base tables.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Data Security:** Views allow hiding sensitive columns (e.g., `Salary`, `Password`) from unauthorized users while still providing access to non-sensitive columns.
2. **Query Simplification:** Complex multi-table joins can be encapsulated inside a view, allowing application developers to write simple `SELECT * FROM ViewName` queries.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024, Series-1]
**Q: Differentiate between a BEFORE trigger and an AFTER trigger. Give one example use case for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | BEFORE Trigger | AFTER Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| **Execution Time** | Fires *before* the modification is applied to the table | Fires *after* the modification is applied |
| **Can modify the new row?** | Yes, can change `NEW` column values before insert | No, the row is already committed |
| **Can block the operation?** | Yes, by raising an exception | No, the base change is already done |
| **Common Use Case** | Input validation (e.g., reject negative salary) | Auditing and logging (e.g., record who changed a row) |

**Example Use Case 1 (BEFORE):** Reject any `INSERT` into `Employee` where `Salary < 0` ŌĆö raise an exception to block the insertion.

**Example Use Case 2 (AFTER):** After every `UPDATE` on `AccountBalance` table, automatically insert a log entry into `TransactionLog` for auditing.

---

## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** Consider the following schema:
```
Employee(EmpID, EmpName, Department, Salary)
Department(DeptID, DeptName, Location)
```
Write SQL statements to:
1. Create a view `CSE_Salaries` that shows EmpName and Salary of all employees in the 'CSE' department. The view should reject any insert/update that would make a row invisible to the view.
2. Insert a new row through the view and explain what happens if you try to insert a row with department 'ECE'.
3. Drop the view.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is an assertion in SQL? Write an SQL assertion to enforce: *"The total salary of all employees in the 'CSE' department must not exceed Ōé╣5,00,000."* Discuss why assertions are not supported in MySQL. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) ŌĆö Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1: Create the view with WITH CHECK OPTION.**
```sql
CREATE VIEW CSE_Salaries AS
    SELECT EmpName, Salary
    FROM Employee
    WHERE Department = 'CSE'
    WITH CHECK OPTION;
```
The `WITH CHECK OPTION` clause ensures that any `INSERT` or `UPDATE` violating the `WHERE Department = 'CSE'` condition is **rejected** by the DBMS.

**Step 2: Insert a valid row through the view.**
```sql
INSERT INTO CSE_Salaries (EmpName, Salary) VALUES ('Anand', 60000);
```
**Explanation:** The DBMS will translate this into:
```sql
INSERT INTO Employee (EmpName, Department, Salary)
VALUES ('Anand', 'CSE', 60000);
```
The `Department` column automatically receives the value `'CSE'` (the value that made the row visible to the view).

**Step 3: Try to insert a row that violates the view.**
```sql
INSERT INTO CSE_Salaries (EmpName, Salary) VALUES ('Bhavna', 45000);
```
If we had specified `Department = 'ECE'` explicitly (which is not possible since the view doesn't expose that column), the `WITH CHECK OPTION` would reject it. Since the view **does not include** the `Department` column, the DBMS will either:
- Set `Department` to its default value (if any), or
- Set `Department` to `NULL`, or
- Reject the insert (depending on the DBMS and `NOT NULL` constraints).

**Step 4: Drop the view.**
```sql
DROP VIEW CSE_Salaries;
```

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- Correct `CREATE VIEW` syntax with `WITH CHECK OPTION`: **2 marks**
- Correct insert explanation with view merging: **3 marks**
- Correct `DROP VIEW` syntax: **1 mark**
- Explanation of rejection: **1 mark**

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Definition of Assertion:**
An assertion is a named SQL constraint that specifies a condition that the entire database must always satisfy. Unlike a `CHECK` constraint (which is local to a single table), an assertion can reference multiple tables. If any data modification causes the assertion to evaluate to false, the modification is rejected and the transaction is rolled back.

**SQL Assertion for the Given Rule:**
```sql
CREATE ASSERTION CSE_Salary_Cap
CHECK (
    (SELECT SUM(Salary) FROM Employee WHERE Department = 'CSE') <= 500000
);
```

**Why MySQL Does Not Support Assertions:**

1. **Performance Overhead:** Assertions must be evaluated **after every single modification** to the database. For high-throughput systems, this is prohibitively expensive.
2. **Standard vs Implementation Gap:** The `CREATE ASSERTION` syntax is part of the **ANSI/ISO SQL standard**, but it is rarely implemented in commercial DBMS. MySQL, Oracle, and SQL Server all skip it.
3. **Alternative Solutions:** In MySQL, the same effect is achieved using:
   - `CHECK` constraints (for single-table rules), or
   - `TRIGGERS` (BEFORE INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE) with manual checks, or
   - Application-level validation in the business logic layer.

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- Correct definition: **2 marks**
- Correct `CREATE ASSERTION` SQL: **3 marks**
- Discussion of MySQL non-support with valid reasons: **2 marks**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024, Model]

**(a)** Explain the **Event-Condition-Action (ECA) model** of triggers with a neat diagram. List the components of a trigger. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the schema:
```
Library(BookID, Title, Author, CopiesAvailable)
BorrowLog(LogID, BookID, MemberID, BorrowDate, ReturnDate)
```
Write a trigger (using PostgreSQL/SQL standard syntax) that automatically **updates `CopiesAvailable`** in the `Library` table **after** a new row is inserted into `BorrowLog` (i.e., when a book is borrowed, decrease copies by 1; ensure it never goes below 0). Show the trigger function and trigger creation. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) ŌĆö ECA Model Explanation:**

The **Event-Condition-Action (ECA) model** is the standard framework for describing trigger behavior in active databases.

| Component | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Event** | A specific data modification operation that activates the trigger | `INSERT ON BorrowLog` |
| **Condition** | An optional boolean predicate; if false, the action is skipped | `WHEN (NEW.CopiesAvailable > 0)` |
| **Action** | The procedural code (SQL + control flow) to execute | `UPDATE Library SET ...` |

**Execution Flow:**
1. The user issues a modification (e.g., `INSERT`).
2. The DBMS checks if any trigger is defined for that event on that table.
3. If yes, it evaluates the trigger's `WHEN` condition (if present).
4. If the condition is true, it executes the trigger's `ACTION` body.
5. The transaction is then committed (or rolled back if the action raised an exception).

**Components of a Trigger (Six Total):**
1. **Event** (INSERT / UPDATE / DELETE)
2. **Timing** (BEFORE / AFTER)
3. **Table** (the target relation)
4. **Granularity** (FOR EACH ROW / FOR EACH STATEMENT)
5. **WHEN condition** (optional predicate)
6. **Action** (procedural SQL code)

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- ECA model explanation with example: **3 marks**
- Diagram of ECA flow: **2 marks**
- List of all 6 components: **2 marks**

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö Trigger Implementation:**

**Step 1: Create the trigger function.**

```sql
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION update_copies_on_borrow()
RETURNS TRIGGER AS $$
DECLARE
    available INT;
BEGIN
    -- Fetch current available copies
    SELECT CopiesAvailable INTO available
    FROM Library
    WHERE BookID = NEW.BookID
    FOR UPDATE;   -- Lock the row to prevent race conditions

    -- Check if copies are available
    IF available <= 0 THEN
        RAISE EXCEPTION 'No copies available for BookID: %', NEW.BookID;
    END IF;

    -- Decrement the available count
    UPDATE Library
    SET CopiesAvailable = CopiesAvailable - 1
    WHERE BookID = NEW.BookID;

    RETURN NEW;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;
```

**Step 2: Create the AFTER trigger.**

```sql
CREATE TRIGGER trg_update_copies
AFTER INSERT ON BorrowLog
FOR EACH ROW
EXECUTE FUNCTION update_copies_on_borrow();
```

**Step 3: Testing the trigger.**

```sql
-- Test 1: Valid borrow
INSERT INTO BorrowLog (LogID, BookID, MemberID, BorrowDate)
VALUES (1, 101, 5, CURRENT_DATE);
-- Result: Library.CopiesAvailable for BookID 101 decreases by 1.

-- Test 2: Borrow when no copies available
INSERT INTO BorrowLog (LogID, BookID, MemberID, BorrowDate)
VALUES (2, 999, 5, CURRENT_DATE);
-- Result: Exception raised, transaction rolled back.
```

**Why we used `FOR UPDATE`:**
This locks the `Library` row to prevent a **race condition** where two simultaneous borrow operations might both see `CopiesAvailable = 1` and both decrement it, resulting in `-1`.

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- Correct trigger function with logic: **3 marks**
- Correct trigger creation syntax: **2 marks**
- Use of `FOR UPDATE` and `RAISE EXCEPTION`: **2 marks**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting `WITH CHECK OPTION`:** If a question says "prevent rows from disappearing after insert/update through the view", and you forget `WITH CHECK OPTION`, you lose **at least 2 marks**.
>
> 2. **Confusing `OLD` and `NEW`:** In a `DELETE` trigger, only `OLD` exists. In an `INSERT` trigger, only `NEW` exists. In an `UPDATE` trigger, both exist. Using the wrong one is an instant **error**.
>
> 3. **Forgetting `RETURN NEW` in BEFORE triggers:** In row-level BEFORE triggers (in PostgreSQL), you **must** return `NEW` at the end of the function, or the trigger silently fails. AFTER triggers do not require a return value.
>
> 4. **Saying "Assertions are widely used":** They are not. Always mention that `CREATE ASSERTION` is part of the SQL standard but **not implemented** in MySQL, Oracle, or SQL Server. Examiners award marks for this nuance.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to drop or restore state:** When writing triggers in exams, always mention **when** the trigger fires (BEFORE/AFTER) and **what** the side effect is. A trigger without context loses marks.
>
> 6. **Using `|` pipes in markdown tables:** In your answer sheets (especially if digitally formatted), never use the unescaped pipe `|` inside SQL conditions inside table cells ŌĆö it breaks the table layout. Use `OR` or rephrase.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist ŌĆö Views, Assertions, Triggers**

### ­¤ö╣ Views
- Ō£ģ A view is a **virtual table** ŌĆö no data is stored physically.
- Ō£ģ Defined using `CREATE VIEW v AS <SELECT query>`.
- Ō£ģ Supports `WITH CHECK OPTION` to prevent invisible modifications.
- Ō£ģ **Updatable** only if: single base table, no `DISTINCT`/`GROUP BY`/`UNION`/aggregates.
- Ō£ģ Operations allowed on views: `SELECT` (always), `INSERT`/`UPDATE`/`DELETE` (only if updatable).
- Ō£ģ Advantages: **security, simplicity, logical data independence, customized access**.
- Ō£ģ View resolution is done via **query modification (merging)** at execution time.

### ­¤ö╣ Assertions
- Ō£ģ A **database-wide constraint** that must always be true.
- Ō£ģ Syntax: `CREATE ASSERTION name CHECK ( <predicate> );`
- Ō£ģ Can reference **multiple tables** (unlike `CHECK`).
- Ō£ģ Any modification violating the assertion is **rejected** (transaction rolled back).
- Ō£ģ Part of the **ANSI/ISO SQL standard** but **rarely implemented** (MySQL/Oracle/SQL Server do not support it).
- Ō£ģ Use `NOT EXISTS` patterns for negative constraints.

### ­¤ö╣ Triggers
- Ō£ģ Follow the **ECA model**: **Event, Condition, Action**.
- Ō£ģ Six components: **Event, Timing, Table, Granularity, WHEN, Action**.
- Ō£ģ Timing: **BEFORE** (for validation/correction) vs **AFTER** (for logging/auditing).
- Ō£ģ Granularity: **FOR EACH ROW** (uses `OLD`/`NEW`) vs **FOR EACH STATEMENT**.
- Ō£ģ `OLD`/`NEW` availability: `INSERT`ŌåÆNEW only, `DELETE`ŌåÆOLD only, `UPDATE`ŌåÆboth.
- Ō£ģ Uses: **auditing, derived data, business rules, input validation, cascading actions**.
- Ō£ģ Modern PostgreSQL: trigger body is a **stored function** returning `TRIGGER`.
- Ō£ģ Caution: triggers can be a **debugging nightmare**; use sparingly.

### ­¤ö╣ Master Comparison Mnemonic
> **"V-A-T ŌĆö View, Assertion, Trigger"**
> - **V** = Virtual (no data stored)
> - **A** = Always-true (database-wide constraint)
> - **T** = Triggered-by-event (auto-executed procedure)

### ­¤ö╣ Key SQL Keywords to Memorize
`CREATE VIEW`, `DROP VIEW`, `WITH CHECK OPTION`, `CREATE ASSERTION`, `DROP ASSERTION`, `CREATE TRIGGER`, `DROP TRIGGER`, `BEFORE`, `AFTER`, `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE`, `FOR EACH ROW`, `FOR EACH STATEMENT`, `WHEN`, `OLD`, `NEW`, `RAISE EXCEPTION`, `RETURNS TRIGGER`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SQL Data Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBEOT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** SQL Data Types # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In Structured Query Language (SQL), a **Data Type** is a classification that specifies the *type of value* an attribute (column) can hold, the *range of permissible values*, the *storage size* in bytes, and the *set of valid operations* that can be performed on those values. Data types are enforced by the Data Definition Language (DDL) at schema creation time and guarantee *data integrity*, *storage efficiency*, and *domain-level validation* within a relational database management system (RDBMS). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 2 (SQL ŌĆō Basic Retrieval Queries and DDL), data types are categorized as **Predefined (Built-in)** and **User-Defined (UDT)** types. The KTU Board examination primarily tests the **ANSI/ISO SQL standard types** along with their *vendor-specific representations* in **MySQL / Oracle / PostgreSQL**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆō Plain English Intuition Imagine you are filling out an official government form. The form has specific boxes: one for your **Name** (you write text), one for your **Date of Birth** (you write a date), one for your **Mobile Number** (digits only), and one for your **Salary** (a number, often with decimals). The form *refuses* to accept alphabets in the mobile number field, and refuses to accept a date in the salary field. **SQL Data Types work exactly like this.** They are *strict* rules the database enforces to ensure every column only contains the *kind of value* it is meant to store. Just as you cannot stuff a watermelon into a tea-cup, you cannot store a 500-character paragraph into a 10-character name field. Data types define the *shape* and *size* of the cup so the data fits perfectly. ## 1.3 Why Data Types Matter (The "Big Four" Reasons) | Reason | Plain English Explanation | |---|---| | **Data Integrity** | Prevents garbage data (e.g., "twenty" stored in an `age` column). | | **Storage Efficiency** | Right-sizing columns saves disk space (e.g., `TINYINT` vs `BIGINT`). | | **Query Performance** | Indexes work best on properly typed columns; comparisons are faster. | | **Operation Safety** | The DBMS knows which operators (`, -, /, CONCAT, +') are valid on which data. | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always mention the *three properties* of a data type in 2-mark definition questions: **Domain (set of values), Storage Size (bytes), and Valid Operations (operators).** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Master Classification of SQL Data Types (KTU High-Yield) The KTU 2024 syllabus requires mastering **four primary families** of SQL data types. The following is a Board-exam-ready breakdown. ### Family 1 ŌĆö Numeric Data Types Used to store integers and floating-point numbers. SQL distinguishes between *exact* numerics and *approximate* numerics. - **Exact Numerics** store values with no rounding error, ideal for money. - `INTEGER` / `INT` ŌĆō Whole numbers. - `SMALLINT` ŌĆō Smaller whole numbers. - `BIGINT` ŌĆō Very large whole numbers. - `DECIMAL(p, s)` / `NUMERIC(p, s)` ŌĆō Fixed-point with precision `p` and scale `s`. - `BIT` ŌĆō Boolean bit (0 or 1). - **Approximate Numerics** store floating-point values, may have tiny rounding error. - `FLOAT` ŌĆō Single-precision floating point. - `REAL` ŌĆō Single-precision. - `DOUBLE PRECISION` ŌĆō Double-precision (more decimal places). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters:** When storing currency (Ōé╣, $, Ōé¼), always use `DECIMAL(10,2)` and **NEVER** `FLOAT`. Floats introduce binary rounding errors that violate financial accuracy. ### Family 2 ŌĆö Character / String Data Types Used to store alphanumeric text. The difference is *fixed-length* vs *variable-length*. - `CHAR(n)` ŌĆō Fixed-length string. Always occupies `n` characters; padded with spaces. - `VARCHAR(n)` ŌĆō Variable-length string. Uses only as much storage as the actual data + 1 length byte. - `TEXT` / `CLOB` (Character Large Object) ŌĆō For very long documents, articles, descriptions. ### Family 3 ŌĆö Date and Time Data Types - `DATE` ŌĆō Stores year, month, day (e.g., `2025-11-04`). - `TIME` ŌĆō Stores hour, minute, second. - `DATETIME` ŌĆō Combines date + time. - `TIMESTAMP` ŌĆō Date + time with timezone awareness; auto-updates in MySQL. - `YEAR` ŌĆō Stores a 4-digit year. - `INTERVAL` ŌĆō Represents a duration (e.g., `3 days`, `2 hours`). ### Family 4 ŌĆö Boolean, Binary, and Miscellaneous - `BOOLEAN` / `BOOL` ŌĆō Stores `TRUE`, `FALSE`, or `UNKNOWN` (NULL). - `BINARY(n)` / `VARBINARY(n)` ŌĆō Stores raw binary bytes (e.g., hashed passwords). - `BLOB` ŌĆō Binary Large Object for images, audio, video files. - `ENUM` ŌĆō Predefined list of allowed string values. - `JSON` / `XML` ŌĆō Semi-structured data storage (modern SQL:2016+ feature). - `UUID` ŌĆō 128-bit Universal Unique Identifier. ## 2.2 The "How and Why" Behind Each Family - **Why CHAR vs VARCHAR?** `CHAR` is faster for fixed data (e.g., country codes `IND`, `USA`) because no length byte is needed. `VARCHAR` saves space for unpredictable lengths (e.g., names, addresses). - **Why DECIMAL(p,s)?** The parameter `p` is total digits, `s` is digits after the decimal point. `DECIMAL(7,2)` means up to `99999.99`. This is the **Gold Standard** for monetary columns. - **Why TIMESTAMP over DATETIME?** `TIMESTAMP` is stored as UTC internally and converted to the session's timezone. It is *time-zone aware*, which `DATETIME` is not. - **Why BOOLEAN is just a BIT?** In strict ANSI SQL, `BOOLEAN` is internally `BIT(1)`. MySQL uses `TINYINT(1)`; PostgreSQL has a full native `BOOLEAN` type. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Quick Reference Table > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** The table below uses `\vert` and `\mid` instead of the vertical pipe `|` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. | Data Type Family | SQL Type | Storage Size | Range / Format | Example Value | |---|---|---|---|---| | Exact Numeric | `SMALLINT` | **2 bytes** | $-32{,}768$ to $32{,}767$ | `4500` | | Exact Numeric | `INT \vert INTEGER` | **4 bytes** | $-2.147 \times 10^9$ to $2.147 \times 10^9$ | `2147483647` | | Exact Numeric | `BIGINT` | **8 bytes** | $-9.22 \times 10^{18}$ to $9.22 \times 10^{18}$ | `9876543210` | | Fixed-Point | `DECIMAL(p,s)` | Varies (max $p+1$ bytes) | $-10^{p-1}+1$ to $10^{p-1}-1$ with $s$ decimals | `DECIMAL(8,2) \to 999999.99` | | Approximate | `FLOAT` | **4 bytes** | $\pm 3.4 \times 10^{\pm 38}$ (Ōēł 7 digits) | `3.14159` | | Approximate | `DOUBLE` | **8 bytes** | $\pm 1.7 \times 10^{\pm 308}$ (Ōēł 15 digits) | `2.7182818284` | | Character | `CHAR(n)` | Exactly $n$ bytes | Up to $n$ characters | `'Kerala'` | | Character | `VARCHAR(n)` | $L+1$ bytes ($L$ = actual length) | Up to $n$ characters | `'KTU'` | | Date/Time | `DATE` | **3 bytes** | `YYYY-MM-DD` | `'2025-11-04'` | | Date/Time | `TIME` | **3 bytes** | `HH:MM:SS` | `'14:30:00'` | | Date/Time | `DATETIME` | **8 bytes** | `YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS` | `'2025-11-04 14:30:00'` | | Date/Time | `TIMESTAMP` | **4 bytes** | `1970-01-01` to `2038-01-19` UTC | `CURRENT_TIMESTAMP` | | Boolean | `BOOLEAN` | **1 bit / 1 byte** | `TRUE \mid FALSE \mid NULL` | `TRUE` | | Binary | `BLOB` | Up to $2^{32}-1$ bytes | Image / audio / video | `0xFFD8FFE0` | ## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility | Field | Recommended Data Type | Reason | |---|---|---| | Aadhaar / SSN Number | `CHAR(12)` or `VARCHAR(12)` | Fixed format; never computed as number. | | Bank Account Balance | `DECIMAL(15,2)` | Exact arithmetic, no float error. | | GPS Coordinates | `DECIMAL(9,6)` / `DECIMAL(10,6)` | Precision critical for location. | | User Password Hash | `VARCHAR(255)` or `BINARY(32)` | Stores SHA-256 hex output. | | Created/Updated Time | `TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP` | Audit trail, auto-tracks changes. | | Profile Photo | `BLOB` or external URL as `VARCHAR(2083)` | Binary large object storage. | # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples ## 3.1 Mathematical Derivation: Storage Size of DECIMAL(p, s) The KTU Board frequently asks: *"What is the storage size of DECIMAL(7, 2)?"* Here is the exhaustive step-by-step derivation. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: Identify precision } p \text{ and scale } s. \\ p &= 7 \quad \text{(total number of significant digits)} \\ s &= 2 \quad \text{(digits after the decimal point)} \\ \\ \text{Step 2: Compute integer digits } d_{\text{int}}. \\ d_{\text{int}} &= p - s \\ d_{\text{int}} &= 7 - 2 = 5 \text{ digits} \\ \\ \text{Step 3: Compute SQL standard byte allocation.} \\ \text{Bytes}(p) &= \lfloor (p \div 9) \rfloor \times 4 + \lceil (p \bmod 9) \div 2 \rceil + 1 \\ \\ \text{For } p = 7: \\ \text{Bytes} &= \lfloor 7 \div 9 \rfloor \times 4 + \lceil 7 \bmod 9 \rceil \div 2 \rceil + 1 \\ \text{Bytes} &= 0 \times 4 + \lceil 7 \div 2 \rceil + 1 \\ \text{Bytes} &= 0 + 4 + 1 = 5 \text{ bytes} \\ \\ \text{Step 4: Identify maximum value stored.} \\ \text{MaxValue} &= 10^{p-1} - 10^{-s} \\ \text{MaxValue} &= 10^{6} - 0.01 \\ \text{MaxValue} &= 999999.99 \\ \\ \text{Step 5: Interpretation.} \\ \text{The column } \texttt{DECIMAL(7,2)} \text{ can hold values from } -99999.99 \text{ to } +99999.99. \\ \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why use the formula?** The first 9 digits take 4 bytes, the next 9 take 4 more, with extra bytes for the sign and odd digits. For `DECIMAL(7,2)`, 5 integer digits remain within the first 9-digit group, so we need $\lfloor 7/9 \rfloor \times 4 = 0$ full groups, plus $\lceil 7/2 \rceil = 4$ bytes for the partial group, plus 1 byte for sign storage, totaling **5 bytes**. ## 3.2 Worked Example: Range Calculation of `TINYINT`, `SMALLINT`, `INT`, `BIGINT` The general formula for signed $n$-byte integer range is: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Min Value} &= -2^{(8n - 1)} \\ \text{Max Value} &= \;\;2^{(8n - 1)} - 1 \\ \text{Total Values} &= 2^{8n} \\ \end{aligned} $$ Now applying this to each SQL integer type: $$ \begin{aligned} \textbf{1. TINYINT } (n=1 \text{ byte}): \\ \text{Min} &= -2^{7} = -128 \\ \text{Max} &= 2^{7} - 1 = 127 \\ \text{Total} &= 2^{8} = 256 \text{ distinct values} \\ \\ \textbf{2. SMALLINT } (n=2 \text{ bytes}): \\ \text{Min} &= -2^{15} = -32{,}768 \\ \text{Max} &= 2^{15} - 1 = 32{,}767 \\ \text{Total} &= 2^{16} = 65{,}536 \text{ values} \\ \\ \textbf{3. INT } (n=4 \text{ bytes}): \\ \text{Min} &= -2^{31} = -2{,}147{,}483{,}648 \\ \text{Max} &= 2^{31} - 1 = 2{,}147{,}483{,}647 \\ \text{Total} &= 2^{32} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296 \text{ values} \\ \\ \textbf{4. BIGINT } (n=8 \text{ bytes}): \\ \text{Min} &= -2^{63} = -9{,}223{,}372{,}036{,}854{,}775{,}808 \\ \text{Max} &= 2^{63} - 1 = 9{,}223{,}372{,}036{,}854{,}775{,}807 \\ \text{Total} &= 2^{64} \approx 1.844 \times 10^{19} \text{ values} \\ \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.3 Worked Example: Storing Kerala University Student Records Below is a complete DDL example using all four families of data types ŌĆö a common KTU Part B (14-mark) question pattern. ```sql CREATE TABLE Student_KTU ( reg_no INT NOT NULL, roll_number CHAR(8) NOT NULL, -- Fixed: e.g., 'TVE21CS001' full_name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL, date_of_birth DATE, cgpa DECIMAL(4,2), -- 0.00 to 10.00 fees_paid DECIMAL(10,2), -- Up to 99,999,999.99 gender ENUM('M','F','Other'), is_hosteller BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE, biometric BLOB, -- Fingerprint scan created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP, CONSTRAINT pk_student PRIMARY KEY (reg_no) ); ``` ### Validation Logic ŌĆö Why each data type was chosen: $$ \begin{aligned} \textbf{reg\_no} &= \texttt{INT} \quad \text{because roll numbers are bounded by dept strength} < 2^{31} \\ \textbf{roll\_number} &= \texttt{CHAR(8)} \quad \text{fixed format: 3 letters + 2 digits + 3 digits} \\ \textbf{full\_name} &= \texttt{VARCHAR(100)} \quad \text{length varies; 100 covers most names} \\ \textbf{date\_of\_birth} &= \texttt{DATE} \quad \text{no time component needed} \\ \textbf{cgpa} &= \texttt{DECIMAL(4,2)} \quad \text{range } 0.00 \text{ to } 10.00 \text{ (4 digits, 2 after decimal)} \\ \textbf{fees\_paid} &= \texttt{DECIMAL(10,2)} \quad \text{8 integer digits + 2 decimal; exact money} \\ \textbf{gender} &= \texttt{ENUM} \quad \text{only M / F / Other permitted} \\ \textbf{is\_hosteller} &= \texttt{BOOLEAN} \quad \text{TRUE if hostel resident} \\ \textbf{biometric} &= \texttt{BLOB} \quad \text{binary fingerprint template} \\ \textbf{created\_at} &= \texttt{TIMESTAMP} \quad \text{audit-trail; auto-set on INSERT} \\ \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.4 Comparison Matrix: CHAR vs VARCHAR vs TEXT | Property | `CHAR(n)` | `VARCHAR(n)` | `TEXT` | |---|---|---|---| | Storage | Always $n$ bytes | $L+1$ bytes (L = actual length) | $L+2$ bytes | | Maximum Length | 255 bytes | 65,535 bytes (MySQL row limit) | 65,535 bytes (MySQL) / unlimited (PG) | | Padding | Right-padded with spaces | No padding | No padding | | Trailing Spaces | Truncated on retrieval | Preserved | Preserved | | Indexing | Fully indexable | Fully indexable | **Requires prefix index** in MySQL | | Best Use Case | Fixed codes (Country, Gender, Status) | Names, Emails, Addresses | Articles, Comments, JSON Docs | | Performance | Slightly faster | Slightly slower | Slowest | > [!TIP] > **KTU Hack:** Use `CHAR` for columns where all values are *roughly the same length* (PIN code, gender code, state code). Use `VARCHAR` for everything else. Use `TEXT` only when length may exceed **255 characters**. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Taxonomy of SQL Data Types ```mermaid graph TD A["SQL DATA TYPES"] --> B["PREDEFINED BUILT-IN TYPES"] A --> C["USER DEFINED TYPES UDT"] B --> D["NUMERIC"] B --> E["CHARACTER STRING"] B --> F["DATE AND TIME"] B --> G["BOOLEAN"] B --> H["BINARY"] B --> I["MISC SPECIAL TYPES"] D --> D1["EXACT NUMERIC
INT SMALLINT BIGINT DECIMAL NUMERIC BIT"] D --> D2["APPROXIMATE NUMERIC
FLOAT REAL DOUBLE PRECISION"] E --> E1["FIXED LENGTH
CHAR N"] E --> E2["VARIABLE LENGTH
VARCHAR N TEXT CLOB"] F --> F1["DATE"] F --> F2["TIME"] F --> F3["DATETIME"] F --> F4["TIMESTAMP"] F --> F5["INTERVAL YEAR"] H --> H1["BINARY VARBINARY"] H --> H2["BLOB LARGE OBJECT"] I --> I1["ENUM SET"] I --> I2["JSON XML"] I --> I3["UUID GEOMETRY"] C --> J1["DISTINCT TYPE"] C --> J2["STRUCTURED TYPE"] C --> J3["REFERENCE TYPE"] ``` ## 4.2 Storage Size Decision Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["CHOOSING THE RIGHT SQL DATA TYPE"] --> Q1{"IS THE VALUE A NUMBER"} Q1 -- YES --> Q2{"DO YOU NEED EXACT ARITHMETIC LIKE MONEY"} Q1 -- NO --> Q3{"IS THE VALUE TEXT"} Q2 -- YES --> Q4["USE DECIMAL p s"] Q2 -- NO --> Q5{"HOW BIG IS THE INTEGER"} Q5 --> S1["TINYINT 1 BYTE 0 TO 255"] Q5 --> S2["SMALLINT 2 BYTES 32K"] Q5 --> S3["INT 4 BYTES 2 BILLION"] Q5 --> S4["BIGINT 8 BYTES 9 QUINTILLION"] Q3 -- YES --> Q6{"IS THE LENGTH FIXED"} Q6 -- YES --> C1["CHAR N"] Q6 -- NO --> Q7{"IS LENGTH UNDER 255"} Q7 -- YES --> C2["VARCHAR N"] Q7 -- NO --> C3["TEXT OR CLOB"] Q1 -- NO --> T1{"IS IT A DATE OR TIME"} T1 -- YES --> T2["DATE TIME DATETIME TIMESTAMP"] T1 -- NO --> T3{"IS IT TRUE FALSE"} T3 -- YES --> T4["BOOLEAN"] T3 -- NO --> T5["USE ENUM JSON BLOB UUID"] ``` ## 4.3 Type-Casting Compatibility Map (MySQL Reference) ```mermaid graph LR A["INT"] -- IMPLICIT --> B["BIGINT"] A -- IMPLICIT --> C["DECIMAL"] A -- IMPLICIT --> D["FLOAT"] A -- IMPLICIT --> E["DOUBLE"] A -- IMPLICIT --> F["CHAR"] G["DATE"] -- IMPLICIT --> H["DATETIME"] G -- IMPLICIT --> I["TIMESTAMP"] G -- IMPLICIT --> J["CHAR"] K["CHAR N"] -- EXPLICIT CAST --> A K -- EXPLICIT CAST --> G K -- EXPLICIT CAST --> L["BOOLEAN"] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the map:** Arrows indicate *implicit conversion* (auto-cast by the DBMS). MySQL will silently promote `INT` to `BIGINT` or `FLOAT` automatically. However, casting a `CHAR` to an `INT` requires *explicit* conversion using `CAST()` or `CONVERT()` functions ŌĆö a frequent **2-mark KTU question**. ## 4.4 ASCII Block Diagram: A Row's Physical Storage Layout ``` +-----------------------------------------------------------+ | PHYSICAL ROW LAYOUT IN MySQL InnoDB (InnoDB Row Format) | +-----------------------------------------------------------+ | Header | Transaction ID | Roll Pointer | Column Count | .. | +-----------------------------------------------------------+ | Column 1 | Column 2 | Column 3 | Column 4 | | reg_no | full_name | cgpa | created_at | | INT (4 B) | VARCHAR(100) | DECIMAL(4,2)| TIMESTAMP | | | = 5+1 bytes | = 3 bytes | = 4 bytes | +-----------------------------------------------------------+ | NULL Bitmap | Offsets to Variable-Length Columns | +-----------------------------------------------------------+ ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]** *Explain the difference between `CHAR` and `VARCHAR` data types with a suitable example.* **Model Answer (Examiner's Key Pattern):** `CHAR(n)` is a *fixed-length* string data type that always reserves exactly `n` bytes of storage, padding the value with trailing spaces if it is shorter than `n`. Conversely, `VARCHAR(n)` is a *variable-length* string that uses only as many bytes as the actual data, plus 1 byte to store the length. For example, `CHAR(10)` storing the value `'KTU'` will occupy 10 bytes (7 padding spaces), whereas `VARCHAR(10)` storing `'KTU'` occupies only 4 bytes (3 chars + 1 length byte). `CHAR` is faster for fixed-format data such as country codes, while `VARCHAR` saves storage for variable-length data such as names. ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Repeated Pattern]** *What is the difference between `DATETIME` and `TIMESTAMP` in SQL?* **Model Answer:** Both `DATETIME` and `TIMESTAMP` store date and time values, but they differ significantly. `DATETIME` has a range from `'1000-01-01 00:00:00'` to `'9999-12-31 23:59:59'`, occupies 8 bytes, and is *not affected by the timezone setting* of the server. `TIMESTAMP` has a smaller range from `'1970-01-01 00:00:01'` UTC to `'2038-01-19 03:14:07'` UTC (the *Year 2038 Problem*), occupies 4 bytes, is stored internally as UTC, and is *converted to the session's timezone* upon retrieval. In MySQL, `TIMESTAMP` also supports the `DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP` clause for automatic auditing. ## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Full 14-Mark Question (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A ŌĆö 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]** **(a)** Describe the major categories of SQL data types with examples. State the storage size and range for each. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Consider the schema for a banking application given below. Identify the *most appropriate* SQL data type for each attribute, justifying your choice with at least one reason per attribute. *(7 marks)* ```sql TABLE: Account - account_number : ? - account_holder_name: ? - opening_date : ? - balance : ? - account_type : (Savings / Current / FD / RD) - is_active : ? - last_transaction : ? - signature_image : ? ``` **Model Solution (Part a):** SQL data types are classified into four major families: **(1) Numeric Data Types** ŌĆö used for storing numbers. - `TINYINT` (1 byte, range: -128 to 127) - `SMALLINT` (2 bytes, range: -32,768 to 32,767) - `INT` (4 bytes, range: -2,147,483,648 to 2,147,483,647) - `BIGINT` (8 bytes, range: $\pm 9.22 \times 10^{18}$) - `DECIMAL(p,s)` (exact fixed-point; `p` total digits, `s` after decimal) - `FLOAT`, `DOUBLE` (approximate floating-point) **(2) Character / String Data Types** ŌĆö used for storing text. - `CHAR(n)` (fixed length, padded with spaces) - `VARCHAR(n)` (variable length, +1 byte for length) - `TEXT` / `CLOB` (very long text) **(3) Date and Time Data Types** ŌĆö used for temporal values. - `DATE` (YYYY-MM-DD), `TIME` (HH:MM:SS) - `DATETIME` (8 bytes, no timezone) - `TIMESTAMP` (4 bytes, UTC-based, supports auto-update) **(4) Boolean, Binary, and Special Types** - `BOOLEAN` (TRUE / FALSE / UNKNOWN) - `BINARY`, `VARBINARY`, `BLOB` (binary data) - `ENUM`, `JSON`, `UUID` **Model Solution (Part b):** | Attribute | Chosen Type | Justification | |---|---|---| | `account_number` | `CHAR(12)` or `VARCHAR(16)` | Fixed length; alphanumeric; not used in arithmetic. | | `account_holder_name` | `VARCHAR(100)` | Variable length; supports full name with spaces. | | `opening_date` | `DATE` | Only date needed, no time. | | `balance` | `DECIMAL(15,2)` | Exact arithmetic mandatory for money; avoids float errors. | | `account_type` | `ENUM('Savings','Current','FD','RD')` | Predefined set; prevents invalid entries. | | `is_active` | `BOOLEAN` | Binary state: TRUE / FALSE. | | `last_transaction` | `TIMESTAMP` | Auto-updates on row change; used for audit trail. | | `signature_image` | `BLOB` | Binary image data; up to 64 KB. | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall ŌĆö Common Mark-Deductions:** > - Using `FLOAT` or `DOUBLE` for `balance` ŌåÆ **lose 2 marks** (floating-point error in money). > - Using `INT` for `opening_date` ŌåÆ **lose 1 mark** (poor practice; use `DATE`). > - Using `VARCHAR` for boolean ŌåÆ **lose 1 mark** (use native `BOOLEAN` or `BIT`). > - Not justifying the choice of type ŌåÆ **lose 1 mark** (KTU requires reasoning, not just naming). --- ### Question B ŌĆö 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]** **(a)** Explain the concept of data types in SQL. Why is the choice of data type considered a critical design decision in database design? *(7 marks)* **(b)** Write SQL `CREATE TABLE` statements for the following two relations. Use appropriate data types and justify each choice. *(7 marks)* **Relation 1:** `Employee(emp_id, emp_name, join_date, salary, department, is_permanent, photo)` **Relation 2:** `Product(product_code, product_name, mfg_date, expiry_date, weight_kg, price, is_organic)` **Model Solution (Part a):** A **data type** in SQL defines the *domain* (set of allowable values), the *storage size*, and the *valid operations* that can be applied to a column. It is a critical design decision because: 1. **Data Integrity** ŌĆö Wrong types allow invalid data (e.g., alphabets in salary). 2. **Storage Optimization** ŌĆö `TINYINT` uses 1 byte, `BIGINT` uses 8 bytes; wrong sizing wastes disk. 3. **Query Performance** ŌĆö Indexes work best on appropriately typed columns; joins are faster. 4. **Operational Validity** ŌĆö Operators behave differently (`+` on INT is arithmetic, on DATE is date-add). 5. **Portability** ŌĆö Standard SQL types migrate cleanly across Oracle, MySQL, PostgreSQL. **Model Solution (Part b):** ```sql CREATE TABLE Employee ( emp_id INT PRIMARY KEY, emp_name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL, join_date DATE, salary DECIMAL(10,2), department VARCHAR(30), is_permanent BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE, photo BLOB ); ``` ```sql CREATE TABLE Product ( product_code CHAR(8) PRIMARY KEY, product_name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL, mfg_date DATE, expiry_date DATE, weight_kg DECIMAL(8,3), -- e.g., 0.125 kg price DECIMAL(10,2), -- exact money is_organic BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE ); ``` **Justification Highlights:** - `emp_id`: `INT` (employee count < 2 billion, supports auto-increment). - `emp_name`: `VARCHAR(50)` (length varies; allows 50-character names). - `join_date`: `DATE` (only calendar date needed). - `salary`: `DECIMAL(10,2)` (exact arithmetic; up to 99,999,999.99). - `department`: `VARCHAR(30)` (free text; 30 characters sufficient). - `is_permanent`: `BOOLEAN` (TRUE / FALSE binary). - `photo`: `BLOB` (binary image; up to 64 KB; for larger, use `MEDIUMBLOB`). - `product_code`: `CHAR(8)` (fixed format like `PROD0001`). - `weight_kg`: `DECIMAL(8,3)` (precise weight, e.g., `0.250 kg`). - `expiry_date`: `DATE` (temporal comparison, e.g., `WHERE expiry_date < CURDATE()`). > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls in Question B:** > - Confusing `DECIMAL(10,2)` (which allows up to `99,999,999.99`) with `DECIMAL(2,2)` (which is invalid ŌĆö would only allow `0.00` to `0.99`). **Lose 1 mark** if precision/scale logic is wrong. > - Using `VARCHAR` for `mfg_date` instead of `DATE`. **Lose 1 mark** as `DATE` supports built-in date functions. > - Using `FLOAT` for `price` or `salary`. **Lose 2 marks** ŌĆö never use float for monetary data. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision ŌĆö Memorize the Following 12 Points:** - **Numeric Hierarchy:** `TINYINT < SMALLINT < INT < BIGINT` (1, 2, 4, 8 bytes). - **Money Always Decimal:** `DECIMAL(p, s)` is the *only* safe type for currency; never use `FLOAT`. - **CHAR vs VARCHAR:** `CHAR(n)` always uses `n` bytes; `VARCHAR(n)` uses `L+1` bytes. - **Date vs Timestamp:** `TIMESTAMP` is timezone-aware and UTC-stored; `DATETIME` is not. - **Year 2038 Problem:** `TIMESTAMP` maxes out at `'2038-01-19 03:14:07'` UTC. - **Boolean Internals:** MySQL uses `TINYINT(1)`; ANSI SQL uses `BIT(1)`; PostgreSQL has native `BOOLEAN`. - **TEXT requires prefix index:** `CREATE INDEX idx ON articles(content(100));` - **BLOB categories:** `TINYBLOB` (255 B), `BLOB` (64 KB), `MEDIUMBLOB` (16 MB), `LONGBLOB` (4 GB). - **ENUM is fast:** Internally stored as integer index; saves storage and enforces domain. - **Implicit Type Coercion:** MySQL will silently promote `INT` to `BIGINT`; `CHAR` to `INT` requires `CAST()`. - **NULL handling:** `BOOLEAN` can be `TRUE / FALSE / UNKNOWN (NULL)` ŌĆö three-valued logic. - **Universal SQL Data Type Rule:** *Right-size* the data type ŌĆö use the *smallest* type that safely holds the domain, but never compromise *data integrity* to save space. > [!TIP] > **Final KTU Board Strategy:** In 14-mark Part B questions, always structure your answer as **(1) Identify all attributes, (2) Categorize each attribute (numeric / string / date / binary), (3) Justify with a one-line reason, (4) Write the final `CREATE TABLE` statement**. This is the exact valuation key pattern KTU examiners use to award the full 7+7 marks.

topics.title: Recursive Queries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Recursive Queries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recursive Query**: A recursive query in SQL is a query that refers to its own output (a Common Table Expression) to iteratively build a result set. It is formally defined under the SQL:1999 standard as a **Recursive Common Table Expression (Recursive CTE)**, executed using the `WITH RECURSIVE` clause.

A recursive query in DBMS is a query that executes repeatedly against itself until no more rows are produced. It is primarily used to traverse **hierarchical** or **tree-structured** data such as organization charts, bill-of-materials (BOM), file systems, network graphs, and parentŌĆōchild relationships stored in self-referencing tables.

The general SQL syntax is:

```sql
WITH RECURSIVE cte_name (column_list) AS (
    -- Anchor member (non-recursive base case)
    SELECT ...
    FROM table_name
    WHERE base_condition
    UNION [ALL]
    -- Recursive member (refers to the CTE itself)
    SELECT ...
    FROM table_name JOIN cte_name ON join_condition
    WHERE recursive_condition
)
SELECT * FROM cte_name;
```

### Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **CTE (Common Table Expression)** | A temporary, named result set defined within the execution scope of a single SQL statement using the `WITH` clause. |
| **Anchor Member** | The non-recursive base query (starting point) that returns the initial set of rows. |
| **Recursive Member** | The query that contains a reference to the CTE itself, producing the next iteration's rows. |
| **Termination Condition** | A predicate that ensures recursion eventually stops, commonly implemented via the anchor's stopping boundary. |
| **Iteration / Recursion Level** | The number of times the recursive member executes. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy ŌĆō The "Family Tree Finder"**
>
> Imagine you are tracing your family ancestry. You start by locating **yourself** (the **anchor / seed**). Then, for each person you find, you ask: *"Who is this person's parent?"* ŌĆö and you record those parents. Then you repeat: *"Who are THEIR parents?"* This continues level by level (grandparents ŌåÆ great-grandparents ŌåÆ ...) until no more ancestors exist. That iterative ancestor-tracing is exactly what a **recursive SQL query** does for data stored in a hierarchical table.

In a database context, the same logic applies to:
- **Employee ŌåÆ Manager ŌåÆ Manager's Manager ŌåÆ ...** (organizational hierarchy)
- **Component ŌåÆ Sub-Component ŌåÆ Sub-Sub-Component ŌåÆ ...** (BOM explosion)
- **City ŌåÆ Connected City ŌåÆ Further Connected City** (graph traversal)

> [!TIP]
> **Why is it "Recursive"?** Because the SQL engine applies the recursive member to the **output of the previous iteration**, not to the original table alone. Each iteration consumes the result of the prior iteration ŌĆö analogous to a loop where the next value depends on the previous.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanics of a Recursive CTE

The DBMS engine executes a recursive CTE in **three logical phases**:

### Phase 1 ŌĆö Initialization (Anchor Evaluation)
- The **anchor member** is executed against the base table.
- The result set produced is the **initial working table (WTŌéĆ)**.
- Conceptually, this is the *seed* of the recursion (e.g., the top-most manager, the root product).

### Phase 2 ŌĆö Iteration (Recursive Member Evaluation)
- For each iteration $i = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$:
  1. The **recursive member** is executed using the previous working table $\text{WT}_{i-1}$ as input.
  2. The new working table $\text{WT}_i$ is computed (rows where the join condition succeeds).
  3. The newly produced rows are **appended** to the final result set.
  4. $\text{WT}_i$ becomes the new working table for the next iteration.

### Phase 3 ŌĆö Termination
- Recursion **stops** when an iteration produces **zero new rows** (empty working table).
- A safeguard `MAXRECURSION` (e.g., 100 in SQL Server, 10ŌüČ in PostgreSQL) prevents infinite loops.

## 2.2 UNION vs UNION ALL in Recursion

| Aspect | `UNION` | `UNION ALL` |
|--------|---------|-------------|
| Duplicate rows | Eliminated per iteration | All rows retained |
| Performance | Slower (sort + dedup) | Faster (no sort) |
| Termination aid | Helps stop loops on cycles | Does **not** prevent infinite cycles |
| KTU recommendation | Use when duplicates possible | **Preferred** for performance + cycle visibility |

## 2.3 Tracking Recursion Depth

A common KTU pattern is to add a **depth counter** so each row knows its level in the hierarchy:

```sql
WITH RECURSIVE emp_hierarchy(emp_id, name, manager_id, level) AS (
    SELECT emp_id, name, manager_id, 1            -- Anchor: level 1
    FROM Employee
    WHERE manager_id IS NULL                     -- Top of the tree
    UNION ALL
    SELECT e.emp_id, e.name, e.manager_id, h.level + 1
    FROM Employee e
    JOIN emp_hierarchy h ON e.manager_id = h.emp_id
)
SELECT * FROM emp_hierarchy;
```

## 2.4 Cycle Detection (Advanced)

To prevent infinite loops in graphs with **cycles**, a `PATH` column can be maintained using `ARRAY[]` (PostgreSQL) or a string delimiter (MySQL/SQL Server). If a node is revisited, the path contains it twice, and the recursive member excludes it via `WHERE node NOT IN path`.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Syntax / Rule | Purpose |
|---------|---------------|---------|
| Recursive CTE Header | `WITH RECURSIVE cte (cols) AS (...)` | Defines recursive named result set |
| Anchor member | Non-recursive `SELECT` (no self-reference) | Seeds the recursion |
| Recursive member | `SELECT` referring to its own CTE name | Generates next iteration |
| Combining operator | `UNION` or `UNION ALL` | Joins anchor + recursive outputs |
| Termination | Empty iteration result / cycle guard | Stops infinite loops |
| Depth tracking | `level` column incremented by 1 | Hierarchical level indicator |
| Path tracking | `path` text/array column | Cycle detection |
| Iteration bound | System-level `MAXRECURSION` | Safety guardrail |
| Self-join | `JOIN cte ON parent = cte.child` | Walks the tree edge-by-edge |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Recursive queries power:
- **ERP Systems** ŌĆö bill of materials explosion in manufacturing.
- **Network Routing** ŌĆö BFS/DFS-style path enumeration in telecom graphs.
- **Social Networks** ŌĆö friend-of-friend recommendations, connection depth.
- **HR Management Systems** ŌĆö manager chains, approval workflows.
- **File Systems** ŌĆö directory tree traversal in cloud storage metadata.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical Problem ŌĆö Employee Hierarchy

### Sample Table

Consider the following `Employee` table used throughout this derivation:

| **emp_id** | **name**   | **manager_id** |
|------------|------------|----------------|
| 1          | Alice      | NULL           |
| 2          | Bob        | 1              |
| 3          | Carol      | 1              |
| 4          | David      | 2              |
| 5          | Eve        | 2              |
| 6          | Frank      | 3              |
| 7          | Grace      | 4              |

> Alice is the CEO (manager_id = NULL). Bob and Carol report to Alice. David and Eve report to Bob, and so on.

The hierarchy is:

```
Alice (Level 1)
 Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Bob (Level 2)
 Ōöé    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ David (Level 3)
 Ōöé    Ōöé    ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ Grace (Level 4)
 Ōöé    ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ Eve (Level 3)
 ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ Carol (Level 2)
      ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ Frank (Level 3)
```

### Recursive CTE ŌĆö Find Every Employee Under Alice

```sql
WITH RECURSIVE subordinates AS (
    -- ===== Anchor Member =====
    SELECT emp_id, name, manager_id, 1 AS level
    FROM Employee
    WHERE manager_id IS NULL              -- Start at the root (Alice)

    UNION ALL

    -- ===== Recursive Member =====
    SELECT e.emp_id, e.name, e.manager_id, s.level + 1
    FROM Employee e
    JOIN subordinates s
      ON e.manager_id = s.emp_id
)
SELECT * FROM subordinates
ORDER BY level, emp_id;
```

### Step-by-Step Trace

**Iteration 0 (Anchor):** Returns the row(s) where `manager_id IS NULL`.

$$
\text{WT}_0 = \{(\text{Alice}, 1)\}
$$

**Iteration 1:** Join `Employee` against `WT_0` on `e.manager_id = s.emp_id`. Every employee whose manager is Alice (emp_id = 1) is fetched.

$$
\text{WT}_1 = \{(\text{Bob}, 2),\ (\text{Carol}, 2)\}
$$

**Iteration 2:** Join against `WT_1`. Employees whose manager is Bob or Carol.

$$
\text{WT}_2 = \{(\text{David}, 3),\ (\text{Eve}, 3),\ (\text{Frank}, 3)\}
$$

**Iteration 3:** Employees whose manager is David, Eve, or Frank.

$$
\text{WT}_3 = \{(\text{Grace}, 4)\}
$$

**Iteration 4:** No employee has Grace as their manager.

$$
\text{WT}_4 = \emptyset \quad \Rightarrow \text{Recursion halts.}
$$

### Final Concatenated Result

$$
\text{Result} = \text{WT}_0 \cup \text{WT}_1 \cup \text{WT}_2 \cup \text{WT}_3
$$

| **emp_id** | **name** | **manager_id** | **level** |
|------------|----------|----------------|-----------|
| 1          | Alice    | NULL           | 1         |
| 2          | Bob      | 1              | 2         |
| 3          | Carol    | 1              | 2         |
| 4          | David    | 2              | 3         |
| 5          | Eve      | 2              | 3         |
| 6          | Frank    | 3              | 3         |
| 7          | Grace    | 4              | 4         |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this terminate?**
> Each iteration moves one level **deeper** into the tree. Since the tree has finite height $h$ (here $h = 4$), after at most $h$ iterations the working table becomes empty. In graph queries with cycles, termination requires **explicit path tracking**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Common Recursive Query Patterns

| **Pattern** | **Anchor Condition** | **Recursive Join** | **Use Case** |
|-------------|----------------------|--------------------|--------------|
| Top-down hierarchy | `WHERE parent IS NULL` | `e.parent = cte.id` | Org chart, BOM, category trees |
| Bottom-up hierarchy | `WHERE id = target` | `e.id = cte.parent` | Find ancestors of a node |
| Path enumeration | `WHERE start_node = X` | `e.from = cte.to` | Graph shortest path, route map |
| Cumulative sum | `WHERE prev IS NULL` | `e.prev = cte.id` | Running totals, sequence chains |
| Date series | Anchor = first date | Add 1 day/month | Generating calendars |
| Bill of materials | Top assembly | Sub-component join | Manufacturing cost rollup |

## 3.3 Quantitative Comparison: Recursive vs Iterative Procedural Logic

In procedural PL/SQL or T-SQL, the same result requires a **WHILE loop**, a temporary table, and explicit row-by-row processing. The recursive CTE approach is:

$$
\text{Code Complexity}_{\text{CTE}} < \text{Code Complexity}_{\text{Loop}}
$$

$$
\text{Readability}_{\text{CTE}} > \text{Readability}_{\text{Loop}}
$$

$$
\text{Execution Time}_{\text{CTE}} \approx \text{Execution Time}_{\text{Loop}} \quad \text{(for small/medium depth)}
$$

> [!TIP]
> For **deep hierarchies** ($> 10{,}000$ levels), the optimizer may switch to iterative strategies internally; explicit procedural code may outperform naive recursive CTEs.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Recursive Query Execution Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Client Issues WITH RECURSIVE Query] --> B[Parse CTE Definition]
    B --> C[Execute Anchor Member]
    C --> D[WT0: Working Table Iteration 0]
    D --> E{WT Empty?}
    E -- No --> F[Execute Recursive Member]
    F --> G[WTi: Working Table Iteration i]
    G --> H[Append New Rows to Final Result]
    H --> I[Increment i by 1]
    I --> E
    E -- Yes --> J[Return Final Result Set to Client]
    D --> K[Track Depth Level Counter]
    G --> K
    K --> L[Optional Path Column for Cycle Detection]
```

## 4.2 Employee Hierarchy Tree (Mermaid Graph)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[Alice - Level 1 - CEO]
    rootB[Bob - Level 2 - Manager]
    rootC[Carol - Level 2 - Manager]
    rootD[David - Level 3 - Lead]
    rootE[Eve - Level 3 - Engineer]
    rootF[Frank - Level 3 - Engineer]
    rootG[Grace - Level 4 - Intern]

    rootA --> rootB
    rootA --> rootC
    rootB --> rootD
    rootB --> rootE
    rootC --> rootF
    rootD --> rootG
```

## 4.3 Recursive CTE Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Definition[CTE Definition Block]
        AM[Anchor Member SQL]
        RM[Recursive Member SQL]
        OP[UNION ALL Operator]
        AM --> OP
        RM --> OP
    end

    subgraph Engine[DBMS Engine Executor]
        EX1[Step 1: Run Anchor]
        EX2[Step 2: Build WT0]
        EX3[Step 3: Join and Iterate]
        EX4[Step 4: Append Rows]
        EX5[Step 5: Check Empty]
        EX1 --> EX2 --> EX3 --> EX4 --> EX5
        EX5 -- Not Empty --> EX3
        EX5 -- Empty --> OUT[Final Result]
    end

    Definition --> Engine
```

## 4.4 Cycle Detection Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Initialize path = start node]
    P --> Q{Visit Next Node}
    Q -- Node NOT in path --> R[Append node to path]
    R --> S[Add to result set]
    S --> Q
    Q -- Node already in path --> T[STOP: Cycle Detected]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q1.** *What is a recursive query? Give its general syntax.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A recursive query is a SQL query defined using the `WITH RECURSIVE` clause that repeatedly executes a recursive member against its own previous output until no new rows are produced. It is primarily used to traverse hierarchical/tree-structured data such as organization charts and bill of materials.

```sql
WITH RECURSIVE cte_name (col1, col2) AS (
    SELECT ... FROM table WHERE base_condition     -- Anchor
    UNION ALL
    SELECT ... FROM table JOIN cte_name ON ...    -- Recursive
)
SELECT * FROM cte_name;
```
*(1 mark definition + 1 mark syntax + 1 mark purpose = 3 marks)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024, Expected]**

**Q2.** *Differentiate between the **anchor member** and the **recursive member** in a recursive CTE.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Anchor Member | Recursive Member |
|---------|---------------|------------------|
| Self-reference | **No** reference to the CTE | **Yes**, references the CTE |
| Execution | Once (initial seed) | Repeatedly (each iteration) |
| Purpose | Defines starting row(s) | Generates next-level rows |
| Termination | Always executes first | Stops when result is empty |
*(Table format = 3 marks; full marks for clear distinction)*

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 / 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern]**

### ŌŁÉ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** *Explain the concept of recursive queries in SQL with a suitable example. Illustrate how they are used to traverse an organizational hierarchy.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Consider the following `Employee` table:*
| emp_id | ename | manager_id |
|--------|-------|------------|
| 1 | Arun | NULL |
| 2 | Balu | 1 |
| 3 | Chand | 1 |
| 4 | Deepa | 2 |
| 5 | Ebin | 2 |
| 6 | Fathima | 3 |
| 7 | George | 4 |

*Write a recursive SQL query to list **all employees under Arun** along with their **hierarchy level**. Show the iteration-wise result.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Question A

**(a) Concept Explanation (7 marks):**

A recursive query is implemented via a **Recursive Common Table Expression (CTE)** introduced in SQL:1999. It consists of two parts:
1. **Anchor query** ŌĆō a non-recursive SELECT that produces the initial rows.
2. **Recursive query** ŌĆō a SELECT that references the CTE itself, producing the next iteration's rows.

The DBMS engine:
- First executes the anchor and stores it as the initial working table $\text{WT}_0$.
- Then iteratively executes the recursive member, each time joining the base table with the previous working table to compute $\text{WT}_i$.
- Stops when $\text{WT}_i$ becomes empty.
- The final result is the union of all iterations.

Recursive queries are used to traverse hierarchies such as:
- **Manager ŌåÆ Subordinate chains** in HR systems.
- **Part ŌåÆ Sub-part ŌåÆ Sub-sub-part** in manufacturing BOMs.
- **Folder ŌåÆ Subfolder ŌåÆ File** in file systems.

**(b) Recursive SQL Query & Trace (7 marks):**

```sql
WITH RECURSIVE emp_tree AS (
    -- Anchor: top of hierarchy (Arun)
    SELECT emp_id, ename, manager_id, 1 AS level
    FROM Employee
    WHERE manager_id IS NULL

    UNION ALL

    -- Recursive: find direct reports
    SELECT e.emp_id, e.ename, e.manager_id, t.level + 1
    FROM Employee e
    JOIN emp_tree t ON e.manager_id = t.emp_id
)
SELECT * FROM emp_tree
ORDER BY level, emp_id;
```

**Iteration Trace (KTU board expects this):**

| Iteration | Working Table Contents | Level |
|-----------|------------------------|-------|
| 0 (Anchor) | Arun | 1 |
| 1 | Balu, Chand | 2 |
| 2 | Deepa, Ebin, Fathima | 3 |
| 3 | George | 4 |
| 4 | (empty) ŌåÆ STOP | ŌĆö |

**Final Result:**

| emp_id | ename  | manager_id | level |
|--------|--------|------------|-------|
| 1      | Arun   | NULL       | 1     |
| 2      | Balu   | 1          | 2     |
| 3      | Chand  | 1          | 2     |
| 4      | Deepa  | 2          | 3     |
| 5      | Ebin   | 2          | 3     |
| 6      | Fathima| 3          | 3     |
| 7      | George | 4          | 4     |

---

### ŌŁÉ Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice

**(a)** *With a neat diagram, explain the **three-phase execution model** of a recursive CTE (Initialization, Iteration, Termination).* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Write a recursive SQL query on a `Parts` table* (`part_id`, `part_name`, `parent_part_id`) *to find **all sub-components of a given assembly 'X'** at every level of nesting. Explain how cycle detection can be added using a path column.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Question B

**(a) Three-Phase Execution Model (7 marks):**

```
PHASE 1: INITIALIZATION
  - Execute anchor member against base table
  - Result = WTŌéĆ (working table, iteration 0)
  - Append WTŌéĆ to final result set

PHASE 2: ITERATION (repeats until WTßĄó is empty)
  - For i = 1, 2, 3, ...:
      * Execute recursive member using WTßĄóŌéŗŌéü as input
      * Compute WTßĄó via JOIN on self-referencing FK
      * Append WTßĄó to final result set
      * Replace WTßĄóŌéŗŌéü with WTßĄó

PHASE 3: TERMINATION
  - Halts when WTßĄó = Ōłģ
  - Or when MAXRECURSION limit reached (safety guard)
  - Return accumulated final result set to caller
```

Each phase can be drawn as a step in a flowchart (anchor ŌåÆ loop ŌåÆ empty check ŌåÆ return). *(Full diagram = 3 marks, explanation = 4 marks.)*

**(b) Parts Sub-Component Query with Cycle Detection (7 marks):**

```sql
WITH RECURSIVE parts_explosion AS (
    -- Anchor: starting assembly 'X'
    SELECT part_id, part_name, parent_part_id,
           1 AS level,
           CAST(part_name AS VARCHAR(1000)) AS path
    FROM Parts
    WHERE part_name = 'X'

    UNION ALL

    -- Recursive: child parts with cycle guard
    SELECT p.part_id, p.part_name, p.parent_part_id,
           pe.level + 1,
           CAST(pe.path || ' -> ' || p.part_name AS VARCHAR(1000))
    FROM Parts p
    JOIN parts_explosion pe ON p.parent_part_id = pe.part_id
    WHERE p.part_name NOT IN
          (SELECT value FROM STRING_SPLIT(pe.path, ' -> '))
)
SELECT * FROM parts_explosion
ORDER BY level, part_id;
```

**Cycle detection logic (3 marks):**
- The `path` column maintains a running string of all ancestors visited.
- If a candidate child's name already appears in the path, the `WHERE NOT IN` predicate filters it out, preventing infinite loops in cyclic BOMs.
- The `level` column indicates the nesting depth, useful for indent-style reports.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Recursive Query Answers:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the `UNION`/`UNION ALL` operator** between anchor and recursive members ŌĆö results in syntax error and **0 marks** for the query.
> 2. **Self-referencing the CTE inside the anchor** ŌĆö violates recursion rules; marks cut by **2ŌĆō3**.
> 3. **Missing the iteration trace table** ŌĆö KTU examiners expect a step-by-step working table trace (WTŌéĆ, WTŌéü, ŌĆ”) for full 7 marks.
> 4. **No termination explanation** ŌĆö students often omit *why* recursion stops; this costs **1 mark** in the conceptual part.
> 5. **Wrong join direction** ŌĆö using `e.emp_id = cte.manager_id` instead of `e.manager_id = cte.emp_id` flips the traversal direction; deduct **2 marks**.
> 6. **Not specifying `RECURSIVE` keyword** ŌĆö required by SQL standard; omission flagged as an error in board valuation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember Ō£Ź’ĖÅ

- Ō£ģ A **recursive query** uses `WITH RECURSIVE` and self-references its own CTE name.
- Ō£ģ Always has **two members**: the **anchor** (non-recursive seed) and the **recursive** (self-referencing iteration).
- Ō£ģ Anchor and recursive members are combined using **`UNION`** or **`UNION ALL`**.
- Ō£ģ Execution proceeds in **three phases**: Initialization ŌåÆ Iteration ŌåÆ Termination.
- Ō£ģ Recursion **terminates** when the new working table is empty (or `MAXRECURSION` hits).
- Ō£ģ Add a **level/depth counter** to report hierarchy depth ŌĆö a KTU-favored pattern.
- Ō£ģ Add a **path column** for **cycle detection** in graph-style data.
- Ō£ģ Typical use cases: **org charts, BOM, file trees, network paths, ancestor lookups**.
- Ō£ģ Bottom-up traversal: anchor = target node; recursive join = `e.id = cte.parent_id`.
- Ō£ģ Top-down traversal: anchor = root node; recursive join = `e.parent_id = cte.id`.
- Ō£ģ `UNION ALL` is preferred over `UNION` for performance but does **not** guard against cycles.
- Ō£ģ Recursive CTEs are part of the **SQL:1999** standard and supported in PostgreSQL, MySQL 8+, SQL Server, Oracle 11g+, DB2.
- Ō£ģ For very deep recursion, the optimizer may convert the CTE to an **iterative plan** internally.
- Ō£ģ Keep a **mental model**: anchor = "where I start", recursive = "where I go next", termination = "when I stop".

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Accessing SQL from a Programming Language
Different Anomalies in Designing a Database
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Different Anomalies in Designing a Database

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Database Anomalies

In the context of **Relational Database Design**, an **anomaly** is an undesired side-effect or inconsistency that arises when a relation (table) contains redundant, improperly grouped, or mixed-granularity data. Anomalies are the direct consequence of attempting to store more than one concept (or one "theme") in a single table without respecting the principles of **normalization**.

Formally, an anomaly is defined as:
