> An **anomaly** in a database is an unintended violation of data integrity ŌĆö an irregularity during `INSERT`, `DELETE`, or `UPDATE` operations ŌĆö that occurs because a poorly designed relation stores related but distinct facts (e.g., student details and course details) in the same row.

The three classical anomalies identified in the KTU 2024 syllabus are:
1. **Insertion Anomaly**
2. **Deletion Anomaly (Deletion Inconsistency)**
3. **Update Anomaly (Modification Anomaly)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A relation that exhibits any of these three anomalies is, by definition, in a **lower normal form** (typically **UNF / 1NF** but not **3NF / BCNF**). Eliminating anomalies is the *primary motivation* for normalization up to 3NF/BCNF.

## 1.2 The Intuition ŌĆö A Real World Analogy

Imagine a single **multi-purpose notebook** used by a college office clerk. Every page is divided into columns:

| Roll No | Student Name | Course ID | Course Name | Faculty | Grade |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|

- The clerk writes a student's information, the course details, the faculty, and the grade ŌĆö **all in one row**.

Now, what happens when:
- ­¤¤ź A new course is offered, but **no student has registered for it yet**? The clerk **cannot add the course details** because the `Roll No` column would be blank. The course is "invisible" to the database. ŌåÆ **Insertion Anomaly**
- ­¤¤ź The **last student** enrolled in a particular course drops out and his record is deleted? The course details, faculty name, and credit info are **permanently lost** from the notebook. ŌåÆ **Deletion Anomaly**
- ­¤¤ź A faculty member changes her phone number, and the same faculty teaches 40 students? The clerk must hunt through 40 different rows and update the same value **40 times**. Forget one, and the data becomes contradictory. ŌåÆ **Update Anomaly**

The **fundamental cure** is to **split the notebook into multiple smaller, single-purpose notebooks** ŌĆö one for students, one for courses, one for enrollments. This is the heart of **normalization**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Anomalies:** They occur because of **redundancy**. If a fact (e.g., "Dr. Sharma teaches DBMS") is stored in many rows, every operation that touches those rows is at risk. Normalization reduces redundancy ŌåÆ eliminates anomalies.

## 1.3 Strict Formal Statement

A relation `R` is said to suffer from anomalies if there exists a functional dependency `X ŌåÆ Y` where `X` is **not a superkey** of `R` and `Y` is **not a prime attribute** (or simply: a non-key attribute depends on part of a key, or a non-key attribute depends on another non-key attribute). The presence of such "bad" FDs is what allows the three anomalies to occur.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Anomalies ŌĆö Detailed Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Insertion Anomaly

An **insertion anomaly** occurs when certain facts **cannot be inserted into the database** because other required attributes are not yet known or do not exist.

- **Operational Logic:**
  - The relation `R` has a primary key `K`.
  - To insert a new fact about entity `B` (which is *not* `K`), we are **forced to also insert a fact about entity `A`** (the key), even when `A`'s fact does not exist.
  - The new row cannot be created because the key column must be `NOT NULL`, but we have no value for it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Variants of Insertion Anomaly:**
> - **Variant 1:** A new real-world entity exists, but cannot be recorded because of a missing related entity.
> - **Variant 2:** A new attribute value for a *non-key* attribute cannot be recorded without inserting a duplicate of the key (causing a primary key violation).

---

### 2.1.2 Deletion Anomaly

A **deletion anomaly** occurs when deleting a single row (representing one fact) **inadvertently destroys another unrelated fact** that was stored in the same row.

- **Operational Logic:**
  - A row in `R` carries information about **two or more different real-world entities** (e.g., a student AND the course they take).
  - Removing the row to delete information about entity `A` also deletes the only remaining representation of entity `B`.
  - The information about `B` is **lost irrecoverably**.

---

### 2.1.3 Update Anomaly (Modification Anomaly)

An **update anomaly** occurs when the same piece of information is **stored in multiple rows**, and updating it requires changing all those rows. Missing one creates **inconsistency**.

- **Operational Logic:**
  - A non-key attribute `A` is functionally dependent on a non-superkey determinant.
  - The value of `A` is repeated in N rows.
  - Updating `A` requires N update operations.
  - If even one update is missed, the database is in an **inconsistent state** (different rows give different answers to the same question).

---

## 2.2 Conditions for Anomaly Occurrence (FD Perspective)

| Anomaly | Root Cause (FD-wise) | Manifestation |
|---|---|---|
| **Insertion** | Partial dependency / Transitive dependency | Cannot insert a fact without inserting an unrelated fact |
| **Deletion** | Mixed granularity in one row | Deleting one fact destroys another fact |
| **Update** | Redundancy of a non-key value | Same value stored N times ŌåÆ update N places |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No numeric equations exist for this topic, but the logical "rules" below are the high-yield memorization points for KTU 2024.**

| # | Concept | Formal Logical Statement | Condition for Anomaly |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Insertion Anomaly** | $\nexists \text{ INSERT}(B) \text{ without } A$ | $\text{Key} \rightarrow \text{Unrelated Fact}$ enforced |
| 2 | **Deletion Anomaly** | $\text{DELETE}(A) \Rightarrow \text{LOSS}(B)$ | Two distinct entity types share one tuple |
| 3 | **Update Anomaly** | $\text{UPDATE}(A) \text{ must occur in } N \text{ rows}$ | $\text{Non-Key Attribute} \rightarrow \text{Non-Key Attribute}$ |
| 4 | **Redundancy Ratio** | $\text{Anomaly Likelihood} \propto \frac{\text{Duplicate Non-Key Values}}{\text{Total Rows}}$ | Higher ratio ŌćÆ Higher risk |
| 5 | **Cure Threshold** | Decompose to **3NF / BCNF** | $\text{Every Determinant} \rightarrow \text{Superkey}$ |
| 6 | **Dependency Rule** | If $X \rightarrow Y$ and $X$ is not a Superkey | **Anomaly probable** |
| 7 | **Lossless Join Test** | $R_1 \cap R_2 \rightarrow R_1 \text{ OR } R_1 \cap R_2 \rightarrow R_2$ | Ensures decomposition preserves data |
| 8 | **Dependency Preservation** | $(F_1 \cup F_2 \cup \ldots \cup F_n)^{+} = F^{+}$ | Ensures decomposition preserves constraints |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In real-world systems (banking, healthcare, e-commerce), failure to eliminate update anomalies causes **silent data corruption** ŌĆö different branches of a bank showing different balances for the same account. Anomaly-free design is the bedrock of ACID-compliant systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Master Example: A Poorly Designed Table

Consider a single relation used by a college to store student enrollments:

$$R(\underline{\text{SID}}, \text{SName}, \underline{\text{CID}}, \text{CName}, \text{Credits}, \text{Faculty}, \text{Grade})$$

The **composite primary key** is $\langle \text{SID}, \text{CID} \rangle$.

Let us populate it with a few rows:

| SID | SName | CID | CName | Credits | Faculty | Grade |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| S01 | Arun | C01 | DBMS | 4 | Dr. Sharma | A |
| S01 | Arun | C02 | OS | 3 | Dr. Verma | B+ |
| S02 | Meera | C01 | DBMS | 4 | Dr. Sharma | O |
| S02 | Meera | C03 | CN | 4 | Dr. Iyer | A+ |

## 3.2 Demonstrating Each Anomaly ŌĆö Step by Step

### 3.2.1 INSERTION ANOMALY ŌĆö Stepwise Derivation

> **Scenario:** The college introduces a brand-new course **"AI" (CID = C04, Faculty = Dr. Nair, Credits = 4)**. No student has registered yet.

**Step 1:** Identify the primary key.
The primary key of `R` is $\text{PK} = \text{SID} \cup \text{CID}$.

**Step 2:** Attempt the insert.
We wish to execute:
$$\text{INSERT INTO } R (\text{SID}, \text{SName}, \text{CID}, \text{CName}, \text{Credits}, \text{Faculty}, \text{Grade}) \text{ VALUES (NULL, NULL, 'C04', 'AI', 4, 'Dr. Nair', NULL)}$$

**Step 3:** Identify the violation.
$\text{SID}$ is part of the primary key, so it **cannot be NULL**. The INSERT fails.

**Step 4:** Conclude.
A new course cannot be added **until at least one student enrolls in it**. This is the **Insertion Anomaly** ŌĆö the inability to record a real-world fact (new course) because an unrelated fact (a student enrollment) does not yet exist.

---

### 3.2.2 DELETION ANOMALY ŌĆö Stepwise Derivation

> **Scenario:** Student **Meera (S02)** drops out of college. We delete her records from `R`.

**Step 1:** Identify the rows to delete.
All rows where $\text{SID} = \text{S02}$:
- Row 1: (S02, Meera, C01, DBMS, 4, Dr. Sharma, O)
- Row 2: (S02, Meera, C03, CN, 4, Dr. Iyer, A+)

**Step 2:** Perform the deletion.
$$\text{DELETE FROM } R \text{ WHERE } \text{SID} = \text{'S02'}$$

**Step 3:** Inspect the damage.
The row `(S02, Meera, C03, CN, 4, Dr. Iyer, A+)` was the **only** row that contained the course `C03 (CN)` and its faculty `Dr. Iyer`. With Meera gone, the database has **lost the fact that course CN is taught by Dr. Iyer with 4 credits** ŌĆö even though other students may take CN later, *currently* this information is gone.

**Step 4:** Conclude.
Deleting a fact about one entity (Meera) destroyed a fact about an unrelated entity (the CN course and its faculty). This is the **Deletion Anomaly**.

---

### 3.2.3 UPDATE ANOMALY ŌĆö Stepwise Derivation

> **Scenario:** Dr. Sharma's phone number is to be added/changed, and Dr. Sharma teaches DBMS to **500 students**.

**Step 1:** Identify all redundant occurrences.
The value `Dr. Sharma` appears in **500 rows** (one for every student enrolled in C01).

**Step 2:** State the required update.
$$\text{UPDATE } R \text{ SET Faculty} = \text{'Dr. Sharma (New Contact)'} \text{ WHERE } \text{CID} = \text{'C01' AND Faculty} = \text{'Dr. Sharma'}$$

**Step 3:** Identify the risk.
The update must touch **all 500 rows** simultaneously. If the system crashes mid-update, or a developer forgets the `WHERE` clause, the database becomes **inconsistent** ŌĆö some rows say "Dr. Sharma (New Contact)" and others still say "Dr. Sharma".

**Step 4:** Conclude.
Updating a redundant, multi-row fact risks **inconsistency**. This is the **Update Anomaly**.

---

## 3.3 The Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Mapping Anomalies to Functional Dependencies

The root cause can be captured precisely via Functional Dependencies (FDs).

| FD in `R` | Type of Dependency | Anomaly Caused |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{SID} \rightarrow \text{SName}$ | **Partial Dependency** | Insertion, Deletion, Update |
| $\text{CID} \rightarrow \text{CName, Credits, Faculty}$ | **Partial Dependency** | Insertion, Deletion, Update |
| $\text{SID, CID} \rightarrow \text{Grade}$ | **Full Dependency** | None (this is correct) |

> **Key Insight:** Any FD where the **left-hand side is a proper subset of the primary key** creates a partial dependency ŌåÆ guarantees anomalies.

## 3.4 The Cure ŌĆö Decomposition

We decompose `R` into three normalized relations:

$$R_1(\underline{\text{SID}}, \text{SName})$$
$$R_2(\underline{\text{CID}}, \text{CName}, \text{Credits}, \text{Faculty})$$
$$R_3(\underline{\text{SID}, \text{CID}}, \text{Grade})$$

**Step 1: Verify Lossless Join.**
The common attribute between `R_2` and `R_3` is $\text{CID}$. We have $\text{CID} \rightarrow \text{CName, Credits, Faculty}$. Since $\text{CID}$ is a key of $R_2$, the join of $R_2$ and $R_3$ on $\text{CID}$ is **lossless**.

**Step 2: Re-test the anomalies.**

| Anomaly | After Decomposition ŌĆö Result |
|---|---|
| **Insertion** | New course `(C04, AI, 4, Dr. Nair)` can be inserted into `R_2` directly. Ō£ģ Resolved |
| **Deletion** | Deleting Meera from `R_3` does not affect `R_2`. The CN course info in `R_2` remains intact. Ō£ģ Resolved |
| **Update** | Dr. Sharma's data is now stored **once** in `R_2`. A single UPDATE fixes it. Ō£ģ Resolved |

**Step 3: Verify Dependency Preservation.**
All original FDs ($\text{SID} \rightarrow \text{SName}$, $\text{CID} \rightarrow \text{CName, Credits, Faculty}$, $\text{SID, CID} \rightarrow \text{Grade}$) are preserved in the decomposed relations. Ō£ģ

---

## 3.5 Formal Decision Rule ŌĆö When Does An Anomaly Exist?

An anomaly is guaranteed to exist in relation `R` if any of the following hold:

$$\exists \, X \rightarrow Y \;\; \text{where} \;\; X \not\supseteq \text{Superkey}(R) \;\; \text{AND} \;\; Y \text{ is a non-prime attribute}$$

Equivalently, the relation has at least one of:
- **Partial Dependency** ŌåÆ Partial key determines a non-key attribute.
- **Transitive Dependency** ŌåÆ A non-key attribute determines another non-key attribute.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 ŌĆö Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Anomaly Generation Flow (Mermaid Diagram)

This diagram shows **how a poorly designed single table** generates the three anomalies and **how decomposition cures them**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Poorly Designed Relation R\n(PK = SID, CID)\nMixed: Student + Course + Enrollment"] --> B{"Is the design normalized?"}
    B -- "NO - Has Partial /\n Transitive Dependencies" --> C["Anomalies Exist in R"]
    C --> C1["INSERTION Anomaly\nNew course cannot be added\nwithout a student"]
    C --> C2["DELETION Anomaly\nDeleting a student loses\ncourse information"]
    C --> C3["UPDATE Anomaly\nFaculty name duplicated\n500 times -> inconsistent updates"]
    B -- "YES - Normalized to 3NF / BCNF" --> D["Decompose R into R1, R2, R3"]
    D --> E["R1: Student(SID, SName)"]
    D --> F["R2: Course(CID, CName, Credits, Faculty)"]
    D --> G["R3: Enrollment(SID, CID, Grade)"]
    E --> H["All Three Anomalies Eliminated"]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I["Lossless Join + Dependency Preservation Verified"]
```

## 4.2 The Anomaly Taxonomy (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Anomalies in\nDatabase Design"] --> B["INSERTION\nAnomaly"]
    A --> C["DELETION\nAnomaly"]
    A --> D["UPDATE\nAnomaly"]
    B --> B1["Cause:\nMissing related entity"]
    B --> B2["Example:\nNew course has no student"]
    C --> C1["Cause:\nTwo facts in one row"]
    C --> C2["Example:\nLast student drops -\ncourse info lost"]
    D --> D1["Cause:\nRedundancy in\nnon-key attribute"]
    D --> D2["Example:\nFaculty name in 500 rows"]
    B1 --> FIX["Cure:\nDecompose to higher NF\n3NF / BCNF"]
    B2 --> FIX
    C1 --> FIX
    C2 --> FIX
    D1 --> FIX
    D2 --> FIX
```

## 4.3 Before vs. After Decomposition ŌĆö Block View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph BEFORE["BEFORE - UNNORMALIZED / 1NF"]
        R["R(SID, SName, CID,\nCName, Credits, Faculty, Grade)\nPK = SID + CID\nANOMALIES PRESENT"]
    end
    subgraph AFTER["AFTER - 3NF DECOMPOSITION"]
        R1["R1: STUDENT\n(SID, SName)"]
        R2["R2: COURSE\n(CID, CName, Credits, Faculty)"]
        R3["R3: ENROLLMENT\n(SID, CID, Grade)"]
    end
    R -- "Decompose via\n2NF/3NF Rules" --> R1
    R -- "Decompose" --> R2
    R -- "Decompose" --> R3
    R1 -. "Join on SID" .-> R3
    R2 -. "Join on CID" .-> R3
```

## 4.4 Root-Cause Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FD["Functional Dependency\nX -> Y present in R"] --> CHK{"Is X a\nSuperkey of R?"}
    CHK -- "NO" --> BAD["BAD FD\nGuarantees Anomaly"]
    CHK -- "YES" --> GOOD["GOOD FD\nNo Anomaly"]
    BAD --> T1["Partial Dependency\n(X is part of PK)"]
    BAD --> T2["Transitive Dependency\n(X is non-key, Y is non-key)"]
    T1 --> A1["Insertion / Deletion /\nUpdate Anomaly"]
    T2 --> A2["Insertion / Deletion /\nUpdate Anomaly"]
    GOOD --> OK["Relation is in BCNF\n(No Anomalies)"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "anomaly" in the context of database design. List the three types of anomalies that occur due to poor database design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **anomaly** is an unintended inconsistency or irregularity in a database that occurs when a relation is not properly normalized ŌĆö i.e., when it contains redundancy due to improper functional dependencies.

The three types of anomalies are:
1. **Insertion Anomaly** ŌĆö Inability to insert a fact because a related (but distinct) fact does not yet exist.
2. **Deletion Anomaly** ŌĆö Unintended loss of valid data when a row is deleted.
3. **Update Anomaly** ŌĆö Inconsistency caused by updating only some of the multiple copies of a redundant value.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three types ŌĆö ┬Į mark each plus ┬Į mark for correct phrasing.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Explain the "Update Anomaly" with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **update anomaly** (also called **modification anomaly**) arises when the same data is stored in multiple rows, and updating it requires changing every row. If even one row is missed, the database becomes inconsistent.

**Example:** Consider a relation `EMP_DEPT(EmpID, EmpName, DeptID, DeptName, Manager)`. If a department's manager changes, the new manager's name must be updated in **every row** belonging to employees of that department. Missing one row leads to conflicting information about who manages the department.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for example, 1 mark for consequence.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) ŌĆö Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Consider the following relation for a publishing house:

`BOOK(BookID, Title, AuthorID, AuthorName, Publisher, Price, ShelfNo)`

The primary key is `BookID`. Functional dependencies are:
- $\text{BookID} \rightarrow \text{Title, AuthorID, Price, ShelfNo}$
- $\text{AuthorID} \rightarrow \text{AuthorName, Publisher}$

**Identify and explain the three anomalies that can occur in this relation. Use specific row examples.** **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Decompose the relation `BOOK` into **3NF** relations. Verify that the decomposition is **lossless** and **dependency-preserving**. Show that all three anomalies are eliminated. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks**

**Step 1: State the anomalies with reference to the given FDs.**

The FD $\text{AuthorID} \rightarrow \text{AuthorName, Publisher}$ is a **partial dependency** (AuthorID is not a superkey; it is a non-prime attribute). This single bad FD is the **root cause of all three anomalies**.

**Step 2: Insertion Anomaly (2 marks)**

Suppose a new author `A99` (AuthorName = "Ruskin Bond", Publisher = "Penguin") signs a contract, but has not yet written any book. We cannot insert `(NULL, NULL, A99, Ruskin Bond, Penguin, NULL, NULL)` because `BookID` (the primary key) cannot be NULL. Hence, the author's details **cannot be added** to the database until they publish a book.

**Step 3: Deletion Anomaly (2 marks)**

Suppose author `A99` has written exactly one book `(B01, "The Blue Umbrella", A99, Ruskin Bond, Penguin, 250, S03)`. If this book is removed from the catalog (perhaps it goes out of print) and the row is deleted, we **permanently lose** the information that Ruskin Bond is an author published by Penguin ŌĆö even though he may write more books in the future.

**Step 4: Update Anomaly (2 marks)**

Suppose Ruskin Bond changes his publisher from Penguin to HarperCollins. His `AuthorID = A99` appears in **N rows** (one per book he has written). The update must touch all N rows. If even one row is missed, the database will show Ruskin Bond as published by **two different publishers simultaneously** ŌĆö a logical contradiction.

**Step 5: Conclude (1 mark)**

All three anomalies arise from the **partial dependency** $\text{AuthorID} \rightarrow \text{AuthorName, Publisher}$. They are eliminated by decomposing the relation into a form where `AuthorID` becomes a key in its own relation.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Decompose into 3NF relations (3 marks)**

We split `BOOK` so that the partial dependency becomes a key dependency:

$$R_1(\underline{\text{BookID}}, \text{Title}, \text{AuthorID}, \text{Price}, \text{ShelfNo})$$
$$R_2(\underline{\text{AuthorID}}, \text{AuthorName}, \text{Publisher})$$

**Step 2: Verify Lossless Join (2 marks)**

The common attribute between $R_1$ and $R_2$ is `AuthorID`. We have:

$$(R_1 \cap R_2) = \text{AuthorID} \rightarrow \text{AuthorName, Publisher} = R_2 - R_1$$

Since the intersection `AuthorID` functionally determines all attributes of $R_2$, the decomposition is **lossless**.

**Step 3: Verify Dependency Preservation (1 mark)**

- $R_1$ preserves: $\text{BookID} \rightarrow \text{Title, AuthorID, Price, ShelfNo}$
- $R_2$ preserves: $\text{AuthorID} \rightarrow \text{AuthorName, Publisher}$

All original FDs are preserved. Ō£ģ

**Step 4: Demonstrate anomaly elimination (1 mark)**

- **Insertion:** New author `(A99, Ruskin Bond, Penguin)` is inserted directly into $R_2$. Ō£ģ
- **Deletion:** Deleting a book from $R_1$ does not affect $R_2$. The author remains. Ō£ģ
- **Update:** Publisher change is a single UPDATE on $R_2$ where $\text{AuthorID} = \text{A99}$. Ō£ģ

---

### **Question B (14 Marks ŌĆö Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

**(a)** A university stores all data in a single relation:

`STUDENT_COURSE(SID, SName, DeptID, DeptName, CID, CName, Credits, Grade)`

Primary Key: `(SID, CID)`. Functional dependencies are:
- $\text{SID} \rightarrow \text{SName, DeptID, DeptName}$
- $\text{CID} \rightarrow \text{CName, Credits}$
- $\text{SID, CID} \rightarrow \text{Grade}$

**Identify the type of each FD and explain the deletion anomaly that can occur in this relation with an example.** **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Suggest a suitable decomposition to eliminate all anomalies. State the normal form achieved and justify your answer using the FDs. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Classify the FDs (2 marks)**

| FD | Type | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{SID} \rightarrow \text{SName, DeptID, DeptName}$ | **Partial Dependency** | SID is a part of the PK, not the full key |
| $\text{CID} \rightarrow \text{CName, Credits}$ | **Partial Dependency** | CID is a part of the PK, not the full key |
| $\text{SID, CID} \rightarrow \text{Grade}$ | **Full Dependency** | Both parts of the PK determine Grade |

**Step 2: Define the deletion anomaly (1 mark)**

A **deletion anomaly** occurs when removing a row causes the loss of an unrelated but valid fact stored in the same row.

**Step 3: Concrete example (3 marks)**

Consider the rows:

| SID | SName | DeptID | DeptName | CID | CName | Credits | Grade |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| S05 | Rahul | D02 | CSE | C01 | DBMS | 4 | A |
| S05 | Rahul | D02 | CSE | C07 | AI | 4 | O |

Suppose Rahul (S05) graduates and his two rows are deleted. If `C07 (AI)` had **only Rahul as its sole enroller**, then the database **loses the fact that the AI course exists with 4 credits**. The deletion of a student record has erased course information.

**Step 4: Conclude (1 mark)**

The anomaly arises because two distinct entity types (Student, Course) and a relationship (Enrollment) are stored in one relation. Decomposition is the cure.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Propose the decomposition (3 marks)**

$$R_1(\underline{\text{SID}}, \text{SName}, \text{DeptID}, \text{DeptName})$$
$$R_2(\underline{\text{CID}}, \text{CName}, \text{Credits})$$
$$R_3(\underline{\text{SID}, \text{CID}}, \text{Grade})$$

**Step 2: Verify normal form (2 marks)**

- $R_1$: PK is `SID`. All non-key attributes (`SName, DeptID, DeptName`) fully depend on the key. ŌåÆ **3NF** (and **BCNF**).
- $R_2$: PK is `CID`. `CName, Credits` fully depend on `CID`. ŌåÆ **3NF / BCNF**.
- $R_3$: PK is `(SID, CID)`. `Grade` fully depends on the composite key. ŌåÆ **3NF / BCNF**.

**Step 3: Justify using FDs (1 mark)**

In each decomposed relation, the left-hand side of every FD is a **superkey** of that relation. This satisfies the **BCNF condition**, which guarantees anomaly-freedom.

**Step 4: Verify Lossless Join (1 mark)**

- $R_1 \cap R_2 = \emptyset$ ŌĆö disjoint. We must rely on $R_3$ as the bridge.
- $R_1 \cap R_3 = \text{SID}$ ŌåÆ `SID` is a key of $R_1$ ŌćÆ Lossless.
- $R_2 \cap R_3 = \text{CID}$ ŌåÆ `CID` is a key of $R_2$ ŌćÆ Lossless.

The full decomposition is **lossless-join**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make (and where they lose marks):**
> 1. **Defining anomalies without an example** ŌĆö KTU examiners deduct 1ŌĆō2 marks if the answer is purely theoretical. *Always* pair the definition with a concrete row-level example.
> 2. **Confusing Deletion Anomaly with Cascading Delete** ŌĆö Deletion anomaly is the **unintended loss of unrelated data**, not a referential action.
> 3. **Forgetting to verify Lossless Join and Dependency Preservation** in decomposition questions ŌĆö These two checks are worth **1 mark each** in 7-mark sub-parts.
> 4. **Saying "an anomaly is an error"** ŌĆö An anomaly is **not** a runtime error; it is a **design flaw** that *may* cause errors.
> 5. **Stating only the anomaly name without explaining the FD root cause** ŌĆö Always tie the anomaly back to the offending FD (partial/transitive dependency).
> 6. **In decomposition questions, not specifying the new primary key** of each new relation ŌĆö Examiners expect `underline` or `PK = ...` notation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö "Anomalies in Database Design"**
>
> ŌśÉ Anomaly = unintended side-effect during `INSERT / DELETE / UPDATE` due to poor design.
>
> ŌśÉ Three types: **Insertion, Deletion, Update**.
>
> ŌśÉ Root cause: **Redundancy** caused by **partial** or **transitive** functional dependencies.
>
> ŌśÉ **Insertion Anomaly:** Cannot insert a fact because a related fact is missing (e.g., new course with no student).
>
> ŌśÉ **Deletion Anomaly:** Deleting a row loses an unrelated fact (e.g., last student of a course drops, course info lost).
>
> ŌśÉ **Update Anomaly:** Same value in N rows ŌåÆ must update N times ŌåÆ risk of inconsistency.
>
> ŌśÉ The cure is **decomposition** to **3NF / BCNF**, verified by **lossless join** and **dependency preservation**.
>
> ŌśÉ Lossless join test: $(R_1 \cap R_2) \rightarrow R_1$ or $(R_1 \cap R_2) \rightarrow R_2$.
>
> ŌśÉ Dependency preservation: $(F_1 \cup F_2 \cup \ldots \cup F_n)^{+} = F^{+}$.
>
> ŌśÉ For KTU 2024: Always answer with **(i) Definition, (ii) FD root cause, (iii) Concrete example, (iv) Decomposition cure** ŌĆö this is the examiner's expected 4-part structure.
>
> ŌśÉ A single table storing two entity types (e.g., Student + Course) **always** produces all three anomalies.
>
> ŌśÉ Remember: **No anomaly Ō¤╣ relation is in BCNF** (Boyce-Codd Normal Form).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Idea of Normalization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBEOT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** The Idea of Normalization # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Normalization** is a systematic, step-by-step, formal process of decomposing (splitting) a relation (table) into multiple smaller, well-structured relations in order to **minimize data redundancy** and **eliminate undesirable characteristics** such as insertion, update, and deletion anomalies, while simultaneously preserving the original information and maintaining the integrity of the database through **lossless joins** and **dependency preservation**. In the context of relational database design, normalization is a **mathematical formalism** rooted in the theory of **functional dependencies (FDs)**. It is governed by Edgar F. Codd's relational model rules and provides a series of progressive "normal forms" (1NF ŌåÆ 2NF ŌåÆ 3NF ŌåÆ BCNF ŌåÆ 4NF ŌåÆ 5NF) that serve as benchmarks for the "well-structuredness" of a relation schema. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö "The Family Filing Cabinet" Imagine a **single messy drawer** in a school office that holds *all* student information ŌĆö names, addresses, phone numbers, courses enrolled, and marks in each subject ŌĆö all on one giant paper sheet. | Problem in the Messy Drawer | Real Database Equivalent | |---|---| | If a student shifts to a new house, you must update the address on **every sheet** where their name appears | **Update Anomaly** | | If you can't enroll a student in a course until they take an exam, you can't record their enrollment | **Insertion Anomaly** | | If a student drops the only course they had, their entire record (name, address) gets wiped out | **Deletion Anomaly** | **Normalization is like reorganizing that drawer into separate, labeled folders** ŌĆö one folder per student, one per course, one per enrollment ŌĆö so that each piece of information lives in **exactly the right place**. Now, updating a house address means touching just one card, and you can enroll a student in a course *before* any exam is taken. > [!NOTE] > **The Golden Rule of Normalization:** A relation is considered *well-normalized* when **every non-key attribute depends on the key, the whole key, and nothing but the key** (Codd's famous dictum). ## 1.3 The Three Anomalies ŌĆö The "Why" of Normalization | Anomaly Type | What Goes Wrong | Root Cause | |---|---|---| | **Insertion Anomaly** | New data cannot be inserted without the presence of other unrelated data | A non-key attribute is part of a composite primary key that is NULL | | **Update Anomaly** | Same data repeated in multiple rows; updating one place but missing another causes inconsistency | Redundancy due to partial dependencies | | **Deletion Anomaly** | Deleting a row unintentionally removes other valuable, unrelated information | Loss of a fact bundled with another fact in the same tuple | ## 1.4 KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping > [!IMPORTANT] > Per the KTU 2024 Scheme (PBEOT504 - DBMS, Module 3), students are expected to: > 1. Define normalization and identify the anomalies it solves. > 2. Identify functional dependencies and compute closure of attribute sets and canonical cover. > 3. Apply 1NF, 2NF, 3NF, and BCNF to a given relation. > 4. Verify lossless-join and dependency-preserving properties of a decomposition. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Foundational Concepts ŌĆö The Building Blocks ### A. Functional Dependency (FD) A functional dependency, written as **X ŌåÆ Y**, means that for any two tuples $t_1$ and $t_2$ in a relation R: $$t_1[X] = t_2[X] \implies t_1[Y] = t_2[Y]$$ In plain English: **"X functionally determines Y"** ŌĆö knowing the value of X uniquely pins down the value of Y. - **X** is called the **determinant** (left-hand side). - **Y** is called the **dependent** (right-hand side). - If X is a **candidate key** of R, then X ŌåÆ R (i.e., X ŌåÆ all attributes). ### B. Types of Functional Dependencies | FD Type | Formal Definition | Real Example | |---|---|---| | **Trivial FD** | $Y \subseteq X$ in $X \to Y$ | $\{StudentID, Name\} \to Name$ | | **Non-Trivial FD** | $Y \not\subseteq X$ in $X \to Y$ | $\{StudentID\} \to Name$ | | **Partial Dependency** | A proper subset of a candidate key determines a non-prime attribute | $\{CourseID, Subject\} \to \text{Faculty Name}$ | | **Full Dependency** | The *entire* candidate key (not any proper subset) is needed to determine Y | $\{CourseID, SubjectID\} \to \text{Marks}$ | | **Transitive Dependency** | $X \to Y$ and $Y \to Z$, then $X \to Z$ (where $Y \to X$ does **not** hold) | $RollNo \to DeptID \to DeptName$ | | **Multivalued Dependency (MVD)** | $X \twoheadrightarrow Y$ : for a given X, a set of Y values is independent of other attributes | Student $\twoheadrightarrow$ Hobbies | ### C. Prime vs Non-Prime Attributes - **Prime Attribute:** An attribute that is a **member of *any* candidate key**. - **Non-Prime Attribute:** An attribute that is **not a member of any candidate key**. ## 2.2 The Normal Forms ŌĆö A Progressive Ladder > [!NOTE] > **Higher Normal Forms = Stricter Rules = Less Redundancy.** Each NF subsumes the previous one: a 3NF relation is automatically in 2NF and 1NF. ### 2.2.1 First Normal Form (1NF) - **Rule:** Every cell of the table must contain an **atomic (indivisible) value**. - **No multi-valued attributes, no repeating groups, no nested relations.** - *Example Violation:* A `PhoneNumbers` column holding "98765432, 91234567" ŌĆö must be split. ### 2.2.2 Second Normal Form (2NF) - **Prerequisite:** Must be in 1NF. - **Rule:** **No partial dependency** of a non-prime attribute on a candidate key. - *Remedy:* Remove partially-dependent attributes into a separate relation with a copy of the determinant. ### 2.2.3 Third Normal Form (3NF) - **Prerequisite:** Must be in 2NF. - **Rule:** **No transitive dependency** of non-prime attributes on a candidate key. - *Formal Condition (Codd's Test):* For every non-trivial FD $X \to A$, **at least one** of the following must hold: 1. $X$ is a **superkey** of R, OR 2. $A$ is a **prime attribute** (part of some candidate key). ### 2.2.4 Boyce-Codd Normal Form (BCNF) - **Prerequisite:** Must be in 3NF. - **Rule:** A stricter version of 3NF. For every non-trivial FD $X \to A$, **$X$ must be a superkey**. - *Key Difference from 3NF:* In BCNF, the "prime attribute escape clause" of 3NF is removed. Even if $A$ is prime, $X$ *must* be a superkey. ## 2.3 Properties of a Good Decomposition > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 examiners frequently ask: *"Is the decomposition valid?"* The answer must check **all three** of the following: | Property | Definition | Why It Matters | |---|---|---| | **Lossless Join (Non-Additive)** | Joining the decomposed relations back must give the **exact** original relation (no spurious tuples) | Guarantees no information loss or false data generation | | **Dependency Preservation** | All original FDs must be enforceable by checking **individual** decomposed relations (without computing a join) | Avoids expensive joins for every constraint check | | **No Redundancy (Minimal Relations)** | Data should not be unnecessarily duplicated | Saves storage and avoids anomalies | ### The Lossless-Join Test (for Binary Decomposition R into R1 and R2) $$R_1 \cap R_2 \to R_1 \quad \text{OR} \quad R_1 \cap R_2 \to R_2$$ If **either** holds, the decomposition is **lossless**. Formally, the common attributes must be a key for at least one of the decomposed relations. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Functional Dependency | $X \to Y$ | $Y$ uniquely determined by $X$ | | 2 | Armstrong's Reflexivity | If $Y \subseteq X$, then $X \to Y$ | Trivial FD | | 3 | Armstrong's Augmentation | If $X \to Y$, then $XZ \to YZ$ | Adds attributes on both sides | | 4 | Armstrong's Transitivity | If $X \to Y$ and $Y \to Z$, then $X \to Z$ | Chain of dependencies | | 5 | Union Rule | If $X \to Y$ and $X \to Z$, then $X \to YZ$ | Derived from Augmentation + Transitivity | | 6 | Decomposition / Project | If $X \to YZ$, then $X \to Y$ and $X \to Z$ | Reverse of Union | | 7 | Pseudotransitivity | If $X \to Y$ and $YW \to Z$, then $XW \to Z$ | Extended transitivity | | 8 | 1NF Test | All attributes atomic | No multi-valued, no repeating groups | | 9 | 2NF Test | No partial dependency of non-prime on CK | Every non-prime attr depends on full CK | | 10 | 3NF Test (Codd) | $\forall X \to A$: $X$ is superkey **OR** $A$ is prime | "Prime escape clause" allowed | | 11 | BCNF Test | $\forall X \to A$ (non-trivial): $X$ is superkey | No escape clause | | 12 | Lossless Join (Binary) | $R_1 \cap R_2 \to R_1$ or $R_1 \cap R_2 \to R_2$ | Common attrs must be a key of one side | | 13 | Dependency Preservation | $(F_1 \cup F_2 \cup \dots \cup F_n)^+ = F^+$ | All FDs derivable from local FDs | | 14 | Attribute Closure | $X^+ = \{A \mid X \to A \text{ is derivable from } F\}$ | Computed using Armstrong's axioms | ## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering - **Banking Systems:** Normalization prevents the same customer address from being stored 50 times across 50 account records, which would otherwise cause critical update anomalies in KYC (Know Your Customer) compliance. - **E-Commerce Platforms:** Product catalog and order-line-item separation (3NF) enables fast product catalog updates without locking the order processing pipeline. - **Healthcare HIS:** PatientŌĆōDiagnosisŌĆōPrescription tri-partition avoids loss of patient demographic data when a single prescription is deleted. - **Telecom Billing:** CDR (Call Detail Record) normalization across 3NF/BCNF enables efficient aggregation queries for monthly billing. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Canonical Worked Example ŌĆö The "Student-Course" Relation Let us work through a single, comprehensive example that will appear in every KTU module test. We begin with a single unnormalized relation. ### Given Relation Schema $$\text{STUDENT\_COURSE}(\underline{\text{StudentID}}, \underline{\text{CourseID}}, \text{StudentName}, \text{DeptID}, \text{DeptName}, \text{CourseName}, \text{Credits}, \text{Grade})$$ ### Functional Dependency Set $F$ $$ F = \{\, \text{StudentID} \to \text{StudentName, DeptID},\quad \text{DeptID} \to \text{DeptName},\quad \text{CourseID} \to \text{CourseName, Credits},\quad \text{(StudentID, CourseID)} \to \text{Grade} \,\} $$ ### Step 1 ŌĆö Find Candidate Keys We use the **attribute closure algorithm**. Start by computing the closure of the composite key $(StudentID, CourseID)^+$. $$ \begin{aligned} (StudentID, CourseID)^+ &= \{StudentID, CourseID\} \\ &\cup \{StudentName, DeptID\} \quad &\text{(by FD1)} \\ &\cup \{DeptName\} \quad &\text{(by FD2, since DeptID is now in closure)} \\ &\cup \{CourseName, Credits\} \quad &\text{(by FD3)} \\ &\cup \{Grade\} \quad &\text{(by FD4)} \end{aligned} $$ The closure includes **all** attributes, so **(StudentID, CourseID)** is a **candidate key**. The only other candidate key attempt would fail because no single attribute determines everything. > [!NOTE] > **Prime attributes:** StudentID, CourseID > **Non-prime attributes:** StudentName, DeptID, DeptName, CourseName, Credits, Grade ### Step 2 ŌĆö Check 1NF (Atomicity) Assume all listed attributes are atomic. The relation **is in 1NF**. ### Step 3 ŌĆö Check 2NF (No Partial Dependencies) Inspect each FD against the composite key **(StudentID, CourseID)**: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{StudentID} &\to \text{StudentName, DeptID} \quad &\text{PARTIAL (StudentID alone, not full key)} \\ \text{DeptID} &\to \text{DeptName} \quad &\text{Indirect partial via StudentID} \\ \text{CourseID} &\to \text{CourseName, Credits} \quad &\text{PARTIAL (CourseID alone)} \\ (\text{StudentID, CourseID}) &\to \text{Grade} \quad &\text{FULL (good)} \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Partial dependencies detected!** The relation **violates 2NF**. ### Step 4 ŌĆö Decompose to 2NF Split STUDENT_COURSE into three relations to eliminate partial dependencies: | Relation | Attributes | Key | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | **STUDENT** | (StudentID, StudentName, DeptID) | StudentID | Remove partial dep on StudentID | | **DEPARTMENT** | (DeptID, DeptName) | DeptID | Eliminate transitive dep of DeptName | | **COURSE** | (CourseID, CourseName, Credits) | CourseID | Remove partial dep on CourseID | | **ENROLLMENT** | (StudentID, CourseID, Grade) | (StudentID, CourseID) | Retains only the full key + Grade | **Decomposition:** $R = \{STUDENT, DEPARTMENT, COURSE, ENROLLMENT\}$ ### Step 5 ŌĆö Verify Lossless-Join (Binary Test for each split) For the split **STUDENT_COURSE ŌåÆ (STUDENT, ENROLLMENT)**: $$ \begin{aligned} STUDENT \cap ENROLLMENT &= \{StudentID\} \\ STUDENT \cap ENROLLMENT \to STUDENT &= StudentID \to \text{(StudentID, StudentName, DeptID)} \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ > Since $StudentID$ is a key of STUDENT, the decomposition is **lossless**. Similar checks hold for the other splits. ### Step 6 ŌĆö Check 3NF (No Transitive Dependencies on Non-Prime Attributes) Consider the **STUDENT** relation: - FD: $\text{StudentID} \to \text{DeptID} \to \text{DeptName}$ - **StudentID is a superkey** ŌåÆ this FD is allowed in 3NF. - However, **DeptName transitively depends on StudentID via DeptID**. Since DeptID is a non-key attribute in STUDENT, this is a **transitive dependency**. > [!WARNING] > **Transitive dependency detected in STUDENT!** The relation **violates 3NF**. ### Step 7 ŌĆö Decompose to 3NF We already split DEPARTMENT out in Step 4. So: - **STUDENT** = (StudentID, StudentName, DeptID) ŌĆö now in 3NF (StudentID is the only determinant). - **DEPARTMENT** = (DeptID, DeptName) ŌĆö in 3NF. - **COURSE** = (CourseID, CourseName, Credits) ŌĆö in 3NF. - **ENROLLMENT** = (StudentID, CourseID, Grade) ŌĆö in 3NF. > [!NOTE] > All four relations are now in **3NF**. ### Step 8 ŌĆö Check BCNF For each relation, every non-trivial FD's left-hand side must be a superkey. | Relation | Non-trivial FD | Is LHS a Superkey? | BCNF? | |---|---|---|---| | STUDENT | StudentID ŌåÆ StudentName, DeptID | **Yes** | Ō£ģ | | DEPARTMENT | DeptID ŌåÆ DeptName | **Yes** | Ō£ģ | | COURSE | CourseID ŌåÆ CourseName, Credits | **Yes** | Ō£ģ | | ENROLLMENT | (StudentID, CourseID) ŌåÆ Grade | **Yes** | Ō£ģ | > [!IMPORTANT] > All relations are in **BCNF**. The decomposition is complete and optimal. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö FDs vs Normal Forms This is a **high-yield** summary table for KTU 14-mark questions. | FD / Anomaly Type | 1NF | 2NF | 3NF | BCNF | |---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | Multi-valued attributes | ŌØī Not allowed | ŌØī | ŌØī | ŌØī | | Repeating groups | ŌØī Not allowed | ŌØī | ŌØī | ŌØī | | Partial dependency (non-prime) | Allowed | ŌØī Not allowed | ŌØī | ŌØī | | Transitive dependency (non-prime) | Allowed | Allowed | ŌØī Not allowed | ŌØī | | FD where LHS is non-superkey + RHS prime | Allowed | Allowed | Allowed | ŌØī Not allowed | ## 3.3 The Algorithm ŌĆö Going from R to 3NF (Synthesis) This is a KTU-favorite 14-mark algorithm question. 1. **Find a minimal cover $F_c$** of F (use the 3-step process: right-singleton, remove extraneous LHS attrs, remove redundant FDs). 2. **For each FD** $X \to Y$ in $F_c$, create a relation schema $R_i = XY$. 3. **If none** of the $R_i$ contains a candidate key of the original R, **add a relation** containing the candidate key attributes. 4. **Eliminate redundant relations** (any $R_j$ that is a subset of another $R_i$). 5. **Result** is a dependency-preserving, lossless-join decomposition in 3NF. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Normalization Ladder (Progressive Refinement) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Unnormalized Relation
Repeating groups, multi-valued attrs]:::unnorm -->|Atomicize cells| B[1NF
First Normal Form] B -->|Remove partial dependencies| C[2NF
Second Normal Form] C -->|Remove transitive dependencies| D[3NF
Third Normal Form] D -->|Enforce every LHS is a superkey| E[BCNF
Boyce-Codd Normal Form] E -->|Remove non-trivial MVDs| F[4NF
Fourth Normal Form] F -->|Remove remaining join dependencies| G[5NF / PJNF
Project-Join Normal Form] classDef unnorm fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef n1 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cca500,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef n2 fill:#d9f2d9,stroke:#339933,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef n3 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef bcnf fill:#e0ccff,stroke:#6600cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef n4 fill:#ffccf2,stroke:#cc0066,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef n5 fill:#cccccc,stroke:#333333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 The "Idea" of Normalization ŌĆö Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT[INPUT LAYER] R0[Raw Single Table
with Anomalies] end subgraph ENGINE[NORMALIZATION ENGINE] FD1[Step 1: Identify FDs] FD2[Step 2: Find Candidate Keys] FD3[Step 3: Compute Attribute Closures] FD4[Step 4: Detect Anomalies] DEC[Step 5: Decompose Relation] VFD1{Step 6: Verify
Lossless Join?} VFD2{Step 7: Verify
Dependency Preserved?} NF1[Step 8: Classify Normal Form] end subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT LAYER] O1[Decomposed 1NF Relations] O2[Decomposed 2NF Relations] O3[Decomposed 3NF Relations] O4[BCNF Relations] end R0 --> FD1 --> FD2 --> FD3 --> FD4 --> DEC DEC --> VFD1 VFD1 -- Yes --> VFD2 VFD1 -- No --> FD1 VFD2 -- Yes --> NF1 VFD2 -- No --> DEC NF1 --> O1 O1 --> O2 O2 --> O3 O3 --> O4 classDef inp fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef eng fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef out fill:#d9f2d9,stroke:#339933,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class R0 inp class FD1,FD2,FD3,FD4,DEC,VFD1,VFD2,NF1 eng class O1,O2,O3,O4 out ``` ## 4.3 Anomaly Classification Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Database Anomalies]:::root --> B[Insertion Anomaly]:::ins A --> C[Update Anomaly]:::upd A --> D[Deletion Anomaly]:::del B --> B1[Cannot add new data
without unrelated info] C --> C1[Same data in many rows
Inconsistency on partial update] D --> D1[Removing one fact
erases another fact] classDef root fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef ins fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cca500,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef upd fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef del fill:#e0ccff,stroke:#6600cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef b1 fill:#fff9e6,stroke:#cca500,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef c1 fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef d1 fill:#f3e6ff,stroke:#6600cc,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 class B1 b1 class C1 c1 class D1 d1 ``` ## 4.4 Decomposition Properties Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD DEC[Perform Decomposition
of R into R1, R2, ..., Rn]:::start DEC --> Q1{R1 Ōł® R2 common
attrs form a key
for at least one Ri?} Q1 -- Yes --> LJ[Lossless Join
VERIFIED] Q1 -- No --> Q2{Use Chase Test
for n-ary case} Q2 -- Empty row --> LJ Q2 -- No empty row --> NOLJ[NOT Lossless] DEC --> Q3{All FDs in F+
derivable from
F1 Ōł¬ F2 Ōł¬ ... Ōł¬ Fn?} Q3 -- Yes --> DP[Dependency
Preservation VERIFIED] Q3 -- No --> NODP[Dependency
Preservation FAILED] LJ --> RESULT[Valid Decomposition] DP --> RESULT classDef start fill:#d9f2d9,stroke:#339933,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef lj fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef dp fill:#e0ccff,stroke:#6600cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef nolj fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef nodp fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef result fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cca500,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 class DEC start class LJ lj class DP dp class NOLJ nolj class NODP nodp class RESULT result ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Define normalization. Explain the three types of anomalies it aims to eliminate.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Normalization is the formal process of organizing a relational database by decomposing large, redundant tables into smaller, well-structured ones to minimize data redundancy and eliminate anomalies. **Three Anomalies:** 1. **Insertion Anomaly:** A new record cannot be inserted into a table without including other unrelated data. *Example:* Unable to add a new department that has no students yet. 2. **Update Anomaly:** The same data item is duplicated in multiple rows; updating one row but missing others causes data inconsistency. *Example:* A student's address stored in 5 rows ŌĆö updating only 4 leaves one outdated record. 3. **Deletion Anomaly:** Deleting one fact inadvertently removes another fact. *Example:* Deleting the last enrollment of a student removes their entire existence from the database. > [!NOTE] > **Marking Tip:** Award 1 mark for the definition and 1 mark each for the three anomalies with brief examples. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Differentiate between 3NF and BCNF with a suitable example.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | 3NF | BCNF | |---|---|---| | **Formal Rule** | For every non-trivial FD $X \to A$: X is a superkey **OR** A is a prime attribute | For every non-trivial FD $X \to A$: X **must** be a superkey | | **Prime Attribute Exception** | Allowed (escape clause) | **Not** allowed | | **Strictness** | More relaxed | Stricter subset of 3NF | **Example Relation:** R(A, B, C) with candidate keys {AB, AC} and FD $B \to C$. - In 3NF: The FD $B \to C$ is allowed because C is a prime attribute (part of key AC). - In BCNF: $B$ is **not** a superkey (B alone cannot determine A), so the relation **violates BCNF**. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often write "BCNF has no transitive dependencies" ŌĆö this is wrong. BCNF is stricter than 3NF, not just about transitivity. The precise differentiator is the *prime-attribute escape clause*. --- ## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > Per the KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3 carries a 14-mark Part B question with internal choice. Both alternatives below are fully solved. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** > Consider the following relation: > > **R(EmpID, ProjectID, EmpName, DeptID, DeptName, ProjectName, HoursWorked)** > > with functional dependencies: > > - $F_1$: EmpID ŌåÆ EmpName, DeptID > - $F_2$: DeptID ŌåÆ DeptName > - $F_3$: ProjectID ŌåÆ ProjectName > - $F_4$: (EmpID, ProjectID) ŌåÆ HoursWorked > > **(a)** Identify the candidate key(s), prime, and non-prime attributes. (7 marks) > **(b)** Normalize the relation up to BCNF. Verify that your decomposition is lossless-join and dependency-preserving. (7 marks) #### **Solution to (a):** **Step 1 ŌĆö Find Candidate Key using attribute closure:** Compute $(\text{EmpID, ProjectID})^+$: $$ \begin{aligned} \{EmpID, ProjectID\}^+ &= \{EmpID, ProjectID\} \\ &\cup \{EmpName, DeptID\} \quad &\text{(by } F_1\text{)} \\ &\cup \{DeptName\} \quad &\text{(by } F_2\text{)} \\ &\cup \{ProjectName\} \quad &\text{(by } F_3\text{)} \\ &\cup \{HoursWorked\} \quad &\text{(by } F_4\text{)} \end{aligned} $$ The closure contains **all 7 attributes**, so **(EmpID, ProjectID)** is a **candidate key**. Trying any proper subset fails to derive all attributes, so this is the **only candidate key**. **Step 2 ŌĆö Classify attributes:** - **Prime attributes:** EmpID, ProjectID - **Non-prime attributes:** EmpName, DeptID, DeptName, ProjectName, HoursWorked #### **Solution to (b):** **Step 1 ŌĆö Check 1NF:** Assume all attributes are atomic ŌåÆ in 1NF. **Step 2 ŌĆö Check 2NF (Partial Dependencies):** | FD | Partial? | |---|---| | EmpID ŌåÆ EmpName, DeptID | **Yes** (EmpID alone, not full key) | | DeptID ŌåÆ DeptName | **Yes** (via EmpID) | | ProjectID ŌåÆ ProjectName | **Yes** (ProjectID alone) | | (EmpID, ProjectID) ŌåÆ HoursWorked | No (full key) | **Relation violates 2NF.** Decompose: | Relation | Attributes | Key | |---|---|---| | **EMP** | (EmpID, EmpName, DeptID) | EmpID | | **DEPT** | (DeptID, DeptName) | DeptID | | **PROJ** | (ProjectID, ProjectName) | ProjectID | | **WORK** | (EmpID, ProjectID, HoursWorked) | (EmpID, ProjectID) | **Step 3 ŌĆö Check 3NF (Transitive Dependencies in EMP):** In **EMP**: $\text{EmpID} \to \text{DeptID} \to \text{DeptName}$ - After DEPT is split out, EMP no longer contains DeptName. - In EMP, the only FD is $\text{EmpID} \to \text{EmpName, DeptID}$, where EmpID is a superkey. Ō£ģ All four relations are in **3NF**. **Step 4 ŌĆö Check BCNF:** | Relation | FD | LHS Superkey? | BCNF? | |---|---|---|---| | EMP | EmpID ŌåÆ EmpName, DeptID | Yes | Ō£ģ | | DEPT | DeptID ŌåÆ DeptName | Yes | Ō£ģ | | PROJ | ProjectID ŌåÆ ProjectName | Yes | Ō£ģ | | WORK | (EmpID, ProjectID) ŌåÆ HoursWorked | Yes | Ō£ģ | **All relations are in BCNF.** **Step 5 ŌĆö Verify Lossless-Join (Binary Test):** For split R ŌåÆ (EMP, WORK): $EMP \cap WORK = \{EmpID\}$, and EmpID is a key of EMP. Ō£ģ **Lossless.** For split EMP ŌåÆ (EMP, DEPT): $EMP \cap DEPT = \{DeptID\}$, and DeptID is a key of DEPT. Ō£ģ **Lossless.** All other splits are similarly lossless by the same logic. **Step 6 ŌĆö Verify Dependency Preservation:** Each original FD can be checked within a single relation: - $F_1$ (EmpID ŌåÆ EmpName, DeptID) ŌĆö checked in EMP Ō£ģ - $F_2$ (DeptID ŌåÆ DeptName) ŌĆö checked in DEPT Ō£ģ - $F_3$ (ProjectID ŌåÆ ProjectName) ŌĆö checked in PROJ Ō£ģ - $F_4$ (EmpID, ProjectID ŌåÆ HoursWorked) ŌĆö checked in WORK Ō£ģ **Decomposition is dependency-preserving.** > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students forget to **prove both lossless AND dependency-preserving** in the 7-mark subpart. KTU explicitly awards 2 marks for each verification. Do not skip! --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** > Consider the relation **R(A, B, C, D, E)** with the following functional dependencies: > > - $A \to B$ > - $BC \to D$ > - $D \to E$ > - $C \to A$ > > **(a)** Compute the canonical cover $F_c$ of F. (7 marks) > **(b)** Using the 3NF synthesis algorithm, obtain a lossless and dependency-preserving decomposition. (7 marks) #### **Solution to (a):** **Step 1 ŌĆö Make RHS a singleton attribute (already satisfied):** $$ F = \{A \to B,\ BC \to D,\ D \to E,\ C \to A\} $$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Remove extraneous attributes from LHS:** For $BC \to D$: Is $B$ extraneous? Compute $C^+$ with only $C \to A$: $$ C^+ = \{C, A\} \quad \text{(then } A \to B \text{ is not in original F)} $$ So $\{A, B\}$ not reachable. $B$ is **not** extraneous. - $C^+ = \{C, A, B\}$ when we also use $A \to B$ ŌĆö wait, this is dependency on a reduced set. The proper test uses **only the FDs whose LHS is a subset of $C$** (i.e., only $C \to A$). - So $C^+ = \{C, A\}$. Since $D \notin C^+$, $B$ **is** extraneous. **Reduced FD:** $C \to D$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Remove redundant FDs:** Test $C \to D$: Can $D$ be derived from $F - \{C \to D\}$? - $C \to A$ (given), $A \to B$ (given), $BC \to D$ removed; but $D \to E$ doesn't help reach D. - $C^+ = \{C, A, B\}$. D not in closure. So $C \to D$ is **not redundant**. Keep it. Test $D \to E$: $D^+ = \{D, E\}$. E in closure. To check redundancy, compute $D^+$ without $D \to E$: $D^+ = \{D\}$. E not in closure. **Not redundant**. Keep it. Test $C \to A$: $C^+ = \{C, A, B, D, E\}$ (using all). Without $C \to A$: $C^+ = \{C\}$. A not in closure. **Not redundant**. Keep it. Test $A \to B$: $A^+ = \{A, B\}$. Without it: $A^+ = \{A\}$. B not in closure. **Not redundant**. Keep it. **Canonical Cover $F_c$:** $$ F_c = \{A \to B,\quad C \to D,\quad D \to E,\quad C \to A\} $$ > [!NOTE] > We can further combine the two C-relations: since $C \to A$ and $C \to D$, by the **union rule**, $C \to AD$. So $F_c = \{A \to B,\ C \to AD,\ D \to E\}$. #### **Solution to (b):** **3NF Synthesis Algorithm:** **Step 1 ŌĆö For each FD in $F_c$, create a relation:** | FD | Relation | |---|---| | $A \to B$ | $R_1(A, B)$ | | $C \to AD$ | $R_2(C, A, D)$ | | $D \to E$ | $R_3(D, E)$ | **Step 2 ŌĆö Find candidate key of R:** Compute $C^+ = \{C, A, D, B, E\}$ ŌåÆ $C$ alone is a **candidate key**. **Step 3 ŌĆö Is candidate key C included in any $R_i$?** - $R_2$ contains C Ō£ģ - No need to add a separate key relation. **Step 4 ŌĆö Remove redundant relations (any $R_j \subseteq R_i$):** - $R_1 = (A, B)$: Is A a subset of any $R_i$? $A \in R_2$. But $B \notin R_2$. So $R_1 \not\subseteq R_2$. Keep. - $R_3 = (D, E)$: $D \in R_2$ but $E \notin R_2$. Keep. **Final 3NF Decomposition:** $$ \{R_1(A, B),\quad R_2(C, A, D),\quad R_3(D, E)\} $$ **Lossless-Join Verification:** - $R_2 \cap R_1 = \{A\}$, and $A \to B$ (key of $R_1$) Ō£ģ Lossless - $R_2 \cap R_3 = \{D\}$, and $D \to E$ (key of $R_3$) Ō£ģ Lossless **Dependency Preservation Verification:** - $A \to B$ in $R_1$ Ō£ģ - $C \to AD$ in $R_2$ Ō£ģ - $D \to E$ in $R_3$ Ō£ģ **All original FDs preserved without joins.** Ō£ģ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Common 2-Mark Loss):** Students often forget to **verify candidate key inclusion** in Step 2 of the synthesis algorithm. If $C$ had not appeared in any $R_i$, you must add $R_4 = (C)$, and omitting this is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Memorize Before Exam Day:** - Ō£ģ **Normalization = Decompose + Preserve Information + Reduce Anomalies.** - Ō£ģ The **three anomalies** are **Insertion, Update, Deletion** ŌĆö know a real example for each. - Ō£ģ **1NF:** Atomicity. No multi-valued, no repeating groups. - Ō£ģ **2NF:** 1NF + **No partial dependencies** of non-prime attributes on a composite key. - Ō£ģ **3NF:** 2NF + **No transitive dependencies** of non-prime attributes. - Ō£ģ **BCNF:** For every non-trivial FD, **LHS must be a superkey**. No escape clause. - Ō£ģ **Lossless Join Test (Binary):** $R_1 \cap R_2 \to R_1$ OR $R_1 \cap R_2 \to R_2$. - Ō£ģ **Dependency Preservation:** Every original FD must be checkable in **one** decomposed relation. - Ō£ģ **Armstrong's Axioms:** Reflexivity, Augmentation, Transitivity ŌĆö derive all other rules (Union, Decomposition, Pseudotransitivity). - Ō£ģ **Canonical Cover** is obtained by: (1) RHS singleton, (2) Remove extraneous LHS, (3) Remove redundant FDs. - Ō£ģ **Prime attribute** = belongs to **any** candidate key (not just the primary key). - Ō£ģ **3NF Synthesis Algorithm** = 5 steps; do not skip the "add candidate key" step. - Ō£ģ **BCNF is stricter than 3NF**; 3NF is stricter than 2NF; every higher NF implies all lower NFs. - Ō£ģ **4NF** removes non-trivial MVDs; **5NF (PJNF)** removes join dependencies. - Ō£ģ **Codd's Dictum:** "The key, the whole key, and nothing but the key." - Ō£ģ In KTU answers, **always show closure computations** and **lossless-join verification explicitly** ŌĆö never just assert them. - Ō£ģ Use $\to$ for FD, $\twoheadrightarrow$ for MVD, and $\subseteq$ for set inclusion in LaTeX-formatted answers.

topics.title: Functional Dependency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Functional Dependency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, a **Functional Dependency (FD)** is formally defined as a constraint that exists between two sets of attributes ŌĆö say **X** and **Y** ŌĆö belonging to a relation schema **R**. We denote this dependency as:

$$X \rightarrow Y$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Standard Formulation):**
> A functional dependency $X \rightarrow Y$ holds on relation schema $R$ if, for **every legal relation instance** $r(R)$, the following condition is satisfied:
> *For any two tuples $t_1$ and $t_2$ in $r$, if $t_1[X] = t_2[X]$, then it must follow that $t_1[Y] = t_2[Y]$.*

In simpler engineering terms, the value(s) of attribute set **X** *uniquely determine* the value(s) of attribute set **Y**. **X** is called the **determinant** (or LHS ŌĆö Left Hand Side), and **Y** is called the **dependent** (or RHS ŌĆö Right Hand Side).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a **Functional Dependency** exactly like a mathematical function you studied in school:

$$f(x) = y$$

If you input the same value of $x$ twice, you will always get the same value of $y$. Functional dependencies behave identically at the database level.

**Example:** Consider the `STUDENT` table:

| Roll_No | Name | DOB | Dept |
|---------|-----------|------------|--------|
| KTU001 | Anjali | 2003-05-12 | CSE |
| KTU002 | Rahul | 2003-08-21 | ECE |
| KTU003 | Anjali | 2003-11-04 | MECH |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** If you tell me the **Roll_No**, I can always tell you the **Name**, **DOB**, and **Dept** with **100% certainty**. Hence:
> - `Roll_No ŌåÆ Name`
> - `Roll_No ŌåÆ DOB`
> - `Roll_No ŌåÆ Name, DOB, Dept`

However, the reverse is **not true**! Knowing the `Name = Anjali` does **not** tell you the `Roll_No`, because two students can share the same name. So **`Name ŌåÆ Roll_No` is FALSE**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology for KTU 2024

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Essential Terms You Must Memorize:**
> - **Super Key** ŌĆö A set of attributes that uniquely identifies a tuple. If $K \rightarrow$ all attributes of $R$, then $K$ is a super key.
> - **Candidate Key** ŌĆö A **minimal** super key. Removing any attribute destroys uniqueness.
> - **Prime Attribute** ŌĆö An attribute that is a member of **some** candidate key.
> - **Non-Prime Attribute** ŌĆö An attribute that is **not** a member of any candidate key.

## 1.4 Categories of Functional Dependencies

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification by Membership of RHS in LHS:**

**1. Trivial FD:** $X \rightarrow Y$ where $Y \subseteq X$
- *Example:* `{RollNo, Name} ŌåÆ Name` (Name is already part of LHS)
- Always true. Trivially satisfied.

**2. Non-Trivial FD:** $X \rightarrow Y$ where $Y \not\subseteq X$
- *Example:* `RollNo ŌåÆ Name` (Name is NOT in the LHS)
- This is the type we genuinely care about for design.

**3. Completely Non-Trivial FD:** $X \rightarrow Y$ where $X \cap Y = \emptyset$
- *Example:* `RollNo ŌåÆ Name` (no overlap whatsoever).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Armstrong's Axioms ŌĆö The Foundation of FD Reasoning

Armstrong's Axioms are a set of **sound** (generate only valid FDs) and **complete** (generate all valid FDs) inference rules used to derive new functional dependencies from a given set.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Primary Axioms (Kear-Gold Standard for KTU Exams):**

| Axiom | Formal Rule | Intuitive Meaning |
|-------|------------|-------------------|
| **Reflexivity (A1)** | If $Y \subseteq X$, then $X \rightarrow Y$ | A superset always implies any of its subsets |
| **Augmentation (A2)** | If $X \rightarrow Y$, then $XZ \rightarrow YZ$ for any $Z$ | Adding the same attributes to both sides preserves the FD |
| **Transitivity (A3)** | If $X \rightarrow Y$ and $Y \rightarrow Z$, then $X \rightarrow Z$ | Dependencies chain together like a relay race |

## 2.2 Secondary Derived Inference Rules

These are **proven by combining Armstrong's axioms** and are extremely useful in solving KTU numerical problems.

| Derived Rule | Statement | Why It Works |
|--------------|-----------|--------------|
| **Union / Additivity** | If $X \rightarrow Y$ and $X \rightarrow Z$, then $X \rightarrow YZ$ | Augment $X \rightarrow Y$ with $Z$, then use decomposition. |
| **Decomposition / Projection** | If $X \rightarrow YZ$, then $X \rightarrow Y$ and $X \rightarrow Z$ | Apply Reflexivity to subsets. |
| **Pseudotransitivity** | If $X \rightarrow Y$ and $WY \rightarrow Z$, then $WX \rightarrow Z$ | Augment first FD, then apply Transitivity. |
| **Composition** | If $X \rightarrow Y$ and $Z \rightarrow W$, then $XZ \rightarrow YW$ | Augment and apply Transitivity. |

## 2.3 The Concept of Closure ŌĆö KTU's Most-Tested Sub-Topic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Critical Closure Concepts:**

**1. Closure of a Set of FDs ($F^+$):**
The complete set of all FDs that can be **logically derived** from a given set $F$ using Armstrong's axioms. Mathematically:

$$F^+ = \{X \rightarrow Y \mid F \models X \rightarrow Y\}$$

**2. Closure of an Attribute Set ($X^+$):**
The complete set of all attributes that are functionally determined by attribute set $X$ under $F$. Formally:

$$X^+ = \{A \mid X \rightarrow A \text{ is derivable from } F\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is $X^+$ so important for KTU exams?**
> It is the **direct, computable method** to verify whether a set $X$ is a super key: simply check if $X^+ = R$ (i.e., $X^+$ contains every attribute of the relation). Computing $F^+$ explicitly is **exponentially hard** (size can be $2^{2^n}$), but computing $X^+$ is linear and tractable.

## 2.4 Algorithm: Computing Attribute Closure $X^+$

**Step-by-step KTU Procedure:**
1. Initialize the result set: $\text{result} = X$
2. **Repeat** for every FD $A \rightarrow B$ in $F$:
   - If $A \subseteq \text{result}$ (i.e., all attributes of LHS are already in `result`),
   - Then add all attributes of $B$ to `result` (i.e., $\text{result} = \text{result} \cup B$)
3. **Stop** when no new attribute can be added to `result` in a complete pass.
4. The final `result` is $X^+$.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these relationships for KTU 2024 board exams:**

| Concept | Mathematical Condition | Interpretation |
|---------|------------------------|----------------|
| $X$ is a **Super Key** | $X^+ \supseteq R$ | $X$'s closure contains all of $R$ |
| $X$ is a **Candidate Key** | $X^+ \supseteq R$ **AND** no proper subset of $X$ has this property | Minimality holds |
| $X \rightarrow Y$ is in $F^+$ | $Y \subseteq X^+$ | Definitive test for FD membership |
| **Minimal Cover Exists** | When $F$ does not contain redundant FDs or extraneous LHS attributes | After canonical reduction |

## 2.6 Engineering Utility

In real-world engineering, functional dependencies are the **mathematical backbone** of:
- **Database Normalization** (1NF, 2NF, 3NF, BCNF) ŌĆö Module 4 of KTU syllabus
- **Schema Refinement** ŌĆö Eliminating update/insertion/deletion anomalies
- **Data Integrity Constraints** ŌĆö Enforcing business rules at the DB engine level
- **Query Optimization** ŌĆö Determining which indexes are functionally sufficient
- **Lossless Join Decomposition** ŌĆö Proving decompositions preserve information

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 ŌĆö Attribute Closure Computation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (Standard KTU Style):**
> Given a relation $R(A, B, C, D, E, G)$ and a set of functional dependencies:
> $$F = \{A \rightarrow B, \;\; A \rightarrow C, \;\; CG \rightarrow D, \;\; B \rightarrow E\}$$
> Compute the attribute closure of $AG$, i.e., $(AG)^+$.

**Solution ŌĆö Exhaustive Step-by-Step Walkthrough:**

**Initialization:**
$$\text{result} = \{A, G\}$$

**Iteration 1 ŌĆö Apply all FDs whose LHS is fully contained in current result:**

- **FD1:** $A \rightarrow B$. Since $A \in \text{result}$, we can fire this FD.
  $$\text{result} = \{A, G\} \cup \{B\} = \{A, B, G\}$$

- **FD2:** $A \rightarrow C$. Since $A \in \text{result}$, we can fire this FD.
  $$\text{result} = \{A, B, G\} \cup \{C\} = \{A, B, C, G\}$$

- **FD3:** $CG \rightarrow D$. Requires $C \in \text{result}$ AND $G \in \text{result}$. Yes, both are present now.
  $$\text{result} = \{A, B, C, G\} \cup \{D\} = \{A, B, C, D, G\}$$

- **FD4:** $B \rightarrow E$. Since $B \in \text{result}$, we can fire this FD.
  $$\text{result} = \{A, B, C, D, G\} \cup \{E\} = \{A, B, C, D, E, G\}$$

**Termination Check:**
After one full pass, the result contains: $\{A, B, C, D, E, G\}$ which equals **all attributes of $R$**.

**Iteration 2 (Verification):** No new attribute can be added. We stop.

**Final Conclusion:**
$$(AG)^+ = \{A, B, C, D, E, G\} = R$$

Since $(AG)^+ = R$, the attribute set $AG$ is a **super key**. To check if it is a **candidate key**, we must test its proper subsets:
- $A^+ = \{A, B, C, E\}$ (does NOT contain $R$ ŌĆö apply $A \rightarrow B, C$ and $B \rightarrow E$, but no way to get $D$ or $G$)
- $G^+ = \{G\}$ (no FD has $G$ alone on LHS)

So neither $A$ nor $G$ alone is a super key. Therefore, **$AG$ is a candidate key**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 ŌĆö Computing Minimal Cover (Canonical Cover)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> Given $R(A, B, C, D)$ and $F = \{A \rightarrow BC, \; B \rightarrow C, \; AB \rightarrow D, \; A \rightarrow B\}$
> Find the **minimal cover** $F_c$.

**Step 1: Make RHS of every FD a singleton** (using decomposition rule)

Original $F$:
- $A \rightarrow BC$ (RHS has two attributes)
- $B \rightarrow C$
- $AB \rightarrow D$
- $A \rightarrow B$

After decomposition:
- $F_1 = \{A \rightarrow B, \; A \rightarrow C, \; B \rightarrow C, \; AB \rightarrow D\}$

**Step 2: Remove extraneous attributes from LHS**

Test the FD $AB \rightarrow D$:

$$\text{Compute } A^+ = \{A, B, C\} \text{ (using } A \rightarrow B, A \rightarrow C\text{)}$$

Wait ŌĆö we must be careful. When testing extraneous attributes, we **temporarily remove** the suspected attribute from the LHS of the FD under test, and use the *other* FDs (excluding the one being tested) to compute the closure.

- Test $B$ as extraneous in $AB \rightarrow D$:
  - Temporarily drop $AB \rightarrow D$ from $F_1$.
  - Compute $A^+$ using $\{A \rightarrow B, \; A \rightarrow C, \; B \rightarrow C\}$:
    - result = $\{A\}$
    - $A \rightarrow B$: result = $\{A, B\}$
    - $A \rightarrow C$: result = $\{A, B, C\}$
    - $B \rightarrow C$: result = $\{A, B, C\}$ (no change)
  - $A^+ = \{A, B, C\}$, which does **not** contain $D$.
  - Therefore, $B$ is **NOT extraneous**. The FD remains $AB \rightarrow D$.

**Step 3: Remove redundant FDs** (FDs that can be derived from the others)

Test each FD:

- **Test $A \rightarrow C$:** Drop it. Compute $A^+$ using $\{A \rightarrow B, \; B \rightarrow C, \; AB \rightarrow D\}$:
  - result = $\{A\}$
  - $A \rightarrow B$: result = $\{A, B\}$
  - $B \rightarrow C$: result = $\{A, B, C\}$
  - $AB \rightarrow D$: result = $\{A, B, C, D\}$
  - $A^+ = \{A, B, C, D\} \supseteq C$ Ō£ō
  - Since $A^+ \supseteq C$, the FD $A \rightarrow C$ is **redundant**. **Remove it.**

- **Test $B \rightarrow C$:** After removing $A \rightarrow C$, set is $\{A \rightarrow B, \; B \rightarrow C, \; AB \rightarrow D\}$. Drop $B \rightarrow C$. Compute $B^+$ using $\{A \rightarrow B, \; AB \rightarrow D\}$:
  - result = $\{B\}$
  - No FD has $B$ on LHS alone. $B^+ = \{B\}$, does not contain $C$.
  - Therefore $B \rightarrow C$ is **NOT redundant**. **Keep it.**

- **Test $A \rightarrow B$:** Drop it. Set becomes $\{B \rightarrow C, \; AB \rightarrow D\}$. Compute $A^+$:
  - result = $\{A\}$
  - No FD fires. $A^+ = \{A\}$. Does not contain $B$.
  - Therefore $A \rightarrow B$ is **NOT redundant**. **Keep it.**

- **Test $AB \rightarrow D$:** Drop it. Set becomes $\{A \rightarrow B, \; B \rightarrow C\}$. Compute $(AB)^+$:
  - result = $\{A, B\}$
  - $A \rightarrow B$: no new
  - $B \rightarrow C$: result = $\{A, B, C\}$
  - $(AB)^+ = \{A, B, C\}$, does not contain $D$.
  - Therefore $AB \rightarrow D$ is **NOT redundant**. **Keep it.**

**Final Minimal Cover:**
$$F_c = \{A \rightarrow B, \; B \rightarrow C, \; AB \rightarrow D\}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification of Minimal Cover Properties:**
> 1. Ō£ō Every RHS is a single attribute.
> 2. Ō£ō No FD can be removed without losing information.
> 3. Ō£ō No extraneous attributes in any LHS.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Properties of $F^+$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Properties of Functional Dependency Closure:**

| Property | Statement | Proof Idea |
|----------|-----------|------------|
| **Reflexivity in $F^+$** | $F^+ \supseteq \{X \rightarrow Y \mid Y \subseteq X\}$ | Direct from A1 |
| **Augmentation in $F^+$** | If $X \rightarrow Y \in F^+$, then $XZ \rightarrow YZ \in F^+$ | Direct from A2 |
| **Transitivity in $F^+$** | If $X \rightarrow Y$ and $Y \rightarrow Z \in F^+$, then $X \rightarrow Z \in F^+$ | Direct from A3 |
| **$F \subseteq F^+$** | Every FD in $F$ is trivially in $F^+$ | Reflexivity of derivation |
| **$(F^+)^+ = F^+$** | Closure is idempotent | By definition of closure |

---

## 3.4 Verification of Derived Rules (Proof Sketches)

**Proof of Decomposition Rule (If $X \rightarrow YZ$, then $X \rightarrow Y$):**

- Given: $X \rightarrow YZ$.
- Since $Y \subseteq YZ$, by **Reflexivity (A1)**: $YZ \rightarrow Y$.
- By **Transitivity (A3)** applied to $X \rightarrow YZ$ and $YZ \rightarrow Y$:

$$X \rightarrow Y \quad \blacksquare$$

**Proof of Union Rule (If $X \rightarrow Y$ and $X \rightarrow Z$, then $X \rightarrow YZ$):**

- Given: $X \rightarrow Y$ and $X \rightarrow Z$.
- Apply **Augmentation (A2)** to $X \rightarrow Z$ with $Y$: $XY \rightarrow YZ$.
- Since $X \rightarrow Y$, by **Reflexivity (A1)**: $XY \rightarrow X$.
- By **Transitivity (A3)** on $XY \rightarrow X$ and $X \rightarrow Y$:

$$XY \rightarrow Y \quad \text{(already known, but useful)}$$

- Apply **Decomposition** to $XY \rightarrow YZ$: $XY \rightarrow Y$ and $XY \rightarrow Z$.
- By **Augmentation** in reverse (and since $X \subseteq XY$, we can simplify back):

$$X \rightarrow YZ \quad \blacksquare$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Dependency Inference Flow (Mermaid Diagram)

The following diagram illustrates the logical flow of FD inference using Armstrong's axioms and derived rules. This is the **conceptual architecture** KTU examiners expect students to reproduce in their answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Given FD Set F] --> ruleA1[Apply Reflexivity A1: Y subset of X implies X to Y]
    startA --> ruleA2[Apply Augmentation A2: X to Y implies XZ to YZ]
    startA --> ruleA3[Apply Transitivity A3: X to Y and Y to Z implies X to Z]

    ruleA1 --> derived1[Derived Rule: Union]
    ruleA2 --> derived2[Derived Rule: Decomposition]
    ruleA3 --> derived3[Derived Rule: Pseudotransitivity]

    derived1 --> closureF[Compute F+: Full FD Closure]
    derived2 --> closureX[Compute X+: Attribute Closure]
    derived3 --> minCover[Derive Minimal Cover Fc]

    closureF --> keyCheck{Does X+ contain R?}
    keyCheck -->|Yes| supKey[Super Key Identified]
    keyCheck -->|No| notKey[Not a Super Key]

    supKey --> minTest{Is any proper subset also a Super Key?}
    minTest -->|No| candKey[Candidate Key Confirmed]
    minTest -->|Yes| elimKey[Not Minimal - Discard]

    minCover --> verifyAll[Verify: Single RHS, No Redundant FDs, No Extraneous LHS]
    verifyAll --> finalFC[Final Minimal Cover Fc]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Attribute Closure Algorithm

The following schematic maps the **block-level data flow** of the closure computation algorithm ŌĆö useful when an examiner asks for an "algorithm-style" answer.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputBlock[Input: Attribute Set X and FD Set F] --> initBlock[Initialize result = X]
    initBlock --> loopStart{Loop Until No Change}
    loopStart --> scanFD[Scan Each FD: alpha to beta in F]
    scanFD --> checkContain{Is alpha contained in result?}
    checkContain -->|Yes| addAttr[result = result union beta]
    checkContain -->|No| nextFD[Skip to Next FD]
    addAttr --> nextFD
    nextFD --> loopStart
    loopStart -->|Converged| outputBlock[Output: X+ = result]
    outputBlock --> superKeyTest{X+ equals R?}
    superKeyTest -->|Yes| markSuper[Mark X as Super Key]
    superKeyTest -->|No| markNotKey[Not a Super Key]
```

## 4.3 Hierarchy of FD Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    fdRoot[Functional Dependency: X to Y] --> type1[Trivial FD: Y subset of X]
    fdRoot --> type2[Non-Trivial FD: Y not subset of X]

    type1 --> trivEx[Example: AB to A]
    type2 --> nonTrivEx[Example: A to B]

    type2 --> strict1[Completely Non-Trivial: X intersect Y = empty]
    strict1 --> strictEx[Example: RollNo to Name]
```

## 4.4 Step-by-Step Schema of Minimal Cover Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startMC[Input: FD Set F] --> step1[Step 1: Decompose FDs to Single RHS]
    step1 --> step2[Step 2: Remove Extraneous LHS Attributes]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3: Remove Redundant FDs]
    step3 --> step4[Output: Minimal Cover Fc]
    step4 --> verify[Verify: Fc equivalent to F and F+]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
