# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Question Paper Style]**

### Question A1: Define Functional Dependency with an Example. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023]**

**Model Answer (Concise ŌĆö Exactly 3-Mark Style):**

A **Functional Dependency (FD)** is a constraint between two sets of attributes $X$ and $Y$ in a relation $R$, denoted as $X \rightarrow Y$. It states that if two tuples agree on the value of attributes in $X$ (i.e., $t_1[X] = t_2[X]$), then they must also agree on the value of attributes in $Y$ (i.e., $t_1[Y] = t_2[Y]$).

**Example:** In a `STUDENT(RollNo, Name, Dept)` relation, the FD `RollNo ŌåÆ Name` holds because the same `RollNo` always maps to the same `Name`. RollNo is the determinant and Name is the dependent attribute.

---

### Question A2: Differentiate Between Trivial and Non-Trivial Functional Dependencies. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer (3-Mark Format):**

| Aspect | Trivial FD | Non-Trivial FD |
|--------|-----------|----------------|
| **Definition** | $X \rightarrow Y$ where $Y \subseteq X$ | $X \rightarrow Y$ where $Y \not\subseteq X$ |
| **Validity** | Always holds by Reflexivity | Must be enforced/specified |
| **Example** | $\{A, B\} \rightarrow A$ | `RollNo ŌåÆ Name` |
| **Usefulness** | Generally ignored in design | Genuinely conveys business constraints |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

### Question A: **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024 Model Pattern]**

> Given a relation schema $R(A, B, C, D, E)$ and a set of functional dependencies:
> $$F = \{A \rightarrow BC, \;\; E \rightarrow C, \;\; CD \rightarrow A, \;\; B \rightarrow D, \;\; D \rightarrow E\}$$
> **(a)** Compute the closure of $B$, i.e., find $B^+$. [7 Marks]
> **(b)** Find all candidate keys of $R$ using attribute closure. [7 Marks]

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Model Solution: Computing $B^+$

**Initialization:** $\text{result} = \{B\}$

**Iteration 1:**
- $A \rightarrow BC$: LHS = $\{A\}$, not in result. Skip.
- $E \rightarrow C$: LHS = $\{E\}$, not in result. Skip.
- $CD \rightarrow A$: LHS = $\{C, D\}$, not in result. Skip.
- $B \rightarrow D$: LHS = $\{B\}$ ŌŖå result. **Fire!**
  $$\text{result} = \{B\} \cup \{D\} = \{B, D\}$$
- $D \rightarrow E$: LHS = $\{D\}$ ŌŖå result. **Fire!**
  $$\text{result} = \{B, D\} \cup \{E\} = \{B, D, E\}$$

**Iteration 2:**
- $A \rightarrow BC$: LHS = $\{A\}$, not in result. Skip.
- $E \rightarrow C$: LHS = $\{E\}$ ŌŖå result. **Fire!**
  $$\text{result} = \{B, D, E\} \cup \{C\} = \{B, C, D, E\}$$
- $CD \rightarrow A$: LHS = $\{C, D\}$ ŌŖå result. **Fire!**
  $$\text{result} = \{B, C, D, E\} \cup \{A\} = \{A, B, C, D, E\}$$

**Iteration 3:** No new attributes. Converged.

**Final Answer:**
$$B^+ = \{A, B, C, D, E\} = R$$

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Model Solution: Finding All Candidate Keys

Since $B^+ = R$, the set $B$ itself is a **super key**. To determine if it is a candidate key, we must check the closure of its proper subsets. Since $B$ is a singleton, the only proper subset is the empty set $\emptyset$, whose closure is trivially $\emptyset$. Therefore, **$B$ is a candidate key**.

To find **other** candidate keys, we use the standard approach: try single attributes and minimal pairs.

- $A^+ = \{A, B, C, D, E\}$ (use $A \rightarrow BC$, $B \rightarrow D$, $D \rightarrow E$) ŌĆö Wait, do we have $E \rightarrow C$? Yes, but result = $\{A, B, C\}$ from $A \rightarrow BC$, then $B \rightarrow D$ gives $\{A, B, C, D\}$, then $D \rightarrow E$ gives $\{A, B, C, D, E\}$. So $A^+ = R$. **A is a candidate key.**
- $C^+ = \{C\}$ ŌĆö No FD has $C$ alone on LHS. Not a super key.
- $D^+ = \{D, E, C, A, B\}$. Apply: $D \rightarrow E$, $E \rightarrow C$, $CD \rightarrow A$, $A \rightarrow BC$. Result: $\{A, B, C, D, E\} = R$. **D is a candidate key.**
- $E^+ = \{E, C, A, B, D\}$. Apply: $E \rightarrow C$, $CD \rightarrow A$, $A \rightarrow BC$, $B \rightarrow D$. Result: $\{A, B, C, D, E\} = R$. **E is a candidate key.**

**Conclusion:** All four singleton attributes $\{A, B, D, E\}$ are candidate keys. There are **no compound candidate keys** because every single attribute is already a key.

$$\text{Set of Candidate Keys} = \{A, B, D, E\}$$

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice): **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Model Pattern]**

> Given a relation $R(A, B, C, D, E, H)$ and a set of functional dependencies:
> $$F = \{A \rightarrow B, \;\; BC \rightarrow D, \;\; D \rightarrow E, \;\; CH \rightarrow A, \;\; D \rightarrow H\}$$
> **(a)** Find the minimal cover $F_c$ of $F$. [7 Marks]
> **(b)** Determine all candidate keys of $R$. [7 Marks]

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Model Solution: Minimal Cover

**Step 1: RHS is already a singleton for every FD.** No decomposition needed.

**Step 2: Remove Extraneous LHS Attributes**

Test the FD $BC \rightarrow D$:
- Drop $BC \rightarrow D$ temporarily. Compute $B^+$ using $\{A \rightarrow B, D \rightarrow E, CH \rightarrow A, D \rightarrow H\}$:
  - $B^+ = \{B\}$ ŌĆö does not contain $D$.
- Compute $C^+$ similarly: $C^+ = \{C\}$.
- So $B$ and $C$ are both non-extraneous. **Keep $BC \rightarrow D$.**

Test the FD $CH \rightarrow A$:
- Compute $C^+ = \{C\}$ ŌĆö not sufficient.
- Compute $H^+ = \{H\}$ ŌĆö not sufficient.
- **Keep $CH \rightarrow A$.**

**Step 3: Remove Redundant FDs**

Test $A \rightarrow B$ (drop it, compute $A^+$ from rest):
- $A^+ = \{A\}$ (no other FD has $A$ on LHS alone). $A \nrightarrow B$. **Keep $A \rightarrow B$.**

Test $BC \rightarrow D$ (drop it, compute $(BC)^+$ from rest):
- $B^+ = \{B\}, C^+ = \{C\}, H^+ = \{H\}$ ŌĆö no derivation of $D$ possible.
- **Keep $BC \rightarrow D$.**

Test $D \rightarrow E$ (drop it, compute $D^+$):
- $D \rightarrow H$ gives $\{D, H\}$.
- $CH \rightarrow A$ needs $C$, not present. Stops at $\{D, H\}$.
- $D \nrightarrow E$ from the remaining FDs. **Keep $D \rightarrow E$.**

Test $CH \rightarrow A$ (drop it, compute $(CH)^+$):
- $C^+ = \{C\}, H^+ = \{H\}$. Cannot derive $A$ without it.
- **Keep $CH \rightarrow A$.**

Test $D \rightarrow H$ (drop it, compute $D^+$ from rest):
- $D \rightarrow E$ gives $\{D, E\}$.
- No way to derive $H$ from the remaining FDs. **Keep $D \rightarrow H$.**

**Final Minimal Cover:**
$$F_c = \{A \rightarrow B, \;\; BC \rightarrow D, \;\; D \rightarrow E, \;\; CH \rightarrow A, \;\; D \rightarrow H\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In this particular example, the minimal cover is identical to the original $F$ because no FD was redundant and no LHS had extraneous attributes.

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Model Solution: Finding Candidate Keys

Since the RHS of $BC \rightarrow D$ is $D$ and $D$ has no incoming FD that starts from outside, attributes $C$ and $H$ are likely needed (as $CH \rightarrow A$ is the only FD that "exports" information from $\{C, H\}$).

**Strategy:** Try candidate keys involving $C$ and $H$.

**Test $CH$:**
- $\text{result} = \{C, H\}$
- $CH \rightarrow A$: result = $\{A, C, H\}$
- $A \rightarrow B$: result = $\{A, B, C, H\}$
- $BC \rightarrow D$: result = $\{A, B, C, D, H\}$
- $D \rightarrow E$: result = $\{A, B, C, D, E, H\}$
- $D \rightarrow H$: result = same.
- $(CH)^+ = \{A, B, C, D, E, H\} = R$ Ō£ō

**Is $CH$ a candidate key?** Test its proper subsets:
- $C^+ = \{C\}$ (no FD has $C$ alone on LHS) ŌĆö **Not a super key**.
- $H^+ = \{H\}$ (no FD has $H$ alone on LHS) ŌĆö **Not a super key**.

Therefore, **$CH$ is a candidate key**.

**Are there other candidate keys?** Test $ACH, BCH, DCH, ECH$:
- These all contain $CH$, so they are super keys but **not** candidate keys (since $CH$ alone is already a key).

**Conclusion:**
$$\text{Set of Candidate Keys} = \{CH\}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Easy Marks:**
> 1. **Not initializing `result` with $X$ itself** ŌĆö Always include the starting attributes in the closure from the very first step.
> 2. **Forgetting to re-iterate** ŌĆö After a new attribute is added in iteration $k$, the algorithm may enable FDs that were previously blocked. KEEP LOOPING until convergence.
> 3. **Wrong minimal cover order** ŌĆö Always perform decomposition FIRST, then LHS simplification, then FD removal. Reversing the order produces WRONG minimal covers.
> 4. **Confusing "extraneous LHS attribute" with "redundant FD"** ŌĆö An extraneous LHS attribute is a single attribute on the LHS that can be removed while preserving the FD. A redundant FD is an entire FD that can be dropped.
> 5. **Reporting $X^+ \subseteq R$ as a super key** ŌĆö The correct test is $X^+ = R$ (i.e., $X^+$ contains **every** attribute of $R$).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Board Exam:**

- **FD Definition:** A constraint $X \rightarrow Y$ meaning $X$ uniquely determines $Y$.
- **Trivial FD:** $Y \subseteq X$ (always true, ignore in design).
- **Non-Trivial FD:** $Y \not\subseteq X$ (genuine business rule).
- **Armstrong's Axioms (3):** Reflexivity (A1), Augmentation (A2), Transitivity (A3).
- **Derived Rules (4):** Union, Decomposition, Pseudotransitivity, Composition.
- **Attribute Closure $X^+$:** Linear-time algorithm to find all attributes functionally determined by $X$.
- **Super Key Test:** $X$ is a super key **if and only if** $X^+ = R$.
- **Candidate Key Test:** $X$ is a candidate key **if and only if** $X^+ = R$ AND no proper subset of $X$ has this property.
- **Minimal Cover Algorithm:** (1) Decompose RHS ŌåÆ (2) Remove Extraneous LHS ŌåÆ (3) Remove Redundant FDs.
- **Equivalence of FD Sets:** $F$ and $G$ are equivalent if and only if $F^+ = G^+$.
- **Prime Attribute:** An attribute that appears in **any** candidate key.
- **Linear Time:** Attribute closure runs in polynomial time ŌĆö the cornerstone of all FD-based algorithms in KTU Module 4 (Normalization).
- **Always re-iterate the closure loop** ŌĆö convergence must be confirmed in a separate pass.
- **Use proper notation** ŌĆö KTU examiners deduct marks for ambiguous symbols. Use $\subseteq$ for subset, $\cup$ for union, $\rightarrow$ for FD.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Armstrong's Axioms (Proofs Not Required)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBEOT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Armstrong's Axioms (Proofs Not Required) # ­¤ōś MODULE 3 ŌĆö ARMSTRONG'S AXIOMS ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Armstrong's Axioms** are a set of **sound** and **complete** inference rules introduced by William W. Armstrong (1974) that are used to deduce (infer) the **logical consequences** ŌĆö i.e., the **closure ($F^+$)** ŌĆö of a given set of functional dependencies $F$ defined over a relation schema $R$. Formally, let $R$ be a relation schema and $F$ be a set of functional dependencies (FDs) on $R$. The closure of $F$, denoted $F^+$, is the set of all functional dependencies that can be **logically implied** by $F$ using Armstrong's inference rules. | Property | Definition | Why It Matters for KTU | |---|---|---| | **Sound** | Any FD derived using these axioms is **guaranteed to be true** whenever $F$ is true. | Ensures we never derive a *false* dependency. | | **Complete** | **Every** FD that is logically implied by $F$ **can be derived** using these axioms. | Guarantees we can derive *all* true dependencies. | --- ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English) > [!TIP] > **Analogy ŌĆö The "Rule-Book" of a Company:** Imagine you are a manager and you are given a small set of company policies (your $F$). The **closure $F^+$** is every single rule the company *must* follow, including those not directly written down but which logically follow from the original ones. **Armstrong's axioms** are the *legal reasoning tools* your HR lawyer uses to deduce all the implied rules from the written ones. They never invent a fake rule (sound), and they always find every real implied rule (complete). In a database context: - $F$ = the FDs we *know* hold in the relation. - $F^+$ = the FDs that **must** hold because of the ones we know. - Armstrong's axioms = the *logical machine* that expands $F$ into $F^+$. --- ### 1.3 The Three Primary (Axiomatic) Rules Let $\alpha$, $\beta$, $\gamma$ be sets of attributes over the relation schema $R$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Three Foundational Axioms (must memorize for KTU ESE):** | # | Axiom | Formal Statement | Logic (Why it works) | |---|---|---|---| | **A1** | **Reflexivity** | If $\beta \subseteq \alpha$, then $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ | Trivial: a superset always determines its subset. | | **A2** | **Augmentation** | If $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$, then $\alpha\gamma \rightarrow \beta\gamma$ | Adding the *same* extra info to both sides preserves the dependency. | | **A3** | **Transitivity** | If $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ and $\beta \rightarrow \gamma$, then $\alpha \rightarrow \gamma$ | Functional dependencies are *chainable*. | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** In **Reflexivity**, the FD $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ is always true for $\beta \subseteq \alpha$ ŌĆö even on an *empty* relation. Such FDs are called **trivial FDs**. ### 1.4 Additional (Secondary) Derived Rules These are *not* new axioms ŌĆö they are **theorems proven using A1, A2, A3**. KTU examiners frequently test them in Part A (3 marks). | # | Rule | Statement | Derived From | |---|---|---|---| | **A4** | **Union (Additivity)** | If $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ and $\alpha \rightarrow \gamma$, then $\alpha \rightarrow \beta\gamma$ | A2 + A3 | | **A5** | **Decomposition (Projectivity)** | If $\alpha \rightarrow \beta\gamma$, then $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ and $\alpha \rightarrow \gamma$ | A1 + A3 | | **A6** | **Pseudotransitivity** | If $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ and $\gamma\beta \rightarrow \delta$, then $\alpha\gamma \rightarrow \delta$ | A2 + A3 | | **A7** | **Composition** | If $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ and $\gamma \rightarrow \delta$, then $\alpha\gamma \rightarrow \beta\delta$ | A2 + A3 | | **A8** | **Augmentation (general)** | If $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ and $\gamma \subseteq \delta$, then $\alpha\delta \rightarrow \beta\gamma$ | A2 | ### 1.5 Why KTU Asks This > [!IMPORTANT] > Armstrong's axioms are the **theoretical foundation** for three pivotal Module-3 algorithms: > 1. Computing the **attribute closure** $\alpha^+$ (used in candidate-key finding). > 2. Testing whether an FD $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ is in the closure $F^+$. > 3. Computing the **minimal cover** $F_c$ (used in BCNF / 3NF decomposition ŌĆö Module 4). > > Therefore, mastering the axioms is *non-negotiable* for Module 3, 4, and 5 (Normalization). --- # ­¤ōś SECTION 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Primary Axioms ŌĆö Deep Conceptual Analysis ### 2.1.1 Reflexivity (A1) ŌĆö "Trivial Dependency Rule" $$\text{If } \beta \subseteq \alpha, \text{ then } \alpha \rightarrow \beta$$ - **What it says:** Any attribute set determines all of its own subsets. - **When to use:** To *break down* a determinant into smaller pieces (Decomposition A5 is built on this). - **Example:** Since $\{A, B\} \supseteq \{A\}$, by reflexivity: $AB \rightarrow A$ and $AB \rightarrow B$. - **Engineering reality:** This is "common sense" ŌĆö knowing a student's *full profile* means you already know their *name*. ### 2.1.2 Augmentation (A2) ŌĆö "Same-Side Extender" $$\text{If } \alpha \rightarrow \beta, \text{ then } \alpha\gamma \rightarrow \beta\gamma$$ - **What it says:** If $\alpha$ determines $\beta$, then adding the *same* attributes to both sides preserves the dependency. - **The critical requirement:** The added set $\gamma$ **must be identical** on both sides. You cannot just add it to one side. - **Example:** From $A \rightarrow B$, by A2: $AC \rightarrow BC$ (with $\gamma = C$). - **Engineering reality:** Knowing the employee's ID gives their salary. If you also know their department, then knowing *ID + Department* gives *Salary + Department*. ### 2.1.3 Transitivity (A3) ŌĆö "Chain Rule" $$\text{If } \alpha \rightarrow \beta \text{ and } \beta \rightarrow \gamma, \text{ then } \alpha \rightarrow \gamma$$ - **What it says:** Functional dependencies behave like transitive functions in mathematics. - **Example:** If `Roll_No ŌåÆ Name` and `Name ŌåÆ DOB`, then `Roll_No ŌåÆ DOB`. - **Engineering reality:** The cornerstone of *normalization* ŌĆö it lets you chain keys to eliminate transitive anomalies. --- ## 2.2 The Derived Rules ŌĆö Why They Matter ### 2.2.1 Union Rule (A4) ŌĆö The "Combiner" $$\alpha \rightarrow \beta \text{ and } \alpha \rightarrow \gamma \;\Rightarrow\; \alpha \rightarrow \beta\gamma$$ **Why useful:** When decomposing relations, we often need to *combine* multiple FDs into a single one. For example, if we split a relation $R$ into $R_1(\alpha, \beta)$ and $R_2(\alpha, \gamma)$, the lossless-join test requires $\alpha \rightarrow \beta\gamma$. ### 2.2.2 Decomposition Rule (A5) ŌĆö The "Splitter" $$\alpha \rightarrow \beta\gamma \;\Rightarrow\; \alpha \rightarrow \beta \text{ and } \alpha \rightarrow \gamma$$ **Why useful:** This is the *only* way the right-hand side of an FD can be **broken apart** safely. It is the formal justification for *projection* of FDs onto a sub-relation during decomposition. ### 2.2.3 Pseudotransitivity (A6) ŌĆö "Partial Transitivity" $$\alpha \rightarrow \beta \text{ and } \gamma\beta \rightarrow \delta \;\Rightarrow\; \alpha\gamma \rightarrow \delta$$ **Why useful:** Handles "missing-link" chains where the middle attribute is augmented with a side attribute. ### 2.2.4 Composition (A7) ŌĆö "Simultaneous Chain" $$\alpha \rightarrow \beta \text{ and } \gamma \rightarrow \delta \;\Rightarrow\; \alpha\gamma \rightarrow \beta\delta$$ **Why useful:** Vital when two FDs come from two different relations and you need a single FD for a *join* query. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Print this table ŌĆö it covers ~70% of KTU Module-3 questions.** | Concept | Symbol | Formula / Rule | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | Closure of FD set | $F^+$ | $\{ \alpha \rightarrow \beta \;\vert\; F \models \alpha \rightarrow \beta\}$ | The set of *all* logically implied FDs. | | Attribute closure | $\alpha^+$ | Iterative: $\alpha^{(0)} = \alpha$; $\alpha^{(i+1)} = \alpha^{(i)} \cup \{A \;\vert\; \exists \beta \rightarrow A\gamma \in F \text{ with } \beta \subseteq \alpha^{(i)}\}$ | Algorithm terminates when $\alpha^{(i+1)} = \alpha^{(i)}$. | | $\alpha \in F^+$ test | ŌĆö | Compute $\alpha^+$; check if $\beta \subseteq \alpha^+$. | Linear in $\vert F\vert$ time per test. | | $\alpha$ is a candidate key | ŌĆö | $\alpha^+ = R$ **and** for every $A \in \alpha$, $(\alpha - A)^+ \neq R$. | Minimality test. | | Minimal cover | $F_c$ | Right side singleton, no extraneous attrs, no redundant FDs. | Used in BCNF/3NF. | | Reflexivity | A1 | $\beta \subseteq \alpha \Rightarrow \alpha \rightarrow \beta$ | Trivial FD. | | Augmentation | A2 | $\alpha \rightarrow \beta \Rightarrow \alpha\gamma \rightarrow \beta\gamma$ | $\gamma$ identical both sides. | | Transitivity | A3 | $\alpha \rightarrow \beta, \beta \rightarrow \gamma \Rightarrow \alpha \rightarrow \gamma$ | The workhorse. | | Union | A4 | $\alpha \rightarrow \beta, \alpha \rightarrow \gamma \Rightarrow \alpha \rightarrow \beta\gamma$ | Combine. | | Decomposition | A5 | $\alpha \rightarrow \beta\gamma \Rightarrow \alpha \rightarrow \beta, \alpha \rightarrow \gamma$ | Split. | | Pseudotransitivity | A6 | $\alpha \rightarrow \beta, \gamma\beta \rightarrow \delta \Rightarrow \alpha\gamma \rightarrow \delta$ | Partial chain. | | Composition | A7 | $\alpha \rightarrow \beta, \gamma \rightarrow \delta \Rightarrow \alpha\gamma \rightarrow \beta\delta$ | Parallel chain. | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Application of Armstrong's Axioms | |---|---| | **Schema Design** | Ensures *all* dependencies are preserved when decomposing into 3NF/BCNF. | | **Query Optimization** | Helps the optimizer recognize *implied* dependencies for join reordering. | | **Data Migration** | Guarantees the migrated schema preserves the *same constraints* as the original. | | **Anomaly Detection** | Detects *transitive* dependencies that cause update/insertion/deletion anomalies. | | **Reverse Engineering** | Recovers the *complete constraint set* $F^+$ from a minimal specification $F$. | --- # ­¤ōś SECTION 3 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU's most frequently asked computation question on this topic** is: *compute the attribute closure $\alpha^+$ using Armstrong's axioms.* Below is the exhaustive, KTU-board-marking-scheme-aligned algorithm, followed by fully worked-out solutions. ## 3.1 Algorithm: Computing the Attribute Closure $\alpha^+$ This is the **most important algorithm** in Module 3. KTU awards **7 marks** for a clean execution of this algorithm (typical Part B sub-question (a)). **Input:** A finite set of attributes $\alpha$, a set of FDs $F$. **Output:** The closure $\alpha^+$. ### Step-by-Step Procedure | Step | Action | Reason | |---|---|---| | **1** | Initialize $\alpha^{(0)} = \alpha$. | Start with the given attributes. | | **2** | Repeat for $i = 1, 2, 3, \dots$ | Iterative expansion. | | **3** | For each FD $\beta \rightarrow \gamma$ in $F$: if $\beta \subseteq \alpha^{(i-1)}$, then set $\alpha^{(i)} = \alpha^{(i-1)} \cup \gamma$. | Apply the FD whose LHS is satisfied. | | **4** | If $\alpha^{(i)} = \alpha^{(i-1)}$, then **stop** and return $\alpha^{(i)}$. | No new attributes ŌĆö fixed point reached. | | **5** | Otherwise, go to step 2 with $i+1$. | Continue. | > [!NOTE] > The algorithm **always terminates** because $R$ is finite, so $\alpha^+$ can grow to at most $\vert R \vert$ attributes. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 1 ŌĆö Direct Application of A2 & A3 (No Closure Algorithm Needed) > **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024 Style Question]** > Given $F = \{ A \rightarrow B, B \rightarrow C, A \rightarrow D \}$, **prove** using Armstrong's axioms that $A \rightarrow BCD \in F^+$. ### Exhaustive Derivation $$ \begin{aligned} &\textbf{Step 1:} \quad A \rightarrow B &&\text{(Given in } F\text{)} \\ &\textbf{Step 2:} \quad A \rightarrow A &&\text{(Reflexivity, A1, since } A \subseteq A\text{)} \\ &\textbf{Step 3:} \quad A \rightarrow AB &&\text{(Augmentation, A2, using Step 1 and Step 2 with } \gamma = A\text{)} \\ &\textbf{Step 4:} \quad B \rightarrow C &&\text{(Given in } F\text{)} \\ &\textbf{Step 5:} \quad AB \rightarrow AC &&\text{(Augmentation, A2, on Step 4 with } \gamma = A\text{)} \\ &\textbf{Step 6:} \quad A \rightarrow AC &&\text{(Transitivity, A3, on Step 3 and Step 5)} \\ &\textbf{Step 7:} \quad A \rightarrow C &&\text{(Decomposition, A5, on Step 6)} \\ &\textbf{Step 8:} \quad A \rightarrow B \text{ and } A \rightarrow C &&\text{(Steps 1 and 7)} \\ &\textbf{Step 9:} \quad A \rightarrow BC &&\text{(Union, A4, on Step 8)} \\ &\textbf{Step 10:} \quad A \rightarrow D &&\text{(Given in } F\text{)} \\ &\textbf{Step 11:} \quad A \rightarrow BC \text{ and } A \rightarrow D &&\text{(Steps 9 and 10)} \\ &\textbf{Step 12:} \quad \boxed{A \rightarrow BCD} &&\text{(Union, A4, on Step 11)} \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned} $$ > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Marking Insight (7 marks):** KTU awards 1 mark for *stating* each axiom used. Missing the axiom name = -1 mark per omission. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 2 ŌĆö Computing $\alpha^+$ Using the Iterative Algorithm > **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Style Question]** > Let $R(A, B, C, D, E, F)$ and $F = \{ A \rightarrow B, B \rightarrow C, C \rightarrow D, D \rightarrow EF \}$. Compute $(AE)^+$. ### Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution $$ \begin{aligned} &\textbf{Initialization:} \quad (AE)^{(0)} = \{A, E\} \\ \\ &\textbf{Iteration 1: } (AE)^{(1)} \\ &\quad \text{Check } A \rightarrow B: \; A \subseteq \{A, E\} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{add } B. \\ &\quad (AE)^{(1)} = \{A, E, B\} \\ \\ &\textbf{Iteration 2: } (AE)^{(2)} \\ &\quad \text{Check } A \rightarrow B: \; A \subseteq \{A, E, B\} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{already in set, no change.} \\ &\quad \text{Check } B \rightarrow C: \; B \subseteq \{A, E, B\} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{add } C. \\ &\quad (AE)^{(2)} = \{A, E, B, C\} \\ \\ &\textbf{Iteration 3: } (AE)^{(3)} \\ &\quad \text{Check } C \rightarrow D: \; C \subseteq \{A, E, B, C\} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{add } D. \\ &\quad (AE)^{(3)} = \{A, E, B, C, D\} \\ \\ &\textbf{Iteration 4: } (AE)^{(4)} \\ &\quad \text{Check } D \rightarrow EF: \; D \subseteq \{A, E, B, C, D\} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{add } E, F. \\ &\quad (AE)^{(4)} = \{A, B, C, D, E, F\} \\ \\ &\textbf{Iteration 5: } (AE)^{(5)} \\ &\quad \text{No new attributes can be added.} \\ &\quad (AE)^{(4)} = (AE)^{(5)} = \{A, B, C, D, E, F\} \\ \\ &\textbf{Conclusion:} \quad \boxed{(AE)^+ = R = \{A, B, C, D, E, F\}} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > Since $(AE)^+ = R$, the set $\{A, E\}$ is a **superkey** of $R$. To check if it is a **candidate key**, we test minimality by computing $A^+$ and $E^+$ separately. ### Checking Candidate-Key Minimality | Subset | Closure | Candidate Key? | |---|---|---| | $\{A\}$ | $\{A\}^+ = \{A, B, C, D, E, F\} = R$ | Ō£ģ **Yes** ŌĆö $A$ alone is a candidate key. | | $\{E\}$ | $\{E\}^+ = \{E\} \neq R$ | ŌØī No. | > [!IMPORTANT] > Since $A$ alone is a candidate key, $\{A, E\}$ is **not** a candidate key (it is a *superkey* but not *minimal*). The unique candidate key of this schema is $\mathbf{A}$. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example 3 ŌĆö Testing FD Membership in $F^+$ via Attribute Closure > **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2023 Style Question]** > Let $R = (A, B, C, D, E, F, G)$ and $F = \{ AB \rightarrow C, C \rightarrow A, D \rightarrow E, E \rightarrow DG, F \rightarrow G \}$. **Test** whether $AB \rightarrow DG \in F^+$. ### Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution We use the **closure test**: $AB \rightarrow DG \in F^+$ if and only if $D \subseteq (AB)^+$. $$ \begin{aligned} &\textbf{Iteration 0: } (AB)^{(0)} = \{A, B\} \\ &\textbf{Iteration 1: } \text{Use } AB \rightarrow C: \; AB \subseteq \{A, B\} \;\Rightarrow\; (AB)^{(1)} = \{A, B, C\} \\ &\textbf{Iteration 2: } \text{Use } C \rightarrow A: \; C \subseteq \{A, B, C\} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{add } A \text{ (already present).} \\ &\quad \text{No new attributes: } (AB)^{(2)} = (AB)^{(1)} = \{A, B, C\} \\ \\ &\textbf{Result:} \quad (AB)^+ = \{A, B, C\} \\ &\text{Since } D \notin (AB)^+, \text{ we conclude } \boxed{AB \rightarrow DG \notin F^+}. \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.5 Worked Example 4 ŌĆö Pseudotransitivity Application > **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2024 Expected Pattern]** > Given $F = \{ A \rightarrow B, CD \rightarrow E, BE \rightarrow F \}$. Prove $ACD \rightarrow F$. $$ \begin{aligned} &\textbf{Step 1:} \quad A \rightarrow B &&\text{(Given)} \\ &\textbf{Step 2:} \quad ACD \rightarrow BCD &&\text{(A2, Augmentation of Step 1 with } \gamma = CD\text{)} \\ &\textbf{Step 3:} \quad CD \rightarrow E &&\text{(Given)} \\ &\textbf{Step 4:} \quad BCD \rightarrow BCD \cup BE = BCDE &&\text{(A2 on Step 3 with } \gamma = BD\text{)} \\ &\textbf{Step 5:} \quad ACD \rightarrow BCDE &&\text{(A3, Transitivity, Steps 2 and 4)} \\ &\textbf{Step 6:} \quad ACD \rightarrow BE &&\text{(A5, Decomposition, on Step 5)} \\ &\textbf{Step 7:} \quad BE \rightarrow F &&\text{(Given)} \\ &\textbf{Step 8:} \quad \boxed{ACD \rightarrow F} &&\text{(A3, Transitivity, Steps 6 and 7)} \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned} $$ > [!TIP] > This is a beautiful application of **Pseudotransitivity (A6)**: $A \rightarrow B$ and $CD \rightarrow E$ and $BE \rightarrow F$ give us $ACD \rightarrow F$ in a single application: $\alpha = A$, $\beta = B$, $\gamma = CD$, $\delta = F$ (since $\gamma\beta = BCD$ is not exactly $BE$, we use the chain). --- ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Rule | Given FDs | Goal | Best Axiom to Use | |---|---|---| | $A \rightarrow B$, $B \rightarrow C$ | $A \rightarrow C$ | **A3 (Transitivity)** | | $AB \rightarrow C$ | $A \rightarrow C$ | Need $A \rightarrow AB$ (A1) then A3 ŌĆö or just augment and project. | | $A \rightarrow B$, $A \rightarrow C$ | $A \rightarrow BC$ | **A4 (Union)** | | $A \rightarrow BC$ | $A \rightarrow B$ and $A \rightarrow C$ | **A5 (Decomposition)** | | $A \rightarrow B$, $CB \rightarrow D$ | $AC \rightarrow D$ | **A6 (Pseudotransitivity)** ŌĆö apply A2 to first, then A3. | | $A \rightarrow B$, $C \rightarrow D$ | $AC \rightarrow BD$ | **A7 (Composition)** | | $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$, want a "bigger" LHS | Need to preserve the FD with extra info | **A2 (Augmentation)** | --- # ­¤ōś SECTION 4 ŌĆö Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## All four practical applications (closure, testing, minimal cover, candidate keys) depend on these rules. --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö Flow of the Attribute Closure Algorithm ```mermaid flowchart TD startNode["START
Input alpha and FD set F"] initNode["Initialise alpha-naught = alpha"] loopStart["Begin Iteration i = 1, 2, 3, ..."] checkFD["For each FD beta to gamma in F

The algorithm terminates when the inner loop produces no change to $\alpha$ ŌĆö this is the **fixed-point** of the closure operation. --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö Mapping F ŌåÆ FŌü║ (Closure of FD Set) ```mermaid flowchart LR Fset["F

Why are they called *sound* and *complete*? #### Model Answer (3 Marks) > [!NOTE] > **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for stating each axiom with formula (Ōēł1.5 marks for all 3) + 1 mark for soundness + 0.5 mark for completeness. **Armstrong's axioms** are a set of inference rules that derive all functional dependencies logically implied by a given set $F$ of FDs. They are: 1. **Reflexivity (A1):** If $\beta \subseteq \alpha$, then $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$. 2. **Augmentation (A2):** If $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$, then $\alpha\gamma \rightarrow \beta\gamma$. 3. **Transitivity (A3):** If $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ and $\beta \rightarrow \gamma$, then $\alpha \rightarrow \gamma$. - **Soundness:** Any FD derived using these rules is guaranteed to be true in every relation satisfying $F$. (So we never infer a *false* FD.) - **Completeness:** Every FD that is logically implied by $F$ can be derived using only these three rules. (So we never *miss* a true FD.) --- ### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Module 3]** > Differentiate between **primary axioms** and **derived axioms** of Armstrong's inference rules. Give **one example** of each. #### Model Answer (3 Marks) > [!NOTE] > **Mark Distribution:** 1.5 marks for difference + 1 mark for primary example + 0.5 mark for derived example. | Aspect | Primary Axioms | Derived Axioms | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Independent, self-justified rules. | Theorems derivable from the primary axioms. | | **Count** | Exactly 3: A1, A2, A3. | At least 5: A4 (Union), A5 (Decomposition), A6 (Pseudotransitivity), A7 (Composition), A8. | | **Independence** | Cannot be derived from one another. | Each is *proven* using A1, A2, A3. | - **Primary example:** $A \rightarrow B \Rightarrow ACD \rightarrow BCD$ (A2). - **Derived example:** $A \rightarrow B$ and $A \rightarrow C \Rightarrow A \rightarrow BC$ (A4 ŌĆö Union). --- ## ­¤ōØ PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024 Pattern]** > **(a)** Given the relation schema $R(A, B, C, D, E, F, G)$ and the set of functional dependencies: > $$F = \{\, A \rightarrow B, \; BD \rightarrow E, \; CD \rightarrow F, \; E \rightarrow C, \; D \rightarrow EG \,\}$$ > Compute $(AD)^+$ using Armstrong's axioms. Also identify whether $\{A, D\}$ is a **candidate key** of $R$. > **(7 Marks)** > **(b)** Using Armstrong's inference rules, **prove** that the FD $A \rightarrow F$ logically follows from: > $$F = \{\, A \rightarrow B, \; B \rightarrow C, \; CD \rightarrow E, \; E \rightarrow F \,\}$$ > List the name of each axiom used at every step. > **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a) (7 Marks) **Goal:** Compute $(AD)^+$ step by step. $$ \begin{aligned} &\textbf{Iteration 0: } (AD)^{(0)} = \{A, D\} \\ \\ &\textbf{Iteration 1: } (AD)^{(1)} \\ &\quad \text{Use } A \rightarrow B: \quad A \subseteq \{A, D\} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{add } B. \\ &\quad (AD)^{(1)} = \{A, B, D\} \\ \\ &\textbf{Iteration 2: } (AD)^{(2)} \\ &\quad \text{Use } D \rightarrow EG: \quad D \subseteq \{A, B, D\} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{add } E, G. \\ &\quad (AD)^{(2)} = \{A, B, D, E, G\} \\ \\ &\textbf{Iteration 3: } (AD)^{(3)} \\ &\quad \text{Use } BD \rightarrow E: \quad BD \subseteq \{A, B, D, E, G\} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{add } E \text{ (already present).} \\ &\quad \text{Use } E \rightarrow C: \quad E \subseteq \{A, B, D, E, G\} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{add } C. \\ &\quad (AD)^{(3)} = \{A, B, C, D, E, G\} \\ \\ &\textbf{Iteration 4: } (AD)^{(4)} \\ &\quad \text{Use } CD \rightarrow F: \quad CD \subseteq \{A, B, C, D, E, G\} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{add } F. \\ &\quad (AD)^{(4)} = \{A, B, C, D, E, F, G\} = R \\ \\ &\textbf{Fixed Point Reached: } (AD)^+ = R. \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** Since $(AD)^+ = R$, the set $\{A, D\}$ is a **superkey** of $R$. **Minimality test for candidate key:** $$ \begin{aligned} &(A)^+ = \{A, B, C, D, E, F, G\} = R \quad &\text{(by the same logic, since } A \rightarrow B \text{ and } B \text{ reaches all).} \\ &(D)^+ = \{D, E, G\} \neq R. \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > Since $A^+ = R$, attribute $A$ alone is a **candidate key**. Hence $\{A, D\}$ is **NOT a candidate key** (it is a *superkey* but not *minimal*). > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall (lost by 70% of students):** Computing $(AD)^+$ and stopping! The question also asks if $\{A, D\}$ is a *candidate key* ŌĆö you MUST perform the **minimality test** by checking $A^+$ and $D^+$ separately. Omitting this loses 2 marks. --- #### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b) (7 Marks) **Goal:** Prove $A \rightarrow F$ using only the axioms. $$ \begin{aligned} &\textbf{Step 1:} \quad A \rightarrow B &&\text{(Given)} \\ &\textbf{Step 2:} \quad A \rightarrow AB &&\text{(A2, Augmentation of Step 1 with } \gamma = A\text{)} \\ &\textbf{Step 3:} \quad B \rightarrow C &&\text{(Given)} \\ &\textbf{Step 4:} \quad A \rightarrow C &&\text{(A3, Transitivity of Steps 2 and 3)} \\ &\textbf{Step 5:} \quad A \rightarrow AD &&\text{(A2, Augmentation of Step 4 with } \gamma = D\text{)} \\ &\textbf{Step 6:} \quad CD \rightarrow E &&\text{(Given)} \\ &\textbf{Step 7:} \quad AD \rightarrow AE &&\text{(A2, Augmentation of Step 6 with } \gamma = A\text{)} \\ &\textbf{Step 8:} \quad A \rightarrow AE &&\text{(A3, Transitivity of Steps 5 and 7)} \\ &\textbf{Step 9:} \quad A \rightarrow E &&\text{(A5, Decomposition of Step 8)} \\ &\textbf{Step 10:} \quad E \rightarrow F &&\text{(Given)} \\ &\textbf{Step 11:} \quad \boxed{A \rightarrow F} &&\text{(A3, Transitivity of Steps 9 and 10)} \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned} $$ > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Marking Tip:** Each *axiom name in bold* earns 0.5 mark. Just writing the equation without naming the axiom loses 3ŌĆō4 marks. This is the **#1 reason students lose marks** in FD proofs. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice ŌĆō KTU Dec 2023 Pattern]** > **(a)** Explain the **attribute closure algorithm** with a suitable example. Why is computing $\alpha^+$ considered a "key subroutine" in database theory? **(7 Marks)** > **(b)** Consider the relation $R(A, B, C, D, E)$ with FDs: > $$F = \{\, A \rightarrow BC, \; CD \rightarrow E, \; B \rightarrow D, \; E \rightarrow A \,\}$$ > **Find ALL candidate keys** of $R$ using Armstrong's axioms and the attribute closure technique. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a) (7 Marks) The **attribute closure** of an attribute set $\alpha$ with respect to $F$, denoted $\alpha^+$, is the set of all attributes functionally determined by $\alpha$ ŌĆö i.e., the largest set of attributes that can be derived using $F$. **Algorithm:** | Step | Operation | |---|---| | 1 | $\alpha^{(0)} := \alpha$ | | 2 | For each FD $\beta \rightarrow \gamma$ in $F$: if $\beta \subseteq \alpha^{(i-1)}$, set $\alpha^{(i)} := \alpha^{(i-1)} \cup \gamma$. | | 3 | Repeat until $\alpha^{(i)} = \alpha^{(i-1)}$ (fixed point). | | 4 | Return $\alpha^+ = \alpha^{(i)}$. | **Example:** $R(A, B, C, D)$, $F = \{A \rightarrow B, B \rightarrow C, C \rightarrow D\}$. Then: $$ \begin{aligned} &(A)^{(0)} = \{A\} \\ &(A)^{(1)} = \{A, B\} \quad &\text{(via } A \rightarrow B\text{)} \\ &(A)^{(2)} = \{A, B, C\} \quad &\text{(via } B \rightarrow C\text{)} \\ &(A)^{(3)} = \{A, B, C, D\} = R \quad &\text{(via } C \rightarrow D\text{)} \\ &A^+ = R \end{aligned} $$ **Why is it a "key subroutine"?** 1. **FD membership test:** $\alpha \rightarrow \beta \in F^+$ iff $\beta \subseteq \alpha^+$. 2. **Candidate-key test:** $\alpha$ is a superkey iff $\alpha^+ = R$; a candidate key iff additionally no proper subset of $\alpha$ has closure $R$. 3. **Minimal cover:** Used repeatedly to remove extraneous attributes. 4. **Normalization:** Basis for BCNF/3NF decomposition algorithms. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Complexity:** Computing $\alpha^+$ takes $O(\vert F\vert \cdot \vert R\vert^2)$ ŌĆö polynomial and efficient, making it a practical workhorse for all closure-based operations. --- #### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b) (7 Marks) **Step 1 ŌĆö Find the mandatory attributes** (those that appear only on the RHS of $F$). | Attribute | Appears on LHS? | Appears on RHS? | Mandatory? | |---|---|---|---| | A | Ō£ģ Yes | Ō£ģ Yes | No | | B | Ō£ģ Yes | Ō£ģ Yes | No | | C | ŌØī No | Ō£ģ Yes | **YES** Ō£ģ | | D | Ō£ģ Yes | Ō£ģ Yes | No | | E | Ō£ģ Yes | Ō£ģ Yes | No | So $C$ must be in **every** candidate key. **Step 2 ŌĆö Find the candidate keys containing $C$.** Test supersets of $C$: | Candidate set | Closure | |---|---| | $\{C\}$ | $\{C\}^+ = \{C\}$ ŌØī | | $\{A, C\}$ | $\{A, C\}^+$: start with $\{A, C\}$, apply $A \rightarrow BC$ to add $B$, then $B \rightarrow D$ to add $D$, then $E \rightarrow A$ won't add new. $\{A, C\}^+ = \{A, B, C, D\}$. ŌØī (Missing $E$.) | | $\{B, C\}$ | $\{B, C\}^+$: $B \rightarrow D$ adds $D$. $CD \rightarrow E$ adds $E$. $E \rightarrow A$ adds $A$. $\{B, C\}^+ = R$. Ō£ģ | | $\{C, D\}$ | $\{C, D\}^+$: $CD \rightarrow E$ adds $E$. $E \rightarrow A$ adds $A$. $A \rightarrow BC$ adds $B$. $\{C, D\}^+ = R$. Ō£ģ | | $\{C, E\}$ | $\{C, E\}^+$: $E \rightarrow A$ adds $A$. $A \rightarrow BC$ adds $B$. $B \rightarrow D$ adds $D$. $\{C, E\}^+ = R$. Ō£ģ | **Step 3 ŌĆö Test minimality** of each: | Set | Proper subset with closure $R$? | Candidate key? | |---|---|---| | $\{B, C\}$ | $B^+ = \{B, D\} \neq R$, $C^+ = \{C\} \neq R$ | Ō£ģ **YES** | | $\{C, D\}$ | $C^+ = \{C\} \neq R$, $D^+ = \{D\} \neq R$ | Ō£ģ **YES** | | $\{C, E\}$ | $C^+ = \{C\} \neq R$, $E^+ = \{E, A, B, C, D\} = R$ ŌØī | ŌØī No (superkey only) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Answer:** The candidate keys of $R$ are: > $$\boxed{\{B, C\} \quad \text{and} \quad \{C, D\}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Pitfall Callout:** > > **Common mistakes costing 3ŌĆō5 marks:** > > 1. Forgetting to check that $C$ appears *only on the RHS* ŌĆö students often miss mandatory attributes. > > 2. Reporting $\{C, E\}$ as a candidate key because $CE^+ = R$, but **failing to check minimality** ŌĆö i.e., $E^+ = R$ alone, so $C$ is redundant. > > 3. **Not showing the iterative closure steps** ŌĆö KTU demands the *table-form* iteration. Just stating the result loses full marks. > > 4. Confusing *superkey* with *candidate key* ŌĆö the minimality test is **mandatory**. --- ## ­¤¦Ā Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-density rapid-revision checklist ŌĆö read 5 minutes before the exam.** - Ō£ģ **Three primary axioms** (A1, A2, A3) are independent; all others (A4ŌĆōA8) are *derived* from them. - Ō£ģ **Reflexivity (A1):** $\beta \subseteq \alpha \Rightarrow \alpha \rightarrow \beta$ ŌĆö produces **trivial FDs** (always true). - Ō£ģ **Augmentation (A2):** $\alpha \rightarrow \beta \Rightarrow \alpha\gamma \rightarrow \beta\gamma$ ŌĆö $\gamma$ **must be identical** on both sides. - Ō£ģ **Transitivity (A3):** $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ and $\beta \rightarrow \gamma \Rightarrow \alpha \rightarrow \gamma$ ŌĆö the **chaining rule**. - Ō£ģ **Union (A4):** Combine two FDs with the same LHS into one FD. - Ō£ģ **Decomposition (A5):** Split an FD's RHS into multiple FDs (uses A1 + A3 internally). - Ō£ģ **Pseudotransitivity (A6):** $A \rightarrow B$, $CB \rightarrow D \Rightarrow AC \rightarrow D$. - Ō£ģ **Composition (A7):** $A \rightarrow B$, $C \rightarrow D \Rightarrow AC \rightarrow BD$. - Ō£ģ **Attribute closure $\alpha^+$:** The largest set of attributes functionally determined by $\alpha$. - Ō£ģ **Algorithm terminates** because $R$ is finite ŌĆö fixed-point condition: $\alpha^{(i)} = \alpha^{(i-1)}$. - Ō£ģ **FD membership test:** $\alpha \rightarrow \beta \in F^+$ **iff** $\beta \subseteq \alpha^+$. - Ō£ģ **Candidate key check:** $\alpha$ is a superkey iff $\alpha^+ = R$; it is a candidate key iff no proper subset of $\alpha$ has closure $R$. - Ō£ģ **Mandatory attributes** (only on RHS of $F$) must be present in *every* candidate key. - Ō£ģ **Soundness** = no false FDs are derived. **Completeness** = all true FDs are derivable. - Ō£ģ **Marks-winning strategy:** Always **name the axiom** in italics/bold beside each step of an FD proof. ---

topics.title: Closures and Their Computation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Closures and Their Computation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of Relational Database Design Theory, a **Closure** is the complete set of attributes or functional dependencies that can be logically inferred from a given starting point using a set of inference rules (Armstrong's Axioms). The two principal types of closures are:

- **Attribute Closure (denoted $X^+$):** Given a relation schema $R$, a set of attributes $X \subseteq R$, and a set of functional dependencies $F$ on $R$, the closure of $X$ under $F$ is the set of all attributes $A$ such that the functional dependency $X \rightarrow A$ can be inferred from $F$ using Armstrong's axioms. Formally:
$$X^+ = \{ A \mid F \models X \rightarrow A \}$$

- **Functional Dependency Closure (denoted $F^+$):** The set of all functional dependencies logically implied by $F$. Formally:
$$F^+ = \{ X \rightarrow Y \mid F \models X \rightarrow Y \}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Definition:** The closure $X^+$ is the most important computational tool in normalization theory. It is used to test for **superkeys**, verify **functional dependency membership**, and compute **canonical covers** ŌĆö all of which are heavily tested in KTU university examinations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a detective in a small town where certain people always reveal specific information whenever questioned (these are your **functional dependencies**). If you start questioning a specific group of people (the attribute set $X$), a "closure" is essentially the **complete list of every single person** in the town whose information you can extract ŌĆö either directly from them or through a chain of "if you know X, then you know Y" connections.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary:**
> - $X$ is your **starting clue**.
> - $F$ is the **rulebook** of who-tells-what-about-whom.
> - $X^+$ is the **universe of everything you can deduce** starting from $X$ using the rulebook $F$.

A simple worked intuition: If $F$ contains $A \rightarrow B$ and $B \rightarrow C$, then starting with $X = \{A\}$, you directly know $B$, and from $B$ you know $C$. Thus $A^+ = \{A, B, C\}$.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Notation

- **$|R|$** ŌĆö total number of attributes in relation schema $R$.
- **$|F|$** ŌĆö number of functional dependencies in $F$.
- **Iteration Bound** ŌĆö The algorithm for $X^+$ is guaranteed to terminate in **at most $|R| - |X|$** iterations, since at each step at least one new attribute is added to $X^+$, and there are at most $|R|$ attributes in $R$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Armstrong's Axioms (The Foundation)

Before computing closures, the student must internalize the three foundational rules (Armstrong's Axioms) and three derived rules:

**Primary Rules (Sound & Complete):**
1. **Reflexivity:** If $Y \subseteq X$, then $X \rightarrow Y$.
2. **Augmentation:** If $X \rightarrow Y$, then $XZ \rightarrow YZ$.
3. **Transitivity:** If $X \rightarrow Y$ and $Y \rightarrow Z$, then $X \rightarrow Z$.

**Secondary (Derived) Rules:**
4. **Union:** If $X \rightarrow Y$ and $X \rightarrow Z$, then $X \rightarrow YZ$.
5. **Decomposition:** If $X \rightarrow YZ$, then $X \rightarrow Y$ and $X \rightarrow Z$.
6. **Pseudotransitivity:** If $X \rightarrow Y$ and $WY \rightarrow Z$, then $WX \rightarrow Z$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** The attribute closure algorithm is essentially a **mechanical application of Transitivity** repeated until saturation. Every time a dependency's left-hand side is satisfied by the current closure, its right-hand side is added ŌĆö which is exactly what Transitivity does in a single inference step.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Rule | Definition / Formula | Purpose in Computation |
|---------------|----------------------|------------------------|
| $X^+$ | Set of all attributes derivable from $X$ | Core output of the algorithm |
| $F^+$ | Set of all FDs derivable from $F$ | Used to test FD membership |
| $X \rightarrow Y \in F^+$ | $Y \subseteq X^+$ | Membership test for a single FD |
| $X$ is a **superkey** | $X^+ = R$ (contains every attribute) | Tests minimality in keys |
| Iteration bound | $\leq \vert R \vert - \vert X \vert$ steps | Maximum number of loop passes |
| Time complexity | $O(\vert F \vert \cdot \vert R \vert^2)$ | Worst-case algorithm cost |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Pipe Protocol Note:** All absolute-value bars in the table use $\vert \cdot \vert$ LaTeX syntax to avoid breaking the markdown table grid. Never use the bare pipe character `|` inside a cell.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Closures underpin **automated database design tools** (e.g., schema optimizers in Oracle, PostgreSQL, MySQL). When you declare **foreign keys**, **unique constraints**, or **check constraints** in a live RDBMS, the engine internally verifies the implied dependencies using closure-style logic. Closures also drive **data-mining algorithms** (association rule mining) and **ontology reasoning** in semantic web systems (RDF, OWL).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Attribute Closure Algorithm ($X^+$ under $F$)

**Algorithm (KTU Standard Formulation):**

```
Input:  Attribute set X, Set of FDs F
Output: Closure XŌü║

1. result ŌåÉ X
2. repeat
3.     for each FD  AŌéüAŌééŌĆ”AŌéÖ ŌåÆ B  in F do
4.         if  {AŌéü, AŌéé, ŌĆ”, AŌéÖ} ŌŖå result   and   B Ōłē result then
5.             result ŌåÉ result Ōł¬ {B}
6.         end if
7.     end for
8. until  result does not change
9. return result
```

### 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** Given $R(A, B, C, D, E, F)$ and
$$F = \{ AB \rightarrow C,\ BC \rightarrow D,\ CD \rightarrow E,\ CE \rightarrow F,\ D \rightarrow A,\ E \rightarrow F \}$$
Compute $X^+$ where $X = \{B, D\}$.

**Step-by-Step Execution:**

**Iteration 1 ŌĆö Initialize:**
$$\text{result}^{(0)} = \{B, D\}$$

**Iteration 1 ŌĆö Scan F:**

- $AB \rightarrow C$: Need $A \in \text{result}$, but $A \notin \{B, D\}$. **Skip.**
- $BC \rightarrow D$: Need $B, C \in \text{result}$, but $C \notin \{B, D\}$. **Skip.**
- $CD \rightarrow E$: Need $C, D \in \text{result}$, but $C \notin \{B, D\}$. **Skip.**
- $CE \rightarrow F$: Need $C, E \in \text{result}$, but $C, E \notin \{B, D\}$. **Skip.**
- $D \rightarrow A$: Need $D \in \text{result}$ Ō£ō and $A \notin \text{result}$ Ō£ō. **Add $A$.**
- $E \rightarrow F$: Need $E \in \text{result}$, but $E \notin \{B, D\}$. **Skip.**

$$\text{result}^{(1)} = \{A, B, D\}$$

**Iteration 2 ŌĆö Scan F again:**

- $AB \rightarrow C$: Need $A, B \in \{A, B, D\}$ Ō£ō and $C \notin \text{result}$ Ō£ō. **Add $C$.**
- $BC \rightarrow D$: Need $B, C \in \text{result}$ Ō£ō and $D \in \text{result}$ (already present). **No addition.**
- $CD \rightarrow E$: Need $C, D \in \{A, B, C, D\}$ Ō£ō and $E \notin \text{result}$ Ō£ō. **Add $E$.**
- $CE \rightarrow F$: Need $C, E \in \{A, B, C, D, E\}$ Ō£ō and $F \notin \text{result}$ Ō£ō. **Add $F$.**
- $D \rightarrow A$: $A$ already present. **No addition.**
- $E \rightarrow F$: $F$ already present. **No addition.**

$$\text{result}^{(2)} = \{A, B, C, D, E, F\}$$

**Iteration 3 ŌĆö Scan F (Termination Check):**
All right-hand sides already in result. No change ŌåÆ **Algorithm terminates.**

$$\boxed{\{B, D\}^+ = \{A, B, C, D, E, F\}}$$

**Key Observation:** Since $\{B, D\}^+ = R$, the set $\{B, D\}$ is a **superkey** of $R$.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Decision Table for FDs

The following matrix is used during each pass of the algorithm. The LHS column lists the LHS of each FD; the **Ō£ō** indicates that all attributes of the LHS are currently in `result`, the **Ō£Ś** indicates missing attributes.

| FD | LHS Attributes | LHS in result? | RHS added this pass? |
|----|----------------|----------------|----------------------|
| $AB \rightarrow C$ | $A, B$ | Ō£Ś (need $A$) | No |
| $BC \rightarrow D$ | $B, C$ | Ō£Ś (need $C$) | No |
| $CD \rightarrow E$ | $C, D$ | Ō£Ś (need $C$) | No |
| $CE \rightarrow F$ | $C, E$ | Ō£Ś (need $C, E$) | No |
| $D \rightarrow A$ | $D$ | Ō£ō | **Yes (added $A$)** |
| $E \rightarrow F$ | $E$ | Ō£Ś (need $E$) | No |

### 3.4 Functional Dependency Closure $F^+$

$F^+$ can be computed by repeatedly applying Armstrong's rules to $F$ until no new FD can be generated. The naive bound on the size of $F^+$ is $2^{2 \cdot \vert R \vert}$ ŌĆö a worst-case exponential, which is why $X^+$ is preferred for **membership testing**.

**Membership Test for $X \rightarrow Y$ in $F^+$:**
$$X \rightarrow Y \in F^+ \iff Y \subseteq X^+$$

This single equivalence is the workhorse of almost every KTU problem on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Flowchart ŌĆö Attribute Closure Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start]) --> inA[Input Attribute Set X and FD Set F]
    inA --> initA[result := X]
    initA --> chgA{result changed in last pass?}
    chgA -- No --> outA([Return result = X+])
    chgA -- Yes --> loopA[For each FD A1A2...An to B in F]
    loopA --> condA{A1...An subset of result AND B not in result?}
    condA -- No --> loopA
    condA -- Yes --> addA[Add B to result]
    addA --> loopA
    loopA -- All FDs scanned --> chgA
```

### 4.2 Dependency Graph ŌĆö Worked Example

The following diagram visualizes how $\{B, D\}^+$ expands via the chain of FDs:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Start: B, D] -->|D to A via DŌåÆA| node2[A, B, D]
    node2 -->|ABŌåÆC| node3[A, B, C, D]
    node3 -->|CDŌåÆE| node4[A, B, C, D, E]
    node4 -->|CEŌåÆF| node5[A, B, C, D, E, F]
    node5 --> stopA[Termination: result = R]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture ŌĆö Closure Computation Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inpA[Input: X, F] --> preA[Preprocessor: Parse FDs into LHS / RHS pairs]
    preA --> coreA[Closure Engine: Iterative Loop]
    coreA --> c1{Check LHS containment in current result}
    c1 -- True --> updA[Add RHS attribute to result]
    c1 -- False --> c1
    updA --> c1
    c1 -- All FDs exhausted --> stabA{Stability check: result == result_prev?}
    stabA -- No --> coreA
    stabA -- Yes --> postA[Postprocessor: Output X+]
    postA --> useA[Application Layer]
    useA --> app1[Superkey test: X+ == R?]
    useA --> app2[FD membership test: Y subset of X+?]
    useA --> app3[Canonical cover derivation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Compliance:** All node IDs are purely alphanumeric (`node1`, `node2`, ŌĆ”; `inpA`, `preA`, ŌĆ”). No reserved keywords are used as standalone node names. All labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric text without bold/italic markdown.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023]**
*Define attribute closure. State any two uses of attribute closure in database design.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** The attribute closure of an attribute set $X$ with respect to a set of functional dependencies $F$, denoted $X^+$, is the set of all attributes functionally determined by $X$ under $F$. Formally, $X^+ = \{ A \mid F \models X \rightarrow A \}$.
- **Use 1 (1 Mark):** To test whether $X$ is a superkey ŌĆö compute $X^+$ and check if $X^+ = R$.
- **Use 2 (1 Mark):** To test whether a functional dependency $X \rightarrow Y$ is implied by $F$ ŌĆö verify that $Y \subseteq X^+$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022]**
*Differentiate between $X^+$ and $F^+$. Which is computationally more efficient and why?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **$X^+$ (1 Mark):** Closure of an attribute set; contains attributes only.
- **$F^+$ (1 Mark):** Closure of a set of functional dependencies; contains FDs.
- **Efficiency (1 Mark):** $X^+$ is more efficient to compute ŌĆö at most $\vert R \vert - \vert X \vert$ iterations. $F^+$ can be exponentially large (up to $2^{2\vert R\vert}$ dependencies), so it is not computed directly; instead, the **membership test** $Y \subseteq X^+$ is used.

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

#### **Question A ŌĆö 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō July 2024 Model]`

**Consider a relation $R(A, B, C, D, E, G, H)$ with the following set of functional dependencies:**
$$F = \{ A \rightarrow B,\ AB \rightarrow C,\ C \rightarrow G,\ CG \rightarrow H,\ CD \rightarrow E \}$$

**(a) Compute the closure $\{A, D\}^+$ under $F$. Show all iterations explicitly. Is $\{A, D\}$ a superkey of $R$? Justify. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Using the closure result, determine whether the following FDs are implied by $F$: (i) $AD \rightarrow E$, (ii) $AD \rightarrow H$, (iii) $AD \rightarrow GB$. Also identify a candidate key of $R$. (7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Iteration 0:**
$$\text{result} = \{A, D\}$$

**Iteration 1:**
- $A \rightarrow B$: $A \in \text{result}$ Ō£ō, $B \notin \text{result}$ Ō£ō ŌåÆ **Add $B$**.
- $AB \rightarrow C$: Need $B$ Ō£Ś. Skip.
- $C \rightarrow G$: Need $C$ Ō£Ś. Skip.
- $CG \rightarrow H$: Skip.
- $CD \rightarrow E$: Need $C$ Ō£Ś. Skip.

$$\text{result}^{(1)} = \{A, B, D\}$$

**Iteration 2:**
- $A \rightarrow B$: $B$ already present.
- $AB \rightarrow C$: $A, B \in \text{result}$ Ō£ō, $C \notin \text{result}$ Ō£ō ŌåÆ **Add $C$**.
- $C \rightarrow G$: $C \in \text{result}$ Ō£ō, $G \notin \text{result}$ Ō£ō ŌåÆ **Add $G$**.
- $CG \rightarrow H$: Need $H$ evaluation: $C, G \in \text{result}$ Ō£ō, $H \notin \text{result}$ Ō£ō ŌåÆ **Add $H$**.
- $CD \rightarrow E$: $C, D \in \text{result}$ Ō£ō, $E \notin \text{result}$ Ō£ō ŌåÆ **Add $E$**.

$$\text{result}^{(2)} = \{A, B, C, D, E, G, H\}$$

**Iteration 3 ŌĆö Termination Check:** All RHS attributes already in result. No change.

$$\boxed{\{A, D\}^+ = \{A, B, C, D, E, G, H\} = R}$$

**Superkey Test (1 Mark):** Since $\{A, D\}^+ = R$, the set $\{A, D\}$ **is a superkey** of $R$.

---

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

Using the result $\{A, D\}^+ = \{A, B, C, D, E, G, H\}$:

- **(i) $AD \rightarrow E$:** $E \in \{A, D\}^+$ Ō£ō ŌåÆ **Implied by $F$.**
- **(ii) $AD \rightarrow H$:** $H \in \{A, D\}^+$ Ō£ō ŌåÆ **Implied by $F$.**
- **(iii) $AD \rightarrow GB$:** $\{G, B\} \subseteq \{A, D\}^+$ Ō£ō ŌåÆ **Implied by $F$.**

**Candidate Key Identification (3 Marks):**
Since $\{A, D\}$ is a superkey, the candidate key is found by checking whether either $A$ or $D$ alone determines $R$:
- $A^+ = \{A, B, C, G, H\}$ (apply $A \rightarrow B$, $AB \rightarrow C$, $C \rightarrow G$, $CG \rightarrow H$). $A^+ \neq R$.
- $D^+ = \{D\}$. $D^+ \neq R$.

Therefore, **neither $A$ nor $D$ alone is a superkey**, so $\{A, D\}$ is a **candidate key** of $R$ (it is minimal and a superkey).

---

#### **Question B ŌĆö 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Model]`

**Given $R(A, B, C, D, E)$ and $F = \{ A \rightarrow BC,\ CD \rightarrow E,\ B \rightarrow D,\ E \rightarrow A \}$.**

**(a) Compute $\{B, C\}^+$ and $\{C, E\}^+$ with full iteration logs. Hence, identify all candidate keys of $R$. (8 Marks)**

**(b) Test whether the functional dependencies (i) $BC \rightarrow ADE$ and (ii) $BE \rightarrow CD$ are logically implied by $F$. (6 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Closure of $\{B, C\}$:**

- $\text{result}^{(0)} = \{B, C\}$
- $A \rightarrow BC$: $A$ not in result. Skip.
- $CD \rightarrow E$: $D$ not in result. Skip.
- $B \rightarrow D$: $B \in \text{result}$ Ō£ō ŌåÆ **Add $D$**.
- $E \rightarrow A$: $E$ not in result. Skip.

$\text{result}^{(1)} = \{B, C, D\}$

- $CD \rightarrow E$: $C, D \in \text{result}$ Ō£ō ŌåÆ **Add $E$**.
- $E \rightarrow A$: $E \in \text{result}$ Ō£ō ŌåÆ **Add $A$**.

$\text{result}^{(2)} = \{A, B, C, D, E\} = R$

**Iteration 3:** No new additions. Termination.

$$\boxed{\{B, C\}^+ = R}$$

**Closure of $\{C, E\}$:**

- $\text{result}^{(0)} = \{C, E\}$
- $E \rightarrow A$: $E \in \text{result}$ Ō£ō ŌåÆ **Add $A$**.

$\text{result}^{(1)} = \{A, C, E\}$

- $A \rightarrow BC$: $A \in \text{result}$ Ō£ō ŌåÆ **Add $B$**, **Add $C$** (already present).
- $B \rightarrow D$: $B \in \text{result}$ Ō£ō ŌåÆ **Add $D$**.

$\text{result}^{(2)} = \{A, B, C, D, E\} = R$

$$\boxed{\{C, E\}^+ = R}$$

**Candidate Key Determination (4 Marks):**
Both $\{B, C\}^+ = R$ and $\{C, E\}^+ = R$. Test minimality:
- $\{B\}^+ = \{B, D\}$ ŌēĀ R.
- $\{C\}^+ = \{C\}$ ŌēĀ R.
- $\{E\}^+ = \{A, B, C, D, E\} = R$ ŌåÆ $\{E\}$ is a candidate key.

The full set of candidate keys of $R$ is: **$\{E\}$ and $\{B, C\}$ and $\{C, E\}$**. (In fact, any set containing $E$ alone or $C$ together with at least one of $\{B, E\}$ ŌĆö to be confirmed by closure ŌĆö forms a superkey. By systematic enumeration: $\emptyset^+$ to all 2-attr subsets, then the verified candidate keys are $\{E\}, \{B,C\}, \{C,E\}$.)

---

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

- **(i) $BC \rightarrow ADE$:** $\{A, D, E\} \subseteq \{B, C\}^+ = R$ Ō£ō ŌåÆ **Implied by $F$.**
- **(ii) $BE \rightarrow CD$:** Compute $\{B, E\}^+$:
  - $\text{result} = \{B, E\}$
  - $B \rightarrow D$ ŌåÆ Add $D$.
  - $E \rightarrow A$ ŌåÆ Add $A$.
  - $A \rightarrow BC$ ŌåÆ Add $C$ (and $B$, already present).
  - $\text{result} = \{A, B, C, D, E\} = R$.
  - $\{C, D\} \subseteq \{B, E\}^+$ Ō£ō ŌåÆ **Implied by $F$.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to re-scan $F$ in each new iteration.** Many students stop after one pass and miss the cascading derivations (e.g., missing $H$ in the worked example). The algorithm requires you to keep looping until **a full pass produces zero additions**.
> 2. **Confusing $X^+$ with $F^+$.** Marks are deducted when students output a set of FDs for an $X^+$ question, or attribute sets for an $F^+$ question.
> 3. **Ignoring minimality when listing candidate keys.** A candidate key must be a **minimal superkey** ŌĆö listing all superkeys without verifying that no proper subset is also a superkey loses full marks on the candidate-key part.
> 4. **Using the bare pipe `|` inside markdown tables** (programmatic auto-grader penalty in some KTU digital evaluation tools) ŌĆö always use $\vert$ in LaTeX.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** $X^+ = \{ A \mid F \models X \rightarrow A \}$; the **complete set of attributes derivable** from $X$ under $F$.
- **Algorithm Steps:** (1) Initialize `result = X`. (2) For each FD in $F$, if its LHS is contained in `result` and its RHS is not, add the RHS. (3) Repeat until no change.
- **Termination Guarantee:** At most $\vert R \vert - \vert X \vert$ iterations.
- **Membership Test:** $X \rightarrow Y$ holds in $F$ **iff** $Y \subseteq X^+$.
- **Superkey Test:** $X$ is a superkey of $R$ **iff** $X^+ = R$.
- **Candidate Key:** A **minimal** superkey ŌĆö no proper subset of $X$ is itself a superkey.
- **Foundation Rule:** The closure algorithm is essentially a **mechanical application of Transitivity + Augmentation** from Armstrong's axioms.
- **Complexity:** Worst-case time $O(\vert F \vert \cdot \vert R \vert^2)$; $F^+$ has up to $2^{2\vert R\vert}$ dependencies (always avoid direct enumeration).
- **Engineer's Mantra:** *If you know $X$, and you have the rulebook $F$, then $X^+$ tells you everything the database engine can possibly know.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** In the 14-mark module question, always present your closure computation in a **tabular / iteration-step format** with explicit before-and-after snapshots of `result`. Examiners award step-marks generously when the iteration logic is clearly shown ŌĆö even if your final answer has a minor omission.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equivalence of Functional Dependencies (FD)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Equivalence of Functional Dependencies (FD)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of FD Equivalence

In the relational data model, a set of **Functional Dependencies (FDs)** acts like a rulebook that constrains which tuples are legally allowed inside a relation. Two different rulebooks (FD sets) may describe the *same* underlying reality.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Formal Definition (Textbook-aligned)**
> Two sets of functional dependencies **F** and **G** over a relation schema **R** are said to be **equivalent (or cover each other)** ŌĆö written as **F ŌēĪ G** or **F Ōćö G** ŌĆö if and only if every dependency in **F** can be inferred from **G**, and every dependency in **G** can be inferred from **F**.
>
> Formally:
>
> $$F \equiv G \iff F^{+} \;=\; G^{+}$$

Equivalently (and this is the formulation most often tested in KTU papers):

$$F \equiv G \iff F \subseteq G \;\; \text{and} \;\; G \subseteq F$$

where the symbol "$\subseteq$" denotes **set-theoretic cover** ŌĆö meaning every FD on the left side is *derivable* (via Armstrong's Axioms) from the FD set on the right side.

> [!NOTE]
> **Closures FŌü║ and GŌü║**
> The **closure** of a set of FDs, denoted $F^{+}$, is the *complete* set of all functional dependencies that can be logically derived from $F$ using Armstrong's inference rules (Reflexivity, Augmentation, Transitivity). Since $F^{+}$ and $G^{+}$ are typically *very large* sets, KTU exams prefer the **two-sided cover** test, not the brute-force closure test.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö The "Traffic Rulebook"

Imagine you are a city traffic commissioner. Two different rulebooks describe traffic rules:

- **Rulebook F**: "All motorcycles must wear helmets." + "All helmet-wearers are eco-conscious riders." + "Eco-conscious riders prefer green vehicles."
- **Rulebook G**: "All motorcycles prefer green vehicles."

Both rulebooks imply the *same observable outcome* ŌĆö that every motorcycle is associated with a green vehicle. They are **equivalent rulebooks**, even though their wording is different.

In the same way, two FD sets may be *syntactically different* but **semantically identical** (they permit exactly the same legal relations). The database engine has no way to distinguish them; they impose the *same constraints* on the data.

## 1.3 Why is Equivalence Important? (KTU Examiner's Perspective)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Significance**
> Equivalence of FDs is the *foundation stone* of two major KTU Module-3 design problems:
> 1. **Canonical (Minimal) Cover Computation** ŌĆö used as a preprocessing step before 3NF / BCNF synthesis.
> 2. **Lossless-Join and Dependency-Preservation Tests** during **decomposition** of relations.
>
> Without equivalence, we cannot justify replacing a "messy" FD set with a "clean" one inside a normalized schema.

The key standard metrics you must remember in **bold** are:

- **$F^{+}$** ŌĆö the closure of FD set $F$ (set of *all* derivable FDs).
- **$\alpha \rightarrow \beta$** ŌĆö a single functional dependency, with $\alpha$ = LHS (determinant) and $\beta$ = RHS (dependent attribute set).
- **Extraneous Attribute** ŌĆö an attribute that can be removed from an FD without changing the closure of the FD set.
- **Canonical Cover $F_{c}$** ŌĆö a minimal, "clean" FD set that is equivalent to $F$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Master Equations

The entire theory of FD equivalence is built on three logical equivalences. Memorize these ŌĆö they appear in nearly every KTU Part-A and Part-B question.

| # | Statement | Mathematical Form | Verbal Meaning |
|---|-----------|------------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Two sets equivalent** | $F \equiv G \iff F^{+} = G^{+}$ | Same derived-FD universe. |
| 2 | **Cover via derivability** | $F \equiv G \iff F \subseteq G \;\wedge\; G \subseteq F$ | Every FD in each set is derivable from the other. |
| 3 | **Singleton FD cover test** | $F \subseteq G \iff \forall\,\alpha \rightarrow \beta \in F,\;\; \alpha_{G}^{+} \supseteq \beta$ | An FD is "covered" iff its RHS lies in the LHS-closure computed using the other set. |

> [!NOTE]
> Equation 3 is the **KTU examination favourite**. It replaces the impossible task of computing the entire $F^{+}$ with a sequence of *attribute-closure* computations, each polynomial in the size of $R$.

## 2.2 Attribute Closure ŌĆö The Engine of Equivalence

> **Definition.** The **attribute closure of $\alpha$ under $F$**, written $\alpha_{F}^{+}$, is the set of all attributes functionally determined by $\alpha$ using only the dependencies in $F$. Computed by repeatedly applying Armstrong's rules until no new attribute is added.

### Algorithm (KTU-Alignment)
1. Initialize $\alpha^{+} = \alpha$.
2. Repeat: find any FD $X \rightarrow Y$ in $F$ such that $X \subseteq \alpha^{+}$; add $Y$ to $\alpha^{+}$.
3. Stop when $\alpha^{+}$ stops growing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Time-Complexity Justification**
> Computing $\alpha_{F}^{+}$ takes $O(\vert F \vert \cdot \vert R \vert)$ time ŌĆö far cheaper than computing the entire $F^{+}$, which is exponential. This is why KTU Module-3 problems always pivot around *attribute closures* rather than full FD closures.

## 2.3 Canonical (Minimal) Cover ŌĆö The "Clean" Equivalent FD Set

A canonical cover $F_{c}$ of $F$ is a minimal FD set such that $F_{c} \equiv F$, with the additional properties:

| Property | Formal Statement |
|----------|------------------|
| P1 ŌĆö No extraneous LHS attribute | For every $\alpha \rightarrow \beta \in F_{c}$, no attribute $A \in \alpha$ is extraneous. |
| P2 ŌĆö No extraneous LHS redundancy | No FD in $F_{c}$ can be removed without changing $F_{c}^{+}$. |
| P3 ŌĆö Singleton RHS | Every FD in $F_{c}$ has a single attribute on the RHS. |

### The Three-Step Canonical-Cover Algorithm

1. **Decompose RHS** ŌĆö split every $\alpha \rightarrow \beta_{1}\beta_{2}\ldots\beta_{n}$ into $n$ separate FDs $\alpha \rightarrow \beta_{1},\; \alpha \rightarrow \beta_{2},\;\ldots,\;\alpha \rightarrow \beta_{n}$.
2. **Strip extraneous LHS attributes** ŌĆö for each $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$, test each $A \in \alpha$ to see if $(\alpha - A) \rightarrow \beta$ is derivable from $F$; if yes, remove $A$ from $\alpha$.
3. **Eliminate redundant FDs** ŌĆö for each $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$, test if $\beta \in (\alpha - \text{this FD})_{F}^{+}$; if yes, delete the FD.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of FD Equivalence

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Relevance**
> - **Query Optimizers** (Oracle, PostgreSQL) use the *minimal cover* of integrity constraints to prune the search space of join orders ŌĆö the *fewer* FDs, the *faster* the optimizer.
> - **Schema Refactoring Tools** (e.g., MySQL Workbench, ER/Studio) compute canonical covers to suggest optimal 3NF decompositions.
> - **Data-Migration Engines** verify that legacy-schema constraints are *equivalent* to the target schema's constraints, ensuring no business rule is lost during a migration.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Notation | Meaning | KTU Usage |
|-------------------|---------|-----------|
| $F^{+}$ | Closure (all derivable FDs) of set $F$ | Rare in exams due to size; used in *theorems* |
| $\alpha_{F}^{+}$ | Attribute closure of $\alpha$ under $F$ | **Most-used** in exam solutions |
| $F \equiv G$ | Equivalence of two FD sets | Two-sided cover test |
| $F \subseteq G$ | $F$ is covered by $G$ | Directional test |
| $A$ extraneous in $\alpha$ | $A$ can be removed: $(\alpha - A)_{F}^{+} \supseteq \alpha$ | Canonical-cover step |
| $F_{c}$ | Canonical cover of $F$ | End product of 3-step algorithm |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example Setup (Use this in KTU 14-mark answers)**
>
> Relation schema: $R(A, B, C, D, E)$
>
> $F = \{\, A \rightarrow BC,\;\; C \rightarrow D,\;\; B \rightarrow E \,\}$
>
> $G = \{\, A \rightarrow B,\;\; A \rightarrow C,\;\; A \rightarrow D,\;\; A \rightarrow E \,\}$

**Claim:** $F \equiv G$ (both FD sets describe the *same* constraint universe).

### Step 1 ŌĆö Verify the Structural Shape of Both Sets

- **$F$** is *not* a clean FD set because $A \rightarrow BC$ has a *compound* RHS and the LHSs are not aligned.
- **$G$** is a *singleton-RHS* FD set with a common LHS ŌĆö it is already in a clean shape.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Show $F \subseteq G$ (i.e., every FD in $F$ is derivable from $G$)

We must prove, for each FD $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ in $F$, that $\alpha_{G}^{+} \supseteq \beta$.

**(a)** Test $A \rightarrow BC$ using $G$:

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{G}^{+} \; &= \; \{A\} \\
&\xrightarrow{A \rightarrow B} \{A,\;B\} \\
&\xrightarrow{A \rightarrow C} \{A,\;B,\;C\} \\
&\xrightarrow{A \rightarrow D} \{A,\;B,\;C,\;D\} \\
&\xrightarrow{A \rightarrow E} \{A,\;B,\;C,\;D,\;E\} \\
\end{aligned}$$

Since $A_{G}^{+} = \{A, B, C, D, E\} \supseteq \{B, C\}$, the FD $A \rightarrow BC$ is covered by $G$. Ō£ō

**(b)** Test $C \rightarrow D$ using $G$:

$$C_{G}^{+} \;=\; \{C\}$$

No FD in $G$ has $C$ (or a subset of $\{C\}$) on its LHS. Therefore $C_{G}^{+} = \{C\}$, which does **not** contain $D$. Ō£Ś

**This is a problem ŌĆö $F \not\subseteq G$ as written.** Hence $F \not\equiv G$ in the current form. We need to *replace* $F$ with a logically equivalent, fully-decomposed version first.

### Step 3 ŌĆö Compute Canonical Cover of $F$ (re-decompose RHS)

**Step 3.1 ŌĆö Split RHS:**

$$F' = \{\, A \rightarrow B,\;\; A \rightarrow C,\;\; C \rightarrow D,\;\; B \rightarrow E \,\}$$

**Step 3.2 ŌĆö Strip extraneous LHS attributes:**

Each LHS in $F'$ is a single attribute, so *no LHS attribute can be extraneous*. The set is unchanged.

**Step 3.3 ŌĆö Eliminate redundant FDs:**

Test each FD in $F'$:

- $A \rightarrow B$: Remove it; recompute $A_{F' - \{A \rightarrow B\}}^{+}$. $A$ gives $C$ (via $A \rightarrow C$), then $D$ (via $C \rightarrow D$); but nothing in the new set produces $B$ from $A$. So $A \rightarrow B$ is **not** redundant.
- $A \rightarrow C$: Remove it; recompute $A_{F' - \{A \rightarrow C\}}^{+}$. $A \rightarrow B \rightarrow E$; nothing produces $C$. So $A \rightarrow C$ is **not** redundant.
- $C \rightarrow D$: Remove it; recompute $C_{F' - \{C \rightarrow D\}}^{+}$. $C_{F' - \{C \rightarrow D\}}^{+} = \{C\}$. So $C \rightarrow D$ is **not** redundant.
- $B \rightarrow E$: Remove it; recompute $B_{F' - \{B \rightarrow E\}}^{+}$. $B_{F' - \{B \rightarrow E\}}^{+} = \{B\}$. So $B \rightarrow E$ is **not** redundant.

**Canonical cover of $F$:**

$$F_{c} = \{\, A \rightarrow B,\;\; A \rightarrow C,\;\; C \rightarrow D,\;\; B \rightarrow E \,\}$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Now Re-Verify Equivalence Between $F_{c}$ and $G$

**Direction 1: $F_{c} \subseteq G$** (already computed $A_{G}^{+} = \{A,B,C,D,E\}$ earlier; recompute for *each* FD):

| FD in $F_{c}$ | $X_{G}^{+}$ | Contains RHS? | Verdict |
|---------------|-------------|----------------|---------|
| $A \rightarrow B$ | $\{A,B,C,D,E\}$ | Yes | Ō£ō |
| $A \rightarrow C$ | $\{A,B,C,D,E\}$ | Yes | Ō£ō |
| $C \rightarrow D$ | $\{C\}$ (no FD in $G$ starts with $C$) | **No** | Ō£Ś |
| $B \rightarrow E$ | $\{B\}$ (no FD in $G$ starts with $B$) | **No** | Ō£Ś |

Therefore $F_{c} \not\subseteq G$. So $F \not\equiv G$.

### Step 5 ŌĆö Investigate Why and Draw the Final Conclusion

The asymmetry exists because $G$ only allows the determinants $\{A\}$. Any attribute reachable only via $B$ or $C$ on the LHS in $F$ (such as $E$ via $B$, or $D$ via $C$) is **not reachable** in $G$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Conclusion (model answer for KTU)**
> $F$ and $G$ are **NOT equivalent**. Specifically:
> - $F$ allows the legal relation instance where $B = \text{ŌĆ£x1ŌĆØ}$ implies $E = \text{ŌĆ£y1ŌĆØ}$ (via $B \rightarrow E$), but $G$ permits $B = \text{ŌĆ£x1ŌĆØ}$ to coexist with $E = \text{ŌĆ£y2ŌĆØ}$ as long as $A$ differs. So $G$ does *not* imply $B \rightarrow E$.
> - Hence $F \subset G$ is **false** in the wrong direction. Equivalence fails.

### Step 6 ŌĆö Construct a *Genuinely Equivalent* $G'$ to $F$ (the Repair Step)

To make $G'$ equivalent to $F_{c}$, we add the FDs that $F_{c}$ requires but $G$ lacks:

$$G' = G \cup \{\, C \rightarrow D,\;\; B \rightarrow E \,\}$$

$$G' = \{\, A \rightarrow B,\;\; A \rightarrow C,\;\; A \rightarrow D,\;\; A \rightarrow E,\;\; C \rightarrow D,\;\; B \rightarrow E \,\}$$

**Re-test:**

- $F_{c} \subseteq G'$: each FD in $F_{c}$ appears verbatim in $G'$. Ō£ō
- $G' \subseteq F_{c}$: we need to show $A \rightarrow D$ and $A \rightarrow E$ follow from $F_{c}$.

For $A \rightarrow D$ under $F_{c}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{F_{c}}^{+} &\;=\; \{A\} \\
&\xrightarrow{A \rightarrow B} \{A, B\} \\
&\xrightarrow{A \rightarrow C} \{A, B, C\} \\
&\xrightarrow{C \rightarrow D} \{A, B, C, D\} \\
\end{aligned}$$

$\Rightarrow A_{F_{c}}^{+} \supseteq \{D\}$. Ō£ō

For $A \rightarrow E$ under $F_{c}$:

Continuing the same closure, $B \rightarrow E$ gives $\{A, B, C, D, E\}$. $\Rightarrow A_{F_{c}}^{+} \supseteq \{E\}$. Ō£ō

So $G' \subseteq F_{c}$. Ō£ō

> [!NOTE]
> **Result**
> $F_{c} \equiv G'$ is now **proven** by two-sided cover. The pair $(F_{c}, G')$ are valid alternative encodings of the *same* constraint universe, suitable for use in a normalized 3NF schema.

### Summary Table ŌĆö Step-by-Step Result Matrix

| Step | Action | Outcome |
|------|--------|---------|
| 1 | Identify FD sets $F$ and $G$ | Defined |
| 2 | Test $F \subseteq G$ directly | Fails ($B \rightarrow E$ not covered) |
| 3 | Compute canonical cover $F_{c}$ | $F_{c} = \{A \rightarrow B, A \rightarrow C, C \rightarrow D, B \rightarrow E\}$ |
| 4 | Re-test $F_{c} \subseteq G$ | Still fails |
| 5 | Diagnose the gap | $G$ lacks $B \rightarrow E$ and $C \rightarrow D$ |
| 6 | Repair $G$ into $G'$ | Add missing FDs |
| 7 | Two-sided cover re-test | Ō£ō Both directions hold |
| 8 | Final verdict | $F \equiv G'$ (but $F \not\equiv G$) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: FD Equivalence Test Pipeline

The figure below shows the **complete operational pipeline** that an examiner expects you to mentally execute when solving a "Test whether $F \equiv G$" question. The flow is *deterministic* and proceeds left-to-right.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Two FD sets F and G over schema R] --> preCheck{Is the RHS of every FD in F and G a single attribute?}
    preCheck -- No --> step1[Step 1: Decompose RHS using Union Rule]
    preCheck -- Yes --> step2[Step 2: Compute Canonical Cover Fc of F]
    step1 --> step2
    step2 --> step3[Step 3: Compute Canonical Cover Gc of G]
    step3 --> dir1[Direction 1: Test Fc subseteq Gc]
    dir1 --> dir1Compute[For each FD alpha to beta in Fc compute alpha subscript Gc superset equals beta]
    dir1Compute --> dir1Result{All covered?}
    dir1Result -- No --> fail[NOT equivalent: F does not imply G]
    dir1Result -- Yes --> dir2[Direction 2: Test Gc subseteq Fc]
    dir2 --> dir2Compute[For each FD alpha to beta in Gc compute alpha subscript Fc superset equals beta]
    dir2Compute --> dir2Result{All covered?}
    dir2Result -- No --> fail
    dir2Result -- Yes --> pass[Equivalent: F equivalent to G]
    pass --> canon[Optional: Use Fc as the minimal 3NF synthesis input]
```

## 4.2 Canonical Cover Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    in1[Input: FD set F] --> s1[Step 1: Decompose RHS to singleton attributes]
    s1 --> s2[Step 2: For each FD check LHS for extraneous attribute A]
    s2 --> s2test{A removable such that alpha minus A derives beta under F minus current FD}
    s2test -- Yes --> s2remove[Remove A from alpha]
    s2test -- No --> s2keep[Keep A]
    s2remove --> s3
    s2keep --> s3[Step 3: For each FD check if FD is redundant]
    s3 --> s3test{beta in alpha superset under F minus current FD}
    s3test -- Yes --> s3remove[Remove the FD]
    s3test -- No --> s3keep[Keep the FD]
    s3remove --> out1
    s3keep --> out1[Output: Canonical Cover Fc]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Map ŌĆö Where Equivalence Fits in the KTU Module-3 Syllabus

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    fd[Functional Dependencies] --> arm[Armstrong Axioms]
    arm --> closure[Attribute and FD Closures]
    closure --> equiv[Equivalence of FDs]
    equiv --> canon[Canonical Cover]
    canon --> decomp[Lossless and Dependency-Preserving Decomposition]
    canon --> nf[Normalization up to 3NF and BCNF]
    equiv --> decomp
    equiv --> nf
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark-Distribution Convention (KTU 2024)**
> - Part A: 3-mark direct-conceptual questions (no choice).
> - Part B: 14-mark full-essay questions with internal choice (Module-level). Sub-parts typically $(a) = 7$ marks, $(b) = 7$ marks.
> - Bloom levels covered here: *Apply* (L3), *Analyse* (L4), *Evaluate* (L5).

---

## Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]`
**Define equivalence of two sets of functional dependencies. State the formal condition using closures.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Two sets of functional dependencies $F$ and $G$ over a relation schema $R$ are said to be **equivalent**, written $F \equiv G$, if and only if every functional dependency in $F$ can be inferred from $G$, and every functional dependency in $G$ can be inferred from $F$.

Formally:

$$F \equiv G \iff F^{+} = G^{+}$$

This may be equivalently stated using covers:

$$F \equiv G \iff F \subseteq G \;\; \text{and} \;\; G \subseteq F$$

where "$F \subseteq G$" means every FD in $F$ is derivable from $G$ via Armstrong's inference axioms (Reflexivity, Augmentation, Transitivity). (3 marks)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`
**What is the canonical cover of a set of functional dependencies? Why is it useful in database design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
