The **canonical cover** $F_{c}$ of an FD set $F$ is a *minimal*, equivalent set of functional dependencies such that $F_{c} \equiv F$, with the additional properties that (i) every FD has a single attribute on the right-hand side, (ii) no attribute on the left-hand side of any FD is extraneous, and (iii) no FD itself is redundant.

**Utility in database design** (3 marks broken down):

- It provides a **minimal, irredundant** description of all integrity constraints on a relation, which is the input to the **3NF synthesis algorithm**.
- It **reduces the storage cost** of integrity constraints in the DBMS catalog.
- It **simplifies verification** of dependency-preservation in decompositions.
- It is used by the **query optimizer** to prune redundant constraint checks.

---

## Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`

**Consider the relation $R(A, B, C, D, E, G)$ and the following two sets of functional dependencies:**

$$F = \{\, AB \rightarrow C,\;\; C \rightarrow A,\;\; BC \rightarrow D,\;\; ACD \rightarrow B,\;\; D \rightarrow EG,\;\; BE \rightarrow C,\;\; CG \rightarrow BD,\;\; CE \rightarrow AG \,\}$$

$$G = \{\, A \rightarrow B,\;\; A \rightarrow C,\;\; B \rightarrow D,\;\; D \rightarrow EG,\;\; C \rightarrow A \,\}$$

**Determine whether $F$ and $G$ are equivalent. Show all intermediate steps clearly.**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Compute the canonical cover $F_{c}$ of $F$ (7 marks)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Decompose RHS to singleton attributes:**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_1 = \{\, &AB \rightarrow C,\; C \rightarrow A,\; BC \rightarrow D,\; ACD \rightarrow B,\; D \rightarrow E, \\
&D \rightarrow G,\; BE \rightarrow C,\; CG \rightarrow B,\; CG \rightarrow D,\; CE \rightarrow A,\; CE \rightarrow G \,\}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Strip extraneous LHS attributes (test each multi-attribute LHS):**

*For $AB \rightarrow C$:* Check if $A$ is extraneous. Compute $A_{F_1 - \{AB \rightarrow C\}}^{+}$. $A$ alone yields nothing (no FD has just $A$ on LHS). So $A$ is *not* extraneous. Similarly for $B$. LHS stays as $AB$.

*For $BC \rightarrow D$:* Compute $B_{F_1 - \{BC \rightarrow D\}}^{+} = \{B\}$ ŌĆö no derivation. So $B$ not extraneous. Compute $C_{F_1 - \{BC \rightarrow D\}}^{+} = \{C, A\}$ (via $C \rightarrow A$). Does not contain $D$. So $C$ not extraneous. LHS stays as $BC$.

*For $ACD \rightarrow B$:* Test $A$: $A_{F_1 - \{ACD \rightarrow B\}}^{+} = \{A\}$ ŌĆö nothing. $A$ not extraneous. Test $C$: $C_{F_1 - \{ACD \rightarrow B\}}^{+} = \{C, A\}$. Does not contain $D$ or $B$. $C$ not extraneous. Test $D$: $D_{F_1 - \{ACD \rightarrow B\}}^{+} = \{D, E, G\}$. Does not contain $A$ or $C$. $D$ not extraneous. LHS stays.

*For $BE \rightarrow C$:* $B$ alone yields nothing; $E$ alone yields nothing. LHS stays as $BE$.

*For $CG \rightarrow B$:* $C$ gives $\{C, A\}$; $G$ alone yields nothing. Neither alone gives $B$, so both kept. LHS stays as $CG$.

*For $CG \rightarrow D$:* $C$ gives $\{C, A\}$; $G$ alone yields nothing. Neither alone gives $D$, so both kept.

*For $CE \rightarrow A$:* $C$ gives $\{C, A\} \supseteq A$ Ō£ō. So $C \rightarrow A$ is derivable without using this FD. Therefore $C$ is extraneous in $CE \rightarrow A$. **Delete $E$.** The FD becomes $C \rightarrow A$, which already exists ŌĆö **drop the redundant FD $CE \rightarrow A$**.

*For $CE \rightarrow G$:* Test $C$: $C_{F_1 - \{CE \rightarrow G\}}^{+} = \{C, A\}$. Does not contain $E$ or $G$. So $C$ not extraneous. Test $E$: $E$ alone yields nothing. LHS stays as $CE$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Remove redundant FDs:**

Test each FD by trying to derive it without itself:

- $AB \rightarrow C$: Without it, $AB$ yields nothing. **Not redundant.**
- $C \rightarrow A$: Without it, $C$ yields $\{C\}$; no other path gives $A$. **Not redundant.**
- $BC \rightarrow D$: Without it, $BC$ gives $\{B, C, A\}$ (via $C \rightarrow A$); no $D$. **Not redundant.**
- $ACD \rightarrow B$: Without it, $ACD$ gives $\{A, C, D, E, G\}$ (via $C \rightarrow A, D \rightarrow E, D \rightarrow G$); no $B$. **Not redundant.**
- $D \rightarrow E$: Without it, $D$ yields $\{D, G\}$; no $E$. **Not redundant.**
- $D \rightarrow G$: Without it, $D$ yields $\{D, E\}$; no $G$. **Not redundant.**
- $BE \rightarrow C$: Without it, $BE$ yields $\{B, E\}$; no $C$. **Not redundant.**
- $CG \rightarrow B$: Without it, $CG$ gives $\{C, G, A, D, E\}$; no $B$. **Not redundant.**
- $CG \rightarrow D$: Without it, $CG$ gives $\{C, G, A, B, E\}$ (via $CG \rightarrow B, C \rightarrow A$, and $BE \rightarrow C$ ŌĆö *but wait, $BE$ is not in the closure yet*). Recomputing carefully: $CG$ yields $C$ then $A$, then nothing else without $CG \rightarrow D$. So $D$ is **not derivable**. **Not redundant.**
- $CE \rightarrow G$: Without it, $CE$ yields $\{C, E, A\}$; no $G$. **Not redundant.**

**Canonical cover $F_{c}$:**

$$F_{c} = \{\, AB \rightarrow C,\;\; C \rightarrow A,\;\; BC \rightarrow D,\;\; ACD \rightarrow B,\;\; D \rightarrow E,\;\; D \rightarrow G,\;\; BE \rightarrow C,\;\; CG \rightarrow B,\;\; CG \rightarrow D,\;\; CE \rightarrow G \,\}$$

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Test $F_{c} \equiv G$ (7 marks)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute $F_{c} \subseteq G$:**

For each FD in $F_{c}$, compute LHS-closure under $G$:

| FD in $F_{c}$ | $\alpha_{G}^{+}$ | Contains RHS? | Covered? |
|---------------|------------------|----------------|----------|
| $AB \rightarrow C$ | $\{A,B,C,A,D,E,G\} = \{A,B,C,D,E,G\}$ | Yes (has $C$) | Ō£ō |
| $C \rightarrow A$ | $\{C\}$ (no $C$-LHS in $G$) | **No** | Ō£Ś |

Since $C \rightarrow A$ fails, $F_{c} \not\subseteq G$, and therefore:

> **Conclusion:** $F$ and $G$ are **NOT equivalent**. The dependency $C \rightarrow A$ is in $F$ but cannot be derived from $G$ (because no FD in $G$ has $C$ on the LHS).

A counterexample relation:

| A | B | C | D | E | G |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $a_1$ | $b_1$ | $c_1$ | $d_1$ | $e_1$ | $g_1$ |
| $a_2$ | $b_1$ | $c_1$ | $d_1$ | $e_1$ | $g_1$ |

This instance violates $C \rightarrow A$ (two tuples with same $C$ but different $A$) but **satisfies every FD in $G$** (every $A$ uniquely determines $B, C, D, EG$). Therefore $G$ does not imply $C \rightarrow A$, confirming $F \not\equiv G$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023]` ŌĆö Alternative Choice

**Let $R(P, Q, R, S, T)$ with $F = \{\, P \rightarrow Q,\; PR \rightarrow S,\; Q \rightarrow T,\; S \rightarrow R \,\}$ and $G = \{\, P \rightarrow Q,\; P \rightarrow S,\; Q \rightarrow T,\; S \rightarrow R,\; P \rightarrow T \,\}$. Show that $F \equiv G$ using the two-sided cover method.**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Compute canonical cover of both $F$ and $G$ (7 marks)

**Canonical cover of $F$:**

Step 1 ŌĆö RHS already singleton. Step 2 ŌĆö test extraneous LHS attribute: in $PR \rightarrow S$, is $P$ extraneous? $R_{F}^{+} = \{R\}$ (no $R$-LHS in $F$); is $S$ reachable from $R$ alone? No. So $R \rightarrow S$ does not hold. Is $P$ extraneous? $P_{F - \{PR \rightarrow S\}}^{+} = \{P, Q, T\}$; does not contain $S$. So $P$ is not extraneous. LHS stays as $PR$.

Step 3 ŌĆö Test redundancy: $P \rightarrow Q$: not redundant (cannot derive from $\{PR \rightarrow S, Q \rightarrow T, S \rightarrow R\}$). $PR \rightarrow S$: not redundant. $Q \rightarrow T$: not redundant. $S \rightarrow R$: not redundant. So $F_{c} = F$ as given.

**Canonical cover of $G$:**

$G = \{\, P \rightarrow Q,\; P \rightarrow S,\; Q \rightarrow T,\; S \rightarrow R,\; P \rightarrow T \,\}$.

Step 2 ŌĆö LHSs are all singletons; nothing to strip. Step 3 ŌĆö Test redundancy:

- Test $P \rightarrow T$: Without it, $P_{G - \{P \rightarrow T\}}^{+} = \{P, Q, S, T, R\}$ (via $P \rightarrow Q \rightarrow T$ already in the set, and $P \rightarrow S \rightarrow R$). So $T \in P_{G - \{P \rightarrow T\}}^{+}$. **$P \rightarrow T$ is REDUNDANT ŌĆö delete it.**
- Re-test the others after deletion:

$G' = \{\, P \rightarrow Q,\; P \rightarrow S,\; Q \rightarrow T,\; S \rightarrow R \,\}$

- $P \rightarrow Q$: not redundant (no other path from $P$ to $Q$).
- $P \rightarrow S$: not redundant.
- $Q \rightarrow T$: not redundant.
- $S \rightarrow R$: not redundant.

So $G_{c} = \{\, P \rightarrow Q,\; P \rightarrow S,\; Q \rightarrow T,\; S \rightarrow R \,\}$.

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Prove $F_{c} \equiv G_{c}$ (7 marks)

We have:

$$F_{c} = \{\, P \rightarrow Q,\; PR \rightarrow S,\; Q \rightarrow T,\; S \rightarrow R \,\}$$

$$G_{c} = \{\, P \rightarrow Q,\; P \rightarrow S,\; Q \rightarrow T,\; S \rightarrow R \,\}$$

**Direction 1: $F_{c} \subseteq G_{c}$** (every FD in $F_{c}$ follows from $G_{c}$)

| FD in $F_{c}$ | $\alpha_{G_{c}}^{+}$ | Contains RHS? | Verdict |
|---------------|----------------------|----------------|---------|
| $P \rightarrow Q$ | $\{P, Q, S, T, R\}$ | Yes (has $Q$) | Ō£ō |
| $PR \rightarrow S$ | $\{P, R, Q, S, T\}$ (via $P \rightarrow S$) | Yes (has $S$) | Ō£ō |
| $Q \rightarrow T$ | $\{Q, T\}$ | Yes (has $T$) | Ō£ō |
| $S \rightarrow R$ | $\{S, R\}$ | Yes (has $R$) | Ō£ō |

All four FDs in $F_{c}$ are covered by $G_{c}$. Ō£ō

**Direction 2: $G_{c} \subseteq F_{c}$** (every FD in $G_{c}$ follows from $F_{c}$)

| FD in $G_{c}$ | $\alpha_{F_{c}}^{+}$ | Contains RHS? | Verdict |
|---------------|----------------------|----------------|---------|
| $P \rightarrow Q$ | $\{P, Q, T\}$ | Yes (has $Q$) | Ō£ō |
| $P \rightarrow S$ | $\{P, Q, T\}$ ŌĆö does **not** contain $S$ | **No** | Ō£Ś |

**Uh-oh ŌĆö $P \rightarrow S$ is not covered.** This means $F_{c} \not\equiv G_{c}$ *as stated*.

> **Re-examination:** $F_{c}$ alone does not imply $P \rightarrow S$. For $P \rightarrow S$ to follow from $F_{c}$, we would need either $P$ to determine $S$ via some chain. The only path is $P \rightarrow Q \rightarrow T$ (which dead-ends) or $PR \rightarrow S$ (which needs $R$ on the LHS ŌĆö not present in $P$).

**Counterexample showing $F_{c} \not\subseteq G_{c}$ direction is fine, but $G_{c} \not\subseteq F_{c}$ direction fails:**

| P | Q | R | S | T |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $p_1$ | $q_1$ | $r_1$ | $s_1$ | $t_1$ |
| $p_1$ | $q_1$ | $r_1$ | $s_2$ | $t_1$ |

In this relation, $P, Q, R, T$ are functionally determined by the keys, but $S$ is *not* uniquely determined by $P$ alone. So $F_{c}$ does not imply $P \rightarrow S$, and therefore:

> **Revised Conclusion:** $F \not\equiv G$ as written. The set $F$ is *missing* the FD $P \rightarrow S$ which $G$ includes. However, $G \subseteq F$ fails in the **opposite** direction: $F$'s $PR \rightarrow S$ is *not* derivable from $G$ either (since $G$ has $P \rightarrow S$ but not $PR \rightarrow S$ as a separate axiom; however, $PR \rightarrow S$ *is* derivable from $G$ via augmentation of $P \rightarrow S$, so this direction is actually fine ŌĆö the asymmetry is on the $P \rightarrow S$ side).

**Final exam-ready answer (model solution):** Equivalence fails. The missing dependency in $F$ is the *single-attribute* form $P \rightarrow S$ (whereas $F$ has the *larger-key* $PR \rightarrow S$). Adding $P \rightarrow S$ to $F$ makes them equivalent.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Don't forget to canonicalize BOTH sets** before testing equivalence. A direct cover test on non-minimal sets gives *false negatives* ŌĆö two equivalent sets may *look* unequal.
> 2. **Order of closure computations matters** for time, not correctness ŌĆö but missing any FD on the LHS under test will cost **at least 1 mark** in the 7-mark sub-question.
> 3. **Compound RHS FDs** like $A \rightarrow BC$ are valid in $F$ but **never** in a canonical cover. Examiners explicitly check for this decomposition. (Penalty: 1ŌĆō2 marks.)
> 4. **Common bug:** confusing "$F \subseteq G$" with "$\{\text{FD strings of }F\} \subseteq \{\text{FD strings of }G\}$" ŌĆö *FD cover is a semantic relation, not a syntactic one.* You MUST verify via attribute closures, not string match.
> 5. **Extraneous-LHS-attribute test:** Always use $F$ *with* the FD itself present, and check if $\alpha - A$ derives $\beta$. Removing the FD before the test is a *different* check (that's redundancy test, not extraneous-LHS test).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the KTU Exam Hall**

- **Equivalence formal test:** $F \equiv G \iff F \subseteq G$ and $G \subseteq F$. Use **attribute closures**, not full $F^{+}$, for tractability.
- **Algorithm to test $F \subseteq G$:** For each FD $\alpha \rightarrow \beta \in F$, compute $\alpha_{G}^{+}$ and check that $\beta \subseteq \alpha_{G}^{+}$.
- **Canonical cover $F_{c}$** must satisfy: (i) singleton RHS, (ii) no extraneous LHS attribute, (iii) no redundant FD.
- **Extraneous-attribute test (LHS):** $A$ is extraneous in $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ if $\beta \subseteq (\alpha - A)_{F}^{+}$. Test using the *current* $F$ with the FD present.
- **Redundant-FD test:** $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ is redundant if $\beta \in \alpha_{F - \{\alpha \rightarrow \beta\}}^{+}$.
- **Canonical cover is a prerequisite** for the 3NF synthesis algorithm ŌĆö questions often chain these.
- **Direction of cover matters:** $F \subseteq G$ does *not* imply $G \subseteq F$. Always test both directions.
- **Counterexamples are the gold standard:** when a cover fails, the surest proof is a *two-tuple relation* that satisfies one set and violates the other.
- **Time budget on a 14-mark question:** allocate ~4 minutes for canonical cover, ~6 minutes for two-sided cover, ~3 minutes for counterexample (if any), ~1 minute for final written conclusion.
- **High-yield memory phrases for KTU answers:** "computed attribute closure under $G$"; "no FD in $G$ has $C$ on its LHS, so the closure equals $\{C\}$"; "since $X_{G}^{+} \supseteq Y$, the FD is covered".
- **Common errors to avoid:** (a) forgetting to split compound RHS, (b) using string-set cover instead of semantic cover, (c) concluding equivalence after testing only one direction, (d) omitting the counterexample relation when one direction fails.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Minimal Cover (Proofs Not Required)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBEOT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Minimal Cover (Proofs Not Required) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Minimal Cover (Canonical Cover) ŌĆö KTU 2024 Definition** > A **minimal cover** (also called **canonical cover**) of a set of functional dependencies $F$, denoted $F_c$, is a set of functional dependencies such that: > 1. **Single-Attribute RHS**: Every functional dependency in $F_c$ has exactly one attribute on its right-hand side (RHS). > 2. **No Redundant FDs**: For every functional dependency $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ in $F_c$, removing it changes the closure $F^+$ (i.e., the dependency is essential). > 3. **No Extraneous LHS Attributes**: For every functional dependency $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ in $F_c$, no attribute $A \in \alpha$ can be removed without changing $F^+$. Such an attribute is called an **extraneous attribute**. Mathematically, $F_c$ is a minimal cover of $F$ if: $$F_c \equiv F \quad \text{(F_c logically implies F, and F logically implies F_c)}$$ $$|F_c| \leq |F| \quad \text{(F_c is at least as compact as F)}$$ and $F_c$ satisfies the three minimality properties above. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: "The Lean Recipe"** > Imagine you have a thick recipe book with hundreds of pages, but you only use **30 essential ingredients** to cook a 5-star dish. The **minimal cover** is like that trimmed recipe ŌĆö it contains only the *absolutely necessary* rules, with no redundant instructions and no extra adjectives (extraneous attributes) cluttering the left-hand side. > > - If a rule repeats (e.g., "Add salt, Add salt again") ŌåÆ **Redundant FD** ŌĆö remove it. > - If a rule says "Add salt AND pepper" but you already know pepper is implied ŌåÆ **Extraneous attribute** ŌĆö remove "pepper". > - The result is a **minimal, irredundant, elegant** set of rules that produces the same dish (the same set of constraints). ### 1.3 Key Terminology | Term | Symbol | Meaning | |------|--------|---------| | Functional Dependency Set | $F$ | The original set of FDs given | | Minimal Cover | $F_c$ | The reduced, canonical version of $F$ | | Extraneous Attribute | $A$ | An attribute that can be removed without affecting $F^+$ | | Redundant FD | $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ | A dependency already implied by the remaining FDs | | Attribute Closure | $X^+$ | The set of all attributes functionally determined by $X$ | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Why is the Minimal Cover Important? The minimal cover is the **backbone of database normalization**. It is the critical pre-processing step before: - Synthesizing a **3NF (Third Normal Form)** decomposition (Synthesis Algorithm). - Testing for **lossless-join** and **dependency-preserving** decompositions. - Eliminating redundancy in design specifications. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Fact**: The minimal cover is **NOT unique** ŌĆö multiple minimal covers can exist for the same set $F$. However, every minimal cover of $F$ is equivalent (i.e., they all have the same attribute closures). ### 2.2 Three-Stage Algorithm to Compute $F_c$ The textbook algorithm to find the minimal cover proceeds in three well-defined stages: #### **Stage 1 ŌĆö Canonicalization (Decompose RHS)** Use the **Union/Decomposition Rule** of Armstrong's axioms to rewrite every FD so that the RHS contains exactly one attribute. $$\text{If } \alpha \rightarrow \beta_1 \beta_2 \ldots \beta_n \text{ exists, replace with } \alpha \rightarrow \beta_1, \; \alpha \rightarrow \beta_2, \; \ldots, \; \alpha \rightarrow \beta_n$$ #### **Stage 2 ŌĆö LHS Minimization (Remove Extraneous Attributes)** For each FD $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ in $F_c$, and for each attribute $A \in \alpha$: - Compute the closure $(\alpha - \{A\})^+$ using the *current* set of FDs. - If $\beta \subseteq (\alpha - \{A\})^+$, then $A$ is **extraneous** ŌåÆ remove $A$ from $\alpha$. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pitfall Tip**: Test attributes **one at a time** within each FD. Removing one attribute may make another non-extraneous, but the order in which we test generally does not affect the final minimal cover. #### **Stage 3 ŌĆö Remove Redundant FDs** For each remaining FD $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$: - Compute the closure $\alpha^+$ using the set $F_c - \{\alpha \rightarrow \beta\}$. - If $\beta \subseteq \alpha^+$, then $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ is **redundant** ŌåÆ remove it from $F_c$. ### 2.3 Extraneous Attribute ŌĆö Formal Definition An attribute $A$ is **extraneous** in $\alpha$ of dependency $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ if: **Case A ŌĆö $A$ is in the LHS ($\alpha$):** $$A \text{ is extraneous in } \alpha \iff \beta \subseteq (\alpha - \{A\})^+ \text{ under } F$$ **Case B ŌĆö $A$ is in the RHS ($\beta$):** $$A \text{ is extraneous in } \beta \iff A \in (\alpha)^+ \text{ under } (F - \{\alpha \rightarrow \beta\}) \cup \{\alpha \rightarrow (\beta - A)\}$$ > [!WARNING] > For the KTU 2024 syllabus, the **RHS-extraneous case is rarely tested** since Stage 1 already guarantees a single-attribute RHS. Focus on **LHS-extraneous** detection. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Rule / Formula | Application | |---------|----------------|-------------| | Decomposition Rule | If $\alpha \rightarrow \beta\gamma$, then $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ and $\alpha \rightarrow \gamma$ | Stage 1 | | Extraneous LHS | $A \in \alpha$ is extraneous if $\beta \subseteq (\alpha - \{A\})^+$ | Stage 2 | | Redundant FD | $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ is redundant if $\beta \subseteq \alpha^+$ under $F - \{\alpha \rightarrow \beta\}$ | Stage 3 | | Logical Equivalence | $F_c \equiv F$ iff $F_c^+ = F^+$ | Validation check | | Union Rule | $\{\alpha \rightarrow \beta, \alpha \rightarrow \gamma\} \models \alpha \rightarrow \beta\gamma$ | Reverse of Stage 1 | | Pseudotransitivity | $\alpha \rightarrow \beta, \gamma\beta \rightarrow \delta \models \alpha\gamma \rightarrow \delta$ | Useful in Stage 2 | | Augmentation | $\alpha \rightarrow \beta \models \alpha\gamma \rightarrow \beta\gamma$ | Adds attributes to LHS | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility**: Minimal covers are used in **enterprise database design** (e.g., banking, ERP systems) to extract the *true business rules* from lengthy requirement documents. They form the input to automated normalization tools used in software like Oracle Designer and IBM InfoSphere Data Architect. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Classic KTU Worked Example #1 > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem Statement** (Typical KTU Board Question): > Given $F = \{A \rightarrow BC, \; B \rightarrow AC, \; C \rightarrow A\}$. Find the minimal cover $F_c$. --- #### **Stage 1 ŌĆö Canonicalization (Single-Attribute RHS)** Apply the decomposition rule to every FD with a multi-attribute RHS: Original FD: $A \rightarrow BC$ becomes $A \rightarrow B$ and $A \rightarrow C$. Original FD: $B \rightarrow AC$ becomes $B \rightarrow A$ and $B \rightarrow C$. Original FD: $C \rightarrow A$ already has single attribute RHS. Resulting set after Stage 1: $$F_1 = \{A \rightarrow B, \; A \rightarrow C, \; B \rightarrow A, \; B \rightarrow C, \; C \rightarrow A\}$$ --- #### **Stage 2 ŌĆö LHS Minimization (Remove Extraneous Attributes)** Since all LHS contain only one attribute, **no attribute can be removed** (removing it would leave an empty LHS, which is meaningless by reflexivity). Therefore, all LHS are already minimal. Result: $F_2 = F_1$. --- #### **Stage 3 ŌĆö Remove Redundant FDs** Test each FD against the remaining FDs. **Test 1: Is $A \rightarrow B$ redundant?** Compute $A^+$ using $\{A \rightarrow C, B \rightarrow A, B \rightarrow C, C \rightarrow A\}$. $$ \begin{aligned} A^+ &= \{A\} \\ &\rightarrow C \;\;(\text{by } A \rightarrow C) \\ &\rightarrow \{A, C\} \\ &\rightarrow A \;\;(\text{by } C \rightarrow A, already have A}) \\ &\rightarrow \{A, C\} \end{aligned} $$ Since $B \notin A^+$, $A \rightarrow B$ is **NOT redundant**. Keep it. **Test 2: Is $A \rightarrow C$ redundant?** Compute $A^+$ using $\{A \rightarrow B, B \rightarrow A, B \rightarrow C, C \rightarrow A\}$. $$ \begin{aligned} A^+ &= \{A\} \\ &\rightarrow B \;\;(\text{by } A \rightarrow B) \\ &\rightarrow \{A, B\} \\ &\rightarrow A \;\;(\text{already have}) \\ &\rightarrow C \;\;(\text{by } B \rightarrow C) \\ &\rightarrow \{A, B, C\} \end{aligned} $$ Since $C \in A^+$, $A \rightarrow C$ is **redundant**. Remove it. **Test 3: Is $B \rightarrow A$ redundant?** Compute $B^+$ using $\{A \rightarrow B, A \rightarrow C, B \rightarrow C, C \rightarrow A\}$. $$ \begin{aligned} B^+ &= \{B\} \\ &\rightarrow C \;\;(\text{by } B \rightarrow C) \\ &\rightarrow \{B, C\} \\ &\rightarrow A \;\;(\text{by } C \rightarrow A) \\ &\rightarrow \{A, B, C\} \end{aligned} $$ Since $A \in B^+$, $B \rightarrow A$ is **redundant**. Remove it. **Test 4: Is $B \rightarrow C$ redundant?** Compute $B^+$ using $\{A \rightarrow B, A \rightarrow C, C \rightarrow A\}$. $$ \begin{aligned} B^+ &= \{B\} \end{aligned} $$ No FD applies. $B^+ = \{B\}$. Since $C \notin B^+$, $B \rightarrow C$ is **NOT redundant**. Keep it. **Test 5: Is $C \rightarrow A$ redundant?** Compute $C^+$ using $\{A \rightarrow B, A \rightarrow C, B \rightarrow C\}$. $$ \begin{aligned} C^+ &= \{C\} \end{aligned} $$ No FD applies. $C^+ = \{C\}$. Since $A \notin C^+$, $C \rightarrow A$ is **NOT redundant**. Keep it. --- #### **Final Minimal Cover** $$F_c = \{A \rightarrow B, \; B \rightarrow C, \; C \rightarrow A\}$$ --- ### 3.2 Classic KTU Worked Example #2 (with Extraneous Attribute) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem**: $F = \{A \rightarrow BC, \; B \rightarrow C, \; AB \rightarrow C\}$. Find $F_c$. #### **Stage 1 ŌĆö Decompose RHS** $$F_1 = \{A \rightarrow B, \; A \rightarrow C, \; B \rightarrow C, \; AB \rightarrow C\}$$ #### **Stage 2 ŌĆö LHS Minimization** Test $AB \rightarrow C$. The LHS has $\{A, B\}$. Test removing $A$ first. Compute $B^+$ using $F_1 = \{A \rightarrow B, A \rightarrow C, B \rightarrow C\}$ (temporarily excluding $AB \rightarrow C$): $$B^+ = \{B, C\}$$ Since $C \in B^+$, attribute $A$ is **extraneous** in $AB \rightarrow C$. Remove $A$. Now $AB \rightarrow C$ becomes $B \rightarrow C$, but $B \rightarrow C$ already exists. Hence **the entire FD $AB \rightarrow C$ can be removed**. Updated set: $F_2 = \{A \rightarrow B, \; A \rightarrow C, \; B \rightarrow C\}$. #### **Stage 3 ŌĆö Remove Redundant FDs** Test $A \rightarrow C$: Compute $A^+$ using $\{A \rightarrow B, B \rightarrow C\}$. $$ \begin{aligned} A^+ &= \{A\} \cup \{B\} \cup \{C\} = \{A, B, C\} \end{aligned} $$ Since $C \in A^+$, $A \rightarrow C$ is **redundant**. Remove it. Test $A \rightarrow B$: Compute $A^+$ using $\{A \rightarrow C, B \rightarrow C\}$. $$A^+ = \{A, C\}$$ $B \notin A^+$, so keep $A \rightarrow B$. Test $B \rightarrow C$: Compute $B^+$ using $\{A \rightarrow B\}$. $$B^+ = \{B\}$$ $C \notin B^+$, so keep $B \rightarrow C$. #### **Final Minimal Cover** $$F_c = \{A \rightarrow B, \; B \rightarrow C\}$$ --- ### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix Use this table to **diagnose** which stage a given FD should be processed in: | Situation Observed | Action to Take | Stage | |---------------------|----------------|-------| | FD has multiple attributes on RHS | Decompose using union/decomposition rule | Stage 1 | | FD's LHS has $\geq 2$ attributes | Test each LHS attribute for extraneousness | Stage 2 | | FD's LHS has only 1 attribute | Skip Stage 2 ŌĆö no LHS minimization possible | Stage 2 | | FD is already proven essential by closure test | Keep the FD | Stage 3 | | $X^+$ (without the FD) already contains the RHS | Remove the FD (redundant) | Stage 3 | | $(X - \{A\})^+$ already contains the RHS | Remove $A$ from $X$ (extraneous) | Stage 2 | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Algorithm Flowchart (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Start: Input Set of FDs F"] --> B["Stage 1: Decompose RHS"] B --> C{"Does any FD have
multiple RHS attributes?"} C -->|"Yes"| D["Split FD into
multiple single-RHS FDs
using Decomposition Rule"] D --> C C -->|"No"| E["Stage 2: Minimize LHS"] E --> F{"Any FD with
multi-attribute LHS?"} F -->|"No"| H["Stage 3: Remove Redundant FDs"] F -->|"Yes"| G["For each attribute A in LHS:
Compute alpha minus A closure
If RHS is in closure,
mark A as extraneous"] G --> I["Remove extraneous
attribute A from LHS"] I --> F H --> J["For each FD alpha to beta:
Compute alpha+ using
remaining FDs only"] J --> K{"Is beta in alpha+?"} K -->|"Yes"| L["Remove alpha to beta
(Redundant FD)"] K -->|"No"| M["Keep alpha to beta"] L --> N["End: Minimal Cover F_c"] M --> N ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Minimal Cover Pipeline) ```mermaid flowchart LR INPUT["Raw FD Set F
Multi-attribute RHS allowed"] --> MOD1["Module 1:
Decomposition Engine"] MOD1 --> STAGE1["Normalized F_c Stage 1
Single-attribute RHS"] STAGE1 --> MOD2["Module 2:
Extraneous Attribute Detector
Uses Attribute Closure"] MOD2 --> STAGE2["Minimized F_c Stage 2
No extraneous LHS"] STAGE2 --> MOD3["Module 3:
Redundancy Checker
Cross-validates each FD"] MOD3 --> OUTPUT["Final Minimal Cover F_c
Compact, Irredundant, Canonical"] MOD2 -.-> AUX["Auxiliary:
Attribute Closure Calculator
Computes X+ using F"] AUX -.-> MOD2 AUX -.-> MOD3 ``` ### 4.3 Property Verification Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD P["Minimal Cover F_c
Must Satisfy 3 Properties"] --> P1["Property 1:
Single-attribute RHS
Every FD has one
attribute on right"] P --> P2["Property 2:
No Redundant FDs
Every FD is essential"] P --> P3["Property 3:
No Extraneous LHS
Every LHS attribute is needed"] P1 --> V1["Validated by
Stage 1 output"] P2 --> V2["Validated by
Stage 3 test"] P3 --> V3["Validated by
Stage 2 test"] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2023, Model Paper]** **Q1.** Define the term **minimal cover** of a set of functional dependencies. State any **two properties** that a minimal cover must satisfy. **Model Answer:** A minimal cover (or canonical cover) $F_c$ of a set of functional dependencies $F$ is a reduced set of FDs that is logically equivalent to $F$ (i.e., $F_c^+ = F^+$) and satisfies the minimality conditions. The three essential properties are: 1. Every FD in $F_c$ has a single attribute on its right-hand side (canonical form). 2. No FD in $F_c$ is redundant ŌĆö each dependency is essential and cannot be derived from the remaining FDs. 3. No attribute in the LHS of any FD in $F_c$ is extraneous ŌĆö every attribute on the LHS contributes to the dependency. The minimal cover is used as a pre-processing step for **3NF synthesis** and is a cornerstone of relational database normalization. --- > **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2022, Model Paper]** **Q2.** What is an **extraneous attribute**? Explain with an example. **Model Answer:** An **extraneous attribute** $A$ in a functional dependency $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ is an attribute that can be removed from $\alpha$ (LHS) or from $\beta$ (RHS) without changing the closure of the dependency set. Formally, $A$ is extraneous in $\alpha$ if $\beta \subseteq (\alpha - \{A\})^+$. **Example:** In the dependency $AB \rightarrow C$, if $B^+ \supseteq \{A, C\}$ (i.e., $C \in B^+$), then $A$ is extraneous and the FD simplifies to $B \rightarrow C$. Extraneous attribute detection is a **Stage 2 operation** in the minimal cover algorithm and uses the attribute closure mechanism. --- ### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024, Module 3]** > *Note: Internal choice is provided ŌĆö students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B.* --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Given the set of functional dependencies on a relation schema $R(A, B, C, D, E, F)$: $$F = \{A \rightarrow BC, \; B \rightarrow D, \; CD \rightarrow E, \; CE \rightarrow FA, \; CF \rightarrow A, \; E \rightarrow F\}$$ Compute the **minimal cover** $F_c$ of $F$. Show all three stages. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Verify whether the dependency $CD \rightarrow E$ is **redundant** in $F$. Justify using the **attribute closure** of $CD$. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for (a):** **Stage 1 ŌĆö Decompose RHS using the Decomposition Rule:** Each multi-attribute RHS FD is split: - $A \rightarrow BC$ becomes $A \rightarrow B$ and $A \rightarrow C$. - $CE \rightarrow FA$ becomes $CE \rightarrow F$ and $CE \rightarrow A$. Updated $F_1$: $$F_1 = \{A \rightarrow B, \; A \rightarrow C, \; B \rightarrow D, \; CD \rightarrow E, \; CE \rightarrow F, \; CE \rightarrow A, \; CF \rightarrow A, \; E \rightarrow F\}$$ **Stage 2 ŌĆö LHS Minimization (Remove Extraneous Attributes):** *Test $CD \rightarrow E$:* Test removing $C$: Compute $D^+$ under $F_1$. $$D^+ = \{D\}$$ Since $E \notin D^+$, $C$ is **not extraneous**. Test removing $D$: Compute $C^+$ under $F_1$. $$C^+ = \{C\}$$ Since $E \notin C^+$, $D$ is **not extraneous**. Hence $CD \rightarrow E$ is fully minimal. *Test $CE \rightarrow F$:* Test removing $C$: Compute $E^+$ under $F_1$. $$E^+ = \{E, F\}$$ $F \in E^+$, so $C$ is **extraneous** in $CE \rightarrow F$. Remove $C$. New FD: $E \rightarrow F$. *Test $CE \rightarrow A$:* Test removing $C$: Compute $E^+$ under updated $F_1$. $$E^+ = \{E, F\}$$ $A \notin E^+$, so $C$ is **not extraneous**. Test removing $E$: Compute $C^+$ under $F_1$. $$C^+ = \{C\}$$ $A \notin C^+$, so $E$ is **not extraneous**. Hence $CE \rightarrow A$ stays. *Test $CF \rightarrow A$:* Test removing $C$: Compute $F^+$. $$F^+ = \{F\}$$ $A \notin F^+$, so $C$ is **not extraneous**. Test removing $F$: Compute $C^+$. $$C^+ = \{C\}$$ $A \notin C^+$, so $F$ is **not extraneous**. Hence $CF \rightarrow A$ stays. Updated $F_2$: $$F_2 = \{A \rightarrow B, \; A \rightarrow C, \; B \rightarrow D, \; CD \rightarrow E, \; E \rightarrow F, \; CE \rightarrow A, \; CF \rightarrow A\}$$ **Stage 3 ŌĆö Remove Redundant FDs:** *Test $A \rightarrow B$:* Compute $A^+$ under $F_2 - \{A \rightarrow B\}$. $$A^+ = \{A, C, F, E, D\} \cup \{B\ missing\}. \text{ Specifically: } A \rightarrow C, C^+ = \{C\}, CF \rightarrow A \text{ (no A initially}), A \rightarrow C \Rightarrow A^+ = \{A, C, F, E, D, A\} = \{A, C, D, E, F\}$$ Wait ŌĆö without $A \rightarrow B$, we get $A \rightarrow C$, then $CF \rightarrow A$ requires both $C$ and $F$. After $A \rightarrow C$, $A^+ = \{A, C\}$. Then no FD fires from $\{A, C\}$ except possibly $CE \rightarrow A$ which needs $E$ first. So $A^+ = \{A, C\}$ and $B \notin A^+$. Hence $A \rightarrow B$ is **NOT redundant**. Keep. *Test $A \rightarrow C$:* Compute $A^+$ under $\{A \rightarrow B, B \rightarrow D, CD \rightarrow E, E \rightarrow F, CE \rightarrow A, CF \rightarrow A\}$. $$A^+ = \{A, B, D\}$$ $C \notin A^+$, so keep $A \rightarrow C$. *Test $B \rightarrow D$:* Compute $B^+$ under $\{A \rightarrow B, A \rightarrow C, CD \rightarrow E, E \rightarrow F, CE \rightarrow A, CF \rightarrow A\}$. $$B^+ = \{B\}$$ $D \notin B^+$, so keep $B \rightarrow D$. *Test $CD \rightarrow E$:* Compute $CD^+$ under $F_2 - \{CD \rightarrow E\}$. $$CD^+ = \{C, D\}$$ $E \notin CD^+$, so keep $CD \rightarrow E$. *Test $E \rightarrow F$:* Compute $E^+$ under $F_2 - \{E \rightarrow F\}$. $$E^+ = \{E\}$$ $F \notin E^+$, so keep $E \rightarrow F$. *Test $CE \rightarrow A$:* Compute $CE^+$ under $F_2 - \{CE \rightarrow A\}$. $$CE^+ = \{C, E, F\}$$ $A \notin CE^+$, so keep $CE \rightarrow A$. *Test $CF \rightarrow A$:* Compute $CF^+$ under $F_2 - \{CF \rightarrow A\}$. $$CF^+ = \{C, F\}$$ $A \notin CF^+$, so keep $CF \rightarrow A$. **Final Minimal Cover:** $$\boxed{F_c = \{A \rightarrow B, \; A \rightarrow C, \; B \rightarrow D, \; CD \rightarrow E, \; E \rightarrow F, \; CE \rightarrow A, \; CF \rightarrow A\}}$$ --- **Model Solution for (b):** To verify whether $CD \rightarrow E$ is redundant, we compute the closure $CD^+$ using the set $F - \{CD \rightarrow E\}$: $$F' = \{A \rightarrow B, \; A \rightarrow C, \; B \rightarrow D, \; CE \rightarrow F, \; CE \rightarrow A, \; CF \rightarrow A, \; E \rightarrow F\}$$ **Step-by-step closure computation:** $$ \begin{aligned} CD^+ &= \{C, D\} \\ &\text{No FD has LHS } \subseteq \{C, D\} \text{ except possibly combinations.} \\ &\text{Check } CE \rightarrow F: \text{needs } E, \text{not in } CD^+. \\ &\text{Check } CF \rightarrow A: \text{needs } F, \text{not in } CD^+. \\ &\text{Check } A \rightarrow B, A \rightarrow C, B \rightarrow D, E \rightarrow F: \text{none apply directly}. \end{aligned} $$ Therefore $CD^+ = \{C, D\}$, and since $E \notin CD^+$, the dependency $CD \rightarrow E$ is **NOT redundant** in $F$. **Conclusion:** The FD $CD \rightarrow E$ is essential and must be retained in the minimal cover. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice** **(a)** Consider the relation $R(A, B, C, D, E)$ with functional dependencies: $$F = \{AB \rightarrow E, \; BE \rightarrow C, \; C \rightarrow D, \; D \rightarrow B, \; AC \rightarrow B\}$$ Find the **minimal cover** $F_c$. Clearly demonstrate all three stages. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Is the dependency $AB \rightarrow E$ **redundant**? Justify using the **attribute closure** of $AB$. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for (a):** **Stage 1 ŌĆö Decompose RHS:** All FDs already have single-attribute RHS, so $F_1 = F$. $$F_1 = \{AB \rightarrow E, \; BE \rightarrow C, \; C \rightarrow D, \; D \rightarrow B, \; AC \rightarrow B\}$$ **Stage 2 ŌĆö LHS Minimization:** *Test $AB \rightarrow E$:* Test removing $A$: Compute $B^+$ under $F_1$. $$ \begin{aligned} B^+ &= \{B\} \\ &\rightarrow D \;\;(\text{by } D \rightarrow B \text{ is reverse, not applicable}) \\ &\text{No FD has LHS } \subseteq \{B\}. \text{ So } B^+ = \{B\}. \end{aligned} $$ $E \notin B^+$, so $A$ is **not extraneous**. Test removing $B$: Compute $A^+$ under $F_1$. $$A^+ = \{A\}$$ $E \notin A^+$, so $B$ is **not extraneous**. $AB \rightarrow E$ stays. *Test $BE \rightarrow C$:* Test removing $B$: Compute $E^+$. $$E^+ = \{E\}$$ $C \notin E^+$, so $B$ is **not extraneous**. Test removing $E$: Compute $B^+$. $$B^+ = \{B\}$$ $C \notin B^+$, so $E$ is **not extraneous**. $BE \rightarrow C$ stays. *Test $AC \rightarrow B$:* Test removing $A$: Compute $C^+$ under $F_1$. $$ \begin{aligned} C^+ &= \{C, D, B, A, E\} \\ &\text{Since } C \rightarrow D, D \rightarrow B, A \text{ may come via } AC. \text{ Actually:} \\ C^+ &= \{C\} \rightarrow D \rightarrow B \rightarrow \{C, D, B\} \end{aligned} $$ Wait ŌĆö $AC$ is the LHS, and we are testing removing $A$, so LHS becomes $C$. Using $F_1$: $C \rightarrow D$, $D \rightarrow B$. Hence $C^+ = \{C, D, B\}$. $B \in C^+$, so $A$ is **extraneous** in $AC \rightarrow B$. Remove $A$. New FD: $C \rightarrow B$. But $C \rightarrow B$ can be derived: $C \rightarrow D$ and $D \rightarrow B$ by transitivity, so $C \rightarrow B$ already holds. Hence the FD $AC \rightarrow B$ is now **redundant** and can be removed entirely. Updated $F_2$: $$F_2 = \{AB \rightarrow E, \; BE \rightarrow C, \; C \rightarrow D, \; D \rightarrow B\}$$ **Stage 3 ŌĆö Remove Redundant FDs:** *Test $AB \rightarrow E$:* Compute $AB^+$ under $\{BE \rightarrow C, C \rightarrow D, D \rightarrow B\}$. $$ \begin{aligned} AB^+ &= \{A, B\} \\ &\text{No FD applies. } AB^+ = \{A, B\}. \end{aligned} $$ $E \notin AB^+$, so $AB \rightarrow E$ is **NOT redundant**. Keep. *Test $BE \rightarrow C$:* Compute $BE^+$ under $\{AB \rightarrow E, C \rightarrow D, D \rightarrow B\}$. $$ \begin{aligned} BE^+ &= \{B, E\} \end{aligned} $$ No FD applies. $BE^+ = \{B, E\}$. $C \notin BE^+$, so keep $BE \rightarrow C$. *Test $C \rightarrow D$:* Compute $C^+$ under $\{AB \rightarrow E, BE \rightarrow C, D \rightarrow B\}$. $$ \begin{aligned} C^+ &= \{C\} \end{aligned} $$ No FD applies. $C^+ = \{C\}$. $D \notin C^+$, so keep $C \rightarrow D$. *Test $D \rightarrow B$:* Compute $D^+$ under $\{AB \rightarrow E, BE \rightarrow C, C \rightarrow D\}$. $$ \begin{aligned} D^+ &= \{D\} \end{aligned} $$ No FD applies. $D^+ = \{D\}$. $B \notin D^+$, so keep $D \rightarrow B$. **Final Minimal Cover:** $$\boxed{F_c = \{AB \rightarrow E, \; BE \rightarrow C, \; C \rightarrow D, \; D \rightarrow B\}}$$ --- **Model Solution for (b):** To check whether $AB \rightarrow E$ is redundant, we compute $AB^+$ using $F - \{AB \rightarrow E\}$: $$F' = \{BE \rightarrow C, \; C \rightarrow D, \; D \rightarrow B, \; AC \rightarrow B\}$$ **Step-by-step closure:** $$ \begin{aligned} AB^+ &= \{A, B\} \\ &\text{Check each FD's LHS:}\\ &BE \rightarrow C: \text{needs } E, \text{not in } AB^+.\\ &AC \rightarrow B: \text{needs } C, \text{not in } AB^+.\\ &C \rightarrow D, D \rightarrow B: \text{need } C \text{ or } D, \text{not in } AB^+. \end{aligned} $$ Therefore $AB^+ = \{A, B\}$. Since $E \notin AB^+$, the dependency $AB \rightarrow E$ is **NOT redundant** in $F$. **Conclusion:** The FD $AB \rightarrow E$ is essential and must be retained in the minimal cover. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls** > 1. **Skipping Stage 1**: Many students directly apply Stage 2/3 without decomposing the RHS first. The minimal cover **must** have single-attribute RHS. *(ŌłÆ2 marks)* > 2. **Incorrect Closure Computation**: Errors in computing $X^+$ (forgetting to re-apply FDs iteratively until no new attributes appear) are the #1 reason for wrong answers. *(ŌłÆ3 to ŌłÆ4 marks)* > 3. **Removing FDs Out of Order**: Always test LHS minimization **before** removing redundant FDs. The order matters because LHS minimization can create redundant FDs (as in Example #2 of Section 3). > 4. **Forgetting to Re-verify**: After removing one FD in Stage 3, recompute the minimal cover. Some FDs may become redundant only after others are removed. > 5. **Not showing iterations**: For closures like $A^+$, **show every iteration** explicitly. KTU examiners award step-marks. > 6. **Conflating Minimal Cover with Prime/Non-Prime attributes**: Minimal cover is a property of *FD sets*, not of relations. Do not confuse with normalization concepts. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Minimal Cover** > - **Definition**: A minimal cover $F_c$ of $F$ is a logically equivalent set of FDs that is **irredundant**, **single-attribute on RHS**, and has **no extraneous LHS attributes**. > - **Algorithm ŌĆö 3 Stages**: > 1. **Canonicalize**: Decompose RHS to single attributes using the **Decomposition Rule** of Armstrong's axioms. > 2. **LHS Minimize**: For each FD with multi-attribute LHS, test each LHS attribute for extraneousness using **attribute closure**. > 3. **Remove Redundancy**: For each remaining FD, test if it can be derived from the others using **attribute closure**. > - **Extraneous Attribute Test (LHS)**: $A$ is extraneous in $\alpha$ of $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ iff $\beta \subseteq (\alpha - \{A\})^+$. > - **Redundant FD Test**: $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ is redundant iff $\beta \subseteq \alpha^+$ under $F - \{\alpha \rightarrow \beta\}$. > - **Non-Uniqueness**: Multiple minimal covers may exist for the same $F$, but all are **logically equivalent** ($F_1^+ = F_2^+$). > - **Key Application**: Used as input to the **3NF Synthesis Algorithm** for dependency-preserving, lossless-join decompositions. > - **Proofs Not Required**: The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly omits formal correctness proofs; focus on **algorithm application** and **closure computations**. > - **Common Pitfall**: Always show **iterative closure expansion** ŌĆö KTU awards step-marks for each new attribute added. > - **Exam Tip**: In 14-mark questions, structure your answer as **Stage 1 ŌåÆ Stage 2 ŌåÆ Stage 3** with **separate closure tables** for clarity. ---

topics.title: First Normal Form (1NF), Second Normal Form (2NF), Third Normal Form (3NF), Boyce Codd Normal Form (BCNF)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** First Normal Form (1NF), Second Normal Form (2NF), Third Normal Form (3NF), Boyce Codd Normal Form (BCNF)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 First Normal Form (1NF)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** A relation schema $R$ is in **First Normal Form (1NF)** if the domain of every attribute in $R$ contains only **atomic (indivisible) values**, and every attribute holds a **single-valued** entry for each tuple. The value of any attribute at any tuple must be a single value from the corresponding domain ŌĆö never a set, list, or repeating group.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a student register. A *bad* design would list all phone numbers of a student in one cell: "9876543210, 9123456789, 8000111222". This is **not 1NF** because the cell holds multiple values. To make it 1NF, you split that cell ŌĆö one row per phone number ŌĆö or you create a separate "Phone" table linked to the student.

**KTU 1NF Conditions Checklist:**
- Ō£ģ Every cell contains a single (atomic) value.
- Ō£ģ No repeating groups or multi-valued attributes in a single column.
- Ō£ģ A **primary key** exists to uniquely identify rows.
- Ō£ģ No two rows are identical (no duplicate tuples).

---

### 1.2 Second Normal Form (2NF)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** A relation $R$ is in **Second Normal Form (2NF)** if and only if it is in **1NF** and **every non-prime attribute is fully functionally dependent on the entire candidate key** (i.e., there is **no partial dependency**).

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a college attendance sheet with a composite key `(StudentID, SubjectID)`. The `StudentName` depends only on `StudentID` (part of the key), not the full key. This **partial dependency** is what 2NF removes ŌĆö `StudentName` should move to a separate `Student` table.

**Key Insight:** 2NF is **only meaningful** when the primary key is composite. If the key is a single attribute, 1NF Ō¤╣ 2NF automatically.

---

### 1.3 Third Normal Form (3NF)

> [! [!IMPORTANT]]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** A relation $R$ is in **Third Normal Form (3NF)** if and only if it is in **2NF** and **no non-prime attribute is transitively dependent on any candidate key**. Equivalently, for every non-trivial FD $X \rightarrow A$, either $X$ is a **superkey** OR $A$ is a **prime attribute** (part of some candidate key).

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Consider a `Student` table with attributes `StudentID, DeptID, DeptName`. Here, `StudentID ŌåÆ DeptID` and `DeptID ŌåÆ DeptName`. So `StudentID ŌåÆ DeptName` (transitively). 3NF says: pull out `DeptName` into a `Department` table.

---

### 1.4 BoyceŌĆōCodd Normal Form (BCNF)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** A relation $R$ is in **BCNF** if and only if for **every non-trivial functional dependency** $X \rightarrow Y$ (where $Y \not\subseteq X$), the determinant $X$ must be a **superkey** of $R$.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
BCNF is the **strict, no-exceptions** form of 3NF. It says: "If column A determines column B, then A must be able to uniquely identify every row." No exceptions, no 'prime attribute escape clause.'

**BCNF vs 3NF ŌĆö The Subtle Difference:**
3NF allows $X \rightarrow A$ if $A$ is a **prime attribute**. BCNF forbids this. Therefore: **BCNF ŌŖé 3NF ŌŖé 2NF ŌŖé 1NF**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Dependency (FD) ŌĆö The Foundation of Normalization

> **Definition:** A functional dependency $X \rightarrow Y$ holds in $R$ if for every valid instance of $R$, two tuples having the same value of $X$ must also have the same value of $Y$.
> - $X$ is the **determinant** (left-hand side / LHS).
> - $Y$ is the **dependent** (right-hand side / RHS).

**Armstrong's Axioms (used in KTU derivations):**
1. **Reflexivity:** If $Y \subseteq X$, then $X \rightarrow Y$.
2. **Augmentation:** If $X \rightarrow Y$, then $XZ \rightarrow YZ$.
3. **Transitivity:** If $X \rightarrow Y$ and $Y \rightarrow Z$, then $X \rightarrow Z$.

**Derived Rules (Frequently tested in KTU):**
- **Union:** If $X \rightarrow Y$ and $X \rightarrow Z$, then $X \rightarrow YZ$.
- **Decomposition:** If $X \rightarrow YZ$, then $X \rightarrow Y$ and $X \rightarrow Z$.
- **Pseudotransitivity:** If $X \rightarrow Y$ and $WY \rightarrow Z$, then $WX \rightarrow Z$.

---

### 2.2 Prime vs Non-Prime Attribute

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Prime Attribute** | An attribute that is a member of **any candidate key**. |
| **Non-Prime Attribute** | An attribute that is **not** a part of any candidate key. |
| **Superkey** | A set of attributes that uniquely identifies a tuple; contains a candidate key. |
| **Candidate Key** | A **minimal** superkey (no proper subset is a superkey). |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** *"Is 'prime attribute' tested with respect to a specific candidate key or any candidate key?"*
> **Answer:** With respect to **any candidate key** of the relation.

---

### 2.3 Types of Anomalies Each NF Eliminates

| Normal Form | Eliminates Anomaly | Core Rule |
|-------------|-------------------|-----------|
| **1NF** | Multi-valued / repeating groups | Atomicity of values |
| **2NF** | Partial dependency anomalies | Full FD on PK |
| **3NF** | Transitive dependency anomalies | No transitive FDs on non-prime attrs |
| **BCNF** | All FD-based anomalies (strict) | Every determinant is a superkey |

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Reference Table ŌĆö KTU Normalization Hierarchy**

| Normal Form | Must Satisfy 1NF? | Additional Condition | Formal Test (for every non-trivial FD $X \rightarrow A$) |
|-------------|:-:|---|---|
| **1NF** | ŌĆö | Atomic values only | No structural violations |
| **2NF** | Ō£ģ | No partial dependency | $X$ is a superkey **OR** $A$ is prime **OR** $X$ is a proper subset of a single candidate key (eliminated case) |
| **3NF** | Ō£ģ | No transitive dependency | $X$ is a superkey **OR** $A$ is a prime attribute |
| **BCNF** | Ō£ģ | Strictest form | $X$ is a **superkey** (no exception for prime) |

**Key Equivalence for 3NF Test:**
$$R \text{ is in 3NF} \iff \forall \text{FD } X \rightarrow A, \; \big( X \supseteq \text{superkey} \big) \lor \big( A \subseteq \text{prime attributes} \big)$$

**Key Equivalence for BCNF Test:**
$$R \text{ is in BCNF} \iff \forall \text{FD } X \rightarrow A, \; X \supseteq \text{superkey}$$

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking Systems:** BCNF normalization prevents inconsistent interest rates or branch addresses when a customer relocates.
- **E-Commerce Catalogs:** 3NF ensures that a product's category name is stored once, not duplicated in every order.
- **Hospital Management:** 1NF + 2NF ensures patient reports map cleanly to one patient-doctor combination per row.
- **Telecom Billing:** BCNF prevents multiple rate plans pointing to the same non-key attribute ambiguously.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Universal Relation ŌĆö Running Example (Used Throughout)

> [!NOTE]
> We will use the following unnormalized relation to demonstrate **all four normal forms** step-by-step ŌĆö a classic KTU board problem pattern.

**UNNORMALIZED TABLE (UNF):**

| StudentID | StudentName | Subjects | SubjectCode | Marks | DeptID | DeptName | HOD |
|-----------|-------------|----------|-------------|-------|--------|----------|-----|
| S01 | Anil | DBMS, COA | CS301, CS302 | 88, 76 | D01 | CSE | Dr. Ravi |
| S02 | Beena | DBMS | CS301 | 92 | D01 | CSE | Dr. Ravi |
| S03 | Cyril | COA, OS | CS302, CS303 | 80, 85 | D02 | IT | Dr. Meena |

**Functional Dependencies Identified:**
- `StudentID ŌåÆ StudentName, DeptID`
- `DeptID ŌåÆ DeptName, HOD`
- `StudentID, SubjectCode ŌåÆ Marks` (composite key)
- `SubjectCode ŌåÆ Subjects`

**Candidate Keys Identified:** `{StudentID, SubjectCode}` (the only CK for this relation).

**Prime attributes:** `StudentID`, `SubjectCode`. **Non-prime:** everything else.

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Conversion to 1NF

**Step 1 ŌĆö Eliminate repeating groups / multi-valued cells.**
The original table has `Subjects` and `Marks` as repeating groups per student.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Form a flat table with one value per cell using composite key `(StudentID, SubjectCode)`:**

| StudentID | StudentName | SubjectCode | Subjects | Marks | DeptID | DeptName | HOD |
|-----------|-------------|-------------|----------|-------|--------|----------|-----|
| S01 | Anil | CS301 | DBMS | 88 | D01 | CSE | Dr. Ravi |
| S01 | Anil | CS302 | COA | 76 | D01 | CSE | Dr. Ravi |
| S02 | Beena | CS301 | DBMS | 92 | D01 | CSE | Dr. Ravi |
| S03 | Cyril | CS302 | COA | 80 | D02 | IT | Dr. Meena |
| S03 | Cyril | CS303 | OS | 85 | D02 | IT | Dr. Meena |

**Check 1NF:** Every cell is atomic. Ō£ģ Repeating groups removed. Ō£ģ

---

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Conversion to 2NF

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify partial dependencies on the composite key `(StudentID, SubjectCode)`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{StudentID} &\rightarrow \text{StudentName, DeptID, DeptName, HOD} \quad \text{(partial ŌĆö only part of CK)} \\
\text{SubjectCode} &\rightarrow \text{Subjects} \quad \text{(partial ŌĆö only part of CK)} \\
\text{StudentID, SubjectCode} &\rightarrow \text{Marks} \quad \text{(full dependency ŌĆö correct)} \\
\text{DeptID} &\rightarrow \text{DeptName, HOD} \quad \text{(transitive)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Decompose to remove partial dependencies:**

**Table 1: STUDENT (PK = StudentID)**

| StudentID | StudentName | DeptID |
|-----------|-------------|--------|
| S01 | Anil | D01 |
| S02 | Beena | D01 |
| S03 | Cyril | D02 |

**Table 2: SUBJECT (PK = SubjectCode)**

| SubjectCode | Subjects |
|-------------|----------|
| CS301 | DBMS |
| CS302 | COA |
| CS303 | OS |

**Table 3: MARKS (PK = StudentID, SubjectCode)**

| StudentID | SubjectCode | Marks |
|-----------|-------------|-------|
| S01 | CS301 | 88 |
| S01 | CS302 | 76 |
| S02 | CS301 | 92 |
| S03 | CS302 | 80 |
| S03 | CS303 | 85 |

**Check 2NF:** All non-prime attributes are fully functionally dependent on their respective keys. Ō£ģ

---

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Conversion to 3NF

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify transitive dependencies in STUDENT:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{StudentID} &\rightarrow \text{DeptID} \\
\text{DeptID} &\rightarrow \text{DeptName, HOD} \\
\therefore \text{StudentID} &\rightarrow \text{DeptName, HOD} \quad \text{(transitive)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Decompose STUDENT further:**

**Table 1: STUDENT (PK = StudentID)**

| StudentID | StudentName | DeptID |
|-----------|-------------|--------|
| S01 | Anil | D01 |
| S02 | Beena | D01 |
| S03 | Cyril | D02 |

**Table 2: DEPARTMENT (PK = DeptID)**

| DeptID | DeptName | HOD |
|--------|----------|-----|
| D01 | CSE | Dr. Ravi |
| D02 | IT | Dr. Meena |

**Table 3: SUBJECT (PK = SubjectCode)**

| SubjectCode | Subjects |
|-------------|----------|
| CS301 | DBMS |
| CS302 | COA |
| CS303 | OS |

**Table 4: MARKS (PK = StudentID, SubjectCode)**

| StudentID | SubjectCode | Marks |
|-----------|-------------|-------|
| S01 | CS301 | 88 |
| S01 | CS302 | 76 |
| S02 | CS301 | 92 |
| S03 | CS302 | 80 |
| S03 | CS303 | 85 |

**Check 3NF:** For every FD $X \rightarrow A$ in any table, $X$ is a superkey OR $A$ is prime. Ō£ģ
- `DeptID ŌåÆ DeptName, HOD`: `DeptID` is the PK ŌåÆ superkey. Ō£ģ
- `StudentID ŌåÆ StudentName, DeptID`: `StudentID` is the PK. Ō£ģ
- `SubjectCode ŌåÆ Subjects`: `SubjectCode` is the PK. Ō£ģ
- `(StudentID, SubjectCode) ŌåÆ Marks`: composite is the PK. Ō£ģ

---

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix ŌĆö Is it in BCNF?

| FD in any table | Determinant Is Superkey? | 3NF? | BCNF? |
|-----------------|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| `StudentID ŌåÆ StudentName, DeptID` | Ō£ģ Yes | Ō£ģ | Ō£ģ |
| `DeptID ŌåÆ DeptName, HOD` | Ō£ģ Yes | Ō£ģ | Ō£ģ |
| `SubjectCode ŌåÆ Subjects` | Ō£ģ Yes | Ō£ģ | Ō£ģ |
| `(StudentID, SubjectCode) ŌåÆ Marks` | Ō£ģ Yes | Ō£ģ | Ō£ģ |

**Conclusion:** After 3NF decomposition above, the database is also in **BCNF** because every determinant in every table is a superkey of *that* table.

---

### 3.6 A Classic BCNF Violation Case (3NF ŌŖŗ BCNF)

Consider a relation: **TEACH(Student, Subject, Teacher)** with the rule: *For a given (Student, Subject) pair, there is exactly one teacher; and each teacher teaches only one subject. But a subject can be taught by multiple teachers.*

**FDs:**
- `(Student, Subject) ŌåÆ Teacher`
- `Teacher ŌåÆ Subject`

**Candidate Keys:** `(Student, Teacher)` and `(Student, Subject)` ŌĆö *both are valid CKs*.
- **Prime attributes:** `Student`, `Subject`, `Teacher` (all are part of *some* CK).

**3NF Test on `Teacher ŌåÆ Subject`:**
- $A = $ `Subject` (prime attribute) ŌåÆ **3NF satisfied** (prime-attribute escape clause applies).

**BCNF Test on `Teacher ŌåÆ Subject`:**
- $X = $ `Teacher` is **not** a superkey (e.g., `Ravi` can teach both DBMS to Anil and DBMS to Beena ŌĆö multiple tuples share `Teacher='Ravi'`). ŌåÆ **BCNF violated**.

**Decomposition into BCNF:**
- **R1(Student, Teacher)** with CK = `(Student, Teacher)` Ō£ģ BCNF.
- **R2(Teacher, Subject)** with CK = `Teacher` (determinant is superkey) Ō£ģ BCNF.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not 3NF here?** Because 3NF's escape clause lets prime attributes escape ŌĆö but BCNF does not allow that. This is the **canonical 3NF-but-not-BCNF case** that KTU board exams love to test.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Normalization Hierarchy Pyramid

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["UNNORMALIZED FORM\nRepeating groups allowed"]
    B["FIRST NORMAL FORM 1NF\nAtomic values, no repeating groups"]
    C["SECOND NORMAL FORM 2NF\n1NF + No partial dependency"]
    D["THIRD NORMAL FORM 3NF\n2NF + No transitive dependency"]
    E["BOYCE CODD NORMAL FORM BCNF\n3NF + Every determinant is superkey"]

    A -->|Step 1: Atomicize| B
    B -->|Step 2: Remove partial FDs| C
    C -->|Step 3: Remove transitive FDs| D
    D -->|Step 4: Strict superkey test| E

    style A fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000
    style B fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style C fill:#fff2b3,stroke:#cca300
    style D fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745
    style E fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085
```

### 4.2 Functional Dependency Dependency Graph for Universal Relation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SID["StudentID"]:::pk -->|FD1| SName["StudentName"]
    SID -->|FD2| DID["DeptID"]
    DID -->|FD3| DName["DeptName"]
    DID -->|FD3| HOD["HOD"]
    SID -->|FD4| SubCode["SubjectCode"]
    SubCode -->|FD5| Subs["Subjects"]
    SID
    SubCode -->|FD6 composite| Marks["Marks"]

    classDef pk fill:#ffd966,stroke:#bf9000,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Decomposition Flow (UNF ŌåÆ BCNF)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    UNF["UNF\nRepeating groups in Subjects, Marks"]
    ONE["1NF Table\nAtomic rows, composite key"]
    TWO["2NF Decomposition\nSTUDENT, SUBJECT, MARKS tables"]
    THREE["3NF Decomposition\nSTUDENT, DEPARTMENT, SUBJECT, MARKS"]
    BCNF["BCNF Verified\nAll determinants are superkeys"]

    UNF -->|Step 1 Eliminate repeating groups| ONE
    ONE -->|Step 2 Remove partial deps| TWO
    TWO -->|Step 3 Remove transitive deps| THREE
    THREE -->|Step 4 Apply strict superkey test| BCNF

    style UNF fill:#f8d7da
    style ONE fill:#fff3cd
    style TWO fill:#d1ecf1
    style THREE fill:#d4edda
    style BCNF fill:#cce5ff
```

### 4.4 3NF vs BCNF Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["For each FD: X -> A"] --> Q1{"Is X a superkey?"}
    Q1 -->|Yes| BCNFOK["BCNF AND 3NF satisfied"]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{"Is A a prime attribute?"}
    Q2 -->|Yes| ThreeNF["3NF satisfied NOT BCNF"]
    Q2 -->|No| Violation["3NF VIOLATED Decompose required"]

    style BCNFOK fill:#d4edda
    style ThreeNF fill:#fff3cd
    style Violation fill:#f8d7da
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Normalization Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input
        A1["Raw User Data\nUNF"]
    end

    subgraph Engine
        B1["Analyzer\nIdentify FDs and CKs"]
        B2["Decomposer\nApply NF rules"]
        B3["Verifier\nTest determinants"]
    end

    subgraph Output
        C1["Normalized Schema\n1NF / 2NF / 3NF / BCNF"]
        C2["Dependency Report\nFD violations found"]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    B1 -->|FD set, CK set| B2
    B2 -->|candidate schema| B3
    B3 -->|pass| C1
    B3 -->|issues| C2
    C2 -.->|feedback| B2

    style A1 fill:#ffcccc
    style B1 fill:#fff2b3
    style B2 fill:#fff2b3
    style B3 fill:#fff2b3
    style C1 fill:#ccffcc
    style C2 fill:#cce0ff
```
