## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Model Paper]**
**What is a Lossless Join decomposition? State the sufficient condition for a binary decomposition R into RŌéü and RŌéé to be lossless.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** A decomposition of a relation R into relations RŌéü, RŌéé is called a **Lossless Join decomposition** if joining the projections of R onto RŌéü and RŌéé gives back the original relation without any spurious tuples. Formally:
> $$\pi_{R_1}(r) \bowtie \pi_{R_2}(r) = r$$

> **Sufficient Condition (2 marks):** For a binary decomposition, the decomposition is lossless if the common attributes form a **superkey of at least one** of the sub-relations. Mathematically:
> $$(R_1 \cap R_2) \rightarrow R_1 \quad \text{OR} \quad (R_1 \cap R_2) \rightarrow R_2 \text{ must be in } F^+$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Lossless Join and Dependency Preserving decompositions. Why are both properties important in database design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Lossless Join** | **Dependency Preserving** |
|---|---|---|
| Concern | Information content (no data loss/gain) | Constraint enforcement (no extra joins) |
| Test | Chase / Tabular algorithm | Closure comparison $(\pi_{R_1}(F) \cup \pi_{R_2}(F))^+$ vs $F^+$ |
| Importance | Ensures reconstruction of original data | Ensures FDs can be enforced efficiently |

> **Why both?** (1 mark): A good database design must preserve information **and** allow efficient constraint enforcement. A lossy design produces **wrong query results**, while a non-DP design forces **expensive joins** every time a constraint must be checked.

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024]**

**(a)** Given relation R(A, B, C, D, E) with functional dependencies:
$$F = \{ A \rightarrow BC, \; CD \rightarrow E, \; B \rightarrow D \}$$
Check whether the decomposition $\rho = \{ R_1(A, B, C), \; R_2(A, D, E) \}$ is **lossless** using the **tabular method**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Is the same decomposition **dependency preserving**? Justify with a step-by-step closure computation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) ŌĆö 7 marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify common and unique attributes.
- $R_1 \cap R_2 = \{A\}$
- $R_1 \setminus R_2 = \{B, C\}$
- $R_2 \setminus R_1 = \{D, E\}$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Build the initial 2-row table.

| | A | B | C | D | E |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| RŌéü | aŌéü | bŌéüŌéü | bŌéüŌéé | bŌéüŌéā | bŌéüŌéä |
| RŌéé | aŌéü | bŌééŌéü | bŌééŌéé | bŌééŌéā | bŌééŌéä |

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Apply A ŌåÆ BC.

Both rows have `aŌéü` in column A. Force B and C in RŌéé to match RŌéü.

| | A | B | C | D | E |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| RŌéü | aŌéü | bŌéüŌéü | bŌéüŌéé | bŌéüŌéā | bŌéüŌéä |
| RŌéé | aŌéü | bŌéüŌéü | bŌéüŌéé | bŌééŌéā | bŌééŌéä |

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Apply B ŌåÆ D.

Both rows have `bŌéüŌéü` in column B. Force D in RŌéé to match RŌéü.

| | A | B | C | D | E |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| RŌéü | aŌéü | bŌéüŌéü | bŌéüŌéé | bŌéüŌéā | bŌéüŌéä |
| RŌéé | aŌéü | bŌéüŌéü | bŌéüŌéé | bŌéüŌéā | bŌééŌéä |

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Apply CD ŌåÆ E.

Both rows have `bŌéüŌéé` in C and `bŌéüŌéā` in D. Force E in RŌéé to match RŌéü.

| | A | B | C | D | E |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| RŌéü | aŌéü | bŌéüŌéü | bŌéüŌéé | bŌéüŌéā | bŌéüŌéä |
| RŌéé | aŌéü | bŌéüŌéü | bŌéüŌéé | bŌéüŌéā | bŌéüŌéä |

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Termination check + result.

No further changes possible. **No row is entirely composed of `a`-symbols** (all rows contain `b` entries).

> **Result: The decomposition is LOSSY (NOT lossless).**

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Conclusion and physical interpretation.

Since no row of the table contains only `a`-symbols, the decomposition is **lossy**. A lossy decomposition will generate **spurious tuples** when RŌéü and RŌéé are joined back, leading to incorrect query results.

#### Model Solution for (b) ŌĆö 7 marks

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute local dependency sets.

- $\pi_{R_1}(F)$: FDs where X Ōł¬ Y ŌŖå RŌéü(A, B, C).
  - A ŌåÆ BC: BC ŌŖå RŌéü Ō£ģ ŌåÆ include
  - CD ŌåÆ E: D Ōłē RŌéü ŌØī ŌåÆ exclude
  - B ŌåÆ D: D Ōłē RŌéü ŌØī ŌåÆ exclude
  - $\pi_{R_1}(F) = \{A \rightarrow BC\}$

- $\pi_{R_2}(F)$: FDs where X Ōł¬ Y ŌŖå RŌéé(A, D, E).
  - A ŌåÆ BC: BC ŌŖä RŌéé ŌØī ŌåÆ exclude
  - CD ŌåÆ E: C Ōłē RŌéé ŌØī ŌåÆ exclude
  - B ŌåÆ D: B Ōłē RŌéé ŌØī ŌåÆ exclude
  - $\pi_{R_2}(F) = \emptyset$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Union of local FDs.
$$F_{local} = \{A \rightarrow BC\} \cup \emptyset = \{A \rightarrow BC\}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute $F_{local}^+$.

- AŌü║ = {A, B, C}
- AŌü║ (continuing) = {A, B, C} ŌĆö no new derivations
- BŌü║ = {B}, CŌü║ = {C}, DŌü║ = {D}, EŌü║ = {E}

$F_{local}^+ = \{A \rightarrow A, A \rightarrow B, A \rightarrow C, A \rightarrow BC, B \rightarrow B, C \rightarrow C, D \rightarrow D, E \rightarrow E, \dots\}$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Compare with FŌü║.

From F = {A ŌåÆ BC, CD ŌåÆ E, B ŌåÆ D}, we can derive:
- B ŌåÆ D Ō£ģ
- A ŌåÆ BC, so A ŌåÆ B and A ŌåÆ C Ō£ģ
- A ŌåÆ B and B ŌåÆ D, so A ŌåÆ D Ō£ģ
- A ŌåÆ C, but no path to E from A in F_local alone.

**Result: The decomposition is NOT Dependency Preserving.** (Specifically, the FD CD ŌåÆ E cannot be derived using only A ŌåÆ BC locally.)

---

**Q4. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Using the **Chase Test**, determine whether the decomposition $\rho = \{ R_1(A, B), \; R_2(B, C), \; R_3(A, C) \}$ of R(A, B, C) with FDs $F = \{ A \rightarrow B, \; B \rightarrow C, \; A \rightarrow C \}$ is **lossless join**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Now consider a different relation R(A, B, C, D) with FDs $F = \{A \rightarrow B, \; B \rightarrow C, \; C \rightarrow D\}$. Test the decomposition $\rho = \{ R_1(A, B), \; R_2(B, C), \; R_3(C, D) \}$ for both **lossless join (using Chase Test)** and **dependency preservation**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) ŌĆö 7 marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Construct the chase table with 3 rows.

| | A | B | C |
|---|---|---|---|
| RŌéü(A,B) | aŌéü | aŌéü | bŌéüŌéā |
| RŌéé(B,C) | bŌééŌéü | aŌéé | aŌéé |
| RŌéā(A,C) | aŌéā | bŌéāŌéé | aŌéā |

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Apply A ŌåÆ B.

Rows with matching A: RŌéü (aŌéü) and RŌéā (aŌéā) ŌĆö **both have `a`-symbols in column A, treated as matching**.

By A ŌåÆ B: Force B in RŌéā to match B in RŌéü (or the smaller-subscript convention: RŌéā B becomes `aŌéü`).

| | A | B | C |
|---|---|---|---|
| RŌéü | aŌéü | aŌéü | bŌéüŌéā |
| RŌéé | bŌééŌéü | aŌéé | aŌéé |
| RŌéā | aŌéā | aŌéü | aŌéā |

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Apply B ŌåÆ C.

Rows matching B: RŌéü (aŌéü) and RŌéā (aŌéü). Both have aŌéü in column B. Force C in RŌéā to match C in RŌéü.

| | A | B | C |
|---|---|---|---|
| RŌéü | aŌéü | aŌéü | bŌéüŌéā |
| RŌéé | bŌééŌéü | aŌéé | aŌéé |
| RŌéā | aŌéā | aŌéü | bŌéüŌéā |

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Apply A ŌåÆ C.

Rows matching A: RŌéü (aŌéü) and RŌéā (aŌéā). Force C in RŌéā to match C in RŌéü. Already bŌéüŌéā in both. No change.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Termination.

No further changes. Stop.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Result.

**Row RŌéā is now `(aŌéā, aŌéü, bŌéüŌéā)`** ŌĆö it is NOT entirely `a`-symbols (it has bŌéüŌéā).

> **Result: The decomposition is LOSSY.**

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Physical interpretation.

The decomposition into RŌéü, RŌéé, RŌéā is lossy because when joined, it can produce extra tuples. For example, if RŌéü = {(1,2)}, RŌéé = {(2,3)}, RŌéā = {(1,3)}, the natural join gives {(1,2,3)} which is valid, but if RŌéā had a tuple (1,4), joining would still produce it even though A=1 should determine C=2, not 4. This causes **semantic ambiguity**.

#### Model Solution for (b) ŌĆö 7 marks

**Part 1 ŌĆö Chase Test (3 marks):**

**Step 1:** Build the chase table.

| | A | B | C | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| RŌéü(A,B) | aŌéü | aŌéü | bŌéüŌéā | bŌéüŌéä |
| RŌéé(B,C) | bŌééŌéü | aŌéé | aŌéé | bŌééŌéä |
| RŌéā(C,D) | bŌéāŌéü | bŌéāŌéé | aŌéā | aŌéā |

**Step 2:** Apply A ŌåÆ B: No two rows match on A. No change.
**Step 3:** Apply B ŌåÆ C: No two rows match on B. No change.
**Step 4:** Apply C ŌåÆ D: No two rows match on C. No change.
**Step 5:** Apply derived FD A ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ D (via transitivity through B and C): No two rows match on A. No change.

**Result:** No row is all `a`-symbols ŌåÆ **LOSSY** (3 marks).

**Part 2 ŌĆö Dependency Preservation (4 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute local dependency sets.

- $\pi_{R_1}(F) = \{A \rightarrow B\}$ (since A ŌåÆ B has X Ōł¬ Y = {A, B} ŌŖå RŌéü)
- $\pi_{R_2}(F) = \{B \rightarrow C\}$
- $\pi_{R_3}(F) = \{C \rightarrow D\}$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Union:
$$F_{local} = \{A \rightarrow B, \; B \rightarrow C, \; C \rightarrow D\}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute F_localŌü║:
- AŌü║ using F_local = {A, B, C, D} (chained)
- BŌü║ = {B, C, D}
- CŌü║ = {C, D}
- DŌü║ = {D}

All derivable FDs include: A ŌåÆ B, A ŌåÆ C, A ŌåÆ D, A ŌåÆ BCD, B ŌåÆ C, B ŌåÆ D, B ŌåÆ CD, C ŌåÆ D.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Comparison with FŌü║:
$F = \{A \rightarrow B, \; B \rightarrow C, \; C \rightarrow D\}$.
- FŌü║ contains all FDs derivable from F.
- F_localŌü║ = FŌü║ (both can derive A ŌåÆ C, A ŌåÆ D, B ŌåÆ D, etc.).

> **Result: The decomposition IS Dependency Preserving.** Ō£ģ

**Summary for (b):**
- Lossless Join? ŌØī **LOSSY**
- Dependency Preserving? Ō£ģ **YES**

This demonstrates the classic KTU point: **a decomposition can be DP without being LJ**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Mistakes (Read Before Exam!)**
> 1. **The "all `a`-symbols" mistake (ŌĆō2 marks)**: Many students conclude "Lossless" just because two rows become identical. **This is WRONG.** The rule is: a row must contain **only `a`-symbols** (aŌéü, aŌéé, aŌéā, ŌĆ”) ŌĆö no `b` symbols allowed.
> 2. **Forgetting the chase test for n-ary decompositions (ŌĆō3 marks)**: The 2-row tabular method works **ONLY for binary decompositions** (R into RŌéü, RŌéé). For 3 or more sub-relations, you **must** use the Chase Test with n rows.
> 3. **Confusing the direction of chase (ŌĆō2 marks)**: When applying X ŌåÆ Y, you change **Y**-columns (not X-columns). The X-columns are the "matching" criteria.
> 4. **Closure computation errors in DP test (ŌĆō3 marks)**: When checking DP, you must compute the **union of local closures**, not just check individual FDs. Use Armstrong's axioms (reflexivity, augmentation, transitivity, union, decomposition).
> 5. **Confusing the "common attribute is a key" sufficient condition with necessity (ŌĆō2 marks)**: $(R_1 \cap R_2) \rightarrow R_1$ is a **sufficient** but **NOT necessary** condition for LJ. The chase test is the **necessary and sufficient** test.
> 6. **Not deriving transitive FDs in chase test (ŌĆō2 marks)**: Forgetting to apply derived FDs (e.g., A ŌåÆ C from A ŌåÆ B and B ŌåÆ C) can lead to wrong LJ results.
> 7. **Tabular method with wrong number of rows (ŌĆō2 marks)**: For R into RŌéü, RŌéé ŌĆö table has **exactly 2 rows**. Adding more rows means the student is confusing it with the chase test.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> ­¤ōŗ **Last-Minute Revision ŌĆö KTU Module 3, Topic: LJ & DP Algorithms**

- **Lossless Join (LJ)** = no spurious tuples on JOIN; equivalently, the original relation is reconstructable.
- **Dependency Preserving (DP)** = all FDs enforceable without JOIN; equivalently, $(F_{local})^+ = F^+$.
- **Tabular Method** is for **binary** decomposition (2 rows); **Chase Test** is for **n-ary** decomposition (n rows).
- **Sufficient condition for binary LJ**: $(R_1 \cap R_2) \rightarrow R_1$ OR $(R_1 \cap R_2) \rightarrow R_2$ (i.e., common attribute is a superkey of at least one sub-relation).
- **Chase Test final condition**: If **any row becomes entirely `a`-symbols** ŌåÆ LJ confirmed.
- **DP check steps**: (1) Compute $\pi_{R_i}(F)$ for each sub-relation. (2) Take union. (3) Compute closure. (4) Compare with $F^+$.
- **3NF guarantees DP** but not always LJ. **BCNF guarantees LJ** but not always DP.
- **Use Armstrong's axioms** (reflexivity, augmentation, transitivity, union, decomposition) when computing FŌü║ and F_localŌü║.
- **Notation convention**: `aßĄó` = "must be the same" (lowercase a with row subscript); `bßĄóŌ▒╝` = "could be different" (lowercase b with row and column subscripts).
- **Chase direction**: For FD X ŌåÆ Y, find rows agreeing on X, then **change Y-columns** to match (replace `b` with `a`).
- **Termination**: Both algorithms terminate when no further changes occur in a complete pass over all FDs.
- **KTU 2024 weightage**: This topic typically carries **7ŌĆō14 marks** in the End Semester Exam (ESE), often appearing as a complete Part B question with sub-parts (a) and (b).
- **Two distinct tests are never mixed up**: Tabular method and Chase test are **separate** algorithms. Do not use tabular method for n-ary decompositions.
- **Spurious tuples = red flag**: Whenever a decomposition is lossy, joining sub-relations will produce tuples that were never in the original relation ŌĆö this is the **#1 cause of incorrect query results** in poorly normalized databases.
- **Trade-off awareness**: In real engineering practice, designers often prefer **3NF with both LJ and DP** over BCNF (which may sacrifice DP) ŌĆö this is a classic KTU viva question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transaction Processing Concepts - Overview of Concurrency Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transaction Processing Concepts - Overview of Concurrency Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Concurrency Control** is the set of techniques and protocols used in a Database Management System (DBMS) to ensure that **concurrent execution of multiple transactions** results in a database state that is equivalent to some **serial execution** of those same transactions, while simultaneously maintaining the **ACID properties** (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability).

In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus, **Transaction Processing Concepts ŌĆō Overview of Concurrency Control** refers to the study of:
- The **need for concurrency** in multi-user database environments
- The **problems (anomalies)** that arise when transactions execute without coordination
- The **schedules** (histories) of transaction operations
- The concept of **Serializability** (Conflict and View) as the correctness criterion
- High-level **concurrency control protocols** (Locking, Time-stamping, Validation/Multiversion)
- **Deadlock** detection and prevention

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** This topic directly addresses **Course Outcome CO3**: "Apply transaction management and concurrency control techniques in relational databases."

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Airport Runway ­¤ø¼

Imagine the **airport runway** as a single shared data item (e.g., a bank account balance) and the **airplanes** as concurrent transactions trying to **read** or **write** to that data item.

| Scenario | DBMS Equivalent | Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| Two planes try to land on the same runway at the same time | Two transactions write to the same row | **Lost Update / Crash** |
| A plane takes off using old, unconfirmed weather data | A transaction reads uncommitted data | **Dirty Read** |
| A passenger gets one boarding pass at booking, but a different seat at check-in | Same query returns different results on re-execution | **Non-Repeatable Read** |
| The airline opens 100 new seats after a phantom ghost block was added | A new row appears in a re-issued range query | **Phantom Read** |

> **Solution:** Air Traffic Control (ATC) acts as the **Concurrency Control Manager** ŌĆö it serializes landings, gives priority, and ensures no two planes collide. Similarly, the DBMS **Lock Manager** serializes access to data.

---

## 1.3 The "Why" ŌĆö Motivation for Concurrency

> [!NOTE]
> Modern DBMS serves **hundreds of concurrent users**. Without concurrency control, **three fundamental problems** emerge:
> 1. **Lost Update Problem** ŌĆö Two transactions overwrite each other's writes.
> 2. **Uncommitted Dependency (Dirty Read)** ŌĆö A transaction reads data written by an uncommitted transaction.
> 3. **Inconsistent Analysis (Non-repeatable Read)** ŌĆö A transaction reads the same data twice and gets different values.

These anomalies violate the **Isolation** property of ACID. Concurrency control protocols exist specifically to **prevent** them.

**Key Metric to Memorize for KTU:** The **Schedule** is the chronological execution order of operations from a set of concurrent transactions. Correctness is measured by **equivalence to a serial schedule**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

### Step 1: Understand Transaction Operations
Every transaction performs **two fundamental operations**:
- **read_item(X)** ŌĆö denoted as `r(X)`
- **write_item(X)** ŌĆö denoted as `w(X)`

Plus **commit (c)** or **rollback / abort (a)** as terminal operations.

### Step 2: Classify Schedules
A **Schedule (S)** is a sequence of read/write/commit operations from a set of `n` transactions, preserving the order of operations *within* each individual transaction.

| Schedule Type | Description | Acceptable? |
|---|---|---|
| **Serial Schedule** | Transactions execute one after another, with no interleaving | Ō£ģ Yes (correct by definition) |
| **Non-Serial Schedule** | Operations of different transactions are interleaved | ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ Only if **Serializable** |
| **Serializable Schedule (SR)** | A non-serial schedule that produces the **same result** as some serial schedule | Ō£ģ Yes |
| **Non-Serializable Schedule** | Result cannot be matched to any serial order | ŌØī Reject (causes inconsistency) |

### Step 3: Identify Problems in Concurrency

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Classical Problems (KTU must-know):**

1. **Lost Update Problem**
   - T1 reads X, computes new value.
   - T2 reads X (same old value), computes new value.
   - T2 writes X (overwrites T1's intended update).
   - T1 writes X (overwrites T2's update).
   - **Result:** Both updates lost.

2. **Uncommitted Dependency (Dirty Read)**
   - T2 reads X written by T1.
   - T1 is rolled back / aborted.
   - T2 has now read a value that **never existed** in the database.

3. **Inconsistent Analysis (Non-Repeatable Read)**
   - T1 reads X = 100.
   - T2 writes X = 200 (commits).
   - T1 re-reads X and gets 200 ŌĆö its analysis is now inconsistent.

4. **Phantom Read** (extension of non-repeatable read)
   - T1 executes `SELECT * FROM Student WHERE dept='CS'`.
   - T2 inserts a new CS student (commits).
   - T1 re-executes the same query ŌĆö a **phantom row** appears.

### Step 4: Serializability ŌĆö The Correctness Criterion

> [!NOTE]
> Serializability is the **gold standard** for testing the correctness of a concurrent schedule. There are two flavors in the KTU syllabus:

#### A. Conflict Serializability
Two operations **conflict** if they belong to **different transactions**, access the **same data item**, and **at least one is a write**.

**Conflict Types:**

| Conflict Pair | Notation | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| ReadŌĆōWrite | `rŌéü(X) ... wŌéé(X)` | T2 overwrites what T1 read |
| WriteŌĆōRead | `wŌéü(X) ... rŌéé(X)` | T2 reads value written by T1 |
| WriteŌĆōWrite | `wŌéü(X) ... wŌéé(X)` | WriteŌĆōwrite order matters |

> ReadŌĆōRead operations on the same item **never conflict** and can be freely reordered.

**Precedence Graph Method:**
- One **node per transaction**.
- A **directed edge TßĄó ŌåÆ TŌ▒╝** exists if any operation of TßĄó conflicts with an operation of TŌ▒╝, and TßĄó's operation **precedes** TŌ▒╝'s in the schedule.
- **Schedule is Conflict Serializable** Ōćö **Precedence Graph is Acyclic**.

#### B. View Serializability
A schedule is **View Equivalent** to a serial schedule if:
1. **Initial Read:** For each data item X, the transaction that performs the **first read** of X in both schedules is the same.
2. **Final Write:** For each data item X, the transaction that performs the **final write** of X in both schedules is the same.
3. **Read-From Relationship:** If TßĄó reads a value of X that was written by TŌ▒╝ in one schedule, the same must hold in the other.

> **KTU Examiner's Hint:** View Serializable is a **superset** of Conflict Serializable. Some view-serializable schedules have **no conflict-equivalent serial** (they contain **blind writes**).

### Step 5: Concurrency Control Protocols ŌĆö The Three Pillars

| Protocol Family | Mechanism | KTU Keywords |
|---|---|---|
| **Lock-Based (Pessimistic)** | Transactions acquire **shared (S)** or **exclusive (X)** locks before accessing data | 2PL, Lock Granularity |
| **Timestamp-Based (Optimistic-Light)** | Each transaction gets a unique **timestamp**; conflicting operations are ordered by timestamps | TS Ordering, Thomas' Write Rule |
| **Validation-Based (Optimistic)** | Transactions execute freely, then validate at commit time | **OCC** (Optimistic Concurrency Control) ŌĆö Read/Validate/Write phases |

### Step 6: Two-Phase Locking (2PL) ŌĆö The Foundation Protocol

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **2PL Rule:** Every transaction must follow **two phases**:
> 1. **Growing Phase** ŌĆö Acquire locks (no releases).
> 2. **Shrinking Phase** ŌĆö Release locks (no new acquisitions).
>
> **The transition point** (last lock acquired, first lock released) is called the **LOCK POINT**.

- **Basic 2PL** guarantees conflict serializability but allows **cascading aborts**.
- **Conservative (Static) 2PL** requires all locks upfront ŌåÆ deadlock-free.
- **Strict 2PL** holds all **exclusive locks until commit/abort** ŌåÆ no cascading aborts and **recoverable**.

### Step 7: Deadlock ŌĆö The Concurrency Trade-off

> [!WARNING]
> **Deadlock** occurs when two or more transactions are **stuck waiting for each other** in a circular chain, holding locks the others need.

**Deadlock Handling Strategies:**
- **Prevention** (e.g., Pre-declaration, Wait-Die, Wound-Wait) ŌĆö kill the younger transaction.
- **Detection** (Build a **Wait-For Graph**; cycle ŌćÆ deadlock) ŌĆö abort one to break cycle.
- **Avoidance** (e.g., Conservative 2PL).

### Step 8: Lock Granularity

> [!NOTE]
> Locks can be held at multiple **granularities**: **Database ŌåÆ Table ŌåÆ Page ŌåÆ Row (Tuple) ŌåÆ Attribute**.
> 
> A **Multi-Granularity Locking (MGL)** tree allows **intention locks** (`IS`, `IX`, `SIX`) to permit efficient locking at higher levels without checking every leaf.

### Step 9: ANSI/ISO SQL Isolation Levels

| Level | Dirty Read | Non-Repeatable Read | Phantom |
|---|---|---|---|
| **READ UNCOMMITTED** | Possible | Possible | Possible |
| **READ COMMITTED** | Prevented | Possible | Possible |
| **REPEATABLE READ** | Prevented | Prevented | Possible |
| **SERIALIZABLE** | Prevented | Prevented | Prevented |

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Term / Formula | Meaning / Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Schedule `S` over `TŌéü, TŌéé, ŌĆ”, TŌéÖ` | Ordered list of `r`/`w`/`c`/`a` ops | Preserves intra-transaction order |
| 2 | Conflict condition | `opßĄó(X)` conflicts `opŌ▒╝(X)` | Same X, diff T, Ōēź1 write |
| 3 | Precedence graph: `TßĄó ŌåÆ TŌ▒╝` | TßĄó must precede TŌ▒╝ in serial order | One edge per conflicting pair |
| 4 | Conflict Serializable Ōćö Acyclic graph | Necessary & sufficient | KTU standard test |
| 5 | **Lock Point** | Last acquire / first release | 2PL transition marker |
| 6 | **Wait-For Graph edge** `TßĄó ŌåÆ TŌ▒╝` | TßĄó waits for TŌ▒╝ to release a lock | Cycle ŌćÆ Deadlock |
| 7 | **Timestamp rule** `TS(TßĄó) < TS(TŌ▒╝)` | Older transaction has priority | Younger aborted/restarted |
| 8 | **OCC validation rule** | For all `TŌ▒╝` in validation: `TS(TŌ▒╝) < TS(TßĄó)` ŌćÆ no overlapping write-set | Otherwise TßĄó aborts |
| 9 | **Thomas' Write Rule** | Ignore obsolete `w(X)` when older TS still pending | Improves CC but breaks serializability class membership |
| 10 | **Isolation level ordering** | RU ŌŖé RC ŌŖé RR ŌŖå SER | Strict containment (KTU) |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking:** Prevents simultaneous withdrawals from causing negative balances.
- **E-commerce (Flipkart/Amazon):** Prevents overselling a product with limited stock.
- **Railway Reservation:** IRCTC uses **pessimistic locking** (FOR UPDATE) to lock a seat row during booking.
- **Air Traffic Control (analogy):** Serializes take-off/landing slots.
- **Distributed DBs (e.g., Google Spanner):** Use **TrueTime + 2PL + Paxos** for global serializability.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Conflict Serializability via Precedence Graph

### The Given Schedule `S`

Consider the following schedule involving two transactions `TŌéü` and `TŌéé` over data items `A` and `B`:

$$
S : r_1(A),\ r_2(A),\ w_1(A),\ r_1(B),\ w_2(A),\ w_1(B)
$$

Subscripts denote the **transaction ID**.

---

### Step 1 ŌĆö Identify All Read/Write Operations and Their Order

List the operations in chronological order with their transaction IDs:

| Order | Operation | Transaction | Data Item | Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `rŌéü(A)` | TŌéü | A | Read |
| 2 | `rŌéé(A)` | TŌéé | A | Read |
| 3 | `wŌéü(A)` | TŌéü | A | Write |
| 4 | `rŌéü(B)` | TŌéü | B | Read |
| 5 | `wŌéé(A)` | TŌéé | A | Write |
| 6 | `wŌéü(B)` | TŌéü | B | Write |

---

### Step 2 ŌĆö Detect All Conflicts (Same Item, Different T, Ōēź1 Write)

Scan the schedule for **conflicting pairs**. For every pair of operations from **different transactions** on the **same item** where **at least one is a write**, draw a precedence edge.

#### Conflict #1: `rŌéé(A)` vs `wŌéü(A)` on data item **A**
- Operation 2 (`rŌéé(A)`) and Operation 3 (`wŌéü(A)`)
- Same item `A`, different transactions (TŌéé, TŌéü), one is a write.
- `rŌéé(A)` **precedes** `wŌéü(A)` in S.
- **Rule:** For a serial order, the transaction that **reads first** must execute first, **OR** the write must be applied on the freshest read.
- In the serial equivalent, TŌéé must come before TŌéü (because TŌéé's read of A happens before TŌéü's write of A in S).
- **Edge drawn: `TŌéé ŌåÆ TŌéü`**

#### Conflict #2: `wŌéü(A)` vs `wŌéé(A)` on data item **A**
- Operation 3 (`wŌéü(A)`) and Operation 5 (`wŌéé(A)`)
- Same item `A`, different transactions, both are writes.
- `wŌéü(A)` **precedes** `wŌéé(A)` in S.
- **Rule:** The first write in S must be the first write in the equivalent serial schedule.
- **Edge drawn: `TŌéü ŌåÆ TŌéé`**

#### Conflict #3: `rŌéü(B)` vs `wŌéü(B)` ŌĆö SKIP
- Same transaction (TŌéü). Intra-transaction operations do not create precedence edges.

> **Total Edges: 2** ŌåÆ `TŌéé ŌåÆ TŌéü` and `TŌéü ŌåÆ TŌéé`

---

### Step 3 ŌĆö Construct the Precedence Graph

```
       TŌéé ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌåÆ TŌéü
       Ōåæ           Ōöé
       ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś
```

- The graph contains a **cycle**: TŌéü ŌåÆ TŌéé ŌåÆ TŌéü.
- By the **Cycle Test Theorem**, a schedule is **Conflict Serializable** if and only if its precedence graph is **acyclic**.

---

### Step 4 ŌĆö Conclusion

$$
\boxed{S \text{ is } \textbf{NOT} \text{ Conflict Serializable}}
$$

> **Why?** A cycle `TŌéü Ōåö TŌéé` means there is **no valid serial order** of TŌéü and TŌéé that preserves the conflict relationships. Forcing TŌéü before TŌéé violates the read order on A; forcing TŌéé before TŌéü violates the write order on A. Hence the schedule is incorrect and must be rejected by the DBMS scheduler.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: A Serializable Non-Serial Schedule

Consider:

$$
S : r_1(A),\ w_1(A),\ r_2(A),\ w_2(A),\ r_1(B),\ w_1(B),\ r_2(B),\ w_2(B)
$$

### Step 1 ŌĆö Identify Conflicts on A and B

| Pair | Same Item? | Ōēź1 Write? | Different T? | Conflict? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `wŌéü(A)` ... `rŌéé(A)` | A | Yes (wŌéü) | Yes | Ō£ģ `TŌéü ŌåÆ TŌéé` |
| `wŌéü(A)` ... `wŌéé(A)` | A | Yes | Yes | Ō£ģ `TŌéü ŌåÆ TŌéé` |
| `rŌéü(B)` ... `wŌéé(B)` | B | Yes (wŌéé) | Yes | Ō£ģ `TŌéü ŌåÆ TŌéé` |
| `wŌéü(B)` ... `wŌéé(B)` | B | Yes | Yes | Ō£ģ `TŌéü ŌåÆ TŌéé` |

### Step 2 ŌĆö Precedence Graph

```
   TŌéü ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌåÆ TŌéé
```

The graph is **acyclic** (single directed edge, no return path).

### Step 3 ŌĆö Conclusion

$$
\boxed{S \text{ is Conflict Serializable with equivalent serial order: } (T_1,\ T_2)}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: 2PL Lock Point Analysis

Given a transaction T:
1. `S-lock(A)`
2. `X-lock(B)`
3. `S-lock(C)` ŌåÉ **Lock Point reached here**
4. `Unlock(A)`
5. `Unlock(B)`
6. `X-lock(D)`
7. `Unlock(C)`
8. `Unlock(D)`

### Step 1 ŌĆö Identify Phases
- **Growing Phase (1ŌĆō3):** Only acquisitions.
- **Lock Point:** Operation 3.
- **Shrinking Phase (4ŌĆō8):** Releases followed by re-acquisition in step 6.

### Step 2 ŌĆö KTU Verdict

> [!WARNING]
> This transaction **violates 2PL**! After step 4 (`Unlock(A)`), the transaction has **entered the shrinking phase**. Acquiring a **new lock in step 6** is a violation. The protocol is **not satisfied** because the two-phase property requires **no acquisitions after the first release**.

The corrected 2PL-compliant version:

| Step | Operation | Phase |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `S-lock(A)` | Growing |
| 2 | `X-lock(B)` | Growing |
| 3 | `S-lock(C)` | Growing |
| 4 | `X-lock(D)` | Growing (lock point at step 4) |
| 5 | `Unlock(A)` | Shrinking |
| 6 | `Unlock(B)` | Shrinking |
| 7 | `Unlock(C)` | Shrinking |
| 8 | `Unlock(D)` | Shrinking |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Common KTU Question Patterns

| Question Type | Test Applied | Pass Criterion | Common Trap |
|---|---|---|---|
| Conflict Serializability | Precedence Graph | Acyclic | Forgetting same-item rule |
| View Serializability | Initial Read + Final Write + Read-From | All 3 preserved | Missing blind-write case |
| Recoverable Schedule | TŌ▒╝ reads from TßĄó ŌćÆ TŌ▒╝ commits **after** TßĄó | Holds for all such pairs | Ignoring abort order |
| Cascadeless Schedule | TŌ▒╝ reads **only committed** values | No dirty reads at all | Allowing committed-then-aborted |
| Strict Schedule | No read/write on uncommitted data | All writes held until commit | Allowing dirty writes |
| 2PL Compliance | Lock Point check | All acquires before any release | Late acquire after release |
| Deadlock Detection | Wait-For Graph | Cycle = Deadlock | Forgetting to abort victim |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Concurrency Control Manager ŌĆö Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    user1["User Transaction T1"] --> ccm{"Concurrency Control Manager"}
    user2["User Transaction T2"] --> ccm
    user3["User Transaction T3"] --> ccm
    ccm --> lp["Lock Manager Module"]
    ccm --> ts["Timestamp Allocator"]
    ccm --> val["Validation Module OCC"]
    lp --> lockTable["Lock Table Hash Map"]
    lp --> waitGraph["Wait For Graph Tracker"]
    ts --> tsCounter["Global Timestamp Counter"]
    val --> valLog["Validation Log Buffer"]
    lockTable --> executor["Transaction Executor"]
    waitGraph --> executor
    tsCounter --> executor
    valLog --> executor
    executor --> buffer["Database Buffer Cache"]
    buffer --> disk["Disk Storage"]
    ccm -.-> log["Recovery / Log Manager"]
    log --> disk
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** All incoming transactions must pass through the **Concurrency Control Manager (CCM)**. The CCM dispatches to one of three strategies: Locking, Timestamp, or Validation. Each strategy writes metadata to a shared data structure (Lock Table, TS Counter, Validation Log) and grants permission to the **Transaction Executor**, which then reads/writes the database. The Log Manager records all changes for **recoverability** (a Module 5 concern but tightly coupled here).

---

## 4.2 Two-Phase Locking (2PL) State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Growing: Transaction Start
    Growing --> Growing: Acquire Lock S or X
    Growing --> LockPoint: Last Lock Acquired
    LockPoint --> Shrinking: First Lock Released
    Shrinking --> Shrinking: Release Lock
    Shrinking --> [*]: Commit or Abort
    Growing --> [*]: Abort Possible
    note right of LockPoint
        Critical State
        No more lock acquisitions
        permitted after this point
    end note
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** A transaction begins in the **Growing** state and may acquire any number of `S` or `X` locks. The moment it releases its first lock, it transitions through the **Lock Point** to the **Shrinking** state. From the Lock Point onward, **no new locks may be acquired** ŌĆö only released. The transaction terminates on **commit** or **abort**.

---

## 4.3 Precedence Graph Example (Cyclic Case)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    t1["T1"] -- w1A then w2A on A --> t2["T2"]
    t2 -- r2A then w1A on A --> t1
```

> **Interpretation:** Edge `T1 ŌåÆ T2` from the `w1(A) ... w2(A)` conflict. Edge `T2 ŌåÆ T1` from the `r2(A) ... w1(A)` conflict. The cycle confirms the schedule is **NOT** conflict serializable.

---

## 4.4 Precedence Graph Example (Acyclic / Serializable)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    t1["T1"] -- All conflicts favor T1 first --> t2["T2"]
```

> **Interpretation:** A single directed edge `T1 ŌåÆ T2` means T1 must execute first in the equivalent serial schedule. The graph is a **DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph)**, hence the schedule is conflict serializable with order `(T1, T2)`.

---

## 4.5 Deadlock Wait-For Graph

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    t1["T1"] -- holds X lock on A --> t2["T2"]
    t2 -- holds Y lock on B --> t3["T3"]
    t3 -- holds Z lock on C --> t1
```

> **Interpretation:** The cycle `T1 ŌåÆ T2 ŌåÆ T3 ŌåÆ T1` indicates a **deadlock**. The DBMS deadlock detector will pick a **victim** (usually the youngest / lowest-priority transaction) and **abort it** to break the cycle.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **Define the following with respect to transaction processing:**
> **(a)** Concurrency Control
> **(b)** Schedule
> **(c)** Serializability

### **Model Answer:**

**(a) Concurrency Control:** Concurrency control is the DBMS mechanism that coordinates **simultaneously executing transactions** so that they produce a result equivalent to some serial execution, thereby preserving the **ACID properties**, particularly **Isolation** and **Consistency**.

**(b) Schedule:** A schedule (or history) `S` is a **chronological sequence of operations** (`read`, `write`, `commit`, `abort`) belonging to a set of concurrent transactions, with the constraint that the **order of operations within each individual transaction is preserved** as specified by that transaction.

**(c) Serializability:** Serializability is the **correctness criterion** for concurrent schedules. A non-serial schedule is **serializable** if its effect on the database is **identical to that of some serial schedule** of the same transactions. It exists in two forms: **Conflict Serializability** (verified via acyclic precedence graph) and **View Serializability** (verified via initial-read, final-write, and read-from equivalence).

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **Explain the Lost Update Problem with a suitable example.**

### **Model Answer:**

The **Lost Update Problem** occurs when two concurrent transactions read and update the **same data item** in such a way that the update performed by one transaction is **overwritten and lost** due to the interleaved execution of the other.

**Example:** Consider two transactions TŌéü and TŌéé updating a bank account balance X (initial value = 1000):

| Time | TŌéü | TŌéé | Database State |
|---|---|---|---|
| tŌéü | ŌĆö | `read_item(X)` ŌåÆ X = 1000 | 1000 |
| tŌéé | `read_item(X)` ŌåÆ X = 1000 | ŌĆö | 1000 |
| tŌéā | ŌĆö | `X := X - 100` (withdraw 100) | 1000 |
| tŌéä | `X := X + 500` (deposit 500) | ŌĆö | 1500 |
| tŌéģ | `write_item(X)` | ŌĆö | 1500 |
| tŌéå | ŌĆö | `write_item(X)` | 900 |

> **Result:** TŌéé's withdrawal of 100 is **LOST** because TŌéé's `write_item(X)` overwrote TŌéü's deposit. TŌéü's update is preserved, but TŌéé's update vanishes. The actual correct final balance should have been 1400 (1000 ŌłÆ 100 + 500 = 1400), but we end up with 900.

**Solution:** Use **Locking** (specifically, TŌéé's `read_item(X)` should be upgraded to an **exclusive lock** that TŌéü cannot bypass) or **Timestamp Ordering** so that the conflicting writes are ordered deterministically.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Both internal choice options are provided below.

---

### **Part B ŌĆö Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **(a)** What is a **Schedule**? Explain the difference between **Serial, Non-Serial, and Serializable** schedules with examples. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** What is **Conflict Serializability**? Explain the **Precedence Graph** algorithm. Test the following schedule `S` for conflict serializability:
>
> `S : r1(X), r2(X), w1(X), r1(Y), w2(X), w1(Y), r3(Y), w3(Y)` **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Schedule:** A schedule `S` is a sequence of database operations (`r`, `w`, `c`, `a`) from a set of concurrent transactions. The intra-transaction order is preserved.

**Types of Schedules:**

| Type | Definition | Example (TŌéü, TŌéé) |
|---|---|---|
| **Serial** | No interleaving; one transaction fully completes before the next begins | `rŌéü(A), wŌéü(A), cŌéü, rŌéé(A), wŌéé(A), cŌéé` |
| **Non-Serial** | Operations of multiple transactions are interleaved | `rŌéü(A), rŌéé(A), wŌéü(A), wŌéé(A)` |
| **Serializable** | A non-serial schedule that is **equivalent to some serial schedule** | `rŌéü(A), wŌéü(A), rŌéé(A), wŌéé(A)` (if equivalent to TŌéü;TŌéé) |

**Example illustrating the difference:**

```
Serial SŌéü:    rŌéü(X) wŌéü(X) rŌéü(Y) wŌéü(Y) | rŌéé(X) wŌéé(X)
Non-Serial:   rŌéü(X) rŌéé(X) wŌéü(X) wŌéé(X) rŌéü(Y) wŌéü(Y)
```

The non-serial example is serializable if it produces the same final state as SŌéü or as the alternate serial `SŌéé: rŌéé(X) wŌéé(X) | rŌéü(X) wŌéü(X) rŌéü(Y) wŌéü(Y)`.

**Significance:** Serializable non-serial schedules allow **parallelism** (better throughput) while guaranteeing **correctness** (same output as serial).

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Conflict Serializability:** A schedule is conflict serializable if it can be transformed into a serial schedule by **swapping only non-conflicting operations**. Two operations conflict if they belong to **different transactions**, access the **same data item**, and **at least one is a write**.

**Precedence Graph Algorithm:**
1. Create one **node per transaction**.
2. For every pair of conflicting operations `opßĄó(X)` and `opŌ▒╝(X)` where `opßĄó` precedes `opŌ▒╝` and `i ŌēĀ j`, draw a **directed edge** `TßĄó ŌåÆ TŌ▒╝`.
3. If the resulting graph is **acyclic**, the schedule is conflict serializable; the **topological order** of the graph gives an equivalent serial schedule.

**Applying to Schedule S:**

`S : r1(X), r2(X), w1(X), r1(Y), w2(X), w1(Y), r3(Y), w3(Y)`

| Step | Op | T | Item | Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `r1(X)` | TŌéü | X | R |
| 2 | `r2(X)` | TŌéé | X | R |
| 3 | `w1(X)` | TŌéü | X | W |
| 4 | `r1(Y)` | TŌéü | Y | R |
| 5 | `w2(X)` | TŌéé | X | W |
| 6 | `w1(Y)` | TŌéü | Y | W |
| 7 | `r3(Y)` | TŌéā | Y | R |
| 8 | `w3(Y)` | TŌéā | Y | W |

**Conflict Pairs Identified:**

| # | Op_i | Op_j | Item | Edge |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Step 2 `r2(X)` | Step 3 `w1(X)` | X | `TŌéé ŌåÆ TŌéü` |
| 2 | Step 3 `w1(X)` | Step 5 `w2(X)` | X | `TŌéü ŌåÆ TŌéé` |
| 3 | Step 4 `r1(Y)` | Step 6 `w1(Y)` | Y | (same T, skip) |
| 4 | Step 4 `r1(Y)` | Step 7 `r3(Y)` | Y | (R-R, no conflict) |
| 5 | Step 4 `r1(Y)` | Step 8 `w3(Y)` | Y | `TŌéü ŌåÆ TŌéā` |
| 6 | Step 6 `w1(Y)` | Step 7 `r3(Y)` | Y | `TŌéü ŌåÆ TŌéā` |
| 7 | Step 6 `w1(Y)` | Step 8 `w3(Y)` | Y | `TŌéü ŌåÆ TŌéā` |
| 8 | Step 7 `r3(Y)` | Step 8 `w3(Y)` | Y | (same T, skip) |

**Precedence Graph:**

```
   TŌéü ŌåÉŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌåÆ TŌéé       TŌéü ŌöĆŌöĆŌåÆ TŌéā
   (cycle)            (acyclic toward TŌéā)
```

> **Verdict:** There is a **cycle TŌéü ŌåÆ TŌéé ŌåÆ TŌéü** on data item X. The schedule is **NOT conflict serializable** because the cycle prevents a valid total ordering of TŌéü and TŌéé. The DBMS scheduler must **reject** this schedule or use a different protocol (e.g., 2PL with rollback) to enforce serializability.

---

### **Part B ŌĆö Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Internal Choice Alternative** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **(a)** What is **Two-Phase Locking (2PL)**? Explain **Basic 2PL, Conservative 2PL, and Strict 2PL** with their trade-offs. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** What is **Deadlock**? Explain **Deadlock Prevention** and **Deadlock Detection** strategies. Given the following wait-for information, construct the **Wait-For Graph** and identify if a deadlock exists:
>
> `T1 waits for T2, T2 waits for T3, T3 waits for T4, T4 waits for T1` **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Two-Phase Locking (2PL)** is a concurrency control protocol that guarantees conflict serializability. The protocol mandates that every transaction execute in **two distinct phases**:

1. **Growing Phase** ŌĆö Transaction may **acquire** locks (`S` or `X`) but **cannot release** any.
2. **Shrinking Phase** ŌĆö Transaction may **release** locks but **cannot acquire** any new locks.
3. The instant of transition (last acquire, first release) is the **Lock Point**.

**Variants of 2PL:**

| Variant | Behavior | Guarantees | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Basic 2PL** | Growing ŌåÆ Shrinking as defined | Conflict serializable | Allows **cascading aborts** (a transaction that read from an aborted transaction must itself abort) |
| **Conservative (Static) 2PL** | Transaction must acquire **all locks upfront** before execution begins | Deadlock-free, conflict serializable | **High overhead**; transaction must declare all needed items in advance (often impractical) |
| **Strict 2PL** | All **exclusive (X) locks held until commit/abort**; `S` locks released after use | Conflict serializable, **recoverable**, **no cascading aborts** | Slightly lower concurrency than basic 2PL; widely used in practice (e.g., MySQL InnoDB) |

**Lock Compatibility Matrix:**

| | Held S | Held X |
|---|---|---|
| **Request S** | Ō£ģ | ŌØī |
| **Request X** | ŌØī | ŌØī |

**Why 2PL Works (Intuition):** Because every transaction's lock acquisitions are bounded by its lock point, and any two transactions in conflict will have their lock points **ordered by serializability**, the resulting schedule is provably conflict serializable.

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Deadlock Definition:** A deadlock is a state in which two or more transactions are **permanently blocked**, each waiting for a lock held by another transaction in the cycle, so that none can ever proceed.

**Deadlock Prevention Strategies:**
- **Pre-declaration (Conservative 2PL):** All locks acquired upfront ŌåÆ no waiting ŌåÆ no deadlock.
- **Wait-Die Scheme:** When TßĄó requests a lock held by TŌ▒╝:
  - If `TS(TßĄó) < TS(TŌ▒╝)` (TßĄó is older), TßĄó **waits**.
  - Else (TßĄó is younger), TßĄó **dies (aborts)** and is later restarted.
- **Wound-Wait Scheme:**
  - If `TS(TßĄó) < TS(TŌ▒╝)` (TßĄó is older), TßĄó **wounds** TŌ▒╝ (TŌ▒╝ aborts).
  - Else (TßĄó is younger), TßĄó **waits**.

**Deadlock Detection:**
- Maintain a **Wait-For Graph** (nodes = transactions, edge `TßĄó ŌåÆ TŌ▒╝` if TßĄó waits for TŌ▒╝).
- Periodically check for **cycles**.
- On detecting a cycle, **abort the victim** (youngest / least work done) to break the cycle.

**Constructing the Wait-For Graph for the Given Scenario:**

Given: `T1 waits for T2`, `T2 waits for T3`, `T3 waits for T4`, `T4 waits for T1`.

Edges:
- `T1 ŌåÆ T2`
- `T2 ŌåÆ T3`
- `T3 ŌåÆ T4`
- `T4 ŌåÆ T1`

**Graph Structure:**

```
   T1 ŌåÆ T2 ŌåÆ T3 ŌåÆ T4
   Ōåæ                Ōöé
   ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś
```

> **Conclusion:** A **cycle exists**: `T1 ŌåÆ T2 ŌåÆ T3 ŌåÆ T4 ŌåÆ T1`. This is a **DEADLOCK**.
>
> **Resolution:** The DBMS will select a **victim transaction** to abort (e.g., T1, the one with the lowest priority or smallest work done) to break the cycle. The aborted transaction is later **restarted** by the system.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Missing intra-transaction conflict check:** Many students draw an edge `TßĄó ŌåÆ TßĄó` for same-transaction operations. **Penalty: ŌłÆ1 mark per such error.**
> 2. **Forgetting Read-Read is NOT a conflict:** Always check that **at least one operation is a write**. Drawing edges on R-R pairs loses marks.
> 3. **Confusing View and Conflict Serializability:** A view-serializable schedule may not be conflict serializable (and vice versa is not true ŌĆö every conflict-serializable IS view-serializable).
> 4. **2PL Lock Point misidentification:** A transaction that acquires a lock AFTER releasing one is **not** 2PL-compliant, even if it serializes correctly.
> 5. **Deadlock vs. Starvation:** Starvation is when a transaction waits **indefinitely** but other transactions complete. A deadlock is a **circular wait**. Don't confuse the two in exam answers.
> 6. **In Wait-For Graph questions, forgetting the arrow direction:** `T1 waits for T2` means edge `T1 ŌåÆ T2` (T1 is the waiter, T2 holds the lock).
> 7. **Not labeling operations with transaction IDs:** In the schedule trace, always use subscripts (`r1`, `w2`) so the examiner can follow your conflict identification.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Bullet Summary ŌĆö Revise before entering the exam hall:**

- Ō£ģ **Concurrency Control** ensures **Isolation & Consistency** of ACID in multi-user environments.
- Ō£ģ **Three classical problems:** Lost Update, Uncommitted Dependency (Dirty Read), Inconsistent Analysis (Non-Repeatable Read).
- Ō£ģ **Schedule = chronological order of operations** from multiple transactions, preserving intra-transaction order.
- Ō£ģ **Serial schedule** is trivially correct. **Non-serial** is acceptable **only if serializable**.
- Ō£ģ **Conflict condition:** Different T, same item, **at least one write**.
- Ō£ģ **Precedence Graph Algorithm:** Nodes = transactions, edge = conflict precedence. **Acyclic ŌćÆ Conflict Serializable.**
- Ō£ģ **View Serializable = Conflict Serializable Ōł¬ Blind-Write schedules** (superset relationship).
- Ō£ģ **2PL = Growing Phase (acquire only) + Shrinking Phase (release only)**. Lock Point is the pivot.
- Ō£ģ **Strict 2PL** holds X-locks until commit ŌåÆ prevents cascading aborts ŌåÆ used in production.
- Ō£ģ **Conservative 2PL** acquires all locks upfront ŌåÆ deadlock-free ŌåÆ high overhead.
- Ō£ģ **Deadlock = cycle in Wait-For Graph**. Resolved by aborting a victim.
- Ō£ģ **Deadlock Prevention:** Wait-Die (older waits, younger dies), Wound-Wait (older wounds, younger waits).
- Ō£ģ **Timestamp Ordering (TSO):** Older timestamp = higher priority. Younger conflicting ops are aborted.
- Ō£ģ **Thomas' Write Rule** allows ignoring obsolete writes ŌĆö improves concurrency but produces schedules that are **NOT** conflict serializable.
- Ō£ģ **Optimistic CC (OCC):** Read ŌåÆ Validate ŌåÆ Write. Validation checks the read/write set against all concurrently committed transactions.
- Ō£ģ **Lock Granularity:** DB ŌåÆ Table ŌåÆ Page ŌåÆ Row ŌåÆ Attribute. **Intention locks (IS, IX, SIX)** enable multi-granularity locking.
- Ō£ģ **ANSI Isolation Levels (in increasing strictness):** Read Uncommitted ŌŖé Read Committed ŌŖé Repeatable Read ŌŖé Serializable.
- Ō£ģ **Phantom Read** = new row appears in a re-issued range query; prevented only at **Serializable** level.
- Ō£ģ **Starvation ŌēĀ Deadlock.** Starvation is indefinite wait; deadlock is circular wait.
- Ō£ģ **Recoverable Schedule:** if TŌ▒╝ reads from TßĄó, then TŌ▒╝ commits only after TßĄó commits.
- Ō£ģ **Cascadeless Schedule:** transactions only read committed values.
- Ō£ģ **Strict Schedule:** no read/write of uncommitted data ŌĆö recoverable + cascadeless.
- Ō£ģ **KTU Hot Topics** (most frequently asked): Lost Update Problem, Precedence Graph, 2PL, Deadlock Wait-For Graph, ANSI Isolation Levels.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transaction Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transaction Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transaction (Elmasri & Navathe, KTU 2024 Definition):** A *transaction* is a logical unit of database processing that includes one or more database access operations (read, write, insert, delete, update) executed as a single, indivisible logical unit of work.

Formally, a transaction is a sequence of operations performed on a database that transforms the database from one **consistent state** to another **consistent state**. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for PBEOT504, a transaction is treated as the fundamental execution unit that the DBMS must control to guarantee data integrity under concurrent access and system failures.

Mathematically, a transaction `T` can be written as:

$$
T : \{ \text{read}(X), \text{write}(X), \text{commit}, \text{abort} \}
$$

Where `X` is a database item (granule). The boundary operators are:
- **Commit** ŌåÆ signals successful end; changes become permanent.
- **Abort / Rollback** ŌåÆ signals failure; all changes are undone.

> [!NOTE]
> **Begin_Transaction** and **End_Transaction** are implicit statement delimiters used in SQL-standard transaction syntax (`START TRANSACTION; ... COMMIT;` or `ROLLBACK;`).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are transferring **Ōé╣5,000** from your **Savings Account** to your **Friend's Account** using a mobile banking app. This single user action actually performs two separate database operations:

1. **Debit** Ōé╣5,000 from your account.
2. **Credit** Ōé╣5,000 into your friend's account.

Now imagine a system crash **exactly between** steps 1 and 2. Your account is debited, but the money never reaches your friend. **Disaster!** To prevent this, the DBMS wraps both operations inside a single **transaction**. Either *both* happen, or *neither* happens ŌĆö never only one.

| Banking Step | Database Operation | Database Item Affected |
|---|---|---|
| Open banking app | `BEGIN_TRANSACTION` | ŌĆö |
| Money leaves your account | `write(Savings, Bal ŌłÆ 5000)` | `Savings` |
| Money reaches friend | `write(Friend, Bal + 5000)` | `Friend` |
| You click "Confirm" | `COMMIT` | ŌĆö |
| App crashes midway | `ABORT` (auto) | `ROLLBACK` undoes step 2 |

> [!TIP]
> The simplest mental model: **A transaction is to the database what a "package deal" is to commerce ŌĆö it is all-or-nothing.**

---

## 1.3 Why Transactions Matter in KTU Perspective

In KTU's Module 4, the Transaction Model is the *gateway concept* to:
- **Concurrency Control** (locking, timestamping, optimistic methods),
- **Recovery Techniques** (log-based, shadow paging, ARIES),
- **Distributed Transactions** (2PC, 3PC).

All higher-level guarantees in a DBMS are layered on top of the **transaction abstraction**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory KTU Key Term:** A transaction is the *smallest unit of work* that the recovery subsystem and concurrency controller treat as atomic.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics in the Transaction Model

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Notation |
|---|---|---|
| `T_i` | The i-th transaction | TŌéü, TŌéé, TŌéā ŌĆ” |
| `R_i(X)` | Read by TßĄó on item X | Operation instance |
| `W_i(X)` | Write by TßĄó on item X | Operation instance |
| `C_i` | Commit of TßĄó | Boundary operator |
| `A_i` | Abort of TßĄó | Boundary operator |
| `S` | Schedule (sequence of ops) | `S = opŌéü, opŌéé, ŌĆ”, opŌéÖ` |
| **ACID** | Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability | **4 mandatory properties** |

The four ACID properties are the **backbone** of the entire Module 4 ŌĆö every concept thereafter (locking, recovery, serializability) ties back to guaranteeing one or more of these.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The ACID Properties ŌĆö The Heart of the Transaction Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ACID** is the universally accepted KTU/KTU-aligned framework. Every KTU exam question on transactions will test at least one of these.

### 2.1.1 Atomicity
A transaction is an **all-or-nothing** unit. Either **all** operations of the transaction are reflected correctly in the database, or **none** are.

- Implementation: **Transaction Manager (TM)** + **Recovery Manager (RM)** using the system log.
- Aborted transactions are **rolled back** using the *before-image* (`old_value`).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real Engineering Utility:** Banking, airline reservation, e-commerce checkout ŌĆö all rely on atomicity to prevent money-loss or seat-duplication.

### 2.1.2 Consistency
A transaction must transform the database from one **consistent state** to another **consistent state**.

- A *consistent state* satisfies **all integrity constraints** declared on the schema (primary key, foreign key, check, trigger).
- The consistency check is performed at **commit time** in most DBMSs.

$$
\text{If } T \text{ starts in consistent state } C_s \Rightarrow T \text{ ends in } C_s' \text{ (also consistent)}
$$

### 2.1.3 Isolation
A transaction must appear as if it is **executed in isolation** from other concurrent transactions, even though the DBMS may interleave operations for performance.

- Implementation: **Concurrency Control Manager (CCM)** using locks / timestamps / multiversion.
- Isolation level choice: `READ UNCOMMITTED` ŌåÆ `SERIALIZABLE`.

### 2.1.4 Durability
Once a transaction **commits**, its effects **must persist** in the database ŌĆö even in the face of power failure, crash, or OS error.

- Implementation: **Forced write** of commit record to the log; **checkpointing**; database flushing.
- *Recovery Manager* uses the log to redo committed transactions after a crash.

---

## 2.2 Transaction States and State Transition Logic

A transaction moves through a finite set of states during its lifetime. The state diagram is a **mandatory KTU diagram** (asked in almost every board exam).

### Step-by-Step Logic of the State Diagram

1. **Active State (initial)** ŌåÆ Transaction starts. Read/Write operations are executed.
2. The transaction evaluates its final state:
   - If all operations succeed and `COMMIT` is issued ŌåÆ **Partially Committed**.
   - If some operation fails or `ABORT` is issued ŌåÆ **Failed**.
3. **Partially Committed State** ŌåÆ All operations executed, but changes are still in the buffer (not yet flushed).
   - If commit is successfully written to log and DB ŌåÆ **Committed (terminal)**.
   - If crash occurs before commit record is safely stored ŌåÆ **Failed**.
4. **Failed State** ŌåÆ Transaction cannot proceed. The system rolls it back.
   - After rollback completes ŌåÆ **Aborted (terminal)**.
5. **Aborted State** ŌåÆ Transaction has been rolled back. The DBMS may *restart* the transaction (optional).

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick (KTU Favorite):** "A-P-P-F-A-C" = *Active ŌåÆ Partially Committed ŌåÆ Partially CommittedŌåÆ Failed ŌåÆ Aborted ŌåÆ Committed*. (The two terminal states are **Committed** and **Aborted**.)

---

## 2.3 Transaction Operations (System-Level Primitives)

| Operation | Symbol | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Read | `read_item(X)` | Reads value of `X` from DB into transaction-local buffer. |
| Write | `write_item(X)` | Writes transaction-local value of `X` to DB. |
| Commit | `commit` | Signals successful termination. |
| Abort | `abort` | Signals failed termination; triggers rollback. |
| Begin | `begin_transaction` | Marks the start of `T`. |

---

## 2.4 Concurrent Transactions and the Need for a Schedule

When multiple transactions execute **simultaneously**, their operations are interleaved by the OS scheduler. The total order of operations executed is called a **Schedule**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Schedule (KTU Definition):** A schedule `S` is a sequence of operations from one or more transactions such that the order of operations within each individual transaction is preserved.

### 2.4.1 Types of Schedules

| Type | Description | KTU Exam Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Serial Schedule** | All operations of TŌéü execute, then all of TŌéé. No interleaving. | Reference benchmark for correctness. |
| **Interleaved (Non-serial) Schedule** | Operations of TŌéü and TŌéé are mixed. | Needs to be tested for serializability. |
| **Serializable Schedule** | An interleaved schedule that produces the same result as some serial schedule. | The "gold standard" for correctness. |
| **Recoverable Schedule** | A schedule where committed transactions never need to be rolled back. | Required for crash recovery. |
| **Cascadeless Schedule** | A schedule where transactions only read committed values. | Prevents cascading aborts. |
| **Strict Schedule** | A schedule where transactions neither read nor write uncommitted values. | Used in ARIES-style recovery. |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains every equation/relation you should memorize for Module 4 Transaction Model.

| Concept | Formula / Notation | Explanation | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transaction boundary | `T_i : {opŌéü, opŌéé, ŌĆ”, op_n}` | Sequential set of operations | DBMS internal model |
| ACID guarantee | `Begin ŌåÆ Operations ŌåÆ Commit / Abort` | All-or-nothing semantic | Fault tolerance |
| Serial schedule | `S = TŌéü TŌéé TŌéā ŌĆ” T_n` | TŌéü fully before TŌéé | Reference benchmark |
| Interleaved schedule | `S = op(TŌéü), op(TŌéé), op(TŌéü), op(TŌéé)` | Concurrency allowed | Performance optimization |
| Serializability condition | `S Ōēł S_serial` (equivalent effect) | Conflict- or View-equivalent | Correctness proof |
| Number of possible serial schedules for `n` transactions | `n!` | For n=3 ŌåÆ 6 | Computational complexity |
| Number of possible interleaved schedules (upper bound) | `(n! \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} (k_i)! )` | Where `k_i` = #ops of T_i | Hard combinatorial problem |
| Read-Dependency | `T_j` reads `X` written by `T_i` | `T_i \rightarrow T_j` | Lost update anomaly |
| Write-Dependency | `T_j` writes `X` after `T_i` writes `X` | `T_i \rightarrow T_j` | Dirty write |
| Read-Write Conflict | `T_i` reads `X`, `T_j` writes `X` | Conflicting pair | Non-serializable |
| Write-Read Conflict | `T_i` writes `X`, `T_j` reads `X` | Conflicting pair | Dirty read |
| Write-Write Conflict | `T_i` writes `X`, `T_j` writes `X` | Conflicting pair | Lost update |

> [!TIP]
> **Watch out:** In the table above, the symbol `|` is *not* used (e.g., for absolute value). The vertical bar is rendered as `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX to avoid breaking markdown tables.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Domain | Transaction Use Case | ACID Property Most Critical |
|---|---|---|
| Banking | Fund transfer, NEFT, UPI | Atomicity + Durability |
| Airline Reservation | Seat booking | Isolation + Atomicity |
| E-Commerce | Order placement + inventory | Atomicity + Consistency |
| Telecom Billing | Call-record charging | Durability |
| Hospital Records | Patient data updates | Consistency + Isolation |
| Stock Trading | Buy/sell orders | Isolation + Durability |

The Transaction Model is, in fact, the **theoretical foundation** of every modern OLTP system. Without it, no enterprise-grade database could guarantee the trust that billions of users place in digital services daily.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Number of Possible Serial Schedules

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **frequently asked KTU problem** worth 2ŌĆō3 marks, often combined with a serializability question.

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement
Given `n` concurrent transactions, how many distinct **serial schedules** exist?

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

Let there be `n` transactions: `TŌéü, TŌéé, ŌĆ”, T_n`.

A **serial schedule** is a total ordering of the transactions, where each transaction is executed completely before the next begins.

Step 1 ŌĆö For the first position in the schedule, we may choose any one of the `n` transactions.
Step 2 ŌĆö For the second position, we may choose any one of the remaining `n ŌłÆ 1` transactions.
Step 3 ŌĆö Continue this process.
Step 4 ŌĆö The number of orderings equals the number of permutations of `n` distinct objects.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of Serial Schedules} &= n \times (n-1) \times (n-2) \times \dots \times 1 \\
&= n!
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Numerical Examples

- `n = 1` ŌåÆ `1! = 1` schedule.
- `n = 2` ŌåÆ `2! = 2` schedules: `TŌéüTŌéé` and `TŌééTŌéü`.
- `n = 3` ŌåÆ `3! = 6` schedules.
- `n = 4` ŌåÆ `4! = 24` schedules.

> [!NOTE]
> The exponential growth of `n!` motivates why brute-force checking of all serial schedules is **infeasible** for large `n`, leading to the development of **conflict serializability** testing using precedence graphs (covered in depth in Module 5).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: The Schedule Equivalence Condition (Conflict Serializability Foundation)

### 3.2.1 Formal Definition of Conflict

Two operations `op_i` and `op_j` from transactions `T_i` and `T_j` (where `i ŌēĀ j`) are said to be in **conflict** if and only if:

1. They belong to **different transactions**.
2. They access the **same database item** `X`.
3. **At least one** of them is a `write_item` operation.

### 3.2.2 Enumeration of the Three Conflict Types

$$
\text{Conflict Types} = \{
\begin{array}{l}
\text{ReadŌĆōWrite (RW): } T_i \text{ reads } X,\ T_j \text{ writes } X \\[4pt]
\text{WriteŌĆōRead (WR): } T_i \text{ writes } X,\ T_j \text{ reads } X \\[4pt]
\text{WriteŌĆōWrite (WW): } T_i \text{ writes } X,\ T_j \text{ writes } X
\end{array}
$$

ReadŌĆōRead (RR) is **not** a conflict, because the order of two reads on the same item does not change the final database state.

### 3.2.3 Conflict Equivalence Theorem

> **Theorem (Conflict Equivalence):** Two schedules `S` and `S'` are *conflict-equivalent* if and only if they involve the same set of transactions and the order of every pair of conflicting operations is identical in `S` and `S'`.

$$
S \equiv_C S' \iff \forall (\text{conflicting pair } (p, q) \in S) : (p, q) \text{ appears in the same order in } S'
$$

### 3.2.4 Logical Consequence

A schedule `S` is **conflict-serializable** if it is conflict-equivalent to *some* serial schedule.

$$
S \text{ is CSR} \iff \exists \text{ serial } S_{ser} : S \equiv_C S_{ser}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Anomalies on the Database

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is a **favorite 14-mark question** in KTU ESE. It tests the understanding of the three classical concurrency anomalies.

| Anomaly | Operations Involved | TŌéü Action | TŌéé Action | Resulting Problem | KTU-Mandated Fix |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Lost Update Problem** | `WŌéü(X), WŌéé(X)` | Reads `X = 100`, writes `X = 150` | Reads `X = 100` (stale), writes `X = 200` | TŌéü's update is overwritten/lost | Use `WŌéé(X)` *after* `WŌéü(X)` is committed, or use lock-based `2PL`. |
| **Dirty Read Problem** | `WŌéü(X), RŌéé(X), AŌéü` | Writes `X = 500`, then aborts | Reads the uncommitted `X = 500` | TŌéé uses a value that never existed | Use **strict 2PL** or **cascadeless** schedule. |
| **Unrepeatable Read** | `RŌéü(X), WŌéé(X), RŌéü(X)` | Reads `X = 100`, then re-reads `X` | Updates `X = 200` and commits | TŌéü sees two different values for the same `X` | Use **lock-based concurrency** with hold time covering all reads. |

### 3.3.1 Step-by-Step Trace of the Lost Update Problem

> [!NOTE]
> A typical KTU Part A question asks: *"Explain the lost update problem with an example."*

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Time } t_1 : R_1(X) \quad \Rightarrow \text{ T}_1 \text{ reads } X = 100 \\
&\text{Time } t_2 : R_2(X) \quad \Rightarrow \text{ T}_2 \text{ reads } X = 100 \\
&\text{Time } t_3 : W_1(X) \quad \Rightarrow \text{ T}_1 \text{ writes } X = X + 50 = 150 \\
&\text{Time } t_4 : W_2(X) \quad \Rightarrow \text{ T}_2 \text{ writes } X = X + 100 = 200 \\
&\text{Final value of } X &= 200
\end{aligned}
$$

But the **correct** final value (if both transactions executed serially as `TŌéü ŌåÆ TŌéé`) should have been:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{After T}_1 : X = 150 \\
&\text{After T}_2 : X = 150 + 100 = 250
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Therefore, the value `200` in the database is "wrong" ŌĆö TŌéü's update of 50 was lost.** This is the **Lost Update Problem**.

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Schedule Analysis Workflow (KTU Method)

The KTU board expects a structured analysis whenever a schedule is given. Follow these **four canonical steps**:

1. **Identify** all operations and group them by transaction.
2. **Detect** all conflicting operation pairs (RW, WR, WW).
3. **Construct** the **precedence graph** (nodes = transactions, edges = conflict direction).
4. **Test** for serializability:
   - If the graph has a **cycle** ŌåÆ **NOT conflict-serializable** ŌØī
   - If the graph is **acyclic** ŌåÆ **conflict-serializable** Ō£ģ and the topological order yields the equivalent serial schedule.

$$
\text{Schedule } S \text{ is CSR} \iff \text{PrecedenceGraph}(S) \text{ is acyclic}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Hack:** When you build the precedence graph, draw an edge `T_i ŌåÆ T_j` for every conflict where `T_i`'s operation comes *before* `T_j`'s operation in `S`. If you can find a cycle, the schedule is non-serializable.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: From Schedule to Conflict-Serializability Test

Consider the following schedule on item `X` with three transactions:

$$
S : R_1(X),\ W_2(X),\ W_1(X),\ R_3(X),\ W_3(X)
$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify conflicts:**
- `R_1(X)` and `W_2(X)` ŌåÆ conflict (RW). `TŌéü` before `TŌéé` ŌåÆ edge `TŌéü ŌåÆ TŌéé`.
- `R_1(X)` and `W_1(X)` ŌåÆ same transaction, no conflict.
- `W_2(X)` and `W_1(X)` ŌåÆ conflict (WW). `TŌéé` before `TŌéü` ŌåÆ edge `TŌéé ŌåÆ TŌéü`.
- `W_1(X)` and `R_3(X)` ŌåÆ conflict (WR). `TŌéü` before `TŌéā` ŌåÆ edge `TŌéü ŌåÆ TŌéā`.
- `W_1(X)` and `W_3(X)` ŌåÆ conflict (WW). `TŌéü` before `TŌéā` ŌåÆ edge `TŌéü ŌåÆ TŌéā`.
- `R_3(X)` and `W_3(X)` ŌåÆ same transaction, no conflict.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Precedence graph edges:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E = \{ T_1 \rightarrow T_2,\ T_2 \rightarrow T_1,\ T_1 \rightarrow T_3 \}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Cycle detection:** The edges `TŌéü ŌåÆ TŌéé` and `TŌéé ŌåÆ TŌéü` form a **cycle** of length 2.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Conclusion:** `S` is **NOT conflict-serializable** ŌØī.

> [!NOTE]
> This example is intentionally simple for illustration. KTU exams usually give 2 transactions with 3ŌĆō5 operations each ŌĆö perfect for a 7-mark sub-question.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Transaction State Transition Diagram (KTU Mandatory Diagram)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most frequently drawn diagram** in KTU Module 4. It appears in nearly every university exam paper. Use it to score full marks in 7-mark questions on the transaction model.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> TXACTIVE : begin_transaction
    TXACTIVE --> TXPARTIALLYCOMMITTED : final operation executed
    TXACTIVE --> TXFAILED : abort or error detected
    TXPARTIALLYCOMMITTED --> TXCOMMITTED : commit record written to log
    TXPARTIALLYCOMMITTED --> TXFAILED : crash before commit record persists
    TXFAILED --> TXABORTED : rollback complete
    TXABORTED --> [*]
    TXCOMMITTED --> [*]
    TXABORTED --> TXACTIVE : restart by DBMS (optional)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **State Labels Used (KTU Standard):**
> - `ACTIVE` ŌĆö transaction is executing.
> - `PARTIALLY COMMITTED` ŌĆö final statement completed.
> - `COMMITTED` ŌĆö all changes made permanent (terminal).
> - `FAILED` ŌĆö normal execution can no longer proceed.
> - `ABORTED` ŌĆö rollback done, DB restored (terminal).

---

## 4.2 ACID Properties as a Layered Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef atomicityStyle fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef consistencyStyle fill:#E0FFE0,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef isolationStyle fill:#E0E0FF,stroke:#4169E1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef durabilityStyle fill:#FFE0E0,stroke:#B22222,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef userStyle fill:#F0F0F0,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    USER([Application User / Program]):::userStyle

    subgraph TXN[Transaction Manager Layer]
        direction TB
        A1[Atomicity Module]:::atomicityStyle
        C1[Consistency Module]:::consistencyStyle
        I1[Isolation Module]:::isolationStyle
        D1[Durability Module]:::durabilityStyle
    end

    subgraph BACKEND[Database Backend]
        direction TB
        LOG[System Log File]
        BUFFER[Database Buffer]
        DISK[(Stable Storage / Disk)]
        RM[Recovery Manager]
        CM[Concurrency Manager]
    end

    USER --> A1
    USER --> C1
    USER --> I1
    USER --> D1

    A1 --> RM
    D1 --> LOG
    D1 --> DISK
    I1 --> CM
    I1 --> BUFFER
    C1 --> BUFFER
    RM --> LOG
    CM --> BUFFER
    BUFFER --> DISK
```

> [!TIP]
> **Block Reading Order (left to right, top to bottom):** User request ŌåÆ ACID modules ŌåÆ Backend managers (Recovery + Concurrency) ŌåÆ Log + Buffer ŌåÆ Disk. This is exactly how a real RDBMS like Oracle, PostgreSQL, or MySQL/InnoDB pipelines a transaction.

---

## 4.3 Precedence Graph Construction Flow (Module 4 Analytical Tool)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef inputStyle fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520,stroke-width:2px
    classDef processStyle fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#9370DB,stroke-width:2px
    classDef outputStyle fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px

    IN1[Step 1: Read the Given Schedule S]:::inputStyle
    IN2[Step 2: Identify all Read and Write operations]:::inputStyle
    PROC1[Step 3: Find all conflicting pairs of operations]:::processStyle
    PROC2[Step 4: For each conflict, draw a directed edge Ti then Tj]:::processStyle
    PROC3[Step 5: Cycle detection in the graph]:::processStyle
    OUT1[OUTPUT: Schedule is Conflict Serializable]:::outputStyle
    OUT2[OUTPUT: Schedule is NOT Conflict Serializable]:::outputStyle

    IN1 --> IN2 --> PROC1 --> PROC2 --> PROC3
    PROC3 -->|Acyclic| OUT1
    PROC3 -->|Cyclic| OUT2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall to Avoid:** A common student mistake is treating *ReadŌĆōRead* pairs as conflicts. They are NOT conflicts. Only ReadŌĆōWrite, WriteŌĆōRead, and WriteŌĆōWrite pairs are conflicts.

---

## 4.4 Schedule Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef strictStyle fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,stroke-width:2px
    classDef cascadeStyle fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px
    classDef recoverStyle fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#1E90FF,stroke-width:2px
    classDef serialStyle fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px

    SCH[Schedule S]:::serialStyle
    SR[Serial Schedule - No Interleaving]:::serialStyle
    SER[Serializable Schedule]:::recoverStyle
    REC[Recoverable Schedule]:::recoverStyle
    CAS[Cascadeless Schedule]:::cascadeStyle
    STR[Strict Schedule]:::strictStyle

    SCH --> SR
    SCH --> SER
    SER --> REC
    REC --> CAS
    CAS --> STR
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inclusion Hierarchy:** `Strict ŌŖé Cascadeless ŌŖé Recoverable ŌŖé Serializable ŌŖé All Schedules`. Strict is the strongest; serial is the simplest reference point.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: Define a transaction in DBMS. List the four ACID properties of a transaction.**

**Model Answer:**

> A *transaction* is a logical unit of database processing that includes one or more database access operations executed as an **indivisible** logical unit of work. It transforms the database from one consistent state to another.

The four ACID properties are:

1. **Atomicity** ŌĆö All-or-nothing execution of the operations of a transaction.
2. **Consistency** ŌĆö A transaction preserves the integrity constraints of the database.
3. **Isolation** ŌĆö Concurrent transactions appear to execute in isolation from one another.
4. **Durability** ŌĆö The effects of a committed transaction persist permanently in the database.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between a serial schedule and a serializable schedule.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Serial Schedule | Serializable Schedule |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | All operations of one transaction execute before the next begins. | An interleaved schedule whose effect is equivalent to some serial schedule. |
| Performance | Poor (no concurrency benefit). | High (allows concurrency with correctness). |
| Use in DBMS | Benchmark for correctness. | Actual execution target. |
| Conflict test | Trivially serializable. | Tested via precedence graph. |

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks ŌĆö Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE Module 4 typically provides a single 14-mark question from the Transaction Model topic. The pattern is *either/or* (internal choice). Below are **two complete alternatives**, each with two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### ŌØ¢ Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Modified]

**(a)** Explain the **ACID properties** of a transaction in detail, with an example for each property. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **states of a transaction** with a neat state transition diagram. Explain what happens when a transaction moves from the *Partially Committed* state to the *Failed* state. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

The ACID properties guarantee reliable transaction processing in a DBMS.

**1. Atomicity:** A transaction is an *atomic unit*; either all its operations are completed or none are. If any part fails, the entire transaction is rolled back.
- *Example:* In a fund transfer of Ōé╣5,000, the **debit** of the sender and the **credit** of the receiver must both happen, or neither must happen.

**2. Consistency:** A transaction must transform the database from one consistent state to another. All integrity constraints (primary key, foreign key, check) must hold before and after the transaction.
- *Example:* If the total balance across all accounts is `S` before the transaction, it must remain `S` after the transaction completes.

**3. Isolation:** Concurrent transactions must not interfere with one another. Intermediate states of a transaction must be invisible to other transactions.
- *Example:* Two customers booking the last seat on a flight must not both succeed; one must be isolated from the other.

**4. Durability:** Once a transaction commits, its effects must persist in the database, even in the case of a system crash or power failure.
- *Example:* After a successful online purchase, the order record remains permanent even if the server crashes immediately afterward.

#### Model Solution to (b)

A transaction goes through **five states**: Active, Partially Committed, Committed, Failed, and Aborted.

| State | Description |
|---|---|
| Active | The initial state. Read/Write operations are executed. |
| Partially Committed | The final operation has been executed, but commit is not yet recorded. |
| Committed | The transaction has successfully completed and its effects are permanently stored. |
| Failed | The transaction can no longer proceed (e.g., due to a constraint violation or system error). |
| Aborted | The transaction has been rolled back, and the database is restored to its pre-transaction state. |

**State transition from Partially Committed to Failed:**

When a transaction enters the *Partially Committed* state, all its operations have executed in the main memory buffers, but the changes have not yet been written to the stable storage (disk). If at this moment a system crash, power failure, or media failure occurs, the commit record cannot be safely written to the log. Consequently, the transaction moves to the **Failed** state. The Recovery Manager then initiates a rollback procedure, using the *before-images* stored in the log to undo all changes that were buffered.

---

### ŌØ¢ Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified]

**(a)** With a suitable example, explain the **Lost Update Problem**, **Dirty Read Problem**, and **Unrepeatable Read Problem** in concurrent transactions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Construct the **precedence graph** for the following schedule `S` and determine whether it is conflict-serializable. If yes, identify the equivalent serial schedule. **(7 marks)**

$$
S : R_1(X),\ R_2(X),\ W_2(X),\ R_1(Y),\ W_1(Y),\ W_2(Y),\ C_1,\ C_2
$$

#### Model Solution to (a)

Let `X = 100` initially.

**1. Lost Update Problem:**

| Step | TŌéü | TŌéé | DB(X) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `RŌéü(X)` reads 100 | ŌĆö | 100 |
| 2 | ŌĆö | `RŌéé(X)` reads 100 | 100 |
| 3 | `WŌéü(X)`: 100 + 50 = 150 | ŌĆö | 150 |
| 4 | ŌĆö | `WŌéé(X)`: 100 + 100 = 200 | 200 |

TŌéü's update of +50 is overwritten by TŌéé. The lost update occurs because both transactions read the *stale* value 100.

**2. Dirty Read Problem:**

| Step | TŌéü | TŌéé | DB(X) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `RŌéü(X)` reads 100 | ŌĆö | 100 |
| 2 | `WŌéü(X)`: 100 + 50 = 150 | ŌĆö | 150 (uncommitted) |
| 3 | ŌĆö | `RŌéé(X)` reads 150 | 150 |
| 4 | TŌéü aborts, rollback | ŌĆö | 100 |

TŌéé has read the *uncommitted* value 150 (which never existed in the committed database). This is a **dirty read**.

**3. Unrepeatable Read Problem:**

| Step | TŌéü | TŌéé | DB(X) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `RŌéü(X)` reads 100 | ŌĆö | 100 |
| 2 | ŌĆö | `WŌéé(X)`: 100 + 50 = 150; commits | 150 |
| 3 | `RŌéü(X)` reads 150 | ŌĆö | 150 |

TŌéü reads `X` twice and gets two different values. This violates the *repeatable read* guarantee expected under full isolation.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify all conflicting pairs:**

| Conflict Pair | Type | Order in S | Edge |
|---|---|---|---|
| `RŌéü(X)` and `WŌéé(X)` | RW | TŌéü before TŌéé | TŌéü ŌåÆ TŌéé |
| `RŌéü(Y)` and `WŌéé(Y)` | RW | TŌéü before TŌéé | TŌéü ŌåÆ TŌéé |
| `WŌéé(X)` and `WŌéü(Y)` | different items, no conflict | ŌĆö | ŌĆö |
| `WŌéü(Y)` and `WŌéé(Y)` | WW | TŌéü before TŌéé | TŌéü ŌåÆ TŌéé |
| `RŌéé(X)` and `WŌéü(Y)` | different items, no conflict | ŌĆö | ŌĆö |

**Step 2 ŌĆö Construct the precedence graph:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1((T1)) --> T2((T2))
```

**Step 3 ŌĆö Cycle detection:** The graph is **acyclic** (no back-edge).

**Step 4 ŌĆö Conclusion:** The schedule `S` is **conflict-serializable** Ō£ģ.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Equivalent serial schedule:** From the topological order `TŌéü ŌåÆ TŌéé`, the equivalent serial schedule is:

$$
S_{serial} = T_1,\ T_2
$$

That is, executing all operations of TŌéü first, followed by all operations of TŌéé, will produce the same final database state as `S`.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 4 Transaction Model:**
