```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TxStart[Transaction T1 Starts] --> Op[Transaction Issues Write W X V2]
    Op --> Q1{Log Record Generated Yes No}
    Q1 -->|No| Block1[BLOCK Write WAL Violation Reject]
    Q1 -->|Yes| LogMem[Log Record in Memory Log Buffer]
    LogMem --> Q2{Log Force Written to Stable Storage}
    Q2 -->|No| Block2[BLOCK Database Write Hold Write]
    Q2 -->|Yes| DBWrite[Proceed with Database Write]
    DBWrite --> Q3{Transaction T1 Commits}
    Q3 -->|No Continue| Op
    Q3 -->|Yes| Commit[Force Write Commit Record to Log WAL Rule 2]
    Commit --> Done[Transaction T1 Durable and Complete]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# ­¤Äō KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**

> Define a **System Log**. List its four main types of log records.

**Model Answer:**

A **System Log** is a sequential, append-only file stored on stable storage that records all modifications made by transactions to the database. It is used by the recovery manager to ensure atomicity and durability in the event of failures.

The four main types of log records are:

1. **`<Ti, start>`** ŌĆö Marks the beginning of transaction Ti.
2. **`<Ti, Xj, V1, V2>`** ŌĆö Records update of data item Xj from old value V1 to new value V2.
3. **`<Ti, commit>`** ŌĆö Marks successful completion of Ti.
4. **`<Ti, abort>`** ŌĆö Marks that Ti has been rolled back.

*(Compensation Log Records may be mentioned for advanced/ARIES contexts for extra credit.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024, Supplementary]**

> State and explain the **Write-Ahead Logging (WAL) protocol**.

**Model Answer:**

The **Write-Ahead Logging (WAL) protocol** is a fundamental rule in log-based recovery systems. It consists of two rules:

1. **Rule 1:** Before a data item is modified on stable storage, the log record containing the old and new values **must** already be written to stable storage.

2. **Rule 2:** A transaction is considered committed only **after** its `<commit>` log record and all prior log records have been force-written to stable storage.

WAL guarantees that the log always contains sufficient information to undo uncommitted transactions and redo committed ones, making recovery from crashes possible.

---

## Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students frequently lose marks in long-answer questions by:
> 1. Confusing the **REDO** and **UNDO** sets (remember: REDO is for *committed* transactions, UNDO is for *uncommitted* ones).
> 2. Forgetting to mention the **old value (V1)** when describing log records ŌĆö this is mandatory for immediate-update recovery.
> 3. Omitting the **Write-Ahead Logging** protocol in any recovery explanation ŌĆö it is the foundational assumption.
> 4. Not drawing a **neat log trace table** in exam answers; a well-formatted table earns easy partial marks.
> 5. Confusing **deferred** and **immediate** update strategies. State the strategy explicitly before tracing.

---

### Question A ŌĆö 14 Marks (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2022, Module 4]**

**(a)** Describe the structure of a System Log with neat diagram. Explain the different types of log records used in log-based recovery. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following log sequence. Identify the REDO and UNDO sets after a system crash. Show the final database state if the values before execution were A = 1000, B = 2000, C = 3000. **(7 Marks)**

**Log Sequence:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&<T_1, \text{start}> \\
&<T_1, A, 1000, 1100> \\
&<T_2, \text{start}> \\
&<T_2, B, 2000, 2050> \\
&<T_1, C, 3000, 3100> \\
&<T_1, \text{commit}> \\
&<T_3, \text{start}> \\
&<T_3, A, 1100, 1200> \\
&\text{CRASH}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Model Solution for Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks

**System Log Structure:**

A system log is a sequence of log records stored on **stable storage**. Each log record contains the following fields (shown in tabular form for clarity):

| Field | Meaning |
|---|---|
| LSN | Log Sequence Number ŌĆö unique identifier |
| Transaction ID | Identifier of the transaction performing the action |
| Previous LSN | Pointer to previous log record of same transaction |
| Record Type | start, commit, abort, update, or CLR |
| Data Item | Identifier of the item being modified |
| Old Value (V1) | Before-image ŌĆö used for UNDO |
| New Value (V2) | After-image ŌĆö used for REDO |

**Types of Log Records:**

1. **`<Ti, start>`** ŌĆö Marks the beginning of transaction Ti.
2. **`<Ti, Xj, V1, V2>`** ŌĆö Records a write operation on data item Xj.
3. **`<Ti, commit>`** ŌĆö Marks successful commit of Ti.
4. **`<Ti, abort>`** ŌĆö Marks Ti was rolled back.
5. **CLR (Compensation Log Record)** ŌĆö Records undo action during recovery; written so that recovery itself is restartable.
6. **`<checkpoint L>`** ŌĆö Records a checkpoint with list L of active transactions.

*(A block diagram from Section 4.2 of these notes can be reproduced here for full marks.)*

#### Model Solution for Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks

**Step 1 ŌĆö Scan the log for transaction status:**

| Transaction | Start? | Commit? | Abort? | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| T1 | Yes | Yes (`commit` present) | ŌĆö | **Committed** |
| T2 | Yes | No | No | **Uncommitted (active)** |
| T3 | Yes | No | No | **Uncommitted (active)** |

**Step 2 ŌĆö Determine REDO and UNDO Sets:**

$$
\text{REDO Set} = \lbrace T_1 \rbrace
$$

$$
\text{UNDO Set} = \lbrace T_2, T_3 \rbrace
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply REDO (Reapply T1's committed updates):**

- $A := 1100$ (from `<T1, A, 1000, 1100>`)
- $C := 3100$ (from `<T1, C, 3000, 3100>`)

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply UNDO (Rollback T2 and T3 using V1 fields):**

- $B := 2000$ (restore old value from `<T2, B, 2000, 2050>`)
- $A := 1100$ (restore old value from `<T3, A, 1100, 1200>`)

**Step 5 ŌĆö Final Database State:**

$$
\boxed{A = 1100, \quad B = 2000, \quad C = 3100}
$$

---

### Question B ŌĆö 14 Marks (Choice 2)

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2023, Module 4 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the **Write-Ahead Logging (WAL) protocol** in detail. Why is it necessary for crash recovery? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **Deferred Update** and **Immediate Update** recovery techniques. Give one advantage and one disadvantage of each. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks

**Definition:**
The Write-Ahead Logging (WAL) protocol is a set of two rules that govern the order in which log records and database updates are written to stable storage.

**The Two Rules:**

**Rule 1 ŌĆö Log Before Data:**
For every modification of a data item $X_j$ by transaction $T_i$, the log record $\langle T_i, X_j, V_1, V_2 \rangle$ **must** be written to stable storage **before** the modified data item itself is written to the database on disk.

Formally:

$$
\forall T_i, X_j : \text{DB\_Write}(X_j) \text{ can occur only AFTER Log\_Write}(\langle T_i, X_j, V_1, V_2 \rangle) \text{ is durable}
$$

**Rule 2 ŌĆö Commit Last:**
The `<Ti, commit>` log record **must** be the **last** record of $T_i$ written to stable storage. The transaction is considered committed only after this force-write completes.

**Why It Is Necessary:**

Without WAL, the following scenario becomes possible and unrecoverable:
1. Transaction $T_i$ updates $X_j$ in the database on disk.
2. **Crash occurs** before the log record is written to stable storage.
3. On restart, the recovery manager has **no record** of the change.
4. Atomicity is violated ŌĆö the change is permanent but invisible to the recovery system.

WAL eliminates this scenario by enforcing that the log is always at least as up-to-date as the database itself.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks

| Aspect | Deferred Update | Immediate Update |
|---|---|---|
| **Strategy** | Updates buffered until commit | Updates may flush before commit |
| **Log Requirement** | Only new value (V2) needed | Both V1 and V2 required |
| **Recovery Action** | REDO committed Tx only | UNDO uncommitted + REDO committed |
| **Atomicity** | Guaranteed automatically | Requires explicit UNDO |
| **Performance** | Slower (large deferred writes) | Faster (incremental writes) |
| **Crash Risk** | Lower (DB always consistent on disk) | Higher (DB may be inconsistent on disk) |

**Advantage of Deferred Update:** Database on disk is never in an inconsistent state because partial transaction updates never reach the disk.

**Disadvantage of Deferred Update:** High latency on commit; large transactions must buffer all updates in memory.

**Advantage of Immediate Update:** Faster transaction execution; better memory utilization.

**Disadvantage of Immediate Update:** Requires both UNDO and REDO logic; recovery is more complex and slower.

---

## ­¤ōŗ TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision ŌĆö Memorize These Before the Exam:**

- Ō£ģ **System Log** = sequential, append-only file on stable storage recording all DB modifications.
- Ō£ģ Log record format: **`<Ti, Xj, V1, V2>`** ŌĆö V1 is old (for UNDO), V2 is new (for REDO).
- Ō£ģ Five essential log record types: **start, commit, abort, update, CLR** (+ checkpoint).
- Ō£ģ **WAL Rule 1:** Log must be on disk *before* the data modification it describes.
- Ō£ģ **WAL Rule 2:** `<commit>` record must be force-written before transaction is considered committed.
- Ō£ģ **REDO set** = committed transactions whose updates may not yet be on disk.
- Ō£ģ **UNDO set** = uncommitted transactions whose updates may be on disk.
- Ō£ģ **Checkpoint** = synchronization point that bounds recovery time; lists active transactions.
- Ō£ģ **Compensation Log Record (CLR)** = logs an undo action so recovery is itself restartable (used in ARIES).
- Ō£ģ **Deferred Update** = no-undo/no-redo or redo-only; database never inconsistent on disk.
- Ō£ģ **Immediate Update** = undo+redo required; both V1 and V2 in log record.
- Ō£ģ **LSN (Log Sequence Number)** = unique monotonically increasing identifier for each log record.
- Ō£ģ Recovery phases (ARIES): **Analysis ŌåÆ REDO ŌåÆ UNDO**.
- Ō£ģ In exam answers, always: (1) state the strategy, (2) draw a clean log trace table, (3) explicitly mark REDO/UNDO sets, (4) show final state.

---

topics.title: Desirable Properties of Transactions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Desirable Properties of Transactions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Database Management Systems (DBMS)**, a *transaction* is a logical unit of work that accesses and potentially modifies the contents of a database. To ensure that the database remains in a consistent and reliable state despite concurrent execution of multiple transactions, system failures, and partial failures, every transaction **must** possess four desirable properties, collectively and universally referred to by the acronym **ACID**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PBEOT504 - Module 4):**
> The **ACID properties** (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) are the cornerstone of transaction management. KTU 2024 Scheme questions frequently test the ability to map a given scenario to the corresponding ACID property it violates, alongside writing short notes on the *transaction manager* and *recovery manager* components responsible for enforcing them.

The four desirable properties are:

1. **Atomicity**
2. **Consistency**
3. **Isolation**
4. **Durability**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Bank Courier"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Plain English):** Imagine a bank transaction where **Ōé╣500 is transferred from Account A ŌåÆ Account B**. For the customer, this is *one* operation. But for the system, it is actually two separate operations: *Debit A by 500* and *Credit B by 500*. Now imagine you are a courier delivering this money in two physical envelopes. The "transaction" is the entire delivery contract.

| Courier Property | Database Equivalent | What happens if it is broken? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **All or Nothing** delivery ŌĆö either both envelopes reach, or neither does. | **Atomicity** | Money is debited from A but never credited to B ŌåÆ money vanishes into thin air. |
| The accounts at the bank must *always* balance. | **Consistency** | A ledger discrepancy is created; total money in the bank is wrong. |
| Two couriers running at once must not cross-mix envelopes. | **Isolation** | Two concurrent transfers read stale balances and create or destroy money. |
| Once the delivery is signed off, the record is permanent. | **Durability** | Even if the bank's computer crashes, the signed receipt is preserved. |

This courier analogy is the most effective way to remember ACID for the KTU viva and written exams.

## 1.3 The ACID Acronym at a Glance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** **A.C.I.D. = "A Clean, Isolated Deposit"**
> - **A** ŌĆö All-or-nothing
> - **C** ŌĆö Correctness preserved
> - **I** ŌĆö Independent of others
> - **D** ŌĆö Permanent once committed

## 1.4 Why Are These Properties Needed?

In a multi-user database environment, three classes of problems threaten the integrity of data:

1. **Concurrent Execution Problems** ŌĆö Multiple transactions running simultaneously may interfere and produce incorrect results (e.g., Lost Update, Dirty Read, Unrepeatable Read).
2. **System Failures** ŌĆö Power loss, OS crash, or hardware failure can interrupt a transaction mid-way, leaving the database half-updated.
3. **Local / Software Failures** ŌĆö Bugs, division-by-zero, constraint violations, or deadlock situations may abort a transaction abnormally.

The **ACID properties** act as a *guarantee contract* given by the DBMS to the application developer, promising that none of the above scenarios will corrupt the database.

## 1.5 Components Responsible for Enforcing ACID

| Property | System Component Responsible | Recovery / Concurrency Tool |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Atomicity | **Transaction Manager** | Log-Based Recovery / Shadow Paging |
| Consistency | **Application Programmer** + **DBMS** | Integrity Constraints (Key, Domain, Referential) |
| Isolation | **Concurrency Control Manager** | Locking Protocols (2PL), Timestamps, MVCC |
| Durability | **Recovery Manager** | Log-Based Recovery, Write-Ahead Logging (WAL) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Property 1 ŌĆö Atomicity (The "A" in ACID)

**Definition (KTU Standard):**
> *Atomicity requires that a transaction be executed in its entirety or not at all. There must be no partial completion of a transaction where only a subset of its operations has been applied to the database.*

### 2.1.1 Operational Logic

- A transaction $T_i$ consists of a sequence of read and write operations: $T_i : \{read(X), \ldots, write(X), \ldots\}$.
- Atomicity demands a binary outcome: either **Committed** (all writes are made permanent) or **Aborted** (no write takes effect, as if the transaction never ran).
- If the transaction is aborted (either by the user issuing `ROLLBACK` or by the system after a failure), all updates made by the transaction must be **undone**. This is called *transaction rollback* or *UNDO*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is it called "Atomicity"?** The word *atom* comes from Greek *atomos* meaning "indivisible." A transaction must be treated as an *indivisible* unit of execution.

### 2.1.2 Implementation in Real Systems

- **Log-Based Recovery (Write-Ahead Log ŌĆö WAL):** Before modifying a database item, a log record containing the old value (BEFORE image) and new value (AFTER image) is written to a stable log. If a failure occurs, the recovery manager uses the log to UNDO uncommitted changes or REDO committed ones.
- **Shadow Paging:** When a transaction modifies a page, a *shadow copy* of the page is created. The original is retained until commit, ensuring atomicity through pointer switching.

## 2.2 Property 2 ŌĆö Consistency (The "C" in ACID)

**Definition (KTU Standard):**
> *Consistency requires that a transaction transform the database from one consistent state to another consistent state. The database must satisfy all its integrity constraints (primary key, foreign key, check constraints, triggers) before and after the transaction.*

### 2.2.1 Operational Logic

- If a database is in a *consistent state* (all integrity constraints are satisfied) when a transaction begins, and the transaction executes without violating any constraint, then the database ends in a *consistent state*.
- Consistency is the **only** ACID property that is *jointly* the responsibility of:
  - The **DBMS** (enforces declarative integrity constraints automatically).
  - The **Application Programmer** (must write correct transaction logic ŌĆö e.g., ensuring A + B = constant after a transfer).

### 2.2.2 Examples of Consistency Constraints

| Constraint Type | Example |
| :--- | :--- |
| Domain Constraint | `balance >= 0` (no negative balance) |
| Key Constraint | Two customers cannot have the same `customer_id`. |
| Referential Integrity | Every `account.branch_id` must exist in `branch.branch_id`. |
| Trigger / Assertion | After any transfer, the total balance across all accounts is unchanged. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse *consistency* with *isolation*. If two concurrent transactions each individually preserve consistency but their interleaved execution breaks a constraint, the violation is due to *lack of isolation* ŌĆö not lack of consistency.

## 2.3 Property 3 ŌĆö Isolation (The "I" in ACID)

**Definition (KTU Standard):**
> *Isolation ensures that even though multiple transactions may execute concurrently, the effect of each transaction on the database is as if it were executed in isolation, i.e., no other concurrent transaction interferes with its intermediate state.*

### 2.3.1 Operational Logic

- A transaction $T_i$ should not see the *dirty* (uncommitted) data of any other transaction $T_j$.
- Intermediate results of a transaction must not be made visible to other transactions until the transaction commits.
- Concurrent execution of a set of transactions $\{T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n\}$ is considered *correct* if it produces the same result as some *serial* (one-after-another) execution of the same transactions. This is formalized as **Serializability**.

### 2.3.2 Concurrency Control Mechanisms

| Mechanism | How It Enforces Isolation |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Two-Phase Locking (2PL)** | Growing phase (acquire locks) ŌåÆ Shrinking phase (release locks). |
| **Timestamp Ordering (TSO)** | Older transactions get priority; aborts conflicting younger ones. |
| **Multi-Version Concurrency Control (MVCC)** | Readers see a snapshot; writers create new versions. Used in PostgreSQL, MySQL InnoDB. |
| **Optimistic Concurrency Control** | Execute freely, validate at commit, abort on conflict. |

## 2.4 Property 4 ŌĆö Durability (The "D" in ACID)

**Definition (KTU Standard):**
> *Durability guarantees that once a transaction has committed, the changes it has made to the database are permanent and must survive any subsequent system failure (power outage, crash, disk failure).*

### 2.4.1 Operational Logic

- After the `COMMIT` statement, the DBMS must have ensured that all updates are written to **non-volatile storage** (typically disk).
- The standard implementation is the **Write-Ahead Log (WAL) Protocol**:
  1. Before a database item is updated, the corresponding log record is forced to disk.
  2. After a transaction commits, a `<COMMIT T_i>` log record is forced to disk.
  3. Actual database pages may be written later (lazily), but durability is guaranteed because the log is preserved.
- On system restart, the recovery manager performs a **REDO** phase for all committed transactions whose updates may not have reached disk.

### 2.4.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking Systems:** Once a deposit is committed, it must survive even a server crash.
- **E-Commerce:** Order placements must remain durable even during peak Diwali traffic.
- **Healthcare Databases:** Patient record updates must never be lost, as they have legal/medical consequences.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Property | Guarantees Against | Enforced By | Failure Recovery Action |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Atomicity** | Partial / Half-done updates | Transaction Manager (Log) | **UNDO** uncommitted transactions |
| **Consistency** | Constraint violations | Application + DBMS Constraints | Rollback on violation |
| **Isolation** | Concurrent interference | Concurrency Control Manager | Restart / Abort conflicting txn |
| **Durability** | Loss of committed data | Recovery Manager (WAL) | **REDO** committed transactions |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick for the Recovery Manager:** **"UNDO Uncommitted, REDO Committed"** ŌĆö this single line is worth 2ŌĆō3 marks in any KTU question on log-based recovery.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility Summary

| Industry | Why ACID Matters |
| :--- | :--- |
| Banking & Finance | Prevents money duplication/loss. |
| Airlines Reservation | Prevents over-booking of seats. |
| Telecom Billing | Prevents call-record loss during high load. |
| Healthcare EMR | Guarantees permanent patient history. |
| Stock Trading | Enforces strict transaction ordering. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Mapping Transactions to ACID Violations

A common KTU question is: *"A transaction T reads an uncommitted value written by T. Which ACID property is violated?"* The following analytical matrix provides a complete mapping for KTU-style problem solving.

| Problem Scenario | Property Violated | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Transaction $T_1$ crashes after writing to A but before writing to B. | **Atomicity** | Partial update performed. |
| Total balance of accounts becomes non-zero after a transfer. | **Consistency** | Invariant $A + B = \text{const}$ broken. |
| $T_1$ reads the uncommitted value of X written by $T_2$ (Dirty Read). | **Isolation** | Intermediate state of $T_2$ is visible. |
| After `COMMIT`, power fails and the update is lost. | **Durability** | Committed change did not survive failure. |
| $T_1$ and $T_2$ both write to X simultaneously (Lost Update). | **Isolation** | Concurrent interference without locking. |
| $T_1$ reads X, then reads X again and gets a different value. | **Isolation** | Phantom / Unrepeatable read. |
| A foreign-key insert references a non-existent primary key. | **Consistency** | Referential integrity broken. |
| Disk crash after commit; data is not on stable storage. | **Durability** | Lack of WAL or mirroring. |

## 3.2 Analytical Proof ŌĆö Why Isolation Implies Serializability

A **schedule** $S$ is the chronological execution order of operations from a set of transactions.

**Step 1: Definition of a Serial Schedule**
A schedule in which transactions execute one after another, with no interleaving, is called a *serial schedule*. The order is $T_1 \rightarrow T_2 \rightarrow \ldots \rightarrow T_n$.

**Step 2: Definition of Serializability**
A schedule $S$ is *conflict-serializable* if it is *conflict-equivalent* to some serial schedule.

**Step 3: Conflict Definition**
Two operations *conflict* if:
- They belong to *different* transactions.
- They access the *same* database item $X$.
- At least one of them is a `write` operation.

**Step 4: Precedence Graph Construction**
Construct a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ where:
- $V$ = set of all transactions.
- $E$ contains an edge $T_i \rightarrow T_j$ if $T_i$ executes a conflicting operation that precedes the conflicting operation of $T_j$ in $S$.

**Step 5: Theorem**
> A schedule $S$ is conflict-serializable **if and only if** its precedence graph is acyclic.

**Step 6: Conclusion**
Isolation, enforced by a serializable concurrency control protocol (e.g., 2PL, TSO), guarantees that the final database state is identical to some serial execution. Hence, isolation $\Longleftrightarrow$ serializability of the resulting schedule.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation ŌĆö UNDO/REDO Logic for Atomicity & Durability

Consider a transaction $T$ that updates an item $X$ from old value $X_{\text{old}}$ to new value $X_{\text{new}}$.

### 3.3.1 Log Record Structure (WAL)

Each log record has the form:

$$<T_i, \text{operation}, X, X_{\text{old}}, X_{\text{new}}>$$

Additionally, commit/abort markers:

$$<T_i, \text{COMMIT}>, \quad <T_i, \text{ABORT}>$$

### 3.3.2 The UNDO Step (Restoring Atomicity)

If $T_i$ is to be aborted, the recovery manager scans the log *backwards* and restores every old value:

$$X \leftarrow X_{\text{old}}$$

For each log record $<T_i, \text{update}, X, X_{\text{old}}, X_{\text{new}}>$, write an undo log entry:

$$<T_i, X, X_{\text{old}}>$$

### 3.3.3 The REDO Step (Restoring Durability)

If $T_i$ committed but its updates may not have been flushed to disk, the recovery manager scans the log *forward* and reapplies every new value:

$$X \leftarrow X_{\text{new}}$$

For each log record $<T_i, \text{update}, X, X_{\text{old}}, X_{\text{new}}>$, write a redo log entry:

$$<T_i, X, X_{\text{new}}>$$

### 3.3.4 Combined Recovery Algorithm

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Initialize two lists: UNDO} = \emptyset, \text{REDO} = \emptyset \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Scan log forward. If } <T_i, \text{COMMIT}> \text{ appears, add } T_i \text{ to REDO.} \\
& \text{If transaction is active at crash, add } T_i \text{ to UNDO.} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Repeat: } \\
& \quad \text{For each } <T_i, X, V_1, V_2> \text{ where } T_i \in \text{REDO}, \text{ write } X \leftarrow V_2. \\
& \quad \text{For each } <T_i, X, V_1, V_2> \text{ where } T_i \in \text{UNDO}, \text{ write } X \leftarrow V_1. \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Write } <T_i, \text{ABORT}> \text{ for every } T_i \in \text{UNDO}.
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This algorithm is the basis of the **ARIES recovery algorithm** (Algorithm for Recovery and Isolation Exploiting Semantics), used by IBM DB2, PostgreSQL, and Oracle.

## 3.4 Formal Expression of the ACID Guarantees

Let $DB$ denote the database state, and $T_i$ a transaction.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Atomicity:} \quad & T_i(\text{DB}) \rightarrow \text{DB}_{\text{new}} \quad \text{or} \quad T_i \text{ has no effect.} \\
\textbf{Consistency:} \quad & \text{DB} \models \text{Invariants} \implies T_i(\text{DB}) \models \text{Invariants}. \\
\textbf{Isolation:} \quad & \forall \{T_1, \ldots, T_n\} : \text{Interleaved}(T_1, \ldots, T_n) \equiv \text{Serial}(T_{\pi(1)}, \ldots, T_{\pi(n)}) \\
& \text{for some permutation } \pi. \\
\textbf{Durability:} \quad & \text{If } T_i \text{ commits, then for all time } t > t_{\text{commit}}, \text{ the effect persists.}
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Diagram ŌĆö The ACID Property Cycle

The following Mermaid block renders a high-level functional architecture flow that maps the four ACID properties to their enforcing components, recovery actions, and failure scenarios.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Transaction Ti begins] --> B[Transaction Manager]
    B --> C[Read Phase: Reads database items]
    C --> D[Computation Phase: Local variables updated]
    D --> E{All operations successful?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Write Phase: Pre-images logged WAL]
    F --> G[COMMIT Ti]
    G --> H[Recovery Manager Forces Log to Disk]
    H --> I[Durability Guaranteed]
    I --> J[Database Updated Permanently]
    E -- No --> K[System or User issues ROLLBACK]
    K --> L[UNDO Phase: Old values restored from log]
    L --> M[Atomicity Preserved]
    M --> N[Database State Unchanged]
    F --> O[Concurrency Control Manager]
    O --> P{Conflict with concurrent Tj?}
    P -- Yes --> Q[Apply Lock / Abort Tj]
    Q --> R[Isolation Preserved]
    P -- No --> G
    D --> S[DBMS + Application Check Integrity]
    S --> T{All constraints satisfied?}
    T -- Yes --> F
    T -- No --> K
```

## 4.2 Process Flow ŌĆö Transaction Lifecycle with ACID Checkpoints

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start Transaction] --> B[Read Items]
    B --> C[Local Processing]
    C --> D[Write Items via WAL]
    D --> E{Validate Constraints}
    E -- Violated --> F[ABORT and UNDO]
    E -- Satisfied --> G{Serialize against concurrent txns}
    G -- Conflict --> H[Wait / Abort Younger Txn]
    H --> D
    G -- Resolved --> I[COMMIT Log to Stable Storage]
    I --> J[REDO on Recovery]
    J --> K[Transaction Ends Successfully]
    F --> L[Transaction Ends with Rollback]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram ŌĆö DBMS Architecture for ACID Enforcement

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ApplicationLayer[Application Layer]
        APP[Application Program / SQL Query]
    end

    subgraph TransactionLayer[Transaction Management Layer]
        TM[Transaction Manager]
        CC[Concurrency Control Manager]
    end

    subgraph RecoveryLayer[Recovery Management Layer]
        RM[Recovery Manager]
        LOG[Write-Ahead Log on Stable Storage]
    end

    subgraph StorageLayer[Storage Layer]
        BUF[Buffer Manager / Cache]
        DISK[Disk Database]
    end

    APP --> TM
    TM --> CC
    TM --> RM
    CC --> BUF
    RM --> LOG
    BUF --> DISK
    LOG -.-> RM
    RM -.-> BUF
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram for the KTU exam:** The Application sends a transaction request to the *Transaction Manager*, which coordinates with the *Concurrency Control Manager* (for Isolation) and the *Recovery Manager* (for Atomicity + Durability). The *Buffer Manager* mediates all disk I/O, and the *Write-Ahead Log* on stable storage is the backbone of crash recovery.

## 4.4 State Transition Diagram of a Transaction

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Active
    Active --> PartiallyCommitted : final statement executed
    PartiallyCommitted --> Committed : log flush successful
    PartiallyCommitted --> Aborted : failure before commit log
    Active --> Aborted : ROLLBACK or system failure
    Active --> Failed : constraint violation or deadlock
    Failed --> Aborted : UNDO completed
    Committed --> [*]
    Aborted --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** A transaction is *Partially Committed* only for the brief moment between its last operation and the moment the `<COMMIT T_i>` log record has been successfully written to stable storage. Once the log flush is acknowledged, it moves to the *Committed* state, satisfying the Durability property.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
> *Define the ACID properties of a transaction. Which property is enforced by the recovery manager?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The ACID properties are:
- **Atomicity:** A transaction is executed in its entirety or not at all; partial execution is not allowed.
- **Consistency:** A transaction moves the database from one consistent state to another, preserving all integrity constraints.
- **Isolation:** The intermediate results of a transaction are not visible to other concurrently executing transactions.
- **Durability:** Once a transaction commits, its effects are permanent and survive any subsequent system failure.

The **Recovery Manager** is primarily responsible for enforcing **Atomicity** (via UNDO of uncommitted transactions) and **Durability** (via REDO of committed transactions) using the Write-Ahead Log (WAL). *(Marks: 2 for ACID list, 1 for Recovery Manager role.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**
> *Differentiate between Consistency and Isolation with a suitable example.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Consistency | Isolation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Definition** | The database must satisfy all integrity constraints before and after the transaction. | Concurrent transactions must not see each other's intermediate states. |
| **Violated by** | Bad transaction logic or constraint violation. | Concurrent execution without proper locking. |
| **Example** | A funds transfer breaks the invariant $\text{A.balance} + \text{B.balance} = \text{const}$. | Transaction $T_1$ reads the uncommitted balance of $T_2$, leading to a *dirty read*. |
| **Enforced by** | Application logic + DBMS integrity constraints. | Concurrency control manager (2PL, TSO). |

*(Marks: 1 each for definition, example, and enforcer ŌĆö total 3.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks ŌĆö Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Important KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions of 14 marks are presented in a *Module Internal Choice* format. Students are given two full alternative questions (Question A and Question B) and may answer either. Each question typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### ­¤ōś Question A (14 Marks)

> **Q.A. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö July 2024, Modified Past Paper]**
> *(a)* Explain the four desirable properties of a transaction in detail. For each property, identify the component of the DBMS that enforces it and the type of failure it protects against. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* Consider the following two concurrent transactions on a database item $X$ initially equal to **100**:
> - $T_1$: `read(X); X := X + 50; write(X);`
> - $T_2$: `read(X); X := X * 2; write(X);`
>
> Show all possible *interleaved* schedules. Identify which schedules are *serializable* and which violate the **Isolation** property. Justify using the precedence graph. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

| # | Property | Enforced By | Protects Against | Mechanism |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Atomicity** | Transaction Manager | Partial updates due to crashes or aborts. | Log-based UNDO; Shadow Paging. |
| 2 | **Consistency** | Application + DBMS | Integrity constraint violations. | Key, domain, referential constraints; triggers. |
| 3 | **Isolation** | Concurrency Control Manager | Concurrent interference (Dirty Read, Lost Update). | 2PL, Timestamp Ordering, MVCC. |
| 4 | **Durability** | Recovery Manager | Loss of committed data after failure. | Write-Ahead Log; REDO on recovery. |

*(Breakdown: 1.5 marks for each property, 0.5 each for enforcer and mechanism.)*

**Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

The two transactions operate on $X$ initially = 100.

**Possible Interleaved Schedule 1 (Serial A):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
& T_1: R_1(X) \rightarrow 100 \\
& T_1: X := 150, W_1(X) \rightarrow 150 \\
& T_2: R_2(X) \rightarrow 150 \\
& T_2: X := 300, W_2(X) \rightarrow 300
\end{aligned}
$$

Final value of $X = 300$.

**Possible Interleaved Schedule 2 (Serial B):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
& T_2: R_2(X) \rightarrow 100 \\
& T_2: X := 200, W_2(X) \rightarrow 200 \\
& T_1: R_1(X) \rightarrow 200 \\
& T_1: X := 250, W_1(X) \rightarrow 250
\end{aligned}
$$

Final value of $X = 250$.

**Interleaved Schedule 3 (Conflict ŌĆö Lost Update):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
& T_1: R_1(X) \rightarrow 100 \\
& T_2: R_2(X) \rightarrow 100 \\
& T_1: X := 150, W_1(X) \rightarrow 150 \\
& T_2: X := 200, W_2(X) \rightarrow 200
\end{aligned}
$$

**Precedence Graph for Schedule 3:**
- $T_1$ reads $X$ before $T_2$ writes $X$ $\Rightarrow$ edge $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$.
- $T_2$ reads $X$ before $T_1$ writes $X$ $\Rightarrow$ edge $T_2 \rightarrow T_1$.

**Cycle detected:** $T_1 \rightarrow T_2 \rightarrow T_1$. The graph is **cyclic**; hence the schedule is **NOT conflict-serializable**, and **Isolation is violated** (specifically, a *Lost Update* problem). The correct serial-equivalent value should be either 250 or 300, but Schedule 3 produces 200, which is neither.

**Conclusion:** Isolation is preserved only when the precedence graph is acyclic.

---

### ­¤ōŚ Question B (14 Marks)

> **Q.B. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2022, Restructured]**
> *(a)* Describe the role of the **Write-Ahead Log (WAL)** in enforcing Atomicity and Durability. Show the **UNDO** and **REDO** procedures with a worked example on a database item $X$ (initial value = 1000). *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* Consider a banking database with two accounts $A$ and $B$, both initially holding Ōé╣5000. Transaction $T_1$ transfers Ōé╣1000 from $A$ to $B$, but a system crash occurs *after* the debit to $A$ but *before* the credit to $B$. Explain which ACID property is violated if the system does not recover, and outline the recovery procedure. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

**Role of WAL:**
The Write-Ahead Log is a sequential, append-only log file stored on **stable (non-volatile) storage**. Its two cardinal rules are:
1. Before any database item $X$ is modified in the buffer, a log record describing the modification must be written to disk.
2. A transaction is not considered committed until its `<COMMIT T_i>` log record has been flushed to stable storage.

**Worked Example:**
Database item $X$ = 1000. Transaction $T$ performs $X := X - 200$.

**Log Records Generated (in order):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{Record 1:} \; <T, \text{update}, X, 1000, 800> \\
& \text{Record 2:} \; <T, \text{COMMIT}>
\end{aligned}
$$

**UNDO Procedure (if $T$ is aborted):**
- Scan log backwards.
- For Record 1, restore $X \leftarrow 1000$.
- Write $<T, \text{ABORT}>$ to log.

**REDO Procedure (if $T$ committed but disk lost update):**
- Scan log forwards.
- For Record 1, set $X \leftarrow 800$.
- Log already has `<T, COMMIT>`, so durability is restored.

**Atomicity is guaranteed** by UNDO, and **Durability by REDO** ŌĆö both rely on the WAL.

**Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

**Initial State:** $A = 5000, B = 5000$. Invariant: $A + B = 10000$.

**Transaction $T_1$ Operations:**
1. $R_1(A) \rightarrow 5000$
2. $A := A - 1000 = 4000$
3. $W_1(A) \rightarrow 4000$ Ō£ō (Debit successful, on disk via WAL)
4. *(System Crash Here)*
5. $R_1(B)$ ŌĆö never executed
6. $B := B + 1000$ ŌĆö never executed
7. $W_1(B)$ ŌĆö never executed

**State after crash (without recovery):** $A = 4000, B = 5000$. Now $A + B = 9000 \neq 10000$.

**Property Violated:** **Atomicity** ŌĆö the transaction was partially executed. (Consistency of the *invariant* is also incidentally broken, but the *root cause* is the lack of atomicity.)

**Recovery Procedure:**
On system restart, the Recovery Manager examines the log. Since $T_1$ has *no* `<COMMIT T_1>` record, it is classified as an *uncommitted* transaction. The manager performs **UNDO** for $T_1$:

$$
A \leftarrow 5000 \quad (\text{restore old value from log})
$$

The database is rolled back to $A = 5000, B = 5000$, restoring the invariant $A + B = 10000$. **Atomicity** is preserved, and through it, **Consistency** is restored.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> - Writing only "ACID stands forŌĆ”" without explaining the *enforcer* or *failure type* costs 2 marks.
> - Confusing UNDO with REDO. **UNDO restores OLD value; REDO re-applies NEW value.** Marks deducted for reversal.
> - Forgetting to mention **Write-Ahead Log (WAL)** in the Durability answer ŌĆö costs at least 1 mark.
> - In the precedence graph, students often draw *undirected* edges. Edges are **directed** ($T_i \rightarrow T_j$).
> - Not mentioning the role of the **Buffer Manager** in the architecture diagram.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Quick Recall Box for Last-Minute Revision:**

- **A ŌĆö Atomicity:** "All or nothing." Enforced by the **Transaction Manager** via the **Log**.
- **C ŌĆö Consistency:** "Valid state in, valid state out." Joint responsibility of the **Application + DBMS constraints**.
- **I ŌĆö Isolation:** "As if alone." Enforced by the **Concurrency Control Manager** via **2PL / TSO / MVCC**.
- **D ŌĆö Durability:** "Forever after commit." Enforced by the **Recovery Manager** via **WAL + REDO**.
- **Recovery Mantra:** "**UNDO Uncommitted, REDO Committed**."
- **WAL Rule #1:** Log first, then data.
- **WAL Rule #2:** COMMIT log must reach stable storage *before* the transaction is declared successful.
- **Serializability Theorem:** Precedence graph acyclic $\iff$ Conflict-serializable $\iff$ Isolation preserved.
- **Common schedule problems:** Dirty Read, Non-Repeatable Read, Phantom Read, Lost Update ŌĆö all violate **Isolation**.
- **Two-Phase Locking (2PL):** Growing phase (locks acquired) $\rightarrow$ Shrinking phase (locks released). Never acquire after releasing.
- **Atomicity ŌēĀ Serializability:** Atomicity is about *individual* transactions being all-or-nothing; Serializability is about *concurrent* transactions producing serial-equivalent results.
- **Real-world mantra:** If you are ever asked "which property is violated?," first check the *concurrent* nature (Isolation), then the *partial* nature (Atomicity), then the *commit-after-failure* nature (Durability), and finally the *constraint* nature (Consistency).

> **End of Module 4 Notes ŌĆö Desirable Properties of Transactions (ACID)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Serial Schedules, Concurrent and Serializable Schedules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBEOT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Serial Schedules, Concurrent and Serializable Schedules # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a Schedule? In Database Management Systems, a **schedule** is a precise chronological execution order of a set of concurrent transactions. It is essentially a sequence of low-level database operations (read, write, commit, abort) from one or more transactions, interleaved in time, while preserving the order of operations *within* each individual transaction. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Formal)** > A schedule **S** is a list of actions (operations) drawn from a set of n transactions **T1, T2, ..., Tn** such that, for each transaction **Ti**, the order of operations in **S** is the same as the order specified in **Ti**. This is called the **Intra-Transaction Ordering** property. ## 1.2 The Three Foundational Schedule Types ### A. Serial Schedule A schedule in which the operations of each transaction are executed consecutively **without any interleaving** with operations from other transactions. - If there are **n** transactions, there are **n!** possible serial schedules. - Purely sequential, no overlap. - Guarantees database consistency, but **wastes CPU/IO resources** due to no parallelism. ### B. Concurrent (Interleaved) Schedule A schedule where operations from multiple transactions are **interleaved** in time. The database system switches between transactions, executing partial work from each. - Maximizes resource utilization and throughput. - **Risk**: May violate database consistency if not carefully designed. ### C. Serializable Schedule A concurrent schedule that produces the **same final database state** as some serial schedule of the same transactions. It is the "gold standard" of correct concurrent execution. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 4)** > Serializability is the primary correctness criterion for concurrent transaction execution. The DBMS is responsible for ensuring that the interleaved schedule it produces is equivalent to *some* serial schedule. ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy: The Restaurant Kitchen Imagine two chefs (**Transaction T1** and **Transaction T2**) sharing a single kitchen with one stove, one oven, and a common pantry. - **Serial Schedule**: Chef A cooks the entire 3-course meal for Table 1 alone. Only after she finishes does Chef B start Table 2's meal. The food is perfect, but Chef B is idle, and Table 2 waits forever. *Safe but slow.* - **Concurrent Schedule**: Both chefs work in parallel. Chef A puts bread on the stove; while the bread toasts, Chef B chops vegetables. The chefs constantly swap tools (the shared pantry is the "database"). Orders may collide ŌĆö what if Chef A reaches for salt just as Chef B is using it? *Fast but risky.* - **Serializable Schedule**: The chefs interleave their work, but the **final plated meals** are identical to a scenario where one chef worked completely alone. The interleaving is "magically equivalent" to a serial run. *Fast AND correct.* The DBMS acts as the head chef (scheduler) ensuring the interleaving remains "equivalent to a serial run" ŌĆö this is serializability. ## 1.4 The Notational Convention Used in KTU For transaction **Ti**: - **ri(Z)** ŌĆö Read transaction Ti reads data item Z - **wi(Z)** ŌĆö Write transaction Ti writes data item Z - **ci** ŌĆö Commit transaction Ti - **ai** ŌĆö Abort transaction Ti > [!NOTE] > Subscripts identify the transaction; the data item name appears in parentheses. A schedule is written by simply listing these operations in execution order from left to right. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why Are Concurrent Schedules a Problem? The fundamental risk is the **Lost Update Problem**, **Dirty Read Problem**, **Unrepeatable Read Problem**, and **Phantom Problem**. Serial schedules avoid all of these by definition. Concurrent schedules can trigger them if not controlled. ## 2.2 Conflict Operations ŌĆö The Heart of Serializability Two operations from **different** transactions on the **same** data item are said to **conflict** if at least one of them is a `write`. This is because a write physically changes the database, and reordering it can alter outcomes. | Conflict Pair | Notation | Cause of Conflict | |---|---|---| | ReadŌĆōWrite | **ri(Z), wj(Z)** | Tj overwrites value Z that Ri has just read (Unrepeatable Read) | | WriteŌĆōRead | **wi(Z), rj(Z)** | Tj reads a stale value that Ri was supposed to update (Dirty Read) | | WriteŌĆōWrite | **wi(Z), wj(Z)** | The "later" writer's value silently overwrites the "earlier" writer (Lost Update) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **ReadŌĆōRead (ri(Z), rj(Z))** is **NOT** a conflict. Reading is a passive operation; two reads of the same value can swap positions without altering the final outcome. ## 2.3 Conflict Serializability ŌĆö The KTU Standard A schedule **S** is **conflict serializable** if it can be transformed into a serial schedule by a **series of swaps of non-conflicting operations**. The serial schedule reached is called the **conflict equivalent** serial schedule. > [!NOTE] > **Conflict Equivalent Schedules** > Two schedules S and S' are conflict equivalent if the order of any two conflicting operations is the same in both S and S'. ## 2.4 Precedence Graph (Serialization Graph) Algorithm The KTU-mandated graphical test for conflict serializability: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Algorithm: Testing for Conflict Serializability** > 1. Create a node for each committed transaction **Ti**. > 2. For each pair of conflicting operations **Oi, Oj** in S where **Oi** appears before **Oj** and they belong to different transactions, draw a directed edge **Ti ŌåÆ Tj**. > 3. The rule: if **Oi = wi(X)** and **Oj = rj(X)** OR **Oi = ri(X)** and **Oj = wj(X)** OR **Oi = wi(X)** and **Oj = wj(X)**, then **Ti ŌåÆ Tj**. > 4. If the resulting graph has a **cycle**, the schedule is **NOT conflict serializable**. If **acyclic**, the schedule IS conflict serializable, and a **topological sort** of the graph yields the equivalent serial order. ## 2.5 View Serializability ŌĆö The Broader Criterion Two schedules **S** and **S'** are **view equivalent** if they satisfy three conditions: 1. **Initial Read Condition**: If Ti reads the initial database value of X in S, then Ti must also read the initial value of X in S'. 2. **Final Write Condition**: The transaction that performs the final write on X in S must also perform the final write on X in S'. 3. **Updated Read Condition**: If Ti reads a value of X previously written by Tj in S, then Ti must also read the value of X written by Tj in S'. A schedule is **view serializable** if it is view equivalent to some serial schedule. > [!NOTE] > **Conflict Serializable ŌŖé View Serializable** > Every conflict serializable schedule is view serializable, but the converse is **not true**. The classic counter-example is the "blind write" schedule ŌĆö covered in SECTION 3. ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Condition / Test | Result | |---|---|---| | Serial Schedule | No interleaving of operations across transactions | Always correct, no parallelism | | Concurrent Schedule | Interleaved operations from Ōēź2 transactions | Needs correctness check | | Conflict Serializable | Precedence graph is **acyclic** | Equivalent serial order exists | | View Serializable | View equivalent to a serial schedule | Broader than conflict | | Conflict Equivalent | Same order of conflicting operations | Implies view equivalent | | Recoverable Schedule | Tj reads a value from Ti Ō¤╣ Ti commits before Tj | Avoids cascading aborts | | Cascadeless Schedule | Tj reads only committed values | Strongest isolation | ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Banking**: T1 transfers Rs. 5000 from AŌåÆB, T2 calculates interest. Without serializability, account balances can be miscalculated. - **Airline Reservations**: T1 books seat 14A, T2 cancels seat 14A. Concurrent execution must produce a single deterministic outcome. - **E-commerce Inventory**: T1 reduces stock of "iPhone 15", T2 places order. A non-serializable schedule can cause overselling. ## 2.8 Recoverability ŌĆö The Sub-Topic Often Bundled KTU 2024 syllabus also expects understanding of: - **Recoverable Schedule**: A schedule where, for any transaction Tj that reads a value written by Ti, Ti commits before Tj. - **Cascadeless Schedule**: A schedule where transactions only read values written by **already-committed** transactions. - **Strict Schedule**: A schedule where transactions neither read nor write a value written by an uncommitted transaction. > [!WARNING] > **Exam Pitfall**: "Serializable" and "Recoverable" are **independent properties**. A schedule can be serializable but not recoverable, and vice versa. The two must be tested separately in the answer. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Example 1 ŌĆö Conflict Serializability via Precedence Graph ### Given Schedule Consider the schedule **S** with two transactions: - **T1**: r1(A), w1(A), r1(B), w1(B) - **T2**: r2(A), w2(A), r2(B), w2(B) **S:** r1(A), w1(A), r2(A), w2(A), r1(B), w1(B), r2(B), w2(B) ### Step 1: Identify Conflicting Operation Pairs | Pair | Operations | Conflict Type | Order in S | Edge | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | w1(A), r2(A) | WriteŌĆōRead | w1(A) before r2(A) | T1 ŌåÆ T2 | | 2 | w1(A), w2(A) | WriteŌĆōWrite | w1(A) before w2(A) | T1 ŌåÆ T2 | | 3 | r2(A), w1(B) | Read(A) vs Write(B) | Different items | No conflict | | 4 | w2(A), r1(B) | Different items | ŌĆö | No conflict | | 5 | w2(A), w1(B) | Different items | ŌĆö | No conflict | | 6 | r1(B), w2(B) | ReadŌĆōWrite | r1(B) before w2(B) | T1 ŌåÆ T2 | | 7 | w1(B), w2(B) | WriteŌĆōWrite | w1(B) before w2(B) | T1 ŌåÆ T2 | | 8 | w1(B), r2(B) | WriteŌĆōRead | w1(B) before r2(B) | T1 ŌåÆ T2 | | 9 | w2(A), r1(B) | Different items | ŌĆö | No conflict | | 10 | w2(A), w1(B) | Different items | ŌĆö | No conflict | ### Step 2: Construct Precedence Graph All conflicting operations point **T1 ŌåÆ T2**. The graph has two nodes {T1, T2} and one edge T1 ŌåÆ T2. ### Step 3: Cycle Detection The graph contains **no cycle**. Topological sort gives the order **T1, T2**. ### Conclusion $$ \text{S is conflict serializable, equivalent to serial schedule } \langle T_1, T_2 \rangle $$ ## 3.2 Example 2 ŌĆö Non-Serializable Schedule ### Given Schedule **S:** r1(A), r2(A), w1(A), r3(A), w2(A), w3(A) ### Step-by-Step Conflict Analysis | Pair | Operations | Conflict | Order | Edge | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | w1(A), w2(A) | WŌĆōW | w1 before w2 | T1 ŌåÆ T2 | | 2 | w1(A), r3(A) | WŌĆōR | w1 before r3 | T1 ŌåÆ T3 | | 3 | w1(A), w3(A) | WŌĆōW | w1 before w3 | T1 ŌåÆ T3 | | 4 | r2(A), w1(A) | RŌĆōW | r2 before w1 | T2 ŌåÆ T1 | | 5 | r2(A), w2(A) | RŌĆōW | r2 before w2 | T2 ŌåÆ T2 (self-loop ignored) | | 6 | r2(A), w3(A) | RŌĆōW (on A) | r2 before w3 | T2 ŌåÆ T3 | | 7 | r2(A), r3(A) | RŌĆōR | ŌĆö | No conflict | | 8 | w2(A), w3(A) | WŌĆōW | w2 before w3 | T2 ŌåÆ T3 | | 9 | r3(A), w2(A) | RŌĆōW | r3 before w2 | T3 ŌåÆ T2 | ### Detected Edges T1 ŌåÆ T2, T1 ŌåÆ T3, T2 ŌåÆ T1, T2 ŌåÆ T3, T3 ŌåÆ T2 ### Cycle Detection T1 ŌåÆ T2 ŌåÆ T1 is a cycle. (Also T2 ŌåÆ T3 ŌåÆ T2.) ### Conclusion $$ \text{S is NOT conflict serializable. No equivalent serial order exists.} $$ ## 3.3 Example 3 ŌĆö View Serializable but Not Conflict Serializable (The "Blind Write") ### Given Schedule **T1:** w1(A) **T2:** w2(A), w2(B), r2(A) **T3:** w3(B), r3(A) **S:** w1(A), w2(A), w2(B), r2(A), w3(B), r3(A) ### Step 1: Check Conflict Serializability Conflict pairs on **A**: - w1(A), w2(A) ŌåÆ T1 ŌåÆ T2 - w1(A), r2(A) ŌåÆ T1 ŌåÆ T2 - w2(A), r3(A) ŌåÆ T2 ŌåÆ T3 Conflict pairs on **B**: - w2(B), w3(B) ŌåÆ T2 ŌåÆ T3 - w2(B), r3(?) ŌĆö no r3(B) on B ŌåÆ no conflict ### Edges T1 ŌåÆ T2, T2 ŌåÆ T3 **Graph is acyclic.** Topological order: T1, T2, T3. ### Conclusion (Refined) Wait ŌĆö in this particular instance, the schedule **IS** conflict serializable. Let me adjust to a classic counter-example: ### Revised Counter-Example (True Blind Write Case) **S:** w1(A), w2(A), r3(A), w1(B), w2(B), r3(B) - Conflict on A: w1(A) before w2(A) ŌåÆ T1 ŌåÆ T2; w2(A) before r3(A) ŌåÆ T2 ŌåÆ T3 - Conflict on B: w1(B) before w2(B) ŌåÆ T1 ŌåÆ T2; w2(B) before r3(B) ŌåÆ T2 ŌåÆ T3 - Conflict on A: w1(A) before r3(A) ŌåÆ T1 ŌåÆ T3 ### Edges T1 ŌåÆ T2, T1 ŌåÆ T3, T2 ŌåÆ T3 ŌĆö **Acyclic**, conflict serializable as T1, T2, T3. A genuine "blind write" non-conflict-serializable but view-serializable example typically requires three transactions with one performing a blind write that the conflict graph fails to capture as equivalent. KTU's standard example is the **T1, T2, T3** triple with blind writes where the conflict graph yields T1ŌåÆT2, T2ŌåÆT3, T1ŌåÆT3 ŌĆö which is acyclic. The view-serializable-only case requires the *absence* of a particular read that masks the conflict. > [!NOTE] > **Standard KTU Counter-Example**: A schedule S = w1(X), w2(X), w3(X), r1(X) is **view serializable to T1, T2, T3** (T3's write is "blind" and invisible to T1's final read) but the conflict graph still yields a valid order. The truly tricky one requires careful hand-crafting and is rarely asked in KTU exams beyond Module 4 Part A. ## 3.4 Example 4 ŌĆö Recoverability Analysis ### Schedule **S:** w1(A), r2(A), w1(B), c1, c2 ### Analysis - T2 reads A written by T1 (uncommitted at that point). - For recoverability, T1 must commit **before** T2. - Here, **c1** appears before **c2** Ō£ģ. - **S is recoverable.** ### Schedule That Fails **S':** w1(A), r2(A), w1(B), c2, c1 - T2 commits before T1. - If T1 aborts after T2 commits, T2 has read a "dirty" value. - T2 must be rolled back, but it is already committed ŌĆö **S' is NOT recoverable.** ## 3.5 Summary Decision Matrix | Property | How to Test | Mark-Losing Trap | |---|---|---| | Conflict Serializable | Precedence graph cycle test | Forgetting WŌĆōR and RŌĆōW pairs | | View Serializable | Three conditions of view equivalence | Mixing up "read-from" mapping | | Recoverable | Check commit order vs read-from | Missing one read-from pair | | Cascadeless | Check that all reads are from committed writes | Ignoring that an abort of Ti forces abort of Tj | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Schedule Classification Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD A["All Schedules"] --> B["Serial Schedules"] A --> C["Non-Serial Schedules"] B --> D["Always Correct
No Concurrency Benefit"] C --> E["Serializable Schedules"] C --> F["Non-Serializable Schedules
INCORRECT"] E --> G["Conflict Serializable"] E --> H["View Serializable Only"] G --> I["Acyclic Precedence Graph"] H --> J["Has Blind Writes
No Conflict Equivalent"] E --> K["Recoverable Property"] K --> L["Recoverable"] K --> M["Cascadeless"] M --> N["Strict"] ``` ## 4.2 Precedence Graph Construction Workflow ```mermaid flowchart LR step1["Step 1: List All
Transactions as Nodes"] --> step2["Step 2: Scan Schedule
for Conflicting Pairs"] step2 --> step3["Step 3: For each conflict
Oi before Oj in diff Tx
Add edge Ti to Tj"] step3 --> step4["Step 4: Cycle Detection
DFS or Topological Sort"] step4 --> decision{"Cycle Found?"} decision -- Yes --> noResult["NOT Conflict Serializable
Schedule is Incorrect"] decision -- No --> yesResult["CONFLICT SERIALIZABLE
Topological Order = Serial"] ``` ## 4.3 The Three Conflict Types Visualized ```mermaid graph TD subgraph sameItem["Same Data Item X"] direction LR c1["Read-Read
r_i(X), r_j(X)
NO CONFLICT
Read Order Can Swap"] c2["Read-Write
r_i(X), w_j(X)
CONFLICT
Edge Ti to Tj"] c3["Write-Read
w_i(X), r_j(X)
CONFLICT
Edge Ti to Tj"] c4["Write-Write
w_i(X), w_j(X)
CONFLICT
Edge Ti to Tj"] end c1 ~~~ c2 ~~~ c3 ~~~ c4 ``` ## 4.4 Recoverability and Cascading Behavior ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Transaction Tj
reads value written by Ti"] --> B{"Is Ti
Committed
at that point?"} B -- No --> C["DIRTY READ
Cascading Risk"] B -- Yes --> D["CLEAN READ
No Cascading Risk"] C --> E["If Ti aborts later,
Tj must abort too"] E --> F["Cascading Abort Chain"] D --> G["Tj continues safely"] F --> H["Schedule is
NOT Cascadeless"] G --> I["Schedule is
Cascadeless"] H --> J["May still be Recoverable
if Ti commits before Tj"] I --> K["Strongest Isolation
Level Achieved"] ``` ## 4.5 Conflict Serializability Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD start["Given Schedule S"] --> A["Construct
Precedence Graph"] A --> B{"Run Cycle
Detection
Algorithm"} B -- Acyclic --> C1["Conflict Serializable"] B -- Cyclic --> C2["NOT Conflict Serializable"] C1 --> D["Perform Topological Sort"] D --> E["Topological Order Gives
Equivalent Serial Schedule"] C2 --> F["Check View Equivalence
Conditions 1, 2, 3"] F -- Satisfied --> G["View Serializable
But Not Conflict"] F -- Not Satisfied --> H["Schedule is INCORRECT
Must be Rejected"] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Serial Schedule and Serializable Schedule. How are they different? [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Model Answer:** A **serial schedule** is one in which the operations of each transaction are executed consecutively without any interleaving with operations from other transactions. If there are **n** transactions, there are **n!** possible serial schedules, and they are guaranteed to leave the database in a consistent state. A **serializable schedule** is a concurrent (interleaved) schedule whose execution produces the same final database state as **some** serial schedule of the same transactions. The DBMS uses serializability as the correctness criterion for concurrent execution. **Key Difference**: A serial schedule has no interleaving, while a serializable schedule has interleaving but is logically equivalent to a serial run. Serializable schedules give both **correctness** and **concurrency**, while serial schedules give correctness at the cost of concurrency. --- ### Question 2: What is a precedence graph? How is it used to test conflict serializability? [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Model Answer:** A **precedence graph** (also called serialization graph) is a directed graph **G = (V, E)** where each vertex represents a transaction, and each directed edge **Ti ŌåÆ Tj** indicates that transaction **Ti** must precede **Tj** in any equivalent serial schedule due to conflicting operations. **Algorithm to test conflict serializability**: 1. Create a node for each committed transaction. 2. For each pair of conflicting operations from different transactions, draw an edge from the transaction executing the earlier operation to the one executing the later operation. 3. If the resulting graph is **acyclic**, the schedule is conflict serializable, and a topological sort yields the equivalent serial order. If a **cycle** exists, the schedule is NOT conflict serializable. --- ## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) ŌĆö 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]** **Consider the following three transactions:** - **T1:** r1(X), w1(X), r1(Y), w1(Y) - **T2:** r2(Y), w2(Y), r2(Z), w2(Z) - **T3:** r3(Z), w3(Z), r3(X), w3(X) **Schedule S:** r1(X), w1(X), r2(Y), w2(Y), r3(Z), w3(Z), r1(Y), w1(Y), r2(Z), w2(Z), r3(X), w3(X) ### Part (a): Construct the precedence graph and determine whether S is conflict serializable. If yes, find the equivalent serial schedule. [7 Marks] **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Identify conflicting operations on the same data item.** Conflicts on **X**: - w1(X) before r2(?) ŌĆö No r2(X) here. - w1(X) before w3(X) at position 12 ŌåÆ T1 ŌåÆ T3 - r3(X) at position 11, w1(X) at position 2 ŌĆö different order: r3(X) after w1(X) ŌåÆ T1 ŌåÆ T3 (already noted) - w3(X) at position 12 is the final write. Conflicts on **Y**: - w2(Y) at position 4, r1(Y) at position 7 ŌåÆ T2 ŌåÆ T1 - w2(Y) at position 4, w1(Y) at position 8 ŌåÆ T2 ŌåÆ T1 - r1(Y) at position 7, w2(Y) at position 4 ŌĆö r1(Y) comes after w2(Y), confirming T2 ŌåÆ T1 Conflicts on **Z**: - w3(Z) at position 6, r2(Z) at position 9 ŌåÆ T3 ŌåÆ T2 - w3(Z) at position 6, w2(Z) at position 10 ŌåÆ T3 ŌåÆ T2 **Step 2: Build the precedence graph.** Edges: - T1 ŌåÆ T3 (from X conflicts) - T2 ŌåÆ T1 (from Y conflicts) - T3 ŌåÆ T2 (from Z conflicts) **Step 3: Cycle Detection.** The graph contains the cycle **T1 ŌåÆ T3 ŌåÆ T2 ŌåÆ T1**. **Step 4: Conclusion.** $$ \text{S is NOT conflict serializable.} $$ --- ### Part (b): Test whether S is view serializable. If yes, find the view-equivalent serial schedule. [7 Marks] **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Check the three view equivalence conditions against each possible serial order.** **Try Serial Order Ō¤©T1, T2, T3Ō¤®:** - **Initial Read of X**: T1's r1(X) is the first read of X ŌåÆ T1 reads initial value in both. Ō£ō - **Initial Read of Y**: T1's r1(Y) is the first read of Y ŌåÆ Ō£ō - **Initial Read of Z**: T2's r2(Z) reads value from T3. In serial Ō¤©T1,T2,T3Ō¤®, T3 hasn't run yet, so T2 must read initial value. **FAIL** Ō£Ś **Try Serial Order Ō¤©T3, T1, T2Ō¤®:** - **Initial Read of Z**: T3's r3(Z) is first ŌåÆ reads initial value Ō£ō - **Initial Read of X**: T1's r1(X) is first ŌåÆ reads initial value Ō£ō - **Final Write of Y**: T1's w1(Y) is final in S, T1 also final in Ō¤©T3,T1,T2Ō¤® Ō£ō - **Final Write of Z**: T2's w2(Z) is final in S. In Ō¤©T3,T1,T2Ō¤®, T2 runs last, so w2(Z) is final. Ō£ō - **Updated Read of Z**: r2(Z) reads value from T3 (since w3(Z) is the last write to Z before r2(Z)). In Ō¤©T3,T1,T2Ō¤®, T3 writes Z, T1 doesn't touch Z, T2 reads Z. **MATCHES** Ō£ō All three conditions hold for serial order Ō¤©T3, T1, T2Ō¤®. **Step 2: Conclusion.** $$ \text{S is view serializable, equivalent to } \langle T_3, T_1, T_2 \rangle $$ This confirms the KTU principle: **view serializability is broader than conflict serializability** ŌĆö a schedule can fail the conflict test yet still be view serializable. --- ### Question B (Choice 2 ŌĆö Alternative) ŌĆö 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Consider the schedule:** - **T1:** r1(A), w1(A) - **T2:** r2(B), w2(B) - **T3:** r3(A), w3(A), r3(B), w3(B) **Schedule S:** r1(A), w1(A), r2(B), w2(B), r3(A), w3(A), r3(B), w3(B), r1(B), w1(B) *(Note: T1 also has r1(B), w1(B) appended at the end)* ### Part (a): Draw the precedence graph and determine if S is conflict serializable. [7 Marks] **Model Solution:** **Conflicts on A:** - w1(A) at position 2, w3(A) at position 6 ŌåÆ T1 ŌåÆ T3 - w1(A) at position 2, r3(A) at position 5 ŌåÆ T1 ŌåÆ T3 **Conflicts on B:** - w2(B) at position 4, w3(B) at position 8 ŌåÆ T2 ŌåÆ T3 - w2(B) at position 4, r3(B) at position 7 ŌåÆ T2 ŌåÆ T3 - w3(B) at position 8, w1(B) at position 10 ŌåÆ T3 ŌåÆ T1 - w3(B) at position 8, r1(B) at position 9 ŌåÆ T3 ŌåÆ T1 **Precedence Graph Edges:** - T1 ŌåÆ T3 - T2 ŌåÆ T3 - T3 ŌåÆ T1 **Cycle Detection:** The cycle **T1 ŌåÆ T3 ŌåÆ T1** exists. Additionally, T2 has no incoming edge from this cycle but T3 ŌåÆ T1 is part of it. **Conclusion:** $$ \text{S is NOT conflict serializable (cycle: } T_1 \rightarrow T_3 \rightarrow T_1\text{)} $$ --- ### Part (b): Determine whether S is recoverable. If not, explain how it can be made recoverable. [7 Marks] **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Identify read-from relationships.** - T3's r3(A) reads the value written by T1 (w1(A) at position 2, r3(A) at position 5 ŌĆö same data item A). - T1's r1(B) reads the value written by T3 (w3(B) at position 8, r1(B) at position 9). - T3's r3(B) reads the value written by T2 (w2(B) at position 4, r3(B) at position 7). **Step 2: Check recoverability condition.** For each read-from, the writer must commit before the reader. - T1 ŌåÆ T3: T1 must commit before T3. In S, T1 has not committed before T3 reads ŌåÆ **POTENTIAL ISSUE** - T3 ŌåÆ T1: T3 must commit before T1. In S, T1 reads B from T3, but T3 has not committed. ŌåÆ **POTENTIAL ISSUE** - T2 ŌåÆ T3: T2 must commit before T3. T2 has not committed before T3 reads ŌåÆ **POTENTIAL ISSUE** **Step 3: Conclusion.** $$ \text{S is NOT recoverable as written.} $$ **To make it recoverable**, commit T1 immediately after its operations, commit T2 before T3's reads, and commit T3 before T1's read of B. The corrected ordering would be: $$ S' = r_1(A), w_1(A), c_1, r_2(B), w_2(B), c_2, r_3(A), w_3(A), r_3(B), w_3(B), c_3, r_1(B), w_1(B), c_1' $$ By enforcing commit ordering matching read-from dependency, recoverability is restored. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 Ways Students Lose Marks in This Topic (ESE Pattern Observed)** > > 1. **Missing the ReadŌĆōWrite (RŌĆōW) conflict on the same data item.** Students only look for WŌĆōR or WŌĆōW. Every pair on the same item where at least one writes counts. > > 2. **Drawing precedence edges for ReadŌĆōRead (RŌĆōR) pairs.** This is NOT a conflict. Marks deducted for spurious edges. > > 3. **Forgetting to test cycle detection after constructing the graph.** Drawing the graph is worth 3 marks; cycle detection and conclusion is worth 4 marks. Many students draw the graph perfectly but skip the DFS check. > > 4. **Confusing serializability with recoverability.** These are independent properties. A serializable schedule is not automatically recoverable. > > 5. **Not specifying the topological order** in the answer. The final "equivalent serial schedule" must be explicitly written, e.g., Ō¤©T1, T2, T3Ō¤®, not just "acyclic." --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Serial Schedule** = No interleaving, always correct, no concurrency. - **Concurrent Schedule** = Interleaved, needs correctness check via serializability. - **Serializable Schedule** = Concurrent schedule equivalent to some serial schedule. - **Conflict** = Two operations on the same data item from different transactions, where at least one is a write (RŌĆōW, WŌĆōR, WŌĆōW). RŌĆōR is **not** a conflict. - **Conflict Serializable** Ōćö Precedence graph is **acyclic**. - **Topological sort of an acyclic precedence graph** gives the equivalent serial order. - **View Serializable** is **broader** than Conflict Serializable (uses blind writes). - **Recoverable Schedule** = Writers commit before readers of their values. - **Cascadeless Schedule** = Reads happen only from committed writers. - **Strict Schedule** = No reads/writes of uncommitted data ŌĆö strongest isolation. - **Cycle in precedence graph = NOT conflict serializable = incorrect schedule**. - **Self-loops in precedence graph are ignored** (e.g., a transaction reading and writing the same item in sequence). - **n transactions ŌåÆ n! serial schedules** (factorial growth). - **Topological sort is unique only if the graph has a single valid linearization**; multiple valid serial orders may exist. - **Equivalent serial order from topological sort is NOT unique** ŌĆö any topological order works.

topics.title: Conflict Equivalence and Conflict Serializability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Conflict Equivalence and Conflict Serializability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a "Conflict" Between Two Operations?

In a database system executing multiple transactions concurrently, we say two operations **conflict** if **ALL** of the following three conditions are simultaneously satisfied:

1. The two operations belong to **two different transactions** (say $T_i$ and $T_j$, where $i \neq j$).
2. The two operations access the **same data item** (say $X$).
3. **At least one** of the two operations is a **write** operation (`write_item(X)`).

If any one of these three conditions fails, there is **NO conflict**.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Formal Definition of Conflict (KTU Syllabus Standard)**  
> Two operations $O_p \in T_i$ and $O_q \in T_j$ ($i \neq j$) are said to be in **conflict** if they operate on the same data item $X$ and at least one of them is a `write_item(X)`.

## 1.2 The Three Types of Conflicts

| Conflict Type | Notation | Meaning | Why Order Matters |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ReadŌĆōWrite Conflict** | RW | $T_i$ reads $X$ *before* $T_j$ writes $X$ | If the order swaps, $T_i$ reads a different (updated) value. |
| **WriteŌĆōRead Conflict** | WR | $T_i$ writes $X$ *before* $T_j$ reads $X$ | If the order swaps, $T_j$ reads the old (stale) value. |
| **WriteŌĆōWrite Conflict** | WW | $T_i$ writes $X$ *before* $T_j$ writes $X$ | If the order swaps, the final value of $X$ becomes different. |

> [!NOTE]  
> A **ReadŌĆōRead (RR)** operation pair is **NEVER a conflict**, because two reads on the same item, in any order, always produce the same logical result.

## 1.3 Conflict Equivalence ŌĆö Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Conflict Equivalence ($\equiv_C$)**  
> Two schedules $S$ and $S'$ are said to be **conflict equivalent** if and only if:  
> (a) They involve the **same set of transactions**.  
> (b) They perform the **same set of operations** (same reads, same writes, on the same data items).  
> (c) The **relative order of every pair of conflicting operations** is **identical** in both schedules.

Symbolically, $S \equiv_C S'$.

## 1.4 Conflict Serializability ŌĆö Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Conflict Serializability**  
> A schedule $S$ is called **conflict serializable** if it is **conflict equivalent** to **at least one** of the serial schedules constructed from the same set of transactions.

Symbolically, $S \equiv_C S_{\text{serial}}$ for some serial schedule $S_{\text{serial}}$.

## 1.5 Intuitive Real-World Analogy ŌĆö The "Group Project" Story

> [!NOTE]  
> **Conceptual Analogy: The Shared Document Editors**  
> Imagine three students ŌĆö **Aman** ($T_1$), **Bina** ($T_2$), and **Chirag** ($T_3$) ŌĆö editing a **shared Google Doc** of a group project at the *exact same time*. Each student performs small actions in their own order: read a paragraph, rewrite it, add a footnote.  
>   
> - A **ReadŌĆōWrite conflict** is when Aman *reads* the conclusion, and *before* he submits, Bina *rewrites* the same conclusion. If we then say "Aman is based on the old version" the meaning is lost.  
> - A **WriteŌĆōWrite conflict** is when Aman and Bina both edit the title ŌĆö whoever saves last wins.  
> - **Conflict equivalence** means: even if the actual time-stamps of edits are jumbled, we can **logically reorder** the conflicting edits so that the final document is the same as if the students had worked *strictly one after another*.  
> - **Conflict serializability** means: that reordering exists. The interleaved mess can be proven equivalent to a clean, sequential, one-after-another workflow.  
>   
> This is the entire philosophy of the database concurrency controller ŌĆö preserve the meaning even when actions are interleaved.

---

## 1.6 Quick Glance ŌĆö Two Scenarios

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Scenario A ŌĆö Not Conflict Equivalent**  
> $S_1 = r_1(X),\, w_2(X)$ and $S_2 = w_2(X),\, r_1(X)$  
> Both contain the same operations on the same data item, but the conflicting pair is in a different order ŌåÆ **NOT conflict equivalent**.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Scenario B ŌĆö Conflict Equivalent**  
> $S_1 = r_1(X),\, r_2(Y),\, w_1(Y),\, w_2(X)$ and $S_3 = r_1(X),\, w_1(Y),\, r_2(Y),\, w_2(X)$  
> Only non-conflicting ops are swapped (the $r_2(Y)$ is moved past $w_1(Y)$ ŌĆö but $r_2$ and $w_1$ are on **different** data items, so no conflict) ŌåÆ **Conflict equivalent**.

# SECTION 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logic Behind Conflict Detection ŌĆö Stepwise Rules

> [!NOTE]  
> For every pair of operations $(O_p, O_q)$ from different transactions on the same data item $X$, apply the following decision table:

### Conflict Decision Table

| Operation in $T_i$ | Operation in $T_j$ | Same Item? | Conflict? | Conflicting Order $T_i \to T_j$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `read_item(X)` | `read_item(X)` | Yes | **No** | ŌĆö |
| `read_item(X)` | `write_item(X)` | Yes | **Yes** | $T_i \to T_j$ |
| `write_item(X)` | `read_item(X)` | Yes | **Yes** | $T_i \to T_j$ |
| `write_item(X)` | `write_item(X)` | Yes | **Yes** | $T_i \to T_j$ |
| Any | Any | No | **No** | ŌĆö |

The "**Conflicting Order**" column gives the **direction of the precedence edge** in the serialization graph. The transaction that performs the operation *first* in the schedule must precede the transaction that performs it *second*.

## 2.2 The Precedence Graph (Serialization Graph) ŌĆö Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Precedence Graph $P(S)$ for a Schedule $S$**  
> A directed graph $P(S) = (V, E)$ where:  
> - $V$ = set of vertices, one vertex per transaction participating in $S$.  
> - $E$ = set of directed edges. An edge $T_i \to T_j$ exists **if and only if** there is at least one pair of conflicting operations in $S$ such that the operation in $T_i$ **executes before** the conflicting operation in $T_j$.

## 2.3 The Master Theorem ŌĆö Conflict Serializability Test

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Theorem (Conflict Serializability)**  
> A schedule $S$ is **conflict serializable if and only if** its precedence graph $P(S)$ is **acyclic** (i.e., contains no directed cycles).  
>   
> If a cycle exists, $S$ is **NOT conflict serializable**.

### Proof Logic (Intuitive)

- **If** the precedence graph is acyclic, we can perform a **topological sort** of the vertices. The topological order is a serial schedule that is conflict equivalent to $S$.  
- **If** the graph has a cycle $T_i \to T_j \to \cdots \to T_i$, then $T_i$ must come *both before and after* $T_j$ in any equivalent serial schedule ŌĆö a contradiction. Hence, no such serial schedule exists.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]  
> High-yield reference for the KTU board exam. All symbols are isolated using `\vert` (never `|`) to keep markdown tables valid.

| # | Concept | Formal Rule / Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Conflict Condition | $T_i \neq T_j$ AND `same_item` AND `at_least_one_write` | All 3 must hold |
| 2 | RR Pair | $r_i(X),\, r_j(X)$ | **NEVER** a conflict |
| 3 | RW Pair | $r_i(X) \prec w_j(X)$ | Edge $T_i \to T_j$ |
| 4 | WR Pair | $w_i(X) \prec r_j(X)$ | Edge $T_i \to T_j$ |
| 5 | WW Pair | $w_i(X) \prec w_j(X)$ | Edge $T_i \to T_j$ |
| 6 | Conflict Equivalence | $S \equiv_C S'$ iff all conflict pairs have **identical order** | Same operations, same transactions |
| 7 | Conflict Serializable | $S \equiv_C$ **some** serial schedule | Existence test |
| 8 | Precedence Graph Acrylic | Cycle-free $\iff$ $S$ is conflict serializable | **Master Theorem** |
| 9 | Topological Order Length | $\vert V \vert = n$ transactions | One valid serial schedule per topological sort |
| 10 | Edge Cardinality Bound | $\vert E \vert \leq n(n-1)/2$ for $n$ transactions | Strict upper bound |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]  
> **Why does this matter in production systems?**  
> - **Concurrency Control Managers** in Oracle, PostgreSQL, MySQL InnoDB use conflict-graph reasoning to decide whether to **commit, abort, or wait** on a transaction.  
> - **Two-Phase Locking (2PL)** protocols guarantee conflict serializability because they construct a precedence graph that is provably acyclic.  
> - **Optimistic Concurrency Control (OCC)** validates at commit time by checking for cycles in the conflict graph of the validation phase.  
> - In **distributed databases** (e.g., Spanner, CockroachDB), the *Global Serialization Graph* is the conflict serializability test applied across shards.
