# SECTION 3 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Algorithm ŌĆö Building the Precedence Graph

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Algorithm: `BuildPrecedenceGraph(S)`**  
>  
> **Input:** A schedule $S$ with $n$ transactions.  
> **Output:** Directed acyclic graph $P(S) = (V, E)$.  
>  
> **Step 1:** Create one vertex in $V$ for each distinct transaction in $S$.  
> **Step 2:** For every pair of operations $O_p$ from $T_i$ and $O_q$ from $T_j$ where $i \neq j$ and both operate on the **same** data item:  
>     **Step 2a:** Check if at least one is a `write_item`.  
>     **Step 2b:** If yes, and $O_p$ appears in $S$ **before** $O_q$, add a directed edge $T_i \to T_j$ to $E$ (if not already present).  
> **Step 3:** If $E$ contains no cycles, then $S$ is **conflict serializable**.  
> **Step 4:** Otherwise, $S$ is **NOT conflict serializable**.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 ŌĆö Acyclic Schedule (Conflict Serializable)

### Schedule SŌéü

$$
S_1 = r_1(X),\, r_2(Z),\, r_3(X),\, r_1(Y),\, w_1(X),\, r_3(Y),\, w_3(Y),\, w_2(Z)
$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify Vertices:**  
$V = \{T_1, T_2, T_3\}$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Scan for Conflicts:**

| # | Pair | Same Item? | Both Write? | Conflict? | Order in $S_1$ | Edge |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $r_1(X),\, r_3(X)$ | Yes ($X$) | No (both read) | **No** | ŌĆö | ŌĆö |
| 2 | $r_1(X),\, w_1(X)$ | Same TX | ŌĆö | Skip | ŌĆö | ŌĆö |
| 3 | $r_3(X),\, w_1(X)$ | Yes ($X$) | Yes | **Yes** | $r_3$ before $w_1$ | $T_3 \to T_1$ |
| 4 | $r_1(Y),\, r_3(Y)$ | Yes ($Y$) | No | **No** | ŌĆö | ŌĆö |
| 5 | $r_1(Y),\, w_3(Y)$ | Yes ($Y$) | Yes | **Yes** | $r_1$ before $w_3$ | $T_1 \to T_3$ |
| 6 | $r_3(Y),\, w_3(Y)$ | Same TX | ŌĆö | Skip | ŌĆö | ŌĆö |
| 7 | $r_2(Z),\, w_2(Z)$ | Same TX | ŌĆö | Skip | ŌĆö | ŌĆö |

**Step 3 ŌĆö Edge Set:**

$$
E = \{T_3 \to T_1,\ \ T_1 \to T_3\}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Detect Cycle:**

$$
T_3 \to T_1 \to T_3
$$

This is a directed cycle of length 2.

**Conclusion:** $S_1$ is **NOT conflict serializable**.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 ŌĆö Acyclic Schedule (Conflict Serializable)

### Schedule SŌéé

$$
S_2 = r_1(X),\, w_1(X),\, r_2(X),\, w_2(X),\, r_3(X)
$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Vertices:** $V = \{T_1, T_2, T_3\}$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Conflicts:**

| # | Pair | Same Item? | At least one write? | Order in $S_2$ | Edge |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $r_1(X),\, w_1(X)$ | Same TX | ŌĆö | Skip | ŌĆö |
| 2 | $w_1(X),\, r_2(X)$ | Yes ($X$) | Yes (WR) | $w_1$ before $r_2$ | $T_1 \to T_2$ |
| 3 | $w_1(X),\, w_2(X)$ | Yes ($X$) | Yes (WW) | $w_1$ before $w_2$ | $T_1 \to T_2$ |
| 4 | $r_2(X),\, w_2(X)$ | Same TX | ŌĆö | Skip | ŌĆö |
| 5 | $w_2(X),\, r_3(X)$ | Yes ($X$) | Yes (WR) | $w_2$ before $r_3$ | $T_2 \to T_3$ |

**Step 3 ŌĆö Edge Set:**

$$
E = \{T_1 \to T_2,\ \ T_2 \to T_3\}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Cycle Check:**  
Path = $T_1 \to T_2 \to T_3$.  
No back edge to $T_1$ or $T_2$. **No cycle.**

**Conclusion:** $S_2$ is **conflict serializable**.  
The equivalent serial schedule is $T_1 \to T_2 \to T_3$.

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 ŌĆö Equivalence Between Two Schedules

### Given

$$
S_a = r_1(X),\, w_1(X),\, r_2(X),\, w_2(X),\, r_3(X)
$$

$$
S_b = r_1(X),\, r_2(X),\, w_1(X),\, w_2(X),\, r_3(X)
$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Check same operations and same transactions:** Both have $T_1, T_2, T_3$ and same reads/writes. Ō£ō

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compare order of each conflicting pair:**

| Conflict Pair | Order in $S_a$ | Order in $S_b$ | Same? |
|---|---|---|---|
| $w_1(X), r_2(X)$ | $w_1$ first | $w_1$ first (after $r_2$ moves before $w_2$) | Ō£ō |
| $w_1(X), w_2(X)$ | $w_1$ first | $w_1$ first | Ō£ō |
| $w_2(X), r_3(X)$ | $w_2$ first | $w_2$ first | Ō£ō |

**Conclusion:** All conflicting pairs preserve the same order ŌåÆ $S_a \equiv_C S_b$. **Conflict equivalent.**

> [!NOTE]  
> Notice that in $S_b$, the $r_2(X)$ was moved *before* $w_1(X)$ ŌĆö but $r_2(X)$ and $w_1(X)$ are **NOT** in conflict (well, actually they are, but the relative order $w_1 \prec r_2$ is preserved in both). The swap is allowed only because no conflicting operation is reordered.

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| # | Property | Test Method | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Schedule is serial | All ops of one TX then all of next | Trivially conflict serializable |
| 2 | Schedule is serializable (general) | View, conflict, or state equivalence tests | Multiple flavors exist |
| 3 | Schedule is **conflict** serializable | Precedence graph **acyclic** | Our current topic |
| 4 | Cycle exists in graph | DFS / topological sort detects | **NOT** conflict serializable |
| 5 | Two schedules are **conflict equivalent** | All conflict pairs in same order | $S \equiv_C S'$ |

# SECTION 4 ŌĆö Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö Precedence Graph Construction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Receive Schedule S] --> B[Identify all distinct transactions]
    B --> C[Create one vertex per transaction]
    C --> D[Scan every pair of operations O_p, O_q]
    D --> E{Different transactions?}
    E -- No --> F[Skip pair]
    E -- Yes --> G{Same data item?}
    G -- No --> F
    G -- Yes --> H{At least one is write_item?}
    H -- No --> F
    H -- Yes --> I[Determine which appears first in S]
    I --> J[Add directed edge T_i to T_j to graph]
    J --> K{More pairs to scan?}
    K -- Yes --> D
    K -- No --> L[Graph construction complete]
    L --> M[Run cycle detection algorithm]
    M --> N{Cycle exists?}
    N -- No --> O[Schedule is CONFLICT SERIALIZABLE]
    N -- Yes --> P[Schedule is NOT CONFLICT SERIALIZABLE]
    F --> K
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö Acyclic Precedence Graph (Example SŌéé)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1((T1)) -- "w1(X) then r2(X)" --> T2((T2))
    T2((T2)) -- "w2(X) then r3(X)" --> T3((T3))
```

**Interpretation:**  
- Vertex $T_1$ has an outgoing edge to $T_2$ (because $w_1(X)$ precedes conflicting $r_2(X)$ and $w_2(X)$).  
- Vertex $T_2$ has an outgoing edge to $T_3$ (because $w_2(X)$ precedes conflicting $r_3(X)$).  
- **No back edges** ŌåÆ **Acyclic** ŌåÆ **Conflict Serializable** ŌåÆ Equivalent to serial schedule $T_1, T_2, T_3$.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö Cyclic Precedence Graph (Example SŌéü)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1((T1)) -- "w1(X) then r3(X) but wait..." --> T3((T3))
    T3((T3)) -- "r3(X) before w1(X)" --> T1((T1))
    T1((T1)) -- "r1(Y) before w3(Y)" --> T3((T3))
```

**Interpretation:**  
- Edge $T_1 \to T_3$ from conflict on item $Y$: $r_1(Y)$ before $w_3(Y)$.  
- Edge $T_3 \to T_1$ from conflict on item $X$: $r_3(X)$ before $w_1(X)$.  
- The cycle $T_1 \leftrightarrow T_3$ makes the schedule **NOT conflict serializable**.

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram ŌĆö Functional Architecture of Serializability Testing

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Input[Input Layer]
        S[Schedule S from Scheduler]
    end
    subgraph Parser[Parser Layer]
        P1[Tokenizer: extract operations]
        P2[Classifier: read vs write]
        P3[GroupBy: by data item]
    end
    subgraph Analyzer[Conflict Analyzer]
        A1[Conflict Detector]
        A2[Edge Constructor]
        A3[Graph Builder]
    end
    subgraph Decision[Decision Engine]
        D1[Cycle Detector - DFS]
        D2[Topological Sorter]
        D3[Verdict Emitter]
    end
    subgraph Output[Output Layer]
        O1[Result: Serializable / Not]
        O2[Equivalent Serial Schedule]
    end
    S --> P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> D1
    D1 -- Acyclic --> D2 --> O2
    D1 -- Acyclic --> D3 --> O1
    D1 -- Cyclic --> D3
```

> [!NOTE]  
> This block diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a real-world conflict-serializability tester, the kind of component embedded inside the concurrency control unit of any RDBMS (PostgreSQL, MySQL InnoDB, Oracle).

## 4.5 Decision Table Visualization (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Two operations O_p in T_i and O_q in T_j] --> Q1{Same transaction?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> End1[Not a conflict - same TX]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Same data item?}
    Q2 -- No --> End2[Not a conflict - different item]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{At least one is write_item?}
    Q3 -- No --> End3[Not a conflict - both reads]
    Q3 -- Yes --> End4[CONFLICT - add edge T_i to T_j if O_p before O_q]
```

# SECTION 5 ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define conflict equivalence between two schedules. State the conditions under which two schedules are said to be conflict equivalent.**

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**  
>   
> **Definition:** Two schedules $S$ and $S'$ are said to be **conflict equivalent** if the order of any two conflicting operations is the same in both schedules.  
>   
> **Conditions for conflict equivalence** (all must hold):  
> 1. Both schedules must involve the **same set of transactions**.  
> 2. Both schedules must perform the **same set of operations** (same reads, same writes, on the same data items).  
> 3. The **relative order of every pair of conflicting operations** must be **identical** in both schedules.  
>   
> **Note:** Operations that are **not** in conflict may appear in any order. *(1 mark for the definition, 2 marks for the three conditions.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: When are two operations said to conflict? List the different types of conflicts possible between two transactions.**

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**  
>   
> Two operations $O_p \in T_i$ and $O_q \in T_j$ (where $i \neq j$) are said to **conflict** if **all** of the following hold:  
> (a) They belong to **different transactions**.  
> (b) They access the **same data item**.  
> (c) **At least one** of them is a **write** operation.  
>   
> **Types of conflicts (3 types):**  
> 1. **ReadŌĆōWrite (RW) conflict:** $T_i$ reads $X$ and $T_j$ writes $X$.  
> 2. **WriteŌĆōRead (WR) conflict:** $T_i$ writes $X$ and $T_j$ reads $X$.  
> 3. **WriteŌĆōWrite (WW) conflict:** Both $T_i$ and $T_j$ write $X$.  
>   
> *(1 mark for the conditions, 2 marks for listing the three types with examples.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Format)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Module carries a 14-mark question. The student must answer **either** Option A **or** Option B. Each option has two sub-parts typically worth 7 marks each.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Consider the following schedule $S$ involving three transactions $T_1, T_2, T_3$:**

$$
S = r_1(X),\ r_2(Z),\ r_1(Z),\ r_3(X),\ r_3(Y),\ w_1(X),\ w_2(Z),\ w_3(Y),\ r_2(Y),\ w_2(Y)
$$

**(a) Construct the precedence graph for $S$ and determine whether $S$ is conflict serializable. Justify your answer. (7 marks)**

**(b) If $S$ is conflict serializable, find an equivalent serial schedule. If not, identify the cycle and explain why no equivalent serial schedule exists. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Vertices:** $V = \{T_1, T_2, T_3\}$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Identify all conflicting operation pairs:**

| # | Pair | Same Item? | At least one write? | Order in $S$ | Edge |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $r_1(X),\, r_3(X)$ | Yes ($X$) | No (RR) | ŌĆö | **No conflict** |
| 2 | $r_1(X),\, w_1(X)$ | Same TX | ŌĆö | Skip | ŌĆö |
| 3 | $r_3(X),\, w_1(X)$ | Yes ($X$) | Yes (RW) | $r_3$ before $w_1$ | $T_3 \to T_1$ |
| 4 | $r_2(Z),\, r_1(Z)$ | Yes ($Z$) | No (RR) | ŌĆö | **No conflict** |
| 5 | $r_1(Z),\, w_2(Z)$ | Yes ($Z$) | Yes (RW) | $r_1$ before $w_2$ | $T_1 \to T_2$ |
| 6 | $r_2(Z),\, w_2(Z)$ | Same TX | ŌĆö | Skip | ŌĆö |
| 7 | $r_3(Y),\, r_2(Y)$ | Yes ($Y$) | No (RR) | ŌĆö | **No conflict** |
| 8 | $r_3(Y),\, w_3(Y)$ | Same TX | ŌĆö | Skip | ŌĆö |
| 9 | $r_3(Y),\, w_2(Y)$ | Yes ($Y$) | Yes (RW) | $r_3$ before $w_2$ | $T_3 \to T_2$ |
| 10 | $r_2(Y),\, w_2(Y)$ | Same TX | ŌĆö | Skip | ŌĆö |
| 11 | $w_3(Y),\, w_2(Y)$ | Yes ($Y$) | Yes (WW) | $w_3$ before $w_2$ | $T_3 \to T_2$ |

**Step 3 ŌĆö Edge Set:**

$$
E = \{\ T_3 \to T_1,\ \ T_1 \to T_2,\ \ T_3 \to T_2\ \}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Cycle Detection (DFS-based reasoning):**  
- From $T_3$ we can reach $T_1$ and $T_2$.  
- From $T_1$ we can reach $T_2$.  
- There is **no edge returning** to $T_3$.  
- Hence, **no directed cycle exists**.

**Verdict:** $S$ is **conflict serializable**. *(7 marks ŌĆö 2 for vertex/edge identification, 3 for full conflict scan, 2 for cycle conclusion.)*

---

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Topological Sort of $P(S)$:**  
The partial order derived from the edges is:

$$
T_3 \to T_1 \to T_2
$$

(The edge $T_3 \to T_2$ is **redundant** ŌĆö it is implied by the path $T_3 \to T_1 \to T_2$, so it does not affect the order.)

**Step 2 ŌĆö Equivalent Serial Schedule:**

$$
S_{\text{serial}} = T_3,\ T_1,\ T_2
$$

Written explicitly:

$$
S_{\text{serial}} = r_3(X),\, r_3(Y),\, w_3(Y),\, r_1(X),\, w_1(X),\, r_1(Z),\, r_2(Z),\, w_2(Z),\, r_2(Y),\, w_2(Y)
$$

**Verification:** All conflicting operations appear in the **same order** as in $S$. Hence $S \equiv_C S_{\text{serial}}$.

> [!NOTE]  
> *(7 marks ŌĆö 3 for topological sort derivation, 2 for the explicit serial schedule, 2 for verification.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Consider the following schedule $S$ of four transactions:**

$$
S = r_2(A),\ w_3(A),\ r_1(A),\ w_1(B),\ w_3(B),\ r_4(B),\ w_2(C),\ r_4(C),\ w_4(C)
$$

**(a) Construct the precedence graph for the schedule and test for conflict serializability. (7 marks)**

**(b) If conflict serializable, write the equivalent serial schedule(s). If not, identify and justify the cycle. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Vertices:** $V = \{T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4\}$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Scan for Conflicts:**

| # | Pair | Same Item? | At least one write? | Order in $S$ | Edge |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $r_2(A),\, w_3(A)$ | Yes ($A$) | Yes (RW) | $r_2$ before $w_3$ | $T_2 \to T_3$ |
| 2 | $r_2(A),\, r_1(A)$ | Yes ($A$) | No (RR) | ŌĆö | **No conflict** |
| 3 | $w_3(A),\, r_1(A)$ | Yes ($A$) | Yes (WR) | $w_3$ before $r_1$ | $T_3 \to T_1$ |
| 4 | $w_1(B),\, w_3(B)$ | Yes ($B$) | Yes (WW) | $w_1$ before $w_3$ | $T_1 \to T_3$ |
| 5 | $w_3(B),\, r_4(B)$ | Yes ($B$) | Yes (WR) | $w_3$ before $r_4$ | $T_3 \to T_4$ |
| 6 | $w_1(B),\, r_4(B)$ | Yes ($B$) | Yes (WR) | $w_1$ before $r_4$ | $T_1 \to T_4$ |
| 7 | $w_2(C),\, r_4(C)$ | Yes ($C$) | Yes (WR) | $w_2$ before $r_4$ | $T_2 \to T_4$ |
| 8 | $w_2(C),\, w_4(C)$ | Yes ($C$) | Yes (WW) | $w_2$ before $w_4$ | $T_2 \to T_4$ |
| 9 | $r_4(C),\, w_4(C)$ | Same TX | ŌĆö | Skip | ŌĆö |

**Step 3 ŌĆö Edge Set:**

$$
E = \{\ T_2 \to T_3,\ \ T_3 \to T_1,\ \ T_1 \to T_3,\ \ T_3 \to T_4,\ \ T_1 \to T_4,\ \ T_2 \to T_4\ \}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Cycle Detection:**  
Notice the edges $T_3 \to T_1$ **and** $T_1 \to T_3$ form a **2-cycle**:

$$
T_3 \leftrightarrow T_1
$$

**Verdict:** $S$ is **NOT conflict serializable**. *(7 marks ŌĆö 2 for vertex ID, 3 for conflict scan, 2 for cycle detection.)*

---

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the Cycle:**  
The cycle is:

$$
T_1 \to T_3 \to T_1
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Source of the Cycle:**

- $T_3 \to T_1$ arose because $w_3(A)$ **preceded** $r_1(A)$ ŌĆö a WR conflict on item $A$.  
- $T_1 \to T_3$ arose because $w_1(B)$ **preceded** $w_3(B)$ ŌĆö a WW conflict on item $B$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Justification for Non-Serializability:**  
For $S$ to be conflict equivalent to a serial schedule, we would need a total order of the transactions in which $T_3$ appears **both before AND after** $T_1$, which is impossible.  
Hence, **no equivalent serial schedule exists** for $S$.

> [!NOTE]  
> *(7 marks ŌĆö 2 for cycle identification, 2 for source of cycle, 3 for impossibility argument.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make (and how to avoid losing marks):**  
>   
> 1. **Forgetting the "different transaction" condition** ŌĆö Many students mark every RW pair as a conflict, even when both operations come from the **same** transaction. Always check transaction IDs first.  
>   
> 2. **Counting ReadŌĆōRead (RR) pairs as conflicts** ŌĆö This is the **most common** error. Two reads on the same item in any order are NEVER a conflict. The KTU key strictly awards 0 marks for conflict graphs that include spurious RR edges.  
>   
> 3. **Drawing the precedence graph in the wrong direction** ŌĆö The edge direction $T_i \to T_j$ means $T_i$ **comes first** in the schedule. Drawing it backward (i.e., $T_j \to T_i$) is an instant **2-mark deduction** in KTU valuation.  
>   
> 4. **Claiming serializability when a 2-cycle exists** ŌĆö A cycle of length 2 ($T_1 \leftrightarrow T_2$) is enough to declare the schedule **NOT conflict serializable**. Don't be fooled by the small size.  
>   
> 5. **Forgetting to verify the equivalent serial schedule** ŌĆö In KTU, just stating the topological order is **not enough**. You must explicitly write the operations in that order and **verify** the conflict pairs match.  
>   
> 6. **Mixing up conflict serializability with view serializability** ŌĆö Conflict serializability is a **stricter** condition. A schedule can be view serializable without being conflict serializable. KTU Module 4 specifically asks for **conflict** serializability.  
>   
> 7. **Missing the "same data item" check** ŌĆö A read on $X$ and a write on $Y$ (different items) are NOT in conflict, even if they belong to different transactions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]  
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Conflict Equivalence & Conflict Serializability**  
>   
> - **Conflict** requires THREE conditions: different TX, same item, at least one write.  
> - **Three types** of conflicts: RW, WR, WW. **RR is never a conflict.**  
> - **Conflict equivalence** = same set of TX, same operations, **identical order of all conflicting pairs**.  
> - **Conflict serializability** = conflict equivalent to **at least one** serial schedule.  
> - **Master test:** Build a **precedence graph** (serialization graph).  
>   - Vertices = transactions.  
>   - Directed edge $T_i \to T_j$ whenever a conflict pair has $T_i$'s operation **before** $T_j$'s operation.  
> - **Acyclic precedence graph $\iff$ conflict serializable schedule.**  
> - If a **cycle exists** $\Rightarrow$ schedule is **NOT** conflict serializable.  
> - Use **Topological Sort** on the precedence graph to derive the equivalent serial schedule.  
> - Edge direction = the order in which the operations **appear in the schedule**, NOT the order in which transactions started.  
> - Cyclic graph of any length (even length 2) means **non-serializable**.  
> - Real DB engines (PostgreSQL, MySQL, Oracle) use this exact reasoning inside their concurrency controllers.  
> - **Conflict serializability** is stricter than **view serializability**; every conflict-serializable schedule is view-serializable, but not vice versa.  
> - **Two-Phase Locking (2PL)** ensures conflict serializability by construction.  
> - **Remember the formula:** $|E| \leq n(n-1)/2$ for $n$ transactions (maximum distinct edges in the precedence graph).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recoverable and Cascade-Less Schedules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBEOT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Recoverable and Cascade-Less Schedules ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Recoverable Schedule** > A schedule **S** is said to be a **recoverable schedule** if, for every transaction **T** in **S** that commits, the commit operation of **T** appears **after** the commit operation of every other transaction **TŌĆ▓** from which **T** had read some data item written by **TŌĆ▓**. In formal logic: $$\text{If } T_j \text{ reads-from } T_i \text{ (i.e., } T_j \text{ reads a value written by } T_i\text{), then } C_i \prec C_j$$ where $C_i$ and $C_j$ denote the commit operations of $T_i$ and $T_j$ respectively, and $\prec$ denotes the precedence (happens-before) relation in the schedule. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cascadeless Schedule** > A schedule **S** is **cascadeless** (also called *cascading-aborts-free* or *avoids-cascading-rollback*) if transactions **only read values that have been written by already-committed transactions**. Equivalently, **no transaction reads a data item written by an uncommitted transaction**. In formal logic: $$\text{For any } r_j(X) \text{ in } S, \text{ the value of } X \text{ was produced by some } w_i(X) \text{ with } C_i \prec r_j(X)$$ ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Group Project" Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The Group Project Submission** > > Imagine **four students** (Transactions $T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4$) collaborating on a single project report: > > - **Student A** writes the introduction and passes his draft to **Student B**, who copies Section 2 from it. > - **Student C** then takes Section 2 from **Student B** and builds the conclusion on top. > - **Student D** integrates all of it. > > **Recoverable Rule:** Before **D** submits the final report to the professor, every student whose work D relied on (B ŌåÆ A ŌåÆ C) must have submitted their parts. If **A** later decides to discard his introduction, we must be able to undo **B, C, and D** as well. > > **Cascadeless Rule:** A stricter rule ŌĆö **B** is *not allowed* to read from **A** until **A** has formally submitted (committed) his introduction. This way, if **A** quits, **B, C, D** are unaffected ŌĆö **no cascade of re-work**. This analogy directly maps to the database concept of avoiding "cascading aborts" ŌĆö when one transaction fails, it should not drag a chain of dependent transactions down with it. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics / Hierarchy Summary > [!TIP] > **Strictness Hierarchy (from weakest to strongest):** > $$\text{Recoverable} \;\subset\; \text{Cascadeless} \;\subset\; \text{Strict} \;\subset\; \text{Serializable}$$ > Every **strict** schedule is cascadeless. Every **cascadeless** schedule is recoverable. The reverse implications are **false**. | Metric | Recoverable | Cascadeless | Strict | |:---|:---:|:---:|:---:| | Allows dirty read | ŌØī Indirectly avoided | ŌØī Forbidden | ŌØī Forbidden | | Avoids cascading aborts | ŌØī Not guaranteed | Ō£ģ Yes | Ō£ģ Yes | | Requires read of uncommitted write | Allowed if writer commits first | ŌØī Forbidden | ŌØī Forbidden | | Allows write of item read by uncommitted tx | Ō£ģ Allowed | Ō£ģ Allowed | ŌØī Forbidden | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ### 2.1 Why These Properties Matter ŌĆö The Underlying Logic > [!NOTE] > The three properties (Recoverable, Cascadeless, Strict) form a **progressively stricter safety ladder** designed to simplify recovery management in a DBMS after a system crash or transaction abort. **The "Why" behind each property:** 1. **Recoverable** ŌåÆ Ensures the system can **always undo the right transactions** if a transaction that another committed transaction depended upon later aborts. It is the *minimum* requirement for the system to be correct in the presence of failures. 2. **Cascadeless** ŌåÆ Eliminates the **cascading rollback problem** entirely. If transaction $T_i$ aborts, no other transaction $T_j$ is forced to abort just because $T_j$ had read from $T_i$. This dramatically reduces recovery overhead. 3. **Strict** ŌåÆ Adds a stronger guarantee: no transaction can write a data item that has been written by an uncommitted transaction. This makes **undo recovery trivial** (just restore the old image, no need to search for dependent transactions). ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Framework **Testing a schedule for Recoverability:** - **Step 1:** Identify all `read_from` dependencies: for every $r_j(X)$, find which transaction $w_i(X)$ produced the value. - **Step 2:** For each such pair $(T_i, T_j)$, check whether $C_i$ (commit of $T_i$) appears before $C_j$ (commit of $T_j$) in the schedule. - **Step 3:** If for **every** such pair, $C_i \prec C_j$ ŌĆö the schedule is **recoverable**. Otherwise, it is **non-recoverable**. **Testing a schedule for Cascadelessness:** - **Step 1:** Identify every read operation $r_j(X)$. - **Step 2:** Check whether the value being read was written by a transaction $T_i$ that has **already committed** (i.e., $C_i \prec r_j(X)$) at the moment $r_j(X)$ executes. - **Step 3:** If **all** reads in the schedule satisfy this, the schedule is **cascadeless**. Otherwise, it is **not cascadeless**. ### 2.3 Cascading Aborts ŌĆö The Problem We Are Trying to Avoid > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cascading Abort / Cascading Rollback:** > A situation in which the abort of a single transaction forces the abort of one or more other transactions, which in turn may force the abort of even more transactions, producing a *cascade* of rollbacks. > > This is highly undesirable because: > - A huge amount of computation is wasted. > - Recovery time after a failure becomes very long. > - The system throughput drops significantly. **Example (Non-Cascadeless, but Recoverable):** | Time | $T_1$ | $T_2$ | $T_3$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | `W(X)` | | | | 2 | | `R(X)` | | | 3 | | `W(X)` | | | 4 | | | `R(X)` | | 5 | | | `W(X)` | | 6 | | `Commit` | | | 7 | `Abort` | | | | 8 | | | `Commit` | Here $T_1$ aborts at step 7. Both $T_2$ and $T_3$ had read uncommitted values from $T_1$ (transitively) ŌåÆ **cascading abort** is triggered. ### 2.4 KTU Formula / Definition Cheat Sheet | # | Property | Formal Condition | Cascading Aborts Avoided? | |:---:|:---|:---|:---:| | 1 | **Recoverable** | $T_j$ reads-from $T_i \Rightarrow C_i \prec C_j$ | ŌØī Not necessarily | | 2 | **Cascadeless** | $T_j$ reads only values produced by transactions that have committed before $r_j(X)$ | Ō£ģ Yes | | 3 | **Strict** | No $r_j(X)$ or $w_j(X)$ reads/overwrites a value written by an uncommitted $T_i$ | Ō£ģ Yes | | 4 | **Conflict Serializable** | Serialization graph is acyclic | Depends | | Symbol | Meaning | |:---|:---| | $r_i(X)$ | Transaction $T_i$ reads data item $X$ | | $w_i(X)$ | Transaction $T_i$ writes data item $X$ | | $C_i$ | Transaction $T_i$ commits | | $A_i$ | Transaction $T_i$ aborts | | $T_j$ reads-from $T_i$ | $T_j$ reads a value of some item that was last written by $T_i$ | ### 2.5 Real-World Utility > [!TIP] > **Engineering Utility in Industry:** > - **Banking Systems** (e.g., ATM networks, UPI transfers): *Strict* schedules are mandatory to avoid financial loss during rollback. > - **E-commerce inventory management:** *Cascadeless* schedules prevent one order failure from canceling hundreds of dependent cart updates. > - **Reservation systems** (airlines, hotels): *Recoverable* scheduling is the bare minimum for legal accountability in transactions. ## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Worked Examples ### 3.1 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Classifying Common Schedule Patterns We will examine six representative schedules and classify each one. Let $r_i, w_i, C_i, A_i$ denote read, write, commit, and abort of transaction $T_i$ respectively. --- #### **Schedule SŌéü ŌĆö The Non-Recoverable Case (BAD)** | Step | $T_1$ | $T_2$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | $w_1(A)$ | | | 2 | | $r_2(A)$ | | 3 | $C_1$ | | | 4 | | $C_2$ | **Analysis:** - **Dependency:** $T_2$ reads-from $T_1$ (it reads $A$ which was last written by $T_1$). - **Check:** Does $C_1$ appear before $C_2$? **YES** ($C_1$ at step 3, $C_2$ at step 4). - **Conclusion:** Schedule is **Recoverable** Ō£ģ. - **Check Cascadeless:** $T_2$ reads $A$ at step 2, but $T_1$ commits only at step 3. So $T_2$ reads an *uncommitted* write ŌåÆ **NOT Cascadeless** ŌØī. --- #### **Schedule SŌéé ŌĆö Non-Recoverable (Truly Bad)** | Step | $T_1$ | $T_2$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | $w_1(A)$ | | | 2 | | $r_2(A)$ | | 3 | | $w_2(A)$ | | 4 | | $C_2$ | | 5 | $A_1$ (abort) | | **Analysis:** - **Dependency:** $T_2$ reads-from $T_1$. - **Check Recoverable:** $C_2$ occurs at step 4, but $T_1$ has not committed (it aborts at step 5). So $T_2$ committed before its source ($T_1$) committed ŌåÆ **NOT Recoverable** ŌØī. - This is the **worst** type ŌĆö if $T_1$ aborts *after* $T_2$ has already committed, the database is in an **inconsistent state** and recovery is impossible. > [!WARNING] > **Recoverability cannot be enforced after the fact.** Once $T_2$ commits reading an uncommitted value from $T_1$, and $T_1$ later aborts, the system has **no way to undo $T_2$'s effects** because $T_2$ is already durable. --- #### **Schedule SŌéā ŌĆö Recoverable but Not Cascadeless** | Step | $T_1$ | $T_2$ | $T_3$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | $w_1(A)$ | | | | 2 | $C_1$ | | | | 3 | | $r_2(A)$ | | | 4 | | $w_2(B)$ | | | 5 | | $C_2$ | | | 6 | | | $r_3(B)$ | | 7 | | | $w_3(C)$ | | 8 | | | $C_3$ | **Analysis:** - **Dependency pairs:** - $T_2$ reads-from $T_1$ (item $A$). $C_1 \prec C_2$? Yes (2 < 5). Ō£ģ - $T_3$ reads-from $T_2$ (item $B$). $C_2 \prec C_3$? Yes (5 < 8). Ō£ģ - **Recoverable?** **YES** Ō£ģ - **Cascadeless?** $T_2$ reads $A$ at step 3, and $T_1$ committed at step 2 (which is *before* step 3) ŌåÆ OK. $T_3$ reads $B$ at step 6, $T_2$ committed at step 5 (before step 6) ŌåÆ OK. So **YES, Cascadeless** Ō£ģ. --- #### **Schedule SŌéä ŌĆö Cascadeless (The Gold Standard)** | Step | $T_1$ | $T_2$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | $w_1(A)$ | | | 2 | $C_1$ | | | 3 | | $r_2(A)$ | | 4 | | $w_2(B)$ | | 5 | | $C_2$ | **Analysis:** - $T_2$ reads $A$ **after** $T_1$ commits ŌåÆ cascadeless Ō£ģ. - $C_1 \prec C_2$ (2 < 5) ŌåÆ recoverable Ō£ģ. - No transaction ever writes a value that was just written by an uncommitted transaction ŌåÆ **Strict** Ō£ģ. --- #### **Schedule SŌéģ ŌĆö Strict Schedule** | Step | $T_1$ | $T_2$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | $w_1(A)$ | | | 2 | $C_1$ | | | 3 | | $r_2(A)$ | | 4 | | $w_2(A)$ | | 5 | | $C_2$ | **Analysis:** - $T_2$ writes $A$ at step 4, but $T_1$ committed at step 2 ŌĆö strictness condition satisfied. - $T_2$ reads $A$ at step 3, $T_1$ committed at step 2 ŌĆö strictness satisfied. - This schedule is **Strict, Cascadeless, and Recoverable** Ō£ģŌ£ģŌ£ģ. --- ### 3.2 Master Classification Table | Schedule | Recoverable? | Cascadeless? | Strict? | Serial? | |:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | SŌéü (delayed commit) | Ō£ģ Yes | ŌØī No | ŌØī No | ŌØī No | | SŌéé (early commit) | ŌØī **No** | ŌØī No | ŌØī No | ŌØī No | | SŌéā (all commit in order) | Ō£ģ Yes | Ō£ģ Yes | ŌØī No | ŌØī No | | SŌéä (full commit-first) | Ō£ģ Yes | Ō£ģ Yes | Ō£ģ Yes | ŌØī No | | SŌéģ (read & write after commit) | Ō£ģ Yes | Ō£ģ Yes | Ō£ģ Yes | Depends on conflicts | ### 3.3 Algorithmic Decision Procedure (For Exam) **Algorithm: Is_Schedule_Cascadeless(S):** 1. Let $n$ = number of operations in $S$. 2. For each operation $op_k$ in $S$ (from 1 to $n$): - If $op_k$ is a read $r_j(X)$: - Find the most recent write $w_i(X)$ before $op_k$. - Find the position of $C_i$ in $S$. If $C_i$ does not exist OR $C_i > k$ (i.e., commit is after read), then: - **Return NOT CASCADELESS** ŌØī 3. If loop completes without returning false: - **Return CASCADELESS** Ō£ģ **Algorithm: Is_Schedule_Recoverable(S):** 1. For each pair of transactions $(T_i, T_j)$ such that $T_j$ reads-from $T_i$: - Find positions of $C_i$ and $C_j$ in $S$. - If $C_i$ does not exist, or $C_j$ appears before $C_i$: - **Return NOT RECOVERABLE** ŌØī 2. If loop completes: - **Return RECOVERABLE** Ō£ģ > [!NOTE] > The time complexity of both algorithms is **$O(n^2)$** in the worst case for a schedule of $n$ operations. This is acceptable for typical exam-sized schedules (10ŌĆō20 operations). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Hierarchy of Schedule Properties (Venn-Diagram Equivalent) ```mermaid flowchart TB node1["Serializable Schedules"] node2["Strict Schedules"] node3["Cascadeless Schedules"] node4["Recoverable Schedules"] node5["All Schedules"] node1 --> node2 node2 --> node3 node3 --> node4 node4 --> node5 style node1 fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff style node2 fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff style node3 fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff style node4 fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff style node5 fill:#7f7f7f,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` **Interpretation:** Every Strict schedule is a Cascadeless schedule, which is a Recoverable schedule, which is a Serializable schedule. The arrows show subset relations ŌĆö moving outward means *looser* the property. ### 4.2 Cascading Abort Flow Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Transaction T1 begins: W(X)"] --> B["Transaction T2: R(X) reads dirty value"] B --> C["Transaction T3: R(X) reads propagated dirty value"] C --> D["Transaction T1 ABORTS at last moment"] D --> E["Cascade Triggered"] E --> F["T2 must be rolled back"] F --> G["T3 must be rolled back"] G --> H["Wasted computation and long recovery"] style A fill:#ff9999 style D fill:#ff4444,color:#fff style E fill:#ffaa00,color:#000 style H fill:#cc0000,color:#fff ``` ### 4.3 Cascadeless Schedule ŌĆö No Cascade Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A["T1: W(X)"] --> B["T1: COMMIT"] B --> C["T2: R(X) reads committed value"] C --> D["T2: W(Y)"] D --> E["T2: COMMIT"] E --> F["T1 ABORTS later if needed"] F --> G["T2 UNAFFECTED ŌĆö no cascade"] style B fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff style G fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff style F fill:#ff9999 ``` ### 4.4 Decision Tree for Schedule Classification ```mermaid flowchart TD start["Given Schedule S"] --> q1{"Are all reads from
committed transactions?"} q1 -- "NO" --> notCL["NOT Cascadeless"] q1 -- "YES" --> CL["Cascadeless"] CL --> q2{"Does any transaction
commit before its
read-from source?"} q2 -- "YES" --> notR["NOT Recoverable"] q2 -- "NO" --> R["Recoverable"] R --> q3{"Does any transaction
write an item written
by an uncommitted tx?"} q3 -- "NO" --> S["Strict Schedule"] q3 -- "YES" --> notS["Not Strict"] notCL --> X["Cascading Aborts Possible"] notR --> Y["System inconsistency possible"] style CL fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff style R fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff style S fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff style X fill:#ff4444,color:#fff style Y fill:#ff4444,color:#fff ``` ### 4.5 Transaction State Machine with Recoverability ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Active Active --> PartiallyCommitted PartiallyCommitted --> Committed Active --> Aborted PartiallyCommitted --> Aborted Committed --> [*] Aborted --> [*] note right of Committed Once committed, a transaction cannot be rolled back individually. Cascading aborts are the only mechanism to undo it. end note ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]** **Define a recoverable schedule. Why is recoverability essential for database systems?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A schedule $S$ is called a **recoverable schedule** if for every transaction $T_j$ in $S$, $T_j$ commits only after the commit of every other transaction $T_i$ from which $T_j$ read a data item. Formally: If $T_j$ reads-from $T_i$, then $C_i \prec C_j$. **Why essential (2 marks):** Recoverability is essential because it guarantees that the database can be restored to a consistent state after a transaction failure. If a transaction $T_i$ aborts after $T_j$ has committed (where $T_j$ read from $T_i$), the system has no way to roll back $T_j$ ŌĆö leading to an irrecoverable, inconsistent database. Recoverability ensures that the commit order respects the dependency order, allowing the recovery subsystem to safely undo or redo operations without breaking the ACID properties. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]** **What is a cascading abort? How does a cascadeless schedule prevent it?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Cascading Abort (1 mark):** A cascading abort is a phenomenon in which the abort of a single transaction forces the abort of one or more other transactions that had read data written by the aborted (or any transitively-dependent) transaction. This creates a *chain reaction* of rollbacks. **How cascadeless prevents it (2 marks):** A schedule is **cascadeless** if every read operation $r_j(X)$ reads a value that was produced by a transaction $T_i$ that has **already committed** (i.e., $C_i \prec r_j(X)$). By enforcing this "read-only-committed" rule, no transaction ever depends on the uncommitted output of another. Therefore, when $T_i$ aborts, no other transaction is forced to roll back ŌĆö the cascade is structurally eliminated. --- ### Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2024 Model]** **(a) [7 marks]** Consider the following schedule $S$ involving three transactions $T_1, T_2, T_3$: | Step | $T_1$ | $T_2$ | $T_3$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | $r_1(A)$ | | | | 2 | | $w_2(A)$ | | | 3 | | $r_2(B)$ | | | 4 | $w_1(B)$ | | | | 5 | | $w_2(C)$ | | | 6 | | $C_2$ | | | 7 | | | $r_3(C)$ | | 8 | | | $w_3(A)$ | | 9 | $C_1$ | | | | 10 | | | $C_3$ | **Determine whether the schedule is (i) Recoverable, (ii) Cascadeless, (iii) Strict. Justify each answer with proper reasoning.** **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1: Identify read-from dependencies (1 mark)** - $T_2$ reads $B$ at step 3. The most recent write to $B$ before step 3 is... $T_1$ writes $B$ at step 4 ŌĆö *after* step 3. So $T_2$ reads the **initial value** of $B$. ŌåÆ No dependency on $T_1$. - $T_3$ reads $C$ at step 7. $T_2$ wrote $C$ at step 5. ŌåÆ $T_3$ reads-from $T_2$. **Step 2: Check Recoverability (2 marks)** Only one dependency: $T_3$ reads-from $T_2$. Does $C_2 \prec C_3$? $C_2$ at step 6, $C_3$ at step 10. $6 < 10$. Ō£ģ **Schedule is Recoverable.** **Step 3: Check Cascadeless (2 marks)** For every read, did the writer commit before the read? - $T_2$ reads $B$ at step 3: writer is the initial state (or self-no write to $B$ before step 3). Ō£ģ - $T_3$ reads $C$ at step 7: writer is $T_2$, who committed at step 6. $6 < 7$. Ō£ģ **Schedule is Cascadeless.** **Step 4: Check Strict (2 marks)** For strictness, no transaction should read or write a value written by an uncommitted transaction. - $T_2$ writes $A$ at step 2. $T_1$ read $A$ at step 1 (before $T_2$'s write), so $T_1$ read the *old* value ŌĆö not a conflict. - $T_1$ writes $B$ at step 4. $T_2$ read $B$ at step 3 ŌĆö but the read was *before* the write, so not a strict violation. - However, $T_2$ wrote $A$ at step 2. $T_3$ writes $A$ at step 8. Between steps 2 and 8, did $T_2$ commit? Yes, at step 6. So $T_3$'s write of $A$ at step 8 is on a committed value. Ō£ģ - $T_3$ reads $C$ at step 7. $T_2$ wrote $C$ at step 5 and committed at step 6. Ō£ģ - $T_1$ writes $B$ at step 4. No other transaction reads or writes $B$ after step 4. Ō£ģ **Schedule is Strict, Cascadeless, and Recoverable.** Ō£ģŌ£ģŌ£ģ --- **(b) [7 marks]** **Explain with a suitable example the concept of a non-recoverable schedule. Show that a non-recoverable schedule leads to an irrecoverable database state upon failure.** **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** A non-recoverable schedule is one in which a transaction $T_j$ commits after reading a value from $T_i$, but $T_i$ later aborts (or fails) before committing. In such a case, the database has no way to undo $T_j$'s effects, because $T_j$ has already become durable. **Example Schedule (3 marks):** | Step | $T_1$ | $T_2$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | $w_1(A, 100)$ | | | 2 | | $r_2(A)$ ŌåÆ reads 100 | | 3 | | $w_2(B, 200)$ | | 4 | | $C_2$ ŌåÉ $T_2$ commits! | | 5 | $A_1$ ŌåÉ $T_1$ aborts! | **Explanation of irrecoverability (2.5 marks):** - $T_2$ committed at step 4 with $B = 200$ (which depended on reading $A = 100$ from $T_1$). - $T_1$ aborts at step 5, which should logically force $A$ to revert to its prior value. - But $T_2$ has **already committed** with effects based on the *dirty* $A = 100$. - The DBMS **cannot roll back $T_2$** because: - $T_2$ is in the committed (durable) state. - The log records show $T_2$ committed successfully. - Therefore, the database is now in an **inconsistent state**: the effect of $T_1$'s abort is not propagated to $T_2$, and the system's recovery procedures cannot restore consistency. **Conclusion:** Non-recoverable schedules are forbidden in any production DBMS. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024 Model]** **(a) [7 marks]** **Compare and contrast Recoverable, Cascadeless, and Strict schedules with proper examples. Draw a hierarchy diagram and explain the relationship between them.** **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Comparison Table (3 marks):** | Property | Recoverable | Cascadeless | Strict | |:---|:---:|:---:|:---:| | Definition | Commit only after all read-from sources have committed | Read only committed values | No read/write of uncommitted data | | Cascading aborts | Possible | Prevented | Prevented | | Recovery complexity | Moderate | Low | Very low (old image suffices) | | Concurrency | Higher | Moderate | Lowest | | Industry usage | Minimum mandatory | Common in OLTP | Used in critical banking | **Hierarchy Diagram (2 marks):** $$\text{Strict} \subset \text{Cascadeless} \subset \text{Recoverable} \subset \text{All Schedules}$$ Every strict schedule is cascadeless, and every cascadeless schedule is recoverable. The reverse is **not** true. **Example Demonstrating Each (2 marks):** - **Only Recoverable (not cascadeless):** $S_1$ shown earlier ŌĆö $T_2$ commits after $T_1$, but reads $A$ before $T_1$ commits. - **Cascadeless but not Strict:** A schedule where $T_2$ reads $A$ *after* $T_1$ commits, but $T_3$ writes $A$ *before* $T_1$ commits. - **Strict:** $S_5$ ŌĆö every read/write of $A$ happens only after the writer commits. --- **(b) [7 marks]** **Given the following schedule, test it for recoverability, cascadelessness, and strictness. Also identify the cascading abort chain, if any.** | Step | $T_1$ | $T_2$ | $T_3$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | $w_1(X)$ | | | | 2 | | $r_2(X)$ | | | 3 | | $w_2(Y)$ | | | 4 | | | $r_3(Y)$ | | 5 | $C_1$ | | | | 6 | | $C_2$ | | | 7 | | | $C_3$ | **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Read-from dependencies (1 mark):** - $T_2$ reads $X$ at step 2. $T_1$ wrote $X$ at step 1. ŌåÆ $T_2$ reads-from $T_1$. - $T_3$ reads $Y$ at step 4. $T_2$ wrote $Y$ at step 3. ŌåÆ $T_3$ reads-from $T_2$. **Recoverability test (2 marks):** - $T_2$ reads-from $T_1$: $C_1$ at step 5, $C_2$ at step 6. $5 < 6$ Ō£ģ - $T_3$ reads-from $T_2$: $C_2$ at step 6, $C_3$ at step 7. $6 < 7$ Ō£ģ - **Schedule IS Recoverable.** Ō£ģ **Cascadelessness test (2 marks):** - $T_2$ reads $X$ at step 2. $T_1$ commits at step 5. So $T_2$ reads $X$ **before** $T_1$ commits. ŌØī - **Schedule is NOT Cascadeless.** ŌØī **Strictness test (1 mark):** - $T_3$ reads $Y$ at step 4. $T_2$ wrote $Y$ at step 3 and commits at step 6. $T_3$ reads at step 4, before $T_2$ commits at step 6. ŌØī - **Schedule is NOT Strict.** ŌØī **Cascading Abort Chain (1 mark):** If $T_1$ aborts after step 5 (say), then $T_2$ (which read $X$ from $T_1$) must also be rolled back. If $T_2$ aborts, then $T_3$ (which read $Y$ from $T_2$) must also be rolled back. **Cascade chain:** $T_1 \to T_2 \to T_3$. --- ### ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:** > > 1. **Confusing "Cascadeless" with "Cascading Aborts Free":** They are the same thing, but students often write definitions that mention *only* the no-dirty-read rule and forget the commit-ordering part. (Lose ~1 mark.) > > 2. **Forgetting the transitively-dependent check:** If $T_3$ reads $Y$ from $T_2$, and $T_2$ reads $X$ from $T_1$, you must check whether the cascading abort from $T_1$ propagates through $T_2$ to $T_3$. (Lose ~1.5 marks in part (b) problems.) > > 3. **Reversing the strictness hierarchy:** Some students write "Recoverable ŌŖé Cascadeless ŌŖé Strict" ŌĆö this is the **OPPOSITE** of the correct order. The correct order is **Strict ŌŖé Cascadeless ŌŖé Recoverable**. (Lose 2 marks if the diagram is wrong.) > > 4. **Missing "Initial Value" cases:** If a transaction reads a data item that was never written by any other transaction in the schedule, it reads the *initial value* ŌĆö this does **not** count as a read-from dependency, and students often wrongly flag it. (Lose 0.5ŌĆō1 mark.) > > 5. **Not drawing the schedule as a TABLE in the answer:** In KTU board exams, the table format is the standard. Writing a raw sequence like "T1: W(X); T2: R(X); ..." is acceptable but may lose 0.5 marks for lack of clarity. --- ### ­¤ōī Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Recoverable schedule** = commit order respects read-from order. (Minimum correctness property.) - **Cascadeless schedule** = every read is from a committed transaction. (No cascading aborts.) - **Strict schedule** = no read/write of uncommitted data. (Easiest recovery ŌĆö just restore old image.) - **Hierarchy (subset relations):** $\text{Serializable} \supset \text{Recoverable} \supset \text{Cascadeless} \supset \text{Strict}$ *(note: strict ŌŖé cascadeless ŌŖé recoverable ŌŖé all schedules; serializable is a separate, orthogonal property).* - **Cascading abort** = chain reaction of rollbacks. Prevented by cascadeless schedules. - **Non-recoverable** = the *worst* kind of schedule. Must never occur in any production system. - **Testing rules:** - Recoverability ŌåÆ check $C_i \prec C_j$ for every $T_j$ reads-from $T_i$ pair. - Cascadeless ŌåÆ check $C_i \prec r_j(X)$ for every read. - Strict ŌåÆ check $C_i \prec r_j(X)$ AND $C_i \prec w_j(X)$ for every read and write. - **Practical tip for exam:** Always list read-from pairs first, then check each condition systematically. - **Bonus insight:** The DBMS recovery manager uses the **log** to track these dependencies. The **write-ahead logging (WAL)** protocol guarantees that the strictness property is enforced at runtime.

topics.title: Locking, Two-Phase Locking and Its Variations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Locking, Two-Phase Locking and Its Variations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Locking in DBMS?

**Locking** is a pessimistic concurrency-control mechanism in which a transaction must *acquire* a **lock** on a database item (a tuple, page, table, or row) *before* it is allowed to read or write that item. The lock is held for the duration of the transaction and is later *released*. The component of the DBMS that grants, tracks, and releases locks is called the **Lock Manager**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (SilberschatzŌĆōKorthŌĆōSudharshan standard, KTU prescribed):**
> *A lock is a variable associated with a data item that describes the status of the item with respect to possible operations that can be applied to it. Generally, there is one lock for each data item in the database. A lock can be in one of two states: locked or unlocked.*

## 1.2 What is Two-Phase Locking (2PL)?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two-Phase Locking (2PL) is a concurrency-control protocol that guarantees conflict-serializability. It requires that in every transaction, all lock requests (the **growing phase**) precede all unlock requests (the **shrinking phase**). The instant at which a transaction obtains its final lock is called the **lock point** of the transaction.**

The protocol was first proposed by **K. P. Eswaran et al. (1976)** in the landmark paper *"The Notions of Consistency and Predicate Locks in a Database System"* and is one of the **highest-weightage topics in KTU Module 4**.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö The "Public Restroom Key" Model

Imagine a single washroom in a railway station. The station master has **one brass key** on a hook.

- A traveller wanting to use the washroom must **take the key** (this is `lock-item(X)`).
- While one person is inside, **no one else can enter** because the key is gone (this prevents lost-update / dirty-read).
- When the traveller comes out and **hangs the key back** (this is `unlock(X)`), the next person may take it.

Now, the 2PL rule for the station master:
1. He will **never give the key to a new person once he has started handing back keys from a previous user** (i.e., all locks must be acquired *before* any unlock).
2. This guarantees that no two travellers will ever be in the washroom at the same time, even if a crowd is forming.

| Real-world Object | DBMS Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Washroom | Data item X (row / record) |
| Brass key | Lock on X |
| Traveller | Transaction T |
| Station master | Lock Manager |
| Hang key back | `unlock(X)` operation |

This is exactly the spirit of 2PL ŌĆö *all locks first, then all unlocks*, with the **lock point** acting as the partition between the two phases.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Locks

### 2.2.1 Binary Lock
A binary lock on data item **X** can be in only one of two states:
- **Locked (1)** ŌĆö X is held by exactly one transaction.
- **Unlocked (0)** ŌĆö X is free.

The operations permitted are:
- `lock_item(X)` ŌĆö sets the lock of X to 1 (only if currently 0).
- `unlock_item(X)` ŌĆö sets the lock of X to 0.

**Limitation:** Binary locks are too restrictive ŌĆö they forbid concurrent *reads* of the same item, which is safe but inefficient.

### 2.1.2 Shared / Exclusive Locks (ReadŌĆōWrite Locks)

This is the **most commonly used** lock model in commercial DBMS (Oracle, PostgreSQL, MySQL InnoDB).

| Lock Mode | Symbol | Allowed Operations | Concurrent Readers? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Shared (Read)** | `S` (or `R`) | `read_item(X)` only | Yes ŌĆö multiple transactions may hold `S` simultaneously |
| **Exclusive (Write)** | `X` (or `W`) | `read_item(X)` *and* `write_item(X)` | No ŌĆö strictly exclusive |

> [!NOTE]
> **Upgrade / Downgrade Rules:**
> - A transaction holding an **S-lock may request an upgrade to X** (this is *lock conversion*).
> - A transaction holding an **X-lock may not acquire any other lock** on the same item.
> - Downgrading X ŌåÆ S is permitted only after the write operation is complete.

## 2.2 Lock Compatibility Matrix (KTU 2024 ŌĆö High-Yield)

The following table tells the lock manager what to do when a transaction $T_j$ requests a lock on an item already held by transaction $T_i$:

| Already Held by $T_i$ \ Requested by $T_j$ | **S-lock request** | **X-lock request** |
|---|---|---|
| **None (free)** | Granted Ō£ō | Granted Ō£ō |
| **S-lock** | Granted Ō£ō (shared) | Wait / Block Ō£Ś |
| **X-lock** | Wait / Block Ō£Ś | Wait / Block Ō£Ś |

Equivalently, the boolean compatibility function:

$$
\text{Compat}(L_i, L_j) \;=\; \text{true} \iff (L_i = S \;\wedge\; L_j = S)
$$

## 2.3 The Two-Phase Locking Protocol (2PL)

A transaction $T$ obeys 2PL if every lock operation by $T$ **precedes** every unlock operation by $T$.

Formally, in the schedule of operations of $T$:

$$
\forall\, \text{lock-}p \in T,\; \forall\, \text{unlock-}q \in T \;:\; \text{pos}(\text{lock-}p) \;<\; \text{pos}(\text{unlock-}q)
$$

The two phases are:

| Phase | Permitted Operations | Forbidden Operations |
|---|---|---|
| **Growing Phase** | `lock_item(X)` (S or X) | `unlock_item(X)` |
| **Shrinking Phase** | `unlock_item(X)` | Any new `lock_item(X)` |

**Lock Point:** The moment between the two phases, i.e., the instant $T$ acquires its *last* (final) lock.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Eswaran et al.):** *If every transaction in a schedule obeys 2PL, then the schedule is conflict-serializable.* This is a foundational KTU result ŌĆö memorise the contrapositive too: *violating 2PL ŌćÆ schedule may NOT be conflict-serializable.*

## 2.4 Variations of 2PL (KTU Favourite Question)

| Variation | Growing Phase | Shrinking Phase | Holds X-lock untilŌĆ” | Holds S-lock untilŌĆ” | Cascadeless? | Recoverable? | Deadlock-Free? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Basic 2PL** | Acquire locks | Release locks | First unlock | First unlock | ŌØī No | ŌØī No | ŌØī No |
| **Conservative 2PL (Predeclaration 2PL)** | All locks pre-declared at start | Release from start | End of transaction | End of transaction | Ō£ģ Yes | Ō£ģ Yes | Ō£ģ Yes |
| **Strict 2PL** | Acquire X-locks | Release S-locks only | Commit / Rollback | First unlock (or commit) | ŌØī No | Ō£ģ Yes | ŌØī No |
| **Rigorous 2PL** | Acquire locks | Release all at end | Commit / Rollback | Commit / Rollback | Ō£ģ Yes | Ō£ģ Yes | ŌØī No |

### 2.4.1 Conservative 2PL ŌĆö Detailed
- The transaction **pre-declares** every lock it will ever need *before execution begins*.
- If any required item is currently locked by another transaction, $T$ **aborts immediately** rather than waiting.
- **Advantage:** Deadlock-free, because all locks are acquired atomically at the start.
- **Disadvantage:** Impractical ŌĆö the set of items a transaction will access is often not known in advance.

### 2.4.2 Strict 2PL ŌĆö Detailed
- Same as basic 2PL, **except** that all **X-locks** are held until the transaction commits or aborts.
- This guarantees that any value written by $T$ is never read by another transaction $T_j$ until $T$ terminates.
- **Practical importance:** Strict 2PL is the basis of the **ARIES recovery algorithm** because the *only* X-locks held at failure time belong to the uncommitted transaction, simplifying undo/redo.
- **Used by:** Most commercial RDBMS (Oracle, SQL Server, PostgreSQL) for write-locks.

### 2.4.3 Rigorous 2PL ŌĆö Detailed
- Both S-locks and X-locks are held until commit/rollback.
- Guarantees **no cascading rollback** and is also conflict-serializable.
- Slightly more restrictive than strict 2PL ŌåÆ may reduce concurrency for read-only transactions.

## 2.5 Problems Inherent to 2PL

| Problem | Root Cause | KTU-Mentioned Solutions |
|---|---|---|
| **Deadlock** | Two transactions each hold a lock the other needs (circular wait) | Wait-Die, Wound-Wait, Timeout, Conservative 2PL |
| **Starvation** | A transaction waits indefinitely for an X-lock while S-locks keep arriving | Fair scheduling; priority aging; queue-based lock manager |
| **Cascading Rollback** | $T_j$ reads uncommitted data from $T_i$; if $T_i$ aborts, $T_j$ must also abort | Strict 2PL / Rigorous 2PL |

## 2.6 Lock Granularity

KTU expects the five-level hierarchy:

```
Database
   ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ Tablespace / File
         ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ Table
               ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ Page / Block
                     ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ Tuple / Record
```

The trade-off:

| Granularity | Concurrency | Lock-Manager Overhead | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Coarse (DB / Table level)** | Low | Very Low | Warehouses, bulk loads |
| **Fine (Tuple level)** | High | High | OLTP systems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multiple Granularity Locking (MGL)** uses **intention locks** ŌĆö `IS`, `IX`, `SIX` ŌĆö to traverse the hierarchy efficiently. Refer to the dedicated KTU Module 4 sub-topic for derivation.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat-Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Number of locks held by $T$ | $L_T = \sum \mathbf{1}_{\text{lock-acquired}}$ | Integer $\geq 0$ |
| 2PL constraint | $\max(\text{lock-time}) \;<\; \min(\text{unlock-time})$ | Strict inequality |
| Lock point time | $t_{LP} = \max\{ t \mid T \text{ acquires lock at } t\}$ | Scalar (logical time) |
| Lock compatibility | $\text{Compat}(L_i, L_j) = \mathbb{1}_{\{L_i = S \,\wedge\, L_j = S\}}$ | Boolean (0/1) |
| Conservative 2PL holds | $t_{\text{release}} \geq t_{\text{commit}}$ | For every lock |
| Strict 2PL holds X-lock until | $t_{\text{release-}X} \geq t_{\text{commit}}$ | X-locks only |
| Rigorous 2PL holds S-lock until | $t_{\text{release-}S} \geq t_{\text{commit}}$ | Both S and X |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** 2PL (in its strict / rigorous variant) is the *de-facto* concurrency control strategy in PostgreSQL, MySQL InnoDB, Oracle, and DB2. It enables the **ACID** guarantees expected from OLTP banking, airline, and e-commerce systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why 2PL Guarantees Conflict-Serializability

**Claim.** *If every transaction in a schedule $S$ follows 2PL, then the precedence graph of $S$ is acyclic, hence $S$ is conflict-serializable.*

**Proof.**

Let $T_i$ and $T_j$ be two transactions in schedule $S$ that are involved in a conflict on data item $X$ (i.e., one writes and the other reads or writes $X$).

There are two cases:

*Case 1: $T_i$ writes $X$ before $T_j$ reads $X$.* Then $T_i$ must have acquired an X-lock on $X$ before $T_j$ acquired any lock on $X$ (lock-compatibility matrix). Since both obey 2PL, the unlock of $X$ by $T_i$ must occur *after* the lock of $X$ by $T_j$. Hence $T_i$ precedes $T_j$ in the lock-acquisition order, so $T_i \rightarrow T_j$ in the precedence graph.

*Case 2: $T_j$ writes $X$ before $T_i$ reads $X$.* Symmetric argument gives $T_j \rightarrow T_i$.

Now consider any cycle in the precedence graph:

$$
T_a \rightarrow T_b \rightarrow T_c \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow T_a
$$

Each edge corresponds to a *lock-then-unlock* sequence. Following the cycle:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_a \text{ unlocks } X_{ab} \text{ after } T_b \text{ locks } X_{ab} \\
T_b \text{ unlocks } X_{bc} \text{ after } T_c \text{ locks } X_{bc} \\
\vdots \\
T_z \text{ unlocks } X_{za} \text{ after } T_a \text{ locks } X_{za}
\end{aligned}
$$

Concatenating these temporal constraints:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{lock}}(T_a, X_{ab}) &< t_{\text{unlock}}(T_b, X_{ab}) \\
&< t_{\text{unlock}}(T_c, X_{bc}) \\
&< \cdots \\
&< t_{\text{unlock}}(T_a, X_{za})
\end{aligned}
$$

But because $T_a$ obeys 2PL, every unlock of $T_a$ must occur *after* the lock point of $T_a$, while every lock of $T_a$ occurs *before* its lock point. So:

$$
t_{\text{lock}}(T_a, X_{ab}) \;<\; \text{LockPoint}(T_a) \;<\; t_{\text{unlock}}(T_a, X_{za})
$$

The chain of inequalities forces a contradiction ŌĆö we cannot have:

$$
t_{\text{lock}}(T_a, X_{ab}) \;<\; \cdots \;<\; t_{\text{unlock}}(T_a, X_{za})
$$

**because the lock $T_a$ acquired first must be unlocked last under 2PL, but the chain demands the opposite.** Therefore the precedence graph cannot contain a cycle. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Trick (KTU):** The contradiction comes from the *total order* of all locks-before-all-unlocks inside one transaction. A cyclic conflict graph would require $T_a$ to both *precede* and *follow* itself, which is impossible.

## 3.2 Worked Example: 2PL Schedule Trace

Consider two transactions:

$$
T_1:\;\text{read}(A),\; \text{write}(A),\; \text{read}(B),\; \text{write}(B)
$$

$$
T_2:\;\text{read}(B),\; \text{write}(B),\; \text{read}(A),\; \text{write}(A)
$$

**Step 1: Identify the lock/unlock operations** using S/X locks (write ŌćÆ X-lock, read ŌćÆ S-lock):

| T1 operation | Lock issued | T2 operation | Lock issued |
|---|---|---|---|
| `read(A)` | `lock-S(A)` | | |
| `write(A)` | `upgrade A ŌåÆ X` | | |
| | | `read(B)` | `lock-S(B)` |
| | | `write(B)` | `upgrade B ŌåÆ X` |
| `read(B)` | `lock-S(B)` ŌåÉ **WAITS** (T2 holds X) | | |
| ... deadlock-prone path | | | |

**Step 2: A 2PL-compliant execution (one valid ordering):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&T_1:\; \text{lock-S}(A),\; \text{upgrade-X}(A),\; \text{write}(A),\; \text{lock-S}(B),\; \text{upgrade-X}(B),\; \text{write}(B) \\
&\hspace{3.5cm} \text{[Lock Point of }T_1\text{]} \\
&\hspace{3.5cm} \text{unlock}(A),\; \text{unlock}(B)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&T_2:\; \text{lock-S}(B),\; \text{upgrade-X}(B),\; \text{write}(B),\; \text{lock-S}(A),\; \text{upgrade-X}(A),\; \text{write}(A) \\
&\hspace{3.5cm} \text{[Lock Point of }T_2\text{]} \\
&\hspace{3.5cm} \text{unlock}(B),\; \text{unlock}(A)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Interleaving obeying 2PL (serializable):**

| t | $T_1$ | $T_2$ | Lock state (A, B) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `lock-S(A)` | | (S, ŌĆō) |
| 2 | `upgrade-X(A)` | | (X, ŌĆō) |
| 3 | `write(A)` | | (X, ŌĆō) |
| 4 | `lock-S(B)` | | (X, S) |
| 5 | `upgrade-X(B)` | | (X, X) **[Lock Point $T_1$]** |
| 6 | `write(B)` | | (X, X) |
| 7 | `unlock(A)` | | (ŌĆō, X) |
| 8 | `unlock(B)` | | (ŌĆō, ŌĆō) |
| 9 | | `lock-S(B)` | (ŌĆō, S) |
| 10 | | `upgrade-X(B)` | (ŌĆō, X) |
| 11 | | `write(B)` | (ŌĆō, X) |
| 12 | | `lock-S(A)` | (S, X) |
| 13 | | `upgrade-X(A)` | (X, X) **[Lock Point $T_2$]** |
| 14 | | `write(A)` | (X, X) |
| 15 | | `unlock(B)` | (X, ŌĆō) |
| 16 | | `unlock(A)` | (ŌĆō, ŌĆō) |

**Step 4: Result.** The serializable order is $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$. Note how:
- All locks of $T_1$ (steps 1ŌĆō5) precede all unlocks of $T_1$ (steps 7ŌĆō8) Ō£ō ŌĆö **2PL obeyed**.
- All locks of $T_2$ (steps 9ŌĆō13) precede all unlocks of $T_2$ (steps 15ŌĆō16) Ō£ō ŌĆö **2PL obeyed**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Strict 2PL vs. Cascading Rollback

**Claim.** *Strict 2PL eliminates cascading rollback.*

Let $T_i$ write $X$ and commit later. Suppose $T_j$ reads $X$ written by $T_i$. Under strict 2PL, the X-lock on $X$ held by $T_i$ is *not released* until $T_i$ commits. Therefore, $T_j$ **cannot** acquire any lock (S or X) on $X$ until $T_i$ commits. Hence $T_j$ reads only *committed* values of $X$, so even if $T_i$ later aborts, $T_j$ does not need to abort.

Formally:

$$
t_{\text{lock}}(T_j, X) \;\geq\; t_{\text{release-X}}(T_i, X) \;\geq\; t_{\text{commit}}(T_i)
$$

$$
\Rightarrow\; t_{\text{read}}(T_j, X) \;\geq\; t_{\text{commit}}(T_i)
$$

$\therefore$ no cascading rollback. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Derivation: Conservative 2PL is Deadlock-Free

**Claim.** *If every transaction follows Conservative 2PL, the schedule is deadlock-free.*

**Proof.** In conservative 2PL, every transaction $T$ acquires *all* the locks it will ever need **atomically, before executing its first operation**. This means:

- Either $T$ gets *all* its locks at once and proceeds, or
- If even one required lock is held by another transaction, $T$ **aborts immediately** (it does not wait).

Hence no transaction ever enters a state where it holds some locks and is *waiting* for another lock held by a different transaction. Circular wait, the *necessary* condition for deadlock (Coffman conditions), is therefore impossible. $\blacksquare$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Derivation Tip:** Always cite Coffman's four deadlock conditions (mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, circular wait) and show that conservative 2PL breaks the **hold-and-wait** condition.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö Lifecycle of a 2PL Transaction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Transaction T starts]) --> initA[Initialise lock-list as empty]
    initA --> growingA{Growing Phase}
    growingA -- "lock-S X or lock-X Y" --> check1{Lock free or compatible?}
    check1 -- Yes --> grant1[Add lock to lock-list]
    check1 -- No --> wait1[Wait in lock queue]
    wait1 --> check1
    grant1 --> more1{More locks needed?}
    more1 -- Yes --> growingA
    more1 -- No --> lockpointA([Lock Point reached])
    lockpointA --> shrinkingA{Shrinking Phase}
    shrinkingA -- "unlock X then unlock Y" --> release1[Remove from lock-list]
    release1 --> more2{All items unlocked?}
    more2 -- No --> shrinkingA
    more2 -- Yes --> commitA([Transaction commits])
    commitA --> stopA([End of T])
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö Variations of 2PL Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root2PL{2PL Variation?}
    root2PL -- Basic --> basic2PL[All locks then all unlocks - no recoverability]
    root2PL -- Conservative --> cons2PL[Pre-declare all locks at start - deadlock free]
    root2PL -- Strict --> strict2PL[Hold X-locks till commit - recoverable]
    root2PL -- Rigorous --> rig2PL[Hold S and X locks till commit - cascadeless]
    basic2PL --> pro1[Pro: Serializable]
    basic2PL --> con1[Con: Not recoverable, may deadlock]
    cons2PL --> pro2[Pro: Deadlock free]
    cons2PL --> con2[Con: Needs full knowledge of items]
    strict2PL --> pro3[Pro: Recoverable; used in ARIES]
    strict2PL --> con3[Con: Still allows cascading aborts on reads]
    rig2PL --> pro4[Pro: Cascadeless and recoverable]
    rig2PL --> con4[Con: Lowest concurrency of all four]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö Lock-Manager Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    txn1[Transaction T1] -->|lock-request X| lmgr[Lock Manager]
    txn2[Transaction T2] -->|lock-request Y| lmgr
    txn3[Transaction T3] -->|unlock X| lmgr
    lmgr --> hashT[Hash Table on item-id]
    hashT --> lockTb[Lock Table entry per data item]
    lockTb --> queue1[Wait queue for X]
    lockTb --> queue2[Wait queue for Y]
    lmgr -->|granted| txn1
    lmgr -->|blocked wait| txn2
    lmgr -->|release wakes| txn3
    lmgr --> dmgr[Deadlock Detector]
    dmgr -->|abort victim| txn1
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö Deadlock Cycle (Two Transactions)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1nodeA[Transaction T1 holds X-lock on item A] -->|waits for| YlockB[Item B currently X-locked by T2]
    YlockB -->|held by| T2nodeB[Transaction T2]
    T2nodeB -->|waits for| XlockA[Item A currently X-locked by T1]
    XlockA -->|held by| T1nodeA
    classDef trap fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
    class T1nodeA,T2nodeB,YlockB,XlockA trap
```

The red cycle in the diagram above illustrates the **circular-wait** condition: $T_1 \rightarrow T_2 \rightarrow T_1$. Neither transaction can proceed; the lock manager's deadlock detector must choose a **victim** (one of $T_1$ or $T_2$) and abort it, releasing its locks so the other can continue.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024 Model Paper, Module 4]**

**Q1. Define Two-Phase Locking (2PL) protocol. What is a *lock point*?**

**Model Answer:**
Two-Phase Locking is a concurrency-control protocol in which every transaction issues all its lock requests *before* issuing any unlock request. The protocol has two phases: a **growing phase** during which the transaction acquires locks (S or X) on data items, and a **shrinking phase** during which it releases them. The instant at which the transaction obtains its *final* (last) lock is called the **lock point** of the transaction. 2PL guarantees conflict-serializability, but does *not* by itself guarantee recoverability. **(3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Distinguish between *Strict 2PL* and *Rigorous 2PL*.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Strict 2PL | Rigorous 2PL |
|---|---|---|
| X-lock held until | Commit / Rollback | Commit / Rollback |
| S-lock held until | **First unlock** (or commit) | Commit / Rollback |
| Cascadeless? | ŌØī No | Ō£ģ Yes |
| Recoverable? | Ō£ģ Yes | Ō£ģ Yes |
| Concurrency | Slightly higher | Slightly lower |

In **Strict 2PL**, only the exclusive (X) locks are retained until termination, so an uncommitted write is never exposed to other transactions. In **Rigorous 2PL**, *both* shared and exclusive locks are retained until termination, so no other transaction can even *read* the data item until the writer commits ŌĆö this eliminates cascading rollbacks. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module-Internal Choice)

### ­¤ö╣ Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö *2PL Variations in depth*

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2024 Expected Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **Two-Phase Locking (2PL)** protocol with reference to its two phases. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the various **lock-based protocols** derived from 2PL: Basic 2PL, Conservative 2PL, Strict 2PL, and Rigorous 2PL. Compare them in terms of (i) conflict-serializability, (ii) recoverability, (iii) cascading rollback, and (iv) possibility of deadlock. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) 2PL Protocol (7 marks)**

Two-Phase Locking is a pessimistic concurrency-control technique ensuring *conflict-serializability*. Each transaction must follow two sequential phases:

1. **Growing Phase:** The transaction may acquire locks (S-locks for reads, X-locks for writes) on data items, but it *cannot release* any lock. New locks can be appended; existing ones are retained.

2. **Shrinking Phase:** The transaction may release locks, but it *cannot acquire* any new lock. Once the first unlock occurs, no more locks may be requested.

The instant between these two phases ŌĆö i.e., the moment the transaction has acquired its **last** lock ŌĆö is called the **lock point**. The lock manager enforces the lock-compatibility matrix. Since all locks are acquired before any unlock, two transactions can never interleave their conflicting operations in a non-serializable way, hence the schedule is conflict-serializable.

*Key consequence:* A transaction $T$ in 2PL behaves as if it were executed *atomically* at its lock point with respect to other transactions.

**(b) Comparison of 2PL Variations (7 marks)**

| Protocol | Conflict-Serializable? | Recoverable? | Cascadeless? | Deadlock-Free? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Basic 2PL** | Ō£ģ Yes | ŌØī No | ŌØī No | ŌØī No |
| **Conservative 2PL** | Ō£ģ Yes | Ō£ģ Yes | Ō£ģ Yes | Ō£ģ Yes |
| **Strict 2PL** | Ō£ģ Yes | Ō£ģ Yes | ŌØī No | ŌØī No |
| **Rigorous 2PL** | Ō£ģ Yes | Ō£ģ Yes | Ō£ģ Yes | ŌØī No |

- **Basic 2PL** ŌĆö the original form; may expose uncommitted data and is not recoverable.
- **Conservative 2PL (Predeclaration 2PL)** ŌĆö transaction pre-declares all required locks; if any is unavailable it *aborts immediately*, breaking the hold-and-wait condition and hence eliminating deadlocks. Drawback: full lock set must be known a priori.
- **Strict 2PL** ŌĆö retains all *X-locks* until commit/abort; guarantees recoverability; used by ARIES-style recovery. Shared locks may be released early, so cascading abort is still possible.
- **Rigorous 2PL** ŌĆö retains *both* S- and X-locks until commit/abort; eliminates cascading rollback; has the lowest concurrency.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part-B 7-mark sub-questions):**
> *Students often write "Strict 2PL prevents cascading rollback" ŌĆö that is **WRONG**. Only **Rigorous 2PL** is fully cascadeless. Strict 2PL only prevents cascading aborts caused by *writes*; reads can still observe uncommitted values via early S-lock release and force an abort cascade. Lose up to **1 mark** for this mis-statement.*

---

### ­¤ö╣ Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö *Lock Types & 2PL Trace Analysis*

**(a)** Describe the **Shared (S) and Exclusive (X) lock** modes. Construct the lock-compatibility matrix and explain its use by the lock manager. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following two transactions. Show a possible schedule that obeys 2PL, identify the lock point of each transaction, and verify that the resulting schedule is conflict-serializable. **(7 marks)**

$$
T_1:\; \text{read}(A),\; A := A - 100,\; \text{write}(A),\; \text{read}(B),\; B := B + 100,\; \text{write}(B)
$$

$$
T_2:\; \text{read}(B),\; B := B \times 2,\; \text{write}(B),\; \text{read}(A),\; A := A \times 2,\; \text{write}(A)
$$

#### Model Solution

**(a) S and X Locks with Compatibility Matrix (7 marks)**

- **Shared (S) lock** ŌĆö also called *read lock*. A transaction holding an S-lock on item $X$ may only *read* $X$. Multiple transactions may hold S-locks on $X$ concurrently because reads do not conflict.

- **Exclusive (X) lock** ŌĆö also called *write lock*. A transaction holding an X-lock on $X$ may *both read and write* $X$. No other transaction may hold any lock (S or X) on $X$ at the same time.

The lock-compatibility matrix is:

| Held / Requested | **S** | **X** |
|---|---|---|
| **None** | Granted | Granted |
| **S** | Granted | Wait |
| **X** | Wait | Wait |

The lock manager consults this table on every request. If compatible, the lock is granted; otherwise the requesting transaction is *added to the wait queue* of the data item. The lock manager may also perform **lock conversion** (S ŌåÆ X or X ŌåÆ S) subject to the same compatibility rules.

**(b) 2PL Schedule (7 marks)**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Translate operations into lockŌĆōunlock pairs:**

- `read(X)` ŌćÆ `lock-S(X)` before, `unlock(X)` after
- `write(X)` ŌćÆ `lock-X(X)` (or upgrade from S) before, `unlock(X)` after

**Step 2 ŌĆö Construct a 2PL-obeying schedule:**

| Time | Action | Locks held by $T_1$ | Locks held by $T_2$ | Lock state (A, B) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_1$: `lock-S(A)` | S(A) | ŌĆō | (S, ŌĆō) |
| 2 | $T_1$: upgrade A ŌåÆ X | X(A) | ŌĆō | (X, ŌĆō) |
| 3 | $T_1$: `write(A)` ($A = A-100$) | X(A) | ŌĆō | (X, ŌĆō) |
| 4 | $T_1$: `lock-S(B)` | X(A), S(B) | ŌĆō | (X, S) |
| 5 | $T_1$: upgrade B ŌåÆ X | X(A), X(B) | ŌĆō | (X, X) |
| 6 | $T_1$: `write(B)` ($B = B+100$) | X(A), X(B) | ŌĆō | (X, X) |
| 7 | $T_1$: **Lock Point of $T_1$** | | | |
| 8 | $T_1$: `unlock(A)` | X(B) | ŌĆō | (ŌĆō, X) |
| 9 | $T_1$: `unlock(B)` | ŌĆō | ŌĆō | (ŌĆō, ŌĆō) |
| 10 | $T_2$: `lock-S(B)` | ŌĆō | S(B) | (ŌĆō, S) |
| 11 | $T_2$: upgrade B ŌåÆ X | ŌĆō | X(B) | (ŌĆō, X) |
| 12 | $T_2$: `write(B)` ($B = B\times 2$) | ŌĆō | X(B) | (ŌĆō, X) |
| 13 | $T_2$: `lock-S(A)` | ŌĆō | X(B), S(A) | (S, X) |
| 14 | $T_2$: upgrade A ŌåÆ X | ŌĆō | X(B), X(A) | (X, X) |
| 15 | $T_2$: **Lock Point of $T_2$** | | | |
| 16 | $T_2$: `write(A)` ($A = A\times 2$) | ŌĆō | X(B), X(A) | (X, X) |
| 17 | $T_2$: `unlock(B)` | ŌĆō | X(A) | (X, ŌĆō) |
| 18 | $T_2$: `unlock(A)` | ŌĆō | ŌĆō | (ŌĆō, ŌĆō) |

**Step 3 ŌĆö Verification of 2PL:**

- All locks of $T_1$ (steps 1ŌĆō5) are acquired *before* the first unlock (step 8). Ō£ģ
- All locks of $T_2$ (steps 10ŌĆō14) are acquired *before* the first unlock (step 17). Ō£ģ
- **Lock point of $T_1$** is step 7; **lock point of $T_2$** is step 15.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Precedence graph:**

- $T_1$ writes A at step 3, $T_2$ writes A at step 16 ŌćÆ edge $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$.
- $T_1$ writes B at step 6, $T_2$ writes B at step 12 ŌćÆ edge $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$.

Single edge $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$; **no cycle** ŌćÆ schedule is **conflict-serializable**, equivalent to the serial order $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$. **(7 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part-B 7-mark trace questions):**
> *Common mistakes: (i) placing the `unlock` *before* the final `write` ŌĆö that breaks 2PL; (ii) forgetting the **lock-conversion** step (S ŌåÆ X) when the same transaction reads and then writes; (iii) using the same lock point for both transactions. Each mistake typically costs **2 marks**. Be meticulous.*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ­¤öæ **Lock** = a synchronisation variable guarding a data item; managed by the **lock manager**.
- ­¤öæ Two main lock modes in practice: **Shared (S)** for reads, **Exclusive (X)** for writes.
- ­¤öæ Lock-compatibility: only **S + S** is compatible; all other pairs force a **wait**.
- ­¤öæ **2PL** = *all locks first, then all unlocks*, with a **lock point** in between.
- ­¤öæ **2PL ŌćÆ conflict-serializable** (Eswaran's theorem).
- ­¤öæ **2PL does NOT imply recoverable** ŌĆö need **Strict** or **Rigorous** 2PL for that.
- ­¤öæ **Conservative 2PL** is the *only* 2PL variant that is **deadlock-free**; it pre-declares all locks.
- ­¤öæ **Strict 2PL** holds X-locks till commit/abort ŌåÆ **recoverable**, but S-locks can be released early ŌåÆ **cascading rollback still possible**.
- ­¤öæ **Rigorous 2PL** holds *both* S and X locks till commit/abort ŌåÆ **cascadeless** and **recoverable**.
- ­¤öæ **Starvation** happens in 2PL when a transaction is perpetually denied an X-lock; mitigation = fair queueing with **aging**.
- ­¤öæ **Lock granularity** ranges from coarse (DB) to fine (tuple); coarse ŌćÆ low concurrency, low overhead; fine ŌćÆ opposite.
- ­¤öæ **Multiple-Granularity Locking (MGL)** uses **intention locks** `IS`, `IX`, `SIX` (separate KTU sub-topic).
- ­¤öæ **Lock manager** stores a **lock table** (hash on item-id) and **wait queues** per item.
- ­¤öæ **Deadlock** in 2PL is detected via *wait-for graph* cycles; victim chosen by cost model.
- ­¤öæ **Theorem to memorise:** Conservative 2PL breaks Coffman's *hold-and-wait* condition ŌćÆ no deadlock.
- ­¤öæ For KTU numerical/trace questions: *always* (i) translate reads/writes into lock-unlock, (ii) mark the **lock point**, (iii) verify no unlock precedes a later lock-acquire, (iv) draw the **precedence graph** to prove serializability.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Log-Based Recovery, Deferred Database Modification, Check-Pointing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Log-Based Recovery, Deferred Database Modification, Check-Pointing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Log-Based Recovery

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):**
> Log-Based Recovery is a fundamental **Recovery Technique** in Database Management Systems that maintains a **log file** (also called a journal or trail) recording every modification operation performed on the database. This log serves as the authoritative source for reconstructing the database state after a system crash, transaction failure, media failure, or any abnormal termination.

The **System Log** is typically stored on a stable storage device (non-volatile, crash-proof disk) and contains enough information to enable both **REDO** operations (re-applying committed transactions) and **UNDO** operations (rolling back uncommitted/aborted transactions).

**Types of Log Records (Standard KTU Notation):**

| Log Record Type | Generic Format | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Transaction Start | `[START_TRANSACTION, T]` | Marks the beginning of transaction *T* |
| Transaction Commit | `[COMMIT, T]` | Marks successful completion of *T* |
| Transaction Abort | `[ABORT, T]` | Marks termination of *T* due to failure |
| Write Operation | `[WRITE_ITEM, T, X, old_value, new_value]` | Records update of item *X* by *T* |
| Read Operation | `[READ_ITEM, T, X]` | (Sometimes omitted in practice) |
| Checkpoint Record | `[CHECKPOINT, L1, L2, ŌĆ”, Lm]` | Snapshot of active transactions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **[WRITE_ITEM]** record is the most critical record type. It captures the **before-image** (BFIM ŌĆö Before Image) and the **after-image** (AFIM ŌĆö After Image) of every database modification. This dual-image mechanism is what enables both forward (REDO) and backward (UNDO) recovery.

### 1.2 Deferred Database Modification

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> In the **Deferred Database Modification** technique (also called the **NO-UNDO/REDO Algorithm**), all updates of a transaction are **deferred** ŌĆö i.e., they are not physically applied to the database on disk until the transaction reaches its **COMMIT** point. Until then, all updates exist only in the log. If a transaction fails before commit, **nothing must be undone** because nothing was ever written to the actual database.

**Key Insight:** Since updates are deferred, the algorithm eliminates the need for **UNDO** operations entirely. The recovery process only performs **REDO** on transactions whose **COMMIT** record is present in the log after the most recent checkpoint.

### 1.3 Check-Pointing

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Checkpoint** is a synchronization point in the log at which the DBMS flushes (writes) all updated database buffers to disk and writes a special `[CHECKPOINT]` record to the log. Checkpointing **bounds the search space** during recovery ŌĆö instead of scanning the entire log, the recovery manager starts scanning from the most recent checkpoint, drastically reducing recovery time.

### 1.4 Real-World Analogies

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö Banking Ledger Book:**
>
