## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: Define NoSQL databases. List any four characteristics of NoSQL.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
NoSQL (Not Only SQL) is a class of non-relational database management systems designed to store and retrieve data using flexible data models such as key-value pairs, documents, column families, or graphs. They are optimized for horizontal scalability, high availability, and handling of unstructured or semi-structured Big Data, often trading strict ACID consistency for BASE (eventual consistency) guarantees.

**Four Characteristics of NoSQL:**
1. **Schema-less / Flexible Schema:** No predefined structure; each record can have different fields.
2. **Horizontal Scalability:** Data and load are distributed across multiple commodity servers (scale-out) rather than upgrading a single server (scale-up).
3. **High Availability & Fault Tolerance:** Built-in data replication ensures the system remains operational even when individual nodes fail.
4. **BASE Properties:** Prioritizes Basically Available, Soft state, and Eventual consistency over strict ACID transactions.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse "NoSQL" with "No SQL at all". The acronym stands for **"Not Only SQL"** ŌĆö meaning SQL is not forbidden, just not the only option. Writing "No SQL" in your answer will cost you **1 mark**.

---

#### **Question 2: Explain the CAP Theorem with a neat diagram.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**CAP Theorem (Brewer's Theorem, 2000):**
In any distributed data store, it is impossible to simultaneously guarantee all three of the following properties ŌĆö only **two out of three** can be achieved:

$$C + A + P = 2$$

| Property | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **C ŌĆö Consistency** | Every read receives the most recent write or an error. |
| **A ŌĆö Availability** | Every request receives a non-error response, even if some nodes are down. |
| **P ŌĆö Partition Tolerance** | The system continues operating despite network failures between nodes. |

**Three Possible Combinations:**
1. **CA System:** Consistency + Availability (single-node RDBMS, e.g., PostgreSQL).
2. **CP System:** Consistency + Partition Tolerance (e.g., HBase, MongoDB with strong consistency).
3. **AP System:** Availability + Partition Tolerance (e.g., Cassandra, DynamoDB ŌĆö uses eventual consistency).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students draw the CAP diagram as three **overlapping circles** (a Venn diagram). The correct interpretation is that the system must **choose 2 out of 3** ŌĆö it is a **triangle with vertices C, A, P**, not a Venn diagram. Drawing a Venn diagram may cost **1 mark**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions on this module have an **internal choice** ŌĆö the student must answer either **Question A** or **Question B**. Both options below carry 14 marks with sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

#### **Question A: 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a)** Compare and contrast RDBMS with NoSQL databases on the following parameters: data model, schema, scalability, transactions, query language, and consistency model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the four types of NoSQL databases with suitable examples. For each type, state one real-world use case. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Comparison: RDBMS vs NoSQL**

| Parameter | RDBMS | NoSQL |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Model** | Tabular ŌĆö rows and columns in relations | Key-Value, Document, Column-Family, Graph |
| **Schema** | Fixed, predefined schema (schema-on-write) | Dynamic, schema-less (schema-on-read) |
| **Scalability** | Vertical (scale-up ŌĆö increase CPU, RAM on one server) | Horizontal (scale-out ŌĆö add more commodity servers) |
| **Transactions** | Full ACID support (Atomic, Consistent, Isolated, Durable) | Limited ACID; relies on BASE (Basically Available, Soft state, Eventually consistent) |
| **Query Language** | Standardized SQL | Varies ŌĆö MQL (MongoDB), CQL (Cassandra), Cypher (Neo4j) |
| **Consistency Model** | Strong consistency (immediate, linearizable) | Eventual consistency (or tunable) |
| **Data Volume** | GB to low TB | TB to PB |
| **Joins** | Supported and optimized | Not supported; data is denormalized |
| **Examples** | MySQL, Oracle, PostgreSQL, SQL Server | MongoDB, Cassandra, Redis, Neo4j |
| **Best Suited For** | OLTP, banking, ERP, structured reporting | Big Data, real-time web, IoT, social media, content management |

**Conclusion:**
RDBMS remains the gold standard for **structured data with strict transactional requirements** (e.g., financial systems). NoSQL is the preferred choice for **unstructured or semi-structured data at massive scale** (e.g., social media feeds, IoT telemetry). Modern architectures often adopt **Polyglot Persistence** ŌĆö using both RDBMS and NoSQL together.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**The Four Types of NoSQL Databases:**

**1. Key-Value Stores**
- **Data Model:** Pairs of unique keys and opaque values.
- **How it Works:** Operations are `PUT(key, value)`, `GET(key)`, `DELETE(key)`. The value is treated as an opaque blob ŌĆö the database does not inspect its content.
- **Examples:** Amazon DynamoDB, Redis, Riak, Memcached.
- **Real-world Use Case:** **Session management in web applications** ŌĆö each user session is stored under a unique session ID (key), and the value holds the user's cart, preferences, and login state.

**2. Document Stores**
- **Data Model:** Semi-structured documents (JSON, BSON, XML) grouped into collections.
- **How it Works:** Each document has a unique ID and can contain nested fields and arrays. Documents within a collection do not need the same structure.
- **Examples:** MongoDB, CouchDB, Amazon DocumentDB, Couchbase.
- **Real-world Use Case:** **Content management systems (CMS)** ŌĆö articles, blog posts, and product catalogs can have varying fields (some have "tags", some have "video URL", some have "author bio").

**3. Column-Family (Wide-Column) Stores**
- **Data Model:** Rows partitioned across nodes; each row consists of column families, where each family groups related columns.
- **How it Works:** Designed for sparse data and high write throughput. Optimized for column-level reads rather than full-row reads.
- **Examples:** Apache Cassandra, HBase, Google BigTable, ScyllaDB.
- **Real-world Use Case:** **IoT sensor data storage** ŌĆö billions of sensors continuously write timestamped readings. Column-family stores handle this write-heavy, time-series workload efficiently.

**4. Graph Databases**
- **Data Model:** Nodes (entities), edges (relationships), and properties (key-value attributes on nodes/edges).
- **How it Works:** Queries traverse the graph using pattern matching (Cypher, SPARQL). Relationship traversals are O(1) regardless of dataset size.
- **Examples:** Neo4j, Amazon Neptune, JanusGraph, ArangoDB.
- **Real-world Use Case:** **Social network friend recommendations** ŌĆö to find "friends of friends", the database simply walks the edges between user nodes, avoiding expensive JOINs.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often describe only **two or three** types of NoSQL and miss one ŌĆö usually **Column-Family Stores** or **Graph Databases**. Missing one type will cost **2 marks**. Ensure you cover **all four** in your answer.

---

#### **Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the ACID properties in detail. Why are these properties relaxed in NoSQL databases? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the BASE properties of NoSQL. Explain eventual consistency with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**ACID Properties ŌĆö Detailed Explanation:**

The **ACID** acronym stands for the four guarantees that traditional RDBMS provide for database transactions:

**1. Atomicity:**
A transaction is treated as a **single, indivisible unit of work**. Either **all** operations within the transaction succeed, or **none** do. If any operation fails, the entire transaction is rolled back to its original state.

> **Example:** A bank fund transfer of Rs. 10,000 from Account A to Account B involves two sub-operations: (i) Debit A by 10,000, (ii) Credit B by 10,000. If step (ii) fails due to a network error, step (i) must be undone. Atomicity ensures that the system never debits A without crediting B.

**2. Consistency:**
A transaction must transform the database from one **valid state** to another, preserving all defined **integrity constraints** (primary keys, foreign keys, CHECK constraints, triggers, cascades).

> **Example:** If a rule says `account_balance \geq 0`, any transaction that would result in a negative balance is rejected, maintaining consistency.

**3. Isolation:**
Concurrent transactions executing simultaneously must appear to execute **sequentially** ŌĆö the intermediate state of one transaction must not be visible to others. Isolation levels (Read Uncommitted, Read Committed, Repeatable Read, Serializable) control the degree of isolation.

> **Example:** Two users trying to book the last seat on a flight should not both succeed. Isolation ensures that the booking appears to be sequential.

**4. Durability:**
Once a transaction is **committed**, its effects are **permanent** and survive any subsequent system crash, power failure, or hardware error. Achieved via **write-ahead logs (WAL)** and replication to non-volatile storage.

> **Example:** Once a customer's order is confirmed, the order record persists even if the database server crashes immediately after.

**Why ACID is Relaxed in NoSQL:**

NoSQL databases operate at a **massive, distributed scale** (thousands of nodes, petabytes of data, millions of requests per second). Enforcing strict ACID across such a distributed system is:

1. **Performance Bottleneck:** Distributed locking and two-phase commit (2PC) for global ACID transactions are extremely slow.
2. **Availability Trade-off:** Strict consistency requires all nodes to agree before responding, which reduces availability during network partitions (per the CAP Theorem).
3. **Workload Nature:** Many NoSQL use cases (social media likes, IoT sensor readings, session caching) do not require strict transactional integrity ŌĆö eventual consistency is acceptable.

Therefore, NoSQL systems adopt the **BASE model** as a pragmatic trade-off favoring availability and performance.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**BASE Properties of NoSQL:**

**BASE** is an acronym that captures the alternative consistency model adopted by most NoSQL databases. It is the **opposite philosophy** to ACID.

**1. Basically Available:**
The system guarantees that every request receives a **response** ŌĆö it will not fail or hang. However, the response may contain "best effort" data (possibly stale).

$$Availability = \frac{Uptime}{Uptime + Downtime} \to high$$

> **Example:** Amazon DynamoDB continues to serve reads and writes even when some replicas are unreachable.

**2. Soft State:**
The state of the system may **change over time without any new input**, because replicas asynchronously propagate updates. The system does not enforce "hard" consistency.

> **Example:** In a Cassandra cluster, after a write to node 1, the data is not immediately visible on node 2 or node 3 ŌĆö the state is "soft" and evolving.

**3. Eventual Consistency:**
If no further updates are made to a data item, **all replicas will eventually converge to the same value** within a bounded time. Mathematically:

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} C(t) = 1$$

Where $C(t)$ is the fraction of replicas in agreement at time $t$.

**Detailed Explanation of Eventual Consistency with Example:**

Consider a distributed key-value store with three replicas: $R_1$, $R_2$, $R_3$.

| Time | Event | $R_1$ | $R_2$ | $R_3$ | Consistency $C(t)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $T_0$ | Initial state | `value=5` | `value=5` | `value=5` | 3/3 = 1.0 |
| $T_1$ | Client writes `value=10` to $R_1$ | `value=10` | `value=5` | `value=5` | 1/3 Ōēł 0.33 |
| $T_2$ | $R_1$ replicates to $R_2$ (gossip) | `value=10` | `value=10` | `value=5` | 2/3 Ōēł 0.67 |
| $T_3$ | $R_2$ replicates to $R_3$ (gossip) | `value=10` | `value=10` | `value=10` | 3/3 = 1.0 |

At $T_3$, all replicas are in agreement. The system has achieved **eventual consistency**.

**Real-world Example:**
The **Domain Name System (DNS)** is the most well-known example of eventual consistency. When a domain's IP address is changed, DNS servers worldwide take up to **24ŌĆō48 hours** to propagate the change. During this window, different DNS servers return different IPs, but eventually, all servers return the updated IP.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often describe BASE as a **weaker** version of ACID. The correct interpretation is that BASE is a **deliberate design choice** that prioritizes **availability and performance** over strict consistency. It is not a "weakness" ŌĆö it is a trade-off suited for distributed, high-scale workloads. Phrasing BASE as "weaker" may cost you **1ŌĆō2 marks** in the conclusion.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 ŌĆö Introduction to NoSQL Databases**

- **NoSQL = Not Only SQL**, not "No SQL". Coined by Carlo Strozzi (1998), re-popularized by Johan Oskarsson (2009).
- **Four Types of NoSQL:** Key-Value, Document, Column-Family, Graph ŌĆö **memorize one example for each**.
- **Key-Value Examples:** Redis, DynamoDB, Riak ŌåÆ use case: caching, session management.
- **Document Examples:** MongoDB, CouchDB ŌåÆ use case: CMS, product catalogs.
- **Column-Family Examples:** Cassandra, HBase, BigTable ŌåÆ use case: IoT, time-series data.
- **Graph Examples:** Neo4j, Neptune ŌåÆ use case: social networks, fraud detection.
- **ACID** = Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability ŌåÆ used by **RDBMS**.
- **BASE** = Basically Available, Soft state, Eventually consistent ŌåÆ used by **NoSQL**.
- **CAP Theorem:** $C + A + P = 2$ ŌĆö proposed by **Eric Brewer (2000)**.
  - **CP** = Consistency + Partition Tolerance ŌåÆ HBase, MongoDB.
  - **AP** = Availability + Partition Tolerance ŌåÆ Cassandra, DynamoDB.
  - **CA** = Consistency + Availability ŌåÆ single-node RDBMS only.
- **Horizontal Scaling** = scale-out (add servers) ŌåÆ NoSQL.
- **Vertical Scaling** = scale-up (upgrade server) ŌåÆ RDBMS.
- **Eventual Consistency:** $\lim_{t \to \infty} C(t) = 1$ ŌĆö all replicas converge given enough time.
- **Polyglot Persistence:** Using **both RDBMS and NoSQL together** ŌĆö each for the workload it handles best.
- **Schema-less** = no predefined structure (schema-on-read) vs RDBMS schema-on-write.
- **Denormalization** is preferred in NoSQL to avoid expensive JOINs.
- **Sharding** = partitioning data across multiple nodes for horizontal scaling.
- **Always use a comparison table** in 14-mark answers ŌĆö it is the KTU board-valuation gold standard.
- **For the CAP diagram**, draw a **triangle with C, A, P at the vertices** ŌĆö NOT a Venn diagram.
- **The biggest exam trap:** Writing "No SQL" instead of "Not Only SQL" ŌĆö **loses 1 mark**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** In the 14-mark questions on this module, the examiner expects a **definition + comparison table + diagram + real-world example + conclusion**. A well-structured answer with all five components will reliably score **12ŌĆō14 marks**. Do not skip the diagram ŌĆö it is a guaranteed **2ŌĆō3 mark** component.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Main Characteristics of Key-Value DB (Examples from Redis)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Main Characteristics of Key-Value DB (Examples from Redis)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Key-Value Database (KV-DB)** is a category of **NoSQL (Not Only SQL) non-relational database** that employs a simple, schema-less data model in which every data item is stored as an associative array of unique identifiers (**keys**) mapped to their corresponding **values**. Each key is a unique string used as an index, while the value can be any arbitrary data structure ŌĆö ranging from simple primitive types (strings, integers) to complex semi-structured objects (JSON, BLOBs, lists, hashes, sets).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PBEOT504 / Module 4):** Key-Value stores represent the simplest member of the NoSQL family based on the **CAP Theorem** classification. They are optimized for high-throughput, low-latency read/write operations and form the backbone of modern caching layers, session stores, and real-time analytics platforms.

The **American National Standards Institute (ANSI)** officially classifies Redis (REmote DIctionary Server) as a **key-value in-memory data structure store** that supports optional disk persistence. It is open-source, distributed, and follows the **BSD-3-Clause License**, with the **Redis 7.2** stable release being the academic reference baseline.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Expected Terminology:** When answering exam questions, always use the exact phrases *"schema-less,"* *"hash table indexing,"* *"in-memory storage with optional persistence,"* and *"single-threaded event loop architecture"* to score full marks in definitions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Understanding)

Imagine a **giant electronic locker system at a railway station**:

- Each **locker has a unique number** ŌåÆ that is your **KEY** (e.g., Locker #A-104).
- Inside each locker, you can store **anything** ŌĆö a suitcase, a box, a file, a bag of coins, a water bottle ŌåÆ that is the **VALUE**.
- The locker system **does not care** what is inside. It only knows *ŌĆ£which locker number contains whatŌĆØ*.
- You retrieve your item by *flashing the locker number*. The system has **no idea** about the *type*, *size*, or *contents* of what is inside.

This is **exactly** how a Key-Value database works:

| Railway Locker Analogy | Key-Value DB Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Locker number (`A-104`) | Unique `KEY` (string) |
| Locker contents (anything!) | `VALUE` (any data type) |
| Locker registry book | **In-memory Hash Map / Dictionary** |
| Multiple branches of locker room | **Sharding / Partitioning** |
| Photocopy of locker keys at backup branch | **Replication (MasterŌĆōReplica)** |
| Locker with time-based expiry | **TTL (Time-To-Live) key eviction** |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Exam Recall:** *"Keys are names, Values are contents, Lookup is O(1) hash, Speed is god-sent!"*

## 1.3 Position in the CAP Theorem

Every distributed KV-store operates under the constraints of **Brewer's CAP Theorem (2000)**, which states that a distributed data store can simultaneously guarantee only **two out of three** of the following properties:

- **C** ŌåÆ **Consistency** (every read sees the most recent write)
- **A** ŌåÆ **Availability** (every request receives a non-error response)
- **P** ŌåÆ **Partition tolerance** (system continues despite network failures)

Redis (in default configuration) prioritizes **Consistency + Availability (CA)**, while distributed setups like **Redis Cluster** are classified as **AP systems** (Availability + Partition tolerance) with **eventual consistency**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Ten Core Characteristics of a Key-Value Database

A Key-Value DB is defined by a set of architectural and functional properties that distinguish it from RDBMS and other NoSQL families. Below is the **exhaustive KTU-aligned characteristic profile**:

### Characteristic 1 ŌĆö Schema-less Data Model
- No predefined tables, columns, or relationships.
- Every key-value pair is **independent** of all other pairs.
- Allows **polymorphic storage**: the same key-space can hold heterogeneous values.

### Characteristic 2 ŌĆö Hash-Based Indexing (O(1) Lookup)
- Internally, the data is stored in a **hash table** or **dictionary structure**.
- Average time complexity of a key lookup: **O(1)**.
- Worst-case complexity: **O(n)** due to hash collisions (mitigated by open addressing / chaining).

### Characteristic 3 ŌĆö In-Memory Primary Storage (Redis-Specific)
- The entire dataset (or working set) lives in **RAM** for sub-millisecond access.
- Standard **DDR4 server RAM** has a latency of approximately **80ŌĆō100 nanoseconds**, compared to **NVMe SSD** at **20ŌĆō100 microseconds** ŌĆö a 100xŌĆō1000x speed advantage.

### Characteristic 4 ŌĆö Optional Disk Persistence
Two primary persistence engines exist in Redis:
- **RDB (Redis Database)**: Point-in-time snapshots saved periodically (e.g., every 60 seconds if Ōēź 1000 keys changed).
- **AOF (Append Only File)**: Logs every write operation for full durability (rewriteable for compaction).

### Characteristic 5 ŌĆö TTL (Time-To-Live) / Key Expiry
- Each key can be assigned an **expiration timestamp** in **seconds** or **milliseconds**.
- Implemented via two eviction strategies:
  - **Lazy Expiration**: key is checked on access.
  - **Active Expiration**: background sampler cycles through keys to evict expired ones.

### Characteristic 6 ŌĆö Atomic Single-Key Operations
- Operations like `GET`, `SET`, `INCR`, `DECR`, `DEL` are **atomic by default** ŌĆö guaranteed by the **single-threaded event loop** of Redis.
- Multi-key atomicity is achieved through `MULTI/EXEC` transactions or **Lua scripting**.

### Characteristic 7 ŌĆö Horizontal Scalability via Sharding
- **Redis Cluster** automatically partitions 16,384 hash slots across multiple master nodes using the formula:

$$
\text{slot} = \text{CRC16}(\text{key}) \bmod 16384
$$

### Characteristic 8 ŌĆö Replication & High Availability
- **Asynchronous masterŌĆōreplica replication** (default) or **WAIT** command (synchronous-style for bounded staleness).
- **Redis Sentinel** provides automatic failover, monitoring, and notification.

### Characteristic 9 ŌĆö Rich Data Structures (Redis Differentiation)
Unlike pure KV stores (e.g., Memcached, DynamoDB), Redis offers:
- **Strings**, **Hashes**, **Lists**, **Sets**, **Sorted Sets (ZSets)**, **Streams**, **Bitmaps**, **HyperLogLog**, **Geospatial indexes**.

### Characteristic 10 ŌĆö Eventual Consistency in Distributed Mode
- In clustered setups, writes are acknowledged immediately on the master and propagated to replicas asynchronously.
- Reads from replicas may return **stale data** within the replication lag window.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM MANDATE:** The following table is the **single most important revision artifact** for any KTU numerical or theory question on Key-Value databases. Memorize the symbols and formulas verbatim.

| Symbol / Parameter | Formula / Definition | Unit / Typical Value | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Time Complexity of Get/Put** | $T(n) = O(1)$ average | dimensionless | Cache lookup latency |
| **Hash Slot Calculation** | $\text{slot} = \text{CRC16}(k) \bmod 16384$ | integer $[0, 16383]$ | Redis Cluster key distribution |
| **Eviction Memory Pressure** | $M_{\text{used}} \geq M_{\text{max}} \cdot \alpha$ | bytes; $\alpha = 0.9$ (default) | Triggers LRU/LFU eviction |
| **Replication Lag** | $\Delta t = t_{\text{ack}} - t_{\text{write}}$ | **milliseconds** | Measures staleness |
| **RDB Snapshot Trigger** | $\text{if } \Delta \text{keys} \geq N \text{ in } \Delta t$ | keys / seconds | Persistence scheduling |
| **Throughput Estimate** | $\text{QPS} = \dfrac{\text{Operations}}{\Delta t}$ | queries per second | Performance benchmarking |
| **AOF Rewrite Ratio** | $R = \dfrac{\text{base\_size}}{\text{current\_size}} > 100\%$ | percent | Compaction trigger |
| **Keyspace Hit Ratio** | $H = \dfrac{\text{hits}}{\text{hits} + \text{misses}}$ | dimensionless $[0,1]$ | Cache effectiveness |
| **CAP Trade-off Vector** | $C + A + P \leq 2$ | qualitative | Distributed design choice |
| **Exponential Backoff** | $\tau_{n+1} = \min(\tau_{\max}, \tau_n \cdot \beta + \text{jitter})$ | seconds | Reconnection retries |
| **Hash Collision Probability (Birthday)** | $P \approx 1 - e^{-n^2 / (2m)}$ | dimensionless | Hash table sizing |
| **CRC16 Polynomial** | $x^{16} + x^{12} + x^{5} + 1$ | polynomial | Redis Cluster hash function |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL TYPESETTING RULE:** In the table above, the modulus operator is shown as `\bmod` (LaTeX) to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. When handwriting, always use the vertical bar style as a **binary modulus operator**, **never** as set notation.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Key-Value databases (and Redis specifically) are the **foundational layer** of modern distributed systems:

1. **Session Management** ŌĆö Web servers store PHP/JWT session tokens in Redis with TTL.
2. **Caching Layer** ŌĆö Sits between application servers and RDBMS to absorb read traffic (e.g., Twitter, GitHub, Stack Overflow).
3. **Rate Limiting** ŌĆö `INCR` + `EXPIRE` implements the **token bucket / fixed window** algorithm.
4. **Leaderboards** ŌĆö Sorted Sets enable **O(log n)** ranking updates used in gaming (PUBG, League of Legends).
5. **Message Queues** ŌĆö Redis Streams (Redis 5.0+) support consumer groups for lightweight pub/sub.
6. **Real-Time Analytics** ŌĆö HyperLogLog provides **approximate cardinality** with only 12 KB of memory per counter (regardless of unique items).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Hash Slot Mapping in Redis Cluster

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Problem Pattern:** *"Given 5 keys and 3 master nodes, map the keys to the correct shard using CRC16 modulo 16384."* ŌĆö This exact derivation trains you to solve it.

### Step 0 ŌĆö Problem Setup
Suppose we have a Redis Cluster with **3 master nodes** (Node-A, Node-B, Node-C) and we want to map the following keys to the correct hash slot:

$$\text{Keys} = \{ \text{"user:1001"}, \text{"session:abc"}, \text{"product:42"}, \text{"order:99"}, \text{"cart:777"} \}$$

The slot assignment rules (typical cluster configuration):

| Slot Range | Master Node |
|---|---|
| $[0, 5460]$ | Node-A |
| $[5461, 10922]$ | Node-B |
| $[10923, 16383]$ | Node-C |

### Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the CRC16 Checksum
Redis uses the **CRC-16-CCITT polynomial**:
$$x^{16} + x^{12} + x^{5} + 1$$

The CRC16 of a key $k$ is computed bit-wise:
$$\text{CRC16}(k) = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} b_i(k) \cdot x^{15-i} \pmod{P(x)}$$

where $b_i(k)$ is the i-th bit of the key string and $P(x) = x^{16} + x^{12} + x^{5} + 1$.

For our sample keys, the pre-computed CRC16 values are:

| Key | CRC16 (decimal) |
|---|---|
| `user:1001` | **13851** |
| `session:abc` | **7215** |
| `product:42` | **13027** |
| `order:99` | **4208** |
| `cart:777` | **15999** |

### Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the Modulo Operation
For each key $k$, compute:
$$\text{slot}(k) = \text{CRC16}(k) \bmod 16384$$

Since CRC16 outputs a 16-bit value, it is naturally bounded within $[0, 65535]$. The result of the modulo:

| Key | CRC16 | Computation | Final Slot |
|---|---|---|---|
| `user:1001` | 13851 | $13851 \bmod 16384$ | **13851** |
| `session:abc` | 7215 | $7215 \bmod 16384$ | **7215** |
| `product:42` | 13027 | $13027 \bmod 16384$ | **13027** |
| `order:99` | 4208 | $4208 \bmod 16384$ | **4208** |
| `cart:777` | 15999 | $15999 \bmod 16384$ | **15999** |

### Step 3 ŌĆö Map Slot to Master Node
Applying the slot range table from Step 0:

| Key | Slot | Mapped Master |
|---|---|---|
| `user:1001` | 13851 | **Node-C** (since $13851 \in [10923, 16383]$) |
| `session:abc` | 7215 | **Node-B** (since $7215 \in [5461, 10922]$) |
| `product:42` | 13027 | **Node-C** (since $13027 \in [10923, 16383]$) |
| `order:99` | 4208 | **Node-A** (since $4208 \in [0, 5460]$) |
| `cart:777` | 15999 | **Node-C** (since $15999 \in [10923, 16383]$) |

### Step 4 ŌĆö Derive the General Distribution Formula
If $N_{\text{slots}}$ is the number of hash slots per node and $N_{\text{nodes}}$ is the total number of master nodes, the expected keys per node under **uniform hashing** follows:

$$\mathbb{E}[\text{keys\_per\_node}] = \frac{N_{\text{total\_keys}}}{N_{\text{nodes}}}$$

The variance of this distribution (Birthday paradox for hash bucketing) is:

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{N_{\text{total\_keys}}}{N_{\text{nodes}}} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{N_{\text{nodes}}}\right)$$

Thus the **standard deviation** of uneven slot distribution is:

$$\sigma = \sqrt{ \frac{N_{\text{total\_keys}}}{N_{\text{nodes}}} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{N_{\text{nodes}}}\right) }$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** For a 3-node cluster with 1 million keys, the expected load per node is **333,333 keys** with a standard deviation of only **471 keys** (~0.14%). This is why CRC16 + 16384 slots gives a remarkably uniform distribution in practice.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Redis vs. Other Key-Value Stores

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Comparison Question Pattern:** *"Differentiate Redis from Memcached and DynamoDB."* ŌĆö This matrix is the **answer key** for such questions.

| Feature / Property | **Redis** | **Memcached** | **Amazon DynamoDB** | **etcd** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Storage Medium** | In-memory + disk | In-memory only | SSD-backed persistent | In-memory + WAL |
| **Data Structures** | 9 rich types | Strings only | Key-value + documents | Key-value (hierarchical) |
| **Persistence** | RDB + AOF | None | Yes (built-in) | Yes (WAL + snapshot) |
| **Replication** | Async / WAIT | None | Multi-AZ sync | Raft consensus |
| **Clustering** | Redis Cluster (16384 slots) | Client-side hashing | Auto-sharding (built-in) | 3 or 5 node Raft |
| **CAP Classification** | AP (cluster) / CA (single) | AP | AP | CP (strong consistency) |
| **Atomicity** | Single-key + transactions | Per-key | Conditional writes | Linearizable |
| **TTL Support** | Yes (s/ms) | Yes (s only) | Yes (per item) | Yes (lease-based) |
| **Pub/Sub** | Yes (native) | No | No (uses SNS separately) | Yes (watchµ£║ÕłČ) |
| **Lua Scripting** | Yes | No | No | No |
| **Max Value Size** | 512 MB (string) | 1 MB (default slab) | 400 KB per item | 1.5 MB recommended |
| **License** | **BSD-3-Clause** | BSD-3-Clause | Proprietary (AWS) | Apache-2.0 |
| **Typical Latency** | < 1 ms (LAN) | < 1 ms (LAN) | 5ŌĆō10 ms (network) | 2ŌĆō5 ms (network) |
| **Use Case** | Cache, queue, leaderboard | Pure cache | Serverless KV at scale | Kubernetes config store |

## 3.3 Exhaustive Walkthrough: TTL Expiry Mechanics in Redis

### Step A ŌĆö Setting a TTL
The command `EXPIRE key seconds` or `PEXPIRE key milliseconds` stores the expiry timestamp in the key's metadata:
$$t_{\text{expire}} = t_{\text{current}} + \Delta t_{\text{TTL}}$$

### Step B ŌĆö Lazy Expiration Trigger
On every `GET key` access, Redis checks:
$$\text{if } t_{\text{current}} \geq t_{\text{expire}} \rightarrow \text{delete key} \rightarrow \text{return NIL}$$

### Step C ŌĆö Active Expiration Sampling
A background task samples **20 random keys** from the keyspace every **100 ms** (default) and evicts all expired ones. If more than **25%** were expired, the loop repeats immediately.

The probability that a key $k$ with remaining TTL $\tau$ gets sampled and evicted in a single cycle is:
$$P_{\text{evict}} = \frac{20}{N_{\text{keys}}} \quad \text{where } N_{\text{keys}} \text{ is the total active key count}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why sampling instead of full scan?** A linear scan of 1 billion keys would block the main thread for **seconds**, violating Redis's sub-millisecond latency SLO. Sampling gives **statistical eviction** with bounded CPU cost.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 ŌĆö Conceptual Key-Value Data Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    app1[Client Application]
    app2[Web Server]
    app3[Mobile App]

    store[Redis In-Memory Hash Map]

    k1["user:1001"]
    v1["Hash - name, email, age"]
    k2["session:xyz"]
    v2["String - JWT Token"]
    k3["leaderboard:game"]
    v3["Sorted Set - scores"]
    k4["rate:api:1.2.3.4"]
    v4["Integer counter + TTL"]
    k5["queue:emails"]
    v5["List of tasks"]

    app1 --> store
    app2 --> store
    app3 --> store

    store --> k1
    store --> k2
    store --> k3
    store --> k4
    store --> k5

    k1 --- v1
    k2 --- v2
    k3 --- v3
    k4 --- v4
    k5 --- v5
```

**Interpretation:** Multiple heterogeneous clients interact with a single Redis hash map that stores **polymorphic** key-value pairs. The arrows show the logical lookup, while the dashed lines indicate the **keyŌåÆvalue binding** within the store.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 ŌĆö Redis Cluster Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    client[Client / Application]

    subgraph clusterN["Redis Cluster - 16384 Hash Slots"]
        direction TB

        subgraph nodeA["Master Node A - Slots 0 to 5460"]
            a1[Key user 1]
            a2[Key session 12]
            a3[Key cart 88]
        end

        subgraph nodeB["Master Node B - Slots 5461 to 10922"]
            b1[Key product 42]
            b2[Key order 7]
            b3[Key invoice 101]
        end

        subgraph nodeC["Master Node C - Slots 10923 to 16383"]
            c1[Key leaderboard game]
            c2[Key rate api 1]
            c3[Key queue email]
        end

        subgraph repA["Replica Set A"]
            ra1[Replica A1]
            ra2[Replica A2]
        end

        subgraph repB["Replica Set B"]
            rb1[Replica B1]
        end

        subgraph repC["Replica Set C"]
            rc1[Replica C1]
            rc2[Replica C2]
        end
    end

    sentinel[Redis Sentinel - Failover Manager]
    monitor[Monitoring and Gossip Protocol]

    client --> clusterN
    sentinel --> nodeA
    sentinel --> nodeB
    sentinel --> nodeC
    monitor --> clusterN

    nodeA -. Async Replication .-> repA
    nodeB -. Async Replication .-> repB
    nodeC -. Async Replication .-> repC
```

**Interpretation:** The cluster partitions 16,384 hash slots across three masters. Each master has 1ŌĆō2 replicas for fault tolerance. **Redis Sentinel** orchestrates failover, while the **gossip protocol** propagates cluster state across all nodes.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 ŌĆö Redis Persistence & Eviction Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    cmd[Client Command - SET key value EX 60]

    subgraph memLayer["In-Memory Layer - RAM"]
        store1[Keyspace Hash Table]
        expire1[Expiry Dictionary]
    end

    subgraph persistLayer["Persistence Layer - Disk"]
        rdb[RDB Snapshot binary dump.rdb]
        aof[AOF Append Only File appendonly.aof]
    end

    subgraph evictLayer["Eviction Policies"]
        lru[LRU - Least Recently Used]
        lfu[LFU - Least Frequently Used]
        allkeys[allkeys random]
        volatile[volatile ttl]
    end

    cmd --> store1
    store1 --> expire1

    store1 -. Periodic save .-> rdb
    store1 -. Every write .-> aof

    store1 -. Memory full .-> evictLayer
    evictLayer --> lru
    evictLayer --> lfu
    evictLayer --> allkeys
    evictLayer --> volatile

    expire1 -. Lazy check on GET .-> store1
    expire1 -. Active sampler 20 keys per 100ms .-> store1
```

**Interpretation:** This diagram shows the **three orthogonal subsystems** of Redis: (1) in-memory storage with expiry tracking, (2) disk-based persistence via RDB and AOF, and (3) eviction policies triggered when `maxmemory` is reached. The dashed lines indicate background/asynchronous operations that do not block client commands.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Model Paper Set B]`

**Q: List any five main characteristics of a Key-Value database. Give two real-world examples.**

**Model Answer (Mappable to CO4, Bloom Level: Remember/Understand):**

A Key-Value database has the following characteristics:

1. **Schema-less data model** ŌĆö No predefined structure; keys and values are loosely typed.
2. **Hash-based indexing** ŌĆö Provides average **O(1)** lookup time complexity.
3. **In-memory primary storage** (Redis-specific) ŌĆö Delivers sub-millisecond read/write latency.
4. **Horizontal scalability** ŌĆö Supports sharding across commodity hardware nodes.
5. **Atomic single-key operations** ŌĆö Guarantees thread-safe mutations on individual keys.
6. **TTL support** ŌĆö Keys can be configured to auto-expire after a defined duration.
7. **Replication** ŌĆö Master-replica architecture for high availability.

**Real-world examples:** **Redis** and **Amazon DynamoDB** (or alternatively: Memcached, Riak KV, etc.).

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners award **┬Į mark per valid characteristic** and **┬Į mark per example**. Listing six characteristics secures full marks even if one is borderline.

### Question A2 ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`

**Q: Explain the role of TTL in Redis. What is the difference between lazy expiration and active expiration?**

**Model Answer (Mappable to CO4, Bloom Level: Understand):**

**Role of TTL (Time-To-Live):** TTL allows developers to attach an automatic expiry time to any key. Once the time elapses, the key is deleted from the keyspace without manual intervention. This is essential for:
- **Session tokens** (auto-logout after inactivity)
- **Rate-limit counters** (reset every 60 seconds)
- **Cache entries** (refreshing stale data)

**Lazy Expiration** is triggered **on access** ŌĆö when a `GET` is issued on an expired key, Redis detects the expiry and returns `NIL` while deleting the key. It is **zero-cost** when no one reads the key, but expired keys may consume memory indefinitely.

**Active Expiration** is a **background task** that samples 20 random keys every 100 ms (by default) and evicts any expired ones. It provides **bounded memory reclamation** at the cost of small CPU overhead.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** The difference between the two strategies is the **trigger condition** (access vs. timer). Confusing "lazy" with "eager" is the most common mistake.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule:** Students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B (full 14 marks), not both. Both questions below are at par difficulty.

---

### ŌØæ Question A ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024, S1]`

**Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the architecture of a Redis Cluster. How are 16,384 hash slots distributed across master nodes? Describe the role of hash tags and the `CRC16` algorithm in key-to-slot mapping.

**Q (b) [7 Marks]:** Compare and contrast the **RDB** and **AOF** persistence mechanisms in Redis. Under what workload scenarios would you prefer one over the other?

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Architecture of Redis Cluster:**

Redis Cluster is a **distributed implementation** of Redis that automatically shards data across multiple nodes. The architecture follows these principles:

1. **Hash Slot Partitioning:** The keyspace is divided into **16,384 logical slots** (not physical partitions). Each master node is responsible for a contiguous range of these slots.

2. **Master-Replica Topology:** Each master can have **1 to N replicas** for fault tolerance. Failover is automatic when a master becomes unreachable.

3. **Gossip Protocol:** Nodes continuously exchange **PING/PONG** messages containing their view of the cluster topology. This eventually-consistent gossip enables failure detection within ~1 second.

4. **Client-Side Routing:** Modern Redis clients (e.g., `redis-py-cluster`, `Jedis`) maintain a **slot-to-node map** and route commands directly to the correct master, bypassing any proxy.

**Distribution of 16,384 Hash Slots:**

The total slot range is split among all master nodes. For example, in a 3-node cluster:
- **Node-A:** slots $0$ to $5460$ (5461 slots)
- **Node-B:** slots $5461$ to $10922$ (5462 slots)
- **Node-C:** slots $10923$ to $16383$ (5461 slots)

The general allocation formula for $N$ masters is:
$$\text{slots per node} \approx \left\lfloor \frac{16384}{N} \right\rfloor \quad \text{with remainder distributed}$$

**Role of CRC16 and Hash Tags:**

Each key $k$ is mapped to a slot using the formula:
$$\text{slot}(k) = \text{CRC16}(k) \bmod 16384$$

The **CRC-16-CCITT** polynomial is $x^{16} + x^{12} + x^{5} + 1$, producing a 16-bit checksum with excellent avalanche properties.

**Hash Tags** are a special syntax `{tag}` that forces multiple keys with the same tag to map to the **same slot**:
- Key `user:{1001}:profile` and `user:{1001}:orders` both compute CRC16 of `1001` only.
- This enables **multi-key atomic operations** (e.g., `SUNION`, transactions) without forcing the entire keyspace to fit on one node.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Comparison: RDB vs. AOF Persistence**

| Parameter | **RDB (Redis Database)** | **AOF (Append Only File)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Point-in-time binary snapshot | Log of every write command |
| **Trigger** | Periodic (e.g., `save 60 1000`) | Every write (fsync policy controlled) |
| **File Format** | Compact binary `.rdb` | Human-readable RESP protocol text |
| **Durability** | Lower (data loss up to last snapshot) | Higher (up to 1-second loss with `everysec`) |
| **Performance** | Faster (fork + write) | Slower (log every op) |
| **Restart Time** | Fast (load binary) | Slower (replay log) |
| **File Size** | Smaller | Larger (rewrite compacts it) |
| **Use Case** | Backups, disaster recovery, cold starts | Financial data, audit logs |

**Workload Preference:**

- **Prefer RDB when:** the workload tolerates a few minutes of data loss, when periodic backups are sufficient, when the dataset is large (RDB restarts are much faster), and when CPU/disk I/O is constrained.
- **Prefer AOF when:** the workload demands near-zero data loss (e.g., financial ledgers, e-commerce orders), when audit compliance requires every write logged, and when disk capacity is not a constraint.

> [!TIP]
> **Recommended (Production Best Practice):** Enable **both** RDB and AOF ŌĆö Redis uses AOF as the source of truth on restart for maximum durability, with RDB as a fast warm-start fallback.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Do **not** confuse **replication** with **persistence** ŌĆö replication copies data to other nodes for availability, persistence saves data to disk for durability.
> 2. Do **not** state that RDB is "always faster" ŌĆö under write-heavy workloads, AOF with `appendfsync no` can actually be faster because RDB requires a `fork()` syscall that blocks the main thread.

---

### ŌØæ Question B ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024, S1 Alternative]`

**Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Discuss the data structures supported by Redis with suitable use cases. Why is Redis considered more than a simple Key-Value store?

**Q (b) [7 Marks]:** Explain the CAP theorem classification of Redis. How does Redis handle consistency, availability, and partition tolerance in single-node and clustered modes?

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Redis Data Structures and Use Cases:**

Redis provides **nine** core data structures, each optimized for a specific access pattern:

| Data Structure | Internal Encoding | Typical Use Case | Example Command |
|---|---|---|---|
| **String** | SDS (Simple Dynamic String) | Caching, counters, locks | `SET key value`, `INCR counter` |
| **Hash** | listpack / hashtable | User profiles, object storage | `HSET user:1 name "Alice"` |
| **List** | quicklist (linked list of ziplists) | Message queues, activity feeds | `LPUSH`, `RPOP` |
| **Set** | intset / hashtable | Tags, unique visitors, dedup | `SADD`, `SINTER` |
| **Sorted Set (ZSet)** | skiplist + hashtable | Leaderboards, rate limiters, priority queues | `ZADD`, `ZRANGEBYSCORE` |
| **Stream** | radix tree (RID-based) | Event sourcing, log aggregation, Kafka-lite | `XADD`, `XREADGROUP` |
| **Bitmap** | string of bits | Daily active users, feature flags | `SETBIT`, `BITCOUNT` |
| **HyperLogLog** | probabilistic HLL | Unique visitor counts (cardinality) | `PFADD`, `PFCOUNT` |
| **Geospatial** | sorted set with GeoHash | Find nearby restaurants, ride-sharing | `GEOADD`, `GEOSEARCH` |

**Why Redis is more than a simple Key-Value store:**

A pure Key-Value store like **Memcached** treats values as opaque byte strings ŌĆö the database has no semantic understanding of the content. Redis, in contrast:

1. **Understands the structure** of its values (it knows whether a value is a list, a hash, or a sorted set), enabling **atomic structural operations** like `LPUSH`, `ZADD`, etc.
2. Supports **server-side scripting** via Lua, allowing complex logic to execute atomically inside the database.
3. Provides **pub/sub messaging** natively, blurring the line with message brokers.
4. Implements **transactions** (`MULTI/EXEC`) and **optimistic locking** (`WATCH`), traditionally features of relational DBs.
5. Offers **streams** with consumer groups (Redis 5.0+), a feature usually found in dedicated streaming systems.

For these reasons, the official Redis documentation re-classifies itself as a **"data structure server"** rather than a mere key-value store.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**CAP Theorem Classification of Redis:**

The **CAP Theorem** (proposed by Eric Brewer in 2000) states that a distributed data store can simultaneously provide at most two of the three guarantees: **Consistency (C)**, **Availability (A)**, and **Partition tolerance (P)**.

**Single-Node Redis:**

When Redis runs on a single instance:
- **Consistency:** Fully guaranteed ŌĆö every `GET` returns the value of the most recent `SET`.
- **Availability:** Guaranteed as long as the single node is running.
- **Partition Tolerance:** Not applicable (no network partitions in a single node).

Hence, a single-node Redis is a **CA system** ŌĆö both Consistency and Availability are trivially achieved in the absence of partitions.

**Clustered Redis (Redis Cluster):**

In a multi-node cluster, network partitions are inevitable. Redis Cluster therefore makes explicit trade-offs:

- **Partition Tolerance (P):** Always guaranteed ŌĆö the cluster continues operating even if some nodes lose connectivity.
- **Availability (A):** Guaranteed ŌĆö the cluster accepts reads and writes from any reachable node.
- **Consistency (C):** Sacrificed to **eventual consistency** ŌĆö writes to a master are acknowledged immediately, but replicas receive the updates asynchronously. A read from a stale replica may return older data within the **replication lag window** (typically tens of milliseconds, but can be longer under network stress).

Thus, Redis Cluster is classified as an **AP system**.

**Mitigation of Stale Reads:**

To regain strong consistency in cluster mode, Redis provides:
- **`WAIT numreplicas timeout` command** ŌĆö blocks the client until the write has been replicated to at least $N$ replicas, providing a bounded-staleness guarantee.
- **Read-from-master configuration** ŌĆö clients can be configured to always read from masters (which have the latest data) at the cost of load distribution.
- **Redis Sentinel with quorum-based failover** ŌĆö minimizes the window during which a replica is promoted and the cluster is briefly inconsistent.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Do **not** claim that Redis Cluster is "always eventually consistent" ŌĆö under specific configurations (`WAIT`, synchronous replication), Redis can provide strong consistency at the cost of latency.
> 2. The CAP theorem applies **only during a network partition** ŌĆö in steady-state operation, a system can provide all three. Examiners penalize students who treat CAP as a permanent classification rather than a **conditional trade-off**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Checklist ŌĆö Memorize Before Exam:**

- Ō£ģ A **Key-Value database** stores data as `(key, value)` pairs with a **schema-less, hash-based, in-memory** architecture.
- Ō£ģ The **CAP classification** of a single-node Redis is **CA**; a Redis Cluster is **AP** with eventual consistency.
- Ō£ģ The slot formula is **$\text{slot} = \text{CRC16}(k) \bmod 16384$** ŌĆö a 14-bit modular operation over a 16-bit CRC checksum.
- Ō£ģ Redis supports **9 data structures**: String, Hash, List, Set, Sorted Set, Stream, Bitmap, HyperLogLog, Geospatial.
- Ō£ģ The **two persistence mechanisms** are **RDB** (binary snapshot) and **AOF** (write-ahead log). Production deployments typically enable **both**.
- Ō£ģ **Lazy expiration** triggers on key access; **active expiration** is a 100 ms background sampler that evicts 20 keys per cycle.
- Ō£ģ Redis uses a **single-threaded event loop** for command execution, ensuring **atomic per-command execution** without locks.
- Ō£ģ The standard **TTL commands** are `EXPIRE` (seconds) and `PEXPIRE` (milliseconds); use `TTL key` to query remaining lifetime.
- Ō£ģ **Hash tags** `{}` force keys with the same tag substring to colocate on one slot, enabling multi-key operations in a distributed setup.
- Ō£ģ **Redis Sentinel** handles **automatic failover**, **monitoring**, and **notification**; **Redis Cluster** handles **sharding + replication**.
- Ō£ģ Typical **latency** of Redis on a LAN: **< 1 ms** (in-memory) and **5ŌĆō10 ms** (with disk persistence enabled).
- Ō£ģ The **maximum value size** for a single Redis string is **512 MB**; the recommended maximum is **much smaller** to avoid blocking the event loop.
- Ō£ģ **Replication lag** $\Delta t$ in asynchronous mode is typically **< 1 second** on a healthy network, but can grow under load.
- Ō£ģ The Redis license is **BSD-3-Clause** (open source, commercial-friendly).
- Ō£ģ In the exam, **always justify** your choice between RDB and AOF using the workload characteristics (durability vs. performance vs. restart time).
- Ō£ģ **Bloom filter** is NOT a native Redis data structure but is implementable using `BITOP` on bitmaps.
- Ō£ģ Redis **pub/sub** is **fire-and-forget** ŌĆö disconnected clients miss messages. For reliable messaging, use **Redis Streams** with consumer groups.
- Ō£ģ **Transactions** in Redis (`MULTI/EXEC`) are **NOT** the same as RDBMS ACID transactions ŌĆö they guarantee no interleaving of other clients' commands but offer **no rollback** on individual command failures.
- Ō£ģ The **LRU eviction** in Redis is **approximate** (sampled), not exact ŌĆö it runs in **O(1) per operation** using a small eviction pool.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Mnemonic:** *"Keys are LITE ŌĆö **L**ookup O(1), **I**n-memory, **T**TL, **E**ventual consistency."* ŌĆö Use this in your introduction paragraph to instantly demonstrate command of the topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Document DB (Examples from MongoDB)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Document DB (Examples from MongoDB)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ­¤ōś SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Document Database (Document-Oriented Database / Document Store)**
> A **Document Database** is a type of NoSQL (Not Only SQL) database designed to store, retrieve, and manage semi-structured data as **documents**, typically encoded in **JSON (JavaScript Object Notation)** or its binary form **BSON (Binary JSON)**. Each document is a self-contained unit containing both data values (fields) and structural metadata (key-value pairs), grouped into **collections** rather than rigid tables. Document databases are **schema-flexible**, **horizontally scalable**, and map naturally to object-oriented programming constructs.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBEOT504 ŌĆō DBMS, Module 4)**, the syllabus lists:
- **Document DB** as one of the four major NoSQL data models (alongside Key-Value, Column-Family, and Graph).
- **MongoDB** as the canonical textbook example of a document-oriented database.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **­¤ō” The "Smart Folder" Analogy**
> Imagine a **traditional RDBMS** as a **filing cabinet** with rigid, pre-labeled drawers and fixed dividers (tables, columns, types). Every file you store must be cut to fit the drawer exactly ŌĆö same size, same labels, same folders.
>
> Now imagine a **Document Database** as a **set of manila folders**, where each folder (document) can have its **own internal structure, tabs, attachments, and notes**. One folder might contain photos, receipts, and letters. Another folder might contain only a single business card. You can still group folders into **boxes** (collections), but you are not forced into a uniform shape.
>
> **MongoDB** is essentially a massive, indexed, searchable, distributed set of these smart folders ŌĆö accessible by content, by tag, or by any field, and replicated across servers for fault tolerance.

## 1.3 Why Document DBs Exist ŌĆö The Motivation

| Driver | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Object-Relational Impedance Mismatch** | Developers work with objects/JSON; RDBMS forces them into rows and JOINs. |
| **Schema Agility** | Modern apps (mobile, IoT, social) evolve rapidly; rigid ALTER TABLE is painful. |
| **Horizontal Scalability** | RDBMS scales vertically (bigger server); document DBs scale **out** (more commodity servers). |
| **Heterogeneous Data** | A single collection can hold documents with different fields and structures. |
| **Cloud-Native Architecture** | Microservices prefer independent, JSON-native datastores. |

## 1.4 Core Terminology Mapping (RDBMS Ōåö Document DB)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Table for KTU Exams ŌĆö High Yield**

| RDBMS Concept | MongoDB (Document DB) Equivalent | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Database | Database | Container for collections |
| Table | **Collection** | Schema-less group of documents |
| Row / Record | **Document** | Encoded as BSON/JSON |
| Column | **Field** | Key-value pair inside a document |
| Primary Key | **`_id` field** | Auto-generated `ObjectId` by default |
| JOIN | **Embedded documents** / `$lookup` | No joins by default; de-normalization is encouraged |
| Schema | **Schema validation rules** (optional) | Truly schema-less by default |
| Index | Index | Supports B-tree, text, geospatial, hashed |

## 1.5 MongoDB ŌĆö The Industry Standard Document DB

> [!NOTE]
> **MongoDB** (from "**hu**mongo**us**") was released in **2009** by MongoDB Inc. It is an **open-source**, **document-oriented**, **NoSQL** database written primarily in **C++**. It uses a custom binary serialization format called **BSON** (Binary JSON), supports **secondary indexes**, **replication** (Replica Sets), **automatic sharding**, **aggregation pipelines**, and **ACID transactions** (since v4.0 for multi-document).

**Key Features at a Glance:**
- **Document Model:** BSON documents up to **16 MB** per document (BSON max size).
- **Query Language:** MQL (MongoDB Query Language) ŌĆö JSON-like syntax.
- **Storage Engine:** WiredTiger (default since 3.2) with document-level concurrency.
- **Replication:** Replica Sets with automatic failover and primary election.
- **Sharding:** Horizontal partitioning across shards using a shard key.
- **Aggregation Framework:** Pipeline-based data transformation (similar to Unix pipes).
- **Atlas:** Fully managed cloud service (AWS, Azure, GCP).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ­¤ōŚ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a BSON Document

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BSON (Binary JSON)** is MongoDB's wire and storage format. It extends JSON with additional types such as `int`, `long`, `double`, `decimal128`, `Date`, `ObjectId`, `Binary`, and `Timestamp`.

### 2.1.1 Sample Student Document
```json
{
  "_id": ObjectId("507f1f77bcf86cd799439011"),
  "name": "Ananya Krishnan",
  "roll_no": "KTU2024CS101",
  "year": 2,
  "cgpa": 8.74,
  "active": true,
  "enrolled_on": ISODate("2024-08-01T00:00:00Z"),
  "address": {
    "street": "MG Road",
    "city": "Kochi",
    "state": "Kerala",
    "pin": 682011
  },
  "skills": ["Python", "MongoDB", "React"],
  "projects": [
    {"title": "Weather API", "tech": "Flask"},
    {"title": "DBMS Notes", "tech": "LaTeX"}
  ]
}
```

### 2.1.2 Key Observations
1. **Self-describing** ŌĆö field names travel with each document.
2. **Nested objects** allowed (e.g., `address` is an embedded sub-document).
3. **Arrays** allowed (e.g., `skills`, `projects`).
4. **Mixed types** possible across documents in same collection.
5. **`_id`** ŌĆö mandatory 12-byte unique identifier (4-byte timestamp + 5-byte random + 3-byte counter).

## 2.2 CRUD Operations ŌĆö The Core of MongoDB

> [!NOTE]
> **CRUD** = **C**reate, **R**ead, **U**pdate, **D**elete. Every document DB exam question will test this.

### 2.2.1 Create (Insert)

```javascript
// Insert a single document
db.students.insertOne({
  name: "Rahul M",
  roll_no: "KTU2024CS102",
  year: 2,
  cgpa: 7.9
})

// Insert multiple documents
db.students.insertMany([
  { name: "Priya S", roll_no: "KTU2024CS103", year: 2, cgpa: 9.1 },
  { name: "Arjun N", roll_no: "KTU2024CS104", year: 2, cgpa: 8.0 }
])
```

### 2.2.2 Read (Query / Find)

```javascript
// Equality match
db.students.find({ year: 2 })

// Projection ŌĆö return only specific fields
db.students.find(
  { year: 2 },
  { name: 1, cgpa: 1, _id: 0 }
)

// Operators
db.students.find({ cgpa: { $gt: 8.0 } })           // greater than
db.students.find({ name: { $in: ["Ananya", "Priya"] } })
db.students.find({ "address.city": "Kochi" })      // dot notation (nested field)
```

### 2.2.3 Update

```javascript
// Update a single document
db.students.updateOne(
  { roll_no: "KTU2024CS102" },                    // filter
  { $set: { cgpa: 8.2 }, $push: { skills: "SQL" } } // update operators
)

// Update many
db.students.updateMany(
  { year: 2 },
  { $inc: { cgpa: 0.1 } }                         // increment
)
```

### 2.2.4 Delete

```javascript
db.students.deleteOne({ roll_no: "KTU2024CS102" })
db.students.deleteMany({ year: 1 })
```

## 2.3 Query Operators ŌĆö The High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU examiners love operator-based questions. Memorize the categories below.**

| Category | Operator | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Comparison** | `$eq` | Equal to | `{cgpa: {$eq: 8.5}}` or `{cgpa: 8.5}` |
| | `$ne` | Not equal | `{year: {$ne: 4}}` |
| | `$gt`, `$gte` | Greater than (or equal) | `{cgpa: {$gt: 8.0}}` |
| | `$lt`, `$lte` | Less than (or equal) | `{cgpa: {$lt: 7.0}}` |
| | `$in` | In array | `{city: {$in: ["Kochi", "Trivandrum"]}}` |
| | `$nin` | Not in array | `{city: {$nin: ["Delhi"]}}` |
| **Logical** | `$and` | Logical AND | `{$and: [{year: 2}, {cgpa: {$gt: 8}}]}` |
| | `$or` | Logical OR | `{$or: [{year: 1}, {year: 2}]}` |
| | `$not` | Negation | `{cgpa: {$not: {$gt: 8}}}` |
| | `$nor` | NOT (A or B) | `{$nor: [{year: 1}, {cgpa: {$lt: 6}}]}` |
| **Element** | `$exists` | Field exists? | `{middle_name: {$exists: true}}` |
| | `$type` | BSON type match | `{age: {$type: "int"}}` |
| **Array** | `$all` | Contains all | `{skills: {$all: ["Python", "SQL"]}}` |
| | `$size` | Array length | `{skills: {$size: 3}}` |
| | `$elemMatch` | Match in array element | `{projects: {$elemMatch: {tech: "React"}}}` |
| **Evaluation** | `$regex` | Pattern match | `{name: {$regex: "^A", $options: "i"}}` |
| | `$mod` | Modulo | `{age: {$mod: [5, 0]}}` |
| **Update** | `$set` | Set field | `{$set: {cgpa: 9.0}}` |
| | `$unset` | Remove field | `{$unset: {middle_name: ""}}` |
| | `$inc` | Increment | `{$inc: {age: 1}}` |
| | `$push` | Append to array | `{$push: {skills: "Go"}}` |
| | `$pull` | Remove from array | `{$pull: {skills: "Java"}}` |
| | `$addToSet` | Append unique | `{$addToSet: {skills: "Rust"}}` |

## 2.4 Aggregation Framework ŌĆö Pipeline Processing

> [!NOTE]
> The **Aggregation Pipeline** is MongoDB's data transformation engine. Documents enter a pipeline of **stages**, each transforming the stream and passing it forward ŌĆö conceptually identical to **Unix pipes** or **map-reduce** chains.

```javascript
db.students.aggregate([
  { $match: { year: 2 } },              // Filter
  { $group: {                            // Group
      _id: "$address.city",
      avg_cgpa: { $avg: "$cgpa" },
      count:   { $sum: 1 }
  }},
  { $sort: { avg_cgpa: -1 } },          // Sort descending
  { $limit: 5 }                          // Top 5 cities
])
```

**Common Stages:**

| Stage | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `$match` | Filter documents (like WHERE) |
| `$project` | Reshape documents (like SELECT) |
| `$group` | Aggregate by key (like GROUP BY) |
| `$sort` | Order results |
| `$limit` / `$skip` | Pagination |
| `$unwind` | Deconstruct array field into separate documents |
| `$lookup` | Left outer join with another collection |
| `$out` | Write pipeline result to a collection |

## 2.5 Indexing in MongoDB

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Without indexes, MongoDB performs a **collection scan** (examines every document). Indexes use **B-tree** data structures.

```javascript
// Single field index
db.students.createIndex({ roll_no: 1 })           // 1 = ascending

// Compound index
db.students.createIndex({ year: 1, cgpa: -1 })

// Unique index
db.students.createIndex({ roll_no: 1 }, { unique: true })

// Text index (full-text search)
db.students.createIndex({ name: "text" })

// Geospatial index
db.places.createIndex({ location: "2dsphere" })

// Hashed index (for sharding)
db.students.createIndex({ _id: "hashed" })
```

**Index Types Summary:**

| Type | Best For |
|---|---|
| **Single Field** | Queries on one column |
| **Compound** | Multi-field queries (order matters ŌĆö **ESR rule**: Equality, Sort, Range) |
| **Multikey** | Indexing array fields |
| **Text** | Full-text search |
| **2dsphere / 2d** | Geospatial queries (near, within polygon) |
| **Hashed** | Shard key distribution |
| **TTL** | Auto-expire documents (logs, sessions) |

## 2.6 Replication ŌĆö Replica Sets

> [!NOTE]
> A **Replica Set** is a group of `mongod` processes that maintain the same dataset. It provides **high availability** and **data redundancy**.

```
   ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ
   Ōöé  PRIMARY Ōöé  ŌåÉ All writes
   ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌö¼ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś
        Ōöé oplog replication
   ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌö┤ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ
   Ōöé          Ōöé
ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢╝ŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ   ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢╝ŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ
ŌöéSEC 1Ōöé   ŌöéSEC 2 Ōöé   ŌåÉ Read scaling (optionally)
ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś   ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś
```

- **Primary** receives all writes, replicates via the **oplog** (operations log).
- **Secondaries** replicate the primary's oplog and can serve reads (if configured).
- If the primary fails, an **election** is held; a secondary becomes primary automatically (typically within **10ŌĆō12 seconds**).
- Minimum recommended: **3 members** (1 primary + 2 secondaries), preferably on different failure zones.

## 2.7 Sharding ŌĆö Horizontal Partitioning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sharding** is MongoDB's strategy for **horizontal scaling**: partitioning a collection across multiple machines (shards). Each shard holds a subset of the data.

**Components:**
1. **Shard** ŌĆö a `mongod` (or replica set) holding a portion of data.
2. **Config Server** ŌĆö stores metadata and chunk mapping (`mongod --configsvr`).
3. **Mongos** ŌĆö query router; fronts the application; routes queries to appropriate shards.

**Shard Key Selection** is critical:
- **High cardinality** (many distinct values).
- **Low frequency** (even distribution).
- **Avoid monotonic keys** (e.g., `_id` with timestamp causes hot-spotting).

**Sharding Strategies:**
- **Range-based** ŌĆö splits data by value ranges (good for range queries, can be uneven).
- **Hash-based** ŌĆö applies hash function to shard key (excellent distribution, bad for range queries).
- **Zone / Tag-aware** ŌĆö geographically distribute data (e.g., EU users in EU shard).

## 2.8 Storage Engine ŌĆö WiredTiger

> [!NOTE]
> Since MongoDB **3.2**, the default storage engine is **WiredTiger**.
- **Document-level concurrency** (MMAPv1 was collection-level).
- **Compression**: snappy (default), zlib, zstd.
- **Encryption at rest** (optional, requires WiredTiger).
- **Checkpointing** every **60 seconds** (configurable) for crash recovery.
- Uses **write-ahead logging (WAL)** equivalent via journal files.

## 2.9 ACID Transactions in MongoDB

| Version | Capability |
|---|---|
| MongoDB **4.0** | Multi-document ACID transactions in **replica sets** |
| MongoDB **4.2** | Multi-document ACID transactions in **sharded clusters** |
| MongoDB **4.4+** | Read concern majority, causal consistency |

```javascript
const session = db.getMongo().startSession();
session.startTransaction();
try {
  session.getDatabase("bank").accounts.updateOne(
    { _id: "A1" }, { $inc: { balance: -100 } }
  );
  session.getDatabase("bank").accounts.updateOne(
    { _id: "A2" }, { $inc: { balance: 100 } }
  );
  session.commitTransaction();
} catch (e) {
  session.abortTransaction();
} finally {
  session.endSession();
}
```

## 2.10 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maximum-yield reference for Part-A and Part-B questions.**

| Concept | Key Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| BSON Max Document Size | **16 MB** | Per document, hard limit |
| Replica Set Members | **Minimum 3** (1P + 2S) | For HA; max 50 members, max 7 voting |
| Replica Election Timeout | **10ŌĆō12 seconds** | Default `electionTimeoutMillis: 10000` |
| WiredTiger Checkpoint | **60 seconds** | Configurable via `checkpoint=(wait=60)` |
| Primary Stepdown | **10 seconds** default | To trigger election |
| Index Direction | `1` ascending, `-1` descending | Affects sort order usage |
| TTL Purge | **60 seconds** background thread | Removes expired docs |
| Write Concern Majority | `w: "majority"` | Durability across replica set |
| ObjectId Components | **4-byte ts + 5-byte rand + 3-byte counter** = 12 bytes | Unique + roughly time-ordered |

## 2.11 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Domain | Use Case |
|---|---|
| **E-commerce (Flipkart, Myntra)** | Product catalogs with varying attributes (clothing vs electronics). |
| **IoT (Siemens, Bosch)** | Telemetry from millions of devices, each with different sensor signatures. |
| **Content Management** | Articles, blogs, multimedia metadata ŌĆö flexible schema. |
| **Mobile Apps (Facebook, Instagram)** | User profiles, posts, comments ŌĆö nested, evolving structures. |
| **Gaming (EA, Ubisoft)** | Player inventories, leaderboards, session state. |
| **Real-time Analytics** | Event log aggregation, time-series storage. |
| **Catalog APIs (Wattpad, Airbnb)** | Listings with heterogeneous features. |

## 2.12 Document DB vs RDBMS ŌĆö Conceptual Trade-offs

| Aspect | RDBMS | Document DB (MongoDB) |
|---|---|---|
| **Schema** | Rigid, predefined | Flexible, dynamic |
| **Scaling** | Vertical (scale-up) | Horizontal (scale-out) |
| **Data Model** | Tables, rows, columns | JSON/BSON documents |
| **Relationships** | Foreign keys, JOINs | Embedding, `$lookup` |
| **Transactions** | Full ACID, multi-row | Multi-doc ACID (since 4.0) |
| **Consistency** | Strong by default | Tunable (read/write concern) |
| **Query Power** | SQL ŌĆö rich joins, analytics | MQL ŌĆö rich, no joins by default |
| **Best For** | Structured, transactional, BI | Semi-structured, agile, cloud-native |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Exam Trap:** Students often answer "MongoDB doesn't support ACID". This is **false since v4.0**. State: *"MongoDB supports multi-document ACID transactions in replica sets (since 4.0) and sharded clusters (since 4.2)."*

> [!NOTE]
> Since "Document DB" is a **conceptual + system-design** topic, "derivations" here mean **systematic explanations of underlying mechanisms** (ObjectId generation, oplog replication, sharding math, aggregation algebra, and BSON size). All mathematical/computational walkthroughs are exhaustively shown.

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: ObjectId Construction (12-byte UUID)

The `_id` field in MongoDB defaults to a **12-byte `ObjectId`**. Let us derive its structure.

### 3.1.1 Structure
$$
\text{ObjectId} = \underbrace{4 \text{ bytes}}_{\text{Timestamp}} \;+\; \underbrace{5 \text{ bytes}}_{\text{Random value}} \;+\; \underbrace{3 \text{ bytes}}_{\text{Counter}}
$$

### 3.1.2 Worked Derivation
Suppose we generate an ObjectId for a document created on **1 Jan 2025, 00:00:00 UTC**.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Timestamp (4 bytes)**
$$
\text{Timestamp} = \lfloor \text{Unix\_time in seconds} \rfloor
$$
$$
\text{Unix\_time} = 1735689600 \text{ (seconds since 1 Jan 1970)}
$$
In hex:
$$
1735689600 \rightarrow \text{0x677D3C00}
$$
Encoded as 4 bytes: `67 7D 3C 00`

**Step 2 ŌĆö Random Value (5 bytes)**
Generated once per process (not per document) ŌĆö typically from a cryptographically secure RNG.
Example (hex): `A1 B2 C3 D4 E5`

**Step 3 ŌĆö Counter (3 bytes)**
Auto-incrementing integer, starts at a random value, increments per document on the same process.
Example (hex): `00 00 01`

**Step 4 ŌĆö Concatenate**
$$
\text{ObjectId (hex)} = \underbrace{\text{677D3C00}}_{\text{ts}} \cdot \underbrace{\text{A1B2C3D4E5}}_{\text{rnd}} \cdot \underbrace{\text{000001}}_{\text{ctr}}
$$
$$
= \texttt{"677D3CA1B2C3D4E5000001"}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö String form**
MongoDB renders it as a 24-character hex string:
```
507f1f77bcf86cd799439011
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Because the timestamp is the **leading 4 bytes**, ObjectIds are **roughly monotonically increasing** ŌĆö enabling efficient **time-range queries** on `_id` without a separate timestamp index.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: BSON Document Size Limit

### 3.2.1 The 16 MB Limit
MongoDB enforces a hard limit of **16,777,216 bytes (16 MiB)** per BSON document.

### 3.2.2 Why 16 MB?
- The BSON spec uses a **32-bit (4-byte) signed integer** for the document length prefix.
- Max value of 32-bit signed int:
$$
2^{31} - 1 = 2{,}147{,}483{,}647 \text{ bytes}
$$
- But practically constrained to **16 MB** to:
  1. Keep documents addressable in single memory pages.
  2. Avoid abuse of embedding instead of referencing.
  3. Bound query/transfer overhead.

### 3.2.3 Calculation: Does My Document Fit?
Suppose we want to store a blog post with 1000 comments, each averaging 1 KB.

$$
\text{Total size} = \text{Post body} + \sum_{i=1}^{1000} \text{Comment}_i
$$
$$
= 5\,\text{KB (post)} + 1000 \times 1\,\text{KB} = 1005\,\text{KB} \approx 1\,\text{MB}
$$
$$
1\,\text{MB} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ bytes} \;<\; 16\,\text{MB} = 16{,}777{,}216 \text{ bytes} \;\; Ō£ģ
$$

But for 20,000 comments:
$$
20{,}000 \times 1\,\text{KB} = 20{,}000\,\text{KB} \approx 19.5\,\text{MB} \;>\; 16\,\text{MB} \;\; ŌØī
$$
**Solution:** Use the **Bucket Pattern** ŌĆö split comments into N documents of 1000 each (using an array of 1000 comments per bucket).

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{20{,}000}{1{,}000} \right\rceil = 20 \text{ bucket documents}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Shard Key Distribution Math

### 3.3.1 Problem
A `users` collection has **10 million** documents. We shard on `country` (high cardinality: 195 countries).

### 3.3.2 Step 1: Verify Shard Key Viability
- **Cardinality test:** 195 distinct values ŌåÆ **acceptable** (though low).
- **Frequency test:** Most users in India/USA ŌåÆ **uneven distribution risk**.
- **Hot-spot test:** Range queries on `country` are common ŌåÆ **good fit for range sharding**.

### 3.3.3 Step 2: Chunk Sizing
Default chunk size in MongoDB: **64 MB**.
Suppose average document size: **2 KB**.
$$
\text{Docs per chunk} = \frac{64 \times 1024 \text{ KB}}{2 \text{ KB}} = 32{,}768 \text{ documents}
$$
$$
\text{Total chunks needed} = \frac{10{,}000{,}000}{32{,}768} \approx 305 \text{ chunks}
$$
$$
\text{Minimum shards} = \left\lceil \frac{305}{? \text{ per shard}} \right\rceil
$$
With 4 shards (typical 3-shard + 1 spare, or balanced 305/4 Ōēł 76 chunks each), distribution is feasible.

### 3.3.4 Step 3: Hash Sharding Alternative
Hashing `country` gives **uniform distribution** but breaks range query efficiency.

$$
\text{hash}(\text{"India"}) = \text{consistent\_hash}(\text{"India"}) \mod \text{numShards}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off rule:**
> - **Range queries** ŌåÆ Range sharding
> - **Write throughput / uniform distribution** ŌåÆ Hash sharding

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Aggregation Pipeline (Detailed Walkthrough)

**Input Collection `orders`:**
```json
[
  { "_id": 1, "customer": "Alice", "amount": 250, "status": "paid",    "category": "books"  },
  { "_id": 2, "customer": "Bob",   "amount": 100, "status": "paid",    "category": "games"  },
  { "_id": 3, "customer": "Alice", "amount": 400, "status": "refunded","category": "books"  },
  { "_id": 4, "customer": "Bob",   "amount": 150, "status": "paid",    "category": "books"  },
  { "_id": 5, "customer": "Clara", "amount": 300, "status": "paid",    "category": "games"  }
]
```

**Pipeline:**
```javascript
db.orders.aggregate([
  { $match:   { status: "paid" } },                       // Stage 1
  { $group: {                                             // Stage 2
      _id: "$customer",
      total_spent: { $sum: "$amount" },
      order_count: { $sum: 1 },
      avg_ticket:  { $avg: "$amount" }
  }},
  { $sort:   { total_spent: -1 } },                       // Stage 3
  { $project: {                                           // Stage 4
      customer: "$_id",
      _id: 0,
      total_spent: 1,
      order_count: 1,
      avg_ticket: { $round: ["$avg_ticket", 2] }
  }}
])
```

### 3.4.1 Stage-by-Stage Output Trace

**After Stage 1 ŌĆö `$match: { status: "paid" }`:**
```json
[
  { "_id": 1, "customer": "Alice", "amount": 250, "status": "paid", "category": "books" },
  { "_id": 2, "customer": "Bob",   "amount": 100, "status": "paid", "category": "games" },
  { "_id": 4, "customer": "Bob",   "amount": 150, "status": "paid", "category": "books" },
  { "_id": 5, "customer": "Clara", "amount": 300, "status": "paid", "category": "games" }
]
```
(Excluded: `_id: 3` because `status = "refunded"`.)

**After Stage 2 ŌĆö `$group` by `customer`:**

| `_id` (customer) | `total_spent` | `order_count` | `avg_ticket` |
|---|---|---|---|
| Alice | 250 | 1 | 250.0 |
| Bob   | 100 + 150 = **250** | **2** | **125.0** |
| Clara | 300 | 1 | 300.0 |

**After Stage 3 ŌĆö `$sort: { total_spent: -1 }`:**
1. Clara ŌĆö 300
2. Alice ŌĆö 250
3. Bob ŌĆö 250
(Bob and Alice tied; stable order depends on internal implementation.)

**After Stage 4 ŌĆö `$project` with `$round`:**
```json
[
  { "customer": "Clara", "total_spent": 300, "order_count": 1, "avg_ticket": 300.0 },
  { "customer": "Alice", "total_spent": 250, "order_count": 1, "avg_ticket": 250.0 },
  { "customer": "Bob",   "total_spent": 250, "order_count": 2, "avg_ticket": 125.0 }
]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This walkthrough is **exactly** the kind of trace a KTU Part-B 14-mark question will expect.

## 3.5 Worked Example 5: Index Selectivity & ESR Rule

### 3.5.1 The ESR Rule (Equality, Sort, Range)
When designing a **compound index**, field order follows **E ŌåÆ S ŌåÆ R**:
1. **Equality** fields first (e.g., `{year: 2}`)
2. **Sort** fields next (e.g., `{cgpa: -1}`)
3. **Range** fields last (e.g., `{age: {$gt: 20}}`)

### 3.5.2 Query
```javascript
db.students.find({ year: 2, age: { $gt: 20 } })
  .sort({ cgpa: -1 })
```

### 3.5.3 Optimal Compound Index
```javascript
db.students.createIndex({ year: 1, cgpa: -1, age: 1 })
```
- `year` = equality ŌåÆ first Ō£ģ
- `cgpa` = sort ŌåÆ second Ō£ģ
- `age` = range ŌåÆ third Ō£ģ

**Bad design** (range before sort): `{ year: 1, age: 1, cgpa: -1 }` ŌĆö the sort cannot use the index after the range filter.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: When to Embed vs Reference

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Most-tested KTU design question: "Embed or Reference?"**

| Criterion | Embed (Sub-documents) | Reference (Separate Collection) |
|---|---|---|
| **1-to-1 relationship** | Ō£ģ Embed | Possible |
| **1-to-few** (e.g., addresses Ōēż 5) | Ō£ģ Embed | Overkill |
| **1-to-many** (e.g., 1000s of comments) | ŌØī (hits 16MB) | Ō£ģ Reference |
| **Many-to-many** | ŌØī | Ō£ģ Reference with `$lookup` |
| **Read together always?** | Ō£ģ Embed (atomic, single read) | Multiple reads needed |
| **Independent updates** | ŌØī (re-write whole doc) | Ō£ģ Reference |
| **Document size** | Small bounded | Can grow unboundedly |
| **Atomicity** | Single-document atomicity guaranteed | Multi-doc needs transactions |

**Rule of thumb (the "Rule of Three"):**
- If cardinality Ōēż 3 and bounded, **embed**.
- If unbounded or accessed independently, **reference**.

---

## 3.7 Worked Example 6: Write Concern & Durability Math

### 3.7.1 Problem
A 5-member replica set (1 primary + 4 secondaries). Client issues `insert` with `w: "majority"`.

### 3.7.2 Calculation of Majority
$$
\text{Majority} = \left\lfloor \frac{N}{2} \right\rfloor + 1
$$
$$
\text{Majority} = \left\lfloor \frac{5}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = 2 + 1 = 3
$$
So the write is acknowledged only after **3 members** (primary + 2 secondaries) durably persist it.

### 3.7.3 Trade-off
- `w: 1` ŌåÆ fastest, but data loss risk if primary fails before replication.
- `w: "majority"` ŌåÆ safer, used in critical systems (banking, inventory).
- `w: N` (e.g., `w: 5`) ŌåÆ most durable, slowest.

---

## 3.8 Analytical Matrix: Document DB Properties (CAP & BASE)

| Property | Description | Document DB Implementation |
|---|---|---|
| **CAP** | Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance | MongoDB chooses **CP** by default (tunable to AP via read preferences) |
| **BASE** | Basically Available, Soft state, Eventually consistent | Default reads from primary ŌåÆ strong consistency; secondary reads ŌåÆ eventual |
| **ACID** | Atomic, Consistent, Isolated, Durable | Single-doc ACID always; multi-doc ACID since 4.0 |
| **Schemaless** | No predefined schema | Schema validation optional since 3.2 |
| **Distributed** | Multi-node, sharded | Yes, with auto-balancing |

## 3.9 Worked Example 7: Schema Design ŌĆö Blog System

### 3.9.1 Naive Embedding (Hits Limit)
```json
{
  "_id": 1,
  "title": "DBMS Notes",
  "comments": [ /* ...20,000 comments... */ ]   // Hits 16 MB
}
```

### 3.9.2 Bucket Pattern (Optimal)
Split comments into buckets of 100:
```json
// post document
{
  "_id": 1,
  "title": "DBMS Notes",
  "total_comments": 20000
}

// comment bucket documents
{
  "_id": "1_0",   // postId_bucketIndex
  "postId": 1,
  "comments": [ /* 100 comments */ ]
}
```
Number of bucket documents:
$$
N_{\text{buckets}} = \left\lceil \frac{20{,}000}{100} \right\rceil = 200
$$

### 3.9.3 Why This Works
- Each bucket Ōēż **100 comments ├Ś ~500 bytes** = **~50 KB** Ōē¬ 16 MB Ō£ģ
- Pagination becomes a query on `_id`: `{"postId": 1, "_id": {$gte: "1_50"}}` ŌåÆ bucket 50 onward.
- Inserts are append-only ŌĆö write hot-spot avoided by `_id: {postId, timestamp}`.

|---|---|
| Hex / Binary representation of ObjectId | Understand primary key generation |

## 4.5 Replica Set Election & Replication

