```mermaid
    participant App as Application
    participant P as Primary
    participant S1 as Secondary 1
    participant S2 as Secondary 2
    App->>P: Write (insert)
    P->>P: Apply to memory + oplog
    P-->>S1: Replicate oplog
    P-->>S2: Replicate oplog
    S1->>S1: Apply oplog
    S2->>S2: Apply oplog
    S1-->>P: ACK
    S2-->>P: ACK
    P-->>App: Write Acknowledged w=majority
    Note over P,S2: Primary fails
    S1->>S1: Heartbeat timeout (10s)
    S1->>S2: Election request
    S2->>S1: Vote
    S1->>App: New Primary - Re-election
```

---

## 4.6 Sharding: Range vs Hash

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RangeSharding[Range Sharding on country]
        r1[Shard 1: A-H countries]
        r2[Shard 2: I-P countries]
        r3[Shard 3: Q-Z countries]
    end
    subgraph HashSharding[Hash Sharding on hashed country]
        h1[Shard 1: hash%3 == 0]
        h2[Shard 2: hash%3 == 1]
        h3[Shard 3: hash%3 == 2]
    end
    RangeSharding -.- noteA[Pros: efficient range queries. Cons: uneven distribution]
    HashSharding -.- noteB[Pros: uniform distribution. Cons: range queries expensive]
```

---

## 4.7 Index Types Mind Map

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((MongoDB Indexes))
    Single Field
      Ascending 1
      Descending minus 1
    Compound
      ESR Rule
      Up to 32 fields
    Multikey
      Arrays indexed
    Text
      Full text search
      One per collection
    Geospatial
      2d
      2dsphere
    Hashed
      Shard key
    TTL
      Auto expire
```

---

## 4.8 Document DB vs RDBMS ŌĆö System Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RDBMSBlock[RDBMS World]
        rdb1[Tables with Fixed Schema]
        rdb2[Rows and Columns]
        rdb3[SQL Query Language]
        rdb4[Vertical Scaling]
        rdb5[JOINs for Relations]
    end
    subgraph DocBlock[Document DB World]
        doc1[Collections with Dynamic Schema]
        doc2[BSON Documents]
        doc3[MQL Query Language]
        doc4[Horizontal Scaling]
        doc5[Embedding and Lookup]
    end
    RDBMSBlock -.- DocBlock
```

# ­¤ōÆ SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions are modeled on **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End Semester Evaluation)** pattern. Marks distribution: Part A = 3 marks each, Part B = 14 marks each (with internal choice).

---

## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the concept of a Document Database. List any two key features of MongoDB.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Document Database is a type of NoSQL database that stores data in **semi-structured documents**, typically in JSON/BSON format, grouped into collections rather than rigid tables. Each document contains key-value pairs and may include nested sub-documents and arrays. **(1 mark)**

**Key Features of MongoDB:** **(2 marks)**
1. **Schema flexibility** ŌĆö documents in the same collection need not share the same structure or fields.
2. **Horizontal scalability** ŌĆö supports **sharding** to distribute data across multiple servers.
3. *Bonus point:* Native BSON storage, rich query language (MQL), aggregation pipeline, replica sets for high availability.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is BSON? How is it different from JSON?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**BSON (Binary JSON)** is a binary-encoded serialization format used by MongoDB to store documents and exchange data over the wire. **(1 mark)**

**Differences:** **(2 marks)**

| Aspect | JSON | BSON |
|---|---|---|
| Format | Textual (UTF-8) | Binary |
| Types | String, number, boolean, array, object, null | Adds `int`, `long`, `double`, `Date`, `ObjectId`, `Binary`, `Decimal128` |
| Parsing | Slower (text parsing) | Faster (binary, length-prefixed) |
| Size | Compact for small data | Slightly larger overhead but efficient traversal |

---

## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 4 ŌĆö Internal Choice Pattern:** Each question has two sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question B1 ŌĆö Option A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Discuss the architecture of MongoDB. Explain the role of Replica Sets and Sharding with suitable diagrams. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**MongoDB Architecture (Overview):** MongoDB follows a **distributed, document-oriented** architecture. A production deployment typically consists of:
- **Application drivers** (Node.js, Python, Java, etc.) connecting via the **wire protocol** over TCP.
- **Mongos** ŌĆö a stateless query router that fronts the application.
- **Config servers** ŌĆö store cluster metadata (chunk ŌåÆ shard mapping).
- **Shards** ŌĆö each shard is a **replica set** storing a subset of the data. **(2 marks)**

**Replica Sets:** A Replica Set is a group of `mongod` instances maintaining identical copies of data. It consists of:
- **One Primary** ŌĆö handles all write operations.
- **Multiple Secondaries** ŌĆö replicate the primary's oplog and can serve reads.
- **Arbiter** (optional) ŌĆö participates in elections but holds no data.

When the primary fails, an automatic **election** (within ~10ŌĆō12 seconds) promotes a secondary to primary. Minimum recommended: **3 members**. **(2.5 marks)**

**Sharding:** Sharding is **horizontal partitioning** of data across multiple shards. Components:
- **Shard** ŌĆö a `mongod` (or replica set) holding a portion of the data.
- **Config Server** ŌĆö stores metadata.
- **Mongos** ŌĆö routes queries to the right shard.

The **shard key** determines data distribution:
- **Range-based** (good for range queries, can be uneven).
- **Hash-based** (uniform distribution, range queries expensive).
- **Zone-based** (geographic / policy-driven).

A sharded collection is divided into **chunks** (default 64 MB), auto-rebalanced by the **balancer**. **(2.5 marks)**

---

**(b) Explain the following MongoDB concepts with examples: (i) Embedded documents (ii) Aggregation pipeline (iii) Indexing. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Embedded Documents (2 marks):**
Embedded documents allow nesting of related data within a single document, modeling **1-to-few** relationships without JOINs.

```javascript
{
  _id: 1,
  name: "Ananya",
  address: {
    street: "MG Road",
    city: "Kochi",
    pin: 682011
  }
}
```
**Advantages:** atomic writes, single read, no JOIN overhead.
**Limitation:** doc size Ōēż 16 MB.

**(ii) Aggregation Pipeline (2.5 marks):**
The aggregation pipeline processes documents through a series of stages ŌĆö each transforming the data stream.

```javascript
db.orders.aggregate([
  { $match:   { status: "paid" } },
  { $group: { _id: "$category", total: { $sum: "$amount" } } },
  { $sort:   { total: -1 } }
])
```
Common stages: `$match`, `$group`, `$sort`, `$project`, `$lookup`, `$unwind`, `$limit`, `$skip`, `$out`.

**(iii) Indexing (2.5 marks):**
Indexes are **B-tree** data structures that speed up queries.

```javascript
db.students.createIndex({ roll_no: 1 })                    // Single
db.students.createIndex({ year: 1, cgpa: -1 })              // Compound (ESR rule)
db.students.createIndex({ name: "text" })                   // Text
db.students.createIndex({ "address.city": 1 })              // On nested field
db.students.createIndex({ createdAt: 1 }, { expireAfterSeconds: 3600 }) // TTL
```
Index types: single, compound, multikey, text, geospatial, hashed, TTL. **ESR rule:** Equality ŌåÆ Sort ŌåÆ Range in compound indexes.

---

### **Question B1 ŌĆö Option B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Compare and contrast RDBMS with Document-oriented databases. When would you prefer a document DB? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Aspect | RDBMS | Document DB (MongoDB) |
|---|---|---|
| Data Model | Tables, rows, columns | Collections, BSON documents |
| Schema | Rigid, predefined | Flexible, schema-less |
| Relationships | Foreign keys + JOINs | Embedding + `$lookup` |
| Query Language | SQL | MQL (JSON-like) |
| Transactions | Full ACID, multi-row | Single-doc atomic; multi-doc ACID since 4.0 |
| Scaling | Vertical (scale-up) | Horizontal (scale-out via sharding) |
| Consistency | Strong by default | Tunable (read/write concern) |
| Best For | Structured, transactional | Semi-structured, agile, cloud-native |

**When to prefer Document DB (2 marks):**
1. **Schema evolves frequently** (mobile, IoT, social apps).
2. **Object-oriented data** fits naturally into JSON.
3. **Massive horizontal scale** is required (millions of writes/sec).
4. **Heterogeneous data** (different fields per document).
5. **Geographic distribution** of data (zone sharding).

Avoid document DB for: complex multi-table JOINs, heavy OLAP/BI, strict ACID legacy systems.

---

**(b) Explain CRUD operations in MongoDB with one example query for each. Also explain aggregation pipeline with a sample pipeline. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**CRUD Operations (4 marks):**

**Create (1 mark):**
```javascript
db.students.insertOne({
  name: "Ananya", roll_no: "KTU2024CS101", year: 2, cgpa: 8.74
})
```

**Read (1 mark):**
```javascript
db.students.find({ year: 2, cgpa: { $gt: 8.0 } })
            .projection({ name: 1, cgpa: 1, _id: 0 })
            .sort({ cgpa: -1 })
            .limit(5)
```

**Update (1 mark):**
```javascript
db.students.updateOne(
  { roll_no: "KTU2024CS101" },
  { $set: { cgpa: 9.0 }, $push: { skills: "MongoDB" } }
)
```

**Delete (1 mark):**
```javascript
db.students.deleteMany({ year: 1 })
```

**Aggregation Pipeline (3 marks):**
```javascript
db.orders.aggregate([
  { $match:   { status: "paid" } },
  { $group:   { _id: "$category", revenue: { $sum: "$amount" }, n: { $sum: 1 } } },
  { $sort:    { revenue: -1 } },
  { $project: { category: "$_id", _id: 0, revenue: 1, n: 1 } }
])
```
**Pipeline semantics:** each stage consumes the output of the previous stage. `$match` filters, `$group` aggregates, `$sort` orders, `$project` reshapes.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **­¤Ü© Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Avoid These to Score 100%)**
>
> 1. **Confusing JSON with BSON:** Many students write "MongoDB stores JSON". It stores **BSON**. JSON is just the textual representation; BSON is the binary on-disk and on-wire format. **(-1 mark)**
>
> 2. **Claiming MongoDB has no ACID:** MongoDB has supported **multi-document ACID transactions** since v4.0 (replica sets) and v4.2 (sharded clusters). State this precisely. **(-2 marks if claimed otherwise)**
>
> 3. **Confusing replica sets with sharding:** Replica Sets = **redundancy / high availability** (same data, multiple copies). Sharding = **horizontal partitioning** (different data subsets). Students often swap these. **(-2 marks)**
>
> 4. **ObjectId mistake:** Stating ObjectId is "random" or "auto-increment integer". It is a **12-byte value** = 4-byte timestamp + 5-byte random + 3-byte counter, **roughly time-ordered**. **(-1 mark)**
>
> 5. **Forgetting 16 MB limit:** When designing embedded models, forgetting the **16 MB BSON document limit** is a frequent design error. **(-1 mark)**
>
> 6. **Index direction blindness:** Not mentioning the **ESR rule** (Equality ŌåÆ Sort ŌåÆ Range) for compound indexes. **(-1 mark)**
>
> 7. **Embedding everything:** Choosing embedding for **1-to-many unbounded** relationships (e.g., 100,000 comments) ŌĆö wrong; use **referencing + bucket pattern**. **(-1 mark)**
>
> 8. **Skipping diagram in 7-mark questions:** KTU valuation keys explicitly allocate marks for diagrams in architecture/explanation questions. Always include a **Mermaid/hand-drawn block diagram**. **(-2 marks)**
>
> 9. **Using "Document DB = MongoDB":** MongoDB is **an example** of a document DB; other examples include **CouchDB, Couchbase, Amazon DocumentDB, Cosmos DB (document API), RavenDB**. Generic definition loses no marks; over-restrictive definition may. **(-1 mark)**
>
> 10. **Forgetting write concern:** When discussing durability, mentioning only "writes go to disk" without **write concern** (`w: "majority"`) and **journaling** is incomplete. **(-1 mark)**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **­¤ōī High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam.)**

### ­¤öæ One-Line Definitions
- **Document DB:** A NoSQL database storing semi-structured data as JSON/BSON documents in collections.
- **MongoDB:** The most popular open-source document database, written in C++, using BSON.
- **BSON:** Binary JSON; supports extra types like `ObjectId`, `Date`, `int`, `long`, `Decimal128`.
- **Collection:** Schema-less grouping of documents (analogous to a table).
- **Replica Set:** Group of `mongod` instances maintaining identical data for high availability.
- **Sharding:** Horizontal partitioning of a collection across multiple shards.
- **Mongos:** Stateless query router fronting the application.
- **Aggregation Pipeline:** Stage-based document transformation engine.

### ŌÜÖ’ĖÅ Critical Numbers (Memorize!)
- BSON max document size: **16 MB** (16,777,216 bytes).
- ObjectId: **12 bytes** (4 timestamp + 5 random + 3 counter).
- Replica set: **min 3 members**, max **50 members**, max **7 voting**.
- Default chunk size: **64 MB**.
- WiredTiger checkpoint: every **60 seconds**.
- Re-election timeout: **~10ŌĆō12 seconds**.
- `w: "majority"`: $\lfloor N/2 \rfloor + 1$ members.
- TTL purge: every **60 seconds** by background thread.

### ­¤¦Ā Operator Quick Reference
- **Comparison:** `$eq, $ne, $gt, $gte, $lt, $lte, $in, $nin`
- **Logical:** `$and, $or, $not, $nor`
- **Element:** `$exists, $type`
- **Array:** `$all, $size, $elemMatch`
- **Update:** `$set, $unset, $inc, $push, $pull, $addToSet`

### ­¤ōÉ Design Heuristics
- **Embed** for 1-to-few, bounded, read-together.
- **Reference** for 1-to-many, unbounded, independently accessed.
- **Bucket pattern** for 1-to-many-unbounded + read together.
- **ESR rule** for compound indexes.
- **Hash shard** for uniform writes; **range shard** for range queries.
- **3 members minimum** for replica set; odd number for elections.

### ­¤åÜ RDBMS Ōåö Document DB Pairs
- Table ŌåÆ Collection
- Row ŌåÆ Document
- Column ŌåÆ Field
- Primary Key ŌåÆ `_id`
- JOIN ŌåÆ `$lookup` / Embedding
- Schema ŌåÆ Schema Validation (optional)

### Ō£ģ MongoDB = ACID-Capable (since 4.0)
Don't write: "MongoDB is not ACID".
Write: "MongoDB supports multi-document ACID transactions in replica sets (since v4.0) and sharded clusters (since v4.2), with single-document atomicity always guaranteed."

### ­¤Ä» Cross-References for Module 4
- **Other NoSQL types** in Module 4: Key-Value (Redis), Column-Family (Cassandra, HBase), Graph (Neo4j).
- **CAP theorem** places MongoDB in CP by default.
- **ACID vs BASE** ŌĆö MongoDB is BASE-flexible with ACID options.

### ­¤¦Š 4 Memory Hooks
1. **"Folders in Boxes"** ŌåÆ Documents in Collections.
2. **"3-1-2"** ŌåÆ Replica Set min **3** members, **1** primary, **2** secondaries.
3. **"16-12-64"** ŌåÆ 16 MB doc, 12-byte ObjectId, 64 MB chunk.
4. **"ESR"** ŌåÆ Equality, Sort, Range (compound index order).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Main Characteristics of Column-Family DB (Examples from Cassandra) and Graph DB (Examples from ArangoDB)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Main Characteristics of Column-Family DB (Examples from Cassandra) and Graph DB (Examples from ArangoDB)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Column-Family Database (Wide-Column Store)

A **Column-Family Database** (also called a **Wide-Column Store** or **Column-Oriented DBMS**) is a schema-flexible NoSQL database that stores data in **tables, rows, and dynamic columns**, where each row can have a different set of columns, and columns are grouped into **column families**. The data is physically stored column-by-column on disk (not row-by-row), making it highly optimized for analytical queries, large-scale distributed workloads, and sparse datasets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** A column-family DB organizes data as a sparse, distributed, multi-dimensional sorted map indexed by a row key, a column key, and a timestamp. The model is "wide" because rows can hold an unbounded number of columns, and "columnar" because the storage engine groups values of the same column together on disk.

**Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Filing Cabinet of Expanding Folders":**
Imagine a huge office filing cabinet. Each drawer is a **Keyspace** (top-level namespace, equivalent to a database in SQL). Inside the drawer are folders representing **Column Families**. Each folder is a collection of **Rows** identified by a unique **Row Key**. But unlike a rigid SQL table where every row has the *same* set of columns, here each folder (row) can contain wildly different numbers of files (columns) and even different *types* of files. One customer record may have 3 columns, another may have 300. The system only reads the files you actually ask for ŌĆö super fast for sparse, evolving data.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it is "Column-Family":** Columns are *not* loose ŌĆö they are pre-grouped into families (e.g., `personal_info`, `address_info`, `login_info`) so the engine can fetch all related columns in a single disk seek. This is the defining trait distinguishing it from pure key-value or document stores.

---

## 1.2 Graph Database

A **Graph Database** is a NoSQL database that uses **graph structures** ŌĆö consisting of **nodes** (entities/vertices), **edges** (relationships), and **properties** (key-value attributes on both nodes and edges) ŌĆö to represent and store data. The relationship itself is a first-class citizen; queries traverse the graph along edges rather than computing expensive JOINs.

**Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Social Network Map":**
Picture Facebook's friendship graph. You are a **node** (a circle). Your friend is another **node**. The line connecting you is an **edge** (a relationship labeled "FRIEND_WITH"). Each node has **properties** (your name, birthday, hometown) and each edge also has properties (since 2018, relationship type). To answer "Find all friends of my friends who live in Kerala," the database just *walks* the graph from node to node ŌĆö no JOINs, no complex index lookups. This is what makes graph databases powerful for relationship-heavy queries (recommendations, fraud detection, social networks, knowledge graphs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight ŌĆö Property Graph Model:** Most modern graph DBs (including ArangoDB's default model) use the **Property Graph Model**: directed, labeled edges; nodes/edges carry arbitrary key-value properties; both can be indexed.

---

## 1.3 Why These Two Categories? The KTU Motivation

| Big Data Problem | Why Traditional RDBMS Fails | Best Fit |
|---|---|---|
| Billions of writes/sec across data centers | Rigid schema + vertical scaling | **Column-Family (Cassandra)** |
| Highly connected data (social, fraud) | JOIN explosion, poor performance | **Graph (ArangoDB)** |
| Sparse, evolving attributes | NULL bloat, schema migrations | **Column-Family (Cassandra)** |
| Multi-hop relationship traversal | Recursive CTEs are slow | **Graph (ArangoDB)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** Module 4 specifically targets *distributed/specialized* NoSQL models. Module 4 is the broadest of the NoSQL modules ŌĆö covering both **aggregate-oriented** (column-family) and **relational/relationship-oriented** (graph) databases.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Apache Cassandra ŌĆö Deep Dive

### 2.1.1 Origin & Design Philosophy
- Originally developed at **Facebook (2008)** for Inbox Search; now an **Apache top-level project**.
- Inspired by **Google Bigtable** (data model) and **Amazon Dynamo** (distribution).
- Designed to run on **commodity hardware** across multiple data centers with **no single point of failure**.

### 2.1.2 Main Characteristics (KTU High-Yield)

1. **Distributed & Decentralized (Peer-to-Peer Architecture)**
   - Every node is identical. No master, no slave, no "primary" node.
   - Uses a **Gossip Protocol** (peer-to-peer communication) for node state dissemination.

2. **Eventual Consistency (Tunable Consistency)**
   - Default consistency is **eventual**; users can tune to **strong** for critical reads/writes.
   - Governed by the **CAP Theorem**: Cassandra is an **AP system** (Availability + Partition tolerance).

3. **Schema-flexible (Wide Rows)**
   - Each row can have a different number of columns.
   - Supports **composite keys**, **clustering columns**, and **collections** (list, set, map).

4. **Linear Scalability**
   - Adding nodes linearly increases throughput.
   - No downtime, no rebalancing required for simple topologies.

5. **High Write Throughput**
   - Writes are first appended to a **commit log** (durability) and then to a **memtable** (in-memory).
   - When memtable fills, it is flushed to an **SSTable** (Sorted String Table) on disk.
   - Writes are *append-only* ŌĆö no in-place updates ŌåÆ very fast.

6. **CQL (Cassandra Query Language)**
   - SQL-like syntax for familiar adoption, but does **not** support JOINs, subqueries, or GROUP BY.
   - Primary data access is **key-based lookup** + **range scans within a partition**.

7. **Partitioning & Replication**
   - **Partition Key** determines node placement using consistent hashing.
   - **Replication Factor (N)** governs how many copies exist.
   - **Consistency Level (CL)**: ONE, QUORUM, ALL, LOCAL_QUORUM, EACH_QUORUM, etc.

8. **Tunable Consistency Formula**
   - For **R** (read replicas) and **W** (write replicas), strong consistency requires:
   $$\text{Strong Consistency Condition} \implies R + W > N$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Hot Topic:** The condition **R + W > N** is a frequently asked question. With N=3, if W=2 and R=2, you get strong consistency (2+2 > 3). If W=1 and R=1, you get eventual consistency.

### 2.1.3 Cassandra Data Model Hierarchy

$$\text{Keyspace} \rightarrow \text{Table} \rightarrow \text{Row (Partition)} \rightarrow \text{Column}$$

- **Keyspace**: Top-level container. Defines replication strategy (`SimpleStrategy` or `NetworkTopologyStrategy`).
- **Table**: Modern CQL terminology (legacy term: "Column Family").
- **Row**: Identified by a **Primary Key** consisting of:
  - **Partition Key** (mandatory) ŌĆö determines which node stores the row.
  - **Clustering Columns** (optional) ŌĆö sorts rows *within* a partition.

### 2.1.4 Cassandra Write Path (Logical Flow)

> 1. Client sends write ŌåÆ Coordinator Node.
> 2. Write is appended to **Commit Log** (sequential disk write, crash recovery).
> 3. Write is added to **Memtable** (in-memory sorted structure).
> 4. When Memtable is full ŌåÆ flushed to **SSTable** on disk.
> 5. Periodically, **compaction** merges SSTables for read efficiency.
> 6. A **hint** is stored on coordinator if a replica is down.

### 2.1.5 Cassandra Read Path

> 1. Client ŌåÆ Coordinator Node.
> 2. Coordinator queries the **Partition Key Index** to find owning replicas.
> 3. Reads from **Bloom Filter** (membership test ŌĆö "may contain?").
> 4. If positive, checks **Partition Key Cache** ŌåÆ **Partition Summary** ŌåÆ **Partition Index** ŌåÆ **Data.db** SSTable.
> 5. Reads from multiple SSTables are merged; latest timestamp wins (tombstones handled).

---

## 2.2 ArangoDB ŌĆö Deep Dive

### 2.2.1 Origin & Design Philosophy
- **ArangoDB** is a **multi-model** NoSQL database supporting **graph, document, and key-value** data models in a single engine.
- Released in **2011** by triAGENS GmbH (Germany).
- Native multi-model design ŌĆö graph is *not* layered on top of documents; it is a first-class citizen.

### 2.2.2 Main Characteristics (KTU High-Yield)

1. **Multi-Model Engine**
   - **Document** model (JSON-like, similar to MongoDB).
   - **Graph** model (nodes + edges, with properties).
   - **Key/Value** model (via documents).
   - All accessed through a single query language: **AQL (ArangoDB Query Language)**.

2. **Property Graph Model**
   - **Vertices** (nodes) and **Edges** (relationships) both have properties.
   - Edges are **directed** and **labeled**.
   - Edges are *first-class* documents stored as JSON.

3. **AQL (ArangoDB Query Language)**
   - Declarative, SQL-inspired syntax.
   - Supports graph traversals, pattern matching, joins, and aggregations.
   - Example pattern: `FOR v, e, p IN 1..3 OUTBOUND 'users/alice' GRAPH 'social'`.

4. **Flexible Schema (Schema-Free)**
   - Documents are JSON ŌĆö no rigid schema, but optional **schema validation** is supported.
   - Schemaless by design.

5. **Horizontal Scalability with SmartGraphs & OneShard**
   - **SmartGraphs** distribute graphs across shards based on vertex attributes to keep traversals local.
   - **OneShard** mode places the entire graph in a single shard for very high-performance traversals.

6. **Foxx Microservices Framework**
   - Server-side JavaScript microservices (built on V8 engine).
   - Allows custom API endpoints directly inside the database.

7. **ACID Transactions**
   - Unlike many NoSQL systems, ArangoDB supports **multi-document ACID transactions**.
   - Even across collections.

8. **Indexes**
   - **Hash**, **Skip-List**, **Persistent**, **Geo**, **Fulltext** indexes supported.
   - Graph-specific: **edge indexes** for fast traversal start points.

9. **Storage Engine ŌĆö RocksDB (default)**
   - LSM-tree based, key-value store.
   - Optimized for write throughput and compression.

### 2.2.3 ArangoDB Data Model Hierarchy

$$\text{Database} \rightarrow \text{Collection} \rightarrow \text{Document (Vertex/Edge)}$$

- **Database**: Logical container (multi-database supported).
- **Collection**: A set of documents. Two special types:
  - **Document Collection** (for vertices)
  - **Edge Collection** (for edges ŌĆö has `_from` and `_to` attributes).

### 2.2.4 Graph Traversal in ArangoDB

A traversal in AQL uses the **`traversal()`** function or a `FOR ... IN` graph loop:

```aql
FOR v, e, p IN 1..3 OUTBOUND 'persons/alice' GRAPH 'myGraph'
  FILTER v.age > 25
  RETURN v.name
```

- `1..3` = minimum 1 hop, maximum 3 hops.
- `OUTBOUND` = direction of edge traversal.
- `v` = vertex, `e` = edge, `p` = full path.

### 2.2.5 Why ArangoDB over Neo4j?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Distinction:** Neo4j is a **pure graph DB** (uses Cypher). ArangoDB is a **multi-model DB with native graph support** (uses AQL). ArangoDB is often preferred when the application also needs document storage. Neo4j has slightly more mature graph algorithms. For exam purposes, ArangoDB's multi-model nature is the key distinguishing point.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Master Comparison Table)

| Property | Cassandra (Column-Family) | ArangoDB (Graph/Multi-Model) |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Model** | Wide-column tables (rows of dynamic columns) | Property graph (nodes, edges, properties) |
| **Primary Use Case** | Time-series, write-heavy, distributed OLTP | Relationship-heavy queries, recommendation engines |
| **Query Language** | CQL (SQL-like, no JOINs) | AQL (SQL-like, supports graph traversal) |
| **Schema** | Flexible (column-family schema) | Schema-free (optional validation) |
| **Consistency** | Eventual (tunable: R + W > N) | Strong (ACID transactions) |
| **CAP Theorem** | **AP** (Availability + Partition tolerance) | Closer to **CP** (Consistency + Partition tolerance) |
| **Distribution** | Masterless / Peer-to-Peer (gossip) | Master-slave / sharded (with replication) |
| **Write Path** | Commit Log ŌåÆ Memtable ŌåÆ SSTable | RocksDB LSM tree (append-only writes) |
| **Indexing** | Primary key, secondary indexes | Hash, skiplist, geo, fulltext, edge |
| **Joins** | Not supported (denormalization required) | Supported (graph + relational in AQL) |
| **Transactions** | Lightweight (per-partition) | Full ACID (multi-document) |
| **Horizontal Scalability** | Excellent (linear) | Good (SmartGraphs, OneShard) |
| **Storage Engine** | SSTable (LSM-based) | RocksDB (LSM-based) |
| **Storage Format** | Columnar on disk | Document (JSON) on disk |
| **Best For** | IoT, sensor data, messaging, logs | Social networks, fraud detection, knowledge graphs |

---

## 2.4 KTU Frequently Tested Concepts

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Recall ŌĆö Cassandra Replication Formula:**
> $$\boxed{\text{Strong Consistency} \iff R + W > N}$$
> $$\text{Where: } N = \text{Replication Factor}, \; R = \text{Read Replicas}, \; W = \text{Write Replicas}$$
> For N=3: typical strong config is **W=2, R=2** (Quorum-based). Eventual: **W=1, R=1**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Recall ŌĆö ArangoDB Graph Components:**
> - **Vertex (Node)** = entity
> - **Edge (Relationship)** = directed, labeled, first-class
> - **Property** = attribute on vertex/edge
> - **Path** = sequence of vertices and edges during traversal

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Cassandra Consistency Calculation

**Problem (KTU-style):**
A Cassandra cluster uses **Replication Factor N = 5**. The application requires strong consistency for write operations but tolerates weaker consistency for reads. Configure the minimum **W** and **R** values to guarantee strong consistency for writes and reads.

### Solution Walkthrough

**Step 1: Recall the strong consistency condition.**

For strong consistency:
$$R + W > N$$

Given:
$$N = 5$$

**Step 2: Determine the minimum values.**

We need integer values where R + W > 5.
Smallest valid pair: R = 3, W = 3 (since 3 + 3 = 6 > 5).

**Step 3: Verify.**
$$R + W = 3 + 3 = 6$$
$$6 > 5 \;\;\checkmark$$

**Step 4: Alternative configurations.**

| Configuration | R | W | R+W | Result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Quorum Read/Write | 3 | 3 | 6 | Strong (6 > 5) |
| Fast Write, Slow Read | 5 | 1 | 6 | Strong but slow read |
| Fast Read, Slow Write | 1 | 5 | 6 | Strong but slow write |
| Weak | 1 | 1 | 2 | Eventual (2 Ōēż 5) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** The condition is **R + W > N**, not **R + W Ōēź N**. Many students lose marks by writing Ōēź instead of >.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Cassandra Data Model ŌĆö CQL Table Design

**Problem:**
Design a CQL table to store user profiles where:
- A user has a unique `user_id`.
- Each user can have a variable number of `email` addresses and a variable number of `phone` numbers.
- The system must allow fast lookup by user_id.

### Solution

**Step 1: Identify Partition Key and Clustering Columns.**

The `user_id` should be the **partition key** (fast direct lookup). Email and phone can be stored as **collections** (set/list) within the same partition.

**Step 2: Write the CQL.**

```sql
CREATE TABLE user_profiles (
    user_id   uuid PRIMARY KEY,
    full_name text,
    age       int,
    emails    set<text>,         -- collection: set of emails
    phones    list<text>,        -- collection: ordered list
    addresses map<text, text>    -- collection: type ŌåÆ value
);
```

**Step 3: Insert sample data.**

```sql
INSERT INTO user_profiles (user_id, full_name, age, emails, phones, addresses)
VALUES (
  uuid(),
  'Anjali S',
  22,
  {'anjali@gmail.com', 'anjali.ku@outlook.com'},
  ['+91-9876543210', '+91-9123456780'],
  {'home': 'Kochi', 'work': 'Trivandrum'}
);
```

**Step 4: Query the table.**

```sql
SELECT full_name, emails FROM user_profiles WHERE user_id = 1a2b3c4d-...;
```

**Step 5: Explanation of design choices.**

> - **`set<text>`** for emails ŌåÆ no duplicates allowed, fast membership test.
> - **`list<text>`** for phones ŌåÆ preserves order, allows duplicates.
> - **`map<text, text>`** for addresses ŌåÆ key-value within the column.
> - All collections live *inside* a single partition ŌåÆ single read, no JOINs.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** In Cassandra, you **cannot** create an index on a collection directly (limited support). For multi-attribute queries, design a *secondary* table with a different partition key.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: ArangoDB Graph Design

**Problem:**
Design an ArangoDB graph for a movie recommendation system. The graph must support:
- Finding all movies a given actor has acted in.
- Finding all actors who acted with a given actor.
- Finding shortest path between two actors (Bacon number).

### Solution

**Step 1: Identify the entities (vertices) and relationships (edges).**

- **Vertices (Document Collections):**
  - `actors` ŌĆö with properties `name`, `dob`, `nationality`.
  - `movies` ŌĆö with properties `title`, `year`, `genre`.

- **Edges (Edge Collection):**
  - `acted_in` ŌĆö with properties `role`, `year`.

**Step 2: Create the collections.**

```aql
// Create document collection
db._create("actors");
db._create("movies");

// Create edge collection
db._createEdgeCollection("acted_in");
```

**Step 3: Define the named graph.**

```aql
var graph_module = require("@arangodb/general-graph");
graph_module._create("movieGraph", [
  graph_module._edgeDefinition("acted_in",
    ["actors"], ["movies"])
]);
```

**Step 4: Insert sample data.**

```aql
INSERT { _key: "tom_hanks", name: "Tom Hanks", dob: "1956-07-09" } INTO actors;
INSERT { _key: "forrest",   title: "Forrest Gump", year: 1994, genre: "Drama" } INTO movies;
INSERT { _from: "actors/tom_hanks", _to: "movies/forrest", role: "Forrest" } INTO acted_in;
```

**Step 5: Run a traversal ŌĆö Find movies of Tom Hanks.**

```aql
FOR v IN 1..1 OUTBOUND "actors/tom_hanks" acted_in
  RETURN v.title
```

**Step 6: Run a 2-hop traversal ŌĆö Find co-actors.**

```aql
FOR v, e, p IN 2..2 OUTBOUND "actors/tom_hanks" acted_in, INBOUND acted_in
  RETURN DISTINCT v.name
```

**Step 7: Run shortest path ŌĆö Tom Hanks to another actor.**

```aql
FOR v, e IN OUTBOUND SHORTEST_PATH
  "actors/tom_hanks" TO "actors/brad_pitt" acted_in
  RETURN { actor: v.name, via_movie: e._to }
```

**Step 8: Explanation of graph model.**

> - **Vertex document**: stores entity properties.
> - **Edge document**: stores relationship and its properties (e.g., `role`).
> - **`SHORTEST_PATH`**: built-in AQL keyword for BFS-style shortest path ŌĆö extremely useful for KTU questions.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Analytical Comparison (Often Asked in 14-Mark Questions)

**Question:** Compare Cassandra and ArangoDB in terms of: (a) data model, (b) consistency model, (c) query language, (d) ideal use cases.

### Model Answer Structure

**(a) Data Model:**
- **Cassandra:** Wide-column store. Data is organized into **keyspaces ŌåÆ tables ŌåÆ rows**. Rows are partitioned by a partition key and contain dynamic columns. Best for sparse, tabular data with high write rates.
- **ArangoDB:** Multi-model. Primary graph model uses **vertices (document collections) and edges (edge collections)** in a property graph. Also supports document and key-value models natively.

**(b) Consistency Model:**
- **Cassandra:** **Tunable / eventual consistency**. Governed by the formula **R + W > N**. Defaults to AP in CAP theorem.
- **ArangoDB:** **Strong consistency**. Supports **ACID multi-document transactions** across collections. Closer to CP in CAP theorem.

**(c) Query Language:**
- **Cassandra:** **CQL** ŌĆö SQL-like but restricted. No JOINs, no GROUP BY, no subqueries. Access is partition-key based.
- **ArangoDB:** **AQL** ŌĆö SQL-inspired. Supports JOINs, graph traversals, pattern matching, aggregations. More expressive than CQL.

**(d) Ideal Use Cases:**
- **Cassandra:** IoT sensor data, messaging platforms, time-series logs, write-heavy OLTP with global distribution. Examples: Netflix, Instagram.
- **ArangoDB:** Social networks, recommendation engines, fraud detection, knowledge graphs, network/IT operations. Examples: Deutsche Bank (fraud), Airbus (manufacturing graphs).

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5: Token Ring & Gossip Protocol Visualization

Cassandra uses a **consistent hashing ring** for partitioning. Given **N=4 nodes** and **replication factor=3**, show the placement of a key with hash value `H = 75` if token space is `0ŌĆō99`.

### Solution

**Step 1: Assign tokens to nodes** (simplified equal division):
- Node A: 0ŌĆō24
- Node B: 25ŌĆō49
- Node C: 50ŌĆō74
- Node D: 75ŌĆō99

**Step 2: Find primary replica for H=75.**

H=75 falls in Node D's range ŌåÆ **Primary = Node D**.

**Step 3: Find next two clockwise nodes for replication.**

- Primary: D (75)
- Replica 1: A (0ŌĆō24, clockwise from 100 ŌåÆ 0)
- Replica 2: B (25ŌĆō49)

**Step 4: Final result.**

Replicas for H=75 = {**D, A, B**}.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When the question asks for replica placement, walk the ring *clockwise* and pick the next N-1 distinct nodes. Don't jump over the partitioner boundary carelessly.

```mermaid
    coord2 -.Gossip.-> coord3

    coord1 --> rep3[Replica Node 3]

    rep2 --> commitlog2[Commit Log]

    memtable2 --> sstable2[SSTable on Disk]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 4]**
> **Q1.** List any **three main characteristics** of a **Column-Family Database**. Give an example.

**Model Answer:**
A column-family database is a NoSQL store that organizes data into rows and dynamic columns grouped into column families. Its three main characteristics are:

1. **Schema Flexibility:** Each row can have a different set of columns, and new columns can be added without altering the schema. This suits evolving, sparse datasets.
2. **Wide-Row Storage:** A single row can hold millions of columns, making it ideal for time-series and large-attribute entities.
3. **Distributed and Decentralized:** Data is partitioned across nodes using consistent hashing, and there is no single point of failure (masterless architecture). It offers linear horizontal scalability.

**Example:** **Apache Cassandra** ŌĆö used by Netflix, Instagram, and Uber for write-heavy, globally distributed workloads.

*(3 marks ŌĆö definition + 3 bullets + example)*

---

| **Primary Strength** | Relationship traversal (multi-hop) | Flexible semi-structured storage |

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 4]**
> **Q3 (a).** Explain the main characteristics of **Apache Cassandra** with its data model. Discuss the **CQL** features with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q3 (b).** Explain the **consistency model** of Cassandra. Derive the condition **R + W > N** and explain tunable consistency with examples. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer ŌĆö Q3 (a)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Apache Cassandra is an open-source, distributed, wide-column NoSQL database originally developed at Facebook in 2008. It is designed to handle massive volumes of write-heavy data across commodity servers with no single point of failure.

**Main Characteristics (4 marks):**

1. **Decentralized / Masterless Architecture:** All nodes are peers. There is no master-slave distinction. State dissemination is handled via a **gossip protocol** running every second.

2. **Linear Scalability:** Adding a node increases throughput proportionally without downtime.

3. **High Write Throughput via LSM-Tree:** Writes are append-only to a commit log and memtable before being flushed to SSTables ŌĆö no in-place updates.

4. **Schema-Flexible Wide Rows:** A row can have billions of columns, and each row in a table can have a different column set.

5. **Tunable Consistency:** Configurable per query using consistency levels (ONE, QUORUM, ALL, etc.).

6. **CQL (Cassandra Query Language):** SQL-like syntax, but supports only key-based access (no JOIN, no GROUP BY, no subqueries).

**CQL Example (2 marks):**

```sql
CREATE KEYSPACE IF NOT EXISTS ktu_university
  WITH replication = {'class': 'SimpleStrategy', 'replication_factor': 3};

USE ktu_university;

CREATE TABLE students (
   reg_no int PRIMARY KEY,
   name text,
   branch text,
   cgpa float,
   skills set<text>
);

INSERT INTO students (reg_no, name, branch, cgpa, skills)
VALUES (101, 'Anjali S', 'CSE', 9.2, {'DBMS', 'AI', 'ML'});

SELECT name, cgpa FROM students WHERE reg_no = 101;
```

**Conclusion (no marks):** Cassandra's blend of high availability, schema flexibility, and SQL-like interface makes it a top choice for globally distributed, write-intensive systems.

---

### Model Answer ŌĆö Q3 (b)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Cassandra offers **tunable consistency**, allowing the application to choose between strong and eventual consistency on a per-query basis. This is achieved by configuring the **consistency level (CL)** for each read and write.

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **N** = Number of replicas (Replication Factor).
- **R** = Number of replicas contacted for a read.
- **W** = Number of replicas that must acknowledge a write.

**Derivation of R + W > N (2 marks):**

For strong consistency, any read must see the most recent write. A read is guaranteed to see the latest write *if and only if* the set of replicas read overlaps with the set that received the latest write. This overlap requires at least one common node between R-reads and W-writes:

$$(R + W) > N$$

If **R + W Ōēż N**, it is *possible* that the read goes to replicas that did not receive the latest write ŌåÆ only **eventual consistency**.

**Worked Example (2 marks):**

For **N = 3**:
- **Strong (Quorum):** W = 2, R = 2 ŌåÆ 2 + 2 = 4 > 3 Ō£ō
- **Eventual:** W = 1, R = 1 ŌåÆ 1 + 1 = 2 Ōēż 3 Ō£Ś
- **Write-Heavy Strong:** W = 3, R = 1 ŌåÆ 3 + 1 = 4 > 3 Ō£ō (but slow writes)

**Common Consistency Levels (0.5 mark):**
- `ONE`: Fastest, weakest.
- `QUORUM`: R/W = ŌīłN/2Ōīē + 1 ŌĆö default for strong.
- `ALL`: Slowest, strongest.
- `LOCAL_QUORUM`: Strong within a single data center.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** Tunable consistency lets developers trade off latency vs. data accuracy based on workload needs.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 4 ŌĆö Internal Choice Alternative]**
> **Q4 (a).** Explain the main characteristics of **ArangoDB** as a graph database. Describe the **property graph model** with an example. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q4 (b).** Write and explain **AQL queries** for: (i) finding all movies an actor has acted in, (ii) finding co-actors within 2 hops, (iii) finding shortest path between two actors. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer ŌĆö Q4 (a)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
ArangoDB is a **multi-model NoSQL database** that natively supports **document, graph, and key-value** data models. It is built on the **RocksDB** storage engine and uses **AQL (ArangoDB Query Language)** for all data access.

**Main Characteristics (4 marks):**

1. **Multi-Model Engine:** A single database and query language for documents, graphs, and key-values ŌĆö no need for multiple databases.

2. **Property Graph Model:** Vertices (entities) and edges (relationships) both store properties as JSON documents. Edges are first-class citizens.

3. **AQL Query Language:** SQL-inspired, supports graph traversals, pattern matching, joins, and aggregations in one unified syntax.

4. **ACID Transactions:** Supports multi-document, multi-collection ACID transactions ŌĆö unusual for NoSQL.

5. **Flexible Schema:** Documents are JSON; optional schema validation available.

6. **Horizontal Scalability:** SmartGraphs and OneShard modes optimize distributed graph traversals.

7. **Foxx Microservices:** Server-side JavaScript for custom API endpoints inside the database.

**Property Graph Example (2 marks):**

A simple social network with two persons and a friendship edge:

| Type | Document |
|---|---|
| Vertex | `{ "_key": "alice", "name": "Alice", "age": 22, "city": "Kochi" }` |
| Vertex | `{ "_key": "bob",   "name": "Bob",   "age": 25, "city": "Trivandrum" }` |
| Edge   | `{ "_from": "persons/alice", "_to": "persons/bob", "since": 2018, "closeness": "best_friend" }` |

**Visual:**
- (Alice) ŌĆö[FRIEND, since 2018]ŌåÆ (Bob)

Here, both vertices and the edge carry **properties** ŌĆö this is the defining feature of the **Property Graph Model**.

---

### Model Answer ŌĆö Q4 (b)

**(i) All movies an actor has acted in (1 hop, OUTBOUND):**
```aql
FOR v IN 1..1 OUTBOUND "actors/tom_hanks" acted_in
  RETURN v.title
```

**(ii) Co-actors within 2 hops (find actors who acted with actors who acted with Tom Hanks):**
```aql
FOR v, e, p IN 2..2 OUTBOUND "actors/tom_hanks" acted_in, INBOUND acted_in
  FILTER v._id != "actors/tom_hanks"
  RETURN DISTINCT v.name
```

**(iii) Shortest path between two actors (e.g., Bacon number):**
```aql
FOR v, e IN OUTBOUND SHORTEST_PATH
  "actors/tom_hanks" TO "actors/kevin_bacon" acted_in
  RETURN { actor: v.name, edge: e }
```

**Explanation (4 marks):**
- `1..n` specifies the minimum and maximum hops in a traversal.
- `OUTBOUND` follows the edge direction; `INBOUND` follows it backward; `ANY` follows both.
- `SHORTEST_PATH` performs a BFS internally to find the minimum-hop path.
- `DISTINCT` ensures no duplicate vertices are returned.
- `p` (path) contains the full route ŌĆö useful for path analytics.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions in Cassandra Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the strict inequality:** Writing **R + W Ōēź N** instead of **R + W > N** (loses 1 mark).
> 2. **Confusing CQL features with SQL:** Claiming CQL supports JOINs or GROUP BY (loses 1-2 marks).
> 3. **Saying "Cassandra is CP":** It is an **AP system** (eventual consistency by default). Wrong ŌåÆ 1 mark lost.
> 4. **Forgetting Commit Log + Memtable:** In the write path, both are mandatory; skipping either loses a mark.
> 5. **Writing "primary" or "master" node:** Cassandra is masterless ŌĆö use "coordinator" or "peer".
>
> **Common Mark Deductions in ArangoDB Questions:**
> 1. **Calling ArangoDB a "pure" graph DB:** It is **multi-model** ŌĆö a critical distinction worth 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing AQL with Cypher:** AQL is the language, Cypher is Neo4j's. Mixing them up loses a mark.
> 3. **Forgetting `_from` and `_to` in edges:** Edge documents in ArangoDB *must* have these attributes; missing them loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Not using `DISTINCT` in multi-hop traversals:** Without it, duplicate vertices flood the result.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> ­¤ōī **Cassandra Key Points:**
> - **Wide-column NoSQL DB**, originally by Facebook, inspired by Bigtable and Dynamo.
> - **Masterless, peer-to-peer** architecture using **gossip protocol**.
> - **Write path:** Commit Log ŌåÆ Memtable ŌåÆ SSTable.
> - **Eventual consistency** by default; tunable via **R + W > N** for strong consistency.
> - **CQL** is SQL-like but **no JOINs, no GROUP BY, no subqueries** ŌĆö denormalize.
> - **AP system** in CAP theorem.
> - Used by Netflix, Instagram, Uber for high-write, distributed workloads.

> ­¤ōī **ArangoDB Key Points:**
> - **Multi-model NoSQL DB** ŌĆö supports **document, graph, and key-value** in one engine.
> - Uses the **Property Graph Model** (vertices + edges + properties).
> - **AQL** (ArangoDB Query Language) is SQL-inspired and supports **graph traversals**.
> - Edge collections store edges with mandatory `_from` and `_to` fields.
> - Supports **ACID multi-document transactions** ŌĆö rare for NoSQL.
> - **Closer to CP** in CAP theorem (strong consistency).
> - Built on **RocksDB** (LSM-tree engine).
> - Used for social networks, fraud detection, recommendation systems, knowledge graphs.

> ­¤ōī **Comparison Quick-Mnemonic:**
> - **Cassandra** = **C**olumnar + **A**vailability (AP) + D**e**centralized.
> - **ArangoDB** = **A**ggregate-of-models + **A**QL + ACID (CP-leaning).

> ­¤ōī **KTU-Mandated Definitions to Memorize:**
> 1. **Column Family** ŌĆö a group of related columns stored together.
> 2. **Property Graph** ŌĆö a graph where nodes and edges can have named properties.
> 3. **Eventual Consistency** ŌĆö all replicas will converge to the same value given no new updates.
> 4. **Gossip Protocol** ŌĆö peer-to-peer communication for cluster state dissemination.
> 5. **SSTable** ŌĆö Sorted String Table, the on-disk format used by Cassandra.
> 6. **SmartGraph** ŌĆö ArangoDB's technique to keep related vertices in the same shard.
> 7. **Replication Factor (N)** ŌĆö total copies of data across the cluster.

> ­¤ōī **Formulas & Conditions to Memorize:**
> - Strong Consistency: $\mathbf{R + W > N}$
> - Quorum Read/Write for N=3: $R = 2, W = 2$
> - Weak/Eventual: $R = 1, W = 1$
> - Bloom filter membership: probabilistic "may contain" ŌĆö false positives possible, no false negatives.
